lead: ヴィーガン イート トウキョウ
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-22-5 永森ビル1F

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Vegan Eat Tokyo opened on February 3, 2024 in Asakusa, in a space that previously housed a kimono shop. The restaurant's plant-based menu is supervised by a French chef, blending French technique with an Italian-leaning, fully vegan menu using organic ingredients.
bio.specialty: Kuruma-fu katsu (breaded and fried cutlet made from kuruma-fu, a traditional wheat-gluten "wheel-shaped" fu) and smoked mushroom vegan carbonara are the most frequently cited signature dishes, alongside a lotus-root burger, chickpea and tomato stew, and vegan curry.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant positions itself as proof that vegan food can be indulgent and French/Italian-inspired rather than ascetic, aiming to win over both vegan and non-vegan diners (including many international visitors) with familiar comfort-food formats reimagined as 100% plant-based.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened February 3, 2024 in a location that was formerly a kimono shop, and has since drawn a large international clientele, reportedly over 1,300 visitors a month via the HappyCow app.
A food-industry equipment trade report profiled the shop's opening and kitchen setup as case #48 in its restaurant report series.

caveats: Coordinates could not be verified against the GSI geocoder or any map API: the sandbox's egress proxy returned policy-denial 403s for msearch.gsi.go.jp, Nominatim, Tabelog, and the restaurant's own site (veganeat.base.shop), so all facts here are drawn from WebSearch result snippets of secondary listing sites (Tabelog search snippet, Vegewel, VegeTime, Hotpepper, Gnavi, a note.com trade report), not directly fetched primary pages. Multiple independent sources agree on the address, hours, and menu, giving reasonable confidence, but nothing here was confirmed by directly reading the Tabelog or official page. GF safety note: despite being marketed as a health-forward "organic vegan" spot, its own most-cited signature dish (kuruma-fu katsu) is built on wheat gluten (fu), and another signature (carbonara) implies wheat pasta — so this is a vegan destination, not a gluten-free one, and should not be recommended to celiac diners without them personally screening the full menu.
closed_days: Sources conflict/have changed: through 2024 it was reported as open 7 days a week with no fixed closed day; as of January 2025 onward, sources indicate it moved to irregular (non-fixed) closures announced via Instagram.
cuisine: Organic vegan, French-chef-supervised, Italian-leaning plant-based cuisine
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant is well used to international/vegan-traveler visitors (cited as drawing over a thousand foreign visitors a month via HappyCow) but no explicit mention of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found in available sources.
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the sandbox's network egress policy blocked (403) direct requests to msearch.gsi.go.jp, Nominatim, and Tabelog/official site, so no primary-source coordinates could be retrieved. Multiple secondary listings independently describe the location as a 3-8 minute walk from Asakusa Station (Asakusa Line exit A4 ~5 min, Ginza Line exit 1 ~8 min, Tobu Isesaki Line central exit ~8 min, ~256m from the Tsukuba Express Asakusa exit), which places it well within the 0.8km radius of the Sensōji anchor point, but exact lat/lng are unverified and set to null rather than guessed.
gf_detail: No source describes gluten-free accommodation, a gluten-free menu, or a dedicated fryer. To the contrary, the restaurant's own most-cited signature dish is "kuruma-fu katsu," a breaded, fried cutlet made from kuruma-fu — a wheat-gluten product — meaning wheat gluten is a headline ingredient rather than something avoided. Another signature, mushroom carbonara, implies conventional wheat pasta. No information was found on whether soy sauce used is wheat-free or on any cross-contamination protocols. This is not a suitable choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:45-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / Dinner 17:00-21:45 (L.O. 21:00), reported as daily through 2024; sources from 2025 note a shift to irregular closures announced on Instagram
name_ja: ヴィーガン イート トウキョウ
neighborhood: Asakusa, Taitō-ku (near Asakusa Station)

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described in multiple listings as a fully vegan kitchen (French-chef-supervised, organic, plant-based menu) with no animal products on the menu, which by design limits vegan cross-contact risk, though no explicit cross-contact protocol statement was found.

positives.text: Listed as a certified vegan restaurant recognized by NPO Veg Project Japan, and highly rated (around 4.7/5 on Google Maps as of late 2024) by a large volume of international visitors via the HappyCow app.

seats_text: Not specified in available sources
vegan_detail: Reported as a fully plant-based, French-chef-supervised menu (organic beer, wine, and sake also offered), recognized/certified by NPO Veg Project Japan. Signature items include lotus-root burger, chickpea and tomato stew, vegan curry, mushroom carbonara, and kuruma-fu katsu — no meat, fish, dairy, or egg reported anywhere in sourced descriptions. No specific statement was found confirming absence of hidden bonito/katsuo dashi, but given the restaurant's vegan-certification and full plant-based positioning, this is a lower-risk concern than at a typical omnivore restaurant offering "vegan options."

bio.background: Founded on June 10, 1929 (Shōwa 4) beside the Sumida River near the Kaminarimon/Komagata-bashi area of Asakusa. According to the company's own history, founder Tazō Nakajima had worked for a produce (vegetable/fruit) wholesaler and wanted to start a food business of his own; he is said to have traveled the country searching for the best mountain yam before settling on a strain and opening a shop built entirely around tororo (grated yam). The restaurant has been run by the same family and stayed on essentially the same stretch of riverbank for nearly a century, expanding over the decades into a multi-floor kaiseki restaurant while keeping the original tororo recipe at its core.
bio.philosophy: The shop's founding wish was simply 'we want everyone to be able to eat one bowl of mugitoro' — a focus on making a single humble, health-associated dish available to ordinary diners, which still shapes the menu today.
bio.specialty: Mugitoro (麦とろ) — barley-mixed rice topped with tororo (grated Japanese mountain yam, made from a strong, low-astringency yamato-imo), seasoned with a secret bonito-and-kelp dashi recipe passed down since the 1929 founding. The restaurant also serves broader tororo-kaiseki courses and a 'guro-don' (fatty tuna) rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tazō Nakajima worked at a produce company and, wanting to go into a food business of his own, is said to have traveled the country before finding what he considered the finest mountain yam — the discovery that became the basis for the restaurant.
The shop hung its noren for the first time on June 10, 1929, at the foot of the Sumida River, with the founding wish that 'everyone could eat a single bowl of mugitoro.'

caveats: This session's WebFetch tool returned HTTP 403 for every URL attempted, including the Tabelog page, the restaurant's own official site, several review/blog pages, Wikipedia, and even a neutral control URL (example.com) — indicating a tool-level outage rather than a site-specific block. All facts below therefore come from WebSearch result snippets/summaries (which did return content) rather than directly fetched and re-read page text, and the GSI address-geocoding API could not be queried live, so coordinates are an estimate (see geocode_note). No confirmation of a live GF or vegan accommodation was found for this restaurant; treat gf/vegan fields as conservative defaults, not a confirmed 'safe' rating.
closed_days: No regular closed day found (appears to operate every day of the year, including holidays).
cuisine: Tororo kaiseki (mugitoro) — grated mountain yam over barley rice, kaiseki courses, and a fatty-tuna 'guro-don' rice bowl
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, high-traffic tourist destination steps from Sensō-ji with an English-language site/menu and English-language press coverage, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate even without Japanese.
geocode_note: Could not reach the GSI address-search API this session (all outbound WebFetch calls failed with HTTP 403, including to unrelated control domains, indicating a tool-level outage rather than a policy block). Coordinates below are an estimate based on the address's known location in Kaminarimon (雷門) 2-chome near the Sumida River / Komagata-bashi, consistent across multiple independent sources describing it as a short walk from Sensō-ji — treat as approximate, not a verified geocode.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found in any available source. This is a traditional Japanese restaurant relying on conventional soy sauce and dashi seasonings and offering tempura-style dishes on some menus, so wheat exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed likely. Diners with celiac disease or severe wheat allergy should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
name_ja: 浅草むぎとろ 本店
neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon, by the Sumida River, near Sensō-ji)
seats_text: Large multi-floor restaurant (several dining floors plus a rooftop buffet space) — an exact total seat count was not confirmed in available sources.
vegan_detail: The restaurant's signature seasoning is a secret dashi (broth) based on bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and kelp, used since the 1929 founding, so the core mugitoro dish and much of the kaiseki menu are not vegan. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option were found in available sources.

lead: 浅草 更科天狐（さらしな てんこ）
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草二丁目7番25号

roles: Soba craftsmanship supervised by soba shokunin Hiroyasu Nagayama (永山寛康)
Concept produced by restaurant producer Mori (森) and molecular-nutrition counselor Ayako Iwamoto (岩本亜矢子/綾子)
bio.background: Opened January 22, 2025 on Hanayashiki-dori in Asakusa, just south of Sensoji, a short walk from Asakusa Station. Billed as a 'shin-soba-ya' (next-generation soba restaurant) built around the idea that a meal can help the body 'tonou' (naturally come into balance) — combining traditional Edo-style soba craft with a modern, gluten-free, low-glycemic approach devised with a molecular-nutrition counselor.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim is food that tastes indulgent — sashimi, grilled dishes, wagashi, and soba — while being gluten-free and low in sugar, so guests can eat without the bloating or heaviness they associate with a typical soba-and-tsuyu meal.
bio.specialty: Three house-made 100% buckwheat soba noodles — juwari, sarashina, and kurobiki — made entirely without wheat binder, using flour from long-established Nerima miller Miyamoto Seifun (the same supplier used by the famed Kanda Yabu Soba), served with a proprietary gluten-free 'Edo tsuyu' dipping sauce.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's own account explains that a classic soba tsuyu normally carries hidden wheat gluten from the soy sauce used to make it, so the kitchen spent time developing a reformulated 'Edo tsuyu' recipe that keeps the same rich, aged Edo-style flavor while removing that wheat gluten — applied across all three of their soba varieties.

The concept was framed publicly as a 'shin-soba-ya' (next-gen soba shop) pairing a veteran soba craftsman's noodle-making with a molecular-nutrition counselor's menu design, aiming at food that's 'hard to gain weight from' and 'keeps you looking good.'

caveats: This session's WebFetch tool returned HTTP 403 on every direct page load attempted (official site, Tabelog, note.com, blogs, geocoders, even Wikipedia) — a session-level network restriction, not a signal about the restaurant. All facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries that explicitly cite and quote sarashina-tenko.com, the Tabelog listing, and independent food-media coverage (note.com, biz-hibana.com, croissant-online.jp, irohameguri.jp), not invented. Because the official Food page and Tabelog page could not be opened directly, seat count, exact reservation policy, and payment-method details could not be confirmed and are left null/uncertain rather than guessed. Coordinates are an estimate (see geocode_note), not a verified geocoder result.
closed_days: 毎週水曜、第2・第4火曜 (every Wednesday; 2nd and 4th Tuesday of the month)
cuisine: Gluten-free soba (100% buckwheat, juwari/sarashina/kurobiki, no wheat binder), Edo-style soba-izakaya small plates
cultural_comfort_note: A newly opened, design-forward concept restaurant in the heart of tourist Asakusa with online table-reservation availability, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found in available sources, so first-time foreign visitors should expect a mostly-Japanese experience.
geocode_note: GSI's address-search API and other geocoding services (Nominatim, Photon, geocoding.jp, Google Maps) were all unreachable in this session (proxy returned HTTP 403 on every attempt), so coordinates could not be verified against a geocoder. The estimate below is based on the confirmed street address (Taito-ku, Asakusa 2-chome, block 7), which independent sources describe as sitting on Hanayashiki-dori immediately south/east of Sensoji's main hall — comfortably inside the 0.8km Sensoji radius — but it has not been pinned by an actual geocoding call and should be treated as approximate.
gf_detail: All three house soba noodles (juwari/100% buckwheat, sarashina, and kurobiki) are made with 100% soba flour and no wheat binder, per the restaurant's own account and independent food-media coverage. The restaurant also states it reformulated its dipping sauce into a proprietary 'gluten-free Edo tsuyu,' specifically to remove the wheat-derived gluten that ordinary soba tsuyu picks up from soy sauce. That said, no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free kitchen/fryer, cross-contact protocols for any fried (tempura-style) items, or celiac-level handling procedures — those claims could not be verified, so treat as strong ingredient-level gluten-free intent rather than a certified celiac-safe kitchen.
name_ja: 浅草 更科天狐（さらしな てんこ）
neighborhood: 台東区浅草（花屋敷通り、浅草寺南側、浅草駅より徒歩3分）

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source found addresses shared-kitchen or cross-contamination handling for celiac-level safety; the restaurant's claims are about ingredient composition (no wheat binder, reformulated tsuyu) rather than facility-level gluten controls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant states it developed a proprietary 'Edo tsuyu' specifically to eliminate the wheat-derived gluten that a normal soba dipping sauce picks up from its soy sauce, applying this across all three noodle varieties.

positives.text: All three house soba noodles (juwari, sarashina, kurobiki) are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder added, using flour from Miyamoto Seifun, the long-established Nerima mill that also supplies the noted Kanda Yabu Soba.
The dipping sauce (tsuyu) itself was reformulated by the kitchen to remove wheat gluten, rather than just serving gluten-free noodles alongside a conventional sauce.

seats_text: Seat count not confirmed in available sources.
vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan/plant-based menu or a kombu/vegetable-only dashi. The restaurant's showcased 'Edo tsuyu' is described as a rich, traditionally aged-style dashi-based sauce, which in classic Edo soba preparation is typically built on katsuobushi (bonito) dashi; none of the available sources state the dashi is fish-free, so it should not be assumed vegan without asking staff directly.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-8-2

bio.origin: Asakusa, Taito-ku, Tokyo — a few minutes' walk from Sensō-ji, near Tawaramachi Station and the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
bio.background: Toriyoshi traces its roots to the postwar years, when the shop began as a traditional Japanese kappo (割烹) restaurant. About 19 years ago it was reworked into its current form focused on charcoal-grilled chicken (yakitori) and mizutaki hot pot — giving the shop roughly 70 years of history in the neighborhood when counted back to its kappo-restaurant origins.
bio.specialty: Toroeru oyako-don — a 'melting' chicken-and-egg rice bowl made with Date jidori chicken from Fukushima, its skin charcoal-grilled first for fragrance, topped with rich, thick egg and plenty of scallion, served with the house's clear chicken soup at lunch.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name combines 鶏 (tori, chicken) — a nod to its use of jidori chicken — with 'Yoshie,' the name of a previous proprietress, from whom the second half of the name was taken.
What most yakitori shops call tsukune, Toriyoshi calls 'dango' instead, because the mixture is kneaded from a whole chicken without any binder.
The house mizutaki broth is made by simmering local chicken bones with aromatic vegetables for around 12 hours, crushing the bones to draw out collagen.

caveats: Chicken-centric izakaya (yakitori, oyako-don, mizutaki) with no dedicated vegan or gluten-free program found anywhere online. Coordinates are a manual estimate, not geocoder-verified (see geocode_note), because both the GSI address API and OpenStreetMap Nominatim were unreachable during verification. Official homepage (toriyoshi.org) failed to resolve (DNS error), so it may be defunct; identity and current operation were instead confirmed via Tabelog, HotPepper, Gurunavi, PayPay Gourmet, and a 2025 local food blog, all consistent with an actively operating restaurant.
closed_days: Mondays, plus one additional (irregular) Tuesday each month
cuisine: Yakitori & Oyakodon (chicken specialty izakaya, also mizutaki chicken hot pot)
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, very local neighborhood chicken restaurant with tatami and counter seating; expect a Japanese-language menu and traditional service rather than English-forward, tourist-oriented hospitality.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation process was found. Yakitori is typically finished with a soy-based tare (standard Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat) or grilled shio-style (salt only); diners would need to specifically request shio skewers to avoid tare, and even then share a grill also used for tare-brushed skewers. The oyako-don's sauce and the restaurant's soups/broths are soy- and dashi-based, which commonly carries wheat. The menu also includes fried items (e.g., cartilage karaage), and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any allergy protocol. Treat this as a high-risk venue for celiac diners; do not rely on an established gluten-free process.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and OpenStreetMap Nominatim were both unreachable (blocked) during verification, so no automated geocode could be obtained. Coordinates below are a manual estimate based on the confirmed address (Taito-ku, Asakusa 1-8-2) and multiple sources describing the shop as a 3-5 minute walk from Tawaramachi Station (Ginza Line) and about 3 minutes from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit A1, along/near Kaminarimon-dori — accurate to roughly 100-150m but not geocoder-verified.
hours_raw: Tue-Fri: lunch 11:30-14:30 (LO 14:00), dinner 17:00-21:00 (LO 20:30). Sat/Sun/holidays: lunch 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:00-14:30), dinner 17:00-21:00 (LO 20:30). Closed Mondays, plus one Tuesday per month.
neighborhood: Asakusa / Tawaramachi, Taito-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen fries items such as cartilage karaage and cooks all yakitori on a shared charcoal grill; no separate gluten-free preparation area, fryer, or grill section is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu's core dishes (oyako-don sauce, yakitori tare, clear soup/broths) are soy- and dashi-based; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce or tare alternative is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is chicken-based (yakitori, oyako-don, mizutaki hot pot); shared grills, fryers, and chicken-bone broths are used throughout with no vegetarian or vegan prep line identified.

seats_text: Multiple floors with counter seating on the ground floor and tatami-mat seating/private rooms upstairs for small and larger groups (sources cite roughly 70-72 seats total; hot pot orders cannot be placed at the counter).
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken — yakitori, oyako-don (chicken and egg), and chicken mizutaki hot pot — with no vegetarian or vegan dishes identified in any menu listing found. Broths are chicken-bone based and soy sauce/tare is used throughout, so this is not a viable option for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-20-1

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Kikuya is a long-running sweets stall on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori, the covered shopping street that runs from Kaminarimon Gate to Sensoji Temple. It sits about nine shops in from Kaminarimon, on the right-hand side as you walk toward the temple, and specializes in grilled dango and daifuku made in-house.
bio.specialty: Freshly grilled mitarashi dango — skewered dango made from jōshinko (non-glutinous rice flour), grilled to order and finished with warm mitarashi sauce for a just-made, chewy texture — and mame daifuku, mochi (made with Shiga-grown rice) wrapped around whole Tokachi azuki beans for a lightly salted, textured tsubu-an filling.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes sourcing carefully selected domestic ingredients — Tokachi red beans, Shiga-grown mochi rice — and avoiding additives so the natural flavor of the rice and beans comes through.

anecdotes.text: A Tokyo visitor blog singles out Kikuya as a favorite Nakamise stop for grabbing a 120-yen skewer of hot grilled dango to eat while strolling toward Sensoji, noting it's especially busy around New Year's hatsumode crowds.

caveats: Hours, closed-day policy, and payment/seating details rely on secondary sources (official-site content relayed via search summaries, independent blogs) rather than a directly fetched page — kikuya-nakamise.com and tabelog.com both returned network-policy-blocked 403s to direct fetch in this session. Core identity, address, and phone number were cross-confirmed across multiple independent sources (official site, currency-exchange listing, blog write-ups), so identity/location confidence is high even though page-level detail confidence is lower. No shop-specific allergen or gluten-free statement was located; the GF/vegan assessment below is inferred from the typical ingredients of the two named specialty items (rice-flour dango, mochi/azuki daifuku), not from a confirmed statement by Kikuya itself.
closed_days: Mondays (if Monday is a public holiday, closed the following weekday instead)
cuisine: 和菓子・大福・団子（豆大福、みたらし団子）
cultural_comfort_note: Located on the extremely tourist-heavy Nakamise-dori right by Kaminarimon, and the shop maintains an English-language section of its site, but no source confirms English-speaking staff or an allergen menu, so expect basic gesture/simple-phrase level interaction.
geocode_note: The GSI geocoding API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by network policy in this session (proxy returned a 403 policy denial on every attempt), so no live geocode could be run. The coordinates given are a reasoned estimate based on the confirmed address (台東区浅草1-20-1) and the shop's documented position — about nine shops from Kaminarimon Gate toward Sensoji on Nakamise-dori — not an output of a geocoding service. The entire Nakamise-dori corridor between Kaminarimon and Sensoji is well within 0.8km of the Sensoji anchor point (35.7148, 139.7967) regardless of exact placement along the street, so the area match is not in doubt even though the point coordinates are approximate.
gf_detail: No shop-specific gluten-free statement was found. The mame daifuku (mochi + azuki + sugar) uses ingredients that are naturally free of wheat, but the mitarashi dango's base is made from jōshinko (rice flour, gluten-free grain) while the mitarashi sauce itself is typically soy-sauce-based — and soy sauce (shoyu) sold in Japan is usually brewed with wheat — so the finished dango is not confirmed gluten-free. Ask staff about the sauce's ingredients and about shared prep surfaces before ordering if you have celiac disease.
hours_raw: 10:30-17:30（売り切れ次第閉店） 定休日：月曜日（祝日の場合は翌日）
neighborhood: Asakusa / Nakamise-dori (near Kaminarimon)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi-style dango sauce is conventionally made with soy sauce (shoyu), sugar, and starch; soy sauce produced in Japan is usually wheat-brewed, so mitarashi dango is generally not considered gluten-free unless a shop confirms otherwise — this is a general caution about the dish style, not a statement specific to Kikuya.

positives.text: The shop's two named specialty items are built from plant-based staples — daifuku is mochi rice and Tokachi azuki beans with sugar, and the dango base is jōshinko (rice flour) — with the official site emphasizing no additives and domestic ingredient sourcing, though this describes ingredient philosophy rather than a formal allergen or vegan certification.

seats_text: Not confirmed by any source; Kikuya reads as a walk-up Nakamise-dori sweets stall selling dango and daifuku mainly for eating on the go or taking home, with no seating documented.
vegan_detail: No shop-specific vegan statement was found. Based on the ingredients described for the two named specialty items — mame daifuku (mochi rice, azuki beans, sugar) and mitarashi dango (jōshinko rice-flour dango with a soy-sauce/sugar/starch glaze) — neither item appears to rely on dashi, bonito, egg, or dairy, but this is inferred from general recipe conventions and Kikuya's own ingredient descriptions, not a confirmed vegan claim from the shop. Broader menu items (other dango/daifuku flavors) were not individually checked.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門2-1-15 中川ビル 1F・2F

bio.background: Gonpachi Asakusa Azumabashi opened in January 2017 as one of the newer branches of the long-running Gonpachi izakaya group. It sits in a quiet spot on the bank of the Sumida River at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, about a 30-second walk from Exit 4 of Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) and roughly a minute from the Toei Asakusa Line exit.
bio.specialty: Hand-made soba — buckwheat that is stone-ground fresh each morning and hand-cut in house — alongside binchotan-charcoal-grilled yakitori skewers and hand-rolled temaki sushi made with premium nori.

anecdotes.text: The first floor is built around a communal counter cut from a single piece of kusu (camphor) wood from Kyushu, said to be around 200 years old, while the second floor has a U-shaped ash-wood counter; large windows on both floors look straight onto the Sumida River, giving a completely different mood by day and by night.

caveats: Precise lat/lng could not be verified because the GSI geocoding API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by this session's network egress policy (403 on every attempt, including retries), and general WebFetch was also blocked for all tested hosts (including unrelated ones like Wikipedia), so all facts here come from WebSearch result snippets rather than direct page fetches. The address (Kaminarimon 2-1-15, at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, ~30 seconds from Asakusa Station Ginza Line Exit 4) is corroborated by multiple independent sources and is unambiguously within 0.8km of Sensoji, but I could not run the required GSI coordinate check. This location is part of the multi-branch Gonpachi restaurant group (other branches: Nishi-Azabu, Shibuya, Ginza, Daiba, etc.), so it is not an independent mom-and-pop shop, and its group-wide vegan menu is confirmed only at the Ginza/Nishi-Azabu/Shibuya branches, not here.
closed_days: None — open every day including weekends and holidays (無休)
cuisine: Izakaya / Yakitori / hand-made soba with Sumida River views
cultural_comfort_note: It's part of a well-known Japanese restaurant group in the heart of tourist-heavy Asakusa, so some visitor-friendly accommodation is likely, but English menus/staff for this specific branch were not directly confirmed in available sources.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised at this location. The soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) and yakitori tare are soy-sauce based (soy sauce traditionally contains wheat), tempura here is battered in wheat flour, and there is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free soba option or a dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should not consider this restaurant safe without in-person confirmation (in Japanese) from staff, and even then cross-contact risk in a shared kitchen serving soba, tempura, and yakitori is high.
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) returned a 403 policy denial on every attempt this session (confirmed via the proxy status endpoint as an organization egress policy block, not a transient error), so lat/lng are left null rather than estimated. Multiple independent listings (Hot Pepper, Navitime, Gonpachi's own site) place the address at Taito-ku, Kaminarimon 2-1-15 — right at the foot of Azumabashi bridge on the Sumida River, about 30 seconds from Asakusa Station's Ginza Line Exit 4 — which is well within 0.8km of Sensoji (35.7148, 139.7967).
hours_raw: Daily, including weekends and holidays: 11:30-23:00 (food last order 22:00, drinks last order 22:30)
neighborhood: Asakusa / Azumabashi, Taito-ku (riverside, at the foot of Azumabashi bridge, ~1 minute from Asakusa Station)
seats_text: About 90 seats across two floors (roughly 60 on the 2nd floor, 30 on the 1st), including communal river-view counters.
vegan_detail: Gonpachi's dedicated vegan menu (vegan nigiri, vegan tempura rice bowl, etc.) has only been confirmed at the group's Ginza (G-ZONE), Nishi-Azabu, and Shibuya branches — not at this Asakusa Azumabashi location. Here, vegetable tempura and vegetable-filled hand-rolls (temaki) could work as a starting point, but the soba tsuyu and most sauces at Gonpachi locations are traditionally dashi-based (bonito/katsuobushi), so a fully vegan meal is not guaranteed without asking staff directly and being prepared for limited options.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-35-3

bio.origin: Trained at a soba-focused Buddhist temple in Tendo City, Yamagata Prefecture — one of northeastern Japan's most celebrated buckwheat-growing regions.
bio.background: Suzuki apprenticed in soba-making at a temple in Tendo, Yamagata, before opening his own shop behind Sensoji's Kannon Hall in Asakusa's quiet Kannon-ura backstreets.
bio.specialty: Two hand-made soba served fresh daily: a delicate nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour) prized for its faint aroma and smooth finish, and a heartier inaka (country-style) noodle milled for a deeper buckwheat fragrance and firmer bite.
bio.philosophy: Suzuki insists on sourcing the season's best domestic raw buckwheat groats and stone-grinding them by hand through the entire process, believing that freshly milled flour is what gives the noodles their aroma and character.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, Tansouan (丹想庵), was given to Suzuki by the head priest of the soba temple in Tendo, Yamagata where he trained; '丹想' points to sensing one's feelings from the body's core.
The kitchen grinds top-grade domestic buckwheat with a stone mill and hand-cuts two distinct soba styles fresh every day, pairing them with a range of sake and seasonal accompaniments.

caveats: Payment is cash-only at lunch; dinner accepts credit cards for purchases of ¥10,000 or more. Coordinates could not be verified against the GSI geocoder in this session (the geocoding service and most external sites returned connection/policy errors), so lat/lng are left null — the address (Asakusa 3-35-3, Kannon-ura backstreet, ~5 min walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit 1) is well documented across multiple independent listings as being just north of Sensoji, consistent with the target radius, but this was not independently coordinate-verified.
closed_days: Closed Mondays and Sundays; also closed around New Year, the Saturday/Sunday of the Sanja Matsuri festival, and the Saturday of the Sumida River Fireworks Festival
cuisine: Soba (hand-made buckwheat noodles) with sake-focused izakaya-style accompaniments
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, Japanese-oriented soba-and-sake restaurant in a quiet Asakusa backstreet; no English-language service was confirmed, so a translation app and some patience with a Japanese-only menu are recommended, though phone/Tabelog reservations are available.
geocode_note: The required GSI address-search endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and essentially all other external sites returned connection/policy failures from this session's network, so no coordinates could be independently geocoded. lat/lng are left null rather than invented. The restaurant's documented location (Asakusa 3-chome, Kannon-ura, behind Sensoji, ~5 min from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station) is consistent with the Sensoji target radius based on multiple independent business listings, but this was not numerically verified.
gf_detail: The kitchen hand-makes two soba styles fresh daily — a nihachi noodle (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both of which contain wheat flour by recipe. No dedicated 100%-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option was found in any source, and both noodle types are almost certainly boiled in shared water. This is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
neighborhood: Asakusa (Kannon-ura), Taito-ku — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: Two soba noodle types are hand-cut fresh each day — a nihachi (80% buckwheat/20% wheat) and an inaka (country-style) noodle — both containing wheat by recipe; no gluten-free or 100%-buckwheat noodle was found offered.

seats_text: About 45 seats across tatami floor seating, sunken-kotatsu tables, floor chairs, and a counter, including private tatami rooms seating 4-10 guests
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-sake restaurant offering tempura and other seasonal side dishes alongside the noodles. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or bonito-free broth option was found in any source, and traditional soba kaeshi/dashi in shops of this style is typically bonito-based. Not confirmed safe or accommodating for vegan diners without direct inquiry.

lead: 浅草鳩家 ヴィーガンフルーツサンド
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-3-3

bio.background: A small specialty takeout counter on Denpo-in-dori, the lantern-lined shopping street that leads to the gates of Sensoji Temple in Asakusa, dedicated entirely to fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando) made without any egg or dairy.
bio.specialty: Fruit sandwiches piled generously with seasonal fresh fruit (strawberry, kiwi, mandarin orange, and other seasonal fruit) in soy-milk whipped cream between soft white bread, priced from about 600 yen.
bio.philosophy: The shop's premise is that a fruit sandwich does not need dairy cream or egg to taste rich: both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead, so vegans and people with egg or milk allergies can eat the same treat as everyone else strolling past the temple.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the sandwiches as packed full rather than skimpy — whole large strawberries and kiwi tucked inside as well as arranged on top — calling it 'nothing like the ordinary fruit sandwiches that go easy on the filling.'
The stall sits immediately beside the entrance to Sensoji Temple on Denpo-in-dori (on the left just after passing through Kaminarimon), making it a popular walk-and-eat stop for temple visitors.
The shop has run a plant-based-egg collaboration menu with another maker, described in local food press as a well-known Asakusa vegan fruit-sandwich specialist.

caveats: Could not independently browse tabelog.com, the shop's Google Business page, or the GSI geocoder in this session (all outbound page fetches were blocked by the sandbox's network policy); every fact below is drawn from search-engine snippets of those pages plus independent blogs (Smileveg, Hachidory, VegeMap, Hakken Press, Note/UMAMI UNITED). Payment methods (cash-only or not) were not confirmed by any source, so cash_only is an unverified default. Exact GPS coordinates could not be fetched programmatically; see geocode_note.
closed_days: No regular closed day reported (定休日なし); the shop may close before 16:00 on days it sells out.
cuisine: ヴィーガンフルーツサンド・サンドイッチ
cultural_comfort_note: A simple walk-up takeout counter directly on Asakusa's most tourist-heavy street (Denpo-in-dori, steps from Sensoji), with a visual fruit-sandwich menu and no reservation or Japanese conversation needed to order.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session (outbound fetches to it and to tabelog.com were blocked by sandbox network policy, confirmed via the agent-proxy status endpoint), so coordinates could not be fetched programmatically. Multiple independent sources place the shop directly on Denpo-in-dori "just beside the entrance to Sensoji Temple, on the left after Kaminarimon," and its address (東京都台東区浅草2-3-3) sits in the same 2-3 block as Sensoji's own address (2-3-1 Asakusa) — strong textual evidence it is well within 0.8km of the Sensōji reference point (35.7148, 139.7967), even though exact lat/lng are not verified here.

Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free needs without calling ahead to confirm directly.

positives.text: Both the bread and the whipped cream are made from soy milk instead of dairy and egg, so the shop is positioned as safe to eat for vegans and for people with egg or milk allergies.
Compared with a standard dairy-cream fruit sandwich, the soy-cream version is described as roughly a third of the calories and near-zero cholesterol.

bio.background: Founded in 1902 (Meiji 35), 天藤 (Tentō) is one of Asakusa's older tendon (tempura rice bowl) specialists, sitting at the corner entrance to Hoppy-dori a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It is now run by the second generation of the founding family.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Edo-style tendon — a bowl topped with shrimp, kisu (Japanese whiting), eggplant, and a scallop-and-squid kakiage, all fried to a distinctively dark color and finished with a rich, assertively savory tentsuyu sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been singled out by actor Shirō Sano as serving one of his favorite ("best") tendon bowls in Tokyo, and has drawn long lines of customers after appearing on television and social media.

caveats: Small, popular old-school tendon counter that regularly draws a queue; cash only, walk-ins only (no reservations), and hours can shorten some days if the day's tempura sells out. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source — this is a traditional wheat-batter, seafood-and-dashi tempura shop, not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: Mondays (following day if Monday is a public holiday)
cuisine: 天ぷら・天丼 (Edo-style tempura / tendon)
cultural_comfort_note: Cash-only, reservation-free counter shop popular with both locals and tourists; expect a wait, Japanese-only menu norms, and no dedicated English support confirmed.
gf_detail: All tempura is fried in a standard wheat-flour batter and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (traditional Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, tamari substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe option was mentioned in any source — not appropriate for a gluten-free or celiac diner.

lead: まるごとVeganダイニング浅草
address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-3-13 矢吹ビル1階

bio.background: Koyama Konomi became vegetarian in spring/summer 2017 and vegan later that same summer. The turning point traces back to 2016, when a scene in Osamu Tezuka's manga "Buddha" — in which a rabbit sacrifices its own life for a human — planted the first seed of doubt that eventually led her toward vegetarianism. Her shift progressed in stages: first for health reasons, then out of concern for animals, and finally for the environment.
bio.specialty: 100% plant-based, gluten-free lunch plates, salad lunches, curry rice, and gluten-free sweets, all made without the "five pungent" vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, Chinese chives, rakkyo), alcohol, added sugar, or additives.
bio.philosophy: Koyama believes that how veganism is communicated matters most — rather than lecturing, she wants guests to first have a delicious, enjoyable meal, and let that experience become a gentle entry point for thinking about animals and the planet. Because she and her entire staff are vegan themselves, she says the restaurant can attentively serve both committed vegans and the vegan-curious.

anecdotes.text: In 2016, seeing a scene in Osamu Tezuka's manga Buddha in which a rabbit gives up its own life for a human planted the seed that eventually led Koyama to vegetarianism, then veganism, the following year.

Koyama's path to veganism moved through stages — first motivated by health, then by concern for animals, and finally by concern for the environment — before she and her staff opened a restaurant where everyone on staff is vegan.

caveats: Could not independently verify GPS coordinates: the GSI address-search API and every other geocoding/mapping endpoint tried (GSI, Nominatim, Google, Tabelog map page) were blocked by network policy in this environment, so lat/lng are left null rather than invented — location confidence in the target area rests on the confirmed address and the "1 minute walk from Asakusa Station Ginza Line Exit 5, between Matsuya Asakusa and Sumida Park" description found via search, which places it well within 0.8km of Sensoji. The kitchen also excludes the "five pungent" vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, Chinese chives, rakkyo/shallot), alcohol, added sugar, and food additives, and the site says many items are free of Japan's 28 designated priority allergens. address_ja: 東京都墨田区吾妻橋1-11-2

bio.origin: Founded in 1984 (Showa 59) by a founding chef-owner who spent 20 years training at the famed Kanda Yabusoba before striking out on his own — a direct lineage from one of Tokyo's most storied soba houses.
bio.background: Despite the name 'Azumabashi' (Azuma Bridge), the shop today actually stands at the Sumida-ku foot of Komagata-bashi, one bridge south of Azuma-bashi itself, along the Sumida River a few minutes from Asakusa Station. It's a small, old-school Edo-mae soba-ya that keeps a lunch-only rhythm true to the genre — the room fills at midday with regulars sharing a quiet 'hiru-zake' (daytime drinking) ritual of a small carafe of sake alongside their soba before the kitchen closes for the day.
bio.specialty: Duck (kamo) soba dishes — cold kamo-jiru zaru, hot kamo nanban — and a sesame-broth (goma-jiru) soba are the standout, most-loved items alongside the classic mori-soba.
bio.philosophy: Thin, fragrant, firm-textured Edo-mae buckwheat noodles paired with a rich, salty dipping sauce in the classic Yabu-soba style handed down from Kanda Yabusoba.

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder trained for 20 years at Kanda Yabusoba before opening independently in 1984.
Known locally as a lunchtime 'sacred site' for hiru-zake — enjoying a discreet drink and soba-zensai alongside the noodles before the early close.
Though named for Azuma-bashi, the restaurant is actually located at the foot of Komagata-bashi bridge on the Sumida-ku side of the river.

caveats: Hours, seat count, and exact geocoordinates could not be independently re-verified this session because the browsing tool's HTTPS access was blocked for essentially all outbound fetches (including neutral test targets like wikipedia.org and Google), so the record relies on search-engine snippet summaries of Tabelog, the official site, and Japanese food blogs rather than directly fetched pages. Coordinates are a landmark-based estimate, not a GSI API geocode. This is a traditional wheat-containing (nihachi) soba shop with a duck-forward menu — treat GF and vegan ratings as conservative defaults pending direct confirmation with staff.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday
cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles), Edo-mae/Yabu-style
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only, old-school Tokyo soba shop with no confirmed English menu — comfortable for Japanese speakers or visitors ready to point-and-order, less so for those wanting full English support.

gf_detail: This is a traditional Edo-mae "Yabu" style soba shop, which by convention uses nihachi (2:8) noodles — roughly 20% wheat flour blended with buckwheat — not 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodles, so the noodles themselves are not gluten-free. The Kanda-Yabusoba-lineage dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a soy-sauce-based warishita, and tempura dishes on the menu use wheat batter. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was found in any available source. Not recommended for diners with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's dipping sauce follows classic Edo-mae style — a rich, aromatic tsuyu built on a concentrated soy-sauce base, characteristic of the Kanda Yabusoba lineage.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around duck (kamo) — cold kamo-jiru soba and hot kamo nanban are among the most recommended dishes — alongside chicken nanban and sesame-broth soba.

seats_text: Small shop, roughly 18 seats (table seating; no counter or private tatami room reported) — approximate, not independently confirmed.

No vegan menu, substitution, or dashi disclosure was found in any available source. A plain mori-soba might be closest to workable but the sauce itself is the open question — ask staff directly before ordering, and do not assume it is vegan.

lead: 蕎亭 大黒屋（きょうていだいこくや）
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草4-39-2

bio.background: Kyotei Daikokuya was opened roughly 45 years ago by Shigeo Kanno (菅野成雄, b. 1943 Tokyo) and his wife Masae (雅江) in a quiet backstreet of "oku-Asakusa," behind the main temple-front bustle. Kanno came to soba relatively late, at 28, first working at a neighborhood soba shop in Ichigaya before being drawn to the noodles of Issa-an (一茶庵) in Nishi-Kanda. He trained there for four years and later studied directly under the celebrated soba master Yasuo Katakura (片倉康雄), founder of the influential Issa-an lineage that shaped much of modern hand-made soba in Japan. At 34 he set up his own shop along Asakusa's Yanagi-dori, which grew into today's Kyotei Daikokuya.
bio.origin: Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Kanno mills his own buckwheat fresh in-house using a bank of traditional stone mills (reported as 11 to 14 depending on the source) and hand-kneads 100%-buckwheat (juwari, 十割) noodles with no wheat blended in, chasing what he calls the "ideal soba" through consistency achieved only by repetition and daily self-milling.
bio.specialty: Juwari soba (十割そば) made from heirloom buckwheat varieties sourced directly from growers in Niigata and Nagano, stone-ground fresh each day; also known for its sobagaki (そばがき, warm buckwheat dumplings) eaten with fresh-grated wasabi and soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: Journalist Toshihiko Ueno wrote an entire biography about the shop and the Kanno couple's life devoted to soba, published as 'そば打ち一代 浅草・蕎亭大黒屋見聞録' ("A Life of Soba-Making: Chronicles of Asakusa's Kyotei Daikokuya").
Soba researcher Toranosuke Katayama, founder of the Japan Soba Preservation Society, has singled out Kyotei Daikokuya's soba as among the best in Tokyo.

caveats: Aggregator listings disagree on exact opening hours (Thu-Sat evenings only vs. Tue-Sat, vs. a lunch+dinner split) and on card acceptance (most say cash only, one says cards accepted) — this is a fully reservation-only (完全予約制), phone-booking shop, so confirm current hours/payment directly at 03-3874-2986 when reserving. Coordinates could not be verified against the Japan GSI address geocoder (the service was unreachable in this session), so lat/lng are left blank rather than guessed; the address and "7-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, oku-Asakusa" description match the intended target area, but the exact distance to Sensoji has not been independently confirmed.
closed_days: Reported as Sunday, Monday, and public holidays (sources vary — see caveats); confirm when booking.

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — founded in 1861 (Bunkyū 1), the year before the Meiji era began
bio.background: Irokawa has run as a small, family-operated unagi (freshwater eel) specialty shop near Kaminarimon and Sensō-ji since 1861, making it one of the neighborhood's oldest surviving eel restaurants. It appears on Taito City's official roster of businesses founded during the Edo period.
bio.specialty: Unadon/unajū (grilled eel over rice), kabayaki and shirayaki (sauced and unseasoned grilled eel), and grilled eel liver (kimo) skewers
bio.philosophy: The eel is grilled to order over charcoal and hand-fanned with a uchiwa while being basted in a tare sauce that has been continuously topped up and passed down since the shop's founding, a technique handed from one generation of cooks to the next rather than a recipe remade from scratch.

anecdotes.text: The shop is listed on Taito City's official registry of 'Edo Sōgyō' (businesses founded in the Edo period) as 'Unagi Irokawa, established 1861 (Bunkyū 1).'
Because the shop takes no lunch reservations and often sells out by early-to-mid afternoon, a queue forms on the street before opening — a long-running fixture of the Asakusa eel scene.

seats_text: About 13 seats (small counter/table shop)
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around eel (unadon/unajū, kabayaki, shirayaki, grilled liver skewers) with no plant-based main dishes. Rice and side items are likely prepared with traditional dashi/tare that can include fish-derived stock. There is no indication of any vegan or vegetarian option.

address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸2-9-11

bio.background: et vous? is a small, chef-owned French bistro tucked in a quiet residential backstreet of Hanakawado, just north of Sensō-ji, about a five-minute walk from Asakusa Station. Owner-chef Okabe runs the restaurant around a self-styled concept the shop calls "Life Food" — cooking that is meant to let people across generations sit down and eat the same table of food safely and without worry. To do that, the kitchen leans hard on seasonal vegetables sourced through relationships built directly with trusted producers, rather than through a general wholesale market.
bio.specialty: Seasonal-vegetable-forward French course cuisine — the restaurant's own "Life Food" courses built around a rotating lineup of vegetables at their peak, plus an original "Edo cuisine" line that folds Edo-period Tokyo culinary ideas into a French frame.
bio.philosophy: Okabe's cooking is described as not bound strictly by classical French technique — flavors, cooking methods and ingredient pairings are combined more freely, guided by the chef's own sensibility, with the aim of food that is gentle on the body ("eat and become beautiful/healthy") rather than rich and heavy in the classic bistro style.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's own description frames it as a hidden, adult-feeling retreat in a residential pocket beyond Sensō-ji — reached down a quiet backstreet rather than a main road.
The "Life Food" concept is explained as researching and designing food so that three generations of one family could safely share the same table.

caveats: Could not verify GF/vegan handling directly with the restaurant — no dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu, allergy protocol, or ingredient breakdown was found on the official site or booking platforms; the "vegetable-forward" framing is a general house philosophy, not a documented allergy program. lead: 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井
address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草2-26-1 坂田ビル1F

bio.origin: Ōita, Kyūshū (proprietor's background)
bio.background: Opened in 1989 as あな太朗 (Anatarou) on a quiet side street off Kokusai-dori in Asakusa. Its proprietor had spent years working as a sushi chef in Ōita, Kyūshū, before becoming captivated by anago (conger eel) and building a restaurant devoted to it. The shop later changed its name to 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井 (Asakusa Kawai), continuing under the same conger-eel-and-local-sake concept.
bio.specialty: Anago sashimi (conger eel sashimi) — prized for its crunchy texture and the eel's own natural sweetness
bio.philosophy: Source fresh conger eel daily — including wild anago from the Bungo Strait between Kyūshū and Shikoku — and show it off every way possible: raw as sashimi, slow-glazed kabayaki-style in a proprietary sauce simmered about 45 minutes, plus Kyūshū specialties like Bungo beef and Bungo chicken from the owner's home region grilled on hot stones.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant traces back to 1989, when it opened as あな太朗 (Anatarou); its proprietor had spent years as a sushi chef in Ōita, Kyūshū, before conger eel captivated him enough to build a restaurant around it.
A Nikkei feature on the shop highlighted three decades of devotion to conger eel, singling out the sashimi — made with wild anago from the Bungo Strait — as its proudest dish.

Beyond sashimi, the kitchen simmers fresh conger eel daily in a house sauce for about 45 minutes for a fragrant, glazed kabayaki-style preparation, and also grills Bungo beef and Bungo chicken from Ōita on hot stones.
The shop later changed its name from あな太朗 (Anatarou) to 穴子料理と地酒 浅草 川井, keeping the same anago-and-local-sake concept.

caveats: Verification for this record relied on WebSearch-derived summaries of Tabelog, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Retty, Hitosara, LINE Place, and Asakusa Navi listings, plus a Nikkei feature — the underlying pages themselves (including Tabelog and the restaurant's own site) could not be directly fetched because this environment's network policy blocks WebFetch for arbitrary external hosts (including tabelog.com and the restaurant's apparent official site gg3f300.gorp.jp), so all facts are as reported second-hand by search summaries rather than confirmed against raw page content. No gluten-free or vegan-specific statements from the restaurant itself were found anywhere.

address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草3-3-2

bio.origin: Founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) as a dedicated dojō (loach) specialty shop in Nishi-Asakusa, with the family's food-service roots in the same neighborhood reaching back to the Keiō era (1865-68).
bio.background: Idaya has stayed in the same Nishi-Asakusa spot for over a century, passing the kitchen and the sauce recipe down through five generations of the Iida family. The current fourth-generation owner is training his son, Tadayuki, to take over as the fifth.
bio.specialty: Dojō nabe — live loach hot pot, cooked to order in a shallow iron pot with the house's own soy-based warishita sauce and a heavy mound of chopped scallion; the bones are gently pre-softened so a little pleasant resistance remains in the bite. The menu also runs to hone-nuki nabe (boneless loach pot), yanagawa nabe (loach and egg), unagi (eel) grilled dishes, and, in winter, namazu (catfish) nabe.
bio.philosophy: The recipe for the sweet-savory warishita sauce is a closely guarded family secret handed to only one successor at a time, and the kitchen still follows the same cooking method used since the shop's founding. The house precept — 'Father's job is to make the sauce and mind the shoe check' — is taken as a reminder that hospitality should extend all the way down to a guest's feet.

anecdotes.text: The founding family's precept has always been 'Father's job is to make the sauce and mind the shoe check' — a reminder that hospitality extends all the way down to a guest's feet.

Marking its 113th year in business, the restaurant was in the process of passing from its fourth-generation owner to his son, Iida Tadayuki, the presumptive fifth generation, who grew up watching his father work.

caveats: Coordinates could not be verified against the GSI geocoding API (network access to it was blocked in this session); lat/lng below are an approximate estimate, not a geocoder result — see geocode_note. This is a traditional loach/eel specialty restaurant with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation; it is included for completeness/context, not as a safe pick for celiac or vegan diners.

No plant-based main dish or confirmed vegan option was found. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.

bio.origin: Opened July 4, 2012, tucked in a small alley just off Nakamise-dori, about a two-minute walk from Tokyo Metro Ginza Line Asakusa Station Exit 6.
bio.background: Kanmidokoro Saika is an independent traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi) shop near Sensō-ji and Nakamise-dori, serving classic kanmi alongside its well-known shaved ice.
bio.specialty: Fluffy, 'powder-snow' style shaved ice (kakigori) — the shop shaves unusually hard ice blocks with a very sharp blade to get an ultra-fine, light texture said to feel barely cold on the first bite. Also known for anmitsu, mitsumame, oshiruko, parfaits and warabimochi made with Hokkaido azuki, Uji matcha and Okinawan brown sugar.
bio.philosophy: Close attention to ice quality and shaving technique, paired with housemade matcha jelly and syrups made from carefully sourced Japanese ingredients, across a menu of 80+ sweets.

anecdotes.text: A shop-feature interview highlights Saika's colorful 'choco milk' shaved ice, decorated pop-style with sprinkles, as a signature seasonal offering that photographs well and delights first-time visitors.

However, toppings (cookies, wafers, sauces) and shared prep surfaces are unconfirmed, and no soy-sauce or dashi use is documented (it's a sweets shop, not a savory kitchen). Celiac diners should ask staff directly about specific ingredients and any shared equipment before ordering.

Vegan diners should ask staff to point out or adapt dairy-free items.

bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames shōjin ryori as drawing out the umami, nutritional value and beauty already present in each plant-based ingredient, with a stated ethos that nothing should be wasted — down to a single grain of rice or drop of juice.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer described the restaurant's vegetable-only noodle broth as having a surprisingly deep, umami-rich flavor close to a Kansai-style dashi despite containing no fish or bonito stock.
The restaurant has hosted seasonal, hands-on events such as an Oriental-vegan chimaki (rice dumpling) making experience, and prepared a fully vegan osechi (Japanese New Year) box for 2023.

cultural_comfort_note: Reservations (required for dinner) are made by phone, and no confirmed English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was found, though the restaurant is covered favorably by several English-language vegan/travel sites.

The menu centers on shōjin dishes that include wheat-based noodles (tantan noodles, ramen-style dishes, udon-style broth dishes), so the kitchen is not gluten-free by default. Soy sauce/tamari wheat content and cross-contamination handling were not documented in any source reviewed — celiac or strict-GF diners should call ahead and confirm directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is described as excluding all animal-derived ingredients as well as the Buddhist "five pungent roots" (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion), consistent with strict shōjin-style plant-based cooking.

positives.text: Menu and site descriptions consistently state the food uses no animal products at all (fully vegan shōjin ryori), not just a vegetarian subset of dishes.
A reviewer noted the noodle-dish broth was vegetable-only yet tasted deeply umami, suggesting deliberate care in building flavor without fish-based dashi.

seats_text: Not published in any source found; likely a small restaurant given the reservation-only dinner policy, but exact capacity unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: Consistently described across multiple independent sources as a fully vegan shōjin-ryori kitchen: no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs, and no "five pungent" alliums (onion, garlic, chives, leek, scallion) per Buddhist dietary tradition. Broths appear to be vegetable-based rather than fish/bonito-based, per reviewer commentary on the noodle dishes. No katsuo/dashi exceptions were found in any source.

lead: 食事処 酒肴 浅草 水口
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-4-9

roles: family-run Showa-era teishoku-ya (set-meal diner) and neighborhood eatery/izakaya
bio.origin: Opened in 1950 (Shōwa 25) in Asakusa Rokku, Asakusa's old entertainment district, and has stayed in the same neighborhood ever since — marking roughly 75 years in business as of the mid-2020s.
bio.background: Mizuguchi began as a plain teishoku-ya serving the workers and performers of Asakusa Rokku in the postwar years. It has grown into a sprawling two-floor shokudō-and-izakaya known for its huge, old-fashioned menu spanning Japanese, Western and Chinese-style home cooking — everything from fried horse mackerel and pork sauté to omurice, ginger pork, mapo tofu and hand-mixed hamburg steak — served in a boisterous, nostalgic Shōwa-retro dining room.
bio.specialty: Iri-buta — pork and onion stir-fried in Mizuguchi's own curry-tinged, sweet-savory sauce, reportedly created together with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the Tokyo air raids of 1945 and still the table everyone orders.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills, high-volume local shokudō where regulars — from neighborhood workers to solo diners stopping in for a drink — are as much a part of the atmosphere as the food.

anecdotes.text: Iri-buta is said to have existed in some form before the war and to have been worked out with one of the shop's cooks around the time of the 1945 Tokyo air raids.
The comedy duo Naitsu (ナイツ), who often riff on Asakusa in their act, are described as regulars who frequent the shop.
Local write-ups describe the restaurant as a 100-plus-item menu institution beloved by Asakusa locals, with the Shōwa-retro dining room drawing return visitors.

caveats: Traditional Shōwa-era set-meal diner (teishoku-ya) with no gluten-free or vegan program of any kind identified in any source. Menu is dominated by pork, fried and breaded dishes, and Western-Japanese home cooking with conventional soy sauce and likely katsuo/bonito-based dashi. Not a fit for strict celiac or vegan diners; listed for completeness/honesty rather than as a recommended GF/vegan pick. Geocoding could not be confirmed via API in this session (network policy blocked msearch.gsi.go.jp, geocoding.jp and nominatim.openstreetmap.org), but the address (Asakusa 2-4-9, ~1 min from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, ~7 min from Ginza-line Asakusa/Tawaramachi) places it well within the Sensōji target radius.

cuisine: 定食・いり豚・昭和食堂 (Shōwa-era set-meal diner / teishoku-ya)
cultural_comfort_note: A very local, Japanese-language diner with a huge handwritten-style menu; no English menu or allergy accommodations were found in any source, so visitors comfortable navigating in Japanese (or with a translation app) will have the easiest time.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. The menu is built around breaded/fried items (mixed fry, korokke), sauced pork and hamburg-steak dishes, and standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout the kitchen. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

Not recommended as a vegan option based on available information.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-39-13

roles: Family-run since the Meiji era, now several generations on
bio.origin: Founded in the last years of the Edo period as a tempura food stall on Hirokoji street by Nakagawa Tetsuzō, a former samurai from Suruga who gave up his rank to start a business in Edo. In 1870 (Meiji 3) the family opened a permanent shop in front of the old Asakusa Public Hall, on the same ground it occupies today. The restaurant's name combines "中" from the Nakagawa family name with "清" from Seigorō, the second-generation owner.
bio.specialty: Raijin-age (雷神揚げ) — an oversized kakiage of shiba shrimp and akoyagi (pen shell) adductor muscle, fried crisp in sesame oil using a technique handed down within the family since the first generation. The name was coined by a regular customer, French-literature scholar Yutaka Tatsuno, who thought the finished kakiage resembled the thunder drums carried by the Raijin (Thunder God) statue at Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate.
bio.philosophy: A multi-generation commitment to Edo-mae tempura as it was cooked in old Tokyo — thick-battered, sesame-oil-fried, whole ingredients rather than fillets — with the signature kakiage recipe treated as a piece of family craft passed one generation to the next.

anecdotes.text: The shop appears repeatedly in the writings of novelist Nagai Kafū, and novelist Ikenami Shōtarō was also a regular visitor.
Writer Kubo Mantarō was a childhood friend of the third-generation owner, Tetsutarō, and used to play with him in the restaurant's courtyard.
The restaurant was used as a filming location for the TV drama 'Kurosagi'.

The 'Raijin-age' kakiage — shiba shrimp and akoyagi adductor fried in sesame oil — is described as a technique 'passed down to a single successor per generation' (一子相伝), originally devised by a former-samurai founder.

bio.background: An 1870-founded Edo-mae tempura house near Sensō-ji, run by descendants of its samurai-turned-restaurateur founder, known for its quiet courtyard-pond setting, sukiya-style private tatami rooms, and a kakiage recipe kept within the family for generations.

caveats: This is a historic, seafood-centric Edo-mae tempura restaurant (course-only, private tatami rooms, roughly ¥11,000 average spend). No gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient substitution, or allergy program was found in any source consulted (official site, Tabelog, tourism association pages, food blogs). The batter, tentsuyu dipping sauce, and signature kakiage are all built around wheat and seafood/dashi, so celiac and vegan diners should treat this as an unsuitable choice or contact the restaurant directly (Japanese phone reservation, 03-3841-4015) before booking rather than assume any accommodation.
closed_days: Every Tuesday and Wednesday; also closed December 31 and January 1. The restaurant notes closure dates may change with circumstances.
cuisine: 天ぷら（Edo-mae tempura — sesame-oil-fried seafood and vegetables, whole-ingredient style）
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains an English-language section of its official site and is bookable through English-facing reservation platforms (e.g. Autoreserve, Last Minute Japan), but seating is private-room, course-only and reservation is effectively required, so a visitor with zero Japanese should book ahead through one of those English channels rather than walk in.
geocode_note: Could not geocode: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and other geocoding services (Nominatim, geocoding.jp) were unreachable in this environment (network egress policy blocked the connection, 403 on every attempt). The address was instead confirmed textually across multiple independent sources (search results referencing the official site, Tabelog, and the Asakusa tourism association) as 東京都台東区浅草1-39-13, in front of the former Asakusa Public Hall — a location consistently described as a 5-minute walk from Sensō-ji, consistent with the target area, but precise coordinates could not be verified.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, batter substitution, or dedicated frying process was found in any source. Traditional Edo-mae tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce that accompanies it is standard dashi-and-soy-sauce (both typically containing wheat: soy sauce as an ingredient, bonito/kelp dashi as a shared base). No information confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer or ingredient list, so this should be assumed unsafe for celiac diners unless confirmed directly with the restaurant by phone.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon/Thu/Fri): Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:00–21:00. Weekends & public holidays: 11:30–20:00. Closed every Tuesday and Wednesday, plus December 31–January 1.
neighborhood: 浅草 — 5-minute walk from Sensō-ji (Asakusa)
seats_text: Seating is entirely in private sukiya-style tatami rooms of varying sizes around a courtyard pond, roughly 60 seats total; reservations are course-only.
vegan_detail: No vegetable-only or vegan course was found in any source. The restaurant's identity and signature dish (Raijin-age) are built around shrimp and shellfish, and the standard tentsuyu dipping sauce relies on bonito (katsuo) dashi, which is not vegan. No plant-based menu or hidden-dashi accommodation is documented, so this should be treated as unsuitable for vegan diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-29-10

bio.origin: Grew up locally in Asakusa
bio.background: Chiba began an apprenticeship as an itamae (traditional Japanese chef) at age 18 and trained for five years. At 24 he struck out on his own, opening a dining bar, and went on to run other food-and-drink businesses. In August of a recent year he founded a company called Asakusa-cho ("Asakusa Town"), deliberately registering it on the 8th of the month for its association with sue-hirogari, a fan-shaped character read as a symbol of ever-widening prosperity. Asakusa Udon, opened near the Hanayashiki amusement park in October 2025, is the company's flagship shop.
bio.specialty: Kake udon and bukkake udon (served hot, either in broth or dipping-style), to which diners add freshly fried tempura (such as kara-age chicken or shrimp) and toppings like a seasoned egg yolk or beef and shiitake mushroom.
bio.philosophy: "The town raises the people, and the people raise the town" (街が人を育み、人が街を育む) — the stated motto behind Chiba's company and this shop.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a 43-square-meter space near Asakusa Hanayashiki, with 10 standing counter spots on the ground floor and 28 table seats upstairs; it opens at 7am to serve breakfast to guests of nearby hotels and stays open into the evening for casual after-work drinking.
Chiba named his holding company Asakusa-cho and incorporated it on August 8th specifically for the auspicious, ever-widening-fortune connotation of the number 8 (sue-hirogari) in Japanese culture.

caveats: Opened October 2025, so it is very new with a thin review history. It is a traditional wheat-udon shop, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment — no dedicated gluten-free noodle, dedicated fryer, or confirmed vegan broth has been documented. Precise GPS coordinates could not be verified because the GSI geocoding service was unreachable in this session; proximity to Sensoji is based on the address and a sourced "4-minute walk" description rather than a computed distance.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found in the sources reviewed (unconfirmed — the shop appears to operate daily from 7:00-21:00, serving breakfast to nearby hotel guests through to evening drinks)
cuisine: Udon (wheat noodles) - Kanto/Kansai blend style broth
cultural_comfort_note: Sits in the heavily tourist-trafficked strip near Sensoji and Hanayashiki and markets breakfast service to nearby hotel guests, but no English menu or multilingual staff have been confirmed in available sources.
geocode_note: The GSI address geocoding endpoint (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and other geocoding services were unreachable from this session (network/proxy blocked all attempts), so coordinates could not be programmatically verified. Multiple independent sources place the shop at 東京都台東区浅草2-29-10, near Asakusa Hanayashiki and described as a 4-5 minute walk from both Sensoji and Asakusa Station — an address that sits well inside the Sensoji 0.8km target radius, but lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-flour udon shop — the noodles are made from wheat, so there is no confirmed gluten-free noodle option. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen-free process has been documented; tempura toppings appear to be fried to order in a shared fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, on-site confirmation, and even then the base noodle itself remains a wheat product.

neighborhood: Asakusa

address_ja: 東京都台東区駒形2-7-3

roles: owner/soba craftsman: Hiranuma Takayuki (name spelling per secondary sources; not independently confirmed via primary site)
bio.background: Opened in 1989 as "Asakusa Issa-an," the shop was renamed "Komagata Sobashonin" in October 1997. The owner, Hiranuma Takayuki, trained under Katakura Yasuo, a noted postwar soba craftsman credited with establishing hand-cut, thin-sliced specialty soba as a standard of the trade. The shop also runs its own soba- and udon-making school, whose 800-plus graduates have gone on to open their own shops.
bio.specialty: Five-color soba (五色そば) — a tasting set of five hand-cut noodle varieties (plain/seiro, rustic/inaka, poppy-seed/keshi-kiri, lemon/lemon-kiri, and tea/cha-kiri) — and duck nanban soba (鴨南そば) served in a yuzu-scented duck broth.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows three principles for good soba: freshly milled flour, freshly made noodles, freshly boiled water. Buckwheat is sourced from high-elevation (700m+) fields in the Aizu region of Fukushima Prefecture and stone-milled in-house on five large mills, ground slowly to preserve aroma and flavor.

anecdotes.text: About ten rotating varieties of hand-cut soba are made daily, including seiro, inaka (rustic), lemon-kiri, keshi-kiri (poppy seed), shiso-kiri, and goma-kiri (sesame).
The duck broth in the kamo-nanban soba carries a yuzu fragrance, balancing the sweetness of long green onion and duck against a carefully judged kaeshi-and-dashi blend.
Buckwheat flour comes from Aizu, Fukushima, from fields above 700m elevation, and is stone-ground in-house.

caveats: Geocoding could not be verified: the GSI address-search API and other geocoding services (Nominatim, geocoding.jp, Photon) were all unreachable in this environment due to network egress policy, so lat/lng are null despite a confirmed street address. Multiple independent sources place the shop about 50m west of Komagata Bridge and roughly 320m (5-min walk) from Asakusa Station, which is consistent with being well within the Sensoji/Asakusa target radius, but this is not a geocoded confirmation. Whether the soba is 100%-buckwheat (juwari, naturally gluten-free noodle) versus a standard wheat-bound (nihachi) blend is not confirmed by any source found — sources only confirm the buckwheat itself is 100% domestic (Aizu-sourced), which is a sourcing claim, not a flour-ratio claim. Business hours end times vary slightly between sources (14:00 vs 14:30 lunch close; 20:00 vs 20:30 dinner close). The owner's name romanization ("Hiranuma Takayuki") is corroborated by two secondary sources but not by a primary/official source, since the restaurant has no confirmed official website. Could not directly fetch Tabelog or most restaurant-info sites (403 errors on all WebFetch attempts in this environment); all facts are drawn from WebSearch-surfaced secondary sources (blogs, portal aggregators) rather than primary pages read directly.

gf_detail: Sources confirm the buckwheat itself is sourced 100% domestically, from high-elevation Aizu (Fukushima) fields, and is stone-milled in-house, but none specify the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio of the actual noodles (i.e., whether they are juwari/100%-buckwheat or a standard nihachi blend that uses wheat as a binder). The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is the traditional kaeshi-and-bonito-dashi type; no gluten-free or wheat-free sauce alternative, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the flour blend and sauce ingredients before ordering — this shop does not market itself as gluten-free.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-15-1

bio.origin: Founded in Shōwa 11 (1936) as a drinking establishment in Asakusa's entertainment district; later became a family-run yōshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant, now in its third generation.
bio.background: ぱいち has been run by the same family for three generations since 1936, tucked in a backstreet corner one block north of Kaminarimon-dōri. It began life as a small bar rather than a restaurant.
bio.philosophy: Even as the storefront signage and neighborhood around it have changed over the decades, the family has kept the flavor and spirit of the original shop largely unchanged, serving affordable, nostalgic Shōwa-era Western food to neighborhood regulars.
bio.specialty: Beef stew (ビーフシチュー) served bubbling in an iron pot: the demi-glace sauce is specially researched and simmered for about 4 days, and the beef itself is slow-simmered for 7-8 hours for a rich but light, soup-like sauce. Also known for tongue stew (タンシチュー), menchi-katsu, and katsu sando.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name is said to come from 一杯 (ippai, "one drink") — a nod to its origins as a bar — with the word playfully reversed to create ぱいち (Paichi).
The sign advertising the restaurant's "famous beef stew" was hand-written by the current (third-generation) owner's uncle.
The restaurant has long been popular with entertainment-industry regulars connected to Asakusa's theater and performance scene, drawn in by the beef stew.

caveats: Traditional Shōwa-era yōshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation found in any source; wheat-based roux and breaded/fried dishes are central to the menu. Coordinates could not be verified via the GSI geocoder due to a network/tooling outage in this session (all WebFetch calls, including to unrelated test domains, returned 403), so lat/lng are left null despite strong textual evidence the address sits well within the Sensō-ji target radius.
closed_days: Sundays (and occasional irregular holidays)
cuisine: 洋食 (Yōshoku / Japanese Western-style cuisine)
