name_ja: 浅草名代らーめん 与ろゐ屋
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), on Denboin-dori, about a 1-minute walk from Sensoji Temple

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free noodle, soy-sauce, or preparation option is advertised anywhere in the menu information found, including for the vegan ramen; standard wheat noodles and wafu shoyu tare appear to be used throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan ramen is a distinct menu item made in a shop that otherwise cooks meat- and fish-based broths (pork bone, chicken, niboshi, katsuobushi) on the same line; no statement about separate vegan-only equipment or pots was found.

positives.text: The vegan ramen's broth is described as made entirely from vegetables and kombu (Chinese cabbage, onion, dried shiitake, kombu, carrot) with no animal-derived dashi, and the noodles used for it are egg-free.
The gyoza is made without pork or garlic, using Oyama chicken thigh and harusame (glass noodles), which may suit some dietary preferences though the wrapper is wheat-based.

seats_text: 32 seats (9 at the 1F counter, 23 at 2F tables)
vegan_detail: There is a dedicated vegan ramen (about ¥1,250) whose broth is made only from vegetables and kombu (Chinese cabbage, onion, dried shiitake mushroom, kombu, carrot) — no katsuobushi, niboshi, or meat stock — finished wafu-shoyu style with sansho pepper leaf and yuzu aroma, and made with egg-free noodles. However, this is one item on an otherwise omnivore, fish/meat-dashi-heavy menu (the standard ramen and most other dishes use pork bone, chicken, niboshi and bonito), so strict vegans should specifically order the vegan ramen and confirm current preparation with staff rather than assuming other menu items are safe.

bio.origin: Founded in Meiji 40 (1907) in Asakusa, Taito-ku — one of the neighborhood's old-line (shinise) unagi specialists, still on its original side street a few minutes from Kaminarimon gate.
bio.background: Hatsuogawa has operated as a dedicated eel restaurant in Asakusa for over a century, through the Meiji, Taisho, Showa and Heisei eras, in a modest shopfront near Kaminarimon. It is one of the district's recognized old-line eel houses and was named to the 2022 'Unagi Hyakumeiten' (100 Notable Eel Restaurants) list and carries a Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition in the Tokyo guide.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style unaju/unadon — eel is not held ready-made; each order is butterflied, steamed, then finished over charcoal (shichirin) after an order comes in, so the fat renders out and the flesh stays soft and light rather than heavy.
bio.philosophy: A made-to-order approach (no pre-grilled eel sitting in a case) paired with a single tare sauce that has been kept going and topped up continuously since the shop opened in 1907, rather than remade from scratch.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name is said to combine 'Hatsu' from the founder's given name Hatsutaro and 'Ogawa,' associated with the eel trade, forming Hatsuogawa (初小川).
The dry-leaning (karakuchi) tare in use today is the same base that has been continuously replenished since the restaurant's 1907 founding.

caveats: This is a traditional, century-old eel (unagi) specialist, not a gluten-free or vegan destination. The whole menu centers on grilled eel; no vegetarian/vegan dishes or confirmed gluten-free tare were identified. Included in the guide as a notable independent old-line restaurant with honest dietary caveats, not as a safe GF/vegan pick.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day; the shop is small and popular, so it's worth calling ahead or booking before visiting.
cuisine: Unagi (eel) — Edomae-style kabayaki/unaju, est. 1907
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only, old-school Asakusa eel shop with no official English-language site found — expect a Japanese-language menu and limited English service, so some Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
geocode_note: Could not verify coordinates: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by network egress policy in this environment (HTTP 403 at the proxy) on every attempt, so no automated geocode was possible. The address is Taito-ku, Kaminarimon 2-chome, i.e. immediately adjacent to the Kaminarimon gate / Sensoji approach, which by known neighborhood geography sits well within 0.8km of the Sensoji anchor point — but exact lat/lng could not be independently confirmed and are left null rather than estimated.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The eel is grilled rather than fried, but the kabayaki tare is a traditional soy-sauce-based sauce continuously replenished since 1907; that style of shoyu is typically wheat-based, and no source confirms a wheat-free/tamari tare, dedicated GF menu, or celiac protocol. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from the restaurant.
hours_raw: 12:00–13:30 and 17:00–19:30 daily (lunch and dinner service); 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly day off)
neighborhood: 浅草・雷門 (Asakusa, near Kaminarimon gate — Asakusa Station area: Tobu/Toei/Metro)

positives.text: Eel is prepared to order (butterflied, steamed, then charcoal-grilled after the order is placed) rather than held pre-cooked, and the cooking method is grilling rather than deep-frying, so there is no shared tempura fryer in the process.

seats_text: About 14 seats — a mix of table seating, a small raised tatami alcove (koagari), and a small tatami room (zashiki)
vegan_detail: Not vegan-appropriate: the entire menu is built around grilled eel (unaju/unadon and related eel dishes), an animal product, and no vegetarian or vegan dishes, plant-based tare, or dashi substitutions are documented anywhere in available sources.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-32-4

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (near the old Asakusa Kemban geisha-registry office, in the quieter "Oku-Asakusa" backstreets)
bio.background: A neighborhood kamameshi (clay-pot rice) shop that has operated in the same Asakusa backstreet since 1968, now run by a third-generation owner who has carried on the original secret rice-cooking method passed down from the shop's founder. The two-story traditional wooden building, with tatami seating throughout, sits near the historic Asakusa Kemban geisha-registry office in the quieter western part of the neighborhood, away from the main Nakamise/Sensoji tourist crush.
bio.specialty: Individually cooked kamameshi (clay-pot rice) — the menu runs to nearly 20 varieties, from gomoku (mixed vegetable) and seafood kamameshi to crab and octopus versions, each pot made to order with seasonal fish, vegetables, mushrooms, or chicken over soy-seasoned rice.
bio.philosophy: Each pot is cooked individually to order — taking roughly 30 minutes — using a closely guarded cooking technique (rather than the classic folk rhyme for rice-cooking) said to produce fragrant broth-infused rice with a good crust without sticking or burning; guests are expected to settle in with a drink and appetizers while they wait rather than expect fast service.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1968, the shop is now on its third generation of family ownership, with the current owner carefully preserving the founder's secret rice-cooking method.

The restaurant is a two-story traditional Japanese house near the old Asakusa Kemban (geisha registry office), with roughly 50 tatami-mat seats across both floors and autographed shikishi from past celebrity visitors on the walls.
A kamameshi order takes about 30 minutes to cook, so regulars typically order appetizers and drinks to enjoy while they wait; the shop offers close to 20 original kamameshi varieties.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog and most restaurant/portal pages was blocked during this verification session (persistent HTTP 403s at the network layer, including on control URLs), so identity, address, hours, and background were cross-confirmed via multiple independent WebSearch-indexed sources rather than by directly reading a single primary page. Coordinates are a chome-level approximation (the GSI government geocoder was also unreachable) triangulated from two nearby addresses on the same block (Asakusa 3-2-4 and 3-25-6) which both cluster tightly around the given lat/lng, roughly 200m from Sensoji. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation information could be found anywhere for this restaurant; as a traditional soy-sauce-seasoned, predominantly seafood/meat-based kamameshi shop with no indication of dedicated allergy handling, it should be treated as not celiac-safe and not vegan without direct, in-person confirmation from staff (likely in Japanese only).
closed_days: Closed Wednesdays, plus alternating Tuesdays (biweekly pattern — check before visiting)
cuisine: Kamameshi (individual clay-pot rice cooked to order with seasonal fish, vegetables, mushrooms, or chicken)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, old-school kamameshi shop near Asakusa Kemban with no evidence of English menus, English-speaking staff, or tourist-oriented signage, so a translation app or basic Japanese is recommended, especially for explaining any dietary restrictions.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API was unreachable in this session; coordinates are a chome-level estimate derived from two nearby known addresses in the same block (Asakusa 3-chome), which agree closely (~35.716–35.717 N, ~139.798 E) and place the shop roughly 200m from Sensoji — well inside the target radius but not a lot-level geocode of 3-32-4 itself.
gf_detail: This is a traditional kamameshi shop where the rice is seasoned with soy sauce (and typically mirin) and topped with fish, seafood, chicken, and mushrooms — soy sauce in Japan is almost always wheat-based unless labeled otherwise. No gluten-free menu, tamari substitution, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source, and it is a small traditional kitchen with no indication of allergen-separated cooking. Should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff (in Japanese), and even then cross-contact via shared broths/rice pots is likely.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O.), Dinner 17:15-22:00. Closed Wednesdays and every other Tuesday.
neighborhood: Asakusa (Oku-Asakusa, near Asakusa Kemban), Taito City / Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station area
seats_text: Approximately 50 tatami-mat (zashiki) seats spread across two floors of a traditional wooden house (a few sources cite up to ~60).
vegan_detail: Kamameshi at this shop is built around fish, seafood, chicken, and (per menu listings) seafood-forward varieties like crab and mixed-seafood kamameshi over dashi-seasoned rice; even vegetable/mushroom versions are very likely cooked in the same bonito-based dashi used throughout the menu. No vegan-specific dishes, dashi substitutions, or plant-based options were mentioned in any source found. Should be treated as not vegan-friendly without a very specific, in-person request to confirm a katsuo-free preparation, which is unlikely to be accommodated at a shop this traditional.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-42-4

bio.origin: Founded 1910 (Meiji 43) in Asakusa by Ozaki Kan'ichi, a former Yokohama customs official
bio.background: Ozaki Kan'ichi left his post at the Yokohama customs house and, drawing on ties to Yokohama Chinatown, brought a team of Cantonese cooks to Asakusa to open 来々軒 in 1910. He adapted the pork-and-noodle 'buta soba' already served in Yokohama's Chinatown into a soy-sauce-based broth suited to Japanese palates -- widely credited as the origin point of Tokyo (shoyu) ramen and of Japan's 'machi-chuka' neighborhood Chinese-restaurant culture. The shop was hugely popular, reportedly serving 2,500-3,000 customers a day at its peak and touching off Japan's first ramen boom. It closed during WWII, reopened near Tokyo Station in 1945, and the family's operation finally ended in 1976. For years afterward the Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum researched the shop's history, tracing the original wheat variety and the soy sauce brand used in the broth. On July 2, 2026, the founder's 92-year-old grandson Takahashi Kunio and his 38-year-old great-grandson Takahashi Yūsaku reopened the shop in Asakusa, reviving the family name after an 80-year absence.
bio.specialty: 百年醤油らうめん (Hyakunen Shōyu Raumen, "hundred-year soy sauce ramen") -- a soy-sauce ramen made with a wheat variety said to carry the genetics of Meiji-era flour and the same Yamasa soy sauce the original shop used from its founding; also known for oversized (60g) shumai and for Tenshindon, a crab-and-egg rice bowl the shop is credited with inventing.
bio.philosophy: To recreate, as faithfully as research allows, the exact flavor Ozaki Kan'ichi served in 1910 -- treating the noodles, broth and soy sauce as a piece of food heritage to be preserved and passed to a new generation of the family.

anecdotes.text: Founder Ozaki Kan'ichi, a former Yokohama customs official, brought a dozen Chinese cooks from Yokohama's Nankinmachi (Chinatown) to open the shop in Asakusa in 1910, converting Yokohama's pork noodle soup into a soy-sauce broth familiar to Japanese diners.
At its peak the Meiji-era shop is said to have served 2,500 to 3,000 customers a day, touching off Japan's first ramen boom.

After the original shop closed, the Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum spent years researching its history, tracing the heritage wheat variety and the original Yamasa soy sauce used in the broth for the revived shop's signature ramen.

The revival was driven by the founder's 92-year-old grandson Takahashi Kunio and his 38-year-old great-grandson Takahashi Yūsaku, who reopened the shop in Asakusa on July 2, 2026, 80 years after the family last ran a shop there.
Tenshindon, the crab-and-egg rice bowl now on the menu, is said to have originated at the shop in response to customers who wanted something they could eat quickly.

caveats: Newly reopened (July 2, 2026) revival of a 1910-founded historic shop; only 6 days old at time of research. No public source confirmed gluten-free or vegan menu options, exact seating count, reservation policy, or payment methods -- treat those fields as unverified/estimated. Coordinates are estimated from nearby geocoded landmarks (a direct address geocode could not be obtained because the geocoding service was unreachable in this session), not a precise rooftop geocode.
closed_days: None reported (無休 / no fixed closed day per available sources)
cuisine: 中華料理 (Chinese / machi-chuka, historic origin-point of Tokyo shoyu ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: A newly reopened, media-spotlighted heritage noodle shop in a very touristy pocket of Asakusa; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found, so expect a Japanese-language ordering experience (pointing at photos or menu items should work fine).
gf_detail: The signature dish, 百年醤油らうめん, is a wheat-noodle ramen made with a heritage wheat variety, and the broth is seasoned with standard (wheat-containing) Yamasa soy sauce, the same brand used since the shop's 1910 founding. The menu also features shumai and wonton, both traditionally wheat-wrapped. No gluten-free noodle, sauce substitution, dedicated fryer, or allergy-menu option was mentioned in any source found.

Coordinates below are estimated by triangulating from nearby geocoded reference points: 千束公園 (35.71868, 139.79394), a nearby condominium at the adjoining block 浅草3-42-7 reported as ~80m from that park and a 6-7 minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station (35.714037, 139.792517). The resulting estimate places the address roughly 450-500m from Sensōji, well within the 0.8km target radius, but it should be treated as approximate rather than a precise geocode.
neighborhood: Asakusa, near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station (浅草三丁目)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature ramen broth uses standard Yamasa soy sauce, the same wheat-containing shoyu the original 1910 shop used, rather than a gluten-free/tamari alternative.

seats_text: Seating capacity not published in sources found.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork- and seafood-based dishes: pork shumai and wonton, and Tenshindon topped with crab and egg. No vegetarian/vegan broth, dashi substitute, or plant-based menu item was mentioned in any source found; assume the ramen broth and most dishes contain animal products.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-9-10

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — operating on the same spot since 1954
bio.background: Yadoroku was opened in Showa 29 (1954) just behind Sensō-ji's main hall by the grandmother of the current owner, who started selling rice balls to support her family after her husband was out of work. It has been run by the same family for three generations and is widely cited as Tokyo's oldest surviving onigiri specialty shop. The current owner, Yosuke Miura, is the third-generation proprietor.
bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri built around a single, carefully chosen rice and roughly 20 traditional fillings — the shop's own most-popular picks are salted salmon, tarako (cod roe), and ami (tiny dried shrimp simmered in a savory sauce).
bio.philosophy: Every November when the new rice crop comes in, Miura tastes around 20 varieties and commits to a single one for the whole year (currently Niigata-grown Koshihikari). The rice is still cooked the way it was in 1954 — in traditional iron pots, in small 1.5-cup batches, four times a day — and Miura has studied academic research on starch and enzyme chemistry to refine the cooking process while keeping the shop's original character.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on a double meaning: Asakusa is just the neighborhood, but 'roku' echoes 'rokudenashi' (good-for-nothing) — a nod to the out-of-work husband the founder, Miura's grandmother, opened the shop in 1954 to support.
Third-generation owner Yosuke Miura tastes about 20 rice varieties every November when the new crop arrives, then selects a single type to use for the entire year.
The rice is still cooked in traditional iron pots, in small batches four times a day, the same method used since the shop opened in 1954.

Onigiri Asakusa Yadoroku became the first onigiri specialist ever awarded a Michelin Bib Gourmand, starting with the 2019 Tokyo guide, and remains the only onigiri shop with a Michelin distinction in Tokyo.

caveats: Exact closing time for dinner service is not fixed (shop closes as soon as the day's rice sells out), so the dinner window given is an approximation drawn from multiple listings rather than a guaranteed schedule. Precise GPS coordinates could not be verified via the GSI geocoder because that host was blocked by this session's network egress policy (403); address and neighborhood are well documented (immediately behind Sensō-ji's main hall, Kototoi-dori side) and clearly fall within the required Sensōji proximity, but lat/lng are left null rather than estimated.
closed_days: Closed all day Sunday; open for lunch only (no dinner service) on Tuesday and Wednesday
cuisine: Onigiri (Japanese rice balls) specialist
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language, photo-illustrated menu is available and the shop is very used to foreign visitors, but there's no reservation system — you queue outside and are called in by order of arrival, so basic patience (and sometimes a real wait) is part of the experience.
gf_detail: Plain rice and nori are naturally gluten-free, but several popular fillings (notably the simmered shrimp "ami") and any seasoning sauces used in the kitchen are likely soy-sauce based, and Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. No source found confirms a wheat-free soy sauce, a dedicated gluten-free prep area, or a formal allergy protocol here — this is a small, decades-old kitchen making everything by hand in one space, so ask staff about the specific filling and any seasoning before ordering if you need to avoid gluten strictly.

Both lunch and dinner end whenever the day's rice sells out, so actual closing times vary. Closed all day Sunday.
neighborhood: Kannonji-ura, East Asakusa (immediately behind Sensō-ji's main hall, Taitō-ku)

positives.text: An English menu with photos is available, and the shop regularly serves foreign visitors, making it easier to identify and ask about individual fillings.

seats_text: Small shop with roughly 14 seats (an L-shaped counter plus two small tables)
vegan_detail: Most of the shop's roughly 20 fillings are fish- or seafood-based (salted salmon, tarako/cod roe, dried baby shrimp, salted squid, salmon roe), so the only clearly plant-based option identified is the umeboshi (pickled plum) filling, or plain salted rice with nori. No source confirms whether the plain rice itself ever touches dashi/bonito stock in preparation, and the same small kitchen handles all the fish fillings, so strict vegans should ask before ordering.

lead: 浅草もんじゃ 風鈴
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-21-7

roles: staff cook monjayaki tableside for guests
bio.background: Opened in November 2023 on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station; the monjayaki base is a homemade chicken-bone broth combined with Japanese dashi, and the okonomiyaki batter is made to be light and fluffy inside with a crisp exterior.
bio.specialty: Mentaiko mochi-cheese monjayaki (明太餅チーズもんじゃ), described in visitor accounts as the shop's most popular dish

anecdotes.text: A food writer covering the November 2023 opening on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise-dori highlighted the staff's lightning-fast spatula technique, cooking monjayaki right in front of guests at the table.

A December 2024 visitor trying monjayaki for the first time ordered the mentaiko mochi-cheese monja and a seafood monja, found the mentaiko mochi-cheese dish lived up to its reputation as the most popular item, and enjoyed watching the texture change as the broth reduced on the griddle over time.

caveats: Could not independently geocode the address this session (GSI address-search API was unreachable due to network/proxy restrictions), so lat/lng are left null even though the address and multiple listings place it a 3-4 minute walk from Asakusa Station on Shin-Nakamise-dori, well within the Sensoji radius. No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, ingredient breakdown, or allergy-handling policy was found in any source checked (Tabelog, official site links, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Jalan, blog write-up, news article) — this is a standard wheat-flour-batter, dashi-based monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant with no documented dietary accommodations, so gf/vegan ratings reflect an honest "no" rather than a confirmed absence of any hidden options.
closed_days: 定休日なし (no regular closed days per restaurant listings)
cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き (Monjayaki & Okonomiyaki teppanyaki)

Both okonomiyaki and monjayaki here use a standard wheat-flour (komugiko) batter, and the monjayaki broth is a chicken-bone-and-dashi base, which in Japanese kitchens typically involves soy sauce (contains wheat) and other seasonings that are not gluten-free by default. No dedicated fryer, gluten-free menu, or celiac-safe process is documented, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.

No vegetarian or vegan menu option, plant-based dashi, or ingredient substitution is documented in any source found, so this is not a suitable choice for vegan diners as currently listed.

bio.background: A retro, no-frills stand-up ramen stall right at the entrance to Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Sensō-ji. It's known locally as one of the cheapest bowls of ramen in central Tokyo, drawing both neighborhood regulars and curious visitors exploring the old shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen: a clear, chicken-bone (torigara) soy-sauce broth with soft medium-curly noodles, chashu pork, menma, nori, bean sprouts and scallion

anecdotes.text: A basic bowl runs about 350–400 yen and a version topped with a seasoned soft-boiled egg is about 470 yen — startlingly cheap for the Asakusa tourist district.
Described as a classic tachigui (stand-and-eat) counter with a couple of outdoor tables for those who want to sit, right by the Hisago-dori entrance.

This is a classic wheat-noodle, chicken-bone, soy-sauce shoyu ramen stall with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation found in any source — not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-38-10

bio.origin: Founded in 1887 (Meiji 20) on Denbōin-dori in Asakusa, originally as a soba noodle shop
bio.background: The shop's founder, Maruyama Hiromasa, was a Kyoto court samurai (公家侍) in the late Edo period. When the Meiji Emperor relocated from Kyoto to Tokyo, Hiromasa came along in service protecting court nobles, and worked as a secretary at the Asakusa residence of court noble Sanjō Sanetomi. He then married into the Daikokuya soba-shop family and was adopted into it, beginning the family's restaurant lineage. Around the end of the Meiji era (c. 1910) the shop switched from soba to tempura, and the name changed from Daikokuya (大黒屋) to Daikokuya (大黒家). It has been passed down through five generations of the same family to the present-day owner.
bio.specialty: Ebi tendon (海老天丼) — a rice bowl topped with four large shrimp tempura pieces, deep-fried in sesame oil and dipped in the shop's proprietary dark, sweet-savory tsuyu sauce, a recipe handed down through the generations and traditionally prepared only by the head of the family.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the same Edo-style tempura technique, the secret tsuyu recipe, and the atmosphere of old Asakusa across five generations of family ownership.

anecdotes.text: Founder Maruyama Hiromasa was a Kyoto court samurai who came to Tokyo during the Meiji Restoration while protecting court nobles, worked as a secretary for the noble Sanjō Sanetomi in northern Asakusa, and then married into and was adopted by the Daikokuya soba-shop family, beginning the restaurant's history.

The restaurant opened in 1887 as a soba shop on Denbōin-dori and converted to a tempura specialist around the end of the Meiji era, changing its characters from 大黒屋 to 大黒家; it is now run by the fifth generation.

caveats: Not screened for gluten-free or vegan diners: this is a historic Edo-style tempura specialist. No gluten-free batter, wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere in available sources. Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified because the GSI geocoding API was unreachable in this session (network policy blocked msearch.gsi.go.jp); proximity to Sensōji was instead confirmed via multiple independent descriptions of the address as directly on Denbōin-dori beside the temple.
closed_days: 無休 (open every day of the year, no regular closed days)
cuisine: 天ぷら・海老天丼 (Tempura / shrimp tempura rice bowl specialist)
cultural_comfort_note: Sits in the heart of tourist Asakusa on Denbōin-dori steps from Sensōji, with an English-language Tabelog listing, but the restaurant is cash-only and no English menu or English-speaking-staff guarantee was found, so bring some basic Japanese or pointing/translation-app patience.
gf_detail: Traditional Edo-style tempura restaurant: tempura is coated in a standard wheat-flour batter and deep-fried in shared sesame oil alongside shellfish, and the proprietary dipping tsuyu is described as a soy-sauce-based sauce (ordinary Japanese shoyu contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, wheat-free soy sauce, allergen-free fryer, or any celiac/gluten-free accommodation is mentioned by the restaurant or in any source reviewed. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was unreachable in this session — outbound requests to it were blocked by network egress policy (confirmed via repeated 403 CONNECT failures), so coordinates could not be programmatically geocoded. Multiple independent sources (travel-history.jp, san-tatsu.jp) describe the address as directly fronting Denbōin-dori (伝法院通り), immediately beside Sensōji's Denbōin, roughly 3-4 minutes on foot from Sensōji's main hall/Kaminarimon and under a minute from Nakamise-dori — well inside the 0.8km Sensōji radius — so the location requirement is satisfied by textual/descriptive confirmation even without a coordinate pair.
hours_raw: 11:10-20:30 (Mon-Fri, Sun); 11:10-21:00 (Sat & holidays); open every day, no regular closed days
neighborhood: 浅草（浅草1丁目）

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant fries all tempura — shrimp, fish, and vegetable/kakiage items — in a shared sesame-oil fryer at a single tempura station; no allergen-segregated or dedicated gluten-free fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tsuyu dipping sauce is described as a dark, concentrated, soy-sauce-based sauce; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat and no wheat-free (tamari) alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura/kakiage is fried in the same oil and at the same station as shrimp and other seafood tempura, so cross-contact with shellfish is likely for any vegetable items ordered.

seats_text: Multiple floors of table and tatami (zashiki) seating, no counter seating
vegan_detail: The house specialty is deep-fried shrimp tempura (ebi tendon), and the menu is built around seafood tempura. Vegetable tempura/kakiage exists on the menu but shares the frying oil with shrimp and other seafood, and no information confirms whether the tsuyu sauce or any broth is free of bonito/fish-derived dashi. No vegan menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 御膳蕎麦 雷門田川

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — opened in 1965 a few dozen steps from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate.
bio.background: The shop opened in 1965 on the little lane just off Kaminarimon Gate and has served Asakusa's temple-district crowds — neighborhood regulars as well as visitors passing under the great lantern — for six decades.
bio.specialty: Gozen-soba (also called sarashina soba): noodles hand-kneaded with hot water using only the pale, starchy center of the buckwheat kernel, prized for their light color, delicate aroma, and smooth texture. The house's 'yonshoku' (four-color) assortment pairs classic sarashina with sesame-, matcha-, and seasonally flavored noodles on one tray.

anecdotes.text: Gozen-soba (sarashina) is made from only the innermost part of the buckwheat groat; because this flour is high in starch and does not bind easily with cold water, it is hand-worked with hot water, giving the noodles their characteristic pale color and refined texture.
Located about 30 seconds' walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate and about 2 minutes from Ginza Line Asakusa Station; open since 1965.

caveats: Direct fetches to tabelog.com, kaminarimon-tagawa.com, and the GSI geocoding API were blocked by this session's network egress policy (repeated 403 on CONNECT); all facts below were confirmed instead via search-engine aggregation of those same pages plus independent secondary listings (soba-aqua.com, asakusanioideyo.com, Retty, Ekiten, NAVITIME), which converged consistently on address, hours, and seat count. Payment method (cash vs. card) and English-menu availability were not confirmed by any source — cash_only and cultural_comfort_level are best-effort defaults, not verified facts. Precise lat/lng could not be geocoded (GSI API blocked); area match is inferred from the repeated, independently-sourced description of the shop as ~30 seconds on foot from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found in any source; this is a conventional soba/udon shop and should be treated as unsafe for celiac or strict vegan diners absent direct confirmation from staff.
closed_days: Tuesdays; also closed the 3rd Wednesday of each month (shifts if that Wednesday falls on a public holiday)
cuisine: Soba (gozen-soba/sarashina specialist) and udon, plus donburi rice bowls; a long-running neighborhood shop popular with local Asakusa regulars
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu was confirmed by any source, but the shop sits directly on the main tourist path 30 seconds from Kaminarimon Gate, so basic greetings and pointing at a Japanese menu should suffice.

Multiple independent sources (official site, Tabelog, soba-aqua.com blog, Asakusa merchants' association site) consistently describe the shop as roughly 30 seconds on foot from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate (35.7148, 139.7967) at address 東京都台東区浅草1-18-3, which places it comfortably within the 0.8km Asakusa/Sensoji target radius even without a geocoded point.

This is a traditional soba/udon shop — udon is wheat-based, and the "gozen-soba/sarashina" specialty, while using a fine buckwheat cut, is not advertised anywhere as 十割 (100% buckwheat); traditional tsuyu dipping sauce made with regular shoyu (which contains wheat) should be assumed. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:00–15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 16:30–20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: 10:30–19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Wednesday of each month.
name_ja: 御膳蕎麦 雷門田川
neighborhood: Asakusa (30 seconds from Sensoji's Kaminarimon Gate)
seats_text: Approximately 30 seats, table seating, non-smoking.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門1-16-1

bio.background: A small, counter-only Edo-mae tempura shop tucked just off Kaminarimon-dori near Tawaramachi Station, in the Asakusa neighborhood. It has operated for decades as a family-style tempura counter rather than a large restaurant, with seating limited to a ground-floor counter and a second counter upstairs.
bio.specialty: The tempura teishoku (set meal) and tendon (tempura rice bowl), fried to order at the counter with shrimp and seasonal vegetables in a light Edo-style batter.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to Edo-mae tempura technique — wrapping the natural flavor of fresh, seasonal ingredients in a light, crisp batter and frying each piece to order rather than in advance.

anecdotes.text: Described by local food blogs as devoted to Edo-style tempura for around 27 years, coating quality ingredients in a light batter and frying them fresh at the counter.
Known as a small, old-school tempura counter that draws a lunchtime queue for its reasonably priced tempura set and tendon, served fresh from the fryer.

caveats: Direct page fetches to Tabelog, gnavi, HotPepper, NAVITIME, Yahoo Map, and the official GSI geocoder API were all blocked by the sandbox network policy (HTTP 403 at the proxy layer) despite repeated attempts with different user agents and via the archive. All facts below were reconstructed from search-engine result snippets of those pages (WebSearch tool), which is a weaker form of verification than a direct page fetch — figures like seat count and lunch hours show minor inconsistencies across secondary sources (e.g., "11 counter seats" vs "10 seats 1F + 7 seats 2F"; lunch window sometimes cited as ending 12:30 vs 14:00) that I could not resolve by reading the primary Tabelog page directly. Coordinates could not be obtained from the GSI address-search API (also blocked); the lat/lng given is a manual estimate anchored to Tawaramachi Station's confirmed coordinates (via a Mapion map link) plus the reported "2-3 min walk from Exit 3," and should be treated as approximate. No source found described specific gluten-free or vegan accommodations, so those assessments are inferred from the cuisine type (wheat-batter tempura, bonito-dashi dipping sauce) rather than a direct statement by the restaurant.

cuisine: Tempura (Edo-mae style) / Tendon
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, cash-only counter shop with no evidence of an English menu — visitors comfortable pointing at a menu or using basic Japanese (and prepared to queue at lunch) will have the easiest time.

The tentsuyu dipping sauce is a soy-sauce-based sauce, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. No gluten-free accommodation was found in any source, so this is not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

No vegan-specific menu, substitution, or accommodation was found in any source, so this cannot be recommended as vegan-safe.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門1-5-9 いさよビル

bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers of Koshu Kenmidori-brand chicken over Kishu binchotan, especially the signature tsukune (chicken meatballs), plus counter dishes like u-tama (raw egg yolk) and kawa-ponzu (charcoal-grilled chicken skin with ponzu)
bio.philosophy: The kitchen relies on the clean, low-smoke heat of Kishu binchotan charcoal to let the quality of the Koshu Kenmidori chicken speak for itself, basting each skewer in its own rendered fat as it grills for concentrated flavor and aroma.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was selected for the Bib Gourmand in the Michelin Guide Tokyo 2022, one of ten Tokyo yakitori spots to receive the distinction.
Regulars single out the tsukune for its notably fluffy, soft texture, and describe the room as a small, homey counter of about 12-14 seats with an intimate, unpretentious atmosphere.
It is noted as a convenient hidden-gem yakitori spot near Tawaramachi Station that takes same-day reservations.

caveats: Two review-aggregator sources give slightly different weekend opening times (17:00 vs 17:30) — the hours below use the more commonly repeated figures. Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified because the required geocoding API was unreachable in this environment; the address itself (Kaminarimon 1-chome, 2 min from Tawaramachi Station, 5 min from Asakusa Station) is well-documented across multiple independent listings and is expected to fall within the Sensoji target radius. No source discusses gluten-free or vegan accommodation specifically for this restaurant — the dietary assessment below is inferred from the omakase-only yakitori format and typical Japanese yakitori tare composition, not a restaurant-specific confirmation, so it should be treated as cautious guidance rather than a verified claim.

Yakitori tare (the dipping sauce used on many skewers) is typically soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat, and the popular tsukune (chicken meatballs) commonly uses breadcrumb-style binders in this style of cooking — neither has been confirmed allergen-free here. Because the course is entirely chef's-choice with no listed way to opt out of sauced items, this should not be considered celiac-safe without a direct, in-Japanese conversation with the restaurant beforehand.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-33-5

bio.origin: The Towada name traces back to roots as far as Meiji 17 (1884) on Asakusa's Nakamise-dori; accounts differ on the exact modern founding (one source ties it to a Taisho-era (1924) confectionery, Kikusuido, that later became a soba shop; another gives Showa 44/1969). What's consistent across sources is that it is a long-running, family-rooted Asakusa soba house with more than one branch, including this Metro-dori location.
bio.background: Towada is part of an old Asakusa soba lineage associated with the Tominaga family. A frequently cited figure, okami (proprietress) Tomoe Tominaga, is remembered locally for helping lead Asakusa's revival after visitor traffic declined following the 1964 Tokyo Olympics — founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai (proprietresses' association) and helping launch the Asakusa Samba Carnival. The shop's soba is handmade by craftsman Kawamura Takashi, who has spent 28 years training and working as a soba artisan and is now described as 'the face of Towada.'
bio.philosophy: Towada sources its buckwheat (Nanbu soba flour) from contract farms in the mountains around Lake Towada, straddling Aomori/Akita/Iwate, with flour delivered fresh to Tokyo daily. The tsuyu dipping sauce is made in-house using only bonito (katsuobushi) flakes, with no MSG or chemical additives, and the shop uses spring water drawn from the Ryusendo limestone cave in Iwate's Iwaizumi. Other ingredients are sourced directly from Tohoku producers — Tono-area vegetables, Aomori duck, Akita hinai-jidori chicken.
bio.specialty: Kuretsubo soba (暮坪そば) — soba noodles served with grated kuretsubo kabu, a rare, sharply pungent mountain turnip from the Kuretsubo district of Tono, Iwate, used in place of wasabi as a condiment. It's cited repeatedly as a dish essentially unique to this shop in Tokyo.

anecdotes.text: Chef Kawamura Takashi has been a soba craftsman, including his apprenticeship, for 28 years and is now the public face of the Towada shop in Asakusa.

Okami Tomoe Tominaga is remembered as a 'legendary proprietress' who helped revive Asakusa after the 1964 Olympics diverted tourism away from the neighborhood, founding the Asakusa Okamisan-kai and helping start the Asakusa Samba Carnival.
The shop's roots are described as tracing back to a Meiji 17 (1884) establishment on Nakamise-dori.
Kuretsubo soba, topped with a rare mountain turnip sourced directly from Tono, Iwate, is called a dish you can only get at this shop in Tokyo.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, the restaurant's official site, and the government geocoding API was blocked by a tool/network outage during verification (every HTTPS fetch attempted, including to unrelated control URLs, returned 403). All facts here are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that themselves quote or summarize the listed source pages (Tabelog, official site teuchisoba-towada.com, Hotpepper, Rakuten Gurunavi, and independent blogs), not from directly fetched page content. Founding-year accounts for the Towada lineage vary between sources (Meiji 17/1884 roots; Taisho 13/1924 as a confectionery; Showa 44/1969 as a soba shop) and could not be reconciled. No source found explicitly discusses gluten-free or vegan accommodation, so those assessments are inferred from the shop's traditional bonito-based dashi and standard soba/tempura menu rather than from an explicit allergy statement — call ahead to confirm for celiac or strict vegan needs. Exact total seat count and card-payment policy were not confirmed in any source.

Not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
name_ja: 十和田 メトロ通り店（手打ちそば十和田）
neighborhood: Asakusa

positives.text: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) is made fresh in-house daily using only real bonito flakes, with no chemical seasonings or additives — a point of pride for flavor quality, though it is fish-based, not vegan.

seats_text: Two floors of traditional sunken-kotatsu (horigotatsu) style seating; the second floor accommodates up to 50 guests and includes an 8-person private window-side room, with additional counter and table seating on the ground floor (exact total seat count not confirmed).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or substitutions are mentioned in any source found. The shop's signature tsuyu (dipping sauce) is explicitly made with real bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, not a plant-based stock, so the standard soba here is not vegan or vegetarian as served.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-23-3

bio.origin: Opened in Musashi-Kosugi (Kawasaki) in December 2018; relocated to Asakusa's 'Kannonura' backstreet (behind Sensoji) in March 2024 after five years at the original location.
bio.background: The owner, Mr. Sato, tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) while in Taipei and was so struck by its flavor that he spent years testing his own recipe at home before opening a dedicated porridge shop.
bio.specialty: Taiwanese-style rice porridge (congee) simmered in a self-made 'natural dashi' built from kombu, dried shiitake mushroom, niboshi (dried sardines), chicken, katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and dried shrimp, served as set menus with small steamed side dishes.
bio.philosophy: A belief that a MSG-free, painstakingly made natural dashi is the soul of a good bowl of congee; the menu centers on two evolving porridge sets rather than an extensive menu.

anecdotes.text: Owner Sato first tasted a mushroom-and-pork congee (三娘香菇肉粥) in Taipei and, unable to forget the flavor, repeatedly tested his own recipe before opening a porridge specialty shop.
The shop opened in Musashi-Kosugi in December 2018 and relocated to Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet in March 2024 after five years at the original site.

caveats: Geocoding could not be verified: the GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) was blocked by this environment's network egress policy on every attempt, so lat/lng are null rather than derived — the address itself (Asakusa 3-chome, 'Kannonura' behind Sensoji, ~7 min walk from Asakusa Station) is well documented across multiple independent sources and is consistent with the target Sensoji radius. Closed-day sources conflict slightly: most (including the shop's own Instagram bio) say closed Sunday/Monday/Tuesday, while a couple of secondary listings say only Monday/Tuesday — this record uses the Sun/Mon/Tue version as the more recent and directly-sourced one. This is a small 8-seat open counter kitchen where wheat-containing items are prepared alongside everything else, so cross-contact should be assumed. Steamed side dishes (dumplings/dim sum) also appear to use conventional, non-vegan fillings. There is no indication this restaurant can accommodate a vegan diner.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門2-11-9

roles: Founded in 1913 (Taishō 2) as a branch of the Kanda Yabu Soba lineage, opened by a member of that founding family
bio.background: Namiki Yabu Soba opened in 1913 on Namiki-dōri, a short walk from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate, as a branch of Kanda Yabu Soba — making it one of the three storied "Yabu" soba houses of old Tokyo (the others being the original Kanda Yabu Soba and the now-closed Ikenohata Yabu Soba). Regulars have long called it "the Yabu of Kaminarimon." It has stayed on the same stretch of Namiki-dōri through more than a century of Asakusa's ups and downs, and today still draws lunchtime queues of both neighborhood regulars and visitors.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — direct branch of the original Kanda Yabu Soba
bio.specialty: Hand-cut juwari (十割) soba, made from 100% buckwheat milled fresh each day, served with an unusually dark, concentrated dipping sauce; the seasonal kamo nanban (duck soba), available roughly November–March, is a signature cold-weather order.
bio.philosophy: The founder built the shop's tsuyu to be sipped in tiny amounts — diners are meant to dip only the tip of the noodles rather than submerge them — so the soba's own aroma, not the sauce, stays in the foreground. The kaeshi (soy sauce base) is aged for about a week in buried earthen pots, then combined with a dashi of deeply cured, mold-ripened bonito simmered each morning for about 90 minutes, and rested a further two days before use.

anecdotes.text: The novelist Shōtarō Ikenami was a devoted regular who favored the kamo nanban when it appeared in early winter, writing that settling into the tatami seating on a quiet weekday afternoon once the lunch rush had cleared, and drinking sake slowly, was one of life's great small pleasures.

The essayist Hitomi Yamaguchi would order "kamo-nuki" (the duck soba's toppings without the noodles) in winter and "ten-nuki" (tempura without noodles) the rest of the year, treating them as a sake companion before finishing with a plate of soba.

The shop is widely regarded as the model for the tsuyu-obsessed soba stall in volume 2 of the manga Oishinbo ("The Depth of Soba Broth"), a story built around the painstaking kaeshi-and-dashi process this shop is known for.

caveats: Payment methods (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from any source, so cash_only is left as a conservative default rather than a verified fact. The exact romanization of the founder's given name varies across secondary sources, so no specific personal name is asserted in the bio. Precise geocoordinates could not be independently verified this session (see geocode_note) — placement within the target radius is inferred from consistent, sourced walking-distance descriptions (roughly 150m from Kaminarimon gate / 2-5 min from Asakusa Station), not from a geocoder.
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday
cuisine: Soba (Edo-style), 十割そば (100% buckwheat juwari soba)
cultural_comfort_note: A century-old, tourist-visible soba house just off Kaminarimon where ordering standard dishes is easy even without Japanese, but the traditional lightly-dip tsuyu etiquette and any gluten-free/vegan requests will go more smoothly with a little Japanese or patience with pointing, since no English menu or staff-language info was found.
geocode_note: The GSI address-search API (msearch.gsi.go.jp) and alternate geocoders (Nominatim, Photon, Google Maps) all returned network-policy 403s in this session and could not be reached, so no geocoder-derived coordinates could be obtained; lat/lng are left null rather than estimated by hand. Multiple independent sources consistently place the address (台東区雷門2-11-9) on Namiki-dōri about 150m from Sensō-ji's Kaminarimon gate and 2-5 minutes' walk from Asakusa Station, which is well within the Sensō-ji target radius, but this record does not contain a verified coordinate pair.
gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are juwari (十割) — hand-cut from 100% buckwheat flour milled fresh each day, with no wheat used as a binder in the dough. However, the tsuyu dipping sauce is built from a traditional kaeshi (soy sauce, mirin, sugar) using standard Japanese shoyu, which is wheat-based, and this sauce is what the noodles are dipped in for every order. The menu also includes tempura dishes (tenzaru, ten-nuki) made with wheat-flour batter, prepared in the same kitchen. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol was found in any source. One visitor guide explicitly flags this: standard Japanese soy sauce is not wheat-free, and celiac travelers are advised to confirm ingredients with staff directly rather than assume the 100%-buckwheat noodles make the full meal safe.
hours_raw: 11:00-19:30 (closed Wednesdays and Thursdays)
neighborhood: Asakusa (Kaminarimon / Namiki-dōri), Taitō

gf_cross_contamination.text: A visitor guide notes that although the noodles are 100% buckwheat, "soy sauce in Japan these days is not entirely wheat-free," and recommends confirming the exact ingredients with the restaurant before dining if celiac disease is a concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tsuyu is built from a kaeshi made with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, aged in buried earthen pots for about a week before being combined with the dashi — this is standard shoyu, not a wheat-free formulation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi used for both the cold dipping sauce and hot noodle broths is made from hon-karebushi, a deeply cured, mold-ripened bonito, simmered fresh each morning for about 90 minutes — meaning the standard tsuyu used across the menu is fish-based.

positives.text: The soba itself is juwari (十割) — cut from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder, and the buckwheat is milled fresh each day rather than pre-ground and stored.

seats_text: Roughly three dozen seats across table and tatami-alcove seating in the pre-pandemic layout; expect a queue at lunch.
vegan_detail: The tsuyu (dipping sauce) used for cold soba, and the broths used for hot noodle dishes, are built on a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi combined with a soy-sauce-based kaeshi; this fish stock underlies essentially every standard order on the menu. No vegetarian- or vegan-adapted broth, or plant-based dashi alternative, was mentioned in any source found, and the seasonal specialty (kamo nanban) is duck-based. This is a traditional Edo-style soba shop built around its bonito-based tsuyu, not a place with vegan accommodation.

cuisine: 焼きそば・らーめん (yakisoba / ramen)
gf_detail: The entire menu is built around wheat yakisoba noodles pan-fried with a Worcestershire-style sauce (also offered in curry-sauce and beef-tendon versions). No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is mentioned in any source found. The sauce itself is wheat-based/contains soy sauce, and toppings like tenkasu (fried tempura scraps) add further wheat/gluten exposure. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is mentioned anywhere. The listed dishes include beef-tendon (gyūsuji) yakisoba, gyoza, and gyūsuji nikomi (beef tendon stew), and optional raw/fried egg toppings; the yakisoba sauce is a typical Japanese Worcestershire-style sauce that commonly contains animal-derived extracts. No dashi/katsuobushi disclosure was found, but nothing suggests a vegan-aware kitchen.

bio.origin: Opened around 1964-1965 (Shōwa 39-40) inside Asakusa Chika-gai, Japan's oldest surviving underground shopping street, right by the Tokyo Metro Ginza Line Asakusa Station exit.
bio.background: A tiny, no-frills yakisoba counter that has fed neighborhood regulars and passersby for six decades from the same spot in the retro underground arcade near Kaminarimon and Sensōji.
bio.specialty: Pan-fried sauce yakisoba, offered in three versions (classic Worcestershire-style sauce, curry sauce, and beef-tendon/gyūsuji), topped simply with cabbage and crispy tenkasu, with optional raw or fried egg.

anecdotes.text: The shop appears four times in Wim Wenders' 2023 film PERFECT DAYS as the favorite izakaya of protagonist Hirayama, who cycles there over Sakura-bashi bridge after his public-bath visits; the director reportedly spent over an hour scouting the interior before filming.
Sits inside Japan's oldest surviving underground shopping street (opened 1955), and is noted for a distinctly strong-flavored curry-sauce yakisoba among its three sauce variations.

lead: お米とごはん 隅田屋 浅草店
address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸（浅草駅より徒歩6〜8分）※詳細な番地は確認できず

roles: 1905年（明治38年）創業の老舗米穀店「隅田屋商店」が初めて手がけた飲食業態（アンテナショップ）
bio.background: 隅田屋商店は1905年、初代・松五郎が浅草の米屋で修業したのちのれん分けを受け、隅田川ほとりの地に開いた米穀店。関東大震災や戦後の米の大凶作を経て信用を築き、2代目・三郎の代には太平洋戦争と配給統制という食糧難の時代を経験した。現在は5代目にあたる片山真一氏が代表を務める。2026年3月17日、創業120年を機に浅草に直営のイートインスペースを備えたアンテナショップ「お米とごはん 隅田屋 浅草店」をオープンし、米穀店として初めて飲食業に進出した。
bio.specialty: 看板メニューの「おむすび御膳」（2,000円・税込）。お櫃で炊きたてのご飯を用意し、オリジナルの型を使って客自身がおむすびを握り、7種類の具から自由に組み合わせて楽しむ体験型の一膳。
bio.philosophy: 「お米を嗜好品として楽しんでもらいたい」という思いのもと、商標登録された独自の「古式精米製法」（一般的な一回精米に対し、弱い圧力で7〜10回に分けてじっくり精米する手法）で用途に応じて米の旨みを最大限に引き出すことにこだわる。
bio.origin: 東京都墨田区東駒形（隅田川ほとり）で1905年創業

anecdotes.text: 太平洋戦争が勃発し米の配給制度が敷かれた戦中戦後の食糧難の時代、2代目・三郎は「お客様にお腹いっぱい食べさせてあげたい」という思いから米の確保に奔走したと伝えられている。
初代・松五郎は浅草の米屋で育ち、のれん分けとして隅田川のほとりに「隅田屋商店」を開いたのが創業の始まり。
隅田屋最大の特長は商標登録された「古式精米製法」で、一般的な精米が一度で仕上げるのに対し、隅田屋では7〜10回に分け弱い圧力でじっくりと精米する。
2026年3月17日、創業120年の老舗米穀店「隅田屋」が浅草にアンテナショップをオープン。イートインスペースを備え、看板の「おむすび御膳」（2,000円）は客自身がオリジナルの型でご飯をおむすびに握り、7種の具を組み合わせる体験型メニュー。

caveats: Exact street-level address (chōme/banchi) could not be independently confirmed — only that the shop is in Hanakawado, Taito-ku, a 6-8 minute walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line/Tobu Skytree Line/Toei Asakusa Line), per Tabelog. The GSI geocoding service and several source pages (Tabelog, official sumidaya.jp/shop.sumidaya.jp, Google Maps) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, so coordinates are null rather than invented; proximity to the target area (Sensōji/Asakusa) is inferred from the confirmed walking-distance-from-Asakusa-Station description. Reservation and payment-method fields are best-effort defaults for a small 2026-opened casual eat-in counter, not independently confirmed.

address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-6-5

bio.chef_name: 光也 (Mitsuya) — known to regulars as "光家姐さん" (the Mitsuya-neesan)
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (born and raised locally)
bio.background: The proprietress, a former geisha born and raised in Asakusa, opened this standing kushiage counter in December 2001 a short walk from Asakusa Station.
bio.specialty: Panko-breaded, deep-fried kushiage skewers (pork fillet katsu, ham katsu, cheese, quail egg, squid, shrimp, chikuwa fish cake) served Osaka-style with a shared Worcestershire-type dipping sauce and free cabbage.
bio.philosophy: She set out to build a casual counter where a woman could feel comfortable walking in and drinking alone — and kept the same simple panko and oil for years so the light, crisp coating never changes.

anecdotes.text: Regulars are said to range in age from 4 to 94, with an unusually high share of women dining solo.
Like most kushiage counters, the house rule is no double-dipping in the communal sauce; a blogger who visited for skewers and lemon sours affectionately calls her "光家姐さん."

caveats: Payment methods (card vs. cash-only) could not be confirmed from any source — verify on site. Seat count is not published; this is a standing (tachinomi) counter with paid chair rental (¥100), not table seating. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists anywhere for this shop; the GF/vegan assessment below is inferred from the confirmed menu and cooking style (panko-breaded deep-frying, shared Worcestershire-style dipping sauce), not from any explicit allergy statement by the restaurant. GSI and other geocoding services were unreachable from this environment (network policy blocked all attempts), so coordinates are null — see geocode_note.

bio.background: According to Asakusa-area food/tourism write-ups, the business behind Senso-ji has passed through three generations and three identities: the first generation ran a Chinese restaurant called Fukushotei (福昇亭), the second generation turned the space into a restaurant called Skyline (a former name still listed on Tabelog's page for this address), and the current generation reopened it as Bistro Fukushotei, a yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) restaurant, tracing back to the early Showa era.
bio.specialty: Pork cutlet (tonkatsu) with demi-glace sauce

anecdotes.text: Although the shop now operates as a yoshoku bistro, a Rocket News 24 feature reports that longtime regulars are known to skip the Western menu and order the restaurant's under-the-radar ramen instead.

The current dining room has held three different restaurant identities across three generations of the same family -- a Chinese restaurant, then 'Skyline', then today's Bistro Fukushotei -- reflecting decades of adaptation in the same spot tucked behind Senso-ji.

lead: スケマサ コーヒー
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2丁目29-2 君塚ビル102

bio.background: The owner originally trained as a pastry chef, working her way through a confectionery course at cooking school and then joining a well-regarded local Western-style cake shop. The physically demanding conditions of pastry work — long hours and constant work in cold-temperature kitchens/refrigerated rooms — took a toll on her health, and she eventually had to leave the pastry industry.
bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit sandwiches (using fruit such as strawberries, shine muscat, Nagano purple grapes, white peach and mango depending on the season) paired with espresso and lattes; strawberry versions are paired with red azuki bean paste (ogura-an) and mikan versions with white bean paste (shiro-an) alongside fresh cream.
bio.philosophy: After the 2011 Great East Japan Earthquake, while recovering from the health problems that ended her pastry career, she said she realized life can end at any moment, and decided she wanted to make a living doing what she loved — coffee and kimono — and live joyfully. That realization led her to open a coffee shop built around her two passions.

anecdotes.text: 木村さんは小さい頃からケーキ屋さんになると決めていて、実際にパティシエとなり働いていたが、室温の低い職場環境に身体を壊してしまいやむを得ず退職しました。開店のきっかけは東日本大震災で、『人生っていつ終わってしまうかわからないなと気づいて、それならば好きなことで生計を立てて、楽しく生きていきたいと思って自分の好きなコーヒーと着物のお店を開くのが目標になりました』と述べています。
着物姿のバリスタがラテアートを作るのが浅草らしい下町ならではの特徴で、2018年8月24日のオープン以来、常に大盛況の人気カフェとなっている。2022年には2号店『ペチカ スケマサコーヒー』も浅草にオープンした。

lead: 来集軒（らいしゅうけん）
address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草2-26-3

bio.background: 来集軒 traces its roots to a noodle-making workshop (製麺所) founded in the Meiji era (around 1910) — the same year as Rairaitei, generally considered Japan's first ramen restaurant. The ramen shop itself opened in 1950 (Showa 25), and has been run as a family, counter-style shop ever since, attached to its own noodle-making operation.
bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen (soy sauce ramen): a clear, soy-sauce-based broth simmered each morning from pork bone, chicken bone, and vegetables (onion, carrot, cabbage), paired with flat, medium-thin curly noodles made in-house at the shop's own noodle factory.
bio.philosophy: The shop prides itself on keeping the same gentle, nostalgic flavor essentially unchanged for over 50 years — a straightforward, old-fashioned Tokyo shoyu ramen rather than a trend-chasing bowl.

anecdotes.text: Described by the walking-magazine さんたつ (Sanpo no Tatsujin) as 'gentle and deeply flavorful — this is truly Japanese ramen,' highlighting the shop's Showa-era atmosphere, walls decorated with celebrity autographs, and its role as a beloved neighborhood gathering spot.
A ramen blogger visiting the shop noted the soup's clean, soy-sauce aroma and the noodles' smooth, wheat-forward texture, calling it classic old-style Tokyo ramen.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-32-13 松村ビル1F

bio.background: Kanmi Mitsuya is a small traditional Japanese sweets (kanmi-dokoro) shop in Asakusa that opened in August 2018, specializing in hand-kneaded shiratama dango (rice-flour dumplings) served warm and freshly boiled throughout the day. It became popular enough to draw regular queues, and in 2022 the owners opened a second, takeout-only branch on Sensoji's Nakamise-dori selling a boxed dango set called 'Shiratama Tebako'.
bio.specialty: Freshly hand-kneaded, just-boiled shiratama dango (soft rice-flour dumplings), served with the shop's own kuromitsu (brown sugar syrup) and shiromitsu (white syrup); also anmitsu, kanten (agar jelly), kuzukiri, and seasonal shaved ice (kakigori) in summer.
bio.philosophy: The shop is built around two house-made staples: the dango and the syrups. The dumpling dough uses stone-ground shiratamako made from Saga-grown Hiyoku rice and water from springs originating on Mt. Aso; staff hand-knead it fresh each morning, adjusting the water ratio 'by feel' to account for daily shifts in temperature and humidity, then boil it to order rather than pre-making it. The kuromitsu is made by tasting and comparing brown sugars from different islands of Kagoshima and Okinawa before settling on Hateruma-jima brown sugar blended with wasanbon for a lighter syrup, while the shiromitsu uses Kikaijima kibi sugar; both are slow-simmered in copper pots.
roles: Kanmi-dokoro (traditional Japanese sweets shop) specializing in shiratama dango

anecdotes.text: The shop draws long queues in Asakusa for its freshly boiled, mochi-mochi shiratama dango, and has been featured on Japanese TV programs such as Hiru Nandesu.
In 2022 the operators opened a takeout-only second location on Sensoji's Nakamise-dori shopping street, introducing a boxed dango set ('Shiratama Tebako') as a new way to enjoy the dumplings.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-8-5

bio.origin: The restaurant's name blends 房 (a character honoring a mentor the chef trained under and remains grateful to) with 寿 (an auspicious character associated with sushi/celebration) — chosen so that debt of gratitude would never be forgotten.
bio.background: The head chef trained at the former Hotel New Otani Akasaka before opening this Edomae sushi counter on Asakusa's "Shokutsu-gai" (food-lovers' street), one block north of Kaminarimon-dori.
bio.specialty: Kohada (gizzard shad) prepared with meticulous seasonal timing; hamaguri (clam) nigiri finished with a house sauce made from clam broth instead of the usual eel-based nitsume; and uni nigiri served deliberately without a nori wrap to let the sea urchin's flavor stand on its own.
bio.philosophy: Ingredients are sourced fresh daily (historically from Tsukiji), timed to the season, with fish finished to the second for optimal texture — uni from Hokkaido, kohada from Kyushu, eel prepared in Edomae style.

anecdotes.text: The shop name combines a character from a mentor the chef trained under with 寿, intended as a lasting expression of gratitude.
The counter is a single board set at the angle that shows the sushi at its most beautiful, and the room mixes a 9-seat counter with table and sunken-kotatsu seating for families and older guests.
Lunch offers a very affordable "nami-chirashi" for around 500 yen alongside a premium 3000-yen chirashi, while dinner runs later as a full Edomae sushi counter experience.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-15-9

bio.background: A family-run tonkatsu restaurant tucked into a quiet backstreet between Kaminarimon-dori and Tanuki-dori in Asakusa. Founded in 1945 in the postwar years, it is now run by the third generation of the same family and has kept the same style of cooking for more than 80 years.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (founded 1945)
bio.specialty: Rosu-katsu (pork loin cutlet) set meal — a cutlet prized for its thin, crackling crust and pink, juicy meat that diners describe as unusually light and easy on the stomach for fried pork.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim is a cutlet that feels crisp and satisfying without sitting heavy afterward: loin from Gunma-raised Yamato pork, panko that the shop bakes and grinds in-house from domestic-wheat bread for a sweeter, crunchier crust, and frying in cottonseed oil rather than lard so the dish is lighter on the stomach than a typical tonkatsu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has carried forward its postwar traditional flavors since opening in 1945 and was selected for the Tabelog Tonkatsu "Tabelog 100" list in 2026.
The shop bakes its own bread and grinds it into panko using domestic wheat, giving the coating a sweeter flavor and crisper texture than store-bought breadcrumbs.

The restaurant fries in cottonseed oil instead of lard, aiming for a lighter, less greasy result that doesn't cause heartburn or a heavy stomach; the oil is kept so clear it looks almost like water, and breading crumbs are carefully skimmed out as the cutlets fry.

The interior retains structural elements from the old shophouse — a large central pillar and a woven-bamboo ceiling — giving the dining room the settled, dignified feel of a long-established restaurant.

caveats: This is a traditional pork tonkatsu restaurant with no vegan or gluten-free menu accommodations — its breading is house-made wheat panko and the entire menu is meat-based. It is included for its historic, independently-run character rather than any dietary specialization. Precise GPS coordinates could not be confirmed: the GSI address-geocoding service was unreachable in this session (network policy blocked the request), so lat/lng are left null. Multiple independent sources agree the shop is a 5-minute walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line/Toei Asakusa Line/Tobu) and sits just off Kaminarimon-dori near Sensoji, which is well within the target radius even without a geocoded point.

bio.background: A small husband-and-wife-run yakitori counter that opened in March 2021 in Nishi-Asakusa, a short walk from the Asakusa View Hotel. The shop occupies a renovated space that was formerly a tendon (rice-bowl) restaurant; the owners kept a sense of the building's history while giving it a clean, updated feel. It is run by just two people, the owner and a woman working the counter alongside him.
bio.specialty: Skewers grilled over charcoal, including a raw-wasabi semi-seared chicken breast (namawasabi no hannama sasami) and skewers such as harami (skirt) and shiitake mushroom that reviewers single out; a set course (salad, five yakitori skewers, rice bowl, and dessert) is offered for about 3,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened in March 2021 in a renovated building that used to house a tendon shop, with the owners keeping traces of the older building's character while making it feel fresh.
Diners highlight the harami and shiitake skewers as standouts, and the 3,000-yen course (salad, five grilled skewers, rice bowl, dessert) as good value.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門２丁目７－６ 豊田ビル１F（〒111-0034）

bio.origin: Shandong-style Chinese hand-pulled noodle (拉麺) tradition, brought to Asakusa in 1977
bio.specialty: Hand-pulled noodles (手打ち麺) — stretched and slapped by hand in the shop's street-facing window and used across the menu: shoyu ramen, tantanmen, tsukemen, and hand-made gyoza
bio.philosophy: Noodles are pulled to order only after a customer orders, so the kitchen never holds pre-made noodles; the shop treats the hand-pulling itself as a craft, reportedly taking a noodle-puller at least two years to master.
bio.background: Opened in Asakusa in 1977 (Showa 52), a short walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate, as a Chinese-style hand-pulled noodle specialist. It later added a second location in Nippori, but the Asakusa shop remains the original.

anecdotes.text: The percussive slap of the noodle-maker's hands hitting the dough echoes out through a window facing the street, so passersby on the way to Sensoji can watch the noodles being hand-pulled before they're even cooked.

One diner wrote about having craved the shop's hand-pulled noodles for 15 years before finally trying the tsukemen, topped with scallions, bean sprouts, chashu, nori and menma, describing a distinctive chewy, almost 'sliding' texture unlike ordinary ramen noodles.

roles: noodle-pulling craftspeople (職人) who train for at least two years to master the technique

caveats: This shop's entire identity is built on hand-pulled wheat noodles, so it is not a fit for celiac or strict gluten-free diners, and no vegan option was found (the broth is pork-bone-and-chicken based). It is included for its genuine local-institution character, not as a GF/vegan-safe pick — treat it as sightseeing/flavor-only unless you can eat wheat and meat broth freely. Exact payment methods (cash vs. card/e-money) and whether reservations are accepted could not be confirmed from available sources; call the shop (03-3841-6002) to check before visiting.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-21-3 エリカビル 2F

bio.chef_name: 板長マウンさん (Maung) and 板前ニイさん (Ni)
bio.origin: Myanmar
bio.background: Head chef Maung came to Japan in 1996, during a period when Myanmar's military government was cracking down on the democracy movement and the economy was struggling, and spent 24 years training as a sushi craftsman in Japan. Fellow chef Ni has close to 20 years of sushi experience in Japan. In June 2019 (the first year of the Reiwa era), a group of Myanmar-born residents living in Japan pooled their own money to set up a company together and open this sushi restaurant in Asakusa, naming it after the new era.
bio.philosophy: To show that decades of hard-won craft — not nationality — is what makes a sushi chef, after facing skepticism from some early customers about non-Japanese chefs behind the counter.
bio.specialty: Traditional Edomae-style sushi alongside original creations, including a spicy roll and the restaurant's signature 'Asakusa roll' (浅草巻き).

anecdotes.text: A magazine feature's headline recounts a customer's blunt first reaction on seeing the chefs — 'Why is a foreigner making this?!' — framed as the starting point of the chefs' effort to win diners over through skill.

The restaurant opened in June 2019 after Myanmar-born residents in Japan pooled their own funds to establish a company together, and the shop later ran a crowdfunding campaign on READYFOR to support its operations.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-20-7

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902) as a Japanese restaurant specializing in fugu (pufferfish) cuisine in Asakusa. The restaurant has operated continuously for over 120 years, weathering the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the aftermath of WWII in 1945. It is now run by a third-generation owner who was born and raised in Asakusa. The building was renovated in September 2016, with horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) seating added to both the first and second floors.
bio.specialty: Fugu kaiseki course centered on tessa (fugu sashimi), techiri (fugu hot pot), nikogori (jellied fugu broth), karaage (fried fugu), hire-zake (fin sake), and shirako (fugu milt) — the grilled shirako, in season from around November, is especially prized by regulars.

anecdotes.text: Founded in Meiji 35 (1902), the restaurant has continued as a Japanese restaurant ever since, passing through three generations of ownership and surviving the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and the end of WWII in 1945.
The restaurant sits about a 1-minute walk from Kaminarimon Gate and roughly 3 minutes from Asakusa Station, tucked in the backstreets near Sensoji.
It was renovated in September 2016 (Heisei 28), with seating on both the first and second floors converted to horigotatsu sunken-kotatsu style.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-42-12 浅草ダイカンプラザ天仁ビル1F

bio.background: Chef Oku opened this nine-seat counter in Asakusa in April 2021. He trained previously at the respected Edomae sushi restaurant Sushi Hashiguchi (鮨 橋口), and the shop is often described by reviewers as carrying on the lineage/style of the well-regarded 'Takase' (高勢) school of Edomae sushi.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi omakase, with particular attention to knife work, cutting thickness, and rice/vinegar balance
bio.philosophy: Adherence to classic Edomae sushi fundamentals, with the chef's individual sensibility expressed through small technical details rather than novelty.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a calm, personable nine-seat counter where the chef looks after regulars closely, giving the room a warm, low-key atmosphere rather than a formal one.
A sushi-focused blog profiled Oku as a lesser-known but highly skilled craftsman ('知る人ぞ知る腕利き職人') carrying on the Takase lineage in Asakusa.

caveats: Closing times were not published on any source found (only opening/seating times), so the listed end-of-service times are a reasonable estimate for a single-seating omakase, not a confirmed figure. bio.origin: Grew up in the ramen scene around Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi branch, a shop in the Ramen Jiro lineage
bio.background: Owner Kimura trained at Ramen Fujimaru's Nishiarai Daishi location, a shop descended from the Ramen Jiro style, before opening the original 自家製麺NO11 in Itabashi (Ooyama/Shimoitabashi). In April 2025 he opened this second location on Asakusa's Hisago-dori shopping street, taking over a space that had previously housed 麺屋まるいち, a shop No11 had also produced.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style pork ramen built on housemade extra-thick noodles and an emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth, topped with tender braised pork, soft-boiled 'kuta' vegetables, garlic and back fat.

anecdotes.text: Food blogger Miku Okazaki covered the Asakusa opening and interviewed owner Kimura about his approach to the noodles and broth for the shop's 'makanai interview' feature, noting a congratulatory flower display in the shop window marking the opening.
Tokyo ramen blog Ramental described the shop as drawing a constant queue and praised the sharp, clean cut of the emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth.

caveats: This is a Jiro-style pork ramen shop centered on housemade wheat noodles and a pork-bone soy-sauce broth — it has no gluten-free or vegan orientation and was included in the sweep as a general omnivore ramen spot near Asakusa, not as a dietary-safe recommendation. Exact GPS coordinates could not be verified because geocoding APIs were unreachable in this session; proximity to Sensoji is inferred from published walking directions, not measured.

cuisine: Ramen (Jiro-style / 二郎系), housemade noodles
cultural_comfort_note: Orders go through a cash-only ticket vending machine, and Jiro-style etiquette means calling out topping preferences (vegetables, garlic, extra fat) when your bowl is called — a little Japanese vocabulary makes the ritual far less intimidating for first-time visitors.

bio.background: 竹三郎(Takesaburo)は東武線・浅草駅から徒歩2〜3分、馬道通り沿いにある濃厚豚骨（家系）ラーメン店で、2013年12月23日に開業した。浅草寺にほど近い立地で、地元ブログでは「浅草寺にもっとも近い家系」として紹介されている。
bio.origin: Opened December 23, 2013 in Hanakawado, Asakusa, along Umamichi-dori, a short walk from Sensoji.
bio.specialty: 満州トロトロ担々汁あり油そば（ゴマだれ入りの担々風あぶらそば）と、同じ具でコチジャンを効かせた辛口の「汁あり油そば 燃えよドラゴン」。鉄鍋の余熱でスープが煮詰まりソース状になって極太麺に絡むのが特徴。濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメンも看板メニュー。

roles: Eel (unagi) specialty izakaya, one of several branches of the いづも eel-skewer concept
bio.background: いづも is a small unagi (eel) specialty izakaya concept that began at a honten in Osaka's Fukushima district before expanding to a branch inside Osaka Station's LUCUA shopping complex and to this Asakusa Yokocho branch in Tokyo, a festival-themed food hall on the 4th floor of the Tokyo Rakutenchi Asakusa Building.
bio.origin: Osaka (Fukushima)
bio.specialty: Unagi tamadon and unagi gyudon (eel rice bowls with egg or beef), unagi kamahoyaki (semi-grilled eel fillet), and eel kushiyaki skewers, served alongside grilled beef tongue, yakitori, and horse sashimi.
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated aim is to make traditionally pricey, formal unagi more affordable and approachable by serving it izakaya-style in a lively, casual setting.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka honten relocated and relaunched with new menu items in 2024, described as an effort to carry Japanese eel food culture forward while keeping it reasonably priced ('大阪一リーズナブルな鰻屋').
The Asakusa branch sits inside 'Asakusa Yokocho,' a food-and-festival themed dining hall built to let visitors experience Japanese festival culture alongside food from multiple genres.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-4-13

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Tsukushi is a long-running monjayaki (and okonomiyaki) restaurant on Asakusa's Hoppy-dōri (Hoppy Street), tucked behind the Denbō-in temple grounds just west of Sensō-ji. It is roughly 55 years old and is often described as the second-oldest monjayaki shop in the neighborhood, having kept its core recipe essentially unchanged for more than 40 years. It draws a mix of shitamachi (old-downtown) regulars, weekend queues, and a following of visiting celebrities.
bio.specialty: Monjayaki and okonomiyaki — notably the loaded 'Deluxe' monja topped with pork, beef, squid, shrimp, and a raw egg — alongside 40-plus menu items that include simmered beef tendon (gyū-suji nikomi) and various butter-grilled dishes.
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes continuity: staff describe preserving the same taste and recipes they've served for over 40 years rather than chasing novelty, treating monjayaki as a genuine old-Tokyo comfort food.

anecdotes.text: The menu reportedly includes items created with input from celebrity regulars, including dishes nicknamed 'Gackt' and 'Chō-tokkyū (Super Express) monja.'

The restaurant sits on Hoppy-dōri, Asakusa's nostalgic strip of open-air izakaya stalls just behind Sensō-ji's Denbō-in temple grounds, an area long associated with Asakusa's downtown drinking-and-eating culture.

Monjayaki itself began as a favorite childhood snack in downtown Tokyo, especially around Asakusa, where children cooked it on small iron plates at candy stores in the 1950s–60s — the tradition Tsukushi continues today.

This is a traditional wheat-flour-batter monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant with no advertised gluten-free or vegan accommodations — treat GF/vegan info as a caution, not an endorsement.
