lead: 粥餐庁 京王モール店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿一丁目 南口地下街1号（京王モールA区画）〒160-0023

bio.background: Kayu Sanchin (粥餐庁) is a Chinese/Taiwanese-style congee (okayu) and noodle specialty chain. The brand's operations were transferred to Green House Foods Co., Ltd. (a major Tokyo-based food-service company headquartered in Nishi-Shinjuku) in 2008, and it now runs multiple branches, including this one tucked into the underground Keio Mall passage directly connected to Shinjuku Station's south exit / west exit area.
bio.origin: Chinese/Taiwanese congee tradition, now run as a Japanese chain restaurant
bio.philosophy: Positioned as a healthy, comforting meal for busy commuters — porridge made with a white-rice and brown-rice blend, served from early morning (7am on weekdays) through late evening, with abundant vegetable and protein toppings to choose from.
roles: Chain restaurant operated by Green House Foods Co., Ltd.
bio.specialty: Congee (rice porridge) made from a white-and-brown rice blend, with topping options such as Taiwanese-style braised pork (lu rou), plum, steamed chicken, shrimp with cheese and tomato, and a multi-vegetable congee (lotus root, daikon, sweet potato, broccoli, cherry tomato, etc.); the shop's special tantan noodles are also a recommended item.

anecdotes.text: A long-running, popular spot inside Keio Mall where lines form at lunchtime for its rice-blend congee.
Open as early as 7:00am on weekdays, making it one of the few sit-down 'proper meal' options near Shinjuku Station before the morning commute rush.

caveats: This is a general Chinese/Taiwanese-style congee and noodle chain, not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free restaurant. It does offer a multi-vegetable congee item on the menu, which makes it vegetarian-leaning and worth knowing about, but the broth base, soy sauce, and kitchen cross-contact have not been confirmed as vegan- or gluten-free-safe. The shop also serves wheat noodle dishes (e.g., tantan noodles) and various meat/seafood congee toppings. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should ask staff directly about broth ingredients (chicken stock vs. vegetable stock) and soy sauce content before ordering.
closed_days: 無休 (no fixed closed days; open year-round, though may close for special circumstances like New Year or mall maintenance)
cuisine: Chinese/Taiwanese-style congee (okayu) and noodles
cultural_comfort_note: The shop's listing indicates menu/signage support for English, Chinese, and Korean in addition to Japanese, and it's a well-trafficked spot inside a major station mall, so ordering should be manageable for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.
geocode_note: Address places the restaurant inside the underground Keio Mall passage directly connected to Shinjuku Station's south exit/west exit area — effectively at Shinjuku Station itself, well within the 0.8km target radius. No independent geocoding performed (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding.
gf_detail: Plain rice congee is naturally gluten-free by grain, but no dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was found. The restaurant also serves wheat noodle dishes (tantan noodles) in the same kitchen, and Japanese/Chinese-style seasoning here likely relies on standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and possibly wheat-thickened sauces for some toppings. No cross-contamination protocol or celiac-specific information was found in available sources. Ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering if you need strict gluten-free food.

positives.text: Menu includes a dedicated multi-vegetable congee option with toppings such as lotus root, daikon, sweet potato, broccoli, and cherry tomato, giving vegetarian-leaning diners a clear plant-forward choice.

seats_text: Small counter seating (reported as roughly 20 seats across counter and facing-counter seating; exact count unconfirmed from a primary source)
vegan_detail: Menu includes a multi-vegetable congee item, which is a helpful vegetarian-leaning option, but this is not a dedicated vegan restaurant. Broth bases at congee/Chinese-style shops like this commonly use chicken stock even in "vegetable" dishes, and this has not been confirmed one way or the other for this location. Dashi/bonito-based seasonings are also common in Japanese-influenced Chinese cooking. Vegans (and anyone avoiding hidden animal stock or fish-derived seasoning) should ask staff specifically what stock/broth is used before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区東1-24-4

bio.origin: The Maruhashi soba lineage traces to a shop founded in Ueno Hirokoji in Taisho 7 (1918); a second-generation owner opened what became the main store in Gakugeidaigaku in 1956, from which roughly a dozen branches (including this Higashi/Shibuya shop, near Namiki-bashi) spread across Tokyo and Kanagawa over the following decades.
bio.background: This Shibuya branch sits down a back alley off the main road near Shibuya's south/east side, marked by a soba sign and blue noren, and has long been a lunchtime fixture for nearby office workers rather than a destination restaurant.
bio.specialty: Tendon (tempura rice bowl), about ¥900, served with miso soup and pickles; also mori/kake/tempura soba from about ¥450 and udon.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills neighborhood soba shop (machi-soba) valued for consistency, affordability, and the quiet pride of an old-school local counter rather than any modern reinvention.

anecdotes.text: A local food blog titled its writeup 'A shop with the pride of a neighborhood soba restaurant,' describing hearty, reasonably priced soba and tendon served in a modest, workaday setting near Shibuya station.

An article on the wider Maruhashi lineage recounts the shop's roots to a 1918 Ueno founding and traces how the family's soba shops, including this Shibuya branch, spread through the city over roughly a century.

caveats: Business hours are reported inconsistently across sources (one lists Mon-Sat 11:00-16:00 straight through; another, more detailed listing gives a split lunch/dinner schedule on weekdays and lunch-only on Saturday) — call ahead (03-3407-1212) to confirm before visiting. No GF/vegan-specific sourcing was found for this shop; the dietary assessment below is based on it being a conventional (non-buckwheat-only) machi-soba shop with no advertised gluten-free or vegan preparation, not on a direct statement from the restaurant. Independent-vs-chain status is a reasonable inference from historical write-ups about the wider Maruhashi lineage, not a shop-specific confirmation. Tabelog page itself could not be directly fetched (blocked); all figures are corroborated via search-result excerpts and secondary sources (Gnavi, Retty, food blogs) that reference the same Tabelog listing.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cuisine: そば・天丼・うどん（町蕎麦）／ Soba, tendon (tempura rice bowl), and udon — neighborhood soba shop
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood soba counter with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff — expect to order in Japanese from a printed menu or wall signage.
gf_detail: No gluten-free preparation, 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodle, or dedicated fryer is advertised anywhere in the sources found. As a standard machi-soba shop, the soba is very likely a wheat-blended (nihachi-style) noodle, and the tsuyu dipping sauce/broth almost certainly relies on wheat-based shoyu; the tendon is deep-fried tempura, with no mention of a separate gluten-free fryer. Not recommended for celiac diners.
geocode_note: No source gives exact coordinates; address is 東京都渋谷区東1-24-4, described consistently across multiple independent sources as a short walk (accounts range from about 3 to 10 minutes) from Shibuya Station's south/east side, down a back alley off the main road toward the Ebisu direction. This is consistent with being within the Shibuya Scramble target radius, though the shop is set back from the crossing itself. Per instructions, lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
hours_raw: Sources conflict: one listing gives Mon-Sat 11:00-16:00 (closed Sun/holidays); a more detailed listing gives Mon-Fri 11:30-15:00 & 17:00-19:00, Sat 11:30-15:00 (closed Sun/holidays). The record below uses the more detailed split-hours version but this should be confirmed by phone (03-3407-1212).
neighborhood: 渋谷区東（渋谷駅南東側）／ Shibuya, Higashi (south/east side of Shibuya Station)
seats_text: About 16 seats, combining counter and table seating
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or option is advertised. As a traditional soba shop, the dashi/broth used for the tsuyu and soups is very likely katsuobushi (bonito)-based, and the tendon batter/frying is standard tempura preparation — not suitable for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation (in Japanese) from staff.

address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷1-14-3 東野ビルB1F

bio.origin: Suidōbashi, Bunkyō-ku, Tokyo — operating from the same basement location for over 40 years
bio.background: Bodaiju has been serving hearty Western-style (yōshoku) comfort food near Suidōbashi Station for more than four decades. It operates under the same restaurant group as Katsuyoshi (かつ吉), a historic tonkatsu specialist founded in 1962, and shares that group's focus on hand-made fried and grilled classics.
bio.specialty: A5 wagyu hamburg steak (A5和牛ハンバーグ) — fresh A5-grade Japanese wagyu ground in-house, formed into hamburg patties, and slow-simmered in a house-made demi-glace sauce; also known for traditional tonkatsu made with daily-sourced branded domestic pork wrapped in fresh panko and slow-fried in a corn-and-sesame oil blend.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen states a commitment to hand-making everything from fresh ingredients and avoiding additives and preservatives.
roles: Yōshoku restaurant within the Katsuyoshi restaurant group

anecdotes.text: The A5 wagyu hamburger lunch set is limited to roughly 20 servings a day and has built a devoted following among lunchtime regulars.
Beyond the hamburg and tonkatsu, regulars are said to come specifically for the shiso gohan (perilla-leaf rice), a labor-intensive side dish that has become something of a hidden signature.
The dining room is described as wood-toned with brick walls and jazz playing throughout, giving the space an unhurried, old-school yōshoku atmosphere.

caveats: This is a conventional Western-style (yōshoku) hamburg-and-tonkatsu specialist, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused restaurant — no GF or vegan accommodations, menu items, or allergy protocols were found in any source. Included for completeness/omnivore coverage near Suidōbashi only; celiac and vegan diners should treat it as unsafe/unsuitable absent direct confirmation from staff. It is part of the same restaurant group as tonkatsu chain Katsuyoshi rather than a fully independent single-owner shop.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; closed December 31, January 1, and January 2 for New Year holidays
cuisine: 洋食（A5和牛ハンバーグ・とんかつ）
cultural_comfort_note: All sources found are Japanese-language (menu, reservation site, blogs); no English menu or English-speaking service was confirmed, so bring a translation tool for any allergy conversation.
geocode_note: Address (東京都文京区本郷1-14-3) and multiple independent sources place it 1–5 minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station (JR East Exit ~3–5 min; Toei Mita Line Exit A6 ~1 min), well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station target radius. Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitutions, or accommodations were found in any source. The signature tonkatsu is coated in fresh wheat panko breadcrumbs, and the hamburg steak is served in a demi-glace sauce (roux-based sauces in this style of cooking are typically wheat-thickened, though the recipe itself was not confirmed). Fried items share cooking oil across the menu with no dedicated allergen-free fryer identified. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
neighborhood: 本郷／水道橋 (Hongo, Bunkyō-ku, near Suidōbashi Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature tonkatsu is coated in fresh wheat panko and fried in a shared corn-salad-oil and sesame-oil blend used across the fried menu; no gluten-free fryer or breading alternative was identified in any source.

seats_text: Roughly 100 seats total across counter seating, tables, semi-private booths, tatami rooms, and a horigotatsu sunken-table area.
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu items, substitutions, or accommodations were found. The menu is centered on wagyu beef hamburg steak and pork tonkatsu, both meat-based dishes; sauces (demi-glace) are typically meat-stock based in this style of cooking, though not explicitly confirmed. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-3-16 東金ビル1F

bio.chef_name: Sano Mai (referred to online as "Mitaba's president," 佐野 / さのまい)
bio.origin: Opened May 22, 2018 in Kabukicho, near Shinjuku Ward Office
bio.background: The shop is run by a female owner-chef who trained under the well-known ramen brand Menya Buichi (麺屋武一) before striking out on her own; the counter is staffed by a mostly female crew, which multiple diner write-ups call out as unusual for a Kabukicho late-night ramen shop.
bio.specialty: 特製濃厚鶏白湯 (Tokusei Nokou Tori-Paitan) — a rich, collagen-heavy chicken paitan broth served with thin noodles; described by reviewers as creamy and full-bodied but not heavy.
bio.philosophy: Positioned as an easy, unintimidating late-night stop in Kabukicho — open from late morning straight through to 6am — welcoming solo diners (including women) into a nightlife district where that isn't always the norm.

anecdotes.text: A blog reviewer described the shop as run by a female owner making 'a bowl that isn't scary,' piled high with vegetables, standing out from Kabukicho's typical late-night ramen scene.
A ramen-focused blog notes the shop is known for chicken-centered bowls spanning rich tori-paitan, lighter clear broths, and Jiro-inspired styles.
Reviewers on Retty specifically highlight that the owner and staff are women running a chicken-paitan specialist in the heart of Kabukicho.

caveats: Seat count is inconsistently reported across sources (one detailed review says 15 counter seats, another ~20), so seats is left as an estimate range rather than a confirmed number. No source discusses gluten-free or vegan accommodations at all — this appears to be a standard wheat-noodle, chicken-broth ramen shop with no allergen program, so it is not recommended for celiac or vegan diners despite the app's GF/vegan focus. Most primary listing pages (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Facebook, several ramen blogs) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so facts below are drawn from WebSearch result summaries that quote/paraphrase those same pages (cited by URL) rather than from directly fetched page text.
closed_days: 無休 (open daily, except New Year's holidays per some listings)
cuisine: Tori Soba (鶏そば) — chicken paitan/chicken broth ramen specialist
cultural_comfort_note: A small, all-Japanese neighborhood ramen counter in Kabukicho with no evidence of an English menu, so a little Japanese (or pointing at photos/the Tabelog menu) goes a long way.
geocode_note: Address (歌舞伎町1-3-16, Tokin Building 1F) is corroborated across multiple independent JP listings and is a reported 1-minute walk from Shinjuku Ward Office and about 5 minutes from both Shinjuku Station and Seibu-Shinjuku Station — comfortably inside the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should use address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option or allergen protocol was found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the tare/seasoning is not confirmed wheat-free. Not recommended for celiac diners.
neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku
seats_text: Counter seating only; reported as roughly 15-20 seats depending on source (exact count unconfirmed)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian broth option was found. The signature and apparently only broth style offered is a rich chicken paitan (鶏白湯) built from chicken bones/collagen, which is inherently non-vegan; dashi/bonito use in any secondary items is not documented either way.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-34-10 ロワール浅草101

bio.background: Asakusa Chisou Nishizawa (浅草馳走にし座わ) opened on July 17, 2024 in Asakusa. According to information associated with the restaurant's Instagram account, the chef previously worked at establishments in both Asakusa and Ginza before opening this restaurant.
bio.philosophy: A seasonal, chef's-choice (omakase) course built around whatever ingredients are best at the time of the visit, served in a calm, refined room alongside a curated selection of Shizuoka-prefecture sake.
bio.specialty: Clay-pot rice (kamameshi-style donabe gohan) made with seasonal fish or sea bream, served as part of a multi-course omakase that also includes sashimi and charcoal-grilled dishes.

caveats: Full page fetches of Tabelog, the official site, and most aggregator pages were blocked (403) by the network's outbound proxy, so this record is built from search-engine result summaries of those pages rather than direct page reads. Regular closed day, exact total seat count, and the chef's full name/career history could not be confirmed. No page found any gluten-free/vegan/allergy-specific information for this restaurant — it is a standard seafood- and dashi-centric kaiseki omakase, so treat all dietary claims as unconfirmed and call ahead (050-8881-7620) before visiting with celiac or vegan needs.
closed_days: Not confirmed in available sources — no regular closed day (定休日) was found; hours are listed for every day of the week.
cuisine: Japanese kaiseki / chef's omakase (日本料理・おまかせ), seasonal ingredients, signature clay-pot rice
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, reservation-only Japanese omakase restaurant booked mainly through Japanese platforms (Hot Pepper, Autoreserve, Tabelog Japanese site); no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found, so some Japanese ability or a hotel/concierge booking will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, statement, or allergy policy was found on any source. As a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant, the omakase course very likely relies on soy sauce and dashi (both commonly containing wheat/gluten and bonito-derived ingredients), and may include tempura or breaded items. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or wheat-free soy sauce. Celiac diners should call ahead (050-8881-7620) to discuss the course in detail before booking.
geocode_note: Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding is blocked in this environment); pin should be derived downstream from address_ja. The address (Asakusa 3-34-10, Taito City) is in the Asakusa 3-chome area and is described by the target brief as within walking distance of Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, which itself is very close to Sensoji — consistent with falling inside the 0.8km Sensoji target radius, though this was not independently geocoded/measured.

neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito City — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station
seats_text: Counter and table seating, with a back area available for private (貸切) parties of 7–12 people; exact total seat count not confirmed.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation was found. The omakase is built around sashimi, charcoal-grilled fish/meat, and clay-pot rice made with seasonal fish, and likely uses bonito (katsuo) dashi throughout — there is no indication the kitchen offers a plant-based course or can rework the set menu to remove fish/dashi.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町24-6 渋ビルヂングB1F

bio.background: Menya Tori Roji is a small, quietly-run ramen counter tucked in the basement of a building on Shibuya's Center-gai, just a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Tori paitan (creamy, slow-simmered all-chicken broth) ramen, offered in three versions: shoyu, shio, and tantan.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name, Tori Roji (鶏路地), is a pun on the Japanese pronunciation of 'trilogy' (torirojii) — a nod to its concept of three chicken-broth ramen styles built from one base stock.

anecdotes.text: The counter is run by a single, quiet ('taciturn') male operator, giving the shop the low-key, no-frills character typical of Tokyo's small hole-in-the-wall ramen counters.

caveats: This is a standard chicken-broth ramen shop, not a gluten-free or vegan specialist — all three broths (shoyu, shio, tantan) are built on chicken stock and the noodles/shoyu are conventional wheat-based products, so it is not celiac-safe and not vegan. Exact seat count, payment methods (card acceptance), and Sunday-specific hours could not be independently confirmed because primary-source pages (Tabelog, Gnavi, ramen blogs) returned access-blocked (403) responses in this environment; hours/closed-days and address are corroborated across multiple independent search snippets instead of a single direct page fetch.
closed_days: 無休 (open daily, no fixed closed days) per aggregated listings
cuisine: Ramen (chicken-based shoyu, shio, and tantan broths)
cultural_comfort_note: A local walk-in ramen counter run by one operator with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff on site; ordering will likely require some Japanese or menu-pointing, though Tabelog offers an English-language listing for reference.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya-ku, Udagawa-cho 24-6) places the shop in Shibuya Center-gai, roughly 150-250m from Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595,139.7005) — well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: Standard ramen shop using conventional wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) broth base; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free preparation, or allergen accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 (Mon-Thu, Sun); 11:00-翌7:00 / 11:00-7:00 next day (Fri-Sat, late night); 無休 (no closed days)
neighborhood: Shibuya Center-gai
seats_text: Small basement shop with counter and a few table seats (exact seat count not confirmed)
vegan_detail: All broths (shoyu, shio, tantan) are built on tori paitan — a slow-simmered all-chicken stock — so there is no vegetable or plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 自家焙煎カフェ豆香房
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-2-13

bio.background: Mamekobo (豆香房) is a self-roasting specialty coffee company with a small group of shops in the Kanda/Suidobashi area of Chiyoda-ku. The Suidobashi branch is a small, traditional coffee-shop-style café a short walk from the station, built around beans the company roasts itself.
bio.specialty: In-house roasted specialty coffee — the drip coffee on offer rotates through different single-origin and micro-lot beans up to three times a day (morning, midday, afternoon).
bio.philosophy: The roastery uses small-scale roasting machines rather than large industrial ones so each batch can be controlled delicately to bring out the individual character of the beans, and beans are sold/served only within about five days of roasting. The shop is a member of the Japan Specialty Coffee Association.

anecdotes.text: The company roasts specialty micro-lot and rare coffee lots in-house on small-batch roasters for delicate flavor control, and is a member of the Japan Specialty Coffee Association.
The coffee selection changes up to three times a day (morning, midday, and afternoon), and all beans sold are roasted within the previous five days.

caveats: This is a specialty coffee café, not a full-service restaurant; food offerings are limited (handmade sandwiches, light bites). No source found with explicit gluten-free or vegan menu labeling, milk-alternative options, or allergen handling procedures — dietary fields reflect that gap honestly rather than assuming accommodation. Several standard reference pages (Tabelog, Retty, official mamekobo.com pages, Gnavi, Navitime) returned 403/blocked on direct fetch, so details were confirmed via search-result excerpts of those same pages rather than full page fetches.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: Self-roasted specialty coffee café
cultural_comfort_note: Small, locals-oriented specialty coffee shop near Suidobashi Station with no confirmed English menu or signage; basic Japanese or a translation app will help when ordering.
geocode_note: No geocoding performed (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null. Address 東京都千代田区三崎町2-2-13 (Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku) is a short walk from JR/Toei Suidobashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: Straight black/drip coffee is inherently gluten-free, but no gluten-free labeling, dedicated equipment, or specific allergen information was found for the food items sold here (e.g., handmade sandwiches, baked goods). Ask staff about ingredients before ordering any food item.
neighborhood: Suidobashi (水道橋), Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Small café seating: two 2-person tables, one 3-person table, and about 4-5 counter seats.
vegan_detail: Plain black/drip coffee is vegan. No confirmation was found of plant-based milk alternatives for lattes/cappuccinos, or of the ingredients (egg, mayonnaise, meat, dashi, etc.) in the handmade sandwiches sold here. Ask staff about milk alternatives and sandwich fillings before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-7 さんらくビル2F

bio.origin: Founded 2007 (originally opened 2004 in Ikebukuro under the name どう楽, produced by ramen critic Hideyuki Ishigami before rebranding and relocating to the current Shinjuku spot on October 25, 2007)
bio.background: A long-running specialty salt-ramen shop just steps from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, currently run by owner Endo Etsuko. When it opened, there were no other ramen shops in the immediate area, which at the time was known more as an adult nightlife district.
bio.specialty: あら炊き塩らぁめん (grilled fish-bone salt ramen) — broth made by first flame-searing the bones and heads of about seven kinds of fresh fish (varies daily, e.g. sea bream, yellowtail) and then simmering them slowly, carefully skimming impurities for a clean, concentrated seafood flavor without any fishy edge.
bio.philosophy: Because the fish delivered each morning changes, the soup's exact flavor shifts day to day — guests are invited to enjoy those daily variations rather than a fixed recipe. The noodles are a custom blend of two wheat flours, thin but firm enough to stand up to the broth.

anecdotes.text: 当時、近辺には他にラーメン店は無く、歓楽街も近い『大人の街』だった (At the time it opened, there were no other ramen shops nearby, and the area was known as an 'adult district' close to the nightlife strip).
入荷する魚は毎日異なるので、日ごとに変わるスープの味を楽しめる (Because the fish delivered varies daily, guests can enjoy how the soup's flavor changes from day to day).

caveats: This is a fish-bone-broth salt ramen specialty shop with no gluten-free or vegan focus; noodles contain wheat and the broth/toppings are fish- and meat-based, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular/occasional closures, no fixed weekly closed day found)
cuisine: あら炊き塩ラーメン (grilled fish-bone salt ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: A local specialty ramen shop with Japanese-only signage and menu conventions typical of Tokyo counter ramen shops; no English menu was confirmed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirmed as 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-7, JR新宿駅東南口徒歩30秒 (30 seconds on foot from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit), well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations found. The custom noodles are made from a blend of two wheat flours (小麦粉/domestic wheat flour, starch, salt, egg white/kansui per ingredient listings), and toppings such as shrimp tsumire and chicken tsukune also contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated fryer was found; celiac diners should not eat here.
neighborhood: Shinjuku Station Southeast Exit (新宿駅東南口)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or process was found; noodles and multiple toppings contain wheat, so cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable in this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-bone based (grilled fish scraps) and toppings include shrimp and chicken, so there is no vegan-safe base at this shop.

seats_text: Not confirmed; small counter-style ramen shop typical of the genre.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature and essentially only broth is made from grilled fish bones/heads (about seven kinds of fresh fish, varying daily), and standard toppings include shrimp tsumire (fish/shrimp meatballs) and chicken tsukune. No plant-based broth or vegan menu option was found.

lead: アーンドラ・ダイニング 渋谷
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町32-7 HULIC&New UDAGAWA 4F

bio.chef_name: Yamara Ramanaiah (ヤマラ・ラーマナイア)
bio.origin: Andhra Pradesh, India
bio.background: Chef Yamara Ramanaiah trained in five-star hotel kitchens in Hyderabad before moving to Japan, where he opened Andhra Kitchen in Okachimachi and is widely credited as a driving force behind Japan's South Indian food boom. Andhra Dining Shibuya is a sister restaurant to Andhra Kitchen and Andhra Dining Ginza, part of a small, family-run group that later added Andhra Dhaba, run by relatives.
bio.specialty: Hyderabad Dum Biryani — a slow-cooked, spice-layered rice dish with meat or vegetables, served on select days
bio.philosophy: Bring the home-style and restaurant flavors of Andhra Pradesh — built on dozens of hand-blended spices — to Japan without watering them down for local palates.
roles: founding/head chef of the Andhra restaurant group

anecdotes.text: Chef Ramanaiah trained in five-star hotel kitchens in Hyderabad before coming to Japan and is credited as a key figure in popularizing South Indian cuisine there.
The Shibuya location sits on the 4th floor, set back from the street-level bustle, giving diners a calmer space to enjoy South Indian cooking just a few minutes from Shibuya Station.
The restaurant's Hyderabad Dum Biryani, rice layered with slow-cooked spices and meat or vegetables, is offered on select days and is one of its most talked-about dishes.

caveats: Vegetarian thali (Andhra Meals) option is confirmed to exist alongside the non-vegetarian version, but vegan (dairy/ghee-free) and gluten-free specifics were not confirmed directly by the restaurant — they are inferred from typical South Indian menu structure. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm ghee use and wheat-flour bread cross-contact with staff before ordering. Some aggregator sites show slightly different closing times for weekend dinner service; hours here follow the version repeated consistently across Tabelog-sourced listings.
closed_days: Open daily except New Year's Day (元日)
cuisine: South Indian (Andhra Pradesh) cuisine, vegetarian-friendly
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known, foreigner-frequented Shibuya spot with an English Tabelog listing and English-language press coverage, so basic English support is likely, though not confirmed as fully bilingual service.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Udagawa-cho 32-7, Shibuya, a 3-4 minute walk from Shibuya Station/Scramble per multiple sources; well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions — network geocoding is blocked in this environment.
gf_detail: The menu includes naturally wheat-free rice dishes (biryani, dosa, idli) alongside wheat-based breads (naan, chapati, poori) made in the same kitchen. No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was confirmed in available sources, so diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about wheat cross-contact before ordering.
name_ja: アーンドラ・ダイニング 渋谷
neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya (Dogenzaka/Udagawa-cho area, ~3-4 min walk from Shibuya Station)

positives.text: A vegetarian version of the Andhra Meals thali is offered as a standard menu option alongside the non-vegetarian version, so vegetarians aren't limited to side dishes.

seats_text: Around 100 seats, including table seating, sofa seating, and semi-private areas, plus a separate bar-style area for drinks.
vegan_detail: A vegetarian version of the Andhra Meals thali is available alongside vegetable curries, dosa, and rice dishes. However, South Indian vegetarian cooking commonly uses ghee and dairy, and no confirmation was found that vegan (fully dairy-free) preparation is offered by default, so strict vegans should ask staff which dishes can be made without ghee/dairy.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-2-19 三権ビル 2F

bio.background: Saamrot has served Thai and Vietnamese food in Kabukicho for more than two decades, tucked in the alleys between the Golden Gai nightlife strip and Hanazono Shrine. It has been recognized by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce / Thai Select as a restaurant serving authentic Thai cuisine.
bio.specialty: Gapao (Thai basil stir-fry) rice served in several versions — beef, pork, seafood, black tiger prawn — plus a tom yum kung the restaurant is known for locally.
bio.philosophy: The name Saamrot means "three tastes" in Thai, referring to the sweet, spicy, and sour balance that Thai cooking is built around.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's tom yum kung is described as having earned top marks from all five judges on a Thai-food television competition.
The name Saamrot ("three tastes") refers to the sweet-spicy-sour balance that anchors the kitchen's approach to Thai cooking.
The restaurant sits on the second floor of a mixed-use building in an alley between Kabukicho's Golden Gai and Hanazono Shrine, with a compact, low-lit dining room of around 30 table seats.

caveats: Direct page fetches (Tabelog, thaifoods.jp, hitosara, hotpepper, gnavi, etc.) were blocked by network/proxy policy in this environment (persistent 403s on CONNECT), so details are drawn from search-engine result snippets of those same pages, cross-checked across several independent, mutually consistent sources (official site, Tabelog listing text, ethnic-food blog, gourmet directories). No explicit gluten-free or vegan certification, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found anywhere; Thai/Vietnamese kitchens typically use soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce (often containing wheat and always non-vegan) even in vegetable dishes, so this is not a place to rely on for strict celiac or vegan needs without asking staff directly.
closed_days: No regular closed day found ("定休日なし" / open daily) per search-indexed directory listings.
cuisine: Thai & Vietnamese
cultural_comfort_note: Tabelog maintains an English-language listing and the restaurant is used to Kabukicho's mixed foreign/local nightlife crowd, but no English menu or English-speaking staff was confirmed, so a few basic Japanese phrases will help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding is disabled in this environment; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding). The confirmed address, 歌舞伎町1-2-19, and multiple sources describing it as ~5 min walk from Higashi-Shinjuku Station between Golden Gai and Hanazono Shrine, place it roughly 0.5km from Shinjuku Station — within the target radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe protocol was found in any source. Thai/Vietnamese dishes here (pad thai, stir-fries, tom yum, gapao) commonly rely on soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce, which frequently contain wheat, and fried items are likely cooked in shared equipment. Diners with celiac disease should ask the kitchen directly about ingredients for each dish before ordering; nothing here should be assumed gluten-free without confirmation.
neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku

positives.text: The menu includes vegetable-forward dishes such as stir-fried mixed vegetables and morning glory (kang kong / water spinach) alongside the standard Thai and Vietnamese offerings.

seats_text: Roughly 33 seats (one source describes about 30 table seats); the restaurant occupies the 2nd floor of a mixed-use building.
vegan_detail: The menu has vegetable-based dishes (mixed vegetable stir-fry, kang kong/water spinach stir-fry, spring rolls) that lean vegetarian, but no dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found. Thai and Vietnamese kitchens routinely season vegetable dishes and broths with fish sauce, oyster sauce, or shrimp paste, and soups may use non-vegan stock, so strict vegans should ask specifically for dishes made without fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste rather than assuming any vegetable dish is automatically vegan.

lead: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan 渋谷本店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神泉町1-20 松濤ビル1F（〒150-0045）

bio.origin: 渋谷区神泉（Shinsen, Shibuya）
bio.background: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avanは、小麦粉を一切使わず、山形県庄内産の米（はえぬき）を丁寧に製粉した米粉だけで作るグルテンフリー専門店として神泉駅近くにオープン。渋谷本店はブランドの中心となる店舗で、後に上野エキュートなど他店舗も展開している。
bio.specialty: 米粉チュロス（Rice flour churros）— 山形県庄内産の極細挽き米粉と米油を使い、外はサクサク、中はもちもちの食感に仕上げた看板メニュー。フレーバー展開も豊富。
bio.philosophy: 「小麦を使わない」ことを店全体で徹底し、スイーツだけでなく総菜メニュー（唐揚げ、タコライスなど）まで含めて小麦粉不使用にこだわることで、グルテンに配慮したい人が安心して選べる場を目指している。
roles: グルテンフリー専門カフェ／米粉スイーツ専門店

anecdotes.text: 「チュロスがやみつきになるグルテンフリーカフェ、神泉『avan 渋谷本店』」として紹介され、米粉チュロスの食感と味が高く評価されている。
米粉タイムズ等のグルメメディアでも、山形県庄内産のブランド米「はえぬき」を極細挽きした米粉を使用したチュロスとして紹介されている。

caveats: Direct fetches of Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, Tripadvisor, and the official site were blocked (403/DNS errors) in this environment; facts below are drawn from Google search-result snippets that quote/summarize those pages, cross-checked across multiple independent listings (Tabelog EN title, Gnavi, Hotpepper, Yahoo Map, autoreserve, gourmet-calendar, official avan-sweets.com references). Closed-day details conflict slightly across sources (some say Tuesday-only closed with irregular 2nd/3rd-Thursday closures, one snippet said "no fixed closed day," another said "Tuesday and Wednesday") — hours_status is marked irregular and travelers should confirm same-day via phone (03-6416-1856) or Instagram (@avan_sweets_). Note: avan also operates other locations (e.g., an Ecute Ueno takeaway-only shop with a different, more limited menu); this record is specifically for the Shibuya/Shinsen honten at 神泉町1-20. One English-language celiac review reported illness after eating the taco rice and cautioned that the shop's "gluten-free" framing may mean "wheat-free" rather than strict celiac-safe handling — reflected in gf_confidence and the safety section below.
closed_days: 火曜定休（情報源により不定休／第2・第3木曜も休みとの記載あり、要事前確認）
cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ（米粉スイーツ・米粉料理） / Gluten-free cafe specializing in rice-flour sweets (rice flour churros) and wheat-free savory dishes
cultural_comfort_note: English menu is available and staff are described as friendly and welcoming, though full English fluency is not guaranteed, so simple Japanese phrases or a translation app help for detailed allergy questions.
gf_detail: The shop states it uses no wheat flour anywhere on the menu, substituting rice flour (Yamagata Shonai "Haenuki" rice) even in savory items like fried chicken and taco rice, which is why it markets itself as a gluten-free specialty shop. However, this is described in sources as a "no wheat flour" commitment rather than a certified strict celiac protocol: at least one English-language celiac reviewer reported becoming ill after the taco rice and cautioned that hidden gluten sources (e.g., wheat in soy sauce/seasonings used in savory dishes) may not be fully screened, and that the shop's definition of "gluten-free" may lean toward "wheat-free." Sweets/churros are more consistently reported as safe than the savory menu. Celiac travelers should ask specifically about soy sauce, dashi, and seasoning ingredients before ordering savory items.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address places the shop in Shibuya-ku Shinsen-cho, about a 3-5 minute walk from Shinsen Station (Keio Inokashira Line) and roughly a 15-minute walk from Shibuya Station/Shibuya Scramble, consistent with the target area description.
hours_raw: 月・水・木・金・土・日 11:00-21:00（ラストオーダー 20:30）、火曜定休。一部情報源では第2・第3木曜日も休みとの記載あり（要確認）。
name_en: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan Shibuya Honten
name_ja: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan 渋谷本店
neighborhood: 神泉 (Shinsen), Shibuya-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: One English-language celiac reviewer reported becoming ill after eating the taco rice, cautioning that the shop's gluten-free claim may only mean wheat-flour-free rather than fully celiac-safe, and recommending caution for celiacs especially with savory menu items.
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search summary of findmeglutenfree.com / celiac travel review aggregation

gf_cross_contamination.text: Another reviewer, noting it functions as a dedicated gluten-free facility, said staff seemed confident about what they could serve even with a language barrier, rating the experience positively (5 stars).
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search summary of findmeglutenfree.com review aggregation

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described across reviews as friendly and accommodating, going out of their way to meet customer requests, with both English and Japanese menus available.
staff_allergy_handling.source: web search summary of celiac/GF review aggregation (findmeglutenfree.com)

positives.text: Entire menu (not just desserts) is made without wheat flour, using rice flour milled from Yamagata Shonai-grown rice, which is the basis for its reputation as a dedicated gluten-free specialty shop.

seats_text: Roughly 28 seats (small café seating, exact count not independently verified)
vegan_detail: The core menu (rice-flour churros, fried chicken, taco rice) is not vegan by default, as it includes meat and likely dairy/egg in some sweets. However, sources note the shop offers seasonal vegan-and-gluten-free-compatible desserts, such as a shaved ice made with homemade almond cream and soy milk cream instead of condensed milk, indicating some deliberate vegan-friendly options exist alongside the main gluten-free-focused menu. Vegan diners should ask about egg/dairy in specific baked goods and about hidden animal-derived seasonings (e.g., dashi, bonito) in savory dishes, which were not specifically documented in available sources.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-16-10

bio.background: 新井英樹氏は調理師専門学校を卒業後、ホテルの洋食シェフや病院の調理職を経て、焼肉の道を志し「叙々苑」で修業。その後、三河島「山田屋」で5年間、牛・豚問わず正肉からホルモンまでの焼肉全般の技術を習得した。2013年に独立し「焼肉ホルモン 新井屋」を渋谷にオープン。当初は豚専門店だったが客足が伸びず、1年ほどで牛タン・牛カルビ・牛ロースを追加して人気店に成長。2018年に阿佐ヶ谷店、2019年に渋谷本店拡大、2021年に「はなれ」、2022年に「にかい」を開業し、ANIKU produced by 新井屋（水道橋）は新井氏が初めて手がけた「プロデュース」業態の一号店。
bio.philosophy: 本店「新井屋」と同じ仕入れルートで新鮮なホルモンと上質な和牛を調達し、「肉のプロの目利き」による選定を看板とする。古民家を改装した落ち着いた空間で、見た目のインパクトと肉そのものの品質を両立させる料理を提供する。
bio.specialty: タワーカルビ（積み重ねた和牛カルビをわさびで食べる、ジェンガのような見た目の名物メニュー）
roles: produce chef / owner of parent restaurant 新井屋

anecdotes.text: タワーカルビは積まれたお肉がジェンガのような見た目でインスタ映えする一品で、わさびをつけてアクセントに食べるのが特徴。和牛ならではの上品な脂の旨みを堪能できる。
ANIKU1本ステーキはドラゴンのように長い迫力のある逸品で、ハサミを使わずに箸で切れるほど柔らかい。ホルモンは新鮮で丁寧に下処理されており、どの部位も臭みは一切ない。
新井英樹さんは2013年に独立し「焼肉ホルモン新井屋」をオープン。最初は豚専門の焼肉店として立ち上げたが客足が伸びず、1年ほど経ってから牛タン・牛カルビ・牛ロースを加えた。2018年阿佐ヶ谷店、2019年渋谷店、2021年「はなれ」、2022年「にかい」と展開。

caveats: Tabelog and several primary listing pages (Tabelog, Ikyu, Epark, Gnavi, the parent 新井屋 official site) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch and could not be read in full; all facts here are corroborated instead through Google search result snippets/summaries of those same pages plus secondary sources (locagoo.co.jp, hitosara magazine, Yahoo News/dancyu). Seat count is inconsistent across sources (34 vs 54 reported) so seats is left null. No gluten-free or vegan information is published anywhere found for this yakiniku restaurant; assessments below are inferred from cuisine type (grilled meat, likely soy-sauce-based tare) rather than any explicit statement by the restaurant.
closed_days: 年中無休（年末年始 12/30〜1/5を除く）
cuisine: 焼肉（和牛・ホルモン）/ Yakiniku (wagyu and offal)
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-style yakiniku restaurant in a renovated old house with no English menu or dietary accommodations documented; expect to navigate in Japanese or with translation help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null); address confirmed as 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-16-10, about a 5-minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius of Suidōbashi Station (35.7017, 139.7539).
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, statement, or dedicated preparation is documented anywhere. Grilled meat cuts themselves are typically naturally gluten-free, but yakiniku tare (dipping/marinade sauce) commonly contains soy sauce made with wheat, and any breaded or sauced side dishes would not be GF. Celiac diners should ask staff directly (in Japanese) about tare ingredients and cross-contact on the shared grill before ordering plain-salt (塩) cuts.
name_ja: ANIKU produced by 新井屋（エーニク プロデュースド バイ アライヤ）
neighborhood: 神田三崎町（水道橋）/ Kanda-Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidōbashi Station)
seats_text: Sources disagree: one lists a total of 54 seats (14 at a 1F counter, 40 at 2F tables) in a renovated traditional wooden house; another lists 34 seats.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric yakiniku restaurant (premium wagyu and fresh horumon/offal) with no vegan menu, vegan options, or plant-based statements found in any source. Broth/tare bases are not documented but yakiniku sauces commonly include dashi/bonito-derived or animal-based ingredients. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-7

bio.background: Founded in Taisho 4 (1915), in the middle of World War I, near what would become Shinjuku Station's southeast exit. The building has been rebuilt twice since — it burned down during World War II and was rebuilt, and the current building dates to the postwar high-growth era when Naganoya came to own the property outright — making it a rare shokudo with over a century of continuous history steps from one of the world's busiest train stations.
bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: 肉どうふ (nikudofu) — beef and tofu simmered until dark brown in a sweet-savory soy broth, served with rice and miso soup; also known for katsudon and katsu curry
bio.philosophy: A working-class daily-use shokudo (cafeteria) rather than a destination restaurant — cheap, filling teishoku served fast to office workers, regulars, and the occasional daytime drinker, preserving a Showa-era dining atmosphere just steps from the ultramodern station.

anecdotes.text: Naganoya was founded in Taisho 4 (1915), amid World War I, and has weathered Shinjuku's constant redevelopment around it ever since; the current building is the third to stand on the site, rebuilt after wartime fire damage and again during the postwar economic boom.

The signature nikudofu (meat and tofu) is simmered until dark brown, the tofu soaking up the soy-based broth for a balance of saltiness and sweetness that pairs well with a drink; it comes with a miso soup containing tofu and seaweed.
Walking in feels like stepping into the Showa era — a woman greets guests at the counter while regulars sit quietly reading the newspaper, just one minute from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.

caveats: Hours listed vary slightly across directory sites (some list 11:30-21:30, others 12:00-22:00); confirm current hours before visiting. Cash-only status is inferred from its old-school character and a customer request for PayPay support on Retty, not independently confirmed by the restaurant. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source; this is a traditional meat-and-fish-centric teishoku shokudo, not a dietary-focused restaurant.
closed_days: Wednesday
cuisine: 定食・ポークカツカレー・家庭料理 (Teishoku, katsu curry, home-style cooking); signature dish is 肉どうふ (nikudofu, simmered beef and tofu)
cultural_comfort_note: A century-old neighborhood shokudo with handwritten menus and a regulars-only feel — no English menu was found in any source, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodations found in any source. The menu centers on katsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet — wheat flour breading) and katsu curry (curry roux is almost always wheat-thickened in Japanese shokudo), plus dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce. No mention of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free substitutions. Not recommended for celiac diners without confirming directly with staff.
hours_raw: 11:30-21:00 or 12:00-22:00 (sources differ); closed Wednesdays
neighborhood: 新宿 東南口 (Shinjuku Southeast Exit)
seats_text: Small counter/table seating, roughly 10 seats reported on the ground floor with additional seating noted upstairs (exact count unconfirmed)
vegan_detail: No vegan options or menu identified in any source. The specialty dish (nikudofu) contains beef and is simmered in a soy-based broth; the accompanying miso soup contains tofu/seaweed and is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi per standard Japanese practice. The rest of the menu (katsudon, grilled mackerel, simmered fish dishes, katsu curry) is meat- and fish-centric. Not suitable for vegan diners without extensive modification, and no evidence staff offer such modification.

lead: オーガニックごはん かえもん 浅草本店
address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-9-1 AUSPICE浅草花川戸(オースピス浅草花川戸) 3F 〒111-0033

roles: Owner / head chef: Uehara Kazunori (上原一徳)
Hall manager: Imai Masafumi (今井雅文)
bio.background: Kaemon opened as an organic restaurant near Asakusa Station built around a self-described "food barrier-free" (食のバリアフリー) concept: a menu meant to be safely enjoyed by guests of any nationality, religion, or dietary restriction. It is run by owner-chef Uehara Kazunori, who has described publicly moving toward extreme minimal eating ("breatharian"-style, at most one meal a day, sometimes weeks without food) since around 2014 while continuing to develop the restaurant's menu. The current flagship (本店) location is on the 3rd floor of a building called AUSPICE in Hanakawado, a couple of minutes from Asakusa Station.
bio.origin: Tokyo (chef born 1964, per his own public profile)
bio.specialty: Soba-flour and organic wheat pancake (そば粉と有機小麦のパンケーキ), a long-running signature item; also a rotating organic-vegetable buffet and set courses ("かえもんディナーセット", "旬の厳選野菜コース")
bio.philosophy: "Food barrier-free" dining: no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy, plus deliberate avoidance of the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot) used in Buddhist shojin cooking, so the restaurant calls its cuisine "Oriental Vegetarian." The kitchen sources seasonal organic produce and reworks the menu around whatever arrives.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's stated aim is to be a "food barrier-free" restaurant so that people can eat without worry regardless of nationality or religion, avoiding onion, garlic, and leek in addition to meat, fish, egg, and dairy.

Produce is sourced from an organic farm in Chiba ("Hyakushou Farm"), and the kitchen aims to develop around 300 new menu items a year, testing new dishes daily to match whatever seasonal vegetables arrive.

Hall manager Imai Masafumi, interviewed by Meat Free Monday All Japan, described Kaemon's cuisine as built on shojin (Buddhist temple cooking) principles with the five-allium restriction, and said the restaurant also runs a volunteer "Fairy System" where helpers are compensated in an internal currency called "MON" rather than as conventional employees.

Owner Uehara Kazunori has publicly described becoming increasingly able to go without food after time spent traveling in mountainous regions, adopting a very-low-food ("breatharian") lifestyle from around 2014 while continuing to run the restaurant's kitchen.

caveats: Multiple primary sources (the Tabelog page itself, the official site organickaemon.com, and several review sites) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment and could only be characterized via search-engine snippets, not full-page verification — treat page-level details (exact seat count, precise current menu) as secondary-sourced. Note also that the current Tabelog listing title reads "オーガニックシェアスペース かえもん 浅草" (Organic *Share Space* Kaemon Asakusa) rather than the original "オーガニックごはん," and Tuesdays are reserved for private events, suggesting the venue now operates partly as a rentable community/event space alongside its regular restaurant hours. An older article (macaroni) tagged a "closed" Kaemon location, but that appears to reference an earlier premises (Vision Center Asakusa, near Asakusa Station's north exit) prior to the move to the current Hanakawado honten address — multiple independent, more recent listings (Retty, Tsuku2 ticketing, the official Asakusa Tourism Federation site e-asakusa.jp, and an event-booking page) consistently show the Hanakawado address as active. Confirm hours and menu directly before visiting.
closed_days: Tuesdays (regular closure; the day is reserved for private events rather than public dining)
cuisine: Organic plant-based / oriental vegetarian (shojin-inspired) comfort food
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant appears on English-language Tabelog and halal/vegan tourist directories aimed at foreign visitors, suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese speakers, but the on-site level of spoken English was not confirmed from available sources.
gf_detail: The kitchen is fully plant-based and miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth rather than bonito dashi, which removes some common hidden-animal risks. However, wheat is present in at least one standard menu item (a soba-flour and organic *wheat*-flour pancake is a signature dish), and no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated cookware/fryer, or wheat-free (tamari-style) soy sauce. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should call ahead and confirm which specific buffet/set dishes are wheat-free that day.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun: 11:00–15:30 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:00). Closed Tuesdays (reserved for private events).
geocode_note: Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address is 花川戸1-9-1, Taito-ku — Hanakawado sits between Asakusa Station and the Sumida River, roughly a 5–7 minute walk (well under 0.8km) from Sensoji Temple, and about 1–2 minutes from Asakusa Station itself per multiple listings, so it falls within the Sensoji/Asakusa target radius.
name_ja: オーガニックごはん かえもん 浅草本店
neighborhood: Asakusa (Hanakawado), roughly 1-2 minutes' walk from Asakusa Station

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen does not prepare meat, fish, egg, or dairy dishes at all — it is a fully plant-based operation, and miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth ("vege broth") instead of bonito-based dashi.

positives.text: Restaurant explicitly markets itself as "food barrier-free," avoiding meat, fish, egg, dairy, and the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot) so that diners with a range of dietary and religious restrictions can eat there.
Listed on Vegewel's curated directory of vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free-friendly restaurants near Asakusa Station.

seats_text: Seat count not confirmed from available sources.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based kitchen: no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish, and soy-based meat substitutes are used in some items. Miso soup/broths reportedly use a vegetable-scrap broth rather than bonito dashi. The kitchen also avoids the five pungent alliums (onion, garlic, scallion, Chinese chive, shallot), which additionally suits Buddhist-style (shojin) vegetarian diners. Format is largely an organic-vegetable buffet plus set courses.

lead: ひない小町 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-15-11 龍昌ビル B1F・1F

bio.origin: 秋田県（比内地鶏の産地）
bio.background: 比内地鶏は名古屋コーチン・薩摩地鶏と並ぶ日本三大地鶏の一つで、秋田県の特産品。当店は毎朝秋田から直送される比内地鶏を備長炭でじっくり焼き上げる串焼き専門店として営業しており、2025年3月に全面改装してリニューアルオープンした。
bio.specialty: 比内地鶏の備長炭焼き鳥（白レバー串が特に人気）ときりたんぽ鍋
bio.philosophy: 秋田直送の比内地鶏と備長炭焼きへのこだわりを軸に、秋田郷土料理を提供する

anecdotes.text: 看板メニューのきりたんぽ鍋は、比内地鶏から取った出汁に野菜・まいたけ・しらたきともちもちの「きりたんぽ」を合わせた一品で、農林水産省の「郷土料理百選」に選ばれた料理。
比内地鶏の串焼きは360円から提供され、白レバー串が最も人気のメニューとされる。

caveats: This is a Hinai-jidori (Akita heritage chicken) yakitori specialty izakaya, not a restaurant curated for vegan or gluten-free diners — no dedicated GF/vegan menu, allergy protocol, or dietary-focused sourcing was found on any page indexed. Direct WebFetch of Tabelog, the gnavi listing, and other detail pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment; facts below are drawn from search-engine result snippets/summaries that cite those pages (Tabelog, Rakuten Gurunavi, Hitosara, EnjoyTokyo), not from directly rendered page content, so treat menu-level dietary detail as unconfirmed rather than verified.
closed_days: 不定休、年末年始休業（12/31〜1/2）
cuisine: 秋田郷土料理・比内地鶏焼き鳥（居酒屋）
cultural_comfort_note: A local Japanese izakaya experience with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help when asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergy protocol was found. Yakitori here is typically offered either tare-glazed (a soy-based sauce that contains wheat) or shio (salt-grilled, no soy sauce) — shio-style skewers would be the safer ask, but cross-contact on a shared charcoal grill and in shared sauces/marinades is unconfirmed either way. The signature kiritanpo nabe hot pot uses a chicken-based dashi broth whose full seasoning (soy sauce, etc.) is not documented. Celiac diners should not assume safety without asking staff directly and should not rely on this listing alone.
name_ja: ひない小町 渋谷店
neighborhood: 神泉（渋谷区道玄坂、神泉駅・渋谷駅から徒歩圏内）

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard yakitori tare glaze is soy-sauce based (contains wheat); no confirmation found of a wheat-free tare or dedicated GF sauce.

positives.text: Shio (salt-grilled, no soy-based tare) yakitori is a standard menu option alongside tare-glazed skewers, which may reduce wheat exposure if confirmed with staff.

seats_text: Roughly 80 seats, including private rooms for 4-18 guests and full-venue buyout for 40+.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken (Hinai-jidori) specialty izakaya; there is no vegan or vegetarian menu. Vegetable yakitori skewers (野菜串) exist as individual items, but they are grilled on the same charcoal grill as meat skewers and the restaurant's signature broths (e.g., kiritanpo nabe) are chicken-stock based, so hidden animal-derived dashi/stock is likely throughout the menu. Not a suitable choice for strict vegans; only worth considering for flexible vegetarians willing to ask detailed questions about individual vegetable items.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-12-7 フェルメール 1F

bio.chef_name: Ishiyama Koichi and Hozumi Eri (石山公一・穂積依里)
bio.origin: The husband-and-wife chef duo previously ran the Thai restaurant "Pissanulok" in Nagoya (opened 2016), which was listed in the Gault&Millau guide three years running. In June 2024 they relocated the restaurant to Tokyo's Jimbocho neighborhood and renamed it KHAO.
bio.background: Ishiyama Koichi (b. 1977) has roughly 30 years of cooking experience; he first visited Thailand at age 35, became captivated by Thai cuisine, and trained at Bangkok's one-Michelin-star restaurant Nahm. Hozumi Eri (b. 1986) moved to Thailand at age 24 to apprentice under a Thai chef and spent about two years studying regional nam prik gaeng (curry pastes) across the country before returning to Japan; the two met in Bangkok.
bio.specialty: Thai curry (gaeng) made with coconut milk the chefs press fresh in-house, paired with hand-made nam prik gaeng (curry paste) developed from Hozumi's research trips around Thailand.
bio.philosophy: Cooking that honors Thai culinary tradition while drawing on Japanese ingredients and technique, served as an omakase counter experience with all guests starting together.

anecdotes.text: The name KHAO means "white" in Thai.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated from restaurant coverage, see sources)

anecdotes.text: Within about a year of reopening in Tokyo, KHAO earned one Michelin star in the MICHELIN Guide Tokyo 2026 — reported as Japan's first Michelin star awarded to a Thai restaurant.
The restaurant seats only 8 at a counter where diners watch the cooking process up close.

caveats: Fine-dining omakase-only Thai restaurant with no confirmed dedicated vegan or gluten-free program. Search results indicate the restaurant may decline reservations from guests with extensive allergies or strong food dislikes, since the tasting menu offers limited room for substitution — anyone with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should contact the restaurant directly before booking to confirm they can be safely accommodated.
closed_days: Wednesday and Sunday (定休日：水・日)
cuisine: タイ料理 (Thai cuisine, omakase course)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, fully reservation-based Japanese fine-dining counter; booking is typically done in Japanese (phone or Japanese reservation platforms) and the restaurant has noted it may decline guests with extensive allergies, so travelers should communicate dietary needs clearly in advance.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer confirmed; omakase format limits substitution and the restaurant may decline guests with extensive allergies.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding is blocked in this environment); downstream geocoding should use the address above. The address (神田神保町2-12-7) is one minute's walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A4, and Jimbocho is well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station, satisfying the target-area requirement.
hours_raw: 月・火・木・金・土・祝日 18:00～21:30（定休日：水・日）／完全予約制、コース開始時間同時スタート
neighborhood: 神保町 (Jimbocho), 千代田区

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant's own listing notes that guests with extensive allergies or strong food dislikes may have their reservation declined, since the menu is a single fixed omakase course.

positives.text: The chefs make their coconut milk and curry pastes (nam prik gaeng) from scratch in-house rather than using pre-made bases, giving them more direct control over ingredients than many Thai kitchens.

seats_text: 8-seat open counter facing the kitchen
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dedicated vegan track has been confirmed. Thai curries and nam prik gaeng at KHAO commonly rely on fish sauce, shrimp paste, and other animal-derived seasonings as part of traditional preparation, and the fixed omakase format limits substitution; the restaurant has indicated it may decline guests with extensive dietary restrictions. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly in advance to confirm whether any accommodation is possible.

lead: タカマル鮮魚店 本館
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-15-1 アラパイトビル1F

bio.background: Founder Masataka Aihara was born in 1972 in Amagasaki, Hyogo Prefecture. He moved to Tokyo at age 22 originally aiming to become an actor, but ended up joining a major seafood company, where he quickly rose to store manager and buyer. He later worked as a "fish concierge," supplying seafood to major supermarket chains and running tuna-breakdown demonstration events to boost sales. In 2004 he founded Takamaru Co., Ltd. and obtained a Tsukiji market trading license, running wholesale and retail-training contracts. Unsatisfied with fixed annual contracts that were not tied to results, he founded Takamaru Sengyoten in 2009 to test his own sales ability directly through retail and restaurant operation.
bio.specialty: Sashimi and sushi made from high-grade fish sourced directly from Tsukiji (now Toyosu) each morning and sold at near-wholesale prices, with the seafood itself as the centerpiece rather than elaborate cooking.
bio.philosophy: Steady, word-of-mouth growth rather than flashy advertising: the first shop opened in 2009 in a converted 18-tsubo garage; as regulars increased and space ran short, the company opened additional branches within walking distance rather than expanding aggressively.
bio.origin: Amagasaki, Hyogo Prefecture (founder's hometown)

anecdotes.text: 2009年に18坪のガレージを改装して1号店を創業し、2011年に徒歩1〜2分の場所に2号店をオープンし、翌年12月に青梅街道沿いに3号店を開店した。各店とも派手な広告宣伝は一切しておらず、開業当初は苦労したが、徐々に常連客が増えて手狭になると、徒歩圏内に新店を出店するという、堅実な展開を進めてきた。
築地まぐろの解体師が運営する魚やで、毎朝とれたて新鮮な高級築地鮮魚を中卸価格で調理・販売する。

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (menu labeling, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol) was found on Tabelog, the official site, or review aggregators. This is a seafood-forward izakaya; assessments below are inferred from the general menu type (raw fish, tempura, soy-sauce-based dishes), not from any confirmed allergy policy. Diners with celiac disease or vegan requirements should call ahead (03-5937-5969) to confirm. Hours/seat/payment details were pieced together from search-engine snippets of Tabelog, HotPepper, Yahoo Map, and Jalan listings since direct page fetches returned 403 errors; figures were cross-checked across at least two independent sources where possible.
closed_days: 無休 (open every day of the year, including holidays and the day before holidays)
cuisine: 海鮮・刺身・居酒屋 (Seafood / sashimi & sushi izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling local seafood izakaya with an open-terrace feel; no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only experience aside from third-party reservation sites (Tabelog, HotPepper) that offer some English/romanized listings.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. Plain sashimi/sushi cuts of fish are naturally gluten-free, but the menu also includes fried items (tempura/karaage-style dishes) and shoyu-based preparations; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no confirmation of a gluten-free soy sauce alternative, a dedicated fryer, or shared-fryer avoidance. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm ingredients dish-by-dish.
geocode_note: Geocoding was not performed per instructions (lat/lng set to null for downstream address-based placement). Address is 西新宿7-15-1, described in multiple sources as a 3-minute walk from Toei Oedo Line "Shinjuku-nishiguchi" Station (D5 exit) and a 6-minute walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station (south exit); both stations sit within the JR Shinjuku Station complex/immediate vicinity, placing the restaurant well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
name_ja: タカマル鮮魚店 本館
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) typically contains wheat; no confirmation was found that Takamaru Sengyoten offers a wheat-free soy sauce alternative for sashimi/sushi service.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: General knowledge of standard shoyu composition; no restaurant-specific source confirms or denies an alternative.

positives.text: The restaurant's core dishes are simply prepared sashimi and sushi cuts of whole fish purchased directly from Tsukiji, which as raw, unseasoned cuts are inherently free of wheat/gluten before any sauce is added.

seats_text: Roughly 50 table seats in an open, terrace-style dining area
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-specialty izakaya built around raw and cooked fish; no vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito-free options were found in any source. Broth and many prepared dishes should be assumed to use fish-based dashi/bonito unless confirmed otherwise by staff. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without direct confirmation.

lead: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 渋谷東急本店前店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-7 プラザ道玄坂1F

roles: soba chain restaurant (no individual chef publicly named)
bio.origin: Part of the Sagatani (嵯峨谷) chain, which opened its first shop in 2011; the Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is now run by For You Inc. (株式会社フォーユー).
bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba) — noodles made from 100% buckwheat with no wheat-flour binder, milled fresh in-house
bio.philosophy: Built around the idea of "五たて" (five freshnesses): freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried, and served fresh — aiming to prove that a fast, affordable stand-and-eat-style soba shop doesn't have to cut corners on using 100% buckwheat.

anecdotes.text: Sagatani installed automated stone mills at its shop fronts so whole buckwheat groats are ground into flour on-site and used the same day, rather than buying pre-milled soba flour.

The chain opened its first branch in 2011 around the idea of selling genuine 100%-buckwheat soba for as little as roughly 350 yen, challenging the convention that cheap stand-and-eat soba shops use flour blended with wheat.
The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is noted among fans for its kamo seiro (duck dipping soba) as a heartier option alongside the classic mori/kake soba.

caveats: Payment method (cash vs. cashless) at this specific location was not confirmed by available sources — order is via a self-service food-ticket vending machine. Older sources report different hours (including one claim of 24-hour operation), which appear outdated; the weekday/weekend split hours given here come from more recent, mutually consistent listings but should be reconfirmed before visiting. This is a mainstream soba chain, not a dedicated gluten-free or vegan restaurant — the gluten-free and vegan notes below are inferred from the standard menu and typical Japanese soba-tsuyu composition, not from a stated allergen policy, so diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should ask staff directly and consider it a moderate-risk choice.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; closed Jan 1–3 for New Year holidays.
cuisine: 十割蕎麦 (100% buckwheat soba)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a self-service Japanese-language food-ticket vending machine (pick a ticket, sit, wait for your number) with no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, though the process is simple to follow visually.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address is 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-7 プラザ道玄坂1F, on Dogenzaka roughly 300–400m (about a 4–5 minute walk) from Shibuya Station/Shibuya Scramble, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 十割 (100% buckwheat, no wheat flour or yamaimo binder), which is a genuinely wheat-free noodle base. However, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a traditional kaeshi made from soy sauce and mirin over bonito dashi, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — no wheat-free tsuyu has been confirmed for this chain. Tempura and kakiage (fried in wheat-flour batter) are also on the menu, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or separate cooking water for the soba has been confirmed. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about the tsuyu recipe and frying setup before ordering, and treat this as a moderate-risk rather than a safe choice.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The soba tsuyu is dashi-based with bonito rather than a kombu-only broth, so the standard bowl is not vegan; no plant-based dashi alternative was found in available menu information.

positives.text: The soba itself is 十割 (100% buckwheat) with no wheat flour or mountain-yam binder added — a genuinely wheat-free noodle, which is unusual for such an affordable chain.

No kombu-only or otherwise plant-based dashi option was found in available menus or reviews, and there is no evidence of a vegetarian-adapted broth at this location despite the dry buckwheat noodles themselves containing no egg or animal product.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町3-3-18 AB River 1F

roles: Tokyo chain izakaya specializing in 80-yen charcoal-grilled yakitori
bio.background: This branch is part of a small Tokyo yakitori chain (multiple branches under the Nobunaga/Hideyoshi names, e.g. Suidobashi, Jimbocho, Kiba) built around a Sengoku (Warring States)-warlord theme, matching its name (Nobunaga = the 16th-century daimyo Oda Nobunaga).
bio.specialty: 80-yen charcoal (binchotan)-grilled chicken yakitori skewers, made with domestic chicken including limited rare cuts; also known for its jumbo-portion 'Baka-mori' side dishes (chicken katsu, fries, salad, bean-sprout stir-fry).
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated corporate philosophy is "まじめにふざけろ" ("be seriously playful"), paired with a promise of "fast, cheap, delicious" (早い・安い・旨い) food and a customer-first attitude.

anecdotes.text: Hanging lanterns give the shop a lively, festival-like feel, and the interior is decorated with katana (sword) objects and folding fans evoking the Sengoku warlord era referenced in the restaurant's name.
The signature 'Baka-mori' (jumbo-portion) side-dish series was created in response to customers asking for bigger servings, reflecting the chain's playful house philosophy.
Course menus are named after Sengoku figures, e.g. the 9-dish 'Sengoku Busho (Warlord) Course' with house-made offal stew, and the 11-dish 'Nobunaga Great Daimyo Course.'

caveats: No confirmed gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found for this restaurant; it is a mainstream chicken-yakitori izakaya chain, not a dietary-focused establishment. Hours and closed-days information come from aggregator listings (Hotpepper/Gnavi/NAVITIME via search) rather than a directly fetched official page, since direct WebFetch to tabelog.com and the restaurant's own domain was blocked by network policy (403) in this environment; details are cross-corroborated across multiple independent listing sources found via search.

cuisine: 焼き鳥 (Yakitori / grilled chicken skewers) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A mainstream Japanese chain izakaya near Suidobashi Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its cheap-skewer format and picture-friendly course menus are easy to navigate with pointing and basic Japanese.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区三崎町3-3-18) and listings confirm JR Suidobashi Station West Exit is a 2-minute walk (Toei Mita Line Suidobashi Exit A2 is 5 minutes), placing it well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station (35.7017,139.7539), matching the target-area requirement.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free options or accommodations were found. As a yakitori-and-izakaya menu, dishes are very likely to use soy-sauce-based tare glaze (contains wheat) on the skewers, and fried items such as chicken karaage and chicken katsu are breaded/battered with wheat flour. No information was found on a dedicated fryer, allergy menu, or staff training for celiac-safe handling, so this is not a safe choice for strict gluten-free/celiac diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.

bio.origin: Rooted in a tempura lineage dating to 1903 (Meiji 36), when founder Kanekichi Ishinabe began a tempura yatai (street stall) in Yotsuya, Tokyo; the stall was later formalized into the restaurant Tenkane (天兼) near Shinjuku Mitsukoshi during the Taisho era.
bio.background: Tenhide (天秀) was later established independently by a member of that same family lineage, continuing the technique and spirit of Tenkane. Sources describe the restaurant as carrying forward four generations of tempura craft rooted in the 1903 origin.
bio.specialty: Edo-mae style tempura courses, tempura rice bowls (tendon), and anago (conger eel) tempura.
bio.philosophy: Emphasis on premium, seasonally sourced ingredients and high-quality frying oil, applying traditional Edo-mae tempura technique passed down across generations.

anecdotes.text: A dedicated restaurant profile describes Tenhide as carrying forward "four generations of skill" rooted in the 1903 Tenkane lineage, with the current shop founded independently by a later-generation family member.

A feature on the Tenkane lineage traces its start to a 1903 tempura yatai stall in Yotsuya Igamachi founded by Kanekichi Ishinabe, later becoming a storefront near Shinjuku Mitsukoshi in the Taisho era.

roles: proprietor family descends from the Tenkane (1903) tempura lineage

caveats: Direct WebFetch of the restaurant's own domains (ten-hide.com, tenhide.jp, tabelog.com, tablecheck.com) returned HTTP 403 in this environment (the outbound proxy rejected the CONNECT for these hosts), so this record is built entirely from WebSearch result snippets/AI summaries that quote and cite those pages, rather than from directly fetched page text. Seat count has a minor discrepancy across sources (one summary says 18 total seats [10 counter + 8 table], another describes counter seating up to 6 plus semi-private horigotatsu tatami seating) — both are reported here as seats_text; the numeric seats field uses the more specific 18-seat figure. address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1丁目23-6

bio.origin: Founded in 1921 (Taisho 10) as a bakery in Iriya, Taito-ku; opened its Asakusa storefront in 1935 (Showa 10) as an eatery, selling steamed pork buns and shumai from a stall beside it.
bio.background: A century-old family-run takeout counter on Asakusa's Shin-Nakamise/Fureai-dori corner, known for keeping its recipe unchanged since before the war. It became a favorite of local Asakusa entertainers and comedians over the decades.
bio.specialty: Nikuman (steamed pork bun) with a juicy, broth-like filling of minced pork and onion, and handmade oversized shumai.
bio.philosophy: A small, old-fashioned shop that prides itself on an unchanged, simple recipe — just pork, onion, and technique — rather than trends.

anecdotes.text: Comedian and filmmaker Beat Takeshi reportedly declared this shop's meat bun 'the most delicious in Japan.'
Entertainer couple Hayashiya Pe & Paako are said to always give this shop's meat buns as souvenirs.
The bun's filling releases a broth-like juice because the pork mince is mixed with finely chopped onion, which gives off moisture as it cooks.

caveats: No English-language source or the shop's own site confirms allergen handling; gluten-free/vegan info below is inferred from ingredients described in food-blog and directory write-ups, not from the shop directly. Takeout-only counter with no seating (dine-in listing on Tabelog reflects the general dim sum/yum-cha category, not table service).
closed_days: None — open year-round (年中無休), per multiple directory listings.
cuisine: Dim sum / yum cha takeout counter — steamed pork buns (nikuman) and shumai, since 1921
cultural_comfort_note: A simple point-and-order takeout window on a busy Asakusa tourist street with a short, priced menu (buns, shumai) — easy to order without Japanese, though no English menu is confirmed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address is Asakusa 1-chome, on Fureai-dori near the Shin-Nakamise intersection — a few hundred meters from Sensoji, well within the Asakusa target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free option is mentioned anywhere. The nikuman wrapper is a steamed wheat-flour bun and the shumai wrapper is also wheat-based, so both signature items contain wheat. No source discusses a dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or celiac-safe handling — treat as unsuitable for a gluten-free/celiac diet.

neighborhood: Asakusa 1-chome, near Shin-Nakamise / Fureai-dori, close to Asakusa Station
seats_text: Takeout-only counter; no seating.
vegan_detail: All signature items are meat-based: the nikuman filling is pork mince with onion, and the shumai is also pork. No vegetarian or vegan version is mentioned in any source found.
