bio.origin: Ebisu, Tokyo (original honten founded 1966)
bio.background: Matsue (松栄) began as a neighborhood sushi shop in Ebisu in 1966 and has since been recognized in Tabelog's Hyakumeiten (Top 100) sushi rankings. The company later expanded to a handful of related locations in Ebisu, and on November 1, 2019 opened this branch on the 12th floor of the newly built Shibuya Scramble Square tower, directly above Shibuya Station at the famous crossing.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi omakase courses, built around seasonal fish selected personally by the restaurant's buyers ('meikiki', an eye for quality) from Toyosu Market and regional markets in Hokkaido and Kyushu.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes careful attention to the temperature and serving order of each piece of nigiri, and to sourcing seasonal, market-fresh seafood rather than a fixed, unchanging menu.
roles: sushi restaurant (multi-location group, Shibuya Scramble Square branch)

anecdotes.text: The Ebisu honten was founded in 1966 as an ordinary neighborhood sushi shop with wax food-sample displays out front; in 1992 it repositioned itself, removing its old signage and printed menu list to focus on an omakase, chef's-choice format.
The Shibuya Scramble Square branch, 'Sushi Matsue,' opened on November 1, 2019, as part of the new commercial tower directly connected to Shibuya Station.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodation was confirmed by any source (official site, Tabelog, or third-party listings). This is a traditional fish-focused Edomae sushi restaurant; assessments of GF/vegan suitability below are inferred from the cuisine type, not from an explicit restaurant statement, so diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should call ahead (03-6452-6338) to confirm.

Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly about wheat-free soy sauce and cross-contact before booking.

bio.background: A tatami-and-horigotatsu (sunken kotatsu) style okonomiyaki and monjayaki izakaya about a one-minute walk from the west exit of Suidobashi Station, geared toward group parties, birthday celebrations, and women's get-togethers with private-room bookings for 36-48 guests.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki topped with yakisoba noodles, and Osaka-style beef tendon and scallion (gyusuji negiyaki) okonomiyaki, alongside an Italian-style cheese-cream monjayaki.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter dashi from katsuobushi (bonito flakes) sourced from Toyosu Market, treating that stock as the foundation of its cooking.

anecdotes.text: For birthday guests, the restaurant presents a heart-shaped okonomiyaki as a surprise gift.

bio.background: eat more SOUP&BREAD is a bakery-café concept operated by Big Eats Co., Ltd. (株式会社ビッグイーツ), which also runs the plant-forward "eat more greens" café in Azabu-Juban. This branch occupies the 7th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly connected to Shinjuku Station's east side.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked bread paired with slow-simmered soups and stews — roughly 20 rotating varieties made from seasonal ingredients, including beef stew, pumpkin potage, lobster bisque, and a cheese-and-onion gratin soup.
bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself as a warm, "cute" bakery café meant to be comforting for both body and mind, built around the simple idea of baking bread fresh in-house every day and pairing it with hearty, seasonal soup.

roles: 店主(オーナー・ラーメン職人) Ishijima Tetsuya（石島鉄也）
bio.background: Owner-chef Ishijima Tetsuya worked as a bartender from his teens into his early twenties before moving into the ramen world, where he trained for roughly a decade at shops around Tokyo. He then spent about two years developing his own wagyu-paitan (beef-bone white broth) recipe, testing it in borrowed kitchens before opening this shop on Miyamasuzaka on April 24, 2025. The space itself was converted from a former izakaya into a compact, roughly 12-tsubo counter shop.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Ishijima set out to build a ramen shop that doesn't look or feel like a typical ramen-ya — a bright, cafe-like, wood-toned room meant to be comfortable for solo women and families alike — while keeping an eye toward eventually introducing the shop's rich wagyu broth to diners from overseas.
bio.specialty: 極上和牛らーめん (Gokujō Wagyu Ramen) — a rich wagyu bone paitan-shio broth topped with wagyu beef chashu cut from three different parts of the cow.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened April 24, 2025 on Miyamasuzaka, a street the neighborhood is positioning as a developing 'ramen street'; the roughly 12-tsubo space was converted from a former izakaya into a 16-seat counter shop with a street-facing window counter.
Ishijima worked as a bartender in his teens and early twenties, then spent about ten years training at ramen shops in Tokyo before spending two years developing his own wagyu white-broth recipe.

caveats: This environment's network proxy blocked direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, the official site (wagyuramengoku.com), and most secondary listing sites (Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Retty, Hitosara, Yahoo Map, Instagram, several blogs) — all returned 403 at the connection level, a broad proxy policy issue affecting many unrelated domains, not specific to this restaurant. All facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages (primarily Shibuya Keizai Shimbun / shibukei.com, Yahoo News, and ramendb.supleks.jp), not from directly fetched page text, so treat granular details (exact seat layout, exact chef-name kanji) as reasonably but not maximally confident. Cash-only status was not stated anywhere and is assumed non-cash-only by default for a 2025-opened Shibuya shop; this is unconfirmed.

cuisine: 和牛豚骨塩ラーメン・つけ麺 (wagyu beef-bone paitan shio ramen and tsukemen)

Not recommended for celiac diners.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷1-6-4 The Neat青山 1F

bio.background: Maguro to Shari is a tuna-bowl specialty shop that opened in Shibuya on October 26, 2021, supervised by the acclaimed Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku. It sources natural wild bluefin tuna through a professional network working with the Sumiyoshi Fisheries Group, choosing fish raised in cold waters for high fat content and rich flavor.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (opened 2021, supervised by Ginza's Hakkoku sushi restaurant)
bio.specialty: Maguro-Shari-Don — a customizable tuna and red-vinegar-rice bowl where diners choose the tuna and rice portions in gram increments, from a standard size up to an extra-large 'Kiwami' portion, with toppings such as sea urchin and salmon roe available.
bio.philosophy: Built around two core elements only — natural tuna and red-vinegar shari rice — letting the quality of the fish and the acidity of the rice carry the dish rather than heavy sauces or many toppings.
roles: supervised by Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku

anecdotes.text: The restaurant grand-opened in Shibuya on October 26, 2021 as a tuna-bowl specialty shop supervised by the Ginza sushi restaurant Hakkoku, using only natural wild tuna raised in cold ocean waters for higher fat content and umami.
Diners can build their own bowl, choosing tuna and rice quantities in 40g and 50g increments respectively, from a standard bowl (130g tuna, 250g rice) up to a 'Kiwami' extreme-size portion.

caveats: Dietary information (gluten-free, vegan, allergy handling) is not documented in any source found; this is a tuna-rice-bowl specialty restaurant with no evident vegan or celiac-specific accommodations. Seat count (7-8) varies slightly between secondary sources; Tabelog listing itself could not be directly fetched (403), so identity and details are corroborated via Gurunavi/RESTA/press and blog sources plus search-result excerpts of Tabelog. Coordinates are not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng null); address places it in Shibuya 1-chome, roughly 4-5 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station per multiple sources, well within the target radius of Shibuya Scramble.

cuisine: 海鮮丼 (Seafood rice bowl — tuna bowl specialty)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, English-menu-friendly bowl counter aimed at casual solo diners and tourists near Shibuya Station, but with no specific gluten-free or vegan accommodations documented, so allergy communication would need to happen in Japanese or with translation help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng set to null. Address (Shibuya 1-6-4, The Neat Aoyama building) and multiple independent sources describe the restaurant as roughly a 4-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi information is documented anywhere in the available sources. The core dish (tuna over vinegared rice) is likely soy-sauce marinated (shoyu typically contains wheat) and no allergy-specific menu or staff protocol was found. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without directly confirming with staff in Japanese.

The restaurant's entire concept is centered on raw/marinated tuna over vinegared rice, so it is not vegan-compatible as currently described, and no vegetarian substitution was mentioned in any source.

lead: Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. Shibuya Ten
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-18-6

bio.origin: Brand founded in Ikebukuro, Tokyo in 2010; this location is the Shibuya branch
bio.background: The brand began as a catchphrase for founder Mibu Hirofumi's first shop, "Ikebukuro Mibu," opened in 2010. It grew into the "Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka." ("Why Do You Put Chili Oil in Soba?") chain, now with roughly 15 locations around Tokyo (including Shinjuku, Akihabara, and Shinbashi) under the company Nomimono Co., Ltd. The concept was conceived as a reaction to the decline of classic soba culture, aiming to reclaim the mid-price dining niche (roughly 500-1000 yen) that ramen had taken over, using a hearty, "junk-food" style meat soba.
bio.specialty: 肉そば (meat tsukesoba / dipping soba) — thick, chewy house-made noodles served with a slightly sweet-savory dipping broth spiked with the shop's own chili oil (rayu)
bio.philosophy: "蕎麦をもっと自由に楽しく" ("Make soba more free and fun") — breaking from traditional soba conventions to build a bold, modern "soba culture."
roles: Founder, Ikebukuro Mibu / Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. chain

anecdotes.text: The provocative shop name itself doubles as an advertisement — its odd phrasing ("Why do you put chili oil in soba?") sparks curiosity and has become a talking point that drives customers to try it.

The Shibuya branch sits along the main street beside Shibuya Stream, about a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station's C2 exit, and is known for very generous noodle portions aimed at office workers looking for a filling, fast meal.

caveats: This is a casual chili-oil tsukesoba chain shop with no vegan or gluten-free program found in any source — it does not fit the app's GF/vegan-focus criteria and is included here only as a verification record. Its address (Shibuya 3-18-6, beside Shibuya Stream, ~5 min walk from Shibuya Station) differs from the "神泉, 8-min walk" description in the task brief, but it still falls well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target point. Tabelog and the official Nomimono brand page could not be directly fetched (blocked by bot protection); details below are reconstructed from search-engine snippets of Tabelog, the official Nomimono shop page, NAVITIME, So-ba Database, and a Mynavi News feature, so some specifics (exact Sunday closing time, cash policy) carry lower certainty.
closed_days: No regular closed day found in available sources (details unconfirmed — could not verify directly with the restaurant).
cuisine: そば（辣油つけそば／肉そば） — chili-oil dipping soba, meat soba
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local chain soba shop near Shibuya Station; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu/photos will help when ordering.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions. Address 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-18-6 is beside Shibuya Stream on the main street south of Shibuya Station, roughly a 5-minute walk (~350-400m) from the Shibuya Scramble crossing — well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, 十割 (100% buckwheat) option, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source. The noodles are described as thick, chewy, house-made soba but not confirmed as 100% buckwheat, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a traditional soy-sauce-based broth, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. There is no mention of allergy protocols or a separate gluten-free process, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 昼の部 11:00〜16:00、夜の部 17:30〜21:30（日曜日は21:00まで、第1・第3日曜日は設備点検のため20:00閉店との情報あり）
name_en: Naze Soba ni Rayu wo Ireru no ka. Shibuya Ten

The chain's signature and most-promoted dishes are meat-based (肉そば/meat soba, chicken soba), and the tsuyu dipping sauce is a traditional soy-based broth that in Japan is typically made with bonito (katsuo) dashi. Treat as not vegan-friendly absent direct confirmation from staff.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿4-1-9 新宿ユースビル(PAX) B1階 (〒160-0022)

roles: Operated by Total Well Co., Ltd., a Shinjuku-based restaurant group
bio.background: PIZZERIA CAPOLI opened in March 2011 in the basement of the Shinjuku Youth Building (PAX), a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south exit. It is a certified member of the Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN, the "True Neapolitan Pizza Association"), the Naples-based body that authenticates pizzerias following strict Neapolitan pizza-making standards.
bio.origin: Naples-style Neapolitan pizza tradition, adapted in Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza — the classic Margherita (tomato, mozzarella, basil) and the cheese-free Marinara (tomato, garlic, oregano, olive oil), plus a house Americana topped with capers, corn, pepperoni, paprika, red onion and cherry tomatoes. Most pizzas can be ordered half-and-half so diners can try two varieties at once.
bio.philosophy: Follows AVPN's traditional Neapolitan pizza discipline: the wood-fired oven was hand-built by Stefano Ferrara, a master oven craftsman from Naples, using stone and sand shipped in from Naples itself. The kitchen uses imported Italian Caputo flour and adjusts dough hydration, water temperature and fermentation time daily to match Tokyo's changing temperature and humidity.

anecdotes.text: The pizza oven was personally built by Stefano Ferrara, known as a master craftsman among Naples oven-makers, using materials specially transported from Naples.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (synthesized web search results referencing multiple Japanese food-blog listings for PIZZERIA CAPOLI)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant recently switched to Caputo flour from Italy, which it credits with improving both the flavor and texture of its dough, and adjusts ingredients daily based on temperature and humidity.
Listed as an AVPN Japan (真のナポリピッツァ協会) certified pizzeria, with contact capoli@totalwell.co.jp.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official company site, or most blog pages — the environment's outbound proxy returned 403/policy-denial for nearly every Japanese restaurant-review domain (tabelog.com, r.gnavi.co.jp, hotpepper.jp direct fetch, 4travel.jp, ameblo.jp, ikkos-films.com, etc.). All facts below were reconstructed from WebSearch's synthesized snippets (which do quote/cite the underlying pages) rather than direct WebFetch page reads, so treat address/hours/seat-count details as high-confidence-but-secondhand. No gluten-free crust or dedicated allergy menu was found anywhere; this is a traditional wheat-based Neapolitan pizzeria, not celiac-safe. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (geocoding blocked); address confirms 1-minute walk from Shinjuku Station south exit, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
closed_days: Open almost every day; closed only for the year-end/New Year holiday period.
cuisine: Italian — wood-fired Neapolitan pizza
cultural_comfort_note: Menu and ordering (via tablet) are primarily Japanese-oriented with an English Tabelog listing for reservations, but no evidence of an English-speaking staff or English allergy menu, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help when asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: This is a traditional AVPN-certified Neapolitan pizzeria using imported Italian Caputo wheat flour for its dough; no gluten-free crust, gluten-free menu, or celiac-specific handling was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri (and days before public holidays): 11:00-15:00, 17:00-22:30 | Sat: 11:00-15:30, 16:00-22:30 | Sun & public holidays: 11:00-15:30, 16:00-22:00. A 330 yen (tax incl.) dinner table charge applies with a 90-minute seating limit.
name_ja: ピッツェリア カポリ
neighborhood: Shinjuku (south exit, Shinjuku Station)
seats_text: Around 66 seats, including sofa seating; roughly half are reservable and half are first-come, first-served.
vegan_detail: The classic Marinara pizza (tomato sauce, garlic, oregano, olive oil — no cheese) is vegan by standard Neapolitan recipe, and it is on the menu here alongside the Margherita. Beyond that, no dedicated vegan menu, dairy-free cheese substitute, or vegan labeling was found; the kitchen and wood oven are shared with meat and dairy-topped pizzas (e.g., pepperoni, mozzarella), so cross-contact is possible and unconfirmed. Best to double-check ingredients with staff in person.

lead: ベジ郎 渋谷総本店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-23-11 1階

bio.origin: Founded by Food Supply Co., a produce (vegetable) wholesaler run by president Atsushi Takekawa (born 1979, Tottori), who founded the company in 2009.
bio.background: During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, Takekawa launched a contact-free 'drive-through vegetable stand' to sell produce directly to consumers after many of the company's restaurant clients closed and growers lost their usual buyers. That effort became the foundation for Vejiro, a meat-and-vegetable stir-fry restaurant designed to move large volumes of fresh vegetables and support the producers who had been left with unsold crops. The Shibuya main store (総本店) opened near Bunkamura in Dogenzaka in December 2021 and quickly became one of Shibuya's most talked-about lunch/dinner spots, known for long lines.
bio.specialty: 肉野菜炒め定食 (meat & vegetable stir-fry set meal), Jiro-ramen-inspired: a mountain of stir-fried vegetables (customers can order it 'mashi-mashi', with reports of roughly 400g of vegetables per plate) tossed with sliced pork or fried chicken, seasoned with a choice of soy sauce, miso, ponzu, or salt-tonkotsu, and topped with an adjustable amount of pork back fat.
bio.philosophy: Turn a produce wholesaler's surplus-vegetable problem into a restaurant concept — build a dish that lets one diner eat a very large quantity of vegetables in a single sitting, giving growers a reliable outlet for their harvest.
roles: Restaurant brand of Food Supply Co., a Tokyo-based vegetable wholesaler

anecdotes.text: A single plate is built around roughly 400g of stir-fried vegetables — described as close to a full day's recommended vegetable intake in one meal.
The restaurant grew directly out of Takekawa's pandemic-era 'drive-through vegetable shop,' created after Food Supply's restaurant clients shut down and growers lost their delivery destinations.

caveats: This is a Jiro-ramen-inspired meat-and-vegetable stir-fry specialist, not a restaurant built around vegan or gluten-free dining. It does offer a vegetables-only (no meat) order, but the wok, cooking fat, and flavoring sauces (shoyu/miso/ponzu) are shared with meat dishes and no source confirms wheat-free soy sauce, allergen-safe frying, or animal-free cooking fat for the vegetable-only option — diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm directly with staff before ordering.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); sources differ on whether it is effectively open daily — no fixed weekly closed day could be confirmed.
cuisine: 肉野菜炒め（Meat & vegetable stir-fry, Jiro-inspired teishoku）
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering happens via a Japanese-language customization system (rice, sauce, vegetable/fat quantity) at a small, often-crowded counter, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board helps.
gf_detail: No source confirms gluten-free practices. The flavor choices include soy sauce (醤油) and miso based sauces, which typically contain wheat in Japan; fried chicken (唐揚げ) is used as a meat option and its coating ingredients were not confirmed. No information found on a dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce (tamari), or cross-contact precautions. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about the shio-tonkotsu (salt) option and sauce ingredients before ordering.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:10 (L.O. 21:40), daily, with irregular (不定休) closures
name_en: Vejiro Shibuya Sohonten (Niku-Yasai Itame Vejiro Shibuya Main Store)
name_ja: 肉野菜炒め ベジ郎 渋谷総本店
neighborhood: 道玄坂（Dōgenzaka）, near Bunkamura, 2 min walk from Shinsen Station, Shibuya

positives.text: The menu includes a vegetables-only order (no meat), for diners who want a plant-forward version of the stir-fry.
The restaurant was created specifically to serve unusually large portions of fresh vegetables per plate, sourced through the operator's own produce-wholesale business.

seats_text: 14 seats (10 counter, 4 table)
vegan_detail: A "vegetables only" (野菜だけ) order without meat is available, but this is a stir-fry restaurant built around pork/chicken and adjustable pork back fat (背脂); no source confirms the vegetable-only dish is cooked without animal fat, or that the shoyu/miso/ponzu sauces are free of bonito (katsuo) dashi. Treat as a possible option to request and confirm with staff, not a guaranteed vegan dish.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷1-11-3 第一小山ビル1F

bio.background: goo ITALIANO is an Italian restaurant group run by Tokyo hospitality company Take Five (株式会社テイクファイブ), with this Shibuya location as its original/flagship (本店) branch, near Miyamasuzaka a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's east exit.
bio.specialty: Spaghettoni al limone (lemon cream spaghettoni made with Setouchi lemons, a play on a Sicilian classic) and pasta made from Gragnano-imported durum wheat, slow-dried at low temperature for a chewy texture and wheat aroma.
bio.philosophy: Built around a "local deliciousness from Italy's 20 regions" concept: the menu is shaped by dishes and ingredients the chef encountered while traveling around Italy, and it is updated whenever something new and impressive is found, with the goal of keeping regional Italian cooking casual, approachable and affordable enough for everyday visits.
roles: Italian regional-cuisine restaurant, part of a small multi-location group (also operates a Yoyogi-Uehara branch)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's food philosophy is to serve only what genuinely surprised or moved the team during trips through Italy, refreshing the menu as new discoveries are made.
The name "goo ITALIANO" is a coined term blending "GOOD Italian" with the growl ("goo") of a stomach craving delicious food.

caveats: No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu, certification, or cross-contamination protocol could be confirmed from any available source (Tabelog page itself could not be fetched directly due to a network block, so this record relies on secondary listings/aggregators plus the operator's brand page). This is a mainstream regional-Italian restaurant, not a GF/vegan specialist; the pasta is traditional wheat pasta imported from Gragnano, Italy. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-6418-8300) and confirm directly, as no allergy-handling or hidden-animal-ingredient information was found in sourced material.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day found; open daily per available listings. Year-end/New Year hours vary and are described as "please inquire."
cuisine: Italian (regional Italian trattoria-style cooking; imported Gragnano pasta, Neapolitan buffalo mozzarella, Ligurian-style pizza); no dedicated vegetarian/vegan menu confirmed
cultural_comfort_note: This is a Japanese-run neighborhood Italian restaurant with an English Tabelog listing and online table booking, but expect Japanese-language menus and staff — basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, especially for allergy questions.
geocode_note: Address (東京都渋谷区渋谷1-11-3) and multiple listings place the restaurant near Miyamasuzaka, about a 5-minute walk from JR Shibuya Station's east exit / Hanzomon-Fukutoshin line exits — well within 0.8 km of Shibuya Scramble (35.6595, 139.7005). Coordinates left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should resolve from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The signature pastas are explicitly traditional wheat pasta imported from Gragnano, Italy, so celiac diners should not assume any gluten-free accommodation and should call ahead to confirm before visiting.
hours_raw: Weekdays and weekends alike: Lunch 11:30-15:00, Dinner 17:00-23:00 (last order 22:00)
neighborhood: 宮益坂, 渋谷
seats_text: Approximately 40 seats (counter, sofa, and table seating); available for private party bookings of roughly 40-50 people.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegetarian or vegan menu was confirmed in any sourced listing. The restaurant serves vegetable-forward Italian dishes (salads, vegetable antipasti) as part of its regional menu, but sauces and dishes may contain meat, seafood (e.g., anchovy), or dairy typical of Italian cooking, and no plant-based substitution policy or hidden-ingredient disclosure was found. Vegan diners should confirm specific dishes with staff before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-16-3 第18フジビル2F

bio.background: Niku Bistro Akari opened in the backstreets of Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome as a new meat-and-wine concept from Zekkocho (株式会社絶好調), the Shinjuku izakaya group led by Masaki Yoshida (of Zekkocho Teppen), built around pairing whole block meat seared over charcoal with a locked, lantern-lit wine cellar guests browse themselves.
bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Block meat -- beef, pork, lamb, and game such as venison and wild boar -- grilled whole over binchotan charcoal and served rare and juicy, alongside a hand-picked cellar of about 150 wines.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing famous meat brands, the kitchen selects cuts by season and quality, rotating 6-8 different kinds of block meat daily and searing them hard over binchotan charcoal so the centers stay rare and juicy.

anecdotes.text: Diners choose their own bottle by stepping into a key-locked, lantern-lit wine cellar stocked with roughly 150 red, white, and sparkling wines.

The kitchen rotates 6-8 kinds of block meat a day -- from standard beef, pork, and lamb to game like venison and wild boar -- chosen for seasonality and quality rather than brand name, then seared hard over binchotan charcoal.
Meats such as Kumamoto 'aka-gyu' beef and Iwate pork are grilled over roaring binchotan charcoal and brought to the table as showstopping platters.
The restaurant opened on July 7 in Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome as a new bone-in-meat-and-wine concept from Zekkocho Teppen founder Masaki Yoshida.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found anywhere in official or third-party sources -- this is a meat-and-wine bistro whose menu includes gluten-containing items (pasta, pâté, aligot) alongside charcoal-grilled block meat. Celiac and vegan diners should call ahead (050-5592-1598) before booking.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly closed day)
cuisine: 塊肉ビストロ / 赤ワイン充実 -- bistro specializing in whole block meat (beef, pork, lamb, game) grilled over binchotan charcoal, paired with a ~150-bottle wine cellar
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant is listed on Tabelog's English site and on English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck), but no dedicated English menu or English-speaking-staff guarantee was confirmed, so basic Japanese or gesture-based ordering should be expected.
geocode_note: Address is Nishi-Shinjuku 7-16-3, described in multiple listings as about a 3-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west-exit D5 exit, placing it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station (35.6896, 139.7006). Do not geocode here; lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol is advertised anywhere. The menu is meat-forward (charcoal-grilled block meat can be naturally simple) but also includes clearly wheat-containing items such as pasta ("シェフのパスタ"), pâté de campagne, and aligot, and courses are often served as fixed sets, so gluten exposure is plausible unless specifically arranged. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead to confirm which grilled-meat items and sauces are wheat-free.
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station / Shinjuku Station west exit
seats_text: Counter 18, tables 32, private room 8 (total 58)
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused charcoal-grill bistro with no vegan menu, plant-based mains, or vegan accommodations advertised in any source found. Sides such as green salad or French fries may be vegetable-based but are not confirmed dairy/animal-broth-free, and the concept centers entirely on block meat and wine.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-15-1 テルイチビル2F

bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.background: The owner has been devoted to yakitori craft for 39 years, with this shop operating at its Shibuya, Meiji-dori-area location for 20 years.
bio.specialty: Shio (salt-seasoned) charcoal-grilled yakitori, made with brand chicken sourced from Tottori (Takumi no Daiyama Chicken and Bisakura Chicken)
bio.philosophy: The owner personally grills every skewer over charcoal each day, favoring careful, attentive preparation over volume.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the shio (salt) seasoning is especially well executed, with several preferring it to the tare (sauce) skewers, and praise the tenderness of items like liver, seseri (neck meat), and gizzard.

caveats: This is a traditional, independently-run yakitori-ya with no confirmed gluten-free or vegan menu. Shio (salt) skewers avoid the wheat-containing tare sauce, but all skewers are grilled on the same shared charcoal grill, so cross-contact with tare-basted items is likely. The menu is chicken-centric (liver, gizzard, seseri, asparagus-wrapped pork, etc.); vegetable side dishes are not confirmed to be free of bonito/katsuo dashi. Diners with celiac disease, wheat allergy, or who are vegan should call ahead (03-3400-0011) and confirm directly with staff before visiting. Some sources list the building as "テルイチビル" (Teruichi Bldg.) and others as "F93Shibuya1," likely reflecting a building rename.
closed_days: Sundays
cuisine: Yakitori (charcoal-grilled chicken skewers) / Izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local yakitori-ya with no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) helps when ordering skewers and specifying shio (salt) vs. tare (sauce).
gf_detail: Skewers are grilled to order over charcoal; ordering shio (salt) seasoning avoids the soy-sauce-based tare, which typically contains wheat. However, no dedicated gluten-free menu, prep area, or fryer has been identified, and shio and tare skewers appear to be cooked on the same shared grill, creating a likely cross-contact risk. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm directly with staff.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町17-10 吉野ビル1F

bio.background: Chuka Soba Sakurazaka opened on September 18, 2004, on Sakura-zaka, a small cherry-tree-lined slope in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka-cho. The shop takes its name from that street. It has kept its 14-seat counter running for over two decades in a corner of Shibuya where restaurants typically turn over quickly.
bio.specialty: Chuka soba and tsukemen (dipping noodles) built on a rich pork-bone and seafood broth (gyokai-tonkotsu) with a creamy, milky body and a pronounced bonito (katsuo) aroma; a shio (salt) soba is also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 2004 and still operating from the same small counter on Sakura-zaka in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka-cho, an area known for how quickly local businesses come and go.

A 2025 visit review describes the shop's weathered wooden facade and a thick, well-worn natural-wood counter inside, and notes the tsukemen broth's sardine (niboshi) and seafood notes over springy medium-thick noodles.

caveats: Payment method could not be independently confirmed on an official page (site uses a ticket-vending-machine ordering system typical of old-school Tokyo ramen counters, which strongly suggests cash/IC-card only, but this was not explicitly stated by a primary source); marked cash_only as a best estimate. Exact closing time varies slightly across aggregator listings (values seen range from 21:00 to 22:00/23:00 with last order ~20:30-21:50); used the most consistent figure (11:00-21:00, closed Sunday) across the most recent listings. No official restaurant website could be located. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere; this is a traditional pork-bone-and-seafood ramen shop with no documented dietary accommodations.

lead: ヴィーガンカフェ モンキーマジック
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-27-16 お宿松むら 1階（郵便番号 111-0032）

bio.background: Saeki graduated from Dokkyo University's Faculty of Foreign Languages (English Department) and worked in administrative roles before turning toward food around 2005. In 2012 she co-opened a shizen-saibai (naturally-cultivated, pesticide- and fertilizer-free) vegetable restaurant, and in January 2020 she opened Vegan Cafe Monkey Magic in a quiet alley behind Hanayashiki in Asakusa, on the ground floor of a small inn called Oyado Matsumura.
bio.philosophy: Saeki was vegan herself for several years but stepped back from strict veganism for health reasons; she says she now channels that firsthand experience into cooking she describes as 'vegan food that nourishes the heart,' built entirely on naturally cultivated vegetables and natural fermentation seasonings with no additives.
bio.specialty: A monthly-changing, multi-course tasting menu (including a seasonal vegan take on sushi) built around 100% naturally cultivated vegetables and house-made fermented seasonings.

anecdotes.text: Once vegan herself, Saeki quit strict veganism for health reasons and has used that perspective to create what she calls 'vegan food that nourishes the heart' rather than just the body.
The chef performs in costume as the restaurant's monkey mascot, 'Sarutchaman,' greeting and entertaining guests table-side as part of the dining experience.

caveats: Direct site/Tabelog fetches were blocked in this environment (403s), so hours and safety details are triangulated from multiple independent search-indexed sources (blogs, Hachidory, Will Fly for Food, Novacircle, HappyCow) rather than a single primary page; reported hours/days have varied across sources over the restaurant's history, so confirm the current schedule before booking. Payment method (cash-only or not) could not be confirmed. This is a fully vegan kitchen, not a dedicated gluten-free one — flag any wheat/gluten concerns when reserving.

cuisine: Vegan / naturally-cultivated (shizen-saibai) organic vegetables, Japanese-style tasting and kaiseki-style comfort food, vegan izakaya

Celiac diners should communicate the severity of their needs clearly before booking and treat any assurance as best-effort rather than certified.

Thursday dinner 19:00-22:00; Friday & Saturday lunch 12:00-14:30 and dinner 19:00-22:00, served as fixed course menus (e.g. a ¥4,000 Friday lunch course and a ¥6,000 Saturday course). lead: フルール ド サラザン
address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草2-14-2 インパレス1 1F

bio.chef_name: Owner (referred to as Mr. Tamakoshi in press coverage) with his wife
bio.origin: Japan (owners spent several years living and working in France before returning)
bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who spent years living and working in France, absorbing French galette-making technique and Japanese-craft-cider culture. The owner additionally trained at a soba restaurant in Kanda to deepen his grounding in Japanese buckwheat craft before opening the shop in Nishi-Asakusa on April 10, 2019.
bio.specialty: Galette made from 100% domestically grown buckwheat, hand-selected and stone-milled fresh in-house every morning; the finished galette is pre-rolled and cut into bite-sized pieces so it can be eaten smartly with chopsticks, Edo-style, and is served with a thickened soba dipping sauce instead of the usual knife-and-fork Breton presentation.
bio.philosophy: To bridge French galette culture and Japanese soba culture by sourcing buckwheat grain directly from domestic producers and milling it on-site each morning, so guests taste the grain's full aroma — served in a distinctly Japanese, chopstick-friendly format alongside a curated list of 30-40+ Japanese domestic craft ciders.

anecdotes.text: Unlike a typical galetterie where the crepe is eaten with a knife and fork, here it arrives pre-rolled and sliced into bite-sized pieces so it can be picked up with chopsticks, paired with a thickened soba dipping sauce rather than a simple cheese-and-ham filling.

A traveler with celiac disease recalled the owner bringing out raw buckwheat grain and demonstrating the in-house stone mill, then walking her through exactly which galettes and sauces were safe to order.

caveats: Closed days are irregular and announced via Instagram/Facebook rather than a fixed weekly schedule; on Sundays/holidays the dinner service can end early once the day's buckwheat dough runs out. This is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen — the galette batter itself is 100% buckwheat, but some fillings and the traditional dipping sauce may involve wheat-containing soy sauce, so celiac diners must flag their allergy and confirm each dish. No dedicated vegan menu was confirmed; sauces may use traditional dashi. Seat count and exact payment methods were not confirmed from available sources.

cuisine: Gluten-free buckwheat galettes (galette de sarrasin) paired with Japanese domestic craft cider
cultural_comfort_note: A small, Japanese-run galette shop with a Japanese-only official site, but it appears on multiple English-language gluten-free travel guides and reviewers with celiac disease report staff patiently walking them through safe menu choices.

However, reviewers confirm this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility: some fillings and the traditional dipping sauce can involve soy sauce or other ingredients containing wheat. Staff (the French-trained owner in particular) are described as knowledgeable and willing to identify safe dishes once a gluten allergy or celiac disease is disclosed, but diners must ask rather than assume every item is safe.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-17-4（アクセス7ビル）

bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (Sanuki udon heritage)
bio.background: Kagawa Ippuku ART is a creative-udon concept shop opened May 9, 2025, by Kagawa Ippuku, a Sanuki udon specialist that has earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand three years running. ART is positioned as a new 'brand' from the same company, built around the idea of reconstructing traditional Japanese udon through modern technique and outside culinary collaborations, in a retro interior mixing wood, tile, and blue-painted panels with 12 counter seats.
bio.specialty: Rotating creative udon collaborations — e.g. a beef/meat udon supervised by the Ginza restaurant Shinohara, and a French-curry udon using curry from the Michelin-starred Kitajima-tei — alongside standards like green curry udon and pork-kimchi chili-oil udon. The kitchen alternates noodle thickness by calendar day (thick noodles on even-numbered days, thin noodles on odd-numbered days).
bio.philosophy: The shop frames 'ART' as a double meaning (French and English) for udon's capacity to take on many different expressions — reworking a traditional Japanese noodle through modern, cross-cultural technique rather than treating Sanuki udon as fixed or purely traditional.
roles: Udon specialty restaurant, new creative-concept branch of Kagawa Ippuku

anecdotes.text: Menu items are developed with outside supervising chefs, including a beef udon overseen by Ginza's Shinohara and a French curry udon using curry from the Michelin-starred Kitajima-tei.
Noodle thickness changes with the calendar: thick noodles are served on even-numbered days and thin noodles on odd-numbered days.

caveats: This is a wheat-udon specialty shop, not a gluten-free or vegan venue — the noodles are the core wheat product and the menu is built around meat toppings (pork, beef, chicken) and traditional Sanuki-style dashi. No gluten-free noodle alternative, dedicated allergen-free fryer, or vegan-labeled dish was found in any source reviewed. Recommend only to omnivore diners; not appropriate for celiac or strict vegan guests.

A visitor blog described trying the shop's tsukemen (dipping noodles) alongside the standard ramen.
A ramen guide notes the owner lacks the intimidating manner often associated with Jiro-style shops, making it easier for first-time customers to order comfortably.

bio.background: Opened in Kabukicho, Shinjuku in the late 1980s (one source cites 1985, another 1987) by an owner who fell in love with the taste of Thai food while traveling in Thailand, at a time when Tokyo had only two or three dedicated Thai restaurants. Nearly 40 years later it remains a well-known fixture of Shinjuku's Thai-dining scene and was selected for Tabelog's 2024 "Tabelog 100" list in the Asian/Ethnic cuisine category.
bio.specialty: Tom Kha Gai (coconut-chicken soup) and Larb Gai (spicy minced chicken salad) are frequently singled out by reviewers; Pad Thai is also a long-standing menu staple.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids premade curry/seasoning pastes, mixing spices fresh for each dish, aiming for a clear, assertive sweet-spicy-sour "Bangkok flavor." The owner still visits regularly to taste-check dishes so the flavor stays consistent.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was founded after the owner fell in love with Thai food while traveling in Thailand and wanted to share that experience, opening at a time when there were only two or three specialist Thai restaurants in all of Tokyo.
The owner still comes by regularly to check the taste, a routine credited with keeping the flavor unchanged for close to 40 years.
The interior is furnished with Thai-made furniture and tableware so diners feel transported to Thailand while still in Japan.

caveats: This spot's Tabelog URL and name match the brief exactly, but the confirmed address (Kabukicho 1-23-14, near JR Shinjuku Station's east exit / Seibu-Shinjuku front entrance) is on the opposite side of the station from the "西新宿 / 新宿西口駅" area named in the brief — it still falls well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station reference point, so it is retained, but the neighborhood label has been corrected to reflect the verified address. seats_text: Roughly 110 seats, spread across sofa seating and table seating.
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu is advertised by the restaurant or confirmed by any source I could access (this contrasts with the brief's cuisine tag). The menu does include vegetable and tofu-based dishes (e.g., stir-fried Thai vegetables in oyster sauce, tofu-and-minced-meat soup), but traditional Thai seasoning relies heavily on fish sauce (nam pla), which is very likely present in most dishes by default, including many that look plant-based, unless the kitchen is specifically asked to omit it. Vegan diners should ask staff before ordering rather than assume any dish is animal-product-free.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-6 和光ビル1F

bio.background: The owner trained at Yokohama Ramen Machida-ya (町田家) in Machida, a disciple shop of the historic green-signboard Iekei ramen lineage founded by Takasago-ya (たかさご家) — a branch of the Iekei family tree separate from the main Yoshimura-ya (吉村家) line. The lineage runs Takasago-ya → Machida-ya (Machida honten) → Samurai (Shibuya). The owner has roughly 20 years of experience in Iekei ramen, including his apprenticeship years.
bio.origin: Yokohama Iekei ramen, Takasago-ya green-signboard lineage via Machida-ya
bio.specialty: 'Gattun'-style concentrated tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: thick, wavy noodles from Sakai Seimen in a rich pork-bone broth finished with chicken oil, topped with large-cut pork chashu, spinach and nori
bio.philosophy: Continuing the distinctive green-signboard Takasago-ya style inherited through Machida-ya, distinct from the mainline Yoshimura-ya branch of Iekei ramen

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated in August 2021 to its current spot on Dogenzaka near Shibuya Station, continuing to fly the green signboard associated with the Takasago-ya lineage of Iekei ramen.

caveats: Standard Iekei tonkotsu-shoyu ramen shop with no vegan or gluten-free accommodations found in any source; wheat noodles and pork-bone broth are used in every bowl. Cash only. No official English-language site was found — information is compiled from Japanese listing/review sites (Tabelog, ramen databases, food blogs) since Tabelog itself returned 403 to direct fetch and was corroborated via search.
closed_days: 不定休（曜日を問わない臨時休業あり、固定の定休日なし）
cuisine: ラーメン（横浜家系ラーメン）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Iekei ramen counter where customers are typically asked (in Japanese) for their preferred noodle firmness, broth richness and oil amount; no English menu was confirmed in any source.
geocode_note: Address (道玄坂2-6-6, Dogenzaka) corroborated across multiple independent Japanese listing sites (search results referencing Navitime, Mapion, Yahoo Map); Dogenzaka 2-chome is immediately adjacent to Shibuya Station, well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble. lat/lng left null per instructions — network geocoding disabled in this environment.
gf_detail: Only standard thick, wavy wheat noodles (from Sakai Seimen) are offered — no gluten-free noodle substitute is mentioned anywhere. The tonkotsu-shoyu tare is soy-sauce based and virtually certain to contain wheat. No source mentions a dedicated fryer, celiac-safe handling, or any gluten-free accommodation; this is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop and should be treated as unsafe for celiac/gluten-free diners.
name_en: Yokohama Iekei Ramen Samurai Shibuya Honten
name_ja: 横浜家系らーめん侍 渋谷本店
neighborhood: 道玄坂／渋谷駅圏内
seats_text: Inverted-L counter with 14 seats plus two 2-person tables at the back (approx. 18 seats total)
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a tonkotsu (pork bone) broth blended with chicken oil in the shop's signature 'gattun' style and finished with pork chashu; no plant-based broth, protein substitute, or vegetarian/vegan option is offered or mentioned in any review or listing.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-21-9 田島ビル1F

bio.background: Stand Hero opened in October 2006, run by owner Katsuji Inoue. Inoue trained for roughly a decade in traditional Japanese kappo cuisine before working at the izakaya group Shofudo (a seafood wholesaler that also ran several izakaya), where he helped launch multiple stores, and then went independent to open his own standing bar near Suidobashi Station.
bio.specialty: Thick-cut ham cutlet (atsugiri hamu-katsu, sliced 2-3cm thick) and a daily-changing sashimi selection sourced fresh each morning.
bio.philosophy: Each day Inoue visits seven or eight fish wholesalers himself and buys whatever looks best that morning, then chalks roughly 40 sashimi items on the blackboard at that day's market price (ranging from about 50 to over 600 yen a piece), so the menu and prices genuinely change day to day.

anecdotes.text: The thick-cut ham cutlet became well known enough to be featured on the TV program Tamori Club.
Squeezed into just 9.5 tsubo (about 31 square meters), the standing bar has been running for more than seventeen years and sees over 80 customers a day.

The owner tours 7-8 seafood suppliers every morning and buys what's freshest, resulting in around 40 sashimi items priced from roughly 50 to 600-plus yen written on a blackboard menu that changes daily; a 3-piece sashimi platter is offered Saturdays only.

caveats: Not a vegan- or gluten-free-oriented spot: the menu is dominated by sashimi, fried kushikatsu, and the signature breaded ham cutlet, with vegetables described as only about 20% of the menu. Kushikatsu-style fried items are wheat-battered and no dedicated gluten-free or vegan options, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodations were found in any source. Cash only, no reservations, standing-room only, and the handwritten Japanese blackboard menu changes daily so items cannot be pre-verified.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: Standing bar (tachinomi) specializing in sashimi and kushikatsu (fried skewers/cutlets)
cultural_comfort_note: A locals' standing bar with a handwritten Japanese-only blackboard menu, cash-only payment, and no English signage found, so some Japanese (or a translation app) will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source. Signature items are wheat-battered/fried (kushikatsu, the famous thick ham cutlet) and sashimi is typically served with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff, and no evidence such confirmation is offered.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null). Address and "2 minutes from Suidobashi Station East Exit" are corroborated across multiple listings (Tabelog, せんべろnet, general web search), placing it well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30); Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); closed Sundays and public holidays. (One secondary source lists Mon-Sat as a uniform 17:00-23:00 — treat closing time as approximate and confirm by phone if precision matters.)
neighborhood: Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidōbashi Station)
seats_text: Standing room only, roughly 30 people
vegan_detail: Menu is heavily fish- and meat-based (sashimi, fried pork/ham cutlets, kushikatsu) with vegetables described as only a small fraction of the offering; no vegan options, plant-based substitutions, or hidden-dashi/bonito disclosures were found in any source. Not recommended for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-8-5 中川ビル3F

bio.background: CONTAINER was opened in 2009 by owner Masahiko Onoe, who left his hometown of Fukuoka for Tokyo to pursue a dream of running his own café, gaining experience at a restaurant-operations company before striking out on his own. He still runs the cafe himself and bakes all the cakes.
bio.specialty: Brazilian-style pudim (purin) pudding — the cafe's best-known dessert — alongside a classic gateau chocolat and a Basque cheesecake, with rotating seasonal cakes.
bio.philosophy: The concept is "kindness to heart and body" (心と体にやさしさを): every cake on the menu is made gluten-free and sweetened only lightly with mineral-rich, unrefined kibi (cane) sugar instead of white sugar.
bio.origin: Fukuoka, Japan (owner's hometown)

anecdotes.text: Tucked into a retro building that also houses the Suehirotei rakugo theater and furnished with antiques and curated music, it's described in local press as "a hideaway café you don't want to tell anyone about," known for unwinding over body-friendly cake and coffee.
A 2023 feature highlighted the Brazilian pudim as the cafe's famous signature item and covered four new autumn dessert releases.

caveats: Business-hours and seat-count details vary noticeably across secondary sources (hour blocks, closed days, and seat counts are not fully consistent), so hours_status is marked irregular and seats is left unconfirmed — call ahead (03-6457-7526) or check official social media before visiting. No source confirms the savory/meal menu or drinks (beyond cakes) are gluten-free, and no explicit vegan claim was found, so treat GF confidence as cake-specific and vegan status as unconfirmed.
closed_days: 日・木曜日（月曜も定休とする情報もあり）、不定休あり — Closed Sundays and Thursdays (some sources also list Mondays as closed), plus irregular additional closures; confirm via official social media before visiting.
cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ・体に優しいケーキ
cultural_comfort_note: A small, independently run neighborhood cafe with no confirmed English menu or signage — basic Japanese or a translation app will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: Multiple independent sources consistently report that every cake on the menu is made gluten-free, sweetened with mineral-rich kibi (unrefined cane) sugar rather than refined white sugar. However, no source confirms whether the savory/meal menu, bar items, or specific sauces are gluten-free, and there is no mention of a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, dedicated fryer, or formal cross-contamination protocol — call ahead to confirm for celiac-level needs.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled). Address is 徒歩1分 (about a 1-minute walk) from Shinjuku Sanchome Station per multiple sources, placing it well within walking distance of Shinjuku Station.
hours_raw: Sources conflict: one consistent pairing (repeated across two independent search summaries, likely both citing the same original listing) gives Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat 13:00–18:00, closed Sun/Mon/Thu plus irregular holidays. Another source gives cafe 13:00–16:30 + dinner 18:00–21:30 (Sun 13:00–17:30), closed only Monday. A third gives Sun 13:00–18:00, Mon closed, Tue–Sat 13:00–17:00 and 18:00–22:00. Hours should be confirmed via the official Instagram/SNS or by phone (03-6457-7526) before visiting.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目

staff_allergy_handling.text: All cakes on the menu are made gluten-free using kibi (unrefined cane) sugar rather than white sugar; for specific allergy needs beyond gluten, callers are directed to confirm ingredients by phone.

positives.text: Every cake on the menu is gluten-free, lightly sweetened with mineral-rich kibi sugar.
Organic coffee and tea are used, including a 97%-decaffeinated coffee option and 100% fruit juices/organic cordial sodas for those avoiding caffeine or seeking lighter drink options.

seats_text: Small, intimate cafe seating; reported seat counts vary by source (roughly 5-12 seats reported), so exact capacity is unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: No source found makes an explicit vegan claim for this cafe — descriptions focus specifically on gluten-free cakes and organic/low-caffeine drinks, with no confirmation of egg-free or dairy-free preparation. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about individual cake ingredients (eggs, butter, dairy) before ordering.

cuisine: African / International (multinational) — dining bar
neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya, near the top of Miyamasu-zaka, ~4 min walk from Shibuya Station)
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷1-10-2 志水ビル1F（〒150-0002）
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment) — lat/lng left null intentionally. Address is corroborated by multiple independent listings (Gnavi, aggregator sites, search snippets referencing Tabelog) as Shibuya 1-10-2, Shimizu Building 1F, described consistently as a 4-minute walk from Shibuya Station at the top of Miyamasu-zaka — this places it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target point (35.6595,139.7005).
seats_text: Roughly 40 seats; the space can also be booked for private parties/events (approx. 20-50 people).
hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 18:00-24:30 daily (some listings show slightly different closing times, e.g. 18:00-00:30) — irregular closures, best to call ahead
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, not fixed weekly days off)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains an English-language version of its own website, but no source confirms English-speaking staff or an English menu on-site, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) is the safer assumption.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac protocol was found. The restaurant's own signature dish, couscous served with mutton and root vegetables in tomato sauce, is wheat-based and contains gluten, so it must be avoided by celiac diners. Other dishes on the multinational menu (Kenyan curry, Thai curry, coco rice, pho, and rotating West African stews like yassa/mafé/moambé) may be rice- or stew-based and could be safer, but wheat-containing soy sauce, shared fryers, and cross-contamination have not been confirmed one way or the other — ask staff directly before ordering.
vegan_detail: A dedicated vegetarian course (ベジタリアンコース, listed at 4,400 yen tax included) is offered alongside the regular meat/seafood-forward multinational menu. Because the cuisine is African/international rather than Japanese, hidden katsuo/bonito dashi is less of a risk than at a typical Japanese restaurant, but the vegetarian course has not been confirmed to be strictly vegan (free of dairy, egg, or honey) — ask staff to confirm for a fully vegan meal.

bio.origin: Chef originally from Burkina Faso; menu draws on Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and South America.
bio.background: Yinega has operated since around 2013 in a quiet spot near the top of Miyamasu-zaka, just a few minutes' walk from the crowds of Shibuya Station. It's run as a personal, well-traveled kitchen rather than a themed chain outlet — reviewers describe it as a cozy hideaway with world/ethnic music (and a permanent DJ booth) that feels far removed from central Shibuya despite the short walk.
bio.specialty: Couscous served with mutton and root vegetables in a tomato sauce — described by one diner as 'the world's smallest pasta,' piled generously with vegetables.
bio.philosophy: A retreat-like, music-filled space serving dishes the owner/chef has encountered while cooking across different cuisines, rather than a single-country tasting menu.

anecdotes.text: The chef previously worked as a cook for an embassy kitchen before opening this multinational restaurant in Shibuya.
The signature couscous, served with mutton and root vegetables in tomato sauce, is called 'the world's smallest pasta' and comes loaded with vegetables — reviewers call it quite good.
The dining room has a permanent DJ booth and plays ethnic/world music regularly, reinforcing the restaurant's identity as a laid-back escape from the Shibuya crowds.

positives.text: The restaurant lists a specific, priced vegetarian course (4,400 yen) as a standing menu option rather than an off-menu special-request-only accommodation.

caveats: Direct fetches of Tabelog, the restaurant's own Wix site, Gnavi, Jalan, Retty, and several blog posts all returned HTTP 403 from the network's outbound proxy, so this record is built entirely from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than full page fetches — treat exact hours, phone number, and precise building-name spelling (sources vary between 志水ビル and a possible alternate transliteration) as reasonably but not perfectly confirmed. No source explicitly addresses gluten-free protocols, dedicated fryers, or wheat content of soy sauce/dashi used in the non-Japanese dishes; this is flagged in gf_detail rather than assumed.

lead: ヴェジェトゥス プラス
name_ja: ヴェジェトゥス プラス（vegetus+）
cuisine: 有機野菜・ベジタリアン・グルテンフリー対応（デリ・惣菜／野菜料理）
neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町（水道橋駅徒歩約6分）
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町3-1-8 天野ビル1F
geocode_note: Address confirmed via multiple independent listings (official site, Tabelog, search aggregators) as 神田三崎町3-1-8, roughly 6 minutes on foot from JR Suidōbashi Station west exit and about 8 minutes from Kudanshita Station — well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station radius.
seats_text: Small shop with table seating for up to 10 plus a 2-seat counter; eat-in space is limited, so many guests order takeout instead.
hours_raw: 月〜金・祝前日 11:00〜15:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：土曜・日曜・祝日
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood lunch deli with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app or simple Japanese is helpful when asking about specific ingredients.
vegan_detail: The daily-changing deli lineup includes vegan-compatible dishes alongside non-vegan ones (the shop's own philosophy is "vegetables plus" animal protein/fat, not exclusively plant-based), so not everything on offer is vegan. Staff can identify which of the day's rotating dishes are vegan. No confirmation was found on whether hidden dashi/bonito is used in the non-vegan items or avoided in the vegan ones — worth double-checking on-site.
gf_detail: Multiple independent listings describe the shop as offering "vegan and gluten-free menu options," built around organic/low-pesticide vegetables, brown rice, and homemade dressings/sauces made without preservatives or chemical seasoning. However, no source specifies whether soy sauce used is wheat-free, whether a dedicated fryer exists, or what cross-contamination precautions are in place in what is a small, all-in-one kitchen — celiac-level safety should not be assumed without asking staff directly.

bio.background: The shop was opened out of a wish to help people eat healthy meals as part of daily life. Before opening, the owner, Hasegawa, read through dozens of books and magazines researching what 'healthy' actually means, having found the word used everywhere but rarely defined with any specificity.
bio.specialty: One-plate lunch set of three rotating deli dishes with green salad and brown rice (around 1,050 yen); the deli menu changes daily with one new item introduced weekly.
bio.philosophy: Built around organic and low-pesticide vegetables grown without chemical fertilizers, cold-pressed rapeseed and extra-virgin olive oil, natural Italian sea salt, and unrefined sugar — with all dressings and sauces made in-house, free of chemical seasonings and preservatives.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from 'vegetus,' the Latin root behind the word 'vegetarian,' meaning 'full of vitality.' The owner added 'plus' because the intent isn't a vegetables-only diet — it's wanting guests to also get energy from animal-based protein and fat for an active life.

positives.text: Meals are made with organic and low-pesticide vegetables, brown rice, and homemade dressings/sauces with no preservatives or chemical seasoning, and the shop explicitly lists vegan and gluten-free options among its rotating deli dishes.

caveats: Vegan and gluten-free info here reflects general shop-level descriptions from the official site, Tabelog, and local press — no source gave celiac-grade detail on shared cooking surfaces, wheat in soy sauce/dashi, or a dedicated fryer, so guests with severe allergies should confirm specifics with staff before ordering.

lead: 番屋 西新宿住友ビル店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿2-6-1 新宿住友ビル49F

bio.background: Part of the 番屋 (Banya) group of private-room izakaya, this Nishi-Shinjuku branch occupies the 49th floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building. Its dining rooms — sunken-kotatsu seating, tatami rooms, and sofa seating — are arranged around floor-to-ceiling windows looking out over the Shinjuku skyline, and it is used heavily for business dinners and private parties.
bio.specialty: Sashimi platters sourced from Tsukiji, and yakitori/tsukune skewers made from selected domestic chicken, with the meal traditionally finished with the restaurant's signature white curry udon.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant markets its private dining rooms, roughly 200 meters above the street, primarily around their night view of Shinjuku — a signature blog post walks through which rooms have the best skyline view for a dinner date or client entertaining.

caveats: This is a large chain izakaya, not a vegan or gluten-free specialty restaurant. The vegetarian course must be arranged in advance and is offered as an accompaniment when a vegetarian guest joins a group otherwise ordering the regular menu, not as a standing daily option. It is not gluten-free: the course itself includes wheat udon and tempura, and standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used throughout the kitchen. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not rely on this restaurant without a very detailed conversation with staff in advance, and even then risk remains high.
closed_days: 年末年始（12/31〜1/5）、ビル休館日 (year-end/New Year holidays and building closure days)
cuisine: 居酒屋・和食（すきやき・しゃぶしゃぶ・焼鳥、要予約でベジタリアンコース対応）
cultural_comfort_note: English-language booking is available through TableCheck and other reservation platforms, but on-site menus and service are primarily Japanese, so bring a translated allergy card or key phrases when discussing dietary restrictions.
geocode_note: Address (Shinjuku Sumitomo Building, Nishi-Shinjuku 2-6-1) is a 1-minute walk from Toei Oedo Line Tochomae Station, a 4-minute walk from Marunouchi Line Nishi-Shinjuku Station, and a 6-minute walk from JR/Odakyu/Keio Shinjuku Station — well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station target circle.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce is documented anywhere for this restaurant. Even the dedicated vegetarian tasting course contains wheat: it includes 梅おろしうどん (ume-oroshi udon, wheat noodles) and tempura (wheat-flour batter), and the kitchen's general soy sauce is standard (wheat-containing). There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation beyond a general willingness to discuss allergies with staff, which the restaurant itself does not guarantee to be fully allergen-free.
name_en: Koshitsu Izakaya Banya Nishi-Shinjuku Sumitomo Bldg.
name_ja: 個室居酒屋 番屋 西新宿住友ビル店
neighborhood: 西新宿・都庁前

positives.text: Rather than leaving vegetarian diners to piece together a meal from a meat- and fish-centric menu, the restaurant has a named, pre-set vegetarian course (8 dishes) explicitly described as free of meat, fish, and dairy, bookable when a vegetarian guest is part of a group.

seats_text: 約200席（座敷席・掘りごたつ席・座椅子席・ソファー席、夜景の見える個室あり）、宴会最大80名対応
vegan_detail: A dedicated vegetarian tasting course (8 dishes — mizuna simmered greens, avocado/yuba sashimi, steamed vegetable platter, mixed-bean salad, asparagus/shiitake/shishito tempura, tofu-and-eggplant in thick sauce, ume-oroshi udon, and soy-milk ice cream — plus 2 hours all-you-can-drink, roughly ¥5,500-6,500) is explicitly described by the restaurant as containing no meat, fish, or dairy. It must be arranged in advance and is offered to accompany a vegetarian guest within a group otherwise ordering the standard menu, rather than being a standalone always-available vegan menu. Hidden dashi/katsuobushi in the rest of the (non-vegetarian-course) menu is not addressed by any source found, so diners should assume standard Japanese kitchen practices (bonito-based dashi) apply outside the designated course.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町29-27 エクセレンスビルディング桜丘町2F

roles: Co-owner: Keita Mera (目良慶太, IT-industry background)
bio.origin: French-trained chef who worked in Tokyo French kitchens (including Akebonobashi's Sucre Salé) and in Paris before becoming owner-chef of Bistro Rigole in Sangenjaya, where he earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand in 2018 and 2019.
bio.specialty: Signature shrimp tempura: a round parcel of shrimp wrapped in a shrimp mousseline (blended with coconut oil, sesame paste and fish sauce), coated in a tomato-tinted, beer-leavened batter, fried in sesame and grapeseed oil, and served over a concentrated Américaine sauce made from the shrimp heads and shells.
bio.philosophy: Kameyatani breaks tempura down into five components — ingredient, batter, frying oil, seasoning and presentation — and reworks each one dish-by-dish, applying French technique (mousselines, reduction sauces, fermented batters) to the traditional tempura format, paired with a roughly 300-bottle list of mostly white and orange natural wines.

anecdotes.text: The signature shrimp dish was conceived before the restaurant even opened, inspired by Southeast Asian shrimp toast; the head-and-shell Américaine sauce is built to ooze out when the piece is bitten into.

Tenki opened in March 2023 as the second restaurant from the team behind KAMERA (a shumai and oolong-highball standing bar in Shibuya's Hyakkendan), and grew into one of the neighborhood's most in-demand counters within its first year.

caveats: Address in the target brief (29-26) could not be confirmed; multiple independent listings (Autoreserve, Hot Pepper, Yahoo Map, restaurant's own reservation channels) consistently give 桜丘町29-27, エクセレンスビルディング桜丘町2F, so that address is used here instead. Hours/closed-day and seat-count details vary slightly across sources (irregular closures vs. a cited Monday closure; 24 vs. ~30 seats) — treated as approximate. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found in any source; this is a seafood- and wheat-forward tempura counter, included here as a verified non-GF/non-vegan reference point rather than a recommended pick for those diets.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day confirmed across sources; one secondary source cites Monday closures, most describe irregular closures
cuisine: Tempura & wine — French-influenced tempura with natural wine pairings, seasonal ingredients
cultural_comfort_note: A trendy, reservation-driven counter bar with no confirmed English menu or multilingual staff; expect a Japanese-speaking experience, best booked via a Japanese-reading concierge or platform.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment; lat/lng set to null for downstream geocoding). Address cross-confirmed across Autoreserve, Hot Pepper, and Yahoo Map listings, all agreeing on 桜丘町29-27, roughly 500m / 10-minute walk from Shibuya Station — well within the Shibuya Scramble 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: The kitchen's core tempura batter (used across most menu items, including the signature shrimp dish) is made with all-purpose wheat flour, cornstarch, rice flour, dry yeast and beer — gluten is built into the standard preparation. No gluten-free batter, substitution, or dedicated fryer has been reported by any source.
hours_raw: 17:00–24:00, irregular days off (不定休); reported closures vary slightly by listing
neighborhood: Shibuya (Sakuragaoka-cho), near Shinsen/Shibuya Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core tempura batter across the menu is a wheat-flour, cornstarch, rice-flour and beer mixture leavened with dry yeast and tinted with tomato powder — there is no separate gluten-free batter or fryer described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature and most-discussed dishes are seafood-based, using shrimp mousseline blended with Vietnamese fish sauce (nyokumamu) and finished with an Américaine sauce made from shrimp heads and shells — animal products run through the core preparation, not just as a garnish.

seats_text: Approximately 24 seats total across counter and standing areas (some listings cite roughly 17 counter seats plus 13 standing spots).
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood tempura with fish-based mousses and sauces — e.g., the signature shrimp dish incorporates fish sauce and a shrimp-shell reduction — and no vegetable-only tasting course or vegan menu has been documented in any source, so it is not vegan-friendly.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町2-2-6 畑山第一ビル1階

bio.origin: Named for "Higo," the historical name for the Kumamoto region, reflecting the owner's Kumamoto ties
bio.specialty: 豚天そば (pork tempura soba) — a bowl topped with an oversized slab of deep-fried pork
bio.background: A small standing-soba counter tucked on a backstreet between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, run day-to-day by its owner. The shop leans into its owner's personal interests — Kumamoto ("Higo") references in the name and decor, plus a visible fondness for the Hanshin Tigers baseball team — giving an otherwise utilitarian tachigui-soba stand a distinct, homey character.

anecdotes.text: The shop's best-known bowl is topped with a single, oversized piece of pork tempura draped over the noodles, priced in the 360–400 yen range — a favorite of nearby office and university workers looking for a fast, filling meal.
A reviewer noted the shop is run by an owner who is an enthusiastic Hanshin Tigers fan, with the counter and walls decorated accordingly.

caveats: Hours/phone corroborated across multiple secondary sources (Ekiten, Tsuku2, NAVITIME/related aggregator snippets) since the Tabelog page itself and several dedicated review blogs (sobadb, tachigui-soba.com, gnavi, hotpepper) returned HTTP 403 and could not be directly fetched. No official website was located. No English-language menu or GF/vegan-specific statements were found anywhere; this is a traditional wheat-noodle standing soba shop, not a dietary-focused establishment.

Soba here is very likely a wheat-buckwheat blend typical of budget standing-soba counters, the tsuyu dipping/broth is soy-sauce (wheat) based, and the signature dish is wheat-battered deep-fried tempura (pork tempura) cooked in what is presumably a shared fryer. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, noodle, or sauce option was documented. Not recommended for celiac diners.

roles: Owner personally curates the natural-wine list
bio.background: A small, narrow second-floor bar tucked up a stairway near Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, in the Shinjuku Sanchome dining area. It is an independently run wine bar (not part of a chain), seating roughly 20 across counter and table seats.
bio.philosophy: Built around natural wine paired with seasonal, Italian/Spanish-leaning small plates — the idea being that the food and the glass-poured natural wines are chosen to enhance each other.
bio.specialty: Small-plate tapas-style dishes such as "Ebi no Taiyo" (shrimp), Iberico pork gyoza, oysters, and a vegetable appetizer platter, alongside 10+ natural wines available by the glass (many under ¥500/glass).

anecdotes.text: The owner carefully selects natural wines said to be gaining attention for their health-conscious appeal, keeping 10+ types available by the glass at all times.
Reviewers describe the space as compact and cozy, centered on a counter, about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog, Hotpepper, or Retty pages (all returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch); facts below are corroborated via multiple independent web-search snippets referencing those pages, but I was not able to read full page text directly. No English official website was located. No source made any explicit vegan or gluten-free claim for this specific restaurant — dietary fields below reflect the general profile of an Italian/Spanish-style wine-and-tapas bar (wheat-containing items like gyoza and likely pasta/bread on the menu) rather than any confirmed accommodation policy, so treat gf_confidence and vegan_status as cautious defaults, not verified claims.

The menu includes wheat-containing items typical of an Italian/Spanish-style bar (e.g., gyoza dumplings, likely bread/pasta-style small plates), and it operates as a general shared kitchen. Celiac diners should not assume safety here — ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.

The menu includes a vegetable appetizer platter (¥910) alongside oysters, shellfish, and Iberico pork gyoza, and Japanese-Italian fusion dishes here commonly rely on bonito/katsuo-based dashi and animal-based stocks that are not disclosed. lead: 京都宇治 藤井茗縁
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-2-3 渋谷フクラス（東急プラザ渋谷）6F

roles: Operated as a branded tea salon of Fujii Chaen, a family-run Uji tea garden (est. Muromachi era)
bio.background: The shop is run by Fujii Chaen, a tea-growing family business in Uji, Kyoto that has cultivated tea on its own roughly 1.2-hectare plantation since the Tenbun era of the Muromachi period (mid-1500s) — about 480 years of continuous tea cultivation. This Shibuya location, opened December 5, 2019 on the 6th floor of Tokyu Plaza Shibuya (Shibuya Fukuras), was one of the garden's first branded retail/dining outposts outside Uji itself.
bio.origin: Uji, Kyoto
bio.specialty: Matcha and hojicha brûlée-style parfaits, kamameshi (iron-pot) rice dishes, an obanzai-style kaiseki tea-meal set, and Uji-matcha soba, all built around the garden's own tea leaves
bio.philosophy: Bring the family tea garden's own Uji leaves — grown and processed under centuries-old practice — directly to Tokyo diners, using the tea itself (sencha, matcha, hojicha) as the centerpiece of both savory dishes and desserts rather than as a mere flavoring.

anecdotes.text: 京都宇治 藤井茗縁 opened December 5, 2019 inside Tokyu Plaza Shibuya, described as a tea salon where guests can enjoy food and sweets made with quality leaves nurtured by the 480-year-old Uji Fujii Chaen tea garden.
Fujii Chaen has continuously grown tea on its own plantation in Uji since the Tenbun era of the Muromachi period, and the Shibuya shop lets guests taste dishes and desserts made from those leaves.

caveats: The app metadata suggested this spot is "VG対応" (vegan-oriented), but no source I could reach (Tabelog, mall tenant pages, blogs) confirms a vegan or gluten-free menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated prep area — treat that tag as unverified. Several primary pages (official Tokyu Plaza / Shibuya Fukuras tenant page, DD Holdings shop page, Instagram, Tabelog Japanese/English detail pages) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, so facts below rely on search-engine snippets and secondary aggregator sites (news release on shinryokuen.net/kyotokan.jp, nihonchaseikatsu.com blog) rather than direct page reads; cross-checked across multiple independent sources where possible but could not verify menu allergen text firsthand. Seat count (48) and exact budget band are from a single aggregated source and unconfirmed by a second independent read.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day found; operates daily, following the Tokyu Plaza Shibuya / Shibuya Fukuras building's operating calendar.
cuisine: 日本茶カフェ・抹茶料理 (Japanese tea salon / matcha cuisine — obanzai, kamameshi, matcha soba, tea sweets)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a major Shibuya Station-front shopping complex (Tokyu Plaza Shibuya / Shibuya Fukuras) with an English Tabelog listing, but no source confirms an English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese will help.
geocode_note: Address (Dogenzaka 1-2-3, Shibuya Fukuras/Tokyu Plaza Shibuya 6F) places it directly adjacent to JR Shibuya Station's south exit, well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble Crossing (35.6595,139.7005). lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; pin to be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer/prep-area statement was found in any source reviewed. The menu includes 宇治抹茶そば (Uji-matcha soba) — most Japanese soba (not 十割/100%-buckwheat) contains wheat flour as a binder — and Kyoto-style obanzai dishes, which typically use shoyu (soy sauce, usually wheat-containing) and dashi. Treat as a higher-risk option for celiac diners and ask staff directly about wheat in the soba, soy sauce, and dashi before ordering.

Weekend/holiday seating may run in four time slots (11:00–13:00 / 13:00–15:00 / 15:00–17:00 / after 17:00) with a 90-minute seating limit per a Tabelog reservation-conditions snippet.

neighborhood: Shibuya (Dogenzaka, inside Tokyu Plaza Shibuya / Shibuya Fukuras, 6F)
