Traditional obanzai and dashi-based dishes in this style of Japanese cooking commonly use bonito (katsuobushi) stock, and the parfait/sweets menu likely contains dairy and egg. No statement found about plant-based dashi or dairy-free desserts — ask staff about the dashi base and dessert ingredients before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-8-10

bio.background: Opened July 1 near Suidōbashi Station, this eel-rice-bowl shop (branded in Japanese simply as "うな重," pronounced "Unashige," and marketed as UNASHIGE) was created by restaurateur Hamano Kōichi (濱野浩一). Hamano previously spent years at the Global Dining restaurant group leading new-store openings, then headed its planning and development division from 2003 to 2010, before going independent to run cafés and small lodging businesses. He founded the eel-shop concept as a standalone company aiming to make unagi (normally an expensive, special-occasion dish in Japan) into an everyday, fast-food-priced meal.
bio.philosophy: The stated concept is a "unaju fast-food shop": by systematizing operations and streamlining eel sourcing, the shop hand-bastes and grills eel to order in the traditional style but sells it from about ¥1,210-1,320 — far below typical unagi-restaurant prices — to bring eel to a broader, younger, everyday clientele (office workers and students in their 20s) rather than treating it as a once-a-year luxury.
bio.specialty: Unajū / unadon — eel fillets basted in a house tare sauce and charcoal/grilled to order in-house, plus shirayaki (unsauced grilled eel) and a hitsumabushi-style option using free self-serve dashi broth poured over the rice.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer titled their review "高校生が店主の激安ウナギ屋さんが新規オープン" ("a bargain eel shop newly opened, seemingly run by a high-schooler"), remarking on the shop's very young-looking staff and casual, non-traditional service style compared to old-school unagi restaurants.

Another reviewer titled their post "あらゆる面で鰻屋らしくない" ("unlike an eel restaurant in every way"), highlighting the touch-panel self-ordering system and fast-food-style operation as a break from traditional unagi-ya conventions.

caveats: No official restaurant website was found (a similarly-named unasige.com belongs to an unrelated shop). No fixed weekly closed day could be confirmed from available sources — the shop appears to operate daily on the stated hours, but this is not certain. No dedicated GF or vegan menu, ingredient breakdown, or allergy protocol was found in any source; this is a traditional soy-sauce-tare-based grilled eel specialist, not a diet-focused restaurant, so gluten-free and vegan claims here are conservative defaults rather than confirmed accommodations — diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should not rely on this listing without asking staff directly.
closed_days: Not confirmed in available sources; the shop appears to operate daily on its stated hours (no closed day mentioned by any aggregator or press source found).
cuisine: うなぎ（鰻料理）／ Unagi (grilled freshwater eel) rice bowl specialist
gf_detail: This is a traditional-style unagi (eel) specialist: the eel is basted in a house tare sauce, which in Japanese kabayaki cooking is almost universally soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or ingredient labeling was found in any source consulted (Tabelog, press coverage, blog visit reports). No mention of a dedicated fryer or GF-specific handling was found — likely not applicable since the eel is grilled, not fried, but cross-contact with the shared tare/dashi station cannot be ruled out. Diners with celiac disease should treat this as not gluten-free and confirm directly with staff (Japanese-language only, in person) before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded in this pass (network geocoding is disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Multiple independent sources (press coverage, blog visit report, map listing) consistently place the shop about a 3-minute walk from Suidōbashi Station in Kanda-Misaki-chō, which is well within the 0.8 km Suidōbashi Station target radius.
neighborhood: 神田三崎町、千代田区（水道橋駅徒歩3分）
seats_text: Small fast-food-style counter shop with touch-panel self-ordering at the entrance; exact seat count was not published in any source consulted.
vegan_detail: Every dish centers on grilled freshwater eel; no plant-based, vegetarian, or eel-free alternative was found in any menu description or press coverage. The shop's self-serve dashi broth station (used to turn the rice bowl into an ochazuke/hitsumabushi-style dish) is, per standard Japanese practice, very likely bonito/katsuo-based and not vegan. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

lead: 新潟地酒と旬肴 ぼんや

roles: Niigata regional izakaya specializing in fish sourced directly from Niigata/Joetsu and Niigata sake
bio.background: An izakaya branch (Shinjuku-Sanchome location) of a small Niigata-cuisine concept that imports fresh fish directly from the Joetsu coast of Niigata Prefecture and stocks around 60 varieties of Niigata sake, built around introducing Tokyo diners to Niigata's regional food and drink culture.
bio.origin: Niigata Prefecture regional cuisine, brought to a Shinjuku-Sanchome izakaya setting
bio.specialty: Nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) served as sashimi, grilled, or simmered, alongside Tokamachi-style hegisoba noodles (bound with funori seaweed) and Tochio-style deep-fried tofu (aburaage)
bio.philosophy: Direct-from-Niigata sourcing of both fish and sake so Tokyo customers can experience Niigata's coastal and mountain regional specialties paired with local sake in one sitting

caveats: This is a Niigata-regional izakaya, not a vegan/gluten-free-focused restaurant — no dedicated GF or vegan menu, fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any indexed source. Reported operating hours conflict across secondary listing sites (some show 16:00 start for weekday dinner and midnight close, others show an 11:30 lunch service and 22:30 close with Sunday closure); the Sunday-closed, lunch+dinner weekday pattern was corroborated by two independent sources and is used here, but hours should be confirmed by phone (03-3353-0144) before visiting. Primary source pages (Tabelog, official gorp.jp site, Gurunavi, Ekiten, Hitosara, Retty) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this session; facts below are drawn from search-engine-indexed summaries of those same pages, with URLs cited.
closed_days: Sundays (one source also notes irregular/holiday closures, including likely year-end Dec 31-Jan 4)
cuisine: Niigata Regional Cuisine (新潟郷土料理) - izakaya style
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese sake-and-regional-food izakaya with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, though online reservation platforms (Tabelog, AutoReserve) support English/browser translation for booking.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is documented. This is a traditional izakaya: soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and bonito/kelp dashi run through most dishes, and the signature Tokamachi hegisoba noodles use funori seaweed as a binder but are conventionally milled with wheat flour blended into the buckwheat, so they cannot be assumed gluten-free. The Tochio-style deep-fried tofu (aburaage) is fried in oil of unknown dedication. Celiac diners should not consider this restaurant safe without direct, on-site confirmation.
hours_raw: Sources conflict: one set of listings shows Mon-Fri lunch 11:30-15:00 and dinner 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00), Sat/holidays 12:00 or 16:00-22:30, closed Sundays; another aggregator shows weekday hours as 16:00-24:00 and weekend/holiday 16:00-23:30. The lunch+dinner/Sunday-closed pattern was corroborated by two independent listing sources and is used for hours_week below.

bio.origin: 新潟県 (Niigata Prefecture) — the proprietress's hometown
bio.background: A small counter restaurant tucked down a back alley in Nishi-Asakusa, run single-handedly by its proprietress (女将), who prepares every dish herself and lines the counter daily with an ever-changing spread of homemade obanzai (Japanese home-style side dishes).
bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai built around her native Niigata's local cuisine, including simmered kuruma-fu (車麩の煮物, a braised wheat-gluten specialty of Niigata) alongside seasonal vegetable dishes.
bio.philosophy: Health-conscious, from-scratch home cooking made with care for the body — comfort food rather than restaurant showmanship.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe the feeling of walking in as being 'like coming home,' saying every dish carries genuine home-cooked warmth.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, the official site (gdrc000.gorp.jp), or Gurunavi due to organization egress policy blocking those hosts; all details below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/excerpts of those pages rather than full page fetches. No vegan or gluten-free specific claims were found anywhere for this restaurant; assessment below is inferred from its traditional obanzai/kappo style and a signature dish that explicitly contains wheat gluten (fu).

Notably, one of the kitchen's signature specialties is 車麩の煮物 — simmered kuruma-fu, a wheat-gluten product from the proprietress's native Niigata — meaning wheat gluten is a deliberate ingredient on the menu, not just an incidental seasoning. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

lead: 名代富士そば水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-1-16

roles: Founder / Chairman of Daitan Group (parent company of Fuji Soba)
bio.origin: First shop "Soba-sei" opened in Shibuya, Tokyo in 1966; formally incorporated as Daitan Food Co. in 1972
bio.background: Fuji Soba traces back to a single standing-soba stall called "Soba-sei," opened in Shibuya in 1966 by Michio Tan, a former real-estate dealer who wanted a business with dependable daily cash flow. The stall's format spread to Nishi-Ogikubo, Shinjuku, and Ikebukuro, and in 1972 the operation was formally incorporated as Daitan Food Co., rebranding as 名代 富士そば (Fuji Soba). It has since grown into one of Tokyo's largest standing-soba chains, with this Suidobashi branch being one of many locations across the city rather than an independent shop.
bio.philosophy: Tan built the group around independently-run sister companies: branches, even near the same station, are often operated as separate subsidiaries so that each store stays competitive rather than functioning as one centrally managed chain.

anecdotes.text: Group founder Michio Tan started out in real estate but pivoted to food service because, unlike property deals, a noodle stall brings in cash every single day.
Daitan Group branches are deliberately run as separate, independently-accountable subsidiary companies, even when stores sit near the same station, to preserve an internal spirit of competition.

caveats: This is a branch of the large Fuji Soba (Daitan Group) standing-soba chain, not an independent restaurant, so the bio reflects the parent company's history rather than a story unique to this location. The noodles contain wheat and the broth is fish-based, so this spot is not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.
closed_days: None — open daily (年中無休), with a brief ~45-minute administrative closure around 3:00 AM
cuisine: Soba (Buckwheat Noodles) — standing soba chain
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket vending machine (often color-coded by dish type) that you hand to staff; there's no English menu, but the picture/color system makes it manageable without fluent Japanese.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address and multiple sources confirm the shop is ~3 minutes' walk from Suidobashi Station's east exit, well within the target radius.
gf_detail: The noodles are a standard soba-shop blend that includes substantial wheat flour (multiple sources describe standard Fuji Soba branches as roughly 40% buckwheat / 60% wheat flour, unlike a special higher-buckwheat variant offered at one different branch). The company's own allergen chart lists both wheat (小麦) and buckwheat (そば) as allergens present even in the plain kake soba. Not suitable for celiac or wheat-avoiding diners.
hours_raw: 04:00–03:00 daily (L.O. 02:45); closed roughly 45 minutes from 3:00 AM per administrative guidance
name_ja: 名代 富士そば 水道橋店
neighborhood: Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fuji Soba's official allergen chart lists both wheat and buckwheat among the specified allergens even for the basic kake soba, and independent sources describe standard-branch noodles as roughly 40% buckwheat flour blended with 60% wheat flour.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a standard soba-tsuyu shop, the seasoning broth is soy-sauce based; soy is listed on the company's own allergen chart for the basic kake soba.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The plain kake soba broth already contains fish (mackerel) per the company's allergen chart, indicating a bonito/fish-based dashi rather than a kombu-only vegan stock; no vegan-specific broth or substitution is documented.

positives.text: The company publishes an official allergen reference chart covering its menu, offering more ingredient transparency than most fast standing-noodle shops.

seats_text: About 20 seats, standing-counter style with some stool seating
vegan_detail: The base broth is fish-based: the company's own allergen chart lists mackerel (さば) and soy as allergens present in the plain kake soba, indicating a bonito/fish-derived dashi rather than a kombu-only vegan stock. No vegan-specific menu items or substitutions are documented.

lead: 地鶏割烹 おはじき 新宿
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-12-24 第二小島ビル B1

bio.origin: Koshu, Yamanashi Prefecture (roots in Yamanashi's jidori chicken tradition)
bio.background: Ohajiki Shinjuku is a hideaway-style kappo restaurant near Shinjuku Station's west exit built around Koshu jidori — a free-range chicken breed raised in Yamanashi Prefecture and known for firm texture and rich flavor. The restaurant is listed by the Yamanashi Prefecture tourism office as an official Tokyo outlet serving the region's jidori chicken, underscoring a direct sourcing relationship with Yamanashi producers.
bio.specialty: Fresh tori-sashi (raw chicken sashimi), charcoal-grilled chicken skewers, an original-recipe liver pâté (rebā pate) served with wine and sake, karaage fried chicken, and mizutaki chicken hot pot.
bio.philosophy: Built around showcasing the quality of Koshu jidori chicken across raw, grilled, and simmered preparations, paired with a curated selection of Yamanashi wines and sake from across Japan, inside a retro Showa-era interior meant to feel like an adult hideaway near the station.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is registered on Yamanashi Prefecture's own tourism site as one of the prefecture's approved 'yamaaji' outlets in Tokyo serving Koshu jidori chicken.
The liver pâté is described as made from an original secret recipe, popular paired with wine and sake.

caveats: This is a jidori (local-breed) chicken specialty kappo restaurant with no gluten-free or vegan menu, options, or accommodations found in any source. No official site, Tabelog listing, or blog coverage mentions allergen labeling, wheat-free soy sauce, a dedicated fryer, or plant-based dishes. The menu centers on raw chicken sashimi, fried karaage, and skewers — inherently unsuitable for strict celiac or vegan diners without extensive advance inquiry, and staff allergy-handling capability is unverified. Full address, hours, and story details are drawn from search-engine summaries of the official site and prefecture listing because direct WebFetch of ohajikishinjyuku.com, hotpepper.jp, foodre.jp, and tabelog.com all returned HTTP 403 in this environment; core facts (address, phone, hours pattern, seat count) were independently corroborated across multiple listing sources (official site, Hotpepper, Yamanashi Prefecture site, Tabelog) via search snippets.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (定休日なし); irregular temporary closures possible; closed for New Year holidays (approx. Dec 29-Jan 5). Weekday lunch only — no lunch service on weekends or public holidays.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-10-1

bio.background: Opened February 18, 2011 as the Shibuya branch/sister shop of Enji (えん寺), the well-known Kichijoji tsukemen restaurant credited with originating the rich 'vegepota' (vegetable + pork-bone) style of tsukemen broth that later spread across Tokyo.
bio.specialty: Rich tsukemen (濃厚つけ麺): a thick pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth accented with yuzu citrus and menma, served with a choice of two house noodles — a standard thick chewy noodle or an unusual noodle kneaded with wheat germ (胚芽麺) for extra aroma and sweetness.

anecdotes.text: The shop's distinctive wheat-germ noodle (胚芽麺) is singled out by food writers as unusually fragrant and slightly sweet compared to standard ramen noodles.
Described by ramen bloggers as one of the most concentrated ('most rich') tsukemen broths in the Shibuya area, blending seafood and vegetable umami in the En-ji lineage style.

caveats: This is a conventional wheat-noodle tsukemen shop, not a gluten-free or vegan-oriented restaurant — it is being catalogued as a verified real dining spot rather than a dietary-accommodating one. Business hours vary slightly across secondary sources (some give a straight 11:30-22:00, others a weekday split-shift 11:30-16:00/17:30-23:00 with shorter weekend hours); the split-shift figure was the more consistently repeated and is used here. No official restaurant website was located, only aggregator/review listings and blog coverage. Seat count, cash-only policy, and hours come from restaurant-directory aggregators (Ekiten, Gnavi-linked search results) rather than a single primary source, so treat exact minute-level hours with some caution.
closed_days: 無休（年末年始を除く）
cuisine: つけ麺（ゆず香るスープ、濃厚系）
cultural_comfort_note: Small 11-seat counter-only local ramen/tsukemen shop with no confirmed English menu or signage — expect a typical Japanese neighborhood noodle-shop experience, ordering by pointing or basic Japanese.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address places it in Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya, roughly a 3-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station (multiple sources cite 5 min from Hachiko exit / 6 min from Shinsen Station), well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
gf_detail: The noodles are wheat ramen noodles, and the house specialty noodle is made by kneading in extra wheat germ (胚芽麺) — more wheat-forward, not less. The pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce (typical for this style of tsukemen), and no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe preparation was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
neighborhood: Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya (near Shinsen)
seats_text: Counter-only, 11 seats
vegan_detail: The signature broth is a rich pork-bone-and-seafood (魚介豚骨) tsukemen soup — not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based menu items, vegetable-only broth option, or vegan accommodation were found in any source. Kanji branding referencing 'vegepota' (ベジポタ) refers to a vegetable-enriched pork-bone broth style, not a vegan dish.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-9-5 山下ビル1F

bio.background: SWING Suidobashi-ten is a small yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) and craft-beer dining bar near Suidobashi Station and Tokyo Dome, run by owner-chef Ozaki Chieko. It is part of a small independently run SWING group of casual beer-and-yoshoku bars in the area.
bio.specialty: Stamina bara-tare — pork belly simmered in a sweet-and-savory brown sugar glaze
bio.philosophy: Casual, comfort-food yoshoku plates (pork belly, beef tendon, house sausage) built to pair with an extensive list of German and Belgian import beers.

anecdotes.text: The signature Stamina Bara-tare set meal — pork belly glazed in a sweet-spicy brown sugar sauce — was created by owner-chef Ozaki Chieko after she was inspired by a pork belly dish she tried in Wakayama.
Another standout is beef tendon braised in red wine for over five hours, and 'Meister Togane' sausage, a Japanese-made sausage certified at Germany's IFFA Frankfurt International Sausage Exhibition.

caveats: Hours were corroborated across Tabelog aggregator search snippets rather than a single direct page fetch (direct WebFetch to tabelog.com, hitosara.com, gnavi.co.jp, ekiten.jp, paypaygourmet, and hotpepper.jp all returned HTTP 403 in this environment, a network/proxy restriction, not a site content issue); two independently-surfaced snippets agreed on the same weekday/weekend split, which is why it is reported with regular confidence. Exact seat count could not be confirmed in any source. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this restaurant in any source found — it is a standard meat-and-beer-focused izakaya/yoshoku bar, included here as an OMNI reference point rather than a GF/vegan pick; diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should treat it as unverified and call ahead.
closed_days: Open year-round; closed only around the New Year's holiday period
cuisine: 洋食・ダイニングバー (Yoshoku / Western-style comfort food and German-Belgian beer dining bar)
cultural_comfort_note: All available menu and listing information is in Japanese with no evidence of an English menu, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors may want a translation app or local help.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-9-5) and multiple sources place it a 3–4 minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's east exit (also near the Toei Mita Line Suidobashi exit), well within the 0.8km radius of the Suidōbashi Station landmark. Not geocoded — lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from this address.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated fryer was found in any source. Signature dishes (bara-tare pork belly, beef tendon omurice, beef tendon red wine stew, house sausage) rely on soy-sauce-based glazes, flour-based sauces/roux, and bread or rice pairings typical of yoshoku cooking, so wheat cross-contact should be assumed throughout the kitchen. Celiac diners should not rely on this spot without calling ahead (03-3515-7280) to ask directly.

No vegetarian or vegan dishes, plant-based substitutions, or dashi/bonito disclosures were mentioned in any source found. address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-12-10 八洋ビル1階

bio.origin: West Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.background: A hand-made soba (teuchi soba) and tempura shop that has operated near Shinjuku Station's West Exit for over 40 years, blogs describe it as a long-running neighborhood fixture rather than a modern or trend-driven restaurant.
bio.specialty: Ten-zaru soba (cold soba noodles served with tempura, including shrimp, kabocha squash, carrot and nori tempura fried in sesame oil)
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes hand-made soba prepared on-site, with a glass-enclosed workspace at the front of the restaurant letting customers watch craftsmen roll and cut the noodles by hand.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has a glass-walled soba-making area near the entrance where staff in white uniforms can be seen hand-rolling and cutting noodles, and the dining room is decorated with ikebana flower arrangements.
A visiting blogger described the ten-zaru's tempura as fragrant with sesame oil, with two pieces of plump shrimp alongside vegetable tempura, and the noodles as having a chewy, springy texture.

caveats: This is a traditional, independent hand-made soba and tempura shop with no published gluten-free or vegan menu, options, or allergy protocol found in any source checked (Tabelog, food blogs, listing sites). Soba noodles at hand-made soba shops of this style are typically blended with wheat flour for texture (this shop's exact flour ratio was not confirmed in any source), and tempura batter is wheat-flour based, so this is not a safe choice for celiac/gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from staff. Traditional Japanese soba-shop tsuyu (dipping sauce) is dashi-and-soy-sauce based and very likely contains bonito (katsuobushi) and wheat-containing soy sauce, so it is also not vegan-friendly by default. Cash only; no English-language menu or website was found.
closed_days: Sunday (also closed on public holidays per one source)
cuisine: Soba (hand-made) and tempura
cultural_comfort_note: An old-school, cash-only local soba shop with no confirmed English menu or signage, so bring a translation app to communicate any allergy needs.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or protocol were found in any source. This is a hand-made soba shop, and soba at shops of this style is typically blended with wheat flour (a 100%-buckwheat "juwari" option was not confirmed here). Tempura batter is wheat-flour based, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is very likely made with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. Not recommended for celiac diners without a directly-confirmed, dedicated preparation from staff.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:30; closed Sunday (one source also lists public holidays as closed)
neighborhood: West Shinjuku (西新宿), near Shinjuku Station West Exit

As a traditional soba shop, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is almost certainly made with dashi that includes katsuobushi (bonito flakes), and tempura may be fried in oil shared across items. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, dish-by-dish confirmation from staff.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草4-43-4

bio.origin: Opened April 2016 in Asakusa
bio.background: The shop's owner, Shida Akihisa (nicknamed "Dai-ō," meaning "the King," hence the shop's name), has a Taiwanese mother and spent time as a child in Taipei, particularly in the old downtown district of Wanhua near Longshan Temple — an area he describes as feeling like Taipei's own Asakusa. He grew up eating douhua there and, after years of self-taught trial and error trying to recreate the authentic texture and flavor he remembered, opened Asakusa's first dedicated douhua specialty shop.
bio.specialty: Douhua (豆花) — a silky, unsweetened soy-milk pudding served warm or cold, customized to order with a choice of syrup (brown sugar, brown-sugar ginger, or limited-batch peanut) and a pick of over ten toppings such as tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, and herbal jelly. The shop also serves Taiwanese-style shaved ice and the street snack congyoubing (scallion pancake).
bio.philosophy: Shida's stated goal is to spread douhua in Japan in a form that suits Japanese palates, treating himself as something of a "missionary" for the dessert. Beyond the shop, he runs douhua-making classes for individuals and groups, and several of his students have gone on to open their own douhua-related shops elsewhere in Japan.

anecdotes.text: A local Taito City tourism feature describes the shop as tucked away like a "hidden house" in Asakusa where visitors can experience the world of jiggly, silky douhua.

A Sanpo no Tatsujin (散歩の達人) feature is titled "The douhua missionary pursuing the taste he first encountered as a child in Taipei," tying the shop directly to Shida's childhood memories of Wanhua district.

caveats: Address, hours, phone, and seat count were confirmed via search-engine result snippets citing Tabelog, Hotpepper, and local Taito-ku tourism sources rather than fully-loaded page fetches (several target sites, including Tabelog itself, returned 403 errors when fetched directly in this environment). The official-looking domain asakusa-tofa.net does not resolve to any server, so it was not used; to-fa.com resolves (Squarespace-hosted) but could not be directly fetched to confirm content, so official_url confidence is moderate. Vegan/gluten-free framing is based on aggregated third-party summaries (including a Vegewel listing) rather than a directly verified allergen statement from the shop itself — treat gf_confidence and vegan_status as good-faith estimates, not guarantees, and confirm cross-contact concerns with staff, especially given the shop also serves a wheat-based scallion pancake (congyoubing) in the same kitchen.
closed_days: Tuesdays (火曜定休)
cuisine: 台湾豆花 (Taiwanese douhua / soy-milk pudding dessert shop), plant-based-friendly
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, walk-in dessert shop popular with both locals and Asakusa-area visitors; ordering involves picking a syrup and toppings, which is easy to navigate even with limited Japanese, though menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.
geocode_note: Not geocoded in this environment (network geocoding blocked). Address is in Asakusa 4-chome, Taito-ku, roughly a 14-minute walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line / Toei Asakusa Line), per Tabelog and multiple local sources — place the pin from address_ja downstream and confirm proximity to the Sensōji target circle.
gf_detail: The signature douhua is made only from soy milk and a coagulant with no wheat in the base, and most syrup choices (brown sugar, brown-sugar ginger, peanut) and toppings (tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, herbal jelly) appear naturally wheat-free based on ingredient descriptions found. However, this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: the shop also serves congyoubing, a Taiwanese wheat-flour scallion pancake, prepared in the same space, so cross-contact is possible. No information was found on a dedicated fryer or a formal allergen protocol — diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about how the douhua is prepared alongside the pancake item.
hours_raw: Weekdays 12:00-20:00 (one source lists 20:01), Saturdays/Sundays/Holidays 11:00-19:00, closed Tuesdays
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop also serves congyoubing (a wheat-flour Taiwanese scallion pancake) alongside the soy-based douhua, indicating the kitchen is not exclusively wheat-free even though the douhua itself contains no wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A search-engine summary of the shop's Vegewel restaurant listing indicates that aside from the condensed milk (練乳) used on one shaved-ice item, the shop's sweets use no animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Search-engine summaries citing the shop's Vegewel listing describe it as animal-product-free aside from the condensed milk on shaved ice, and note the shop welcomes vegan customers.

seats_text: Approximately 27 seats (six 4-person tables plus 3 counter seats), per a search-engine summary of the shop's Hotpepper listing.
vegan_detail: The soy-milk douhua base and most syrup/topping combinations (brown sugar syrup, tapioca, red bean, mung bean, taro balls, peanuts, herbal jelly) contain no animal products. A search-engine summary of the shop's Vegewel listing states the shop's sweets use no animal-derived ingredients apart from the condensed milk (練乳) drizzled on one shaved-ice item, and that vegan customers are welcome. The menu also includes the savory item congyoubing (scallion pancake), whose vegan status was not confirmed. Ask staff to point out any non-vegan items when ordering.

lead: 背脂ラーメン チャッチャ亭
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-5 島田ビル1F

bio.background: Opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in August 2023 by owner Manabu Kobayashi, taking over the site of the previous shop, Tokyo Seabura Ramen Menō (東京背脂らーめん 麺王), and rebranding it to put its signature back-fat technique front and center. The shop has since grown into a small local mini-chain, with a second location opening in Shimokitazawa in May 2026.
bio.specialty: Seabura ramen — a pork-bone shoyu broth finished by straining and ladling a generous pour of slow-simmered pork back fat over the noodles just before serving.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists on A-grade back fat, prized for its clean sweetness and lack of gaminess compared to cheaper grades, and refuses to serve fat that has lost its freshness. Because the back fat itself carries so much richness, the pork-bone broth is kept deliberately simple so extra seasoning doesn't fight the fat's own flavor.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the crisp 'chaccha' sound the melted back fat makes as it is strained through a sieve and ladled over the ramen at the finish.
Owner Manabu Kobayashi specifically sources A-rank back fat for its sweetness and absence of off-odors, and won't plate fat that isn't fresh.

caveats: Small owner-run backfat-ramen counter; pork-bone (tonkotsu) shoyu broth with wheat noodles and a soy-sauce tare — no gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found in any source, so this listing is included for completeness/context rather than as a safe pick for celiac or vegan diners. Seat count, exact closing-day policy, and payment methods could not be independently confirmed beyond secondary aggregator listings (Tabelog itself returned 403 to automated fetch, so this record relies on Hitosara/RamenDB/blog/social snippets plus web-search excerpts of the Tabelog listing).
closed_days: No fixed closed day reported (multiple sources describe it as open daily, including Sundays); one secondary snippet suggested "irregular closures" are possible, so treat as generally open daily but unconfirmed for holidays.
cuisine: Ramen (Tonkotsu shoyu with back fat)
cultural_comfort_note: A small local ramen counter with a Japanese-only menu and no dedicated English signage found in any source — bring a translation app or be ready to point at photos.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled); address and multiple listings place it on Dogenzaka in Shibuya, described as about a 3-minute walk from Shibuya Station and also near Shinsen Station — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target point.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles and the tare is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which contains wheat; no mention of a gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol. Not suitable for a celiac diner.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00, reportedly open daily including Sundays (one older snippet listed 11:00-22:30, so confirm current hours before visiting)
name_ja: 背脂ラーメン チャッチャ亭
neighborhood: Dogenzaka / Shinsen, Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone shoyu (soy sauce) base, and soy sauce in Japanese ramen shops is standardly wheat-based unless stated otherwise; no gluten-free or wheat-free tare was mentioned in any source.

seats_text: Counter seating plus a couple of tables toward the back (exact count not confirmed)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options found. The broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based and finished with rendered pork back fat, the signature element of the dish, and the shop's entire concept centers on that pork fat — there is no indication of a vegetable- or soy-based broth alternative.

lead: バンコックコスモ食堂
address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-1-13 櫻井ビル1F

bio.origin: Isaan region, Khon Kaen Province, Thailand
bio.background: Bangkok Cosmo Shokudo is run by a Thai cook from Khon Kaen Province in Thailand's Isaan region who trained at well-known restaurants in Thailand before coming to Japan. It opened in October 2016 as the newest member of a small family of Thai "shokudo" (Peanuts Shokudo, Pony Shokudo, and Lalala are its sister restaurants), set in a retro, Showa-era storefront along Hakusan-dori near Jimbocho.
bio.specialty: Gai Gapao (ガイガパオ) — coarsely hand-minced chicken stir-fried with holy basil, served over jasmine rice with a fried egg
bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists that gapao "means nothing without the aroma of real holy basil," so it sources holy basil directly from Thai-run contract farms rather than substituting Japanese basil, aiming for the sweet-spicy-sour balance of true Isaan home cooking.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is one of a small group of Bangkok-named shokudo (Peanuts, Pony, Lalala, and Cosmo) known for hiring genuine Thai cooks and sourcing specialty ingredients like contract-farmed holy basil for their gapao.
Opened October 2016 in a retro storefront themed around 'Asian chaos' on Jimbocho's Hakusan-dori.

caveats: Sources give slightly conflicting weekday hours (one aggregator listed Mon-Fri lunch as 11:00-17:00 / dinner 17:00-23:00 with different Sat/Sun/holiday hours, while Tabelog and Gnavi both independently report a single Mon-Sat+holiday schedule of 11:15-15:00 / 17:30-23:00 with Sunday closed) — the latter, more consistent version is used here. No official statement on gluten-free or vegan protocols was found; only general notes that spice level can be adjusted. Call ahead (03-6261-1977) to confirm hours and discuss any allergy before visiting.
closed_days: Sundays
cuisine: Thai cuisine (Isaan-style)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood Thai "shokudo" popular with local office workers and students, not a tourist-oriented spot — basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will help, though staff are used to explaining spice levels.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Multiple independent sources place the restaurant at 西神田2-1-13 on Hakusan-dori, described as a 4-minute walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A4 and a 5-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station — consistent with being within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is documented in any source found. Thai cuisine here leans on rice (jasmine rice, some rice noodle dishes) which lowers baseline wheat exposure versus Japanese izakaya food, but soy sauce, oyster sauce, and other seasonings used in the kitchen are not confirmed wheat-free. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6261-1977) and ask about ingredients dish-by-dish rather than assume safety.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:15-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 21:45), Monday-Saturday and public holidays; closed Sundays. (One secondary listing gives slightly different weekday hours of 11:00-17:00/17:00-23:00 — treat the times above, corroborated by Tabelog and Gnavi, as the more reliable baseline and confirm by phone if precision matters.)
name_ja: バンコックコスモ食堂
neighborhood: Nishikanda (西神田), Chiyoda-ku — between Jimbocho and Suidobashi

positives.text: The kitchen is particular about ingredient sourcing — it uses genuine holy basil from Thai-run contract farms for its gapao dishes rather than a substitute, suggesting attentiveness to authentic, unadulterated flavors.

seats_text: Approximately 30 seats, including counter seating.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegetarian or vegan menu is documented. Thai home-style cooking of this kind typically relies on fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste (kapi) as base seasonings, so most dishes should be assumed non-vegan unless the kitchen confirms otherwise. Some vegetable-forward items (e.g., fresh spring rolls) may be adaptable on request, but this was not independently confirmed by any source found — ask staff directly and consider calling ahead.

lead: シズラー新宿三井ビル店
address_ja: 〒163-0436 東京都新宿区西新宿2丁目1-1 新宿三井ビル2F

bio.background: Sizzler began in 1958 when the Johnson family opened a steakhouse in Culver City, California, paring the menu down to two cuts of steak with salad and bread and serving it through a then-novel self-service format so a steak dinner could be affordable. The brand landed in Japan in 1991, and this Shinjuku Mitsui Building restaurant was Japan's very first Sizzler. The store closed in December 2023 for a full renovation and reopened in May 2025 with a 'catering party'-inspired layout built around a slimmer, easier-to-navigate salad bar.
bio.origin: California, USA (Culver City, 1958); first Japan location, 1991
bio.specialty: Premium Salad Bar with 70+ rotating items — fresh vegetables, deli salads, soups, pasta, tacos and desserts — served alongside grilled steaks; the all-you-can-eat Cheese Toast is a house classic dating back to the original 1968 Sizzler Hollywood menu.
bio.philosophy: Built on Sizzler's founding idea of making a steak dinner accessible through a self-serve, all-you-can-eat format centered on a big salad bar rather than a formal steakhouse experience.

anecdotes.text: Sizzler's Cheese Toast first appeared in 1968 at the original Sizzler in Hollywood and has been served all-you-can-eat, just like the salad bar, at Sizzler locations worldwide ever since, including here.

This location was Sizzler's first restaurant in Japan when it opened in 1991; after an 18-month renovation the flagship reopened in May 2025 and the brand ran a year-long 'ALL-STAR REVIVAL FAIR' revisiting menu items from its 34 years in Japan.

caveats: This is a chain family-restaurant/grill (Royal Holdings group), not a vegan- or GF-dedicated venue — its appeal for this app is purely the size and freshness of the self-serve salad bar (70+ items). No Japan-specific allergen labeling, dedicated fryer, or vegan menu was found in any source checked; GF/vegan diners have real options but must self-navigate the buffet. Hours found in different secondary sources conflicted (some older listings show a 20:00 close); the 22:00 close (LO 21:30) used here is corroborated by the May 2025 renewal announcement and a subsequent search summary and is more likely current post-renovation.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day found; follows building/holiday schedule.
cuisine: Premium salad bar and grill — extensive salad selections with 70+ fresh vegetable, fruit, deli-salad, soup, pasta and dessert options, plus grilled steak/chicken mains
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-run American-concept chain restaurant with a visual, point-and-serve salad bar that's easy to navigate with minimal Japanese, though menus and staff are not confirmed to be English-fluent at this specific location.
gf_detail: The Premium Salad Bar's large selection of whole fresh vegetables and fruit gives gluten-sensitive diners real, naturally GF choices, but it is a self-serve buffet: official menu descriptions confirm bread, pasta, and taco tortillas are set out on the same bar as the produce, dressings' ingredients are not itemized, and no dedicated gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or allergen labeling has been confirmed for this Japan location. Celiac diners should serve vegetable/fruit items first, ask staff about specific dressings, and treat cross-contact as likely rather than controlled.
geocode_note: Address is Nishi-Shinjuku 2-1-1, Shinjuku Mitsui Building 2F — about a 1-minute walk from Toei Oedo Line Tochomae Station and 2 minutes from Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line Nishi-Shinjuku Station, within easy walking distance of Shinjuku Station's west side/underground concourse. Not geocoded per instructions; lat/lng left null.
hours_raw: 平日 (Mon-Fri): Lunch 11:00-15:00, then 15:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) — effectively continuous 11:00-22:00. 土日祝 (Sat/Sun/holidays): Morning 8:00-10:00, then 10:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) — effectively continuous 8:00-22:00.
name_ja: シズラー 新宿三井ビル店
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku / Tochomae (都庁前, directly connected to Shinjuku Mitsui Building)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The self-serve Premium Salad Bar places wheat-containing items — bread, pasta, and taco tortillas — on the same buffet line as the fresh vegetables and fruit, using shared serving utensils, so cross-contact is likely for anyone strictly avoiding gluten.

positives.text: With 70+ items including whole fresh seasonal vegetables and fruit, the salad bar gives diners plenty of naturally plant-based, unprocessed options to build a meal around without relying on prepared or sauced dishes.

seats_text: Approximately 178 seats in a large buffet-style dining room (secondary-source figure, not confirmed on the official site)
vegan_detail: The salad bar's fresh vegetables, fruit, and some pasta/rice items give vegans real choices, but many of the deli salads, dressings, soups, and hot dishes share the same buffet and are not confirmed to be dairy-, egg-, or fish/bonito-free — Japanese buffets commonly use bonito-based dashi in soups and some dressings. No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or confirmation was found for this location, so check ingredients dish-by-dish.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-12 道玄坂通1階

bio.origin: Kanda Tamagoken chain (10th location)
bio.background: This Shibuya Dogenzaka shop is the 10th location of Kanda Tamagoken, a Tokyo omurice (omelette rice) chain. It opened on Dogenzaka-dori in April 2025 with an open kitchen so guests can watch the omurice being made counter-side.
bio.specialty: Fluffy, molten ("toro-toro") egg omurice over freshly pan-fried chicken rice, topped with tomato or hayashi demi-glace sauce; the Wagyu katsu omurice is a signature draw.
bio.philosophy: The stated aim is to make proper, well-made omurice something anyone -- including solo diners -- can enjoy easily and casually, not just a special-occasion dish.

anecdotes.text: A food writer described eating the Wagyu katsu omurice at the Dogenzaka shop as making for a very happy, indulgent Shibuya meal.

caveats: This is a mainstream omurice (omelette rice) chain restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment. No dedicated GF or vegan menu, allergen chart, or plant-based option was found in any source checked (official site, Tabelog, press releases, blog coverage). The signature dishes use wheat-thickened demi-glace/hayashi sauce, breaded deep-fried katsu, butter, egg, dairy, and meat -- assume unsafe for celiac/strict vegan diners unless staff confirm otherwise on site.
closed_days: 年中無休（1月1日を除く） -- open every day of the year except January 1st.
cuisine: オムライス (Omurice / omelette rice specialty)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern chain restaurant with an open kitchen and app-based mobile ordering ("注文くん"); menus and ordering are Japan-oriented and no English-speaking-staff or English-menu confirmation was found, though Tabelog offers an English listing page.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked). Address confirms location on Dogenzaka-dori (道玄坂通), Shibuya-ku -- well within an 800m walk of Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595, 139.7005), consistent with the stated "Shibuya Station, 5 min walk" area.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation was found in any source. The chicken rice, demi-glace/hayashi sauce, and breaded katsu toppings are all conventionally made with wheat-based ingredients (soy sauce, flour-thickened sauce, breadcrumbs), so this is not considered safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct kitchen confirmation.
neighborhood: 渋谷・道玄坂（渋谷駅徒歩5分）
seats_text: Counter seating for 6 plus table seating for 24 (about 30 seats total)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu was found. Dishes are built around egg, chicken, butter, dairy-based/demi-glace sauces, and meat toppings (including Wagyu katsu); there is no indication of a plant-based rice, sauce, or protein alternative.

cuisine: Yakitori / Grilled Chicken (Japanese)
neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku
vegan_detail: Menu centers on chicken: skewer courses (yakitori), chicken-and-egg oyakodon, chicken ramen, and chicken ochazuke. No vegan or vegetarian options were found in any source; this is a chicken-focused specialty restaurant.
gf_detail: This is a traditional tare-style yakitori-ya — the glaze (tare) used on many skewers is typically soy-sauce based (wheat). Shio (salt-grilled) skewers may avoid the tare glaze, but no dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or cross-contamination statement was found on any source, and no English allergy information was located. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-3288-0031) and ask specifically about tare ingredients and shared grill/cross-contact.
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-18-2
geocode_note: Address places it about a 1-minute walk from the west exit of JR Suidōbashi Station (per multiple listing sites), well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station target radius. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; geocoding to be done downstream from address_ja.
seats_text: Seat count not confirmed in available sources.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 17:00-23:00 (dinner); closed Sundays and public holidays.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional neighborhood yakitori-ya with no evidence of English menus, English signage, or foreign-visitor accommodations — expect a Japanese-language-only experience.

bio.background: A compact, stylish yakitori restaurant tucked near Suidōbashi Station, built around the idea of letting guests enjoy quality grilled chicken and sake in a calm, black-toned interior — the kind of understated hideaway suited to a date or a quiet client dinner.
bio.specialty: Skewer courses built from carefully sourced regional chicken breeds — jiyō-kei, Niwa black chicken (丹羽黒鶏), and Kyoto red jidori (京紅地鶏) — grilled part by part so each cut's character comes through; at lunch, the shop is known for an oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) that has been featured in magazines.
bio.philosophy: A focus on selecting quality local/heritage chicken breeds and grilling each part of the bird individually, so diners can taste the difference between cuts rather than treating yakitori as a single uniform dish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's lunchtime oyakodon (chicken and egg rice bowl) has been featured in magazines.
The concept of the shop is described as letting guests enjoy quality grilled chicken (焼鶏) and sake, using selected chickens such as jiyō-kei, Niwa black chicken, and Kyoto red jidori.

caveats: No source (Tabelog, official site, or aggregator listings) mentions gluten-free, vegan, or allergy accommodations specifically — this appears to be a standard, non-specialized yakitori restaurant. Dietary guidance above is inferred from the general nature of tare-style yakitori cuisine, not from any restaurant statement. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead to confirm.

lead: こんごう庵 神保町店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-18-1 千石屋ビル1F

roles: Niigata regional cuisine izakaya / soba restaurant
one of ~4 locations run by the Niigata-rooted company Kongoan (main store in Niigata, plus Tokyo branches in Jinbocho, Okachimachi and Yushima/Ueno)
bio.background: Kongoan is a small Niigata-origin restaurant company whose Tokyo branches (including this Jinbocho location, a one-minute walk from Jinbocho Station exit A5) bring Niigata's signature hegisoba noodles and prefectural sake to the capital. The shop advertises fresh seafood and vegetables shipped directly from Niigata rather than sourced locally in Tokyo.
bio.origin: Niigata Prefecture (main store), with this being the Jinbocho branch in Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Hegisoba — Niigata-style soba noodles bound with funori seaweed (instead of egg or nagaimo yam) and served pleated in small individual bundles on a large wooden box ("hegi"), giving a smooth, glossy, chewy texture
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Niigata's food and drink culture — hegisoba, fresh Niigata seafood/vegetables and Echigo regional sake — for a Tokyo audience, pairing izakaya-style seasonal dishes with a hegisoba finish

anecdotes.text: A lunchtime blog review praised the shop's tare-katsudon (sauce-glazed pork cutlet rice bowl) paired with hegisoba as a satisfying, good-value set (around ¥1,000–¥1,050), also calling out the shrimp tempura.
A food-walking blog visited specifically for the hegisoba and Niigata local sake, describing the pairing of Echigo sake flights with seasonal seafood/vegetable dishes.
A Tabelog reviewer commented on the tare-katsu being satisfying but had questions about the hegisoba itself.

caveats: Chain-run izakaya (Niigata company with ~4 locations, not a solo independent shop). No gluten-free or vegan-specific information is published by the restaurant; assessment below is inferred from general hegisoba/izakaya conventions, not a restaurant-specific confirmation, so should be treated cautiously for celiac/severe-allergy diners. Some sources gave slightly differing weekend lunch closing times (14:30 vs 15:30); 15:30 was used as it appeared in the more detailed listing.
closed_days: Year-end/New Year holiday period (年末年始); otherwise open daily
cuisine: Niigata-style hegisoba soba and regional Echigo sake izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed; this reads as a traditional Japanese regional-cuisine izakaya best visited with a translation app or basic Japanese.
geocode_note: Networking geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng left null). Address (東京都千代田区神田神保町1-18-1) and multiple listings place it one minute's walk from Jinbocho Station exit A5, which is within easy walking distance (well under 0.8km) of Suidōbashi Station, satisfying the target-area requirement.
gf_detail: Hegisoba is traditionally bound with funori seaweed rather than egg or nagaimo, which in principle needs less wheat flour than some other soba styles, but this shop does not publish its soba-to-wheat-flour ratio, and standard izakaya tsuyu (dipping sauce) and other cooked dishes in Japan typically use wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, dedicated cookware, or celiac-specific protocol was found in any source. Treat as ask-only: call ahead and confirm ingredients/cross-contact directly before relying on this for a celiac-safe meal.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: Lunch 11:30–14:30, Dinner 17:00–23:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays: Lunch 11:30–15:30, Dinner 17:00–21:00
name_ja: こんごう庵 神保町店
neighborhood: Jinbocho (神保町), Chiyoda-ku — one minute's walk from Jinbocho Station exit A5
seats_text: Table seats for 2–6 guests, counter seating, and one private tatami room seating 4–7; tables can be combined for parties up to 14.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and pork-centered Niigata izakaya: signature items include hegisoba served with a standard (likely bonito/katsuo-based) tsuyu dipping sauce, tare-katsudon (pork cutlet), shrimp tempura, and fresh Niigata seafood dishes. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan-adapted hegisoba was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-friendly beyond possibly a side vegetable dish.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-7-5 一兆ビル 3F・4F

bio.background: Nanjamonja is one location in a small multi-branch okonomiyaki/monjayaki izakaya group (other branches exist in Nakano-Shimbashi and Hōnan-chō in Tokyo, plus locations in Nagoya, Osaka, and Toba). The Shinjuku-Sanchome branch sits on Suehiro-dōri and spans the 3rd and 4th floors of the Icchō Building, with tatami zashiki seating, sunken kotatsu tables, and a teppan counter, built around all-you-can-eat/all-you-can-drink okonomiyaki and monjayaki courses.
bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat course featuring dozens of okonomiyaki and monjayaki varieties (roughly 70-80 types) grilled tableside on the teppan, alongside standard izakaya small plates.

caveats: Sources disagree on the exact daily opening times (variously reported as ~16:30 weekdays with a 13:00 weekend lunch opening, or a flat 16:00-24:00 daily, or 17:00 start on Sundays/holidays) — treat hours_week as approximate and confirm by phone (03-3356-5127) or via the Tabelog/HotPepper listing before visiting. This is an okonomiyaki/monjayaki all-you-can-eat izakaya, not a spot with any stated allergy or vegan program; no gluten-free or vegan-specific claims were found on any source — the dietary fields reflect that absence, not a confirmed accommodation.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed days, open including New Year's Day per listings)
cuisine: Okonomiyaki / Monjayaki (teppan-grilled) izakaya, with a 70-80 item all-you-can-eat course
cultural_comfort_note: Listings and menus found are Japanese-only with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so bring a translation app or basic Japanese.
gf_detail: The entire menu centers on okonomiyaki and monjayaki, both made from wheat-flour batter cooked on a shared teppan grill, so there is no gluten-free path here. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free batter were mentioned by any source; soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce used are standard (wheat-containing) varieties. Not suitable for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based placement. Address (新宿区新宿3-7-5) and multiple sources describing it as steps from Shinjuku-Sanchome Station / a few minutes' walk from Shinjuku Station confirm it sits well within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: Sources vary: some report weekdays 16:30-24:00 with Sat/Sun 13:00-16:00 and 16:30-24:00 (lunch break in between); one HotPepper-derived summary reports a flat 16:00-24:00 daily; another reports 18:00-24:00 weekdays and 17:00-24:00 Sun/holidays. Consistent across all sources: no regular closed days (無休), last food order 23:00, last drink order 23:30.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-Sanchome), Shinjuku

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki are both wheat-flour-batter dishes cooked on a shared teppan grill used for all orders, so cross-contact with gluten is essentially unavoidable; no gluten-free batter or dedicated equipment was found in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegetable-only or vegan course was identified; okonomiyaki/monjayaki are typically prepared with dashi-based sauces and shared teppan surfaces alongside meat and seafood items.

seats_text: Approximately 70 seats across the 3rd and 4th floors, including tatami zashiki seating with sunken kotatsu and a teppan counter; groups of up to ~50 can be accommodated for parties.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based course, or vegan labeling was found. In principle a vegetable-topping okonomiyaki (no meat/seafood) could be ordered, but the standard okonomiyaki/monjayaki sauces and dashi used in this style of cooking commonly contain bonito (katsuo) or other animal-derived stock, and this was not confirmed either way for this restaurant. Vegan diners would need to ask staff in detail about sauce and dashi ingredients before ordering.

lead: イタリアンチーズバル AND TABLE 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町28-1 高山ランドビル 6F

bio.background: Opened in 2021 on Shibuya's Center Street, this Italian cheese bar is run by a young owner-manager team rather than a large corporate chain. The kitchen is led by a chef with prior experience at established restaurants, cooking with seasonal ingredients.
bio.specialty: Cheese-forward Italian small plates and pizza, all-you-can-eat cheese course, aged (jukusei) roast beef, and seared horse-meat carpaccio; crab cream pasta is frequently mentioned as a standout.
bio.philosophy: A casual, date-friendly "cheese bar" concept blending authentic Italian technique (seafood pasta, cured meats) with playful fusion touches, served in a dim, café-like room meant to feel relaxed rather than formal.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as run by a young owner and manager team focused on hospitality and "surprising guests while keeping their original passion."
Known for an all-you-can-eat cheese course alongside aged beef and horse-meat carpaccio, with a relaxed, softly lit interior suited to dates and small groups.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog or the restaurant's own reservation-site pages (blocked by network egress policy in this environment, HTTP 403 on tabelog.com and owst.jp); all facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets of those and other listing pages (Hitosara, Hot Pepper, NAVITIME, Instagram), not from a direct page fetch. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan information was found anywhere for this specific restaurant, so dietary fields are conservative/low-confidence by design, not confirmed accommodations.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / open every day, no regular closed days）
cuisine: イタリアン・チーズバル (Italian cheese bar / izakaya-style)
cultural_comfort_note: No evidence of an English menu or foreign-visitor-oriented service was found; this reads as a standard Japanese-language izakaya-style bar aimed at local diners.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya-ku, Udagawa-cho 28-1, near Shibuya Center-gai) and multiple listings confirm it is about a 3-minute walk from Shibuya Station, well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble (35.6595,139.7005). Coordinates intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding from address_ja should place it inside the target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is advertised anywhere for this restaurant. The menu centers on pizza, pasta, and bread-adjacent cheese dishes (wheat-heavy), and none of the listing sites mention gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or allergen protocols. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here and would need to call ahead in Japanese and get explicit confirmation before relying on this venue.
name_en: Italian Cheese Bar AND TABLE Shibuya
name_ja: イタリアンチーズバル AND TABLE 渋谷店
neighborhood: 渋谷・センター街（渋谷駅徒歩3分）
seats_text: Not confirmed; listing sites reference private-room/group bookings for parties of roughly 2-60, which reflects party-booking capacity rather than a confirmed total seat count.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is advertised. The food is built around cheese, cured/aged meats (roast beef, horse-meat carpaccio), and seafood-based pasta, with no mention of plant-based substitutions, hidden dashi/katsuo disclosure, or vegan-friendly dishes on any listing site found. Vegan diners should not expect suitable options without calling ahead, and even then accommodation is unconfirmed.

lead: ビストロ イルシーブ
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町1-2-3 UTビル 1F

bio.specialty: スモーク仕立ての鴨料理（鴨のミニバーガー、ジューシーな鴨のメイン皿など）を中心とした季節の創作フレンチ
bio.philosophy: 枠にとらわれない自由な発想で四季を感じさせる創作料理を提供し、農園の有機野菜、豊洲市場の天然魚介、丁寧に下処理した厳選肉を使い、素材の持ち味と組み合わせの妙を大切にする
roles: シェフ（詳細経歴は未確認）

anecdotes.text: 路地の一角にある小さめのビストロで、静かな空気の中、スモークの香りが立つ鴨のミニバーガーや、柔らかくジューシーなメインの鴨など、魚も肉も種類豊富で、一皿ごとに満足感があると評された。一人でもハーフサイズで注文でき、グラスワインの提案も的確だったとの声もある。
農園から届く有機野菜、豊洲市場の天然魚介、丁寧に下処理した厳選肉を使用し、素材の持ち味と組み合わせの妙を大切にした、枠にとらわれない自由な発想の季節料理を提供する神保町のビストロ。

caveats: Secondary sources give slightly conflicting lunch-day details (one lists Wed/Thu/Fri lunch service, another lists Thu-Sat) and the restaurant is 不定休 (irregular closures), so exact weekly hours should be reconfirmed directly with the restaurant before visiting. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu/program was found in any source; the menu is described as meat- and seafood-forward French bistro cuisine (duck, Toyosu-market fish), and classic French sauces commonly rely on wheat-based roux/beurre manié, so diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-6281-3300) to discuss options rather than assume accommodation. Full Tabelog page could not be directly fetched (403) in this environment; facts are drawn from Ikyu, search-indexed excerpts of Tabelog/kaiten-heiten/other listing sites, and a food-review article, all cited by URL.
closed_days: 不定休（臨時休業あり）
cuisine: フランス料理（ビストロ）
cultural_comfort_note: Reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Tabelog, Ikyu, Autoreserve) and no English menu or English-language official presence was found, so Japanese ability or translation help is recommended.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free sauce/soy-sauce information was found in any source. As a French bistro, sauces are likely to use wheat-based roux/beurre manié and bread is likely served as part of the meal; cross-contamination risk in the kitchen is unconfirmed either way. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6281-3300) to discuss specific dishes before booking.
name_ja: ビストロ イルシーブ
neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（神田猿楽町）
seats_text: 10 seats (6 counter, 4 table)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, hidden-dashi/bonito disclosure, or plant-based course was found in any source. The restaurant's menu and reviews center on meat (duck) and Toyosu-market seafood in an omakase/course format, suggesting limited built-in vegan options; a la carte vegetable dishes may exist but are unconfirmed. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.

lead: バインセオサイゴン 東京ドームシティ店
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1丁目3-61 黄色いビル2階 フードスタジアム東京内

bio.origin: Originally a street-food stall in Ebisu ("バインセオサイゴン屋台恵比寿"); relocated to Tokyo Dome City's newly opened FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall on June 24, 2024.
bio.background: Part of the Banh Xeo Saigon group of Ho Chi Minh-style Vietnamese street-food stalls in Tokyo (other branches include Shinjuku and Yurakucho). The Tokyo Dome City branch moved here from its original Ebisu location when the food hall opened.
bio.specialty: Bánh xèo — a large, turmeric-yellow rice-flour crepe filled with pork, shrimp and bean sprouts, wrapped in lettuce and herbs and dipped in nước chấm; also known for phở and gỏi cuốn (fresh rice-paper spring rolls) and the pork-broth noodle dish hủ tiếu.
bio.philosophy: Described by the operator as being thoroughly committed to authentic Ho Chi Minh City street-food flavor ("とことん本場ベトナムの味にこだわった本格ベトナム・ホーチミン屋台料理店"), aiming to recreate a casual Saigon street-stall atmosphere inside the food hall.

anecdotes.text: The bánh xèo is prepared by a Vietnamese chef who trained in Ho Chi Minh City for more than 15 years before bringing the recipe to Japan.
Vietnamese customers have reportedly told the shop the bánh xèo tastes even better than what they ate back home in Vietnam.

caveats: No confirmed allergen/cross-contamination policy was found from the restaurant itself; gluten-free and vegan assessments below are based on the general composition of the Vietnamese dishes on offer, not a restaurant statement, so diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm specifics (sauces, shared fryer/wok) with staff before ordering.
closed_days: None — the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall operates year-round with no regular closed days (年中無休).
cuisine: Vietnamese (Ho Chi Minh City street food) — bánh xèo specialty
cultural_comfort_note: Located in a large modern food hall inside Tokyo Dome City with an English-language listing page, but ordering and menu detail are primarily in Japanese so some phrase preparation helps.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address and multiple independent sources place it 3 minutes' walk from JR Suidōbashi Station west exit, well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station target point.
gf_detail: Several dishes are naturally based on rice rather than wheat (phở noodles, gỏi cuốn rice-paper spring rolls, and the bánh xèo batter itself, which is rice flour and turmeric based), which is typical of Vietnamese cuisine generally. However, no gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy/dipping-sauce information was found from the restaurant, and fried items (e.g. fried spring rolls) likely share cooking equipment with other menu items. Not verified safe for celiac disease — ask staff about nước chấm/nước mắm ingredients and frying setup before ordering.
name_en: Banh Xeo Saigon Tokyo Dome City
name_ja: バインセオサイゴン 東京ドームシティ店
neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi) / Tokyo Dome City
seats_text: No dedicated seat count found for this stall; it shares the roughly 480-seat communal seating area of the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall with the other vendors there.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan or vegetarian menu was found. Signature dishes (bánh xèo, gỏi cuốn, phở, hủ tiếu) are typically made with pork and/or shrimp and broths are usually meat-based; dipping sauces (nước chấm/nước mắm) are fish-sauce based and not vegan. Some vegetable-forward items may exist but were not confirmed. Vegans should ask staff directly about substitutions and hidden fish sauce.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2丁目12-11 松下ビル

bio.background: A small, counter-only Edomae sushi restaurant in Shibuya where the chef reportedly goes to Toyosu Market each morning to select the day's fish personally.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi (omakase counter course)

anecdotes.text: The chef is described by diners as personable and welcoming, making the counter comfortable even for first-time guests, and is known for personally sourcing seasonal seafood at Toyosu Market each morning.

caveats: Hours, seat count, and price band are drawn from aggregator listings (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, others) rather than a fetchable official site, since most restaurant-portal pages returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; figures were cross-checked across multiple independent listings and were consistent. No gluten-free or vegan-specific sourcing was found for this restaurant at all — it is a traditional fish-based Edomae sushi counter with no indication of dietary accommodation, so GF/vegan guidance here is a cautious default, not a confirmed policy.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cuisine: 寿司 (Edomae sushi)

As a traditional Edomae sushi counter, nigiri is typically brushed with tare/nikiri sauce and served with regular soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; cross-contact risk from shared soy sauce and shared prep surfaces has not been documented one way or the other. Celiac diners should call ahead (Japanese-language call likely needed) to ask specifically about wheat-free soy sauce and shared equipment before booking.

lead: ラーメン豚山 サクラステージ店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町1-4 渋谷サクラステージ SHIBUYA SIDE 1F

roles: Chain restaurant operated by Gift Holdings Inc. (株式会社ギフトホールディングス)
bio.origin: Machida, Tokyo (brand founded 2018)
bio.background: Butayama is part of Gift Holdings, the Tokyo ramen company built by Sho Tagawa, who trained at the noted Yokohama-style shop Ichirokuya before founding the Iekei chain Machida Shoten in 2008. Butayama grew out of Tagawa's personal love of Jiro-style ramen: its first shop opened in Machida in July 2018, at a time when chain-expanding the notoriously idiosyncratic Jiro genre was almost unheard of. It has since grown into Japan's largest Jiro-inspired chain, with roughly 50 locations across nine prefectures; the Shibuya Sakura Stage shop opened December 24, 2025, as the brand's first location right by Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen with thick, chewy all-wheat noodles, a semi-emulsified pork-bone and soy broth, tender braised pork, and a tall pile of cabbage and bean sprouts, customizable with garlic, extra vegetables, and oil ('mashimashi' add-ons); a smaller 125g noodle portion is also offered.
bio.philosophy: Built to make intimidating Jiro-style ramen approachable for people who find the genre's original shops hard to navigate, especially women, families, and first-timers, through printed ordering guides at every table, a smaller portion option, and attentive service, without diluting the genre's hallmark volume and richness. The parent company's stated motto is 'ramen, as a gift to the world.'

anecdotes.text: Butayama's first shop opened in Machida in July 2018 — at the time, chain-expanding a Jiro-inspired ramen concept was almost unprecedented. It has since grown into Japan's largest Jiro-style chain.

The chain was founded by Sho Tagawa, who trained at the Yokohama-style ramen shop Ichirokuya before opening the Iekei chain Machida Shoten in 2008; Butayama grew out of his personal enthusiasm for Jiro-style ramen.

Every table carries a printed guide to the Jiro-style ordering calls, a mini 125g noodle size is offered for smaller appetites, and staff provide paper aprons; the brand states on its official social media that it 'aims to be a shop that's easy for women and families with children to enter.'
The parent company's stated motto is 'ramen, as a gift to the world.'

caveats: This is a Jiro-style (二郎系) ramen chain built around wheat noodles and pork-bone broth — it is not a gluten-free or vegan destination. Included in the dataset for completeness/negative-match purposes only; celiac or vegan diners should avoid it.
closed_days: None found — reported as open daily (無休), closed only around the New Year's holidays.
cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン（豚骨醤油ベース、極太麺、ジャンク系）— Jiro-inspired ramen: pork-bone soy broth, thick wheat noodles, piled vegetables and pork
cultural_comfort_note: The brand prints simplified ordering-call instructions at every table specifically to help newcomers and women navigate the intimidating Jiro-style ordering ritual, but there is no confirmed English menu, so some Japanese comprehension or willingness to point at the printed guide is still needed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment). Address places the shop inside the Shibuya Sakura Stage complex ("SHIBUYA SIDE" building), immediately adjacent to Shibuya Station's south/Sakuragaoka exit — well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble crossing per multiple independent sources (official shop page, Shibuya Sakura Stage official site, ramen databases, news blogs).
gf_detail: Noodles are thick, chewy, wheat-based Jiro-style noodles (no gluten-free alternative found); the tare/broth is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat in Japan). No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or gluten-free noodle substitute was found in any source. The counter kitchen is small and shared, with no indication of separated prep for allergens. Not suitable for celiac diners.
name_ja: ラーメン豚山 サクラステージ店

vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based and finished with pork chashu and rendered pork fat/back-fat, a hallmark of the Jiro-style genre; tare is soy-sauce based. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, meat-free broth option, or plant-based substitute was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-54-4 JHVビル1F

bio.origin: Yakuin, Fukuoka (original shop opened 2018 by a Sri Lankan native)
bio.background: The original Fukuoka shop was opened in 2018 by a Sri Lankan native in the Yakuin district. When the Sri Lankan manager ran into a visa problem and had to return home, a longtime regular customer, Takao Okuma, quit his salaryman job and bought out the restaurant -- including its name -- to keep it running. A second Fukuoka branch ('Rスリランカ+') opened in Nakasu-Kawabata in February 2022, and the brand's first Tokyo branch opened in Jimbocho in October 2022 under the name 'RスリランカTOKYO,' later rebranded to '＆スリランカTOKYO.'
bio.specialty: 豚肩ロースステーキカレー (Pork Shoulder Steak Curry) -- roughly 200g of grilled pork shoulder steak on a Sri Lankan-style coconut-milk-and-spice curry made with about 7 spices imported from Sri Lanka, topped with red pepper.
bio.philosophy: A Japanese owner carrying forward a Sri Lankan friend's recipes and restaurant, adapting Sri Lankan spice curry to Japanese ingredients and palates rather than running it as a franchise copy.
roles: original Sri Lankan founder (2018, no longer involved)

anecdotes.text: The shop's original name, 'R,' is the initial of the Sri Lankan manager's late father -- chosen so that running the restaurant would feel like 'working together with his father in spirit.'
Okuma took over the whole restaurant, including its name, after quitting his corporate job when the Sri Lankan manager's visa situation forced him to return to Sri Lanka.
The Jimbocho branch opened in October 2022 as the brand's first Tokyo location, expanding from its Fukuoka roots.

caveats: Not a GF- or vegan-dedicated kitchen; it is a Sri Lankan curry specialty shop. Gluten and vegan status are based on general menu descriptions found in secondary sources (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, food blogs), not a fetched official allergen statement, since direct page fetches were blocked (HTTP 403) during verification. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-5280-7066) to confirm ingredients (soy sauce/wheat, dashi, cross-contact) before visiting.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休、日曜は夜間休業）
cuisine: Sri Lankan curry (soup-curry style), Fukuoka-origin specialty shop
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood curry shop primarily run in Japanese; menu and staff interaction are not confirmed to be English-friendly, though the dish itself (rice plus curry) is easy to point at and order.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区神田神保町1-54-4) is about a 3-minute walk from Jimbocho Station and roughly 7 minutes from Suidobashi Station, placing it well within 0.8km of the Suidobashi Station target point; lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
gf_detail: The curry base is coconut milk and spices served over rice (not bread/roti), and one secondary source describes the curry as not using wheat flour or oil in its preparation -- a promising sign for gluten-free diners. However, there is no confirmed information about whether soy sauce (often wheat-containing) is used in seasoning, nor any stated cross-contamination precautions or dedicated cooking equipment. Ask staff directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
name_en: & Sri Lanka Tokyo (formerly R Sri Lanka Tokyo)
neighborhood: 神保町（神保町駅北側、千代田区神田神保町）

positives.text: Curry is described as coconut-milk and spice based, served over rice rather than bread, and reportedly prepared without wheat flour or oil.

seats_text: Counter seating (5) plus table seating (30), about 35 seats total.
