Sri Lankan curry preparations can involve fish-based stock or dried shrimp (not confirmed either way here), so vegans should call ahead (03-5280-7066) to ask whether a vegetable-only curry can be made without hidden animal-derived stock.

lead: 京都 茶寮翠泉 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿2-6-1 新宿住友ビル1F

bio.origin: Kyoto (main honten in Takatsuji, Kyoto; this Shinjuku shop opened July 2020 as the brand's first Tokyo outlet)
bio.background: Saryo Suisen is a Kyoto-born matcha specialty teahouse brand, with its Shinjuku shop sitting on the ground floor of the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building directly connected to Tochomae Station, near the Tokyo Metropolitan Government building.
bio.specialty: Freshly made warm warabimochi (出来立て温わらび餅) kneaded to order from genuine warabi starch, matcha and wasanbon sugar, and 100%-buckwheat matcha soba (十割抹茶そば) made in-house daily.
bio.philosophy: The shop is built around Uji matcha selected by Kyoto tea appraiser Haruhide Morita, and deliberately uses koicha-grade (thick-tea) matcha — the richer, more ceremonial grade usually reserved for formal tea ceremony — in its sweets and drinks rather than the thinner usucha grade most matcha cafes use.
roles: Kyoto matcha teahouse / sweets shop, Tokyo (Shinjuku) branch

anecdotes.text: The shop's warm warabimochi has been featured on Japanese TV (Hirunandesu!) as a specialty best eaten within about 10 minutes of being made, while still warm and soft.
Matcha used across the brand is chosen by tea connoisseur Haruhide Morita, and the shop emphasizes serving koicha (thick tea) grade matcha rather than the usucha typically used in matcha desserts.

caveats: Address, hours, seat count, matcha sourcing and menu details were confirmed via web search summaries drawing on the official site and several secondary listings (NAVITIME, Yahoo Map, food/lifestyle blogs); the official saryo-suisen.com pages themselves returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so details are corroborated across multiple independent secondary sources rather than a single primary fetch. No store-specific allergy/celiac policy statement was found from the restaurant itself.
closed_days: None listed — follows the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building's own holiday schedule (no fixed weekly closed day found)
cuisine: 甘味処 (Japanese sweets & matcha cafe, with 十割そば soba)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, tourist-adjacent café inside an office building right at Tochomae Station near the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building, so some visitor familiarity is likely, though no explicit English menu/staff confirmation was found — basic Japanese or pointing at photos should suffice.
gf_detail: The house soba is marketed as 十割そば (juwari — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder), including a matcha version, which is a promising sign for wheat-avoidance. However, no source found any statement from the shop about the soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) ingredients — Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce — nor about dusting-flour practices, shared equipment, or celiac-level cross-contamination controls. There is no evidence of a formal gluten-free menu, labeling, or staff allergy protocol. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu and any dusting flour before ordering the soba, and should not assume the matcha sweets (warabimochi, parfaits, etc.) are wheat-free without asking, since ingredients like cookie/cracker garnishes are common in Japanese matcha parfaits.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30)、定休日なし（施設に準ずる）、予約不可

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) ingredients are not specified in any source found; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, but this has not been confirmed for this shop specifically.

positives.text: The house soba (including the signature matcha soba) is made in-house daily as 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder in the noodle itself.

The signature warm warabimochi is made from warabi starch, matcha, and wasanbon sugar with no dairy mentioned, though it is typically served with sweetened accompaniments that may not be vegan. The soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi, as is standard for Japanese soba, which would make the soba dish non-vegan unless a special sauce is offered — this has not been confirmed either way. Matcha parfaits and lattes commonly include dairy cream/milk. No vegan menu, labeling, or staff accommodation policy was found from the shop itself.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-2 藤山恒産第一ビル1F

bio.background: A Shibuya neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka) dating back to the 1950s. The original founder had no successor, so the current owner's father took over the shop in 1987 and later expanded it in 1995 by absorbing the space of an adjacent yakitori restaurant. This Dogenzaka branch opened in 2002 in the newly completed Fujiyama-Kosan building, alongside the original Udagawa-cho location.
bio.specialty: Ruusu Chahan (ルースーチャーハン) — a loose, saucy pork-and-vegetable stir-fry served over fried rice; reportedly sold over 2,000 servings a month before the pandemic.
bio.philosophy: Fast, filling, wallet-friendly Chinese comfort food served from early morning (7am) through late night, aimed at commuters, students, and Shibuya office workers alike.

anecdotes.text: Founded in the mid-1950s by a restaurateur named Tachihashi; with no successor, the current owner's father took over the shop in 1987 and expanded it by taking over the neighboring yakitori shop's space in 1995.
The signature loose-style fried rice (ルースーチャーハン) reportedly sold more than 2,000 servings a month before the pandemic.
Choraku collaborated with Nissin's 'Ra-oh' instant noodle line on a shop-supervised Cantonese-style noodle product, reflecting its local cult following.

caveats: Sources conflict slightly on weekly closures: most listings (ekiten, enjoytokyo-linked summaries, general aggregator info) describe the shop as open every day with no fixed closed day, but one search summary suggested a Monday closure. No English-language menu, gluten-free labeling, or vegan menu information was found in any source — this is a traditional wheat-noodle-and-meat-forward neighborhood Chinese diner, not marketed toward those diets. Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Ekiten, Retty, San-tatsu, Tripadvisor, and the official site was blocked (HTTP 403) in this environment; all facts below are drawn from WebSearch result summaries of those pages, which quote/paraphrase the source content and are cited by URL.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (無休) per most sources — open daily. One source suggested Monday closures; worth confirming by phone (03-5459-2300) if a Monday visit is planned.
cuisine: Chinese (machi-chuka neighborhood diner) — ramen, fried rice, gyoza, Chinese set meals
cultural_comfort_note: Old-school neighborhood diner with verbal seated ordering and cash-only payment; no English menu was found in any source, so some Japanese ability or pointing at photos/other diners' dishes helps.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-centric Chinese diner: ramen noodles, hand-made gyoza wrappers, and fried rice are all wheat-based, and standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used throughout. No source mentions gluten-free preparation, a wheat-free menu, or a dedicated fryer — fried items almost certainly share oil with breaded/battered and wheat-containing foods. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

Some vegetable side dishes may exist but are not confirmed to be free of meat-based stock, oyster sauce, or lard, and are likely cooked in shared woks with meat. Not recommended for vegans without on-site clarification with staff (Japanese needed).

address_ja: 〒102-0073 東京都千代田区九段北1-4-4 伊東ビル2F

bio.background: Piemon is a long-running neighborhood Italian pasta shop near Kudanshita Station, founded in 1980 (Showa 55) and still run more than 40 years later out of a renovated two-story former traditional house (kominka). Lunchtime, it operates as a dedicated fresh-boiled pasta counter and is known locally as a queue-forming spot; dinner service expands into pizza and a wide range of tapas alongside the pasta menu.
bio.philosophy: Pasta is boiled to order and served in a distinctly Japanese-inflected style (wafu), pairing Italian technique with Japanese ingredients and flavor combinations rather than sticking to classic Italian sauces.
bio.specialty: Wafu-style fresh pasta, with the tarako (cod roe) pasta and a rich Gorgonzola cream pasta as standout dishes; portions are selectable in three sizes (280g regular, 380g large, 480g extra-large).
roles: Casual Italian dinner and party venue

anecdotes.text: Bloggers describe the tarako and shiso tuna pasta as well-balanced in saltiness, with a smooth mouthfeel, and note approvingly that it is not drowned in mayonnaise the way typical Japanese tuna pasta often is.
A regular describes returning for the plum-shiso tuna and tarako pastas, praising the al dente texture and the seaweed garnish that rounds out the dish.
The restaurant is noted as a renovated old machiya-style two-story building a one-minute walk from Kudanshita Station exit 7, used for everyday meals as well as parties and dates.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found on any source (official site, Tabelog, gourmet portals, or blogs). This is a traditional fresh-wheat-pasta specialty shop with wafu-style dishes commonly featuring fish roe, tuna, meat sauce, and cheese; there is no evidence of gluten-free pasta substitution or a vegan menu. Treat as not suitable for celiac or vegan diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays
cuisine: Italian (fresh pasta & pizza)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, Japanese-oriented neighborhood pasta shop with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking service, so bring basic Japanese or a translation app.
geocode_note: No network geocoding performed (lat/lng set to null by design). Address is 千代田区九段北1-4-4, and multiple sources confirm the restaurant is a 1-minute walk from Kudanshita Station Exit 7. Kudanshita Station sits roughly 0.65-0.7km from Suidōbashi Station, placing the restaurant within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free pasta or menu options are mentioned anywhere. The restaurant's core identity is fresh-boiled wheat pasta ("yudetate pasta"), and no source discusses gluten-free substitutions, dedicated cooking equipment, or allergy accommodations. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation.
hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 22:00, some sources note close at 22:45); 土 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); 日・祝 定休日。不定期にディナー営業延長あり (occasional irregular dinner extension).
neighborhood: Kudanshita, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Approximately 35 seats total, including counter seats, sofa seating, and semi-private table rooms for 4-6 guests; the whole restaurant can be reserved for 30-35 people.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or dedicated vegan options were found. The signature dishes (tarako cod-roe pasta, tuna pastas, Gorgonzola cream pasta, meat sauce) are all animal-based, and wafu-style pasta sauces in Japan commonly rely on bonito dashi or similar fish-based stock, though this was not explicitly confirmed for this restaurant. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation.

lead: かっぺ亭 新宿思い出横丁本店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-2-1

roles: kitchen/hall staff of a Shinjuku-based izakaya operator
bio.background: Kappe-tei's Shinjuku Omoide Yokocho honten is the flagship location of a yakitori/motsu-yaki izakaya brand operated by Takashige Food Service Co. (株式会社高繁フードサービス), a Shinjuku-founded (1952) restaurant company that also runs the yakiniku chain Gyū-Shige. This location sits in Omoide Yokocho, the narrow postwar alley of small taverns just west of Shinjuku Station, and has undergone a renewal reopening under the company's 'kodawari sakaba' (particular-taste tavern) concept.
bio.specialty: Three signature dishes: reba-teki-zashi (low-temperature-aged liver sashimi), binchotan-charcoal-grilled yakitori hand-skewered fresh from raw (not frozen) chicken daily, and gyu-motsu nikomi (beef offal stew) simmered in a miso-based broth that is continuously topped up ('tsugitashi') over years for a consistent flavor.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes same-day hand-prepared ingredients — skewers are strung by hand daily rather than pre-made, the offal stew is simmered for two or more days, and cuts of offal vary with each day's delivery rather than being standardized.

anecdotes.text: The beef offal stew uses whatever cuts come in with each delivery, simmered in a miso sauce that has been continuously replenished over the years to keep an unchanging taste.
Yakitori skewers are hand-strung fresh daily from raw chicken and grilled over strong binchotan charcoal, served with either a traditional tare sauce or aged salt.

caveats: Tabelog page itself returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so Tabelog-listed details (budget, hours) are corroborated via search-engine snippets of the Tabelog listing plus independent directory sites (shinjuku-omoide.com official Omoide Yokocho page, gnavi, navitime) rather than a direct page read. Payment method (cash-only vs. card-accepted) could not be confirmed from any single reliable source — conflicting/unclear signals — so cash_only is a best-guess default rather than a confirmed fact. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists anywhere for this restaurant; it is a traditional meat/offal-focused izakaya with no dietary-accommodation signaling found in any source.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / open every day, no regular closed days）
cuisine: 炭火焼き鳥・牛もつ煮込み居酒屋（charcoal-grilled yakitori and beef offal stew tavern）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Japanese-language alley tavern in tourist-frequented Omoide Yokocho — menus and ordering are primarily in Japanese, though staff are used to some foreign visitors given the location.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. This is a traditional yakitori/motsu-nikomi izakaya: yakitori is typically served with a soy-based tare sauce (contains wheat) or salt, and the beef offal stew is a long-simmered miso-based sauce (miso and Japanese soy sauce commonly contain wheat) continuously topped up over time, making a wheat-free version of the house sauce essentially impossible to isolate. Skewers are grilled together over a shared binchotan grill, so cross-contact between tare-brushed and unsauced items is likely. Diners with celiac disease should not consider this restaurant safe without direct, in-person confirmation of ingredients — no such confirmation is available from published sources.

This is a meat- and offal-centric izakaya (yakitori, liver sashimi, beef offal stew) with no plant-based dishes identified; the house sauces (miso-based nikomi broth, yakitori tare) are built around animal-derived stock and are not vegan. There is no indication of a vegetable/vegan menu section.

lead: Ashiyu Cafe Mominoki House 渋谷道玄坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-28-3 道玄坂クラトスビル8階

bio.origin: Founded in Japan in 1985
bio.background: Mominoki House began in 1985 as an acupuncture and shiatsu practice in Japan, building up traditional Chinese five-element (in-yo gogyo) massage technique for over two decades. In its ongoing search for more effective wellness methods, the company encountered Ayurveda and in July 2004 opened its own Ayurvedic facility in Mount Lavinia, Sri Lanka, relocating it to Colombo in October 2006. The Sri Lanka operation trained around 100 staff in herbal-oil selection and use, dosha assessment, and traditional massage technique. Today the company partners with a Sri Lankan Ayurveda graduate school and has its treatments supervised by an in-house Ayurvedic doctor. The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is one of several Mominoki House 'ashiyu' (foot-bath) cafe and Ayurveda salon locations across Japan, combining a 45-minute herbal foot bath with a cup of Sri Lankan herbal tea, alongside optional Ayurvedic oil and shiatsu treatments.
bio.specialty: 45-minute herbal foot bath served with a cup of Sri Lankan herbal tea
bio.philosophy: Holistic wellness rooted in traditional Sri Lankan Ayurveda, combining herbal treatments with a relaxing tea/foot-bath ritual rather than a conventional sit-down meal.
roles: Ayurveda salon and foot-bath cafe chain, est. 1985

caveats: This is primarily an Ayurvedic foot-bath spa and massage salon (Sri Lankan-style Ayurveda, est. 1985) rather than a conventional restaurant. Its food/drink offering is limited to herbal tea (included with the foot bath), plus a small menu of curry/tea-rice dishes and homemade sweets from around 330 yen. No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu information is published for this location, so celiac or vegan diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering.
closed_days: 年末年始 (New Year's holidays); some sources also note possible closures during Obon and Golden Week or for irregular maintenance
cuisine: Ayurvedic foot-bath cafe / Sri Lankan-style wellness salon — herbal teas, light curry and tea-rice dishes, homemade sweets
cultural_comfort_note: Staff-facing signage and booking are mostly in Japanese, but the brand maintains an English Tabelog listing and bilingual social accounts, so basic greetings/simple English should get you through a visit.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated-fryer information is published for this location. The light food menu (curry, tea-rice, sweets) likely uses standard curry roux and soy sauce, both common wheat sources, and cross-contamination has not been addressed publicly. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering or eating any food item; the herbal tea itself is not a wheat risk.

The herbal tea served with the foot bath is plant-based, but full meals should not be assumed vegan without asking staff.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-23-9 JEWEL浅草新仲見世通りビル

bio.chef_name: 松本 彪雅 (Matsumoto Hyoga) — listed as head chef on directory profile
bio.background: Opened in July 2024 in the Shin-Nakamise shopping arcade just steps from Sensoji, the restaurant is run as a direct extension of its parent company's own eel farm in Yamanashi Prefecture, letting it serve domestically raised eel at unusually accessible prices.
bio.specialty: Unagi kabayaki (grilled eel) made from the restaurant's own Yamanashi-farmed 'blue eel' (青うなぎ), served alongside A5-rank wagyu and beef tongue.
bio.philosophy: Vertical integration from farm to table — because the eel is raised on the company's own farm using Yamanashi's clean spring water and ocean minerals, the restaurant can offer high-grade domestic unagi that would normally be expensive at a reasonable price.

anecdotes.text: The eel served here is raised on the company's own farm in Yamanashi, using the prefecture's clear water and ocean minerals in an original aquaculture method; the top-grade 'blue eel' is hand-grilled and finished with a tare sauce that has reportedly been continuously replenished for over 100 years.
In May 2025 the restaurant's Mt. Fuji spring-water eel kabayaki (富士山湧水鰻蒲焼) won the Grand Prix at the Japan Food Selection awards.
The restaurant sits on Shin-Nakamise, the busy shopping street connecting to Sensoji, and pairs its eel with carefully selected A5 wagyu and beef tongue.

caveats: This is an eel (unagi) and wagyu specialty restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused spot — no gluten-free or vegan menu, allergen list, or accommodation policy was found on any source. Direct WebFetch access to the restaurant's official site, Tabelog page, and several directory/blog pages was blocked by network policy (403 at the proxy gateway); the record below relies on WebSearch's synthesized snippets from those same pages (goguynet, gorp.jp, prtimes.jp, irohameguri.jp) rather than raw page fetches. Chef name is drawn only from a directory page title and could not be independently confirmed.
closed_days: なし（年中無休 / no closed days, open every day of the year)
cuisine: Unagi (eel) & Wagyu — Japanese eel kabayaki and A5 wagyu/beef tongue restaurant
cultural_comfort_note: Located on Asakusa's busy, tourist-heavy Shin-Nakamise arcade with an English-language Tabelog listing and online reservation options, so some English support can be expected even without on-site confirmation of an English menu.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen list, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination policy was found on the official site or any directory. This is a grilled-eel and wagyu specialty restaurant; kabayaki tare (eel glaze sauce) is traditionally soy-sauce based and commonly contains wheat, though no source specific to this restaurant confirms its exact ingredients. Celiac diners should not assume safety and should ask directly before ordering.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都台東区浅草1-23-9 (JEWEL浅草新仲見世通りビル), on the Shin-Nakamise shopping street, ~2 minutes on foot from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station exit A1 and ~5 minutes from the Tobu/Toei/Metro Asakusa Station — this places it well within 0.8km of Sensoji (35.7148, 139.7967). No network geocoding was performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from this address.

seats_text: Not specified in available sources
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or meat-free options were found on any source. The restaurant's entire concept is built around farmed eel and A5 wagyu/beef tongue, making it unsuitable for vegan diners.

cuisine: Tsukemen / Ramen (chicken white broth, additive-free)
neighborhood: Shinsen, Shibuya
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町7-2
geocode_note: Address confirmed via multiple independent sources (search snippets citing Tabelog/NAVITIME data); network geocoding disabled per instructions, so lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding. Location (Maruyama-cho 7-2, ~2 min walk from Shinsen Station) falls well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing landmark.
seats_text: 22 seats total, unusual for a ramen/tsukemen shop in that all seating is in individual private rooms
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), lunch service only; closed Mondays. Limited to roughly 100 servings per day.
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: The entrance is tucked down an alley and easy to miss without a map app, and the lunch service runs on a Japanese-style ticket/ordering flow with no indication of an English menu, so a little Japanese comfort helps.
vegan_detail: Every noodle dish is built on a chicken white broth (100% domestic chicken, simmered about 10 hours daily); the organic Yamaguchi vegetables are served as a topping/garnish on the chicken-broth tsukemen rather than as a standalone vegan preparation, and no plant-based broth or vegan menu item was found in any source.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen/ramen specialist using custom noodles from Murakami Seimen; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free prep, or allergen menu was found in any source, and the dipping sauce/tare is a standard soy-based broth (soy sauce typically contains wheat in Japan) with no stated wheat-free alternative.

bio.origin: Originally opened in Sangenjaya (Setagaya) in February 2015 as "Tsukemen Takekawa," then relocated to Maruyama-cho, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station) in August 2019.
bio.background: The shop runs out of the same tiny space as an address-undisclosed, reservation-based chicken hot pot (mizutaki) restaurant; during lunch hours the space becomes Mendokoro Takekawa, serving additive-free chicken white broth tsukemen and ramen, before switching back to the hot pot restaurant in the evening.
bio.specialty: Additive-free special chicken white broth tsukemen (無添加特製つけ麺), topped with pesticide-free organic vegetables (turnip, kabocha squash, potato, and other seasonal produce).
bio.philosophy: The concept grew out of a wish to let more people taste the pesticide-free, organically grown vegetables shipped directly from the owner's own farm in Yamaguchi Prefecture, which are normally reserved for the evening mizutaki restaurant; the chicken white broth is made from 100% domestic chicken simmered daily for around 10 hours, with an emphasis on no additives.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's entrance is hidden down an alley and is genuinely hard to find without a map app — one visiting blogger described spending nearly an hour searching for it before finally sitting down to eat.
Unusually for a ramen or tsukemen shop, all 22 seats are arranged as individual private rooms, so diners eat without other customers in view.
The noodles are a custom mid-thick straight noodle made exclusively for Takekawa by noodle maker Murakami Seimen.

caveats: No official restaurant website was located (only a Yamaguchi prefectural tourism promo page and a PR Times press release). No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation, allergen menu, or cross-contamination information was found in any source — this is a conventional wheat-noodle, chicken-broth tsukemen shop, not a GF/vegan-focused spot. Evening hours listed for the space (as mizutaki restaurant "Suisui Hatano") are a separate reservation-only business and are not reflected in the hours given here, which cover only the lunchtime Takekawa tsukemen service.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町3-9-3 CSビル1F

bio.background: 台湾豆乳大王 opened on May 11, 2020 on a side street between Kudanshita and Jimbocho stations in Chiyoda-ku, focused on Taiwanese-style soy-milk breakfast (早餐) fare rather than a full dinner menu. It has since drawn heavy media attention as one of Tokyo's go-to spots for authentic Taiwanese breakfast, including a May 31, 2025 feature on Fuji TV's 'Ariyoshi-kun no Shojiki Sanpo,' where comedian Hiroyuki Ariyoshi praised the shop.
bio.origin: Taiwan (regional Taiwanese breakfast-shop style, 早餐店)
bio.specialty: 鹹豆漿 (xián dòu jiāng / savory soy milk soup) made with soy milk pressed fresh each morning, and 魯肉飯 (braised pork rice)
bio.philosophy: The shop presses its own soy milk fresh every morning and centers the menu on that daily-made soy milk, in the tradition of a Taiwanese 早餐店 (breakfast shop).

anecdotes.text: On Fuji TV's 'Ariyoshi-kun no Shojiki Sanpo' (aired May 31, 2025), comedian Hiroyuki Ariyoshi tried the shop's food and said, 'I could have this every morning, it's so delicious.'

A June 2025 blog visit describes the savory soy milk soup as made with rich, freshly pressed soy milk topped generously with chili oil for a sour-spicy kick, and the braised pork rice as tender, sweet-savory pork over rice.

caveats: Hours reported vary somewhat between sources (most consistently: Mon-Fri 8:00-14:00, closed Sat/Sun/holidays, prepayment counter-service style with both cash and cashless accepted); a couple of secondary listings suggested different weekday windows and even Thursday/Friday dinner hours, which could not be confirmed against a primary source, so hours should be reconfirmed before visiting. No dedicated GF or vegan menu was found — the shop is a general Taiwanese breakfast counter (soy milk, pork rice, egg crepes, pepper buns) where wheat and animal ingredients (shrimp, pork, chicken, egg) are common; only the plain soy milk drink looks safely vegan. No official restaurant website was located, only an Instagram account and third-party listings/blogs.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cuisine: Taiwanese breakfast / soy milk specialty shop (soy milk dishes, braised pork rice, egg crepes, pepper buns)
geocode_note: Address (神田神保町3-9-3, Chiyoda-ku) and multiple sources place the shop a 1-4 minute walk from Kudanshita Station, on a side street between Kudanshita and Jimbocho stations, consistent with the target-area proximity to Suidōbashi Station; lat/lng left null per instructions for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented anywhere in available sources. The signature savory soy milk soup is topped with a deep-fried wheat-dough stick (油條); the egg crepe (蛋餅) uses a wheat-flour wrapper; the pepper bun (胡椒餅) is a wheat-dough bun; and the braised pork rice (魯肉飯) is soy-sauce marinated (soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-based). No GF-labeled items, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol were found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: Most consistent report: Mon-Fri 8:00-14:00, closed Sat/Sun/public holidays, prepayment counter service, cash and cashless both accepted. One secondary aggregation suggested Mon-Wed 11:00-14:30, Thu-Fri 11:00-14:30 & 18:00-22:00, Sat 11:00-14:00 (closed Sun/holidays) — this could not be confirmed against a primary source and may reflect a different period or a mixed-up listing; call ahead to confirm.
name_en: Taiwan Tonyu Daiou (Taiwan Soy Milk King)
neighborhood: Kudanshita / Kanda-Jinbocho, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: About 10 counter seats plus 4-5 table seats (small counter-style shop)
vegan_detail: Only the plain soy milk drinks (豆漿/甜豆漿) look vegan-friendly if made without animal additives; the signature savory soy milk soup (鹹豆漿) is topped with shrimp, and the rest of the menu centers on braised pork rice (魯肉飯), chicken rice (鶏肉飯), and egg-based crepes (蛋餅) and pepper buns. No vegan-labeled dishes, no stated dashi/bonito avoidance, and no indication this is a vegetarian-oriented kitchen — it is a general Taiwanese breakfast shop, not a vegan or vegetarian specialist.

Historically known for a vegan "veggie taco rice" (ベジタコライス) made with soy meat (大豆ミート) instead of ground beef, plus vegetarian/vegan-friendly lunch boxes and organic drinks (azuki-bean amazake latte, house-made fermented juice). The venue is a rotating multi-tenant space — different independent cooks/pop-ups run the kitchen on different days — and the vegan taco rice was specifically tied to a Wednesday guest kitchen (reported as recently as late 2023/early 2024). Not every day's menu is confirmed fully vegan (some rotating tenants serve general Japanese/Asian home cooking that may include fish-based dashi or other animal products), so vegan diners should ask which kitchen is operating and check the dish list for that day.
gf_detail: A gluten-free guide site lists this venue, stating that at least one of its rotating kitchens avoids gluten, dairy, white sugar, trans fats, GMO grains and food additives across its dishes and sweets — consistent with the macrobiotic, brown-rice-vegetarian style of the Wednesday guest kitchen. However, because the venue is shared among several independent tenants on different days, this standard has not been confirmed to apply uniformly to every operator, and no information was found on dedicated fryers, wheat content in soy sauce used on other days, or formal cross-contact protocols. Celiac diners should confirm directly with staff which kitchen is running that day and ask specifically about soy sauce, cross-contact, and shared prep surfaces before ordering.
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-1-32 新宿ビル1号館 2F・3F
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng set null). Address places it at Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit C5 (about 1 minute on foot) and roughly a 6-7 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, within the required 0.8km radius of Shinjuku Station.
seats_text: Counter seating confirmed; total seat count not published in available sources.
hours_raw: General framework reported across multiple years: café service roughly 11:00-17:00 and bar service roughly 18:00-24:00 (midnight). This is a multi-tenant venue — a different independent café/kitchen operator runs the space on different days/timeslots (examples reported over time include ノムカフェ, f-on, Teru cafe/テルカフェ on Wednesdays serving the vegan taco rice, and more recently CAFE AmaLie and an Asian home-cooking kitchen "百福"). Because the operator (and sometimes exact hours/menu) rotates and could not be confirmed for the current week via a live source, exact hours for a specific visit day should be reconfirmed via the venue's Instagram (@shinjukudialogue) or Facebook page.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closure day was found in available sources; the venue appears to operate daily via its rotating tenant system, though the specific kitchen/hours in effect on a given day should be reconfirmed before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: An LGBTQ+-friendly, diversity-themed community café/bar built around casual conversation ("dialogue"/対話) and SDGs topics; welcoming to newcomers, though no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed.
caveats: This venue could not be directly fetched from Tabelog or its own social pages during verification (fetch tool returned 403 on every site attempted, including tabelog.com, vegewel.com, daily-shinjuku.tokyo, rurubu.jp, timeout.jp, hitosara.com and even example.com, suggesting a broad tool-level block rather than a site-specific one). All facts below are drawn from search-engine snippets of secondary sources (press/blog coverage from 2019 through 2025, directory listings) rather than a direct primary-source read, so treat hours, current tenant lineup, and the taco rice's day-of-week availability as best-effort and confirm via the venue's Instagram before visiting. The venue's core identity, address, and general concept (SDGs-themed, LGBTQ+-friendly, multi-tenant café & bar with a vegan soy-meat taco rice specialty) are corroborated across many independent sources spanning several years, and the location was reported as open as of August 2025.

bio.background: Opened in spring 2019 in Shinjuku Sanchome as an SDGs-themed café and bar. Rather than a single fixed kitchen, the space is run day-to-day and time-slot by time-slot by a rotating cast of independent café and food operators, so visitors encounter different cooks, cuisines, and stories depending on when they come.
bio.specialty: A vegan 'taco rice' made with soy meat in place of ground beef — historically served by a Wednesday guest kitchen rooted in macrobiotic, brown-rice vegetarian cooking — alongside organic drinks such as an azuki-bean amazake latte and house-fermented enzyme juice.
bio.philosophy: The name 'Dialogue' reflects the founders' wish to create a place where conversation about the UN Sustainable Development Goals happens naturally over food and drink. Staff — including transgender team members — dress in their own clothes rather than uniforms, and the venue is deliberately run as an LGBTQ+-friendly space where visitors are invited to encounter a range of gender expressions and backgrounds.

anecdotes.text: 毎週水曜日に出店している「Teru cafe」がタコライスを提供しており、マクロビオティックをベースにした玄米菜食のお料理やスイーツを作っている（動物性食品・白砂糖・精製塩・化学調味料・保存料・着色料は一切不使用）。
ドリンクを注文するごとにコインが1枚渡され、それを「環境問題」や「貧困」と書かれた項目の箱に入れると、その社会問題に取り組んでいる団体に寄付される仕組みになっている。
店長やコックなど、スタッフ全員が私服で、トランスジェンダーを含め性別の枠にとらわれないカラフルな個性の人々が集まっており、訪れた人にはいろいろなジェンダーの在り方に触れてほしいと考えている。

positives.text: Listed on a Tokyo gluten-free guide site, which states the venue's menu/sweets avoid gluten, dairy, white sugar, trans fats, GMO grains and food additives (attributed to its macrobiotic-style kitchen).

The Wednesday guest kitchen's dishes and desserts were reported to use no animal products (meat, fish, eggs, dairy, butter), no white sugar, no refined salt, no chemical seasonings, no preservatives, and no artificial coloring.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-23-12 フォンティスビル2F

bio.chef_name: 川野祐嗣 (Yuji Kawano, current operator)
bio.origin: 宮崎県 (Miyazaki Prefecture)
bio.background: Gyossantei's Shibuya branch traces back to the Nikko Group, a Miyazaki-based family business that ran a tonkatsu restaurant ('Niko/二幸') and a Miyazaki-cuisine restaurant called Gyosantei back home. The Tokyo branch opened in 1984 near Miyagizaka, moved to its current Bunkamura-dori/Dogenzaka location in 2008, and today the founder's son runs it through the company Nisshusha (日州舎).
bio.specialty: Chicken nanban (チキン南蛮) — Miyazaki's signature dish of deep-fried chicken thigh dipped in sweet vinegar and topped with a housemade tartar sauce loaded with finely chopped aromatics — alongside hiyajiru (冷や汁), a cold miso soup with summer vegetables like cucumber and myoga, ladled over barley rice.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on ingredients flown in directly from Miyazaki — fish and shellfish from the Hyuga-nada coast, organic vegetables from Aya town, Miyazaki beef and free-range local chicken — aiming to bring an authentic taste of Miyazaki hometown cooking to Shibuya.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was opened in 1984 by the Nikko Group founder's family (who also ran a tonkatsu shop and a Gyosantei restaurant in Miyazaki); it started near Miyagizaka and relocated to its present Bunkamura-dori spot in 2008, and is now run by the founder's son, Yuji Kawano, via the company Nisshusha.

The Shibuya branch's chicken nanban is fried a bit harder and dipped more thoroughly in sweet vinegar than the original Miyazaki-airport shop's version, giving it a bolder flavor; the tartar sauce differs between the two locations.
Hiyajiru (cold miso soup with summer vegetables) poured over barley rice is served alongside the chicken nanban as a refreshing counterpoint to its rich, sweet-and-sour flavor.

caveats: Hours vary slightly across listing sites (some show a uniform 11:30-14:00/17:30-24:00 daily schedule, others show Monday and Saturday without lunch service) — recommend confirming by phone (03-5489-6350) before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient breakdown, or allergy-accommodation statement was found in any source; this is a traditional Miyazaki izakaya, not a dietary-focused restaurant, so GF/vegan diners should call ahead and expect limited options.
closed_days: Sunday (日曜定休)
cuisine: 宮崎県郷土料理 (Miyazaki regional home-style cooking / izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local izakaya serving Miyazaki home-style dishes; no evidence of an English menu, English-speaking staff, or foreign-tourist-oriented service was found in any source.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation information was found in any source. The signature dish, chicken nanban, is deep-fried in a wheat-flour batter and served with sweet vinegar sauce and tartar sauce; other dishes (sashimi, grilled local chicken, Miyazaki beef, hiyajiru cold soup) likely rely on standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce and miso, which typically contain wheat. Not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based placement. Address (Dogenzaka 2-23-12, Shibuya-ku) is on Bunkamura-dori, roughly a 5-6 minute walk from Shibuya Station and close to Shinsen Station, placing it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target landmark.
hours_raw: Listings vary: some sources give a uniform Tue-Sat schedule of 11:30-14:00 and 17:30-24:00 (Sunday closed); a more detailed breakdown found gives Mon 17:30-23:00 (no lunch), Tue-Fri 11:30-14:10 and 17:30-24:00, Sat 17:30-23:30 (no lunch), Sun closed. Recommend phone confirmation (03-5489-6350).
neighborhood: 道玄坂/神泉 (Dogenzaka/Shinsen), Shibuya

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's signature chicken nanban is deep-fried in a wheat-flour batter; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or allergy-accommodation policy was found in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The specialty cold soup hiyajiru (冷や汁) is a miso-based dish traditionally made with dashi stock (commonly bonito-based) in Miyazaki-style preparations; no vegan-specific menu or substitution was found.

seats_text: Approximately 50 seats
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. The restaurant centers on seafood, chicken, and Miyazaki beef; the specialty hiyajiru cold soup is miso/dashi-based (dashi in Miyazaki-style versions is commonly bonito-derived) and the signature chicken nanban is meat-based with a mayonnaise-based tartar sauce. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without direct, specific confirmation from staff.

lead: 濃厚海老つけ麺 ともやま
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-19-7 第110東京ビル1F

anecdotes.text: Reviewers highlight the broth's standout aromatic shrimp fragrance and its unusually thick, coating consistency, calling it a stand-out among Suidobashi's dense ramen/tsukemen scene.
The shop also offers a chicken-broth tsukemen option for guests who don't care for shrimp, and lets diners choose warm or cold noodles — a flexibility uncommon among tsukemen specialists.

bio.background: Opened in spring 2024 just a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, this small tsukemen specialty shop built its identity around a single idea: a rich, shrimp-forward dipping broth mixed fresh in-house.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emulsifies its shrimp broth with a mixer on site to concentrate the shellfish aroma and body, aiming for a soup thick enough to fully coat the noodles with every dip.
bio.specialty: Rich shrimp tsukemen (濃厚海老つけ麺) — thick, medium-straight noodles served with a viscous, emulsified shrimp dipping broth, finished with a half-boiled egg, scallion, bamboo shoots, and nori.

caveats: Hours and closures are irregular (不定休); confirm current status before visiting. Ordering is Japanese-language (small counter/table shop, ~10 seats). No confirmed gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source — the signature dish is a wheat-noodle, shrimp-broth tsukemen.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day found in sources
cuisine: Rich shrimp dipping noodles (tsukemen)
cultural_comfort_note: A small local tsukemen counter shop with a Japanese-only menu; basic Japanese or pointing at photos should be enough to order.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment). Multiple independent sources place the shop at Kanda-Misakicho 2-19-7, described as a 1-minute walk from Suidobashi Station's west exit, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialty shop; the noodles are wheat-based and the dipping broth is built on a shrimp (and likely soy-sauce/wheat) base. No source mentions any gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol — assume not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily; closed days irregular (不定休)
name_ja: 濃厚海老つけ麺 ともやま
neighborhood: Suidōbashi
seats_text: Approximately 10 seats (counter and table seating)
vegan_detail: The signature dish is a shrimp-based dipping broth (with a chicken-broth alternative also offered) — both animal-based. No plant-based, vegetable-broth, or vegan option was found in any source, so this is not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 〒160-0023 東京都新宿区西新宿1-1-5 ルミネ新宿1 6F

bio.origin: Operated by CAFE COMPANY, a Tokyo restaurant-brand operator
bio.background: plant more opened on March 4, 2024, on the 6th floor of Lumine Shinjuku 1, directly connected to Shinjuku Station. It is a café brand created by CAFE COMPANY.
bio.specialty: Plant-based meal plates (e.g. falafel with homemade tahini sauce, hummus, ratatouille) and all-plant-derived desserts, including cakes and soft-serve sundaes.
bio.philosophy: The café's stated concept is to let people with different dietary styles and preferences share the same table together in a borderless way — the menu offers 100%-plant-derived plates alongside meat and fish dishes so everyone can choose according to preference.
roles: Café brand operated by CAFE COMPANY

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described plant more as having a natural, spacious atmosphere, discovered via an Instagram account that shares dairy-allergy-friendly restaurants; all desserts there are plant-based.
Menus display plant-based and allergen markings so that customers with allergies can order with more confidence.

caveats: The menu is plant-based-focused but not exclusively vegan — it also offers meat and fish dishes, so strict vegans should look for the plant-based markers on the menu. Gluten-free and allergen labeling exists and staff reportedly explain ingredients on request, but no source confirms dedicated fryer/cookware or wheat-free soy sauce, so celiac-level diners should double-check specifics with staff.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; follows Lumine Shinjuku 1's building closure schedule (occasional building-wide closures, e.g. maintenance days), per NAVITIME listing.
cuisine: プラントベース・ヴィーガンカフェ (plant-based / vegan-friendly café)

The menu displays plant-based and allergen markings so that allergy sufferers can order with more confidence.
Listed in WE TABLE's directory of Shinjuku restaurants with food-allergy accommodations.

vegan_detail: Plant more's meal plates come in a 100%-plant-derived version (e.g. falafel with homemade tahini sauce, hummus, ratatouille) and all desserts (cakes, soft-serve sundaes) are made only with plant-derived ingredients with no dairy or eggs. However, the menu also includes meat and fish dishes for diners who prefer them, so it is not an exclusively vegan menu — look for the plant-based markers when ordering. No source confirms whether hidden animal-derived ingredients like katsuo dashi are used in any dishes.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区松濤1-28-5

bio.background: A small, unassuming neighborhood soba shop tucked into the quiet backstreets of Shoto (松濤), between Shibuya's Bunkamura cultural complex and Shinsen Station. It has the well-worn, nostalgic look of an old-school machi-soba (town soba) shop — faded walls, hand-written menu boards, no background music, and a handful of four-person tables — and appears to be run by a couple, giving it a warm, homely feel that stands out in an increasingly fast-changing part of Shibuya.
bio.specialty: Local reviewers single out the curry nanban soba (curry-flavored broth with pork and green onion) for its rich, thick broth that clings well to the thin noodles; classic kake soba and tempura zaru soba are also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: One Tabelog reviewer titled their review 'I hope they keep going — a good old-fashioned soba shop,' reflecting the affection long-time regulars have for this increasingly rare type of retro Shibuya-area soba shop.

A Tokyo food blog described it as 'the machi-soba of Shoto,' noting the calm, unadorned dining room — no background music, five four-person tables — and praised the curry nanban broth for its rich, thick consistency that clings well to the thin soba noodles.

caveats: Hours vary by source: an older (2016) listing cited lunch-only hours (12:00-15:00), while more recent listings describe 11:30-19:30 on Tue/Wed/Fri-Sun with Mon/Thu fixed closures plus occasional additional irregular closures. Recommend confirming by phone (03-3461-6624) before visiting. Cash-only status could not be confirmed from any source. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations, dedicated fryer info, or allergy protocols were found in any source — this is a standard traditional soba shop, not a diet-specialized one; celiac and strict vegan diners should treat it as unsuitable/ask-only rather than assume safety. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding blocked); address-based estimate places it roughly 0.6-0.8km from the Shibuya Scramble hub, within the Shoto/Shinsen-Station-area described by the source listing.

No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was found in any source — not recommended for celiac diners.

No vegan-specific options, plant-based dashi substitutions, or vegan menu items are mentioned in any review or menu source found.

address_ja: 東京都文京区小石川1-9-6

bio.background: Founded in Showa 55 (1980) by an owner who had previously worked as the head of a company's accounting department (経理部長) before leaving to open his own tavern.
bio.specialty: Motsu-nikomi (simmered beef and pork offal, especially the version made with tofu) and motsu-yaki (grilled offal), alongside sashimi platters of seasonal fish such as chutoro (medium fatty tuna), buri, octopus, aji, and sweet shrimp.
bio.philosophy: The owner buys fresh offal and seasonal fish at the market himself each morning; the miso-and-salt seasoning used for the signature motsu-nikomi is his own original recipe.

anecdotes.text: The owner, a former corporate accounting department manager, opened the shop in 1980 and devised the shop's miso-and-salt seasoned motsu-nikomi recipe himself, resulting in a mild dish with no unpleasant gaminess.

The first floor has table seating while a spacious tatami room upstairs accommodates larger groups, giving the shop a warm, hometown izakaya atmosphere for both casual lunches and lively evening gatherings.

caveats: Target brief listed this spot as being in 千代田区神田春日 (Chiyoda-ku), but every source that surfaced a real address places it at 文京区小石川1-9-6 (Bunkyo-ku, Koishikawa), roughly 230m/3-min walk from Kasuga Station — the station is correct, only the ward name in the brief appears mislabeled. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found in any source; this is a traditional offal/meat- and fish-focused izakaya with no dietary-accommodation signaling. Tabelog and several aggregator pages (ekiten, Retty, loco.yahoo, paypaygourmet, ameblo blogs) returned 403 on direct fetch in this environment, so hours/address were cross-confirmed via search-engine synthesis of those pages rather than a direct page fetch; treat hours as reasonably reliable but not Tabelog-page-verified firsthand. The official site domain (ensyuya-koishikawa.jp) that appeared in search results failed to resolve (DNS) when fetched, so its content could not be confirmed — listed as a probable official URL only.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: Izakaya — motsu-nikomi (simmered offal) and motsu-yaki (grilled offal) specialist, also sashimi
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, decades-old neighborhood izakaya with no indication of English menus, signage, or staff — best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or with a translation app.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source. As a classic izakaya serving miso/soy-seasoned simmered offal, grilled skewers, and sashimi with soy sauce, wheat exposure via shoyu and miso-based seasoning should be assumed; no information on a dedicated fryer or gluten-free substitutions was found. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.

lead: HealthyTOKYO CBD ショップ&カフェ 新宿マルイ本館
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-30-13 新宿マルイ本館 B1F

bio.chef_name: マイケル・ボブロフ（Michael Bobrov、HealthyTOKYO創業者・代表取締役）
bio.origin: 2011年設立。2016年に日本市場へCBDオイルを導入した先駆者的企業で、2018年には羽田空港内に日本初の完全ヴィーガン空港カフェ（世界初の空港内CBD販売店）を開設。新宿マルイ本館店はグループ最大規模の店舗として開業。
bio.background: HealthyTOKYOはCBD（カンナビジオール）とヴィーガン食を組み合わせた日本のパイオニア企業として、有機栽培のヘンプを使ったCBD製品と、すべて植物性のスイーツ・軽食を提供している。
bio.specialty: CBD入りモンブラン（グルテンフリー・ヴィーガンのCBDクッキー土台に栗のペーストと生クリーム風のヴィーガンクリームをのせたシグネチャーケーキ、CBD20mg配合）
bio.philosophy: 「動物性食品の消費を減らすことは、私たちの健康はもちろん、環境や動物たちにも良い影響を与えられる」という考えのもと、日本におけるヴィーガン食の普及を目指し、洗練された落ち着いた空間でCBDフードを楽しんでもらうことを大切にしている。
roles: 創業者兼代表取締役社長

anecdotes.text: HealthyTOKYOは日本において「ヴィーガン」へのポジティブな理解を広めるため、毎年何万食ものヴィーガン料理を提供してきたと述べている。
新宿マルイ本館店はApple Storeの真下（地下1階）に位置し、グループ5店舗目・最大規模の店舗として2023年4月29日にオープンした。
店内はケーキやドリンクとともにCBDオイルなども扱い、都会の喧騒から離れてリラックスできるオアシスとしてデザインされている。

caveats: 座席数は情報源により10席・16席と表記が分かれる（公式サイトは16席）。グルテンフリー対応は「ほとんどのメニュー」が対象であり全メニューではない点、また専用フライヤーの有無やクロスコンタミネーション対策の詳細は情報源からは確認できなかったため、セリアック病等重度アレルギーの方は来店前に直接店舗へ確認することを推奨。
closed_days: なし（年中無休、新宿マルイ本館の休館日に準ずる）
cuisine: ビーガン・カフェ・グルテンフリー（CBD専門ショップ併設）
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a major department store (Shinjuku Marui) with an English-language official website and international CBD-wellness branding, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate.
gf_detail: CBDスイーツは小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使用し、グルテンフリー・ラザニアやキッシュなど「ほとんどのメニュー」がグルテンフリーと案内されているが、「全メニュー」ではない点に注意。専用フライヤーの有無、醤油・だし等の小麦混入やクロスコンタミネーション対策についての具体的な情報源は見つからなかったため、セリアック病レベルの厳格な除去が必要な場合は事前に店舗へ直接確認することを推奨する。
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng set to null. Address confirms location is B1F of Shinjuku Marui Honkan at Shinjuku Sanchome, directly adjacent to Shinjuku Sanchome Station exits A1/B1 (0-minute walk) and roughly 300-400m from JR Shinjuku Station, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius.

name_ja: HealthyTOKYO CBD ショップ&カフェ 新宿マルイ本館
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目・新宿マルイ本館地下1階

positives.text: 店舗の全ての食品・ドリンクメニューが100%ヴィーガン（動物性食品不使用）で提供されている。
CBDクッキーやケーキ類は小麦粉の代わりに米粉を使用するなど、ほとんどのメニューがグルテンフリーで作られている。

seats_text: 約16席（情報源により10席との記載もあり）
vegan_detail: HealthyTOKYOは「100%ヴィーガン」を掲げる企業で、当店のカフェメニュー（ケーキ、パニーニ、ラザニア、キッシュ、ドリンク等）はすべて動物性食品不使用の植物性メニューとして提供されている。だし・かつお節等の隠れ動物性食品の使用は確認されていない（サイト上でも明確にヴィーガンと案内）。

lead: スケロク ダイナー
address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-11-1 あゆみビル 1F・2F

roles: 代表 / オーナー (Representative, Epietriz Inc.)
bio.background: SUKE6 DINER opened in March 2015 on the corner of Sukeroku Yume-dōri, a cherry-tree-lined street along the Sumida River embankment in Asakusa — the street's name is where the restaurant's name comes from. The building is a renovated former footwear-wholesaler warehouse; the diner occupies the 1st and 2nd floors, while the 3rd floor houses an in-house bakery workshop called "Manufacture" that bakes the bread served downstream and sells baked goods on weekends. It is run by Epietriz Inc., which also operates a few other concept cafés in Tokyo.
bio.specialty: Avocado toast and the skillet-served English breakfast are among its best-known dishes, alongside a signature burger ("Sukeroku Burger") and a weekday morning set.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's guiding concept is "EAT GOOD" — food they would be happy to serve their own families, without chemical seasonings. Beyond sourcing organic/low-pesticide produce, the kitchen makes as much as possible in house: bacon, sausage and other charcuterie, salad dressing, ginger ale and lemonade are all handmade rather than bought in.

anecdotes.text: Owner Matsuura, who lives in Hayama, personally drives Miura-peninsula vegetables and local farm eggs to the restaurant most mornings; the shop also draws seasonal produce from contracted farms in Kyushu and from Epietriz's own farm in Yamanashi.

About 40% of the diner's customers are visitors from overseas; the bilingual (Japanese/English) menu and social posts reflect a stated aim of making the space welcoming to everyone regardless of nationality, age, or gender.
The diner's concept of hand-making processed foods and seasonings in-house, down to condiments, was covered as part of Epietriz's "EAT GOOD" philosophy at the restaurant's 2015 opening.

caveats: Vegan/vegetarian and gluten-free options exist but are limited (a handful of marked dishes such as a gluten-free buddha bowl and vegan drinks), not a dedicated menu. The restaurant runs its own in-house bakery producing fresh bread daily, so wheat is present throughout the kitchen — no dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer was confirmed in any source. Diners with celiac disease or severe allergies should discuss specifics with staff before ordering rather than relying on menu labels alone. Some listings show slightly conflicting generic hours (likely template/boilerplate data); the 08:00–18:00, Wednesday-closed schedule is the most specific and recently corroborated (effective Feb 2025).
closed_days: Wednesdays (regular weekly closure, effective from February 2025); also closed Dec 29–Jan 3 for New Year
cuisine: Cafe / Modern Diner — organic and low-pesticide vegetables, avocado toast, homemade charcuterie and baked goods, whole-grain/health-conscious brunch fare
cultural_comfort_note: Bilingual (Japanese/English) menu, English-speaking staff, and roughly 40% international clientele make this an easy, low-friction stop for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.
gf_detail: Reviewer/listing sources tag the restaurant with a "gluten free options" label and mention a gluten-free buddha bowl, but this is a bread-and-charcuterie-forward diner with its own bakery (\"Manufacture\") on the 3rd floor baking fresh bread daily on-site — meaning wheat is handled throughout the kitchen. No source confirms a dedicated gluten-free prep area, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi practice. Celiac travelers should treat this as "ask and verify," not GF-safe by default.
geocode_note: Address (花川戸1-11-1) sits about 1 minute's walk from Tobu Asakusa Station's north exit and roughly 500m from Sensō-ji — well within the 0.8km Asakusa target radius. No network geocoding performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream derivation from address_ja.
name_ja: スケロク ダイナー
neighborhood: 花川戸 (Hanakawado), Asakusa

gf_cross_contamination.text: The diner operates its own on-site bakery ("Manufacture") baking fresh bread daily, meaning wheat flour is present and handled throughout the kitchen; no source confirms separated gluten-free prep.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described as a Western-food diner and bakery with only a few vegetarian and vegan menu items, rather than a vegan-focused kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guidance for visitors is to tell staff about vegetarian/vegan preferences and any allergies before choosing a menu item, rather than relying on menu marks alone.

positives.text: Menu carries English translations and vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free tags on select items (e.g., a gluten-free buddha bowl, a soy/green-tea latte), and the kitchen avoids chemical seasonings, making ingredients more transparent than a typical diner.
All charcuterie, dressings, and drinks (bacon, ginger ale, lemonade, etc.) are handmade in-house from named ingredient sources rather than bought pre-made, reducing hidden additive risk.

seats_text: About 43 seats across the ground and 2nd floors, including a large communal table, counter seating, and some outdoor/terrace seating.
vegan_detail: Vegan/vegetarian items are limited but present — sources mention a vegan buddha bowl, a soy/green-tea latte, and easy vegetarian options like quinoa-honey bread. As a Western-diner-and-bakery concept built around eggs, homemade charcuterie (bacon, sausage), and dairy-based dishes, the bulk of the menu is not vegan; no confirmation of dashi/katsuo/bonito use was found, but Japanese-style hidden animal stocks are less likely here than at a washoku restaurant given the Western-diner format. Ask staff to confirm ingredient specifics per dish.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-19-3 ライオンズマンション道玄坂 1F

bio.background: Kushitoku (串徳) is a kushiage (deep-fried skewer) specialty shop that traces its roots to 1978 in the Dogenzaka backstreets of Shibuya. It is now run by the founder's successor, a second-generation owner who works the counter personally, carrying forward the frying technique passed down from the shop's earlier years while adding his own refinements.
bio.specialty: An omakase-style seasonal kushiage course — roughly 30 to 35 skewers per sitting, from classic staples to seasonal and limited items, coated in a light batter and fried in a house blend of three different oils to keep each ingredient's natural flavor and texture intact.
bio.philosophy: A small, quiet, counter-only room built around watching the chef fry each skewer to order and talking with him directly, rather than a high-volume izakaya approach — the emphasis is on freshness, seasonality, and precision more typical of a sushi counter than a typical kushikatsu chain.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1978 in Shibuya's Dogenzaka area and has been carried on by a second-generation owner who maintains the original frying technique while introducing his own seasonal variations.

caveats: Kushitoku is a traditional kushiage (deep-fried, breaded skewer) restaurant built around an omakase course. No source found any mention of gluten-free or vegan accommodation, dedicated fryer oil, or allergy protocol. Kushiage is battered/breaded (wheat flour and often panko) and typically served with tsuyu dipping sauce and other condiments that usually contain wheat-based soy sauce; the course is centered on meat and seafood skewers. This is very unlikely to be safe for celiac diners or suitable for vegans without direct, in-person confirmation and menu customization by the chef, which was not documented in any source consulted.
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日)
cuisine: 串揚げ・揚げ物 (Kushiage / deep-fried skewers)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional counter-only shop where the owner interacts directly with guests in Japanese; reservations are normally made by phone in Japanese, though one source noted international visitors have reserved via the shop's Instagram, so a translation app or Japanese-speaking companion is recommended.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The restaurant's entire concept is deep-fried, breaded (wheat flour/panko) kushiage skewers cooked in shared frying oil, almost certainly served with wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) dipping sauce/tsuyu. There is no indication ingredients or breading can be substituted. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
geocode_note: No coordinates geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Multiple independent listings place the address at Dogenzaka 2-19-3, Shibuya-ku, described as about a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station and categorized by Tabelog under the adjoining Shinsen (神泉) area — consistent with being well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target landmark.
