gf_cross_contamination.text: No source documented whether frying oil is shared across different ingredients (meat, seafood, vegetables) or whether any items are fried separately; the shop uses a blend of three oils but this refers to the type of oil, not fryer separation for allergen purposes.

seats_text: 16 counter seats (counter-only, no private rooms)
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegetable-only kushiage option, or plant-based accommodation was found in any source. The course is an omakase selection of roughly 30-35 skewers that includes meat and seafood as its specialty, and traditional kushiage tsuyu dipping sauce typically contains bonito-derived dashi. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation and course customization by the chef.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-21-9

bio.origin: Tokyo (Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku)
bio.background: Tomita Shoten occupies a bookstore building from the early Showa era in Kanda-Misakicho, a short walk from Suidobashi Station. In 2017 the old bookshop was renovated into a charcoal-grill bistro by architect Kuma Taichi, son of renowned architect Kengo Kuma, who kept the wooden bookshelves and warm, mid-century atmosphere while turning the space into a restaurant. It is run by the local company Nasuya (owner Mitsufuji Kimihisa), which also operates the nearby motsu-dining spot Umakamon in Suidobashi.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled wagyu sirloin steak, and the restaurant's signature concept of recreating dishes that appear in famous books, manga, and anime (e.g. Sanji's crispy potato paille from One Piece, the meatball spaghetti from Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro, and the castella pancake from the picture book Guri and Gura), served alongside seasonal charcoal-grilled vegetables and a rotating natural wine list.
bio.philosophy: The concept ties the space's former life as a bookstore to the menu: each table is given a picture book that doubles as part of the menu, and diners can order dishes inspired by scenes from beloved stories, paired with charcoal-grilled seasonal produce and meats and natural wine in a renovated, book-lined dining room.
roles: restaurant/bistro run by Nasuya, part of a small local hospitality group with a sister izakaya nearby

anecdotes.text: The building is a renovated early-Showa-era bookstore in Suidobashi/Misakicho, redesigned in 2017 by architect Kuma Taichi (son of Kengo Kuma), preserving a book-lined, mid-century interior.

Each table's menu takes the form of a picture book, and diners can order the actual dish described in that book's story — e.g. Sanji's crispy potato paille (One Piece), the meatball spaghetti from Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro, and a castella pancake inspired by Guri and Gura.
The restaurant is operated by Nasuya, a small Chiyoda-based hospitality company owned by Mitsufuji Kimihisa, which also runs the motsu-dining izakaya Umakamon near Suidobashi.

caveats: Reported hours and closed days vary across booking platforms — some list weekday lunch service (roughly 11:30-14:30) plus a Sunday closure, while others (Hitosara, Regli) show weekday dinner-only hours (17:00-23:00) with weekend/holiday through-service (11:30-23:00) and no fixed closed day. This record uses the latter, more consistently corroborated pattern, but hours should be confirmed by phone (03-6272-9758) or the official site before visiting. No vegan or gluten-free menu, dedicated-fryer, or allergy-protocol information was found in any source — this is a meat- and wagyu-centered charcoal-grill bistro with pasta and flour-based items on the menu, so celiac/vegan diners should call ahead and discuss needs directly with staff rather than assume safety.
closed_days: No fixed closed day confirmed by most current sources (reported as open daily); one older source listed holidays/day-before-holidays as a closed day. Confirm before visiting.
cuisine: Charcoal-grilled Bistro - Seasonal vegetables, charcoal-grilled meats, natural wine selection
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-concept restaurant (book-themed charcoal-grill bistro) with no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff, though it appears on English-language Tabelog and online reservation platforms; basic Japanese or a translation app is useful for describing dietary restrictions.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address (神田三崎町2-21-9) and multiple sources place it a 1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station East Exit, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce were confirmed in any source. The menu includes wheat-containing items (pasta such as meatball spaghetti, castella pancakes, breaded/fried preparations), so cross-contact risk is likely. Plain charcoal-grilled meats and vegetables could be a safer option, but sauces and seasonings are unconfirmed — celiac diners should call ahead (03-6272-9758) and confirm specifics with staff before ordering.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Sat/Sun/Holidays: through-service 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Some sources additionally report a weekday lunch service (approx. 11:30-14:30/15:00) and a Sunday closure — hours are inconsistently reported across platforms, so confirm before visiting.
neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku (神田三崎町)
seats_text: About 54 seats (roughly 14 at the 1F counter and 40 on the 2F); a private room accommodates 20-30+ guests, and standing receptions up to about 60.
vegan_detail: Seasonal, charcoal-grilled vegetables are part of the restaurant's concept and menu, but the overall menu is centered on wagyu beef, local chicken, and meat-based book-inspired dishes; no vegan menu, dashi/bonito-free labeling, or animal-stock-free assurances were found. Vegetables are likely grilled on the same charcoal grill as meat and may be seasoned with meat-based stock or sauces — confirm specifics with staff.

lead: 郷土料理 くらわんか
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-36-6 大安ビル B1・B2F

bio.origin: Rotating regional dishes sourced from across Japan, prepared in Shinjuku
bio.background: An izakaya specializing in regional Japanese cuisine (郷土料理) located one minute from Shinjuku Station's Central East Exit, in operation for several decades under representative director Shinichi Yasuda. The restaurant occupies the basement floors of the Daian Building, alongside affiliated shops in the same building group.
bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating selection of traditional dishes drawn from different regions of Japan, served alongside regional sake, shochu, and awamori sourced directly from breweries nationwide, plus a wine list curated by the owner-sommelier. A limited daily lunch box called "Tamatebako Lunch" and a lunch set course (starters, sashimi, grilled dish, simmered dish, rice or cold udon, and dessert) are also offered.
bio.philosophy: The concept is to let diners in Tokyo take a culinary tour of Japan without leaving Shinjuku, cycling the menu each month so that guests can sample specialties from a different prefecture or region alongside that region's own sake.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant maintains direct relationships with sake breweries across the country, bringing in regional sake and shochu that it pairs with that month's rotating lineup of local specialty dishes.

Seating is arranged around named private and semi-private rooms styled after regional pottery traditions (Mashiko counter seats, Bizen table seats, Hagi and Imari/Karatsu private rooms), reflecting the restaurant's regional-Japan theme.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, labeling, or allergy accommodation was found in any source. This is a traditional Japanese regional-cuisine izakaya whose rotating monthly menu relies on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, which typically contains wheat) and bonito-based dashi; sourcing did not surface any statement about dedicated fryers, allergen substitution, or cross-contamination handling. Treat gf_confidence and vegan_status as conservative defaults based on cuisine type, not on any explicit restaurant statement.
closed_days: Sundays (regular closing day); if the following Monday is a public holiday, the restaurant instead opens on Sunday and closes on the Monday holiday)
cuisine: Regional Japanese cuisine (郷土料理) — rotating monthly specialties from different regions of Japan, paired with regional sake/shochu/awamori
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local izakaya aimed at Japanese diners, with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service, so some Japanese-language ability or a translation app will help.
geocode_note: Address (東京都新宿区新宿3-36-6, Daian Building B1/B2F) and multiple independent sources confirm the restaurant is a 1-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's Central East Exit, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius. Lat/lng left null per instructions; geocoding to be handled downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. As a standard Japanese regional-cuisine izakaya, dishes are likely to use ordinary soy sauce (contains wheat), bonito/kombu dashi, and fried items (tempura-style preparations), which are not confirmed to be handled separately from other cooking. Celiac diners should not assume safety here without directly and explicitly confirming ingredients and preparation with staff in Japanese before ordering.
hours_raw: Weekdays: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 13:50) and 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30). Saturdays and public holidays: 15:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays, except that if the following Monday is a public holiday the restaurant opens Sunday and closes Monday instead.
name_ja: 郷土料理 くらわんか
neighborhood: Shinjuku Station Central East Exit (新宿駅中央東口)
seats_text: About 80 seats total, split across a counter (Mashiko, 12 seats), table seating (Bizen, 8 seats), a semi-private room (Hagi, 4 seats x2 rooms), and private rooms (Imari, 20 seats; Karatsu, 12 seats)
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. Regional Japanese cuisine of this type typically relies on bonito-based dashi and features meat/seafood specialties as the core of the rotating monthly menu, so most dishes are unlikely to be vegan by default. Vegetable-forward regional dishes may exist in a given month's rotation, but this would need to be confirmed with staff on a visit-by-visit basis, and dashi/broth bases should be assumed to contain fish unless stated otherwise.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-8-6 宮野ビル2F

bio.background: Tucked away on the 2nd floor above Shibuya's Miyamasu-zaka slope, near Aoyama Gakuin, this tempura bar bills itself as a 'kakureya' (hideaway) and has been operating in the same spot for around nine years.
bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable and seafood tempura paired with Japanese sake, umeshu, and other drinks.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, adult-oriented izakaya atmosphere — jazz playing, soft lighting — meant for lingering over course-by-course fried tempura rather than a quick meal.

anecdotes.text: The shop is described as sitting quietly above Miyamasu-zaka in Shibuya, near Aoyama Gakuin, with a jazz soundtrack and soft lighting meant to let solo diners, couples, and friends relax over tempura and drinks.

The restaurant offers a dedicated all-vegetable tempura course of seven pieces for ¥1,380 per person, introduced with the line 'we'd like vegans to enjoy this' — fried-to-order seasonal vegetables only.

caveats: Hours and the Saturday/holiday closure appear to be a temporary policy layered on top of the restaurant's normal Sunday-only closed day, per secondary listings — worth confirming directly before a visit. No page found that specifically addresses gluten-free accommodation (wheat-based batter and likely soy-based tentsuyu are the default), so this is not recommended for strict celiac diners. The vegan tempura course is well documented, but no source confirms whether the dipping sauce/salt served alongside it is free of bonito dashi.
closed_days: Sunday (regular closed day); currently also closed Saturdays and holidays per a temporary notice
cuisine: 天ぷら（天ぷらバル・旬の肴・日本酒） / Tempura bar — seasonal small plates and Japanese sake
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, Japanese-language izakaya-style tempura bar aimed at local adult diners (jazz, low lighting); an English Tabelog listing exists but there's no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null); address and '6 min walk from Shibuya Station east exit / 12-min exit, 7 min from Omotesando exit B1' place it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
gf_detail: This is a traditional tempura specialist: the batter is standard wheat flour and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is typically soy sauce (contains wheat) plus bonito dashi — both common gluten sources. No source found mentions a gluten-free batter, alternative flour, dedicated fryer, or any other celiac-safe accommodation, so it should not be considered safe for a strict gluten-free/celiac diet without direct (Japanese-language) confirmation from the restaurant.
hours_raw: Lunch: Mon, Tue 11:30-14:30. Dinner: Mon-Fri 17:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Regular closed day: Sunday; a temporary notice also lists Saturdays and holidays as closed.
neighborhood: 渋谷（渋谷駅より徒歩6分）

positives.text: Offers a dedicated all-vegetable tempura course (7 pieces, ¥1,380 per person) explicitly aimed at vegan guests, using seasonal vegetables fried fresh to order rather than a modified version of a meat/seafood dish.

seats_text: About 20 seats; private parties of 15-25 can be accommodated.
vegan_detail: A specific all-vegetable tempura course is offered — 7 pieces of seasonal vegetables, fried to order, for ¥1,380 per person — introduced by the restaurant as being for vegan diners. This is a genuine, dedicated vegan option rather than an ad hoc substitution. However, no source confirms whether the accompanying dipping sauce/salt is free of bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, which is the most common vegan pitfall with Japanese tempura; vegan diners should ask for salt or a confirmed plant-based dip instead of the standard tentsuyu.

lead: アジアンダイニング&バー SAPANA 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-20-8 FUNDES水道橋 1F

bio.origin: Nepal
bio.background: According to the Global SAPANA Group's company history, founder Bhuvan Adhikari was born in Nepal and moved to Japan after finishing high school, working jobs including dishwashing at an Indian restaurant. In 2001 he opened his first two Asian restaurants in Iidabashi (Asian Kitchen & Bar SAPANA and Iidabashi Prano), which grew into the multi-branch Global SAPANA Group that now includes this Suidobashi location.
bio.specialty: Cheese naan made with an original cheese blend, tandoori chicken, and Indian/Thai-style curries with adjustable spice levels (mild to level 6).

anecdotes.text: Founder Bhuvan Adhikari moved from Nepal to Japan after high school and worked washing dishes at an Indian restaurant before opening his first SAPANA restaurants in Iidabashi in 2001.

caveats: This is a 24-hour multi-branch chain restaurant (Global SAPANA Group), not an independent shop. GF/vegan details below are inferred from menu descriptions (vegetarian set, curry/naan offerings) found via search aggregation, not a direct staff confirmation of allergy protocols — celiac/severe-allergy diners should call ahead (03-3512-3970) to confirm.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed days; open 24 hours, but a daily cleaning window around 6:00-9:00 may restrict entry)
cuisine: Indian & Thai / Asian Dining Bar (Nepalese, Vietnamese and Korean dishes also on menu), with a vegetarian set available
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains an English-language corporate website and English Tabelog listing and serves a broad, multicultural clientele near Tokyo Dome/Suidobashi's business and student district, so English-speaking diners should find it easy to navigate.
geocode_note: Address (三崎町2-20-8 FUNDES水道橋 1F) is described consistently across sources as a 1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station West Exit, well within the 0.8km Suidobashi target radius.
gf_detail: Many curries are rice-based and could be gluten-free, but the restaurant also serves wheat naan, roti and other breads made in the same open kitchen, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or dedicated prep area is publicized. Diners with celiac disease should confirm specific dishes and cross-contact risk directly with staff before ordering.
hours_raw: 24時間営業（年中無休）。ただし6:00〜9:00頃は清掃のため入店できない場合あり、深夜帯も営業していない場合がある。
name_ja: アジアンダイニング&バー SAPANA 水道橋店
neighborhood: 水道橋西口 (Suidobashi Station West Exit), Chiyoda-ku

positives.text: A vegetarian set is offered, including samosas, a spinach-cheese curry, a vegetable curry, salad, and a choice of naan or rice.

seats_text: Approximately 100 seats, including private rooms; the venue can host private parties for up to about 150 people standing.
vegan_detail: A vegetarian set (samosa, spinach-cheese curry, vegetable curry, salad, naan or rice) is available, but curries commonly use ghee, butter, or dairy cream typical of Indian/Nepalese cooking, so strict vegans should confirm dairy-free preparation and any honey/animal-derived ingredients with staff before ordering. No dedicated vegan menu was found.

lead: 鶏そば かぐら屋 西新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-16-10 西新宿ミカエラビル1F

bio.origin: Part of the 鶏そば かぐら屋 (Tori Soba Kaguraya) chicken-ramen family, whose first shop opened near Suidobashi in January 2013
bio.background: Kaguraya built its name on a single idea: ramen made entirely from chicken, with no pork or beef anywhere in the broth. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop is one of several branches (Suidobashi, Ueno-Okachimachi and others) run under that same recipe and philosophy.
bio.specialty: 濃熟鶏そば (Noujuku Tori Soba) — a rich, collagen-laden chicken paitan ramen
bio.philosophy: The kitchen simmers large quantities of Oyama-brand chicken bones with vegetables, scallop shell and Hidaka kelp for roughly 12 hours, cracking the bones partway through to pull out marrow before re-filtering and re-emulsifying the broth, aiming for a smooth, chicken-only soup with no pork or beef backbone. Noodles are custom-ordered from Taisei Shokuhin, a noodle maker founded in 1917.

anecdotes.text: The concentrated white broth is made by slowly simmering whole Oyama chickens and bones with vegetables and kombu, then simmered again for 3-4 hours with chicken fat, with the bones cracked to extract marrow before a second filtering and emulsifying pass — about 12 hours total.
The tsukemen tare is built from chicken thigh simmered down with soy sauce, then layered with katsuobushi, saba-bushi, niboshi and konbu dashi, paired with thick Hokkaido-wheat noodles.

caveats: Business-hours figures vary across secondary listing sites (some show 11:00-23:00 with no closed days, others show 11:30 to 2:00am with Sun/holiday closing at midnight); the direct Tabelog and official-site pages could not be fetched (403), so this record relies on Tabelog search snippets and third-party aggregator/blog sources. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere — this is a standard chicken-broth ramen/tsukemen shop with wheat noodles and fish-based dashi in its tare, not a spot suited to strict GF or vegan diners.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found; one source describes it as 不定休 (irregular/occasional closures), another lists it as open daily. Treat as generally open every day.
cuisine: Tori Soba (鶏そば) — chicken-bone ramen and tsukemen
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood chicken-ramen shop near Seibu-Shinjuku Station with a compact counter-and-table layout; expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of small ramen shops, though it does list on Uber Eats/Demae-can for English-app-assisted delivery ordering.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option, allergen menu, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination protocol was found in any source. The noodles are wheat-based (custom-ordered from a Hokkaido-flour noodle maker), and the tare for the shoyu-style bowls and tsukemen is soy-sauce based — standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-containing unless specifically labeled otherwise, and no gluten-free labeling was found. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address captured from Tabelog/aggregator listings as 東京都新宿区西新宿7-16-10 西新宿ミカエラビル1F. Multiple independent sources place the shop roughly 210-320m from Seibu-Shinjuku Station and about 288m / 4 min walk from Shinjuku Station's West Exit, well within the Shinjuku Station target radius.
hours_raw: 11:30〜翌2:00 (日・祝は〜24:00) — some listings alternatively show 11:00〜23:00, 無休
name_ja: 鶏そば かぐら屋 西新宿店
neighborhood: West Shinjuku (Seibu-Shinjuku area), Shinjuku-ku

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-style and tsukemen tare are built on soy sauce combined with katsuobushi, saba-bushi, niboshi and konbu dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based and no gluten-free alternative was mentioned in any source.

seats_text: Small shop with a 6-seat counter plus three 4-person tables (about 18 seats total)
vegan_detail: All broths are chicken-bone based (paitan or clear chicken soup), and the tsukemen/shoyu tare additionally relies on katsuobushi (bonito flakes), saba-bushi (mackerel flakes) and niboshi (dried sardines) for dashi. No plant-based or vegetarian menu items were found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-20-7

bio.background: Opened in 2020 just off Nakamise-dori, a short walk from Kaminarimon Gate. The counter is run by two tempura cooks who each trained more than 30 years in Asakusa before opening their own shop.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen deliberately fries without sesame oil in its blend, aiming for a lighter, less heavy tempura while still keeping the classic Edo-mae style alive.
bio.specialty: Tokusei Tendon (special tempura rice bowl) with conger eel (anago), two shrimp, a soft-set egg, and three seasonal vegetables (shishito pepper, kabocha squash, shiitake mushroom) over rice, alongside a larger 'mega tendon' version.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the tempura as fried fresh to order and coated in a well-balanced sweet-savory sauce, with plump shrimp and a finish that isn't too heavy or greasy.

caveats: Hours/closed-day info varies slightly between listing sites (some say open year-round, others say irregular closures) — call ahead to confirm. This is a standard Edo-style tempura shop, not a dedicated GF or vegan kitchen: the batter is wheat-based and vegetable tempura is fried in the same oil as shrimp and conger eel, so it is not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diners despite offering vegetable tempura on the menu.
closed_days: No single fixed weekly closed day found; sources disagree between "open year-round" (年中無休) and "irregular closures" (不定休) — recommend calling ahead, especially around holidays.
cuisine: Tempura and tempura rice bowls (tendon), including a large "mega tendon" and vegetable tempura
cultural_comfort_note: Located steps from Kaminarimon Gate in a heavily touristed stretch of Asakusa, and reviews mention staff being welcoming to foreign visitors, though no dedicated English menu was confirmed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null); address 東京都台東区浅草1-20-7 is reported as roughly a 2-3 minute walk from Asakusa Station and just off Nakamise-dori near Kaminarimon, i.e. well within 0.8km of Sensōji (35.7148,139.7967).
gf_detail: Tempura batter is traditional wheat-flour based, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based in standard Edo-style preparation (soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free batter, alternate flour, or dedicated fryer was mentioned by any source — not a safe choice for celiac diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–14:30 & 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:30–15:00 & 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:00). One aggregator listed a single continuous 11:00–23:00 block with a 14:00–17:00 break instead — treat the split lunch/dinner service as the more consistently reported pattern.
neighborhood: Asakusa
seats_text: About 30 seats, including counter seating and second-floor seating
vegan_detail: The menu centers on shrimp and conger-eel tempura rice bowls; vegetable tempura (shishito pepper, kabocha, shiitake) is fried in the same oil as seafood, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is traditionally dashi-based (bonito). No vegan-specific preparation, substitution, or dedicated frying setup was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-safe.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町10-13 サンライズ渋谷1F

bio.origin: Zentsuji, Kagawa Prefecture — the historic home of Sanuki udon
bio.background: The shop traces back to a Sanuki udon shop founded in 1936 in Zentsuji, Kagawa Prefecture. It later moved to Matsuyama, then to Tokyo in the 1970s — first to Shibuya's Udagawa-cho, at a time when Sanuki-style udon was still largely unfamiliar in the Kanto region, then to Tomigaya. In 2013 it settled into the current Maruyama-cho location near Shinsen Station, now carried on by the fourth generation of the founding family.
bio.specialty: Hand-made Sanuki udon, served bukkake-style (noodles topped with a poured sauce) or with tempura, in a Kansai-style bonito dashi broth
bio.philosophy: Continuing the hand-made noodle-making technique passed down across four generations since the shop's 1936 founding in Kagawa

anecdotes.text: The current Maruyama-cho shop is the latest stop in a lineage that runs from a 1936 Zentsuji (Kagawa) udon shop through Matsuyama, Shibuya Udagawa-cho, and Tomigaya, before settling here in 2013 under the fourth generation.
The interior has a retro, subculture atmosphere, decorated with vintage anime figurines and movie posters.

caveats: This is a traditional hand-made wheat udon shop — the noodles themselves contain gluten and the broth is fish-based, so it is not a safe choice for celiac/gluten-free or vegan diners despite being included for locational/cultural reference. Sources give slightly conflicting hours (a simple 11:00-17:00-or-sold-out pattern vs. a more elaborate day-by-day lunch/dinner split); the shop is known to close early once the day's hand-made noodles run out, and some sources note irregular Tuesday closures (1st/3rd/5th) plus New Year holidays. No credit cards accepted, though PayPay is.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休); some sources note closures on the 1st, 3rd, and 5th Tuesdays of the month, plus the New Year holiday period. Also closes early on any day once the hand-made noodles sell out.
cuisine: 讃岐うどん（Sanuki Udon）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, locally-loved neighborhood udon shop with no documented English menu or foreign-visitor accommodations, though it is easy to point-and-order.
gf_detail: The noodles are traditional hand-made Sanuki udon, i.e. wheat flour — inherently not gluten-free, with no gluten-free noodle alternative documented anywhere. The dipping/pouring sauces are standard Japanese shoyu-based tsuyu, which conventionally contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or noodle option is mentioned in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners.
geocode_note: Do not geocode (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null. Area confirmed via multiple independent sources citing the address as 円山町10-13 (Sunrise Shibuya bldg, 1F), 3 minutes/230m on foot from Shinsen Station, and approximately 7-10 minutes on foot from Shibuya Station/Scramble Crossing — within the required 0.8km radius of Shibuya Scramble (35.6595,139.7005) given typical walking-route overestimation versus straight-line distance.
hours_raw: Most consistently reported as 11:00-17:00 daily, closing earlier if the day's hand-made noodles sell out ("麺無くなり次第終了"). One source lists a more elaborate day-specific split (shorter Mon/Tue lunch-only hours, Wed-Fri lunch 11:30-16:00 plus dinner 18:00-21:00/21:30, weekends 11:30-21:30) — sources are not fully consistent, so treat hours as irregular and confirm same-day if possible.
name_ja: 麺㐂 やしま 円山町店
neighborhood: 円山町 (Maruyama-cho), Shibuya — near Shinsen Station

vegan_cross_contact.text: The udon broth is a Kansai-style dashi made with bonito (katsuobushi), used throughout the menu, including for tempura dipping sauces.

seats_text: 18 seats (4 at the counter, 14 at tables)
vegan_detail: The broth used across the menu (including bukkake and tempura udon) is a bonito (katsuobushi) fish-based Kansai-style dashi — not vegan or vegetarian. No vegetable-only broth, plant-based dashi, or vegan menu option is documented in any available source.

lead: 海鮮と炉端焼き 囲炉裏 新宿
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-18-13 近江ビル3F

bio.background: A charcoal-grill robatayaki izakaya operating as the Shinjuku flagship ("新宿本店") of a multi-branch robatayaki concept, located about a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's Keio New Line Exit 5 / Keio Mall Exit 3. The restaurant offers fully private-room seating for parties as well as open dining, built around a charcoal robata grill, fresh seafood, and Kyoto-style dashi oden.
bio.specialty: 刺身の桶盛り — thick-cut sashimi heaped in a wooden tub, made from fish shipped in from Sado, Niigata, alongside seafood sourced from the Toyosu market; and 土鍋ご飯 — charcoal-finished clay-pot rice served in seasonal versions (e.g. salmon and salmon roe, charcoal-grilled mackerel, Wagyu sukiyaki-style).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature dish is 刺身の桶盛り: thick-cut sashimi from carefully selected fish, shipped directly from Sado in Niigata Prefecture, served heaped in a wooden tub for the table.
Its clay-pot rice (土鍋ご飯) course changes seasonally, with versions such as salmon-and-roe, charcoal-grilled mackerel, and Japanese-beef sukiyaki-style rice, cooked to order in individual pots.

caveats: Primary listing pages (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Hitosara, Yahoo Map, Navitime) returned access-restricted (403) responses when fetched directly in this session; all details below were confirmed via search-engine excerpts of those same pages, which were cross-consistent across at least 4 independent listing sites (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Jalan). No dedicated official restaurant website could be confirmed — an operator name (\"RH Group\") surfaced in search results but could not be verified as this specific location's operator, so official_url is left blank. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation, ingredient disclosure, or allergy program was found in any source; this is a standard seafood/robatayaki izakaya where dashi, soy sauce, and oden broths should be assumed to contain wheat and bonito/fish stock unless staff confirm otherwise. Cash/card policy and exact closed-day (if any) were not explicitly stated in any source found — hours are listed identically for every day of the week including holidays, suggesting no regular closed day, but this is inferred rather than explicitly confirmed.
closed_days: Not explicitly stated in any source found. Listed hours (12:00–24:00) are given identically for 月-日、祝日、祝前日 (Mon–Sun, holidays, and pre-holidays), suggesting the restaurant is open daily with no regular closed day, but no source explicitly confirms this.
cuisine: Charcoal-grilled seafood & robatayaki (炉端焼き) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu, English-speaking staff, or foreign-visitor amenities were confirmed in any source; a Tabelog English-language listing page exists (auto-translated), but the restaurant itself appears to operate in Japanese, so visitors without Japanese should expect to rely on translation apps or picture menus.
geocode_note: Address (西新宿1-18-13, Oumi Building 3F) is described in listings as roughly a 1-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's Keio New Line Exit 5 / Keio Mall ground-level Exit 3 — effectively inside Shinjuku Station itself, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station (35.6896,139.7006) target radius. lat/lng left null per instructions (no geocoding performed); pin to be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer program was found in any source. As a robatayaki/oden izakaya, soy sauce (含む小麦/wheat) and dashi are near-certain wheat/bonito bases across sashimi tare, oden broth, and grilled-item glazes; no ingredient substitution or celiac-safe protocol is documented. Not recommended for strict gluten-free diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff (in Japanese) of ingredient sourcing.
name_en: Kaisen to Robatayaki Irori Shinjuku (Seafood & Robatayaki Irori Shinjuku)
name_ja: 海鮮と炉端焼き 囲炉裏 新宿
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), Shinjuku Station area

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free/gluten-free soy sauce alternative is advertised on any listing; as a standard robatayaki/oden izakaya, regular (wheat-containing) shoyu should be assumed throughout the menu.

seats_text: Listings state capacity of up to 150 guests total, with fully private-room seating available for parties plus additional open/counter seating; individual room sizes are not broken out in available sources.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan accommodation was found in any source. The restaurant is built around seafood (sashimi, grilled fish/shellfish) and dashi-based oden, which almost certainly uses katsuobushi (bonito) stock throughout; not viable for vegan diners without a fully custom, pre-arranged order confirmed directly with staff.

lead: ラーメン凪 豚王 渋谷本店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区東1-3-1 カミニート1F

roles: Founder/Chef: Satoshi Ikuta (生田智志), originally from Fukuoka
bio.background: Ramen Nagi began in 2004 as a tiny 8-seat, one-day-a-week shop in Shinjuku's Golden Gai, founded by Fukuoka-born chef Satoshi Ikuta. In 2006 the company launched its tonkotsu (pork-bone) sub-brand, Butao, and this Shibuya location opened June 15, 2006 (renewed/reopened March 2, 2020). Butao later became an unexpected overseas hit as 'BUTAO' after opening a flagship in Hong Kong's Central district in 2010, and that overseas popularity helped cement its reputation back in Japan.
bio.origin: Shinjuku Golden Gai (2004) → Butao brand and this Shibuya shop (2006)
bio.specialty: 豚王 (Butao) — a rich yet clean-finishing Hakata-style tonkotsu pork-bone ramen, simmered 10-12 hours, served alongside three creative variations: 赤王 (Akao, made with a house-made rayu chili-oil base and sweet aromatic vegetables), 黒王 (Kurao, roasted garlic mayu oil with black sesame and squid ink for deep umami), and 翠王 (Suio, a basil and olive-oil accented broth).
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim with the tonkotsu broth is to avoid the heavy, off-putting pork-bone odor common to the style while still delivering concentrated flavor, resulting in a broth that finishes lighter than its richness would suggest.

anecdotes.text: Butao's pork-bone ramen first broke out internationally as 'BUTAO' at a Hong Kong Central district location opened in November 2010, and that overseas success is credited with amplifying the brand's profile back home in Japan.
The shop at 渋谷区東1-3-1 first opened June 15, 2006, and was renewed with an updated tonkotsu recipe on March 2, 2020.

caveats: This is a classic Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen specialty shop, not a restaurant with any gluten-free or vegan focus — it appears in this sweep only because it falls within the Shibuya target radius. No GF or vegan accommodation was found in any source; the pork-bone broth base makes it unsuitable for vegan/vegetarian diners, and no allergen chart or gluten-free noodle option was located. Payment method (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed with certainty — the shop uses a ticket vending machine typical of Tokyo ramen counters, which sources describe as being limited to smaller cash denominations, but current card/e-money acceptance is unverified. Seat count also could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: None found — sources describe the shop as open every day (無休)
cuisine: 豚骨ラーメン (Hakata-style tonkotsu pork-bone ramen) — signature 豚王 plus 赤王/黒王/翠王 variations
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known, internationally recognized ramen chain (with overseas branches including Hong Kong) that draws many foreign visitors, but ordering is still via an in-store ticket vending machine typical of Japanese ramen shops.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment); lat/lng set to null. Multiple sources describe the address as roughly 7-8 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's East Exit, placing it within the Shibuya Scramble target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen chart was found in any available source. The ramen uses standard wheat noodles, and the tare/flavor bases (soy-based tare, rayu chili oil, garlic mayu oil, basil-olive oil) are not documented as wheat-free. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners without direct confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 10:00-22:30 (Sun/holidays 10:00-20:00); open daily (無休)
name_ja: ラーメン凪 豚王 渋谷本店
neighborhood: Shibuya (East exit area, near Shibuya Higashi)
seats_text: Not confirmed from available sources
vegan_detail: Every ramen is built on a tonkotsu (pork bone) broth; no vegetarian, vegan, or plant-based broth alternative is documented in any source found.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-5 北沢ビル（〒101-0051）

bio.origin: Kanda Jimbocho, Tokyo
bio.background: The building has been in the Imoto family's book trade since her grandfather founded Kitazawa Bookstore here in 1902, later run as a foreign-language bookstore by her father. A children's picture-book shop called "Bookhouse Jimbocho" occupied the same spot from 2007-2017 with backing from major publishers, but closed in 2017 once it was felt to have fulfilled its cultural role. Yoshiko Imoto, who had raised her own children on visits to that shop, felt Jimbocho (Tokyo's famous book district) could not be allowed to lose its only picture-book specialist, and took over the business herself despite doubts from family, friends and neighboring booksellers. She reopened it as Book House Cafe on May 5, 2017, built on four pillars -- bookstore, cafe, events, and rentable gallery/event space -- to keep it financially sustainable.
bio.specialty: Beef and cheese curry made with roux from a well-known Jimbocho curry shop, plus a house baked curry-bread and hayashi rice; it has been entered in the Kanda Curry Grand Prix.
bio.philosophy: Keep Jimbocho's only new-picture-book specialty store alive as a place where people connect over books, pairing over 10,000 picture books with a cafe, an evening picture-book bar ("Liliput"), and gallery/event space for readings and exhibitions.

anecdotes.text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, when business was in serious difficulty, Imoto posted a direct, crowdfunding-style appeal for support on the shop's own website, and customers rallied to help the store survive.
The shop occupies the site of the earlier publisher-backed picture-book store "Bookhouse Jimbocho" (2007-2017); Imoto took over rather than let Jimbocho lose its only picture-book specialist.

caveats: Primarily a children's picture-book store with an attached cafe (and an evening picture-book bar called Liliput), not a dedicated dining destination. No vegan or gluten-free information could be found on the official site or any review source; the cafe menu (beef/cheese curry with roux, pasta, sandwiches, curry bread, hayashi rice) is wheat- and meat/fish-heavy with no indication of allergen accommodation, so treat it as a general-audience cafe rather than a GF/vegan pick. cash_only and reservation_required are inferred (not explicitly confirmed) since no source stated payment methods or booking policy; hotpepper.jp lists it as bookable online. Coordinates are intentionally null per instructions -- address confirms it is about 1-2 minutes on foot from Jimbocho Station Exit A1, comfortably within 0.8km of Suidobashi Station.
closed_days: 年末年始のみ休業（それ以外は無休）
cuisine: Café / coffee / children's picture-book store & café
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-language picture-book shop and cafe aimed at local families; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help most for ordering and asking about ingredients.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address places it about 1-2 minutes' walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A1, which is well within 0.8km of Suidobashi Station (one of the target-area anchors), confirming it is in the target area.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, wheat-free preparation, or allergen accommodation are mentioned by the official site or any review/press source found. The signature dish is a roux-based curry (roux typically contains wheat flour) served with rice, plus pasta and bread sandwiches -- all likely to contain wheat, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. Assume this is not a safe option for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff, which could not be verified here.
neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町 (Kanda Jimbocho), near Jimbocho Station
seats_text: Around 30 seats spread across the shop's 1st-3rd floors (bookstore, cafe, and gallery/bar spaces)
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based substitution options are mentioned anywhere. The featured dishes are meat- and fish-based (beef curry, mentaiko/cod-roe pasta, tuna sandwich, egg sandwich), and there is no indication of dashi/bonito disclosure or vegan accommodation. Treat as not vegan-friendly unless confirmed directly with staff.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿8-15-19 桜山ビル1F

bio.background: Opened in November 2020 as a signless, word-of-mouth bakery tucked one street off Ome-kaido in Nishi-Shinjuku. With no storefront sign, it built its following almost entirely through regular customers and its X (Twitter) account, which the shop uses to announce each day's small-batch stock, sometimes with same-day sell-out or schedule-change notices.
bio.specialty: Hand-shaped natural-yeast bagels, generously filled with rotating flavors (matcha red bean butter, honey blue cheese, salted butter caramel, zunda red bean, ham & cheese), plus made-to-order scone sandwiches on Tuesdays.

anecdotes.text: The shop has no sign and sits down an alley off Ome-kaido; it grew a devoted following purely by word of mouth and through its X (Twitter) account (run under the handle 'ぱん屋'), which posts each day's small-batch bread lineup and lets regulars reserve items ahead of pickup.
Reviewers describe the natural-yeast bagels as satisfyingly chewy ('mucchiri') and heavily filled, calling it a distinctive small bakery worth seeking out in the Nishi-Shinjuku office district.

caveats: Not a gluten-free or vegan-focused shop: it is a traditional wheat-based bakery (natural-yeast bagels and scones) with no gluten-free items or vegan labeling found in any source. Menu items commonly include butter, cream cheese, ham, and honey. Cash only, takeout only (no seating), and open just four days a week (Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat) with stock/schedule announced via the shop's X (Twitter) account — worth checking @panya0411 before visiting since hours and which days bread is available can shift week to week.
closed_days: Mondays, Thursdays, Sundays, and public holidays (定休日: 月・木・日・祝)
cuisine: 天然酵母パン・地元の小さなベーカリー (naturally leavened bagels and scones, small independent neighborhood bakery)
cultural_comfort_note: There is no storefront signage and daily stock/schedule updates are posted only in Japanese on the shop's X (Twitter) account, so some Japanese ability (or a companion who can read it) helps you find the shop and know what is available that day.
gf_detail: This is a conventional wheat-flour bakery specializing in naturally leavened (天然酵母) bagels and scones. No gluten-free bread, substitutions, or dedicated preparation area were mentioned in any source found, so it is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Address is 西新宿8-15-19, reported as roughly 200m (about a 2-4 minute walk) from Nishi-Shinjuku Station Exit 1, and separately described as about a 10-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's west side — placing it at or near the edge of the 0.8km radius around the Shinjuku Station landmark point. lat/lng left null per instructions (no geocoding performed); downstream geocoding of the address should confirm exact placement.
hours_raw: 11:00~19:00頃（営業は火・水・金・土のみ、売り切れ次第終了。営業日・在庫はX(Twitter) @panya0411 で要確認）
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku) / near Nishi-Shinjuku Station
seats_text: Takeout-only counter/showcase sales; no indoor seating (there is a small table out front useful for organizing bags, not for dining in).
vegan_detail: Menu centers on dairy- and egg-rich items — ham & cheese scone sandwiches, honey blue cheese bagels, salted butter caramel, matcha red bean butter — with no vegan-labeled items, plant-based substitutions, or vegan menu callouts found in any source. A plain bagel exists on the menu but its exact ingredients (e.g. honey/malt in the boil) were not confirmed as vegan; best to ask staff directly if visiting.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町15-1 渋谷パルコ 4階

roles: Owner-produced by chef Kan Morieda (森枝幹)
bio.background: Chef Kan Morieda trained at Tetsuya's in Sydney, at the Kyoto kaiseki restaurant Kogetsu (湖月), and at the molecular-gastronomy Tapas Molecular Bar inside the Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, before going independent in 2011 and later opening CHOMPOO in Shibuya PARCO in November 2019.
bio.philosophy: An additive-free, MSG-free take on Thai food built on generous use of fresh herbs and spices — described by the chef's team as not 'Thailand's first Thai food' nor 'just spicy', but a comforting, deeply flavorful ('しみじみおいしい') style of Thai cooking, with colorful, photogenic plating.
bio.specialty: カオヤム (Kao Yam) — a vividly blue butterfly-pea-rice herb salad that has become the restaurant's signature, widely shared on social media.

anecdotes.text: CHOMPOO opened in November 2019 on the 4th floor of Shibuya PARCO as one of the dining anchors of the rebuilt mall, produced by chef Kan Morieda (who also ran Shimokitazawa's Salmon and Trout).
The restaurant has run seasonal menu refreshes highlighting fresh herbs and spices for spring/summer, and collaborations such as a limited-time terrace pairing with Veuve Clicquot.

caveats: Primarily an omnivore modern-Thai restaurant, not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free kitchen. It offers a distinct multi-course vegan tasting menu (6-8 dishes, plant/herb/vegetable/bean based, no animal products) that has been listed by the vegan directory VegeVege with V/VG/GF/organic tags, but no source found gives dish-by-dish detail on wheat-containing sauces (soy sauce, oyster sauce) or dedicated-fryer/cross-contact protocols for the regular (non-vegan) menu, so celiac-level gluten-free confidence could not be independently confirmed beyond the general tag.
closed_days: Effectively no fixed weekly closed day; hours follow Shibuya PARCO's own opening calendar (one source: "定休日：渋谷PARCOに準ずる"; another: "定休日なし").
cuisine: Modern Thai cuisine (with a dedicated vegan tasting course)
cultural_comfort_note: Sits inside the very tourist-trafficked Shibuya PARCO complex near Shibuya Scramble, so basic English support and card payment are likely, but the menu, reservation platforms (Ikyu, OZmall, Hot Pepper) and course descriptions found are all in Japanese.
gf_detail: The vegan-focused directory VegeVege lists CHOMPOO with a "GF" tag alongside V/VG/organic tags, and search summaries note the restaurant asks guests to report allergies and disliked ingredients in advance (same-day requests may not be accommodated). However, no source examined gave dish-level confirmation of which sauces are gluten-free — Thai cooking commonly uses soy sauce and oyster sauce, both of which can contain wheat — or confirmed a dedicated fryer/prep area. Celiac-level diners should call ahead (03-6455-0396) and confirm sauce ingredients directly.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null. Address is Shibuya PARCO, Udagawa-cho, immediately adjacent to Shibuya Scramble Crossing / Center-gai, well within the 0.8km Shibuya target radius.
neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya PARCO 4F, Udagawa-cho / Center-gai area)

staff_allergy_handling.text: Restaurant listing guidance indicates food allergies and disliked ingredients should be communicated in advance, and requests made on the day of the visit may not be accommodatable.

positives.text: CHOMPOO offers a dedicated multi-course vegan tasting menu (6 to 8 dishes) made only from seasonal vegetables, fruits, beans and herbs with no animal products — including appetizers, a Kao Yam herb salad, soup, a warm main, and plant-based dessert with tea.
The restaurant is additive-free and MSG-free across its menu.

vegan_detail: A distinct vegan Thai tasting course is available (6-8 dishes: multiple appetizers, Kao Yam herb salad, soup, a warm plant-based main such as vegan gapao/green curry, and dessert with herbal tea), made only with vegetables, fruit, beans and herbs — no fish sauce, oyster sauce, meat, egg or dairy in that course. This is a set course rather than freely substitutable a-la-carte dishes across the whole regular menu, so vegans dining outside the vegan course should still confirm individual dishes for fish sauce or oyster sauce, which are common in Thai cooking.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1丁目14-9 ソシアル道玄坂 1F

bio.background: According to restaurant-opening coverage, chef Tetsuo Ohira trained in cooking across roughly 20 countries and spent 8 years (from 2009) as owner-chef of a restaurant called "Lotus & Flowers One" before opening PEACE TABLE in Shibuya in 2020 as an all-vegan flagship. The restaurant was later renovated to build out a full Ayurveda program, developed with input from Dr. Kazuo Uemba, described in press coverage as a leading figure in Japanese Ayurveda.
bio.specialty: A personalized Ayurveda-based vegan set course: guests fill out a dosha questionnaire (Vata / Pitta / Kapha) and are served a matching course of soup, bread, salad, main and dessert — for example a creamy mushroom-and-truffle pasta on the Kapha menu.
bio.philosophy: The name PEACE TABLE expresses the idea that people can share good food and a moment of peace regardless of nationality, religion or skin color; the restaurant's peace-sign logo doubles as the "V" of "vegan."

anecdotes.text: Guests are given a warming Agni drink to aid digestion while completing a dosha questionnaire (an English version is available), then receive a course tailored to their result — one visitor's Kapha-type meal came with a creamy mushroom truffle pasta and a gluten-free bread described as fresh and soft.

caveats: Detailed WebFetch of the primary Tabelog listing, official site, and several blogs returned HTTP 403 in this environment; facts below were assembled from Google-indexed search snippets of those same pages (ikyu.com, Tabelog JP party/menu pages, note.com blog, enjoytokyo.jp, cookingwithyoshiko.com) rather than direct page fetches. Two Tabelog IDs exist for this restaurant: 13246808 is marked \"[Relocated]\" and points to the current listing 13261855 (the target URL), matching a June 2020-era address change from 道玄坂1-16-15 (NKビル) to 道玄坂1-14-9 (ソシアル道玄坂). Seat count and exact cash/card policy could not be confirmed from any source.
closed_days: 2nd and 4th Saturdays and Sundays of each month, plus additional irregular closures per Tabelog
cuisine: Vegan / Ayurveda / plant-based Italian-influenced vegetable cuisine (curry, pasta, personalized dosha-based set courses)
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language dosha questionnaire and personalized menu guidance are offered, making the concept accessible to non-Japanese-speaking diners even though it requires more explanation than an average restaurant.
geocode_note: No network geocoding performed (blocked in this environment); lat/lng left null. Address (道玄坂1-14-9) and multiple sources' "3-minute walk from Shibuya Station" description place it well inside the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius.
gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and no official allergen/cross-contamination statement was found. However, the plant-based, largely Italian/Ayurveda-influenced menu (no traditional soy-sauce/dashi-based Japanese cooking) reduces typical wheat-via-shoyu risk, and at least one diner reported being served fresh, soft gluten-free bread as part of a standard course, suggesting gluten-free substitutions are available. Celiac travelers should confirm cross-contamination handling directly with staff before dining.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (last seating around 19:30). Closed 2nd & 4th Saturdays and Sundays, plus irregular additional closures.
name_ja: PEACE TABLE 渋谷道玄坂店（ピーステーブル）
neighborhood: Shibuya Dogenzaka (渋谷道玄坂), 3-minute walk from Shibuya Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: A visiting food blogger noted her Kapha-type set course came with gluten-free bread that was "super fresh and soft," though no formal cross-contamination or dedicated-equipment statement was found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Described as a fully vegan restaurant serving Ayurveda-style meals without dairy (notable since traditional Ayurvedic cooking often uses ghee/dairy), suggesting deliberate control over animal-derived ingredients.

staff_allergy_handling.text: On arrival, guests fill out a dosha (constitution) questionnaire — an English version is available — and staff/kitchen tailor the course to the guest's answers, which provides a natural touchpoint to flag allergies or gluten-free needs.

positives.text: 100% vegan, personalized menu system with an English questionnaire and a welcome digestive (Agni) drink before the meal.

seats_text: Seat count not specified in available sources
vegan_detail: Entirely vegan kitchen (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs). Being an Ayurveda/Italian-leaning concept rather than a traditional Japanese kitchen, it does not rely on katsuo/bonito dashi. The set courses are built around vegetables, curry, and pasta, with a reviewer noting the meal was explicitly dairy-free — unusual and deliberate given Ayurvedic cooking often defaults to dairy (ghee).

name_ja: 東京メルカート 東京ドームシティ店
cuisine: イタリアン・ピッツァ・ワインバー (Italian / wood-fired Neapolitan pizza / wine bar)
neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi, Tokyo Dome City Meets Port 3F)
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 東京ドームシティ MEETS PORT 3F
geocode_note: Network geocoding blocked in this environment; pin to be derived downstream from address_ja. Address places it inside Tokyo Dome City, roughly 1 minute on foot from Suidōbashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
seats_text: Seated approx. 70; can host up to 120 standing for parties, with an open terrace area
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (food last order 21:00, drink last order 21:00); last order may extend to 21:30 on Tokyo Dome event days
closed_days: No regular closed days (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: Sits inside the Tokyo Dome City shopping/entertainment complex, a heavily tourist-trafficked area, so basic English signage and picture menus are likely, though full English service is not confirmed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free pizza dough could be confirmed from any source. The signature dishes are wood-fired Neapolitan-style pizza and pasta, both wheat-based, and no source mentions gluten-free adaptations. Assume standard shared kitchen; not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. Every dish is served with a fresh vegetable salad side, and vegetable-topped pizzas/pastas exist on the menu, but cheese, butter, and animal stock are standard in the kitchen and no source confirms dairy-free preparation. Vegetarians could likely find something with cheese; strict vegans would need to ask staff carefully about hidden animal products.
caveats: Dietary accommodation details (gluten-free, vegan) are not published by the restaurant; the above assessment is inferred from the general Italian/pizza menu and should be confirmed directly with staff before visiting for celiac or strict vegan needs.

roles: pizza chef trained in Ischia, Southern Italy
bio.origin: Part of the TOKYO MERCATO brand (operated by Aquaplannet/Carvino group), with this branch inside Tokyo Dome City's Meets Port building
bio.background: TOKYO MERCATO is designed to recreate the feel of an Italian open-air market (mercato) inside Tokyo Dome City, combining a wood-fired pizzeria, an illy-brand espresso bar, and a sommelier-curated wine selection under one roof.
bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza (crispy outside, chewy inside), baked by a pizza chef trained on Ischia Island in Southern Italy, alongside seasonal pasta dishes.
bio.philosophy: To let guests experience the lively, market-like atmosphere and food culture of Italy in a single open, terrace-style space, suited to everything from casual meals to parties.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's pizza chef trained on Ischia Island in southern Italy, a region known for its historic Neapolitan pizzerias, before bringing that wood-fired technique to the Tokyo Dome City location.

The space is built around the concept of a Mercato (Italian market), pairing the pizzeria with an illy espresso bar and sommelier-recommended wines so guests can sample multiple facets of Italian food culture in one visit.

lead: TEXMEX FACTORY 渋谷公園通り店
name_ja: TEXMEX FACTORY 渋谷公園通り店（テクスメクスファクトリー）
cuisine: メキシコ料理・タコス（テクスメクス／Tex-Mex）
neighborhood: 渋谷・公園通り（渋谷駅徒歩5分）
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神南1-19-3 ハイマンテン神南ビル2F
geocode_note: Address is Shibuya Jinnan 1-19-3, roughly a 5-7 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit and Tokyu Shibuya exit 13 per multiple listings — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble anchor point. Not geocoded (lat/lng left null per instructions); pin should be derived downstream from this address.
seats_text: 約109席（喫煙ルームを含む）
closed_days: 無休(年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A high-volume Tex-Mex chain restaurant a few minutes from Shibuya Station with an English-language Tabelog listing and a picture-heavy taco/fajita menu, so ordering should be manageable with basic gestures even without shared language.
vegan_detail: Weekday lunch and the regular taco line-up include a plant-based 'Veggie Meat' (ベジタブルミート) taco option alongside the meat tacos, and make-your-own fajitas let diners skip meat and load up on guacamole, pico de gallo and beans. However standard dishes are built around cheese, sour cream and meat, no dashi/bonito-free stock has been confirmed, and there is no dedicated vegan menu or certification — vegan diners should specify no cheese/sour cream and confirm ingredients with staff.
gf_detail: Both gluten-free corn tortillas and wheat 'flower' tortillas are offered, so a corn-tortilla taco is possible in principle, but this is a busy all-you-can-eat buffet-style operation (Endless Tacos) where stations, grills and serving utensils are shared with wheat tortillas and other wheat-containing items. No dedicated gluten-free fryer, prep area, or celiac protocol has been documented anywhere found, so cross-contact risk is real and unconfirmed — not recommended for celiac-level sensitivity without direct confirmation from staff at time of visit.
caveats: No allergy menu, dedicated-fryer statement, or celiac-specific protocol was found in any source checked (official site, Tabelog, Hot Pepper, gourmet blogs). This is a large chain casual-dining/bar concept (operated by Watami Co., Ltd.) built around all-you-can-eat tacos, not a GF- or vegan-focused establishment; the veggie-meat and corn-tortilla options are conveniences, not dedicated allergen accommodations.

roles: Watami Co., Ltd. — operating company (chain casual dining & bar concept)
bio.background: TEXMEX FACTORY opened in Shibuya on August 22, 2016 as a new Tex-Mex ('old-school Mexican culture' filtered through Texas-style flavors) casual dining restaurant and bar launched by major Japanese restaurant operator Watami Co., Ltd. The Koendori-area branch sits a short walk from Shibuya Station and has since become known for its lively, colorful interior and all-you-can-eat taco format.
bio.specialty: Endless Tacos — all-you-can-eat tacos, taco bowls and burritos with unlimited soup and drink-bar refills (weekday lunch), plus sizzling-iron-plate fajitas that diners wrap themselves in tortillas with guacamole, pico de gallo and sour cream.
bio.philosophy: The concept leans into a bold, playful take on Tex-Mex: vivid primary colors, a pink key color, skull motifs throughout, and interior decor imported directly from Mexico and Texas, aiming to transport diners into a fun, festive 'south of the border' atmosphere rather than pursue formal authenticity.

anecdotes.text: A visitor report described lines forming before opening on weekdays in Shibuya for the ¥1,380 all-you-can-eat taco/taco-rice lunch, noting the festive pink-and-skull decor as a highlight of the visit.

A local Shibuya gourmet write-up described the restaurant as offering an authentic-feeling Mexican dining experience just a short walk from the station, citing the imported decor and taco/fajita/burrito line-up.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant serves both corn and wheat 'flower' tortillas side by side as part of an all-you-can-eat buffet-style taco service, with no mention anywhere of separate gluten-free prep, utensils, or stations.

positives.text: Corn tortillas (naturally gluten-free) are offered as an alternative to wheat 'flower' tortillas across the taco menu.

bio.chef_name: 薄田貴志・薄田宜子 (Usuda Takashi & Usuda Yoshiko)
bio.origin: Owner Usuda Takashi is from the Imabari / Shimanami area of Ehime Prefecture
bio.background: Chat Gatto opened in 2016, run by a husband-and-wife chef duo: Takashi, who trained in Italian cooking, and Yoshiko, who trained in French cooking. The restaurant's name blends the French word "chat" and the Italian word "gatto" (both meaning cat), symbolizing the two chefs working freely together across their two culinary traditions.
bio.specialty: Tajarin, a traditional Piedmont-style egg pasta made with Ehime-prefecture eggs, served with gold sesame and anchovy
bio.philosophy: Home-style French and Italian cooking centered on vin nature (natural wine) and ingredients shipped directly from Ehime Prefecture — vegetables and seafood from the owner's native Imabari/Shimanami area, plus local poultry, pork, venison and wild boar.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is tucked in a quiet back alley between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations with only about 10 seats, giving it a hideaway feel — regulars recommend booking ahead.

The menu's tajarin pasta, a traditional dish from Italy's Piedmont region, is made with eggs sourced directly from Ehime Prefecture, along with vegetables, fish and meats from the owner's home region.
Menu highlights spotted by diners include Ehime vegetable and fish marinades, a charcuterie platter, roasted wild boar, and tajarin with gold sesame and anchovy.

roles: operated by INGS Co., Ltd. (also known for らぁ麺はやし田 and other ramen brands)
bio.background: Nibo-shi Chuka Soba Suzuran is a niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen specialty shop near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, run by the INGS restaurant group, which operates several Tokyo ramen brands including Raa Men Hayashida.
bio.specialty: 濃厚煮干中華そば (rich niboshi ramen) and つけ麺 (tsukemen), made with a triple soup combining tonkotsu (pork bone), chicken, and dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi, topped with low-temperature-cooked rare chashu; noodles come in a choice of medium-thick curly or low-hydration thin styles.
bio.philosophy: The shop aims for a niboshi broth with little of the bitterness (エグ味) that puts off people who usually dislike dried-sardine ramen, so the flavor stays refined, balanced, and clean-finishing despite its richness.

anecdotes.text: The shop periodically runs a quantity-limited special, 特製炭火焼鯵煮干そば (charcoal-grilled horse mackerel niboshi soba), alongside its regular tsukemen.

caveats: Standard niboshi/tonkotsu-chicken ramen shop, no evidence of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation; noodles are wheat and broth is animal-based (fish, pork, chicken). Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official INGS, or blog pages (all returned HTTP 403); facts are drawn from WebSearch result snippets of those sources, which converged consistently across multiple independent listings (Tabelog, ramen databases, Hot Pepper, local blogs). lead: ラーメン二郎 新宿小滝橋通り店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-5-5

roles: Directly-managed Ramen Jiro branch (formerly an independently licensed "Foods-kei" satellite)
bio.background: Opened February 8, 1999. It began life as one of the licensed "Jiro Foods System" (フーズ系) satellite shops operating under the Jiro name but outside the founder's direct lineage. After Ramen Jiro's trademark was formally registered in 2003, most of these satellite shops were forced to rebrand or fold — leaving only the Kanda shop as a lingering Foods-kei holdout — and the Kotakibashi-dori shop transitioned to direct management under Jiro headquarters.
bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Standard Jiro-style ramen: thick wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) pork-bone broth, piled with cabbage, bean sprouts, and thick-cut chashu, customizable via the shop's shouted "call" system (garlic, vegetables, pork fat, sauce). Unusually for a Jiro shop it also offers table seating and a cheaper "student ramen."

anecdotes.text: During its years as a Foods-kei satellite the shop had a rocky reputation and was jokingly nicknamed "the worst Jiro" among devoted fans (ジロリアン); its standing has since recovered, with reports of the recipe improving further around 2017 and the noodles becoming noticeably thicker around 2019.
Unlike most Jiro shops, which are counter-only, this location has a 4-person and a 2-person table alongside its 12-seat counter.

caveats: Ramen Jiro's tonkotsu-shoyu style is built entirely on wheat noodles and a wheat-soy-sauce-based tare, and there is no indication of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation at this or any standard Jiro branch — this listing is included for completeness/awareness, not as a GF/vegan recommendation. Address confirmed from multiple independent listings (Tabelog, blog write-ups, restaurant directories); precise walking-distance claims (2-3 min from Shinjuku-nishiguchi / Seibu-Shinjuku stations) place it close to but not dead-center on Shinjuku Station — exact lat/lng could not be verified since geocoding is disabled in this environment.
closed_days: Open every day except New Year's Day (January 1)
cuisine: 豚骨ラーメン — Jiro-style (二郎系) tonkotsu-shoyu ramen
cultural_comfort_note: Ramen Jiro shops run on an unwritten etiquette and a shouted "call" system for customizing toppings (garlic, vegetables, oil, sauce) that can feel intimidating without some Japanese-language prep, though many foreign fans do fine with a quick primer beforehand.
gf_detail: This is a classic Jiro-style shop: thick house-made wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare/broth, meaning wheat is baked into both the noodles and the soup itself. There is no gluten-free noodle substitute or separate GF menu, and the tiny open kitchen (shared vats, one ladle line) makes cross-contact effectively unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

bio.origin: 1970年創業の老舗喫茶チェーン「珈琲館」（現C-United株式会社／コロワイドグループ）
bio.background: Kohikan is a long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) chain founded in 1970. In 2021 the original Kohikan Co. merged with Chatnoir (Café Veloce's parent) to form C-United, now part of the Colowide restaurant group. This Shibuya branch sits on the ground floor of Shibuya Cross Tower, a short walk from Shibuya Station's east exit.
bio.specialty: Sumibi Kohi (charcoal-roasted coffee), brewed from beans roasted over premium Japanese binchotan charcoal, alongside the chain's original house blend and seasonal specialty coffees.
bio.philosophy: The chain's long-standing motto is "one cup of coffee, made with all our heart" (一杯のコーヒーに心をこめて), reflecting a full-service, unhurried kissaten style rather than a quick-turnover coffee bar.

anecdotes.text: Kohikan built its identity around binchotan charcoal-roasted coffee and a wide, comfortable seating area meant to welcome solo customers and groups alike for a relaxed, old-school Japanese cafe experience.

roles: Japanese kissaten (coffee shop) chain, this location seats solo diners and groups on the ground floor of Shibuya Cross Tower

address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-24-6 鶴切ビル1F

bio.background: Ra-men Hosenka opened on July 26, 2018 in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, as a sister shop to the ramen brands 煮干中華そば鈴蘭 (Niboshi Chuka Soba Suzuran) and らぁ麺はやし田 (Ra-men Hayashida), all operated by the restaurant company INGS Co., Ltd. (founded 2009), which runs seven ramen shops plus the Italian restaurant chain CONA. Within the INGS ramen family, each shop has its own broth identity — Suzuran is dried-sardine (niboshi) based, Hayashida is chicken based, and Hosenka is the group's seafood-based shop, built around kinmedai (golden-eye snapper).
bio.specialty: Kinmedai ramen (金目鯛らぁ麺) — a light, shio-style bowl made from a dashi of kinmedai and madai (sea bream) bones, extracted with careful temperature and time control to keep the broth delicate rather than heavy. The chashu is sous-vide pork shoulder loin served rare for a moist, tender texture, topped with two large, crisp menma shoots. For an extra ¥200, diners can finish the meal with kinmedai chazuke, pouring the remaining dashi over rice.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps no condiments on the tables, asking diners to taste the kinmedai broth exactly as the chef intends rather than adjust it themselves; the shop is also promoted as 無化調 (no added MSG), extracting its umami from the fish bones alone.

anecdotes.text: 店内には卓上調味料がなく、スープや具材へのこだわりが徹底されています (There are no condiments on the tables — the shop is thoroughly committed to its soup and toppings as prepared).
チャーシューは豚肩ロースを低温調理したレアタイプで、しっとりとしてやわらかな食感が特徴 (The chashu is sous-vide pork shoulder loin served rare, prized for its moist, tender texture).
鯛塩系のラーメン...最後に金目鯛茶漬けで二度オイシイ (A tai-shio style ramen finished, for an extra charge, with kinmedai chazuke for a second course).

roles: signature dish: kinmedai (golden-eye snapper) shio ramen

caveats: This is a standard fish- and pork-based ramen shop, part of the INGS restaurant group (not an independent mom-and-pop operation, and not GF- or vegan-focused). Noodles are wheat-based (a blend including whole wheat) with no gluten-free alternative documented, and the broth/chashu are built on fish dashi and pork, so it is not celiac-safe or vegan. Cash-only, ticket-machine ordering, and no documented English menu mean limited accessibility for non-Japanese-reading diners. Coordinates are intentionally left null per instructions; the address places it roughly a 4-5 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station (multiple sources cite Seibu-Shinjuku Sta. 1 min / Shinjuku-nishiguchi Sta. 4 min / JR Shinjuku Sta. 5 min), well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.

Because the core product is wheat noodles in a shared open kitchen, this is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
