Address corroborated across Tabelog, Ramen Database, and multiple blog/listing sources as 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-24-6 (鶴切ビル1F), with walking-distance figures (Seibu-Shinjuku Sta. ~1 min, Shinjuku-nishiguchi Sta. ~4 min, JR Shinjuku Sta. ~5 min) placing it well inside the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.

lead: 海鮮居酒屋 魚秀 渋谷宇田川店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町35-6 下田ビルB1（〒150-0042）

roles: seafood izakaya run directly by a fish wholesaler/fishmonger (鮮魚卸直営)
bio.background: This Shibuya Udagawa-cho shop is one location of the small Tokyo seafood-izakaya group 魚秀 (UOHIDE), which is run directly by a fish wholesaler and operates a handful of seafood-focused restaurants around Tokyo (including branches in Kichijoji and Tsukiji) in addition to this Shibuya location, a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Ochoko-don (おちょこ丼) — small rice bowls piled high with a variety of fresh seafood toppings — alongside seasonal sashimi platters and a rotating raw seafood 'yukhoe'-style tartare fair, sourced through the group's own fish wholesale line.

caveats: This is a standard seafood-focused izakaya, not a dietary-specialty restaurant. No gluten-free or vegan menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated-fryer information could be confirmed from any source checked (Tabelog, official site, Gnavi, Hot Pepper, Hitosara). As a seafood izakaya run by a fish wholesaler, the menu centers on fish, shellfish, and seafood rice bowls (ochoko-don) — sashimi and raw items are naturally low-gluten but shared soy sauce, dashi, and any fried items should be assumed to contain wheat and bonito/fish stock unless staff confirm otherwise. Diners with celiac disease or vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; this listing should not be presented as GF- or vegan-friendly beyond "ask staff."
closed_days: No fixed regular closed day reported (some listings note occasional irregular closures, 不定休); most sources state open daily including weekends and holidays.
cuisine: 海鮮 (Seafood izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: Standard Japanese-run seafood izakaya near Shibuya Station; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or translation help is advisable.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found in any source checked. As a seafood izakaya, sashimi and raw seafood items are naturally low in gluten, but shared soy sauce (typically wheat-based), dashi, tempura, and sauces should be assumed to contain wheat unless confirmed otherwise with staff. No information on a dedicated fryer or formal allergy protocol was found — celiac diners should ask directly before ordering.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null (network geocoding blocked); address_ja (Udagawa-cho 35-6, Shibuya-ku) places this restaurant in the Udagawacho district immediately adjacent to Shibuya Station/Scramble Crossing — multiple sources state a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station Ginza/Hanzomon/Fukutoshin Line Exit A2, well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble target radius.
hours_raw: Mon–Sun, holidays, and days before holidays: 12:00–23:15 (food L.O. 22:45, drink L.O. 22:45). Lunch 12:00–15:00 / Dinner 15:00–23:15.
name_ja: 海鮮居酒屋 魚秀 渋谷宇田川店（UOHIDE）
neighborhood: 渋谷（宇田川町、渋谷駅近く）
seats_text: 64 seats total; semi-private tatami seating available for groups, banquet capacity up to 40 seated
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or vegan-specific information was found in any source. This is a seafood-focused izakaya run by a fish wholesaler, so dashi/bonito-based stocks and fish are central to nearly all dishes; it should not be presented as vegan-friendly. Vegan diners would need to ask staff about plain vegetable side dishes and confirm no fish stock is used, with no guarantee of a suitable option.

address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 黄色いビル 2F（東京ドームシティ「FOOD STADIUM TOKYO」内）

bio.origin: Sister branch (4th location) of the original 神保町すしわさび (Jimbocho Sushi Wasabi), opened June 24, 2024 inside the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall at Tokyo Dome City.
bio.background: Operated by GYRO HOLDINGS, this is a casual all-counter sushi izakaya set inside a lively, neon-lit sports-and-entertainment food hall next to Tokyo Dome, built as a livelier spin-off of its quieter Jimbocho sister shop.
bio.specialty: Bara chirashi (scattered sushi rice bowl) — the shop's signature dish, alongside hand-pressed nigiri sets (toro, seared, Hokkaido seafood) and seafood rice bowls at lunch.
bio.philosophy: A pop, high-energy take on the neighborhood sushi bar — fresh market fish and chef-made original small plates served at a counter, meant for solo diners, casual meetups with friends, or a relaxed date, with big screens and neon signage around the food hall.
roles: sushi izakaya / sister branch of Jimbocho Sushi Wasabi

anecdotes.text: 水道橋すしわさびは、神保町駅から徒歩2分ほどの「神保町 すしわさび」の4店舗目となる姉妹店。ネオン看板が目を引く全席カウンターの寿司酒場で、派手でポップな空間で味わう新感覚の寿司体験が特徴。夜はお酒と創作おつまみ、昼は海鮮丼やランチ握り、名物のバラちらしが楽しめる。

厳選鮮魚と絶品つまみ — casual sushi tavern offering sushi, good sake, and appetizers, featuring fresh fish sourced from the market and the chef's original creative small dishes; open 11:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drinks L.O. 22:30).

caveats: Address is in Bunkyo ward (文京区後楽, inside Tokyo Dome City), not Chiyoda ward as stated in the brief — but it is confirmed ~3 minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station, well within the required 0.8km radius, so the location itself checks out. This is a standard sushi izakaya (4th branch of a small chain, GYRO HOLDINGS) with no dedicated vegan or gluten-free program found anywhere across official pages, press release, or gourmet-site listings — treat any GF/vegan claim with caution; soy sauce and tempura-style items should be assumed to contain wheat unless staff confirm otherwise. Several source pages (Tabelog, gorp.jp shop sites, r.gnavi, Navitime) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so details below are drawn from search-result snippets of those pages rather than full-page fetches, though the core facts (address, hours, seats, concept) were corroborated across multiple independent sources.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day (open 7 days); hours may be altered during New Year holidays, Golden Week, and Obon.
cuisine: Sushi / Nigiri / Izakaya (fresh seafood, creative appetizers, bara chirashi)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, Japanese-language-first sushi izakaya inside a busy local food hall — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will get you through, but don't expect English-speaking staff or an English allergy menu.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address places it inside the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall at Tokyo Dome City, which multiple independent sources describe as a 3-minute walk from Suidōbashi Station — comfortably within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any official or review source found. As a sushi/izakaya spot, standard shoyu (contains wheat) is almost certainly used for dipping and glazes, and fried/tempura-style appetizers are on the menu. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should ask staff directly before ordering; nothing here should be assumed safe by default.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 daily (food last order 22:00, drink last order 22:30); hours may vary during New Year, Golden Week, and Obon periods.
neighborhood: 水道橋駅 徒歩3分 / 文京区後楽（東京ドームシティ内）
seats_text: 50 seats, all counter seating
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/bonito-free preparations were found in any source. As a fish-centric sushi bar, dashi and sauces should be assumed to contain katsuo (bonito) by default. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without prior direct confirmation from staff.

lead: 地鶏と焼酎 とりちゅう
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-13-6

roles: Izakaya specializing in Kagoshima free-range chicken (jidori) and shochu
bio.background: Part of the Sun Dining restaurant group, this Suidobashi izakaya is built around slow-reared free-range chicken from Kagoshima and a deep shochu list, bringing a Kyushu-style chicken-and-spirits izakaya experience near Tokyo Dome/Suidobashi Station.
bio.origin: Kagoshima / Amami, Kyushu — the chicken breed and the rice dish both trace to Kagoshima and Amami culinary traditions
bio.specialty: Chiran-dori (知覧どり), a Kagoshima free-range chicken raised roughly 450 days (far longer than typical broiler chicken), served as kurogayaki (black-seared grilled skewers) and tataki, alongside signature tori-zuke nanban (marinated fried chicken) and Amami-style tori-meshi chicken rice simmered daily in Chiran-dori broth
bio.philosophy: Centers the menu on long-reared, high-quality jidori chicken paired with one of the larger izakaya shochu selections in Tokyo (roughly 200 labels, including about 30 ready-mixed shochu drinks), aiming to bring Kyushu chicken-and-shochu izakaya culture to central Tokyo

caveats: This is a chain location (part of the Sun Dining restaurant group, which operates multiple "とりちゅう" branches), not a standalone independent shop, so independent=false and it does not qualify as MOM_AND_POP despite being a small izakaya. No gluten-free or vegan program, menu, or allergy protocol was found in any source — GF/vegan assessments here are inferred from the restaurant's cuisine type (tare-glazed chicken yakitori/izakaya) rather than a confirmed statement from the restaurant. Several primary pages (official site torichu0410.gorp.jp, gnavi, hitosara, hotpepper) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; facts about hours, address, seating, and concept are corroborated across multiple independent listing sources via search rather than a single direct page fetch.
closed_days: Sundays and holidays (日・祝日定休)
cuisine: 居酒屋 Izakaya - Kagoshima Jidori (Free-range chicken) and shochu
cultural_comfort_note: Menu, signage, and online reservations are geared toward Japanese-speaking guests with no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment); address and multiple sources place it in Misaki-cho, Chiyoda-ku, about a 4-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station West Exit, well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station (35.7017,139.7539).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free tare, or celiac protocol was found in any source. As a yakitori/izakaya centered on tare-glazed grilled chicken, dishes very likely use wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) in the tare/marinades, and some menu items (e.g., tori-zuke nanban, described as marinated fried chicken) appear to be breaded/fried, raising cross-contamination risk in shared fryers. No source confirms a wheat-free option, dedicated fryer, or ingredient disclosure. Not recommended for strict gluten-free or celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30), Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. food 22:00 / drink 22:30). Fri (and day before holidays): Lunch 11:30-15:00, Dinner 17:00-23:30. Sat: Lunch 11:30-14:30, Dinner 16:30-23:00. Closed Sundays and holidays.
name_en: Jidori to Shochu Torichu (Torichu — Free-Range Chicken & Shochu)
name_ja: 地鶏と焼酎 とりちゅう
neighborhood: 水道橋 Suidobashi
seats_text: Counter, table, and tatami-mat seating (approximately 8 counter, 14 table, 30 tatami — about 52 seats total)
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around jidori chicken (grilled skewers, tataki, marinated fried chicken, chicken rice) with meat as the centerpiece of virtually every dish; rice dishes such as tori-meshi are cooked in chicken broth. No vegan or vegetarian menu, plant-based dishes, or accommodations were found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-10-1 吉岡ビル1F

bio.background: SOBA-JU opened in Shinjuku Sanchome in August 2019 as part of a small local restaurant group (alongside the wine bar Margo), reimagining soba as a bar-friendly dish to pair with wine and sake.
bio.specialty: Niku-soba (meat soba) with duck, pork, or beef, served over thick, chewy, coarse-ground (hikigurumi) buckwheat noodles in a broth built from a mackerel-and-bonito dashi blend, given a subtle kick from domestic sansho pepper and chili oil.
bio.philosophy: A stylish, counter-only 'soba bar' concept that treats soba as a food to linger over with a glass of wine, rather than a quick lunchtime bite, while still keeping the noodles themselves the centerpiece.

anecdotes.text: A visiting soba blogger described SOBA-JU's noodles as thick, full-grained, and close to juuwari (100% buckwheat), with a fragrant aroma and pronounced chewy texture, and specifically praised the beef soba (gyu-soba).

caveats: Sources for hours, seat count, address, and card acceptance are aggregator listings (Gnavi, Hotpepper, NAVITIME, Yahoo/Goo maps) synthesized via search rather than directly fetched, since the official site, Tabelog, Hotpepper, and Gnavi pages all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; multiple independent aggregators agree on the core facts. No direct fetch of the official site was possible, so the exact buckwheat percentage and full ingredient list (e.g. soy sauce brand) could not be confirmed.
closed_days: Sunday (Monday is lunch-only, no dinner service)
cuisine: 蕎麦 (Soba / buckwheat noodles) with an evening soba-bar / izakaya menu (meat soba, offal dishes, wine)
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-only neighborhood soba bar with counter seating and no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese or pointing/translation-app use will likely be needed.
gf_detail: The noodles are made from coarse-ground (hikigurumi) whole buckwheat flour, and at least one visitor described them as close to juuwari (100% buckwheat), but the restaurant's own materials do not state an exact flour ratio or confirm a wheat-free blend, so a two-eight (wheat-containing) mix cannot be ruled out. The signature broth is a mackerel-and-bonito dashi that, like most Japanese soba tsuyu, is very likely seasoned with ordinary (wheat-containing) soy sauce. There is no mention of a dedicated gluten-free prep area, dedicated boiling water, or wheat-free soy sauce. Celiac or severely gluten-sensitive diners should call ahead and ask directly about the buckwheat ratio and soy sauce ingredients before relying on this spot.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Mon-Sat); Dinner 18:00-25:00/1:00am (Tue-Sat); Monday dinner and all day Sunday closed; hours may vary before public holidays
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area, cookware, or boiling water is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house broth is a mackerel-and-bonito dashi typical of soba tsuyu, which in Japan is almost always seasoned with standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari option was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core menu (meat soba, mackerel-bonito dashi broth, offal dishes at dinner) is built around fish and meat stock, so vegan diners cannot rely on shared broth or sauces being incidentally plant-based.

positives.text: The soba flour is a coarse whole-grain (hikigurumi) buckwheat, and one visiting blogger judged the noodles close to juuwari (100% buckwheat) with a fragrant, hearty texture.

seats_text: 15 counter-only seats
vegan_detail: The kitchen's signature dishes are all built on animal ingredients: the standard soba broth is a mackerel-and-bonito (katsuo) dashi, and the lunchtime specialty is meat soba topped with duck, pork, or beef; the evening menu leans further into meat/offal izakaya dishes served with wine. No vegan-specific broth, noodle, or dish was found in any source, so this is not a workable option for vegan diners without a from-scratch off-menu request that the restaurant may not be able to accommodate.

cuisine: Japanese / Soba (そば・天ぷら)
neighborhood: 円山町（Maruyama-chō）/ 神泉（Shinsen）, Shibuya
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町6-1
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng left null). Multiple independent sources (Tabelog aggregate summary, Retty, Hitosara, official site) consistently place the restaurant at 東京都渋谷区円山町6-1, roughly a 6-minute walk from JR Shibuya Station and a 2-3 minute walk from Keio Inokashira Line Shinsen Station — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target point. One secondary listing (Mapion, different phone number) cites 円山町6-6, likely a stale/mismatched aggregator entry rather than this location.
seats_text: Approximately 26 seats split between counter seating (where guests can watch the soba and tempura being made) and table seating, across a main house and a smaller annex.
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional hideaway soba-and-tempura restaurant inside a renovated century-old house; expect Japanese-language menus and reservation channels (TableCheck/Ikyu/AutoReserve in Japanese) rather than English signage or staff.
gf_detail: This is a classic nihachi (二八) soba restaurant — the noodles are milled at roughly 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour as a binder, and the tempura is a wheat-flour batter, so neither the noodles nor the tempura are gluten-free. No dedicated fryer, gluten-free noodle option, or wheat-free menu is documented anywhere in available sources. Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping broth) is also typically built on regular (wheat-containing) shoyu. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: As a traditional soba-and-tempura restaurant, the dipping broth (tsuyu) is conventionally made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and the menu includes wagyu, tuna, and other animal-based dishes alongside seafood-inclusive tempura. No vegan menu, dashi substitution, or plant-based broth is documented in any source reviewed.
caveats: Reported hours vary slightly across booking sites (dinner closing time is listed as 21:00-23:00 depending on the source), and at least one older write-up describes an earlier wagyu/tuna-kaiseki-focused menu before the restaurant narrowed its focus to soba and tempura — confirm the current menu and exact hours when booking. This is not a gluten-free or vegan-oriented restaurant; it is included for general dietary transparency only.

roles: soba and tempura chefs (individual chef not named in available sources)
bio.background: Opened in May 2012 inside 'Maruyamachō Sanchō,' a Japanese wooden house more than 70 years old in Maruyama-chō, Shibuya — an area that once flourished as a geisha district. The restaurant's menu has since narrowed from a broader wagyu/tuna kaiseki concept toward hand-made soba and freshly fried tempura as its centerpiece.
bio.specialty: Nihachi (two-eight) soba, made from buckwheat sourced seasonally from carefully chosen regions, served alongside freshly fried tempura that guests can eat with rock salt or dip into the tsuyu broth.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes its cooking as layering a modern sensibility onto a foundation of Japanese tradition, set within a space meant to evoke the atmosphere of old Maruyama-chō's geisha-district era.

anecdotes.text: Guests enter through a corridor from the street that leads into 'Maruyamachō Sanchō,' a century-old house whose hallways and fittings still carry traces of the shamisen music and lantern-lit geishas that once filled the neighborhood at night.

The dining space is split into a main house ('母屋') and a separate annex ('はなれ'), both built around counter seating so diners can watch the chefs' handiwork while a small courtyard garden changes with the seasons.
The restaurant's tempura is plated separately from the soba so it stays crisp, meant to be eaten first with a touch of rock salt before the noodles are dipped in the broth.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-16-1

bio.background: Opened in Jimbocho in August 2025, run by a chef who spent many years honing his craft in Edomae-style sushi before striking out on his own with this small counter.
bio.chef_name: Ichikawa (surname as referenced in restaurant listings; not independently verified via a fetched primary source)
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi (traditional vinegared, cured, and aged preparations rather than plain raw fish)
bio.philosophy: Described as blending traditional Edomae technique and carefully selected seasonal ingredients with the chef's own sensibility, aiming for a refined, beautifully presented, calm counter experience.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note that the roughly ¥20,000 course delivers quality comparable to high-end Tokyo sushi-ya that typically charge over ¥30,000, calling it remarkable value for the craftsmanship shown.

caveats: Chef name and some biographical detail come from search-engine result summaries of the Tabelog/TableCheck listings rather than a directly fetched page (Tabelog and other Japanese review sites returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment), so treat the chef-name attribution as medium confidence. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation of any kind is documented; this is a traditional Edomae sushi counter, not a dietary-focused restaurant.
closed_days: Sunday, Monday
cuisine: Edo-style (Edomae) sushi
cultural_comfort_note: A small, reservation-only traditional sushi counter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; booking is via phone or Japanese-language reservation platforms, so English-speaking diners may want help booking.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address and multiple listings place it about 100-130m from Jimbocho Station exit, which is roughly 0.7km from Suidōbashi Station — within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented anywhere for this restaurant. Traditional Edomae sushi relies on nikiri (a soy-based glaze) and regular shoyu for dipping, both of which normally contain wheat, and none of the sources found mention a gluten-free alternative, a dedicated fryer, or celiac-aware handling. Diners with celiac disease or a serious gluten allergy should not assume safety here and should call ahead in Japanese before booking.
hours_raw: Online reservation slots run from 17:30 through 24:00 (last bookable start time), Tuesday through Saturday, dinner service only; closed Sunday and Monday. Exact posted opening/closing times were not found independently of the reservation-slot list.
neighborhood: Jimbocho (Kanda-Jinbocho), Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Counter seating on the ground floor only; parties of 3 or more are seated on the 2nd floor.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi restaurant built around raw, cured, and vinegared fish and shellfish; no vegan menu, substitutions, or plant-based omakase option is documented in any source found. Traditional Edomae preparations typically use bonito (katsuo) dashi in the tamagoyaki and other components, so it is not a workable option for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿2-12-4 アコード新宿ビル2階

roles: Owner is a self-described tiramisu enthusiast who developed the cafe's signature tiramisu
bio.background: CAFE ZUKKE is an independently run cafe/bar in an antique-furnished space in Shinjuku Sanchome, a 1-2 minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station exits C5, C6 and C8. The shop's name is said to come from an Italian expression meaning roughly 'cheer me up.'
bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.philosophy: The stated house motto is "delicious and fun" (美味しくて楽しい) - creating an exciting, comfortable moment for guests in a relaxed antique-and-jazz atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Slow-braised beef tongue stew (タンシチュー) and handmade pizza (Margherita and Quattro Formaggi), alongside made-in-house desserts such as tiramisu and Basque cheesecake.

anecdotes.text: The owner is described as a tiramisu enthusiast who put particular care into perfecting the cafe's tiramisu recipe.

The interior is furnished with antique-style furniture, dark gray concrete-look walls and jazz played through curated vacuum-tube amplifiers/speakers, aiming for a calm 'hideaway' feel near Shinjuku Sanchome.
Signature beef tongue stew is made by slow-braising thick-cut beef tongue in red wine until very tender.

caveats: No vegan or gluten-free-specific menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. The menu centers on beef (tongue stew, hamburger, steak), wheat-based pizza dough, and dairy-based desserts (tiramisu, cheesecake), so it should NOT be treated as vegan- or celiac-safe without direct confirmation from staff. Suitable mainly as a general Italian-inflected cafe, not a dedicated dietary-accommodation spot. Hours/closed-day info comes from aggregator listings (Hot Pepper, blog posts) since the official site and Tabelog page itself could not be directly fetched (403 errors); cross-checked across multiple independent search results for consistency.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day found in sources
cuisine: Cafe, Italian/Western
cultural_comfort_note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; expect a standard Japanese-language cafe experience, though ordering by pointing at photos should be manageable.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null). Address and station-exit references (Shinjuku Sanchome Station exits C5/C6/C8, "5 minutes on foot from Shinjuku Sanchome Station" per Hot Pepper) place it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The signature dishes include wheat-based pizza dough and beef tongue stew typically served with bread; no information on soy sauce/dashi wheat content, shared fryers, or cross-contamination protocols was available. Do not assume celiac safety — call ahead if this matters.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 daily (food last order 22:00, drink last order 22:30); lunch service 11:00-15:00. Closed days: irregular (不定休).
name_ja: CAFE ZUKKE（カフェ ズッケ）
neighborhood: Shinjuku Sanchome
seats_text: About 30 seats total, mixing counter seating (~6 seats), antique-style sofa seating (5-6 people), and table seating.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-adapted dishes were found. The core menu is meat- and dairy-centric (beef tongue stew, hamburger, steak, cheese-topped pizza, dairy desserts); no information found on hidden dashi/katsuo use in savory dishes. Not recommended as a vegan option without direct confirmation.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's dinner course menu centers on organic vegetables — an 8-dish, 3-hour all-you-can-drink course pairs a whole grilled assortment of organic vegetables with a slow-simmered young chicken in cream sauce, plus a smoothie bar.
Listed job postings describe the shop as a casual bar proud of its organically and low-pesticide grown vegetables ("有機・減農の野菜が自慢のカジュアルバル"), located on Dogenzaka a short walk from Shibuya Station.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog page, the (apparently defunct/non-resolving) official domain nokivege.com, or Retty/Ekiten/Facebook pages — this record is built from WebSearch result snippets/synthesis of those pages rather than direct page fetches, since WebFetch and direct curl both returned 403 for tabelog.com, ekiten.jp, retty.me and r.gnavi.co.jp (either bot-blocking or organization egress policy). No review or listing dated 2024-2026 was found, so current (2026) operating status is inferred from the still-live, still-indexed Tabelog listing (rating 3.10, active course/party pages) rather than confirmed directly; no explicit 閉店/掲載保留 flag was found. Seat count, reservation policy, payment methods, and any gluten-free/vegan-specific accommodation details were not found in any source and are left as unknown rather than assumed.

roles: chef (South Indian, trained in Tamil Nadu)
bio.background: Sri Balaji (シリバラジ) began as a South Indian restaurant in Nakameguro and later opened this Suidobashi branch in 2015 in Nishikanda, a spot wedged between Suidobashi and Jimbocho stations. The restaurant takes its name from Sri Balaji, one of South India's most widely worshipped deities — regarded as a god of wealth — who also gives his name to a major Hindu temple.
bio.origin: Tamil Nadu, South India (kitchen team's background)
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to serve South Indian food 'exactly as it tastes locally,' without adapting flavors for Japanese diners, using spices and herbs imported directly from India.

anecdotes.text: The chef comes from South India and previously worked at a well-known hotel in Tamil Nadu; the restaurant imports its spices and herbs directly from India rather than substituting local ingredients.
The restaurant's name, Sri Balaji, refers to one of South India's most famous deities, worshipped as a god of wealth, and is also the name of a major Hindu temple in South India.

caveats: The menu includes non-vegetarian dishes (tandoori chicken, chicken biryani, chicken curry) alongside its extensive South Indian vegetarian offerings (dosa, idli, vada, sambar, vegetable korma, meals/thali), so this is not a dedicated vegetarian, vegan, or gluten-free venue. bio.background: 168 Tenshin Yamucha & Yakuzen Nabe is the Shibuya Hikarie branch of Gozenbo (御膳房), a well-known Chinese medicinal-cuisine restaurant group with sister locations in Roppongi and Ginza. It opened on the 6th floor of Shibuya Hikarie in April 2022, bringing chefs recruited from mainland China to a mall setting one minute from Shibuya Station.

bio.philosophy: Built on the Chinese principle of isshoku-dogen (医食同源) — 'medicine and food share the same source' — using organic Japanese produce and ingredients chosen for both flavor and wellness.

The medicinal mushroom hot pot is nicknamed 'taberu esute' (an edible spa treatment) in Yunnan, China, where it has traditionally been eaten by women for its skin-beauty benefits.
Dim sum is handmade following the isshoku-dogen ('medicine and food, same source') philosophy, using organic Japanese ingredients selected by chefs trained at top mainland Chinese restaurants.

bio.origin: Nepal

Search-result summaries of a dancyu magazine feature describe her as the smiling proprietress (女将) of the shop, with the sign out front showing a woman cheerfully holding a giant naan and a beer mug.
bio.specialty: Dal bhat (Nepal's everyday rice-and-lentil set) at lunch, and Newari-style Nepalese izakaya dishes at dinner, including deep-cut items like simmered pig's-foot jelly and stir-fried pig's ear alongside spice-grilled chicken made with over 20 spices imported directly from India and Nepal.
bio.philosophy: Bringing authentic home-style Nepalese and Newari (Kathmandu-region) cooking-not found elsewhere around Suidobashi-into an izakaya format for pairing with drinks.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is run as a family operation by Rami-san, her husband, and their son, serving deep-cut Newari Nepalese dishes alongside Japanese izakaya-style drinking food.

Lunch centers on naan-and-curry sets and dal bhat; dinner turns into an ethnic-cuisine izakaya featuring Newari specialties from Nepal's capital region, using more than 20 spices flown in directly from India and Nepal.

bio.origin: Nihonbashi, Tokyo
bio.background: Katsukichi opened in 1962 near Korakuen/Tokyo Dome and has kept its retro Showa-era interior almost unchanged ever since. The founder, Yoshida Kichizaemon, was the son of Iwatani Shohei, a well-known Meiji-era tobacco magnate. The dining room is filled with roughly 2,000 antiques — Imari porcelain tableware and reclaimed wood and water pipes salvaged from the old Tamagawa Canal — giving it the feel of a small private museum.
bio.specialty: Tonkatsu made from select branded domestic pork fillet, hand-coated in fresh nama-panko breadcrumbs and slowly fried in fresh corn oil; also known for a steak served with a distinctive soy-sauce-based sauce rather than the usual demi-glace.
bio.philosophy: A craftsman's commitment to quality ingredients — branded pork, fresh breadcrumbs, and fresh oil — carried forward largely unchanged since the shop's founding over 60 years ago.

anecdotes.text: Novelist Yukio Mishima, who trained at a nearby gym in Korakuen, was a regular. According to a well-known story, when a steak he ordered wasn't cooked the way he liked, he sent it back untouched and left without eating — and his preference for soy sauce over the restaurant's original demi-glace sauce is said to be the origin of the soy-sauce steak that later became a house specialty.
Author Yasunari Kawabata, a close acquaintance of the founder, gifted the restaurant a calligraphy work reading '亥' (boar), which is still displayed on the restaurant's wood paneling today.

caveats: This is a historic, meat-focused tonkatsu specialist, not a gluten-free or vegan destination. No source mentions a gluten-free menu, rice-flour breading, dedicated fryer, or vegan dishes — the signature dish is deep-fried, wheat-panko-breaded pork. Diners with celiac disease or who eat vegan should look elsewhere in this app; this record is included for completeness/context only. Reservations are reportedly advisable even for weekend lunch per diner reviews, though not confirmed as a strict house policy.
closed_days: Open year-round (無休) except the New Year holiday period, approximately December 31–January 2.
cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet) and seasonal Japanese dishes
cultural_comfort_note: A historic, traditional Japanese tonkatsu restaurant with a retro Showa-era atmosphere and antique-filled dining room; no English menu was confirmed, so a little Japanese or pointing at the picture menu may help.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-2-10

bio.origin: Rooted in the old Ueno Ikenohata kappo restaurant Kameya Issuitei (亀屋一睡亭); opened as a stand in Shinjuku Nishiguchi Omoide Yokocho in Showa 46 (1971).
bio.specialty: 元祖天玉そば (Ganso Ten-tama Soba) — soba topped with freshly fried kakiage tempura and a soft hot-spring egg
bio.philosophy: Fast, no-frills soba built for standing-counter speed, carried by a deeply concentrated soy-based tsuyu broth the shop has refined for over 50 years.
bio.background: Kameya opened in 1971 as one of the early stalls in Shinjuku Nishiguchi Omoide Yokocho (the old 'Shomben Yokocho'), tracing its roots to the historic Ueno Ikenohata restaurant Kameya Issuitei. It is widely credited in Tokyo food writing as the birthplace of ten-tama soba — soba topped with tempura and a soft egg — and has since grown into a small group of standing soba counters across Tokyo (Kanda, Okachimachi, Shimbashi, Ginza) while its original Shinjuku counter keeps running nearly around the clock.

anecdotes.text: Kameya is credited as the originator of ten-tama soba, topping kakiage tempura with a soft-cooked hot-spring egg instead of a raw one — a combination now considered a Tokyo standing-soba classic.
The Shinjuku shop has no doors or walls, built like an open-air stall — a look it has kept for more than 50 years since opening in 1971.
The shop's dark, richly concentrated soba tsuyu is a deliberate house style, giving even a hurried standing-counter meal deep, memorable flavor.

caveats: Traditional standing (tachigui) soba and tempura counter with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations: the noodles are wheat-blended (standing soba shops typically use a higher wheat ratio so noodles hold up to fast handling), tempura batter is wheat-based, and the tsuyu dipping broth is soy-sauce and (very likely) bonito-based. The signature dish is topped with egg. Included for cultural/local-flavor context near Shinjuku Station only — not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diners.
closed_days: Sundays (定休日：日曜日); hours also taper on Saturday overnight before the Sunday closure.
cuisine: Soba / tachigui (standing) soba and tempura, since 1971
cultural_comfort_note: Orders are placed and paid for verbally with staff at the counter — no ticket machine and no confirmed English menu — so a little Japanese or pointing at neighbors' bowls helps.
gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners. Reporting on tachigui soba shops generally (including this one) notes that standing-counter soba noodles use a higher wheat-flour ratio than seated shops so the strands survive fast handling, meaning the soba itself carries significant wheat. The tempura batter is also wheat-based, and the concentrated tsuyu dipping broth is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle, batter, or sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere, and there is no indication of allergen-aware kitchen handling at this fast, open-counter operation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature ten-tama soba is topped with a soft hot-spring egg (onsen tamago) and kakiage tempura over a non-vegan tsuyu broth.

No plant-based menu items or accommodations were found.

lead: にょろ助 東南屋 浅草
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-33-5

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (site southeast of Sensō-ji Temple)
bio.background: The restaurant occupies a building that has kept its Shōwa-era (mid-20th-century) street-front appearance. It previously operated under the related name たつみ屋 (Tatsumiya) and reopened as にょろ助 東南屋 浅草 on November 11, 2006, run by the Tokyo restaurant group 際コーポレーション (Kiwa Corporation), which operates a small number of similarly branded 'Nyorosuke' eel/loach restaurants. The shop's name, 東南屋, literally 'southeast house,' refers to its position southeast of Sensō-ji Temple and is read 'Tatsumiya' as a nod to the old zodiac-direction name for that compass point.
bio.specialty: 地焼きうなぎ (jiyaki-style grilled eel) — eel grilled directly without pre-steaming, a Kansai-region technique that yields a crisp exterior, tender flesh, and more pronounced eel flavor than the steamed Kantō style; also known for どぜう鍋 (Asakusa-style loach hot pot), a downtown Edo-period commoners' dish served heaped with scallions.
bio.philosophy: Keeps old shitamachi (downtown Asakusa) dining traditions alive by serving unagi, dojō (loach), tempura, and sukiyaki together under one roof, in a room with sunken-kotatsu tatami seating reminiscent of an earlier era.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's jiyaki grilling method — grilling the eel without pre-steaming — is a distinctly Kansai technique, said to give the eel a crisper bite, tender flesh, and stronger aroma than the more common steamed Kantō style.

The shop's name, Tōnan-ya (東南屋), refers to its location southeast of Sensō-ji Temple and is read 'Tatsumiya,' echoing the old zodiac-direction name for that compass point; the restaurant previously traded under the related name Tatsumiya before rebranding as にょろ助 東南屋 in 2006.

caveats: Sources give slightly conflicting weekend closing times (11:00-21:30/L.O.21:00 per one search snippet vs 11:00-21:00/L.O.20:00 per another aggregator); used the more commonly repeated 21:30 close. No dietary-accommodation information (vegan/gluten-free) was found on any source — this is a traditional eel/loach/tempura specialist with no indication of allergy-friendly menu options. Primary sources were search-result snippets (WebFetch was blocked with 403 on tabelog.com, kiwa-group.co.jp, retty.me, and r.gnavi.co.jp in this environment), so wording is drawn from WebSearch summaries of those pages rather than direct page fetches.

bio.background: The Shibuya branch of Gapao Shokudo is led by a veteran Thai head chef from Thailand's northeastern Isan region, described in restaurant write-ups as one of Japan's ten most highly regarded Thai chefs ("Isaan cooking master") with roughly 40 years of culinary career and over 20 years living and cooking in Tokyo.
bio.origin: Isan (Isaan), northeastern Thailand
bio.specialty: Gapao Rice (fresh holy-basil chicken stir-fry with chili and oyster sauce over rice) — the restaurant's namesake signature dish
bio.philosophy: Focuses on authentic, home-style Isan/northeastern Thai cooking rather than a Japan-adapted version of Thai food, drawing on the chef's own regional roots.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as sitting up a small hill from Shibuya Station's west/south side — a cherry-blossom-pink, cute little eatery — and is a Thai Select-certified restaurant recognized for serving authentic Thai cuisine.
Alongside the namesake Gapao Rice, the menu highlights Larb Gai, a specialty dish of the chef's native Isan region.

caveats: Standard Thai restaurant, not a dedicated gluten-free or vegan establishment. No confirmed dedicated fryer or allergen-free prep line; oyster sauce and soy sauce (both common wheat-gluten sources) and fish sauce/dried shrimp appear standard across the menu (e.g., in the signature Gapao Rice and likely in papaya salad). One listing notes staff invite allergy inquiries ("please feel free to contact us if you have any allergies"), but no menu-level allergen chart or ingredient breakdown was found. Celiac and strict vegan diners should call ahead (03-6455-1817) and confirm specific dishes before visiting.
closed_days: No fixed closed days reported (open daily) per restaurant listings.
cuisine: Thai cuisine (タイ料理), Isan/northeastern Thai specialties
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language Tabelog page and delivery listing (Uber Eats) exist, and staff reportedly welcome allergy questions, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff on-site was found, so basic Japanese (or a translation app) is recommended.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya-ku, Sakuragaoka-cho 14-10) and multiple listings describe the restaurant as a 4-minute walk from JR Shibuya Station, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595,139.7005). Per instructions, lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or dedicated fryer was confirmed in available sources. Thai cooking here relies heavily on oyster sauce and soy sauce (both typically contain wheat) as base seasonings, including reportedly in the signature Gapao Rice, so most dishes should be assumed to contain gluten unless kitchen staff confirm otherwise. One listing indicates the restaurant welcomes allergy inquiries, so celiac diners should call ahead (03-6455-1817) and ask specifically about wheat-free substitutions and cross-contact in the wok before dining.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00), reported as open daily with no fixed closed days.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as Gapao Rice are prepared with oyster sauce and fish sauce as base seasonings; oyster sauce and any soy sauce used typically contain wheat, and this has not been confirmed as gluten-free at this restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A restaurant listing states customers with allergies are welcome to contact the restaurant, implying staff will field allergy questions on request, though no formal allergen menu was found.

seats_text: Approximately 36 seats (counter and table seating); a private room is reportedly available for parties of 3 or more with advance reservation.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu found. Thai dishes at this restaurant commonly use fish sauce (nam pla), oyster sauce, and other animal-derived seasonings as standard base ingredients (e.g., the signature Gapao Rice uses chicken and oyster sauce/fish sauce). Vegetable-forward Thai dishes may be orderable but are likely to contain hidden fish sauce or shrimp paste unless the kitchen is specifically asked to omit them; vegan diners should call ahead and confirm ingredient substitutions.

bio.background: trevo (トレボ) opened on May 8, 2022, tucked into a hard-to-find spot near Tokyo Dome, about a 1-5 minute walk from Suidobashi Station. It's a tiny hideaway cafe with just five two-person tables.
bio.specialty: Monthly-rotating Seasonal Plate (シーズナルプレート) — a vegetable-packed toast/sandwich plate; the July-August corn butter toast version is locally famous and draws long lines before opening.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's seasonal plates, especially the summer corn butter toast, are popular enough that customers queue before the shop opens on weekdays.
Described as a 'hideaway cafe' (隠れ家カフェ) near Suidobashi in a hard-to-spot location by Tokyo Dome.

caveats: Multiple secondary sources (blogs, Retty, aumo, cafesnap, LINE Place, official Tabelog page) could not be directly fetched due to environment network restrictions (403s on most non-search domains); facts below were assembled from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than direct page fetches, so treat hours and menu specifics as reasonably but not perfectly confirmed. address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町2-26-1 永和第一ビル B1・1F・2F（〒160-0021）

bio.background: いけす無門 新宿 is operated by Eiwa Group (永和グループ), a Tokyo restaurant company also known for its 月亭 shabu-shabu brand. The Shinjuku Kabukicho location is built around a giant in-house fish tank (いけす) at the center of the dining room, with counter seats overlooking it on the first floor and private tatami/horigotatsu rooms upstairs and in a waterfall-accented basement floor.
bio.specialty: 泳ぎいか (live squid sashimi) — squid kept swimming in the tank until ordered, then hand-cut to order by the chef.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's concept centers on 活魚 (live fish) cooked to order: fish, spiny lobster and abalone are sourced daily from regions across Japan and kept alive in the tank until a customer orders, so the seafood served is caught and prepared within minutes rather than pre-cut.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's official site notes that freshly caught live squid has a distinctive semi-translucent appearance rather than plain white, and that this freshness is what gives it its characteristic sweetness and texture — a point of pride the restaurant highlights as its signature dish.

roles: Live seafood (活魚) specialty restaurant, part of Eiwa Group

caveats: This is a live-seafood and shabu-shabu specialty restaurant with no vegan or gluten-free menu track found in any source; tempura and other fried items are on the menu, and standard shoyu (soy sauce, typically wheat-containing) appears to be the default condiment for sashimi. No allergy-specific policy, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination statement was found in any source, so GF/vegan diners should ask staff directly and expect limited accommodation.
closed_days: 12/31, 1/1 only (open essentially every other day of the year, including regular holidays)
cuisine: Live seafood / sashimi specialty (活魚), plus black-pork and beef shabu-shabu
cultural_comfort_note: All located sources (official site, Tabelog, Gnavi, Hotpepper, Ikyu, Hitosara) are Japanese-language; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found, though online reservation systems used are standard tourist-accessible platforms.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The menu includes fried items (天ぷら盛り合わせ / tempura assortment, フライ / fried dishes such as 子持ち昆布のフライ) prepared in shared fryers, and sashimi is normally served with standard shoyu, which typically contains wheat. Celiac diners should not assume any dish is safe without asking staff directly and should be cautious of cross-contact from the shared tempura/fry station and wheat-based soy sauce.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat: 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 17:00-23:00 last order 22:00 (dinner). Sun & holidays: 11:30-14:30 (lunch), 16:30-22:30 last order 21:30 (dinner).
neighborhood: Kabukicho, near Seibu-Shinjuku Station (西武新宿駅), Shinjuku
seats_text: Approximately 130 seats total, including counter seats overlooking the tank, table seating, and 10 private rooms (horigotatsu and tatami-style, seating 2 to 50 depending on room combination).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based dishes were found in any source. The restaurant's identity is built entirely around live seafood (activelyswimming squid, fish, spiny lobster, abalone) plus black-pork/beef shabu-shabu, and dashi/broths used in shabu-shabu and set courses are very likely fish- or bonito-based. Vegan diners have essentially no confirmed safe options here beyond possibly plain rice or a side vegetable, and would need to negotiate directly with staff with no guarantee of accommodation.

bio.background: Opened in March as a new concept from the Daishō Group (the company behind the Shōya izakaya chain), this Suidōbashi shop sources its lamb directly from Niku no Yamamoto, a long-established butcher in Chitose, Hokkaido.
bio.origin: Hokkaido (lamb sourcing)
bio.philosophy: Rather than the typical jingisukan hot-pot method, the shop net-grills the lamb over an open flame, which drains excess fat and reduces gaminess so the natural flavor of the meat comes through.
bio.specialty: Tokujō katarosu (特上肩ロース) — a premium raw lamb shoulder-roast cut, yielding only about 700g per lamb, grilled tableside.
roles: Hokkaido-lamb-focused jingisukan izakaya, part of the Daishō restaurant group

anecdotes.text: A food writer's visit described working through a full course of jingisukan cuts and drink pairings, highlighting the shoulder roast as the standout dish and the retro Shōwa-tavern atmosphere with sports on large TV screens.

caveats: This is a mainstream chain izakaya specializing in grilled Hokkaido lamb (jingisukan), not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment. No dedicated GF or vegan menu, allergen protocol, or dedicated fryer was found in any source consulted — celiac and vegan diners should not treat this as a safe default without calling ahead. Exact seat count and precise opening date were not published in any source found.

lead: 焼きあご塩らー麺 たかはし 新宿本店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-27-3 KKビル1F（〒160-0021）

bio.origin: 新潟県 (Niigata Prefecture)
bio.background: Founder and president Takahashi Yuka grew up in Niigata eating a local specialty: ramen made with yakiago (grilled flying fish) dashi. Wanting to bring that hometown flavor to Tokyo, she founded the company in 2011 and, with no prior experience in the ramen industry, opened the first shop as a two-person, husband-and-wife operation in Myogadani in 2012. Business was slow at first, but after the shop relocated/expanded to a spot in Kabukicho, Shinjuku in February 2015, it became an instant hit — the store is widely credited with popularizing yakiago (grilled flying fish) ramen as a nationwide trend when the ingredient was previously little known. The brand celebrated its 10th anniversary in February 2025 and has since grown into a small multi-location company.
bio.specialty: 焼きあご塩らー麺 (Yakiago Shio Ramen) — a salt ramen built on a broth of pork bone and chicken carcass stock combined with grilled flying fish (yakiago), dried sardines (niboshi), and kelp, finished with a proprietary flavored oil that concentrates the grilled-fish aroma. The noodles are a hand-kneaded style made from premium center-cut wheat flour co-developed with a long-established Kyoto noodle maker, and the tare (sauce base) uses Sekigahara tamari soy sauce paired with a generous amount of dried sardines.
bio.philosophy: Recreate and share a nostalgic, hometown Niigata flavor — yakiago-based ramen — with Tokyo diners, elevating a regional ingredient that was largely unknown in the capital before this shop popularized it.

anecdotes.text: Takahashi started the company in 2011 with no ramen-industry background, opening a small husband-and-wife shop in Myogadani in 2012 that struggled with slow business before finding success after moving into Kabukicho in 2015.
The Kabukicho shop is credited with sparking the modern 'yakiago (grilled flying fish) ramen' boom in Tokyo, and the brand marked its 10th anniversary in February 2025.

caveats: Business-hour details differ slightly across sources (closing time cited as anywhere from 22:30 to 24:00 on regular days, with Fri/Sat extending to roughly 2:00-3:00am) — hours_week below is a best-effort consolidation and should be treated as approximate. No source confirmed gluten-free or vegan menu options, dedicated fryer status, or payment methods (cash_only is an unconfirmed best guess, not sourced); these should be verified by phone before a celiac or vegan visit. Do not confuse with the separate nearby 歌舞伎町店 (Kabukicho-ten) branch, which is a different location from this 新宿本店 (Shinjuku Honten).
closed_days: No regular weekly closed day reported; possible closure around New Year holidays (unconfirmed).
cuisine: ラーメン（焼きあご塩らー麺 / grilled flying-fish salt ramen）
cultural_comfort_note: Small Japanese-style counter ramen shop in Kabukicho; expect Japanese-language menu and ordering norms, though the shop is well known and has drawn international visitors and English-language press coverage.
gf_detail: Noodles are wheat-based — a hand-kneaded style made from premium center-cut wheat flour co-developed with a Kyoto noodle maker — and the tare (sauce base) is built on Sekigahara tamari soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated allergen protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

bio.background: The owner trained at the well-regarded ramen shop Menya Yu (麺屋雄) in Ichinoe before striking out on his own and opening Usagi as a tiny counter shop in Shinsen in May 2007.
bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen built on a whole-chicken (marutori) broth, alongside a broth-less "shirunashi" tantan-men with adjustable Sichuan-pepper numbing and chili heat.

anecdotes.text: The owner is the younger brother of the acclaimed hip-hop producer Nujabes, and the shop plays his music throughout service as a quiet tribute.
Usagi opened in Shinsen in May 2007 after the owner trained at the respected ramen shop Menya Yu in Ichinoe; it has since spun off sister shops including Kousagi in Otsuka and Usatan in Itabashi.

caveats: Ticket-machine ordering system strongly implies cash-only, but no source explicitly confirmed payment methods, so cash_only is an inference rather than a direct citation. No source mentioned any gluten-free or vegan accommodation whatsoever — this is a traditional wheat-noodle, soy-based ramen-ya with no dietary-alternative menu found. Several source pages (Tabelog itself, Hotpepper, Retty, Shiblog, Norman.jp, Chawanbushi, Vacan magazine) returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, so address/hours/seat/background facts rely on search-engine snippet extraction from those indexed pages rather than a full page read; core facts (address, hours, seats, founding story) were corroborated across multiple independent snippets. lat/lng left null per instructions since network geocoding is blocked; distance to Shibuya Scramble was estimated using Shinsen Station's public coordinates (35.657093, 139.693546, ~680m from the Scramble) plus the stated ~4-minute walk from the station, placing the restaurant within the 0.8km target radius.

cuisine: Ramen (shoyu ramen and tantan-men / spicy noodle soup)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic hole-in-wall ramen counter that uses a Japanese ticket-vending machine to order, with no English menu found online, so some comfort with ticket-machine ordering helps.

Address 東京都渋谷区神泉町8-13 is described as ~4 minutes on foot from Shinsen Station; Shinsen Station's public coordinates (35.657093, 139.693546) are roughly 680m from the Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595, 139.7005), so the restaurant falls within the 0.8km target radius of Shibuya Scramble.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are the only noodle option, and the shoyu (soy sauce) broths are the traditional Japanese kind, which is almost always wheat-brewed, so the base broth is not gluten-free. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy-safe process was mentioned in any source — not a safe choice for celiac diners.
hours_raw: Lunch Mon–Sat 11:30–15:00; Dinner Mon–Fri 18:00–22:00; closed Sundays
name_ja: うさぎ（神泉のらぁめん屋 うさぎ）
neighborhood: Shinsen, Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is built on a traditional shoyu (soy sauce) broth; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed from both wheat and soybeans, so the broth is not gluten-free.

seats_text: Small counter seating, about 11 seats
vegan_detail: The signature dishes are built on whole-chicken broth with ground pork toppings; no plant-based broth, noodle, or menu option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 串だおれ 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-11-10 白菊ビル1F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain operated by Kings Know Co., Ltd.)
bio.background: Kushidaore is a Tokyo-based kushikatsu (deep-fried breaded skewer) izakaya chain with multiple branches across the city (Shimbashi honten, Suidobashi, Ichigaya, Gotanda, Shimokitazawa, Kanda, and others), built around bringing Osaka-style kushikatsu culture to Tokyo at very low prices.
bio.specialty: Kushikatsu (deep-fried breaded skewers of meat, seafood and vegetables) starting from around 50-80 yen per skewer, served alongside Kansai-style oden and yakitori.
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated concept is "安くてウマイは当たり前" ("cheap and delicious is a given") - serving a wide variety of skewers and cheap drinks in a lively, no-frills izakaya setting; like traditional Osaka kushikatsu shops, the classic "no double-dipping the sauce" (ソースの二度づけ禁止) rule applies.

anecdotes.text: 串だおれ operates under the concept "安くてウマイは当たり前" (cheap and delicious is a given), centering the menu on kushikatsu and Kansai-style oden with kushikatsu priced as low as ~50-80 yen per skewer.
The chain has multiple Tokyo locations including a Shimbashi main store, Suidobashi, Ichigaya, Gotanda, Shimokitazawa, Hatchobori and Kanda branches.

caveats: Several primary listing pages (Tabelog, the official kushidaore.com site, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Retty, Hitosara) returned 403/blocked responses when fetched directly during this verification session, so details are pieced together from web-search snippets that summarize those same pages rather than from directly fetched page text. Reported closing times vary slightly across sources (23:00 vs. 24:00 on weekdays) - worth confirming late-night hours before a late visit. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere for this location; this is a standard fried-skewer izakaya with no indication of dietary accommodation.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed day, per multiple listings)
cuisine: 串揚げ・串カツ・関西風おでん・居酒屋 (Kushikatsu/deep-fried skewers, Kansai-style oden, izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: A boisterous, budget Japanese izakaya chain with menus and service oriented to a local/Japanese-speaking clientele; no evidence of English menus or foreign-visitor-specific accommodation was found.
gf_detail: This is a deep-fried skewer (kushikatsu) specialist: skewers are breaded in wheat flour and panko before frying, the signature dipping sauce is a Worcestershire/tonkatsu-style sauce that typically contains wheat, and the Kansai-style oden broth commonly relies on soy sauce and dashi that may contain wheat-based seasonings. No gluten-free menu, wheat-free batter option, or dedicated fryer was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 月〜金・祝前日 15:00〜24:00、土・日・祝日 12:00〜23:00（年中無休）※一部情報源では平日15:00〜23:00または15:00〜24:30とする表記もあり、詳細は要確認
name_ja: 串だおれ 水道橋店
neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (Suidobashi Station East Exit, ~1-3 min walk)
seats_text: Multi-floor seating (1F-3F) with private-room options; can accommodate group bookings/parties of up to roughly 150 guests according to listing sites.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan labeling, or plant-based accommodation was found for this location. The menu centers on meat, seafood, and fried skewers plus Kansai-style oden (broth commonly made with katsuo/bonito dashi and fish-based items like chikuwa/hanpen). Some vegetable skewers (e.g., eggplant, mushroom, lotus root) may be orderable, but the batter, shared fryer, and sauce ingredients are unconfirmed, so vegan diners would need to ask staff directly and should not assume a fully plant-based preparation.

lead: 肉汁水餃子 餃包 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-13 裕永ビル3F

bio.origin: Roppongi, Tokyo (original flagship location)
bio.background: Gyopao (餃包) began as a water-dumpling specialty izakaya at Roppongi Crossing, where it became the No.1-ranked restaurant in Tokyo on TripAdvisor in its category and won TripAdvisor's Travelers' Choice award for two consecutive years. In July 2020 the brand opened this Shinjuku branch about a two-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's southeast/central-east exits, bringing the Roppongi store's signature dumplings to Shinjuku for the first time.
bio.specialty: Nikujiru Suigyoza (肉汁水餃子) — plump boiled dumplings packed with black-pork filling that burst with hot broth when bitten into, similar in effect to xiaolongbao but served izakaya-style. Notably, the menu has no yaki-gyoza (pan-fried dumplings) at all; the boiled, broth-filled dumpling is the sole signature.
bio.philosophy: Described by the restaurant itself as a fusion of Japanese izakaya drinking culture and Taiwanese Chinese cooking, built entirely around perfecting one dish rather than offering a broad menu.

anecdotes.text: The original Roppongi restaurant is ranked No.1 in Tokyo on TripAdvisor for water-dumpling specialty restaurants and also has the most Google reviews in Tokyo in its category.
The Shinjuku branch opened in July 2020, a one-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, opening up Roppongi's previously exclusive menu to Shinjuku for the first time.
A gluten-free chicken chimaki (glutinous-rice dumpling wrapped in bamboo leaf) has been promoted on the blog as a recommendation popular with female customers specifically because it is gluten-free.

caveats: Opening-hours info is inconsistent across secondary listing sites. Rakuten Gurunavi listed all-week 12:00-23:45 with no closed days, while another source citing the official site gave weekday 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30) and weekend/holiday 15:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Direct WebFetch access to the official site (gyopao-shinjuku.com) and Tabelog was blocked (403) in this environment, so hours/menu/dietary details below rely on search-engine snippets of those pages rather than a direct page read; visitors should reconfirm current hours before visiting. Seating capacity for this specific branch could not be confirmed (a separate sister branch, Shinjuku Higashiguchi-Ekimae, is reported to have 40 seats, but that is a different location). Gluten-free detail: the core menu is a wheat-wrapper dumpling restaurant (boiled gyoza skins are wheat-based) and standard soy sauce typically contains wheat unless stated otherwise — no gluten-free soy sauce or dedicated allergen protocol was confirmed; only a specific chicken chimaki item was promoted as gluten-free, and even that likely carries cross-contact risk from the wheat-dumpling-heavy kitchen.
closed_days: No regular closed day reported (定休日: なし per one listing); open daily.
cuisine: Chinese/Taiwanese-influenced izakaya specializing in juicy boiled water dumplings (水餃子)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant sits in a heavily touristed pocket of central Shinjuku two minutes from Shinjuku Station and has an English-language Tabelog reservation page, but the official website and in-store materials appear to be Japanese-only, so basic English support rather than a fully English-run experience should be expected.
gf_detail: The core menu is a wheat-wrapper dumpling restaurant (boiled gyoza skins are wheat-based), and standard Japanese soy sauce used for dipping typically contains wheat unless otherwise stated — no gluten-free soy sauce or dedicated allergen protocol was confirmed. However, the restaurant has specifically promoted a gluten-free chicken chimaki (glutinous rice wrapped in bamboo leaf) as a menu item for gluten-sensitive guests. Because the kitchen is centered on wheat-dumpling production, cross-contact risk for a celiac-level allergy is likely high even when ordering the chimaki; no dedicated fryer or separate prep area was confirmed in available sources.
hours_raw: Two conflicting sets reported: (a) Rakuten Gurunavi listing: daily 12:00-23:45, no closed days; (b) source citing official site: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30), Sat/Sun/Holidays 15:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Using set (b) as likely more current/authoritative (official-site sourced).
geocode_note: Address (Shinjuku 3-35-13) is reported to be a 2-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's southeast/chuo-higashi exits and a 2-minute walk from Marunouchi Line Shinjuku-Sanchome Station Exit A5, placing it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
name_en: Nikujiru Suigyoza Gyopao Shinjuku-ten (Juicy Water Dumplings "Gyopao" Shinjuku Branch)
name_ja: 肉汁水餃子 餃包 新宿店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome), two minutes from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or allergen protocol is confirmed; the kitchen's primary output is wheat-wrapper boiled dumplings, so cross-contact risk for a strict celiac diet is likely significant even when ordering the gluten-free chimaki item.

positives.text: The restaurant has proactively promoted a gluten-free chicken chimaki on its own blog as a recommendation for gluten-sensitive guests, indicating at least some staff awareness of gluten-free demand.

seats_text: Not confirmed for this specific branch.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan-adapted dishes, or plant-based broth options were found in available sources. The signature dish and most of the menu center on black-pork-filled dumplings and meat-based broth. Treat as not vegan-friendly absent further confirmation.

lead: 本格タイ料理バル プアン 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-5-9 ザ・レンガビル 2F

roles: Thai chef-run Thai bar/izakaya, third branch of a small Thai restaurant group
bio.origin: Thailand (dishes prepared by veteran Thai chefs)
bio.background: Puan Shibuya is the third branch of a small Thai restaurant group (Spice Kitchen Co., Ltd.) that began in Sangenjaya, where the original shop was built inside a renovated 60-year-old traditional Japanese house. The Shibuya branch opened a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's Inokashira Line ticket gate, following an earlier second branch near Gakugei-Daigaku Station.
bio.specialty: Hand-rolled fresh spring rolls, tom yum kung noodle soup, and a shrimp-and-avocado green curry are cited as signature dishes.
bio.philosophy: The name "Puan" (プアン) means "friend" or "companion" in Thai, reflecting the restaurant's stated aim of pairing warm, friendly hospitality with authentic Thai cooking and Thai drinks (Thai beer, wine, and whisky highballs).

anecdotes.text: "プアン"とはタイ語で友人・仲間を意味し、本場のベテランタイ人シェフの作るタイ料理と、タイビール、タイワイン、タイウィスキーを使ったハイボールなど様々なタイのお酒が用意されている。3号店となる渋谷店は渋谷駅井の頭線改札徒歩1分にオープン。
三軒茶屋本店は、茶沢通り沿いにあり、築60年の古民家をリノベーションして設計したおしゃれな店内と凄腕タイ人シェフが作る絶品タイ料理が魅力。

caveats: Primary sources (Tabelog, official site, HotPepper, Gnavi, etc.) returned HTTP 403 through the sandboxed fetch tool (proxy/egress policy blocks these hosts), so all facts here are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that quote or summarize those pages rather than from directly fetched page content. No dedicated vegan or gluten-free menu, allergy protocol, or cross-contamination information was found for this specific location in any source consulted — treat gf_confidence/vegan_status as conservative "ask" ratings, not confirmations. Seat count and exact payment methods were not found in available sources.
closed_days: 不定休（年末年始）／ Irregular closures, plus New Year holidays
cuisine: タイ料理（バル・居酒屋スタイル）
cultural_comfort_note: Reservation/menu pages are Japanese-first (HotPepper, Gnavi, official site) though Tabelog and Uber Eats list an English store name, suggesting basic but not full English accessibility.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source consulted. As a standard Thai izakaya, dishes likely rely on soy sauce and other wheat-containing condiments in curries, noodles (e.g., pad thai sauce), and stir-fries. Not verified safe for celiac diners — ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.

Address (東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-5-9, Dogenzaka) and multiple sources describing it as "a 1-minute walk from Shibuya Station's Inokashira Line ticket gate" place it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
