As a general Thai bar/izakaya, some vegetable-forward dishes exist (e.g., stir-fried morning glory, vegetable dishes), but standard Thai seasonings used throughout the menu (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste) are not confirmed to be omitted or substituted on request. Vegan diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering; do not assume any dish is vegan by default.

lead: 欧風カレー ボンディ 神保町本店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-3 神田古書センタービル2F

bio.chef_name: Koichi Murata (founder, 1973); now run by second-generation owner Shinsuke Murata
bio.origin: Founded in 1973 (Showa 48) in Kanda-Jimbocho by Koichi Murata, who had previously run an earlier curry shop called Indira in Takashimadaira, Tokyo.
bio.background: In his twenties Murata traveled to France, ostensibly to study art, and ended up spending several years there. He worked in a friend's restaurant, where he became captivated by the depth and technique of classic French brown sauce (sauce espagnole/demi-glace). Bringing that sauce-making tradition back to Japan and folding it into curry produced what he named '欧風カレー' (European-style curry).
bio.philosophy: Murata summed up his curry as 'the aroma of India, the taste of Europe' — rather than a straightforward Indian-style curry, the sauce is built like a French brown sauce: butter-and-flour roux simmered for hours with beef or pork stock, wine, and fruit and vegetables cooked down slowly in butter, giving a rounder, less fiery, dairy-rich flavor with sweetness balancing the spice.
bio.specialty: European-style beef or pork curry, served with a whole boiled potato and a pat of butter presented separately on its own plate before the curry arrives.

anecdotes.text: Murata spent several formative years in France in his twenties; what began as an art-study trip turned into an apprenticeship of sorts in a friend's restaurant kitchen, where he fell for the depth of French brown sauce and later built Bondy's curry around it.

Bondy is credited as the Jimbocho curry shop that first served a whole boiled potato alongside the curry. In an interview, second-generation owner Shinsuke Murata explained his father served the potato first, echoing the 'first course' convention of the French meals he had trained on.

Before founding Bondy, Murata had run an earlier, lesser-known curry shop called 'Indira' in Takashimadaira, Tokyo — part of the winding path from Itabashi to Jimbocho that curry historians trace for the shop.

caveats: Very famous, so expect lines at peak lunch/dinner hours; it is walk-in only (no reservations) and cash only. Hours around the New Year holiday period vary by year — call ahead if visiting late December/early January.
closed_days: New Year holidays (nenmatsu-nenshi); otherwise open daily
cuisine: 欧風カレー (European-style/French-influenced Japanese curry)
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only, no-reservation, old-school local institution — ordering is straightforward from a picture-friendly menu, but there is no particular English-language accommodation.
geocode_note: Address (Kanda-Jinbocho 2-3, Chiyoda-ku) is a 1-minute walk from Jimbocho Station and roughly 0.7km from Suidobashi Station, placing it within the Suidobashi Station target radius. No geocoding performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: The signature 欧風カレー is built on a classic French-style brown sauce: a roux made from butter and wheat flour, simmered for hours with beef/pork stock, wine, and vegetables/fruit. Every curry on the menu shares this wheat-flour-thickened base, and no gluten-free preparation or substitution is offered. Not suitable for celiac disease or strict gluten-free diets.
name_ja: 欧風カレー ボンディ 神保町本店
neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda City (near Jimbocho Station / Suidobashi Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: All curries share one wheat-flour-and-butter brown-sauce base (roux simmered with meat stock, wine, fruit and vegetables) — there is no separate gluten-free sauce or preparation area.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry's richness comes from generous butter and dairy plus beef/pork stock cooked into the single shared sauce base; no vegan menu or dairy/meat-free substitution was found.

seats_text: 41 seats (counter and table seating on the 2nd floor)
vegan_detail: The sauce base is rich with butter and dairy and is built on beef or pork stock; there is no vegan menu, no plant-based stock option, and no indication the kitchen offers a dairy/meat-free version of the curry. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians avoiding animal stock.

lead: 舎鈴 新宿センタービル店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-25-1 新宿センタービルB1F（〒163-0690）

bio.origin: Sister brand of the famous Rokurinsha (六厘舎) tsukemen shop, launched by Matsufuji Foods Co.
bio.background: Sharin was created by Matsufuji Foods, the company behind the celebrated Rokurinsha tsukemen shop, with the idea of building a tsukemen you could realistically eat every single day rather than just as an occasional indulgence. This branch operates inside the basement food court of the Shinjuku Center Building skyscraper.
bio.specialty: 創業つけめん (Sōgyō Tsukemen / "Founding Tsukemen") — a revival of a recipe Rokurinsha served for only one month back at its 2005 opening and later brought back as a legendary 'phantom' dish; it pairs a rich double soup of chicken-and-pork stock with seafood dashi, sharpened with a soy-based tare and a house spice blend.
bio.philosophy: Where Rokurinsha's tsukemen leans thick and heavy, Sharin deliberately dials the richness back so the dipping sauce stays craveable without being fatiguing — the stated goal is noodles "you could eat every day and never get tired of."

anecdotes.text: The founding tsukemen recipe was served for just one month in 2005 at Rokurinsha's original Osaki shop and gained cult status as a 'phantom' menu item before Sharin brought it back; it has since sold over 200,000 bowls chain-wide.

caveats: This is a standard tsukemen (dipping noodle) chain shop with wheat noodles and a fish/meat-based double soup — it is not a gluten-free or vegan destination. Included for completeness of the ramen/tsukemen layer; celiac and vegan diners should treat it as unsuitable rather than adaptable. Hours and the 'no reservations' / cash-only details come from third-party listings and the brand's own store page, not a first-party confirmation call.
closed_days: No regular weekly closed day; closed only around New Year (年末年始), per the shop's official listing. Hours have also been adjusted with notice in the past, so confirm before visiting.
cuisine: つけ麺 (Tsukemen — dipping ramen noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: Self-service ticket-machine ordering (buy a ticket, sit, get called by number) is picture-driven and manageable without Japanese, but no English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are used, and the chain's own allergen sheet lists wheat (小麦) among ingredients used across its menu. Sharin's allergen disclosure explicitly states it cannot guarantee against secondary cross-contamination and that cooking equipment/tableware are not kept separate by dish. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe process is indicated anywhere.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled); address is West Shinjuku 1-25-1, basement of Shinjuku Center Building, a short walk west of JR/Odakyu/Keio Shinjuku Station and near Tochomae Station — well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
name_ja: 舎鈴 新宿センタービル店
neighborhood: 都庁前 / 西新宿 (Tochomae / Nishi-Shinjuku)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sharin's chain-wide allergen notice states that individual sensitivity to allergens varies, that it cannot guarantee a customer won't react, that ingredients can pick up trace allergens during sourcing or in-kitchen prep, that secondary cross-contamination cannot be fully prevented, and that cooking tools and tableware are not dedicated per dish.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A chain-wide printable allergen list (covering items such as shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat, egg, dairy, peanut and more) is published by the brand for customers to check ingredients themselves; there is no branch-specific note about staff proactively managing allergy requests.

seats_text: 41 seats, counter and table seating
vegan_detail: The tsukemen dipping sauce and ramen soup are built from a fish-and-meat double stock (seafood dashi layered with pork/chicken bone broth), and the tare is soy-sauce based. There is no vegetarian or vegan broth/noodle option indicated anywhere in the brand's materials — this is a straightforward meat-and-fish-based tsukemen specialist.

lead: haishop cafe 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア 7F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (brand also has a Yokohama location)
bio.background: haishop cafe is run by Innovation Design Inc., founded by Tarō Ishizeki. The company opened its first omiyage (souvenir) shop and restaurants in 2019, then pivoted fully toward sustainability in early 2020 after the founder saw how normalized sustainable living had become while visiting New York. Partnerships with local farmers working to reduce food loss, and a confectionery workshop that employs people with disabilities, shaped the brand's approach. The Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened on the 7th floor in September 2022, directly connected to Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Soy-meat vegan burger and a vegan tartare bagel sandwich, alongside all-vegan soft-serve ice cream and seasonal fresh smoothies.
bio.philosophy: The concept is "solving social issues through food": ingredients are chosen with attention to environment, sourcing, and social impact, and the cafe welcomes vegan and vegetarian diners with a menu that is roughly 80% vegan. Customers who bring their own tumbler or container get 100 yen off drinks.

anecdotes.text: haishop cafe's operator, Innovation Design, was reported as the first in Japan to receive a three-star rating from the Sustainable Restaurant Association (Japan) for its sustainability practices, around the time the Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened with its 80%-vegan menu.

The brand's shift to a sustainability-first business came after the founder's trip to New York, where sustainable living felt normalized; back in Japan at the start of 2020 the company decided to reorient the business around sustainability.

caveats: Direct WebFetch to Tabelog and the official Shibuya Scramble Square / haishopjapan.com pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment (network policy), so this record is built from WebSearch-returned excerpts of those and other pages (PR Times press release, Shibukei, table-source.jp, FruOats, mymizu) rather than raw page fetches. GF-specific safety details (dedicated fryer, cross-contamination protocol, soy sauce wheat content) were not found in any source and are left blank rather than guessed — this is a vegan-forward sustainable cafe, not a GF-dedicated kitchen, so celiac diners should ask staff directly. lat/lng intentionally null per instructions (geocoding disabled); address_ja confirms the spot is inside Shibuya Scramble Square, directly connected to Shibuya Station, well within the Shibuya Scramble target radius.
closed_days: Open daily; reported as open year-round aside from possibly January 1st (follows building/facility holiday schedule) — not independently confirmed on an official page.
cuisine: Sustainable cafe / vegan (approx. 80% vegan menu: smoothies, soy-meat burgers, vegan bagel sandwiches, all-vegan soft-serve, fair-trade coffee)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside Shibuya Scramble Square, a major shopping complex directly connected to Shibuya Station and heavily used by international visitors, so some English menu signage and tourist-accustomed staff are likely, though this wasn't independently confirmed.
gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The cafe has appeared in local blog roundups of gluten-free-friendly cafes in Shibuya (e.g., FruOats Magazine), suggesting some menu items may be wheat-free (smoothies, some deli items), but no official gluten-free menu, ingredient list, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination policy was found. Soy sauce/dashi wheat content and shared-equipment risk are unconfirmed — celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.
hours_raw: Mon–Sun (including holidays): 10:00–21:00
name_ja: haishop cafe 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷スクランブルスクエア7F, directly connected to Shibuya Station)

positives.text: Roughly 80% of the food and drink menu is vegan, including a soy-meat vegan burger, a vegan tartare bagel sandwich, and all-vegan soft-serve ice cream.
Ingredients are selected with attention to environment, sourcing, and social impact as part of the cafe's core concept.

seats_text: Small counter/cafe seating, roughly 10 seats, no private rooms
vegan_detail: The cafe explicitly markets itself as a sustainable/ethical cafe with roughly 80% of its menu vegan or vegetarian: soy-meat vegan burger, vegan tartare bagel sandwich, all-vegan soft-serve ice cream, seasonal fresh smoothies, and fair-trade coffee. It is not a 100%-vegan kitchen (the remaining ~20% of items are presumably non-vegan), so vegans should still confirm individual items; no specific mention of hidden dashi/katsuo(bonito) in vegan-labeled dishes was found, which is reassuring but not independently verified per dish.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-12-1 富田ビルB1

bio.origin: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: Opened in 1976 (Showa 51) in the basement of the Tomita Building near Jimbocho Station, in the heart of Tokyo's famous secondhand-bookstore district. The founding couple's daughter, Miwa Tokuko, is now the second-generation owner, carrying on the taste and atmosphere passed down from her parents for nearly half a century.
bio.specialty: Gratin toast (グラタントースト), the signature dish since the shop's founding; also known for a mackerel-and-vegetable curry toast sandwich and, more recently, an ogura (red bean) butter toast served with coffee.
bio.philosophy: A family-run, old-fashioned kissaten (Showa-retro coffee shop) philosophy centered on preserving the original recipes and unhurried atmosphere of the founders rather than chasing trends.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Trois Bagues, is French for "three rings" (trois = three, bague = ring) — a direct translation of the owner family's surname Miwa (三輪, literally "three wheels/rings"), and is also meant to evoke the connection between customer, staff, and coffee cup.
Second-generation owner Miwa Tokuko continues to run the cafe, having inherited it from her parents who opened it in 1976.
The cafe was selected for Tabelog's 'Hyakumeiten' (top 100) kissaten list in 2022, and continues to draw devotees of its nel-drip 'Trois Blend' coffee and gratin toast.

caveats: This is a traditional Showa-retro kissaten built around wheat-bread toast dishes (gratin toast, curry toast, ogura butter toast); no gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source. Seat count varies slightly between sources (roughly 30-34 total). Reservation policy and precise per-item allergen handling were not documented online — celiac or vegan diners should not assume safety and would need to ask staff directly.
closed_days: Sundays (holidays follow Saturday's shorter hours)
cuisine: 喫茶・カレートースト・昭和レトロ (kissaten / curry toast / Showa-retro cafe)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic old-school Japanese kissaten with a Japanese-only menu and no particular English support, though its long-running fame in the bookstore district means walk-in visitors are common.
gf_detail: All signature dishes are built on wheat bread — gratin toast (bread with a flour-based white sauce and cheese), a mackerel-and-vegetable curry toast, and ogura butter toast. No gluten-free bread, alternative flour, or dedicated GF preparation was mentioned in any source, so this is not a safe option for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address places it roughly 1-minute walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A5, approximately 0.7km from Suidōbashi Station, within the required 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 10:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); Sat & holidays 12:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); closed Sundays
neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku (1 min walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A5)
seats_text: Approximately 30-34 seats (counter seating plus table seating), reports vary slightly by source
vegan_detail: The menu centers on dairy- and egg-rich toast dishes (butter, cheese and flour-based white sauce in the gratin toast) and includes fish (mackerel in the curry toast); no vegan-labeled dishes or substitutions were found in any source. Coffee itself is vegan, but the food menu is not.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-14-1 新宿伊勢丹本館7階レストラン街「イートパラダイス」

roles: 店長 大之木さん (Store Manager Onoki-san)

anecdotes.text: Store manager Onoki-san: 'Through Italian food that anyone can feel at home with, we hope this can become a place where all kinds of people can gather around the same table' — explaining why the restaurant pairs its modern Italian menu with an extensive vegan menu.
The name TORCIA is Italian for 'torch,' chosen because the owners wanted to create the warm, welcoming feeling of people gathered around a hearth or torchlight to share a meal.

The restaurant is operated by CHAYA Macrobiotics, which renewed its long-running 'CHAYA Macrobi Isetan Shinjuku' concept into Green Italian TORCIA, keeping the macrobiotic, egg/dairy/meat/refined-white-sugar-free approach for its vegan dishes while adding a full modern Italian menu alongside it.

bio.background: Opened inside Isetan Shinjuku's main building as a rebrand/renewal of the long-running CHAYA Macrobiotics restaurant at that location, Green Italian TORCIA pairs a conventional modern Italian menu with a parallel, clearly-labeled vegan menu built on macrobiotic principles (no meat, egg, dairy, or refined white sugar in the vegan dishes).
bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan — CHAYA Macrobiotics brand
bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic-inspired 'natural Italian' cooking meant to let vegans, vegetarians and omnivores sit at the same table together, each ordering what suits them, in a warm 'gathered around the torchlight' atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Soy-meat bolognese with soy-milk vegan cheese and the Zero Meat plant-based burger are the most-mentioned vegan mains; vegan Mont Blanc and strawberry cake are the standout desserts.

caveats: No specific cross-contamination protocol, dedicated fryer, or soy-sauce-wheat statement was found in available sources; gluten-free and vegan claims are based on menu labeling (Vegewel lists it with a gluten-free menu; CHAYA Macrobiotics states the vegan menu is meat/egg/dairy/refined-sugar-free) rather than a confirmed celiac-safe kitchen protocol. Celiac-level diners should confirm cross-contact handling with staff before ordering.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closure; follows Isetan Shinjuku's occasional store-holiday schedule (伊勢丹休館日に準ずる).
cuisine: Natural/modern Italian with an extensive parallel vegan menu (vegan pasta, vegan pizza, plant-based mains and desserts)
cultural_comfort_note: Located on Isetan Shinjuku's dedicated restaurant floor with English-language coverage from multiple vegan travel blogs (itravelforveganfood, HappyCow), online reservation systems, and vegan items clearly marked in Japanese and English on the menu.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null). Address places it on the 7th floor of Isetan Shinjuku's main building, directly above/adjacent to Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line Shinjuku-sanchome Station (1-minute walk) and roughly 600m from JR Shinjuku Station — within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: Vegewel's restaurant guide (which screens specifically for vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free-friendly restaurants) lists this location as having a gluten-free menu option. No source found confirming a dedicated gluten-free fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, or a formal cross-contamination protocol — treat as 'ask before ordering' for strict celiac safety rather than a fully dedicated GF kitchen.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (Lunch 11:00-15:00 L.O.; Tea time 15:00-17:00; Dinner 17:00-22:00, L.O. 21:00). Follows Isetan Shinjuku Main Building's store hours/holidays.
name_ja: グリーン イタリアン トルチャ
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku

positives.text: Listed on Vegewel's restaurant guide as offering a gluten-free menu option, and reviewers note being glad a gluten-free menu is available.
All vegan dishes are made without meat, egg, dairy, or refined white sugar, and are clearly marked 'Vegan' on the menu, so vegan diners can identify safe dishes easily.
The restaurant is designed so vegan and non-vegan diners can eat together, each ordering from the appropriate part of the menu, which reviewers highlight as useful for mixed dietary groups.

seats_text: About 58 seats (50 table seats plus an 8-seat counter), including sofa seating and a terrace area.
vegan_detail: Runs a full, clearly labeled vegan menu alongside its standard Italian menu — soy-meat bolognese, a plant-based 'Zero Meat' burger, vegan pizza, vegan pasta, and vegan desserts (Mont Blanc, strawberry cake) — all free of meat, egg, dairy, and refined white sugar. A 3-course all-vegan lunch set has been reported by reviewers. No specific statement found about hidden bonito/katsuo dashi in the non-vegan menu items, so vegan diners should stick to items explicitly marked 'Vegan.'

lead: ホームズパスタ 渋谷本店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町13-16 国際ビルA館3F（〒150-0042）

bio.origin: 渋谷区宇田川町
bio.background: 1986年に渋谷・宇田川町で創業した、日本におけるスープスパゲッティのパイオニア店。創業シェフ本田氏の「本当においしいスープスパゲッティを食べてもらうためのこだわり」を守り続けてきた。2026年2月、老朽化に伴い一時休業（長年腕を振るった料理長も引退）、同年4月28日にリニューアルオープンした。現在は東京・大阪・名古屋・岡山など全国に展開するスープスパゲッティ専門チェーンの本店にあたる。
bio.specialty: 絶望のスパゲッティ（Zetsubo no Spaghetti / "Despair Spaghetti"）— じっくり煮込んだ自家製ミートソースに生クリームとオリーブを合わせ、ニンニクと唐辛子を効かせた濃厚ミートクリームスープパスタに、炒めたキノコとベーコンをトッピング
bio.philosophy: 本当に美味しいスープスパゲッティを届けることへのこだわりを創業以来守り続けている

anecdotes.text: 看板メニュー「絶望のスパゲッティ」は1994年、新宿新店舗開業時の新メニュー開発から誕生。導入当初は全く売れず苦戦したが、メディア関係者が食べて絶賛したことから注文が増え、行列ができるほどの一番人気メニューに成長した。
2026年2月2日、老朽化のため渋谷本店は一時休業。長年キッチンを担ってきた料理長も引退し、同年4月28日にリニューアルオープンした。

caveats: This is a wheat-pasta specialty restaurant (the signature dish is a meat/cream-based soup spaghetti); no gluten-free pasta alternative or vegan menu was found in any source, so it is not a suitable pick for celiac or vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff. cash_only, reservation_required, and under30 pricing were not directly confirmed in sourced material and are best-guess based on the restaurant's known casual chain format.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found in sources; posted hours cover all seven days (open daily per official listing).
cuisine: パスタ・スープパスタ専門店（Soup Spaghetti Specialty）
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known, high-traffic local chain flagship near Shibuya Station with an all-Japanese menu and no confirmed English support — approachable with basic Japanese or pointing at the menu, but not set up for allergy conversations in English.
gf_detail: No gluten-free pasta or menu accommodation is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's official site, press coverage, or blog reviews. The entire menu is built around wheat-flour spaghetti (fresh pasta), so celiac or gluten-avoidant diners should not plan to eat here without calling ahead, and even then options are unlikely.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed). Address is 宇田川町13-16, immediately adjacent to Shibuya Station and well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius based on the confirmed street address.
name_ja: ホームズパスタ 渋谷本店
neighborhood: 宇田川町 / 渋谷駅徒歩5分
seats_text: 21 seats, non-smoking
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options are documented. The restaurant's signature and flagship dishes (e.g., "絶望のスパゲッティ") are meat-sauce and cream-based, and general menu descriptions found in sources reference meat sauce, bacon, and cream throughout — no plant-based soup base or dashi/katsuo-free option was identified.

lead: こってりらーめん せきやけ 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-11-11 タカギビル1F

roles: Suidōbashi branch of こってりらーめん せきやけ, an independent spin-off of the well-known Chiba back-fat ramen shop こってりらーめん なりたけ (Naritake)
bio.background: Sekiyake began as an independent offshoot of Naritake, the popular Chiba back-fat ramen chain, opening its original shop in Shin-Koiwa (新小岩) before this Suidōbashi branch was added a short walk from the station's east exit.
bio.origin: Shin-Koiwa, Tokyo (original Sekiyake location); this Suidōbashi shop is the later branch
bio.specialty: Kotteri (rich) miso or shoyu ramen loaded with pork back fat (背脂) over medium-thick wavy noodles

anecdotes.text: Diners can freely adjust the amount of pork back fat and the spice level in the miso ramen, and noodle portions up to 'medium' plus rice refills are free — a generous, customizable style associated with the Naritake ramen lineage.

caveats: Traditional pork-based ramen counter shop; no vegan or gluten-free accommodations were found in any source reviewed, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. Reported hours are inconsistent across sources and appear to vary around Tokyo Dome event schedules — confirm current hours via the shop's official X/Twitter before visiting. Cash only, ticket-vending-machine ordering, counter seating only.
closed_days: No single fixed closed day is consistently reported; weekend/holiday hours are shortened and overall hours appear to vary with nearby Tokyo Dome events — check official social media before visiting.
cuisine: Rich (kotteri) tonkotsu/miso/shoyu ramen with pork back fat, Naritake-style
cultural_comfort_note: Order and pay in cash at a ticket vending machine near the entrance; little to no English support, so knowing basic ramen ordering terms (broth type, noodle firmness, fat amount) helps.
geocode_note: Multiple independent sources place the shop about a 2-3 minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station's East Exit, well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station radius. Do not geocode here; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: Uses standard wheat ramen noodles; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source reviewed. Both the shoyu and miso tare are conventional soy-sauce-based preparations typical of Naritake-style ramen, so wheat exposure should be assumed throughout the dish. Not appropriate for celiac diners.
hours_raw: Sources conflict somewhat: weekday hours reported as 11:00-20:00 (with some earlier listings showing 11:00-23:00), weekend/holiday hours reported as 11:00-15:00; one source also reports the shop closed Saturdays and Sundays entirely. A more recent report notes hours may extend into the evening depending on Tokyo Dome events, announced via the shop's official X (Twitter) account. Confirm current hours directly before visiting.
name_ja: こってりらーめん せきやけ 水道橋店
neighborhood: 水道橋駅・千代田区神田三崎町
seats_text: 10-seat L-shaped counter with partitions between seats; counter seating only
vegan_detail: Broths are pork-bone (tonkotsu-style) and back-fat based, and the shoyu/miso tare are conventional soy-based preparations; no vegan options, plant-based broth, or substitutions were found in any menu or review source. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 火鍋 小肥羊 新宿西口店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-15-3 オムニクスビル3F

roles: Chinese hot pot specialty chain restaurant
bio.background: Xiaofeiyang (小肥羊) is a well-known Chinese hot pot chain built around a 'medicinal herbal hot pot' concept; the Shinjuku West Exit branch is one of several Tokyo-area locations operating under this brand, about three minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit.
bio.origin: Chain originating in China, operated in Japan under the Xiaofeiyang/Hinabe brand
bio.specialty: Herbal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) with a choice of two broths — a mild white broth (白湯スープ) or a spicy mala broth (麻辣スープ) — each simmered with dozens of Chinese medicinal ingredients, served with a build-your-own selection of dipping sauces (scallion sauce, sesame-adjacent condiments, fermented tofu, ponzu, chili oil, etc.).
bio.philosophy: Rooted in the idea of 医食同源 (food and medicine share the same origin): the broths are steeped with herbs and aromatics such as garlic, goji berry, ginseng, angelica root (当帰) and Sichuan pepper, with a dedicated in-house 'soup master' (鍋師) overseeing the recipe.

anecdotes.text: 唐辛子や花椒、当帰や良姜など、数十種類の漢方食材から抽出した秘伝のエキスがたっぷりと含まれている。
The white soup base is made with chicken extract, beef extract powder, and bonito extract in addition to the herbal blend, crafted under a dedicated 'pot master' recipe used chain-wide.

caveats: This is a mainstream Chinese hot pot chain restaurant, not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free venue. Both signature broths (白湯 and 麻辣) are made with chicken extract, beef extract, and bonito (fish) extract, so despite the health-oriented herbal branding, the hot pot broth itself is not vegan or vegetarian. No gluten-free menu, process, or celiac-specific accommodation was found; standard dipping sauces (soy-based ponzu, fermented tofu, oyster sauce, miso-style sauce) commonly contain wheat. Diners with celiac disease or who eat vegan should not rely on this spot without asking staff in detail beforehand. Several key pages (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Foodre, PayPay Gourmet, iko-yo) returned 403 errors when fetched directly, so details were corroborated via search-indexed content from those and other pages (official xfy.co.jp/hinabe.net brand sites, direct.hinabe.net product pages) rather than direct fetch of every listing.
closed_days: No regular closed day (open year-round except around the New Year holidays)
cuisine: Chinese hot pot (火鍋) with medicinal herbal broth
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language Tabelog listing and broad payment support (major credit cards, PayPay, Alipay) suggest some accommodation for overseas visitors, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or process was identified. The build-your-own dipping sauces (soy-based ponzu, fermented tofu, miso-style sauce, oyster sauce) typically contain wheat, and the shared hot pot is used table-side alongside other diners' orders which may include wheat-based items (dumplings, noodles), so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should ask staff carefully about sauce ingredients and request a broth eaten without the standard condiments.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都新宿区西新宿1-15-3 オムニクスビル3F, about 3 minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit — well within the Shinjuku Station target radius (0.8km). Lat/lng left null per instructions; the pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
name_ja: 火鍋 小肥羊 新宿西口店
neighborhood: Shinjuku West Exit (新宿西口)

vegan_cross_contact.text: The white and spicy mala hot pot broths are made with chicken extract, beef extract, and bonito (fish) extract as their base, so the broth itself is not vegan or vegetarian even when only vegetables and tofu are added to it.

seats_text: Seats roughly 132 guests across table seating, box seating, and two private rooms (capacity 8-13 people each).
vegan_detail: Despite the health-oriented herbal branding, both the white (白湯) and spicy mala (麻辣) broths are built on chicken extract, beef extract, and bonito (fish) extract, so the hot pot broth itself is not vegan or vegetarian. Vegetables and tofu can be added to the pot, but they cook in the same animal- and fish-derived broth as the meat and seafood, and common condiments (oyster sauce, fermented tofu with possible non-vegan components) are offered as defaults.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町24-8 新南平台マンション102号室

bio.background: Operated by Shibuya-based restaurant group SSCY, with sister Thai Food Laboratory locations in Shimokitazawa and Fuchu; dishes are made by cooks trained in Thailand, including hotel kitchens.
bio.specialty: Course menus that range from beginner-friendly Thai classics to lesser-known Isaan (northeastern Thai) regional dishes
bio.philosophy: Stated focus on ingredients, cooking technique, atmosphere, and hospitality, aiming to introduce diners to both familiar and regional Thai cooking styles.
roles: Cooks trained in Thailand, including hotel kitchens

anecdotes.text: Offers three course menus, from dishes suited to Thai-food beginners to advanced regional dishes from northeastern Thailand (Isaan), prepared by cooks who trained in Thailand.
Positioned by its parent company as 'Thai Food Laboratory,' a Thai cuisine specialist with locations in Shibuya and Shimokitazawa.

caveats: No dedicated vegan or gluten-free menu, certification, or allergy protocol could be confirmed from available sources; this is a standard Thai restaurant with naturally vegetable-forward dishes rather than a verified GF/vegan specialist. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should call ahead (03-6455-3229) to confirm ingredients (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste, soy sauce, sauces that may contain wheat).
closed_days: None — open every day (定休日なし)
cuisine: Thai cuisine (rice/noodle dishes, curries, Isaan regional specialties)
cultural_comfort_note: Located a few minutes from Shibuya Station with an English-language company webpage, but an English menu or English-speaking staff at this specific branch was not confirmed — reasonably tourist-adjacent but not guaranteed easy for non-Japanese speakers.
gf_detail: Many Thai dishes (steamed rice, rice noodles, most curries) are naturally wheat-free, but sauces and soy sauce used in the kitchen may contain wheat gluten, and no dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contact protocol was found in available sources. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm specific dishes and sauces with staff.
geocode_note: Address (渋谷区桜丘町24-8) is a 4–6 minute walk from Shibuya Station's south exit per multiple listings, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble; lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
name_en: Thai Food Laboratory (Thai Ryori Kenkyujo) — Shibuya
name_ja: タイ料理研究所 渋谷店
neighborhood: 桜丘町, Shibuya
seats_text: 23 seats
vegan_detail: Standard Thai menu includes vegetable and tofu-based dishes, but curries, stir-fries, and sauces commonly rely on fish sauce, oyster sauce, or shrimp paste (kapi) unless specifically requested without; no dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found. Ask staff to confirm ingredients per dish.

address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷2-3-15 元町館 地下1階(B1F)

bio.background: This kaiseki restaurant relocated to Hongo (near Suidobashi) from its original location in Hiroo, Shibuya-ku. As "Hiroo Ichie," the predecessor establishment held a Michelin star for eight consecutive years before the move. It now operates as a completely reservation-only restaurant that serves only one party per day.
bio.origin: Relocated from Hiroo, Shibuya-ku, where it was known as Hiroo Ichie.
bio.specialty: Omakase kaiseki built around house-made dashi, with seasonal set menus that have featured suppon (soft-shell turtle) dishes and bincho-charcoal grilled items.
bio.philosophy: The owner personally prepares every dish, with what multiple sources describe as an uncompromising focus on dashi (broth) as the foundation of the cooking.

anecdotes.text: A repeat diner wrote: "ここの出汁が、すごく好きでした。10年振りの再訪ですが、この点は、変わりません" ("I've always loved the dashi here — even after 10 years away, that hasn't changed").

caveats: Address/access info was corroborated via WebSearch snippets of Tabelog, the official site, Gnavi, and Hitosara; direct WebFetch of these pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so exact phrasing of hours/closed-days/payment methods could not be independently double-checked against the raw page HTML. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere for this restaurant — it appears to be a traditional dashi-focused kaiseki restaurant with no dietary-accommodation marketing.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found; restaurant operates by complete reservation only (one party per day), so effective availability depends on booking rather than a published weekly closure.
cuisine: Japanese kaiseki (dashi-focused omakase course, one-party-per-day)
cultural_comfort_note: A completely reservation-only, one-group-per-night restaurant reached by phone (03-3868-2889); no indication of English menus or English-speaking service, so non-Japanese-speaking guests should expect to arrange details via a Japanese speaker or hotel concierge.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is blocked in this environment; lat/lng left null. Multiple independent sources (official site, Gnavi, Yahoo Map listing, Retty) describe the address as 東京都文京区本郷2-3-15 元町館B1, roughly a 5-minute walk from Suidobashi Station (Toei Mita Line, exit A1) — well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. As a traditional dashi- and soy-sauce-based kaiseki restaurant, standard shoyu (which typically contains wheat) and tempura-style items are likely on the menu. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should call ahead (03-3868-2889) before booking to confirm ingredient handling, since this is a fully custom, one-party-per-day omakase where the kitchen could plausibly accommodate a request but has no documented gluten-free protocol.
hours_raw: 18:00-22:00, nightly, completely reservation-only (one party seated per day)
neighborhood: Hongo, Bunkyo-ku (near Suidobashi Station)
seats_text: One party (group) seated per night by complete reservation only; no fixed public seat count found.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented anywhere. The restaurant's identity is explicitly built around traditional dashi (multiple sources emphasize its dedication to broth), which in standard kaiseki preparation is katsuobushi (bonito)-based, making it very unlikely to be vegan-friendly without extensive advance customization. No evidence of a kombu-only or vegan dashi alternative was found.

lead: Salad Cafe 京王百貨店新宿店
name_ja: Salad Cafe 京王百貨店新宿店
name_en: Salad Cafe Keio Department Store Shinjuku
cuisine: Salad, Delicatessen, Vegetables
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku, Keio Department Store / Shinjuku Station West Exit
gf_detail: This is a weighed-salad deli counter (量り売り) in the basement food hall of Keio Department Store, not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. Dressings and prepared sides commonly use soy sauce or soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat, and salads are displayed and scooped in an open counter format where cross-contact between trays is likely. No dedicated gluten-free process, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free labeling was confirmed in available sources — celiac diners should ask staff to check ingredient labels per item before choosing.
vegan_detail: The counter offers 15-18 rotating types of vegetable-forward salads and deli sides sold by weight, plus sandwiches, bento, and a Shinjuku-exclusive appetizer platter, so some individual salads may be vegetable-only. However, the shop is not vegan-focused: many salads and sides commonly include egg, mayonnaise, ham, or other animal ingredients (the parent company, Kenko Mayonnaise, is a mayonnaise/dressing maker), and dashi or bonito-based seasonings are common in Japanese-style salads. Ingredient composition varies daily; vegan diners should check the posted ingredient/allergy labels for each item or ask staff.
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-1-4 京王百貨店新宿店 中地階（Keio Deli内）
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address places it inside Keio Department Store Shinjuku, directly at Shinjuku Station's West Exit — well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark.
seats_text: Takeout-only deli counter in a department-store basement food hall (depachika); no customer seating on site.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 10:00-20:30, Sun and public holidays 10:00-20:00
closed_days: Follows Keio Department Store Shinjuku's own holiday schedule (rare, irregular closures tied to the department store, not a fixed weekly closed day)
cultural_comfort_note: A self-service, weigh-and-pay department-store deli counter where you point to what you want, so it's easy to navigate with little Japanese, though ingredient details are mainly in Japanese.
caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official site, or several news pages due to 403 errors from the source sites; details below are drawn from search-result excerpts of those same pages (Tabelog, salad-cafe.com official shop page, Kenko Mayonnaise press release, Gourmet Press opening article) rather than full page fetches. No GF/vegan-specific statements were found from the brand; dietary ratings are inferred conservatively from the format (open salad-bar deli) and parent company (a mayonnaise/dressing maker), not from an explicit allergy policy for this location.

roles: Salad Cafe Inc. (subsidiary of Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd.)
bio.background: Salad Cafe is a weighed-salad ('ryōrigai') deli shop brand operated by Salad Cafe Inc., a group company of the long-established Japanese seasoning and mayonnaise maker Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd. The brand runs salad and deli counters inside department stores and shopping malls across the Kanto and Kansai regions. This Shinjuku location opened on November 22, 2022 in the basement food hall of Keio Department Store's Shinjuku store, near Morozoff, opening the same day as a second new shop, Shinjuku Takano's Keio Shinjuku Salad shop, in the same building.
bio.origin: Kenko Mayonnaise Co., Ltd. group (Japan)
bio.specialty: Weighed, made-to-order salads sold by weight (15-18 rotating varieties including deli-style sides), with a Shinjuku-store-exclusive appetizer platter and a popular Japanese-style onion-slaw potato salad (和風オニスラポテトサラダ)
bio.philosophy: The brand's stated concept is 'サラダのある毎日で、お客様の笑顔と健康をサポートします' — supporting customers' smiles and health through everyday salads — with an emphasis on vegetable-forward, colorful, health-oriented prepared foods.

anecdotes.text: Salad Cafe 京王百貨店新宿店 opened November 22, 2022, launching alongside a second new store, Shinjuku Takano's Keio Shinjuku Salad shop, in the same basement food hall of Keio Department Store Shinjuku.
The Shinjuku store's line-up centers on 15-18 kinds of weighed salads and deli items, plus bento, sandwiches, and a platter exclusive to this location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese-style salads and deli sides at Salad Cafe commonly use soy-sauce-based dressings and seasonings, which typically contain wheat; no wheat-free dressing option was confirmed for this location.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Salad Cafe's parent operations state a commitment to providing accurate allergy and nutritional information for its products so customers can make informed choices.

positives.text: The shop offers a wide daily rotation of 15-18 vegetable-based salads and deli items sold by weight, so diners can visually select and portion exactly what they want rather than ordering a fixed set dish.

lead: 台南担仔麺 新宿本店

bio.origin: Tainan, Taiwan (dish origin); restaurant based in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.background: 台南担仔麺 新宿本店 is a long-running Taiwanese restaurant in Kabukicho (multiple sources describe it as roughly 30 years in business), styled after a Tainan night-market food stall, marked by a red lantern sign. It is one of two Shinjuku-area locations of the same small operation.
bio.specialty: 担仔麺 (tanshimen/tami) — a small bowl of noodles in a light chicken-and-pork-bone broth topped with the restaurant's original slow-cooked meat miso (rousō)
bio.philosophy: To let guests casually enjoy the atmosphere of a Taiwanese night-market food stall, offering over 90 kinds of Taiwanese small plates alongside the signature noodle bowl.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant recreates the feel of a Tainan night-market food stall with red-lantern signage in Kabukicho, and serves its signature 担仔麺 in a small bowl of chicken-and-pork-bone broth with a proprietary meat miso, alongside more than 90 varieties of Taiwanese small plates.

caveats: Could not directly fetch tabelog.com, tainan-taami.com, hotpepper.jp, or other listing pages — the sandbox's outbound proxy returned 403/connect-rejected for every one of these hosts. No gluten-free or vegan information exists in any source found — this is a conventional meat/seafood-forward Taiwanese noodle-and-small-plates restaurant with wheat noodles as its signature item. lead: 大衆すし酒場 ろんじ
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-7-1 れすぽうる1・2階

bio.origin: Tokyo (For You Group, headquartered in Toshima-ku), produced jointly with Yokohama izakaya operator Dynac
bio.background: 大衆すし酒場 ろんじ opened February 26, 2026 in an alley behind Dogenzaka, near Shibuya Mark City. It was co-produced by Tokyo restaurant operator For You Group (株式会社フォーユー, 35 stores nationwide) and Yokohama-based Dynac, marking For You's return to izakaya operations after the company pivoted toward soba (十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷) and tonkatsu (とんかつ わか葉) concepts during the pandemic.
bio.specialty: Nigiri sushi and tempura from 99 yen a piece (squid, octopus from 99 yen; salmon 165 yen; scallop 209 yen), with a wagyu-butter eel nigiri (286 yen) as a signature higher-end item.
bio.philosophy: Catchphrase '味は本格、価格は大衆' — authentic quality at mass-market prices — built around an escalating drink-price system (390/290/190 yen for 1st/2nd/3rd-plus drinks) aimed at customers in their 30s-50s.
roles: Chain izakaya operated by For You Group in partnership with Dynac

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'ろんじ' is sushi-kitchen insider slang, borrowed as a nod to the trade — inspired directly by Dynac's popular 'Taishu Sushi Sakaba Menoji' in Yokohama's Noge district.
For You Group had shifted away from izakaya operations after COVID-19 toward soba and tonkatsu restaurants; teaming up with Dynac on Ronji represents its renewed push back into the izakaya business.

caveats: This is a mass-market chain sushi/tempura izakaya (co-produced by For You Group and Dynac, part of a 35-store operator) rather than an independent shop, and no source mentions any gluten-free or vegan accommodation, so it is included here only as a verified, currently-operating spot within the target area — not as a GF/vegan-focused recommendation. Cash/card payment methods were not confirmed in any source. Could not directly load Tabelog, hitosara, tokyofrontline, foryou-group, or gf-support pages (403 errors); facts below are drawn from search-engine snippets/summaries of those pages plus successfully cited URLs.
closed_days: No regular closed day (open every day per available sources)
cuisine: 寿司・天ぷら・居酒屋
cultural_comfort_note: No source mentions an English menu or foreign-language support; it reads as a standard Japanese-language, locals-oriented budget izakaya chain.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, substitutions, or dedicated-fryer information appears in any source. The menu centers on tempura (wheat-flour batter) and nigiri sushi, which in standard izakaya practice use regular wheat-containing shoyu; nothing found confirms tamari, a gluten-free soy sauce option, or a separate fryer for tempura versus other fried items. Celiac diners should treat this as not gluten-free-safe absent direct confirmation from staff.
geocode_note: Address (道玄坂2-7-1) places the restaurant in an alley immediately behind Shibuya Mark City, well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595, 139.7005). No coordinates were geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null).

seats_text: 42 seats across two floors (about 17.5 tsubo total) — ground floor built around counter seating facing the open kitchen, second floor with table seating.
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or vegan menu options, dashi/broth substitutions, or plant-based accommodations are mentioned anywhere. This is a fish- and meat-centric sushi/tempura izakaya (sushi, wagyu-butter eel, tempura) with no indication of vegan awareness.

This is a meat- and cheese-centered classical French bistro (game dishes/ジビエ, braised meatloaf, roast chicken thigh, and the namesake aligot — mashed potato whipped with cheese). No vegan menu, vegan-marked items, or plant-based substitution options turned up in any source, and the cuisine style (butter, cream, cheese, animal stocks) makes it a poor fit for vegan diners without advance arrangement.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated prep found in any source. It is a classic French bistro with unlimited baguette refills as a stated draw, and sauces in this style are typically roux/flour-thickened. The open kitchen and shared cookware make cross-contact very likely. Celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without calling ahead and getting explicit confirmation.

geocode_note: Not geocoded (blocked in this environment). Address is on Jimbocho 1-chome; multiple sources confirm it is a 3-minute walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A5, which places it well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station (approx. 0.7km straight-line).
seats_text: 55 seats total across a lively standing/open-kitchen bar area, tatami floor seating (low tables), a private room, and counter chairs
closed_days: 日曜、祝日
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood-feel bistro in an old Japanese house with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so confident but ordinary Japanese-language comfort is expected to navigate the menu smoothly.
caveats: No vegan or gluten-free accommodation could be confirmed from any source; this listing is included for general reference only and is not recommended for celiac or strict vegan diners without direct confirmation by phone.

roles: chef: 村田シェフ (Murata, head chef)
operating company: 夢屋 (Yumeya), representative director 小林研
bio.background: Bistro Aligot occupies a roughly 65-year-old traditional Japanese house on a back alley in the used-bookstore district of Jimbocho — the building formerly housed an ice shop (氷屋), and its old sign was kept as part of the renovation. It opened as a hearty ('骨太') bistro concept run by the Yumeya restaurant group, with chef Murata bringing classical French bistro training to the kitchen.
bio.specialty: Aligot — potato mashed and whipped with cheese into a stretchy, elastic texture, a specialty of France's Auvergne region — served alongside unlimited baguette refills.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids frozen ingredients and pre-made products, building each dish by hand ('手仕事') in the classical bistro style.

anecdotes.text: 築65年の日本家屋を、元氷屋の看板を残したまま改装し「骨太ビストロアリゴ」として再生してオープンした。
冷凍商材を使わず、既製品を使わず、手仕事の温かみのある料理を一つ一つ作り上げることにこだわっている。
「昭和」の古民家で骨太フレンチを愉しめる神保町の名店として紹介されている。

lead: つけ麺 五ノ神製作所
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区千駄ヶ谷5-33-16 シャトレー新宿御苑第一 1F

bio.origin: Grew out of らーめん いつ樹 (Ramen Itsuki), a ramen shop originally based in Ome City in western Tokyo; the shrimp-tsukemen concept was spun off into its own specialty brand, with this Shinjuku Sanchome shop as a flagship location of that brand.
bio.background: Operated by 株式会社五ノ神製作所 (Gonokami Seisakusho Co., Ltd.), a small Tokyo ramen-group company that also runs the original Itsuki shop plus related meat and seafood-focused offshoots, and has expanded the shrimp-tsukemen concept to additional domestic outlets and overseas locations in Taiwan.
bio.specialty: 海老つけ麺 (Ebi Tsukemen / Shrimp Dipping Noodles) — a rich, pale-orange dipping broth built on a pork-bone base concentrated with luxuriously simmered sweet shrimp and a proprietary shrimp powder, served with thick wheat noodles; menu variations include Ebi Miso Tsukemen and Ebi Tomato Tsukemen.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is to take ingredients and byproducts that would otherwise be discarded and turn them into ramen/tsukemen, pushing to extract maximum shrimp flavor into the soup base.
roles: Shrimp-tsukemen specialty shop, one of several brands run by Gonokami Seisakusho Co., Ltd.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for its tagline inviting customers to "drink shrimp" (エビを飲む), reflecting how concentrated the shrimp flavor is in the dipping broth.
The Shinjuku Sanchome shop has been selected for Tabelog's Ramen Tokyo "Hyakumeiten" (Top 100) list multiple years running, including 2025.

caveats: Environment network restrictions blocked direct WebFetch/curl access to Tabelog, the official site, and most blogs today (all returned HTTP 403 at the proxy level); all facts below are triangulated from WebSearch result snippets that quote/summarize those same pages (Tabelog, ramendb, r.gnavi, official gonokamiseisakusho.com pages, PR Times, Ichibiki press release, food blogs), cross-checked across multiple independent sources for consistency. Hours are reported slightly inconsistently across sources (11:00-21:00 vs. 11:00-21:30 with LO 21:00); I used the most frequently repeated figure. No source discusses gluten-free noodle substitutes, dedicated fryers, or specific allergen cross-contamination protocols — this is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialty shop with no indication of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation, so it should be treated as unsafe for celiac/strict-vegan diners. Payment method (cash-only vs. cards/IC) was not confirmed in any source; defaulted to false but unverified.
closed_days: No fixed closed day reported (無休) in most sources, though this was not confirmed on the official site directly due to fetch restrictions.

cultural_comfort_note: A popular, often-queued tsukemen counter shop with an English-language Tabelog listing (suggesting some foreign-visitor traffic), but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — basic Japanese or pointing at photos/menu items is advisable.

lead: バンコクスパイス 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-14-4 小黒ビル 1F

bio.background: Part of the Thai Garden / Bangkok Spice group of Thai restaurants, the Shinjuku branch is an officially certified "Thai Select" restaurant, a designation awarded by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce to establishments recognized for serving authentic Thai cuisine.
bio.specialty: An extensive menu of more than 100 Thai dishes including desserts, spanning classics like tom yum goong alongside specialties such as crab claws in tamarind sauce (kung thot sauce makham) and shrimp-paste fried rice (khao khluk kapi).
bio.philosophy: Aims to serve the true taste of Thailand -- built on umami depth rather than spice and sourness alone -- inside a stylish, resort-like hideaway dining room.

anecdotes.text: Bangkok Spice Shinjuku is listed as an officially certified Thai Select restaurant by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce, a program recognizing restaurants abroad for authentic Thai cooking.

caveats: Hours, seating, and payment details are aggregated from restaurant listing/reservation sites (Tabelog, Hotpepper, Gnavi) rather than a single authoritative page, since the official site and several listing pages returned access errors when fetched directly. No dedicated vegan or gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or cross-contamination policy was found on any source -- Thai cuisine here likely relies on fish sauce, shrimp paste, and oyster/soy sauces by default, so diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm specifics with staff before ordering.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day (open daily); closed for New Year holidays Dec 30-31 and Jan 1-3.
cuisine: Thai
cultural_comfort_note: Booking and site content are in Japanese via mainstream reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Gnavi), with no confirmed English menu, so a bit of Japanese or gesture-based ordering will help.
geocode_note: Address (西新宿1-14-4, Nishi-Shinjuku) is described in multiple sources as a 2-3 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, placing it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station; per instructions lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or dedicated-fryer information was found on any source. As a Thai restaurant, sauces (soy sauce, oyster sauce) and some prepared items may contain wheat; safety for celiac diners cannot be confirmed and should be checked directly with staff before ordering.

lead: 自然派ワインとフランス郷土料理 シュシュ
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町30-10 青野ビル 1F

bio.origin: Kanagawa Prefecture (per one profile snippet)
bio.background: Owner-chef with roughly two decades of experience in French cuisine at well-regarded restaurants in Tokyo before opening this bistro in the Shinsen/Shibuya area.
bio.specialty: Roasted Ezo venison (エゾ鹿のロースト); the bouillabaisse (crispy-skinned sea bream, prawn, monkfish) is also cited as a signature dish.
bio.philosophy: Pairs French regional cooking with natural ('vin nature') wines chosen from producers the chef knows personally, aiming to make natural wine approachable even for guests with little wine knowledge; sources fish daily from Toyosu market and bakes roughly 15 kinds of bread in-house each morning to accompany meals.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Chouchou' comes from the French word for 'favorite/darling,' reflecting the chef's aim to make the restaurant a beloved neighborhood spot for natural wine lovers.

The restaurant is built around daily fresh fish from Toyosu market, game dishes (venison, duck, wild boar), and a natural wine list (cited variously as roughly 800-1000+ bottles across sources) that the chef helps guests navigate.

caveats: Primary sources (Tabelog, the restaurant's own chouchou-nature.com/.jp sites, Hitosara, Hotpepper, Gnavi, TimeOut) could not be directly fetched in this environment (network policy blocked tabelog.com and several other domains at the proxy level), so hours/address/chef details below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that quote or summarize those pages rather than from full page fetches. No mention of vegan or gluten-free accommodation was found anywhere; the concept is meat/game/seafood-forward with unlimited house-baked (wheat) bread, so this is not a dietary-focused pick — include only as a general French/natural-wine bistro, and advise anyone with celiac disease or strict vegan needs to call ahead (03-6416-3868) before booking.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, options, or allergy protocol were found in any source. The concept centers on roughly 15 kinds of house-baked wheat bread served unlimited alongside the meal, plus classic French sauces/roux that typically use wheat flour, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, advance confirmation from the restaurant.

lead: ワインビストロ トミーグリル
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-9-11

bio.background: Tommy Grill began roughly 70 years ago as a Western-style (yoshoku) restaurant with roots near Kanda. Current owner Masaki Nakatomi is the third generation to run it, having inherited the restaurant through his wife's family; he earned a sommelier qualification before taking over and relaunched the shop as a wine-focused bistro, marking his 10th year at the helm as of 2024.
bio.specialty: Hamburg steak (ハンバーグ) made with Australian 'budougyu' (grape-fed beef) — cattle raised on feed blended with grape pomace left over from winemaking, prized for its balance of lean meat and fat.
bio.philosophy: Owner-sommelier Nakatomi personally selects roughly 120 bottles of wine, alongside a broad by-the-glass list, to pair with seasonal fish and beef dishes in a casual bistro setting.

anecdotes.text: About 70 years after the restaurant's founding as a Kanda-area yoshoku shop, third-generation owner Masaki Nakatomi — who married into the family and became a certified sommelier before taking over — relaunched it as a wine bistro, reaching his 10th year running the shop in 2024.
The signature hamburg steak uses Australian grape-fed beef ('budougyu'), and the owner curates about 120 bottles of wine plus a wide glass-pour list to go with seasonal fish and meat dishes.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source. The signature dish is a classic Japanese-style hamburg steak (beef patty with demi-glace-style sauce), which typically involves breadcrumbs and a wheat-flour-thickened sauce, and the menu otherwise centers on wine-paired meat and seafood dishes. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should not assume safety here without calling ahead in Japanese.

The signature hamburg steak and other Western-style dishes typically rely on breadcrumbs and flour-thickened sauces, and no information confirms soy sauce, dashi, or sauce ingredients as wheat-free. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from the restaurant.

vegan_detail: This is a traditional Hakata-style tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen shop; the broth is pork-bone based (sources describe it as a lighter 魚介豚骨 fish-and-pork-bone style), so it is not vegan and no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are used, with free kaedama (wheat noodle refills) as a signature feature of the shop; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.

bio.background: A small Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen counter shop in the Nishikanda/Jinbocho area, in operation since around 2007. It has long operated under a yellow side-panel sign advertising 'new Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen' (新博多とんこつラーメン), located just off Hakusan-dori next to a taiyaki shop.
bio.specialty: Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen with thick-cut chashu, ajitsuke tamago (flavored soft-boiled egg), and a free kaedama (noodle refill) included with the bowl.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills, old-fashioned neighborhood ramen counter — Showa-retro in feel — prized by regulars for its unpretentious, friendly atmosphere as much as for the food.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run single-handedly by an older ('初老の') male owner, with a Showa-retro interior and a friendly, convivial atmosphere shared with regular customers.
The shop remade its signage after being featured on the TV program 'Adobake Tengoku' (出没！アド街ック天国).
The ramen has a lighter fish-and-pork-bone (魚介豚骨) character rather than a heavy classic Hakata tonkotsu, with fine, springy noodles and free kaedama.

caveats: No official restaurant website could be located (searched and confirmed via multiple queries). Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog and most third-party listing sites (Hotpepper, Hitosara, Ramendb, Enjoy Tokyo, Gnavi) returned HTTP 403 from the outbound proxy for this session, so all facts were sourced via WebSearch result summaries citing those pages rather than raw page fetches; address, hours, and menu details were independently corroborated across 2-3 sources each. This is a standard wheat-noodle, pork-bone-broth ramen shop with no vegan/gluten-free accommodations documented anywhere.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町2-11-4 Lee3ビル8F

bio.background: 農おおはし is a producer-run izakaya connected to Ohashi Farm (大橋農園), an actual working farm in Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture. The restaurant describes itself as a bridge between farmers and diners: it runs its own agriculture and livestock operation, sources seasonal vegetables directly from the family farm, and frames its mission around raising the standing of producers and giving diners traceability they can trust.
bio.origin: Nakatsugawa, Gifu Prefecture (Ohashi Farm)
bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable tempura (旬の野菜天ぷら) and vegetable-forward dishes such as colorful seasonal salads and a farmer's meat-and-vegetable stir-fry
bio.philosophy: Build a direct line from a real farm to the plate — showcasing seasonal, farm-grown vegetables in simple preparations (salads, tempura) alongside creative cooked dishes, paired with a curated list of organic wine, sake, and shochu.

anecdotes.text: The Ohashi family's farming story goes back to wartime hardship, when the current farm's founder's grandmother, Masako Ohashi, began growing a fiery heirloom chili called ajime pepper (あじめこしょう) — a variety cultivated in the same corner of Gifu for roughly 400 years. She called it a kind of 'magic powder,' saying even a pinch could turn a poor wartime meal into something delicious; the farm still grows it today alongside corn, broccoli, garlic, and sweet potatoes that supply the Tokyo restaurant.

caveats: Most listing sites (Tabelog, official gorp.jp site, Hotpepper, Gurunavi, Retty, Yahoo Map, Hitosara) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment; facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that quote and cite those pages, plus one working fetch attempt to the affiliated farm's own profile page. Hours/closed-day info is slightly inconsistent across sources (most say no fixed closed day / 無休, but at least one secondary listing mentioned a Tuesday closure) — confirm before visiting. No explicit gluten-free or vegan menu/policy statement was found on any source; the "vegetable-focused" positioning is about sourcing and menu emphasis, not a dietary-restriction program.
closed_days: No fixed closed day reported by most sources (year-round / 無休); at least one secondary listing indicated a possible Tuesday closure — call ahead (03-6273-8779) to confirm.
cuisine: Vegetable-focused Japanese izakaya (farm-to-table)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu, English-language site, or English-speaking-staff confirmation was found in any source, so go in expecting a Japanese-only experience.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Multiple independent sources place it at 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町2-11-4 Lee3ビル8F, described consistently as about a 5-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's east exit, which is well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was documented in any source. The menu includes tempura (wheat-flour batter, likely a shared fryer) and standard Japanese seasonings, which typically contain wheat-based shoyu and dashi. This is a vegetable-forward izakaya, not a gluten-free-oriented restaurant — celiac diners should call ahead and ask about specific ingredients and fryer use rather than assume safety.
neighborhood: Kabukicho, Shinjuku

positives.text: The restaurant's own description emphasizes fresh, seasonal vegetables shipped directly from its affiliated Ohashi Farm in Gifu, forming the base of colorful salads, vegetable tempura, and other vegetable-centered dishes, which gives naturally plant-forward options on the menu even though it is not a dedicated vegan restaurant.

seats_text: Approximately 26 seats, including 3 private rooms in a Japanese-modern interior
vegan_detail: The menu is vegetable-forward (seasonal vegetable salads, vegetable tempura, vegetable stir-fries) but is not a vegan restaurant — it also serves meat (wild boar, farmer's meat-and-vegetable stir-fry) and seafood (garlic shrimp) dishes, and traditional dashi/sauces used in Japanese cooking commonly contain bonito or other animal-derived stock. No dedicated vegan menu, marking, or policy was found in any source — vegans should ask staff directly which dishes and sauces are plant-based.

seats_text: Not specified in available sources.

Note: the July 2025 opening press release described different, later dinner-only bar hours (Mon-Sat 17:00-29:00 last order 28:00, Sun 17:00-23:00 last order 22:00) with no lunch service, suggesting hours have since changed to add lunch and/or sources conflict.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodations were found. Signature dishes include a doria (bechamel/rice/pasta bake) and breaded, fried whole lobster tail (frit), both wheat-based preparations; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol was mentioned anywhere.
caveats: Direct page fetches to Tabelog, Hotpepper, Jalan, Retty, Hitosara, PR Times, and other listing sites all returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all details below are drawn from search-engine result summaries of those pages rather than the raw pages themselves. Operating hours conflict between the July 2025 opening press release (dinner-only bar until 29:00) and current listing aggregators (which add a lunch service and end around 23:00); the more recent aggregator hours were used as primary. Seat count and any allergy/dietary accommodation policy could not be confirmed and should be verified by phone (03-6455-0877 / 080-4126-1765) before a celiac or vegan visit.

roles: Shinsuke Yamada — proprietor, sommelier-certified at age 23, formerly executive vice president at the 'Ore no' restaurant group
Akito Hashimoto — chef, previously worked at Ginza L'écrin and led the kitchen at 'Ore no Italian'
bio.background: Opened July 10, 2025 in Shibuya's Dōgenzaka nightlife district by restaurant company Eatwing Inc., pairing sommelier Shinsuke Yamada with chef Akito Hashimoto (ex-Ginza L'écrin, ex-'Ore no Italian').
bio.specialty: Neo-Milan Doria (a reinvented take on classic Milan-style doria) and whole lobster tail fritto; also 14-month aged Slovenian prosciutto with melon.
bio.philosophy: Reinterprets nostalgic 'itameshi' (Japanese-Italian fusion) comfort-food dishes from the late-1980s to 1990s Itameshi boom with a modern, 'neo-itameshi' twist, served alongside craft sours, sangria, and natural wines.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Apero' is drawn from the Italian aperitivo culture — the owners wanted guests to casually enjoy a drink amid the energetic nightlife atmosphere of Shibuya's Dōgenzaka.
The restaurant was originally positioned as a late-night Italian bar open until 5am (last order 4am) on weeknights, reflecting its 'enjoy until late night' concept at launch.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-12

bio.origin: Ikebukuro, Tokyo (chain founded 2009)
bio.background: Kasugatei is a Tokyo-born abura-soba (oil noodle) specialty chain that began at its Ikebukuro honten and has since grown into a multi-location chain across Tokyo (and beyond, including Nagoya and Niigata). This Dogenzaka shop is the original Shibuya branch, run as part of the Kasugatei Group.
bio.specialty: Shoyu abura-soba, and the shop's popular Torimen (chicken-and-pork) abura-soba — noodles served oil-and-sauce-coated rather than in broth, meant to be tossed tableside with vinegar, rayu chili oil and garlic before eating.
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated commitment (kodawari) centers on noodles made with 100% Hokkaido-grown wheat flour, paired with a secret tare blended from several Kyushu soy sauces, backed by a chicken-bone and pork-bone stock simmered for two days to concentrate its flavor.

anecdotes.text: In spring 2025 Kasugatei announced it was suspending PayPay and other cashless payment options at several locations — including its Ikebukuro flagship, this Shibuya (Dogenzaka) shop, and its Akihabara and Shibuya Center-gai branches — citing the burden of card-processing fees on a roughly 1,000-yen average ticket, and asked customers to "please pay in cash wherever possible."

caveats: This is a mainstream wheat-noodle abura-soba/ramen chain outlet with no vegan or gluten-free orientation — flagged in a GF/vegan-aware app only as a place to AVOID or approach with extreme caution for those diets. Reported closing hours varied slightly between sources (11:00-24:00 vs 11:00-23:00 on weekdays); went with the more frequently cited 24:00 close. No direct site fetch was possible (tabelog.com, kasugatei.com, and most other restaurant-listing domains returned 403 at the network/proxy level in this environment), so all facts here are drawn from search-engine snippet extracts of those pages rather than full page fetches — flagging lower confidence on granular details like exact seat count and payment methods, though the address, cuisine type, and general operating pattern were corroborated across multiple independent listings (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Hitosara, official kasugatei.com, ramen-fan blogs).
closed_days: None listed as a regular weekly closed day (open daily, "無休"); closed only during the New Year holiday period (年末年始)
cuisine: 油そば、まぜそば (Abura-soba / oil noodles, mazesoba)
cultural_comfort_note: Typical ticket-vending-machine (食券) ordering system common to Japanese ramen/abura-soba shops; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app or pointing at the photo menu is recommended.
gf_detail: Noodles are made with 100% Hokkaido wheat flour and there is no gluten-free noodle alternative identified. The tare (sauce) is a blend of several soy sauces from Kyushu, which typically contain wheat. As a wheat-noodle specialty shop with no gluten-free options found, this is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Address is Shibuya-ku Dogenzaka 2-6-12, a short walk (well under 0.8km, roughly a few hundred meters) from the Shibuya Scramble crossing, between Shibuya Mark City and Dogenzaka. soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature tare is a secret blend of several soy sauces sourced from Kyushu, and the noodles are made with 100% Hokkaido wheat flour — a standard wheat-based soy-sauce noodle shop with no gluten-free noodle or sauce alternative identified.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tare and stock base are built on chicken-bone (torigara) and pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock simmered for two days, so the standard dishes are not vegetarian or vegan; no plant-based broth or noodle option was found.

vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options were identified.
