lead: SACYA 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア B2F（東急フードショーエッジ内）

bio.origin: Daikanyama, Tokyo (original SACYA location); this Shibuya Scramble Square store is the brand's second shop
bio.background: SACYA is a matcha specialty café that began in Daikanyama (the original shop opened in 2023 under the name SOPHIA COFFEE and was rebranded to SACYA the following year). This Shibuya Scramble Square location opened July 28, 2025 inside the Tokyu Food Show Edge food hall on B2F, directly connected to Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Matcha made from Yame-grown tea leaves supplied by Hoshino Seichaen, a Fukuoka tea garden that has produced matcha since 1989. Signature items include a 'kinuchino' (a cappuccino-style matcha drink topped with pink foam) and colorful matcha lattes such as strawberry matcha and lavender matcha latte; matcha is whisked to order at the counter in front of customers.
bio.philosophy: The brand name SACYA comes from Konohanasakuyahime, the Japanese mythological goddess associated with the origin of sakura (cherry blossoms). SACYA presents itself as a 'modern teahouse,' reinterpreting traditional Japanese tea culture through a contemporary, visually driven café format.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya store's exclusive menu includes a vegan, gluten-free sakura-shaped matcha cookie sandwich made with rice flour.
SACYA uses Yame matcha from Hoshino Seichaen, one of Kyushu's early matcha producers dating to 1989.
The name SACYA is derived from Konohanasakuyahime, the goddess said to be the origin of the word sakura.

caveats: Direct fetch of tabelog.com, sacya.co.jp, tokyu-dept.co.jp, and shibuya-scramble-square.com pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so facts below are drawn from web-search snippets/summaries that quote and cite those pages rather than from the raw HTML itself. Hours, exact closed-days, and seat count in particular should be treated as best-available rather than independently page-verified. This is a takeout-format matcha counter inside a department-store food hall, not a full-service restaurant.
closed_days: No store-specific closed days found; the shop appears to follow Shibuya Scramble Square / Tokyu Food Show Edge building hours.
cuisine: Matcha specialty café — premium matcha and tea-based beverages (matcha, matcha lattes, "kinuchino"), matcha sweets (Basque cheesecake, matcha cookies), organic/single-origin tea focus.
geocode_note: Address (2-24-12 Shibuya, Shibuya Scramble Square B2F) places this shop directly at/adjacent to Shibuya Station, well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble intersection landmark. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions — network geocoding is blocked; downstream pin derivation should use address_ja.
gf_detail: Plain matcha and matcha lattes are naturally gluten-free (no reported wheat-based syrups or additives). The Shibuya-exclusive matcha cookie sandwich is made with rice flour and marketed as gluten-free as well as vegan. However, other bakery items such as the signature matcha Basque cheesecake are wheat-based, and there is no confirmed dedicated gluten-free prep line — the counter appears to handle both wheat-containing and rice-flour items, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Celiac-level diners should confirm with staff before ordering baked goods.
hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 (reported daily hours; specific weekday variation not confirmed)
name_ja: SACYA（サクヤ）渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
neighborhood: 渋谷（Shibuya Scramble Square B2F, directly connected to Shibuya Station）

positives.text: Offers a Shibuya-exclusive vegan and gluten-free matcha cookie sandwich made with rice flour, plus soy milk and oat milk substitute options for matcha lattes.

seats_text: Takeout-format counter with no dedicated seating of its own; shared eat-in tables are available on the same B2F food-hall floor (Tokyu Food Show Edge).
vegan_detail: Matcha lattes can be made with soy or oat milk instead of dairy, and the sakura-shaped matcha cookie sandwich (Shibuya-exclusive) is fully plant-based. Default items such as the matcha Basque cheesecake and the pink-foam "kinuchino" use dairy unless a substitution is requested. As a matcha/tea café with no savory food menu, there are no dashi/bonito-based items to worry about.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町2-7-6 浅野屋ビル1F

bio.background: Founded in 1925 (Taishō 14) as a noren-wake (independent branch) of the historic Asanoya soba lineage in Kanda, whose original shop traces back to 1872 (Meiji 5). Founder Kobayashi Kiichi opened this Sarugakuchō branch after receiving the noren (shop name and blessing) from the main Asanoya, and the shop has operated at the same Suidōbashi-area address for a century.
bio.origin: Kanda, Tokyo — traditional Edo-style soba lineage
bio.philosophy: Keeps the unhurried, old-fashioned atmosphere of a classic Tokyo soba-ya, serving soba-mae (small dishes meant to be enjoyed with sake before the noodles) alongside hand-prepared tempura, in a calm room with both table and tatami seating.
bio.specialty: Gobo-ten (burdock-root tempura) and tonpura (pork loin deep-fried in tempura batter)

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature gobo-ten burdock tempura is cut using a 'hyōshigiri' technique the cook mastered during his apprenticeship, giving the tempura an unusually hearty, crunchy bite that has become the dish diners order it for.
For roughly 15 years the shop has served itawasa (sliced fish cake with wasabi) made with Suzuhiro kamaboko, and the owners say customers regularly compliment its texture and flavor.

caveats: No official restaurant website was found for this specific branch (distinct from Asanoya Honten in Kanda); details were compiled from Tabelog and independent food/history blogs. No source discusses allergy accommodation, gluten-free substitutions, or vegan-specific preparation — this is a traditional soba shop, not a dietary-focused one. gf_confidence and vegan_status are set to "no" based on the standard composition of nihachi soba, soy-sauce-based tsuyu, and bonito dashi described in sources, not on an explicit denial by the restaurant.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (日曜・祝日)
cuisine: Soba, Udon (traditional Edo-style soba-ya)
cultural_comfort_note: A century-old neighborhood soba shop with no English menu found in sources — comfortable for diners with some Japanese dining experience, but not set up for English-only visitors.
geocode_note: Address confirmed via multiple sources as 千代田区神田猿楽町2-7-6, roughly a 6-minute walk from the east exit of JR Suidōbashi Station and about 7 minutes from Jinbōchō Station — well within the Suidōbashi Station 0.8km target radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; geocoding is to be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: The shop serves nihachi soba (二八蕎麦), i.e. noodles made from roughly 80% buckwheat and 20% wheat flour, so the soba itself contains gluten. The dipping tsuyu is soy-sauce based (soy sauce typically contains wheat) and the tempura items (gobo-ten burdock tempura, tonpura pork cutlet) are coated in wheat flour batter and fried in the shared kitchen. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific preparation is mentioned in any source — not suitable for celiac diners.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00 & 17:00-20:00; Sat 11:00-14:00; closed Sun and public holidays
neighborhood: Kanda Sarugakuchō, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidōbashi / Jinbōchō)
seats_text: Small traditional soba-ya with a mix of table seating (including a table for four) and tatami mat seating; exact seat count not stated in available sources.
vegan_detail: The broth/tsuyu for both hot and cold soba is built on bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, and the menu includes meat and fish soba-mae dishes such as tonpura (pork loin tempura). No vegan or dashi-free preparation is mentioned in any source, so this is not suitable for vegan diners without prior arrangement (which is unconfirmed).

lead: 月島もんじゃ おこげ 浅草
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-35-9 ザグローヴ

bio.origin: Tsukishima, Tokyo (the historic monjayaki district)
bio.background: This Asakusa shop is a branch of the 月島もんじゃ おこげ (Tsukishima Monjya Okoge) group, which traces its roots to a Tsukishima monjayaki restaurant run by a seafood wholesale company founded in Meiji 4 (1871). The Asakusa branch opened on March 4, 2022, about a 1-2 minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6 and roughly 5 minutes from Sensoji, and quickly became popular with lines forming from opening day.
bio.specialty: 明太もちもんじゃ (mentaiko mochi monjayaki) - a pollock-roe-and-mochi monjayaki that has become the shop's signature, alongside other seafood-forward monjya such as ika-sumi (squid ink) monjya.
bio.philosophy: Because the parent company is a longstanding seafood wholesaler, the kitchen leans on seafood ingredients for its monjya and okonomiyaki. Skilled staff grill the monjayaki tableside for guests, so first-timers who have never cooked monjayaki themselves can still enjoy it without doing the work.
roles: monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant, part of a small Tsukishima-origin monjayaki group

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened March 4, 2022, and has drawn steady lines of customers since its opening day.
The signature 明太子もちもんじゃ (mentaiko-mochi monjya) sells for about ¥1,618 and is frequently singled out by visitors as the must-order dish.
The shop's roots trace back to a Tsukishima monjayaki restaurant operated by a seafood wholesale company founded in Meiji 4 (1871), which is why seafood dishes are recommended.

caveats: This is a traditional monjayaki/okonomiyaki restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment. The wheat-flour batter, likely wheat-containing soy sauce/Worcestershire-style sauces, and a shared teppan grill make it a poor fit for celiac diners. No vegan-specific menu or dashi substitution was found; the shop's specialty dishes are seafood-based (mentaiko, squid ink), and traditional monjya broth typically relies on bonito/fish dashi. Two locations exist under similar names in Asakusa (this one at 浅草1-35-9, and a separate '浅草分店' branch at 浅草1-1-10, Tabelog ID 13284695) — do not confuse them.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed days; open every day of the year per listed sources)
cuisine: もんじゃ焼き・お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Monjayaki / Okonomiyaki / Teppanyaki)
cultural_comfort_note: A popular tourist-adjacent monjayaki spot near Sensoji where staff cook tableside for beginners, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found in sources — expect a mostly Japanese-language experience.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address 東京都台東区浅草1-35-9 (Asakusa 1-chome) is confirmed by multiple independent sources to be about a 1-2 minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6 and roughly 5 minutes on foot from Sensoji, placing it well within 0.8km of the Sensoji (Asakusa) landmark.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free batter, or dedicated GF preparation was found in any source. Monjayaki and okonomiyaki batter is wheat-flour based by nature, sauces and soy sauce used in Japanese teppan cooking typically contain wheat, and the dish is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside other wheat-containing orders, creating cross-contamination risk. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
name_ja: 月島もんじゃ おこげ 浅草
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito-ku - about 5 minutes' walk from Sensoji

positives.text: Skilled staff grill the monjayaki tableside for guests, which some reviewers note makes it approachable for first-timers, though this addresses cooking skill rather than allergen safety.

seats_text: Described as a spacious L-shaped interior extending toward the back; exact seat count not published in available sources.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian-specific menu was found. The shop's specialty dishes are seafood-forward (mentaiko/pollock roe, squid ink), and traditional monjayaki broth is typically made with bonito (katsuo) or other fish-based dashi, plus Worcestershire-style sauces that often contain fish extracts. Vegetable-only monjya could potentially be ordered, but no confirmation of a dashi substitution or animal-product-free broth was found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町22-4 B1（センター街入口、みずほ銀行向かいのドラッグストア地下）

bio.background: An 80-plus-year-old neighborhood soba shop tucked in the basement at the very entrance of Shibuya Center-gai, just past the Scramble Crossing. Despite sitting in one of the busiest, loudest corners of Tokyo, the shop keeps a hushed, Showa-era dining room down a narrow staircase — a deliberate contrast that regulars and food writers alike remark on.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Hand-made futsu (two-eight ratio) Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles, including the house specialty sankensoba (三根そば) and classic mori/zaru soba, alongside donburi and udon dishes.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen selects wheat and buckwheat flour by season, choosing whichever is 'most delicious at that time of year,' and adjusts the water ratio in the dough daily to account for changing weather and humidity — a craft the shop attributes to accumulated years of the noodle-makers' experience and intuition rather than a fixed recipe.

anecdotes.text: Located at the entrance of Center-gai in the basement of a building across from Mizuho Bank, the shop is often described as an island of calm below the crowds and noise of one of Shibuya's busiest streets.
The shop has been serving its Sarashina-style noodles for over 60-80 years, adjusting flour selection and dough hydration daily according to the season and weather.

caveats: Reported hours conflict across directories (one set says Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00/Sun 11:00-20:30; another, likely outdated, says Mon-Sat until 22:30/Sun until 21:30) — confirm by phone (03-3461-0335) before visiting. No vegan or gluten-free menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated-fryer information could be confirmed from any source; this is a traditional soba shop, not a diet-specialized one, so celiac and strict-vegan diners should not assume safety without calling ahead. Official site (shibuyasarashina.com) could not be directly fetched (DNS blocked in this environment) but is corroborated by multiple independent search results as the shop's real homepage.
closed_days: None regularly — open year-round (無休), though likely closed for New Year's holidays per standard Japanese practice; not independently confirmed.
cuisine: Soba (traditional two-eight/Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles), also udon and rice-bowl (donburi) dishes
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, local, Japanese-language-first basement soba shop with no confirmed English menu or vegan/GF signage — diners with dietary restrictions should be prepared to explain their needs in Japanese or via translation app.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is blocked in this environment; lat/lng left null. Address (Shibuya-ku, Udagawa-cho 22-4) places the shop directly at the entrance of Shibuya Center-gai, immediately adjacent to Shibuya Scramble Crossing (well within 0.8km), consistent with the target-area requirement.
gf_detail: The shop's noodles are described consistently across sources as 二八そば (nihachi / "two-eight") — an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat-flour blend — meaning the soba itself contains wheat gluten and is not gluten-free. No 十割 (juwari, 100% buckwheat) noodle option was found in any menu source. Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping/broth sauce) is standardly made with wheat-containing shoyu; no gluten-free tsuyu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol could be confirmed for this shop. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: Most-cited: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), Sun & holidays 11:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). One older/conflicting source lists Mon-Sat 11:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), Sun 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Sources disagree — call 03-3461-0335 to confirm current hours.
neighborhood: Shibuya, Center-gai / Udagawa-cho

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodles are two-eight (nihachi) soba — roughly 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour — so the noodle itself contains gluten; standard soba tsuyu also relies on wheat-based shoyu.

seats_text: Roughly 42 seats in a basement dining room (one source describes it as an intimate room seating around 20).
vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu, vegan-adapted dashi, or any vegan accommodation at this shop. Traditional soba tsuyu in Japan is standardly made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, which is not vegan; nothing found indicates this shop uses a kombu/shiitake-only vegetarian dashi instead. Vegan diners should call ahead (03-3461-0335) to ask whether a plain noodle order or modified sauce is possible before visiting.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-11-12 アイロン三崎町ビル B1F

bio.background: 店長の川邊優太さんはソムリエ資格を持ち、水道橋駅から徒歩1分の地下1階でこのカジュアルフレンチビストロを営んでいる。
bio.specialty: 低温調理のコンフィ製法で仕上げる豚肉のグリル（ロースト前にオイルでじっくり火入れするコンフィ仕立て）
bio.philosophy: 開店にあたり広告宣伝費をかけず、その分の予算をランチの原価に充てることで、リーズナブルな価格で本格フレンチを楽しんでもらうという姿勢を貫いている。

anecdotes.text: ランチは全7種類のメニューがあり、メインに加えてライスまたはパン、スープ、ソフトドリンクが付いて1,200円という、コストパフォーマンスの高さで知られる。
ディナーではソムリエ資格を持つ川邊さんが世界各国から集めた50種類以上のワインやビールをグラス単位で提供し、ビストロフレンチと共に楽しめる。

caveats: No online source mentions gluten-free or vegan accommodations; this is a classic meat-and-wine French bistro (menu tagline "肉&ワイン カジュアルフレンチ"), so dishes very likely rely on wheat-based sauces (roux), bread, and animal-based stock/butter throughout. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (0362614656) rather than assume any accommodation. Saturday is dinner-only (no lunch service); closed Sundays and public holidays.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日
cuisine: フレンチ・ビストロ
cultural_comfort_note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service; ordering will likely require Japanese or pointing at photos.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng set to null); address confirms location approximately 1 minute on foot from JR/Toei Mita Line Suidōbashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on Tabelog, the restaurant's Instagram, or food-media coverage. As a French bistro featuring roux-based sauces, bread service, and confit/fried preparations, wheat exposure across the kitchen should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise by phone.
neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋
seats_text: 40席（地下1階の店内）
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based labeling, or dashi/bonito-avoidance information was found in any source. The restaurant is explicitly marketed as "肉&ワイン カジュアルフレンチ" (Meat & Wine Casual French), a meat-centric concept, so a full vegan meal is unlikely to be available without prior arrangement; vegetarians/vegans should call ahead to check for any adaptable vegetable-forward dishes.

lead: 間借りカレー シモカワスパイス
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-19-22 新宿ダイカンプラザシティ1F「新宿天幕-CIRCUS-」内（間借り営業）

bio.background: A one-person "magari" (borrowed-kitchen) spice curry pop-up that takes over a Nishi-Shinjuku bar for lunch service a few days a week. The owner (who posts as @high007 / @shimokawa.spice) also runs occasional pop-ups elsewhere in Tokyo (Koenji) and makes research trips abroad, including to India, to study regional curry traditions.
bio.specialty: A rotating yearly "signature" curry built around one theme mastered per year — butter chicken (2021), pork vindaloo (2022), sashimi-fish coconut curry (2023), rendang (2024), and a vegetable-forward "W-vegetable curry" (2025, e.g. jackfruit rendang and Assam taro curry) — usually served alongside a rotating daily curry, plus a mixed "3-curry forest bowl."
bio.philosophy: "Ichinen-ichi-curry" (one curry per year): rather than a fixed menu, the shop spends a full year deeply refining a single spice-curry concept before moving to the next, treating each year's dish as a dedicated study.

anecdotes.text: A blog post title notes the owner traveling to India in January for curry research ("1月は店主インドへ"), reflecting the shop's practice of sourcing techniques directly from source regions each year.
The shop's core concept of mastering one new curry theme per year (butter chicken, pork vindaloo, fish-coconut curry, rendang, vegetable curry) is described across independent write-ups.

caveats: Payment method (cash-only vs. card/QR) could not be confirmed from available sources. As a "magari" (borrowed-kitchen) pop-up, operating days are irregular and change week to week per the owner's Instagram — the hours listed are the stated regular pattern, not a guarantee. No allergy/dietary policy statement from the shop itself was found; gluten-free and vegan claims below are inferred from general menu descriptions only, not staff confirmation.
closed_days: 日・月・火 (Sun/Mon/Tue), plus additional irregular closures for off-site events — confirm current schedule via Instagram @shimokawa.spice before visiting
cuisine: Spice curry (Indian-influenced), rotating yearly signature theme; vegetable-forward options in the current year's menu
cultural_comfort_note: A very local one-person pop-up whose schedule and updates are posted only in Japanese on Instagram, with no confirmed English menu or signage — best visited with some Japanese support or translation help.
geocode_note: Multiple independent sources place the pop-up in Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome (西新宿7-19-22), described as roughly a 4-minute walk from Shinjuku Station (all lines) / Toei Oedo Line Shinjuku-nishiguchi Station exit D5 — well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark. Coordinates intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should resolve the address above.
gf_detail: No gluten-free policy or ingredient statement from the shop was found. Spice curries here are rice-based rather than roux-thickened, which is favorable, but wheat exposure is unconfirmed for shoyu/marinades used in dishes like the pork vindaloo, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or cross-contamination protocol. Ask staff directly about wheat in sauces/marinades before ordering.
hours_raw: 水〜金 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30)、土 11:30-14:30、日・月・火定休。間借り営業のため出店日・時間は変動あり、Instagramで要確認
name_ja: 間借りカレー シモカワスパイス
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), near Shinjuku Station West Exit
seats_text: Approximately 14 seats (counter and table), shared with the host bar's space
vegan_detail: No formal vegan menu or claim was found. The shop's core menu (butter chicken curry, pork vindaloo) is meat-based and not vegan. However, the current yearly theme (2025) is a vegetable-forward "W-vegetable curry" featuring dishes such as jackfruit rendang and Assam taro curry, which are plant-based in concept but not confirmed free of dashi, fish sauce, or ghee. Vegan diners should ask staff which items are fully plant-based on the day they visit.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区東1-14-12 常盤松マンション1F（〒150-0011）

bio.background: Vegeaterは渋谷駅東口から徒歩約10分の場所にある、大豆ミートを使わず動物性食材を一切使用しない100%ヴィーガンのハンバーガー専門店。数種の豆をベースにした自家製の厚みのあるオリジナルパティを使用し、卵・乳製品不使用のバンズで仕立てている。
bio.specialty: プルドジャックバーガー（豆ベースのオリジナルパティに加え、ジャックフルーツ、コールスロー、貝割れ大根、バーボンBBQソースを合わせた看板メニュー、¥1,500）
bio.philosophy: 大豆ミートに頼らず、複数の豆を組み合わせた独自レシピのパティでボリュームと食べ応えを追求し、動物性食材ゼロでも満足感のあるハンバーガーを提供することを掲げている。
roles: ヴィーガンハンバーガー専門店

anecdotes.text: 「食べ応え充分なヴィーガンバーガー！」と評され、プルドジャックバーガーの厚みのあるパティとジャックフルーツの組み合わせが紹介されている。
食べログのユーザーレビューでは「ビーガンレストランと侮るなかれ！」「ヘルシーでオシャレでボリュームのあるヴィーガンバーガー専門店」と高評価されている。

caveats: Hours conflict across secondary sources (one lists 平日・土のみ 11:30-14:00/17:30-23:00, another lists 月-日 11:30-15:00/17:00-23:00 every day); one guide explicitly advises checking Instagram for actual operating days, so hours are marked irregular/tentative. Direct WebFetch of Tabelog, official site (vegeater.jp did not resolve via DNS), Vegewel, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, Retty and blog pages all returned 403/DNS errors in this environment, so details below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than full page fetches — treat phone number, exact seat count, and precise hours as unconfirmed. Hamburgers (the signature dish) are reportedly lunch-only and require advance notice/reservation for dinner service. No explicit gluten-free claim was found for the buns (standard wheat bun appears likely); this is a vegan-only spec, not a GF-dedicated one.
closed_days: 不定休（情報源により差異あり／Instagramでの要確認が推奨されている）
cuisine: ハンバーガー（100%ヴィーガン・動物性食材不使用）
cultural_comfort_note: There is an English-language Tabelog listing and an English/Japanese-mixed Instagram presence, but menu and staff communication appear primarily Japanese, so basic Japanese greetings will help.
gf_detail: No source found confirming gluten-free buns or a gluten-free menu; this is a 100%-vegan (not gluten-free) concept and the burger buns appear to be standard wheat buns. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here and should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
hours_raw: 情報源により差異あり：（Hotpepper系）月〜日 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:30) / 17:00-23:00 (L.O.22:30)。（ぐるなび系）平日・土 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-23:00。ハンバーガーはランチのみ提供、ディナーで食べる場合は予約時に要事前連絡。営業日の詳細はInstagramでの確認が推奨されている。
neighborhood: 渋谷（東、渋谷駅東口より徒歩約10分）

vegan_cross_contact.text: 店は動物性食材を一切使用しない100%ヴィーガン業態で、大豆ミートも使わずオリジナルの豆ベースパティを使用しているとされる。

positives.text: 動物性食材不使用・大豆ミート不使用を明記した100%ヴィーガン専門店。

seats_text: Seat count not confirmed in available sources; restaurant offers a private room bookable for parties of 4+.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan concept: no animal-based ingredients and no soy-meat substitute, using an original bean-based patty instead. The signature Pulled Jack Burger (¥1,500) uses jackfruit, coleslaw, daikon sprouts and a bourbon BBQ sauce alongside the bean patty. No specific mention was found of hidden dashi/bonito risk since the whole menu is built vegan from the ground up, but exact sauce/condiment ingredient lists were not independently verified.

lead: 追分だんご本舗 新宿本店

bio.origin: 1947年（昭和22年）創業。起源はさらに古く、1455年に太田道灌が高井戸で中秋の名月の宴を催した際、地元の名族から献上された手作りの団子を大いに気に入り、その後も所望したという言い伝えに遡る。
bio.background: The shop's roots trace back to 1455, when the warlord Ota Dokan, while building Edo Castle, held a moon-viewing party at Takaido and was presented with hand-formed dango by a local family — he loved them so much he kept requesting them. The name 'Oiwake' (追分) refers to the fork in old Naito-Shinjuku where the Koshu Kaido and Ome Kaido roads split, a busy travelers' crossroads. In the summer of 1947, just after the war, founder Fujii Toemon opened a small shop called 'Yanagiya' in Shinjuku Sanchome; having heard the old story of Oiwake dango from town elders, he set out to revive its taste on the very spot where the roads once met, and the business grew into today's Oiwake Dango Honpo.
bio.specialty: Otona no Mitarashi (大人のみたらし, "Adult's Mitarashi") — dango glazed in the shop's mitarashi sauce blended with sansho pepper and fresh shichimi spice, giving the classic sweet-salty soy glaze a warmer, spicier edge.
bio.philosophy: Dough, red bean paste, and beans are all hand-prepared fresh in the shop's own kitchen every morning before the dango are made and sold the same day.
roles: 和菓子店・喫茶室 (wagashi shop and tea room)

anecdotes.text: 太田道灌が江戸城築城中、高井戸で中秋の名月の宴を開いた際、地元の名族が手づくりの団子を献上し、道灌がその味をいたく気に入って以後も所望した、という室町時代の逸話が「追分だんご」の起源とされる。
終戦間もない1947年夏、新宿三丁目に「やなぎ家」という白い暖簾を掲げたのが現在の追分だんご本舗の始まり。創業者・藤井藤右ヱ門が町の古老から追分だんごの由来を聞き、新宿追分の地でその味を復活させようとしたという。
名物「大人のみたらし」は山椒と生七味を効かせた甘辛いタレが特徴で、起源は室町時代にまで遡ると紹介されている。

caveats: Traditional wagashi shop and tea room (甘味処), not a GF- or vegan-dedicated business — no allergen certification, English menu, or dedicated fryer/prep line was found in sources. Dango are rice-based, but the standard mitarashi glaze uses ordinary shoyu (typically contains wheat in Japan) and menu includes dairy items like cream anmitsu; confirm specifics with staff before ordering if you have celiac disease or are strictly vegan.
closed_days: 1月1日・1月2日（元日・1月2日）
cuisine: 甘味処・和菓子・喫茶（Japanese sweets / dango / tea room）
cultural_comfort_note: A historic, primarily Japanese-oriented neighborhood wagashi shop and tea room — no confirmed English menu, though it sits in the very central, tourist-frequented Shinjuku Sanchome area.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null (network geocoding disabled in this environment). Address (新宿区新宿3-1-22) and multiple sources place the shop about 6 minutes' walk (~450-500m) from JR Shinjuku Station East Exit and about 2 minutes (~150m) from Tokyo Metro/Toei Shinjuku-sanchome Station, comfortably within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius.
gf_detail: Dango here are rice-flour based (not wheat), and the anko (red bean paste) is normally wheat-free, but the shop's mitarashi sauce is a standard sweet soy-sauce glaze — ordinary Japanese shoyu typically contains wheat, and no gluten-free soy sauce, dedicated allergen protocol, or GF menu labeling was found in any source. No dedicated-fryer confirmation either way was found. Anyone with celiac disease should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering.
hours_raw: 物販: 10:30〜19:00（毎日）／喫茶室: 12:00〜18:00（L.O.17:30）、土・日曜日・祝日は11:30から。生菓子完売の場合は営業時間内でも早く閉店することがある。
name_ja: 追分だんご本舗 新宿本店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（Shinjuku Sanchome）
seats_text: Reports vary between roughly 27 and 30 seats in the second-floor tea room (kissa); exact current count unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: Plain dango, anko-based sweets, and basic mitsumame/anmitsu components (rice dumplings, red beans, agar, syrup) are typically made without animal products, but the menu also includes items like cream anmitsu (dairy whipped cream) and other seasonal additions that are not vegan. No dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or staff confirmation was found in any source — vegan diners should ask about specific ingredients, especially anything involving cream or dairy toppings.

lead: 浅草おにぎりはるちゃん
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-13-4

bio.background: Opened in April 2024 on Kokusai-dori in Asakusa, a short walk from the Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station. The shop occupies a building styled like an old Japanese house, with shoji-paper windows, wooden nameplate menus, and a hinoki (cypress) counter, aiming for a warm, nostalgic atmosphere. The shop's name, "Haruchan," is the name of the proprietress's own mother — the onigiri are presented as her mother's home-style recipe.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood onigiri counter built around the idea of Japanese soul food made fresh to order: rice balls hand-shaped to order using carefully sourced ingredients, served either to eat in or take away.
bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri (rice balls) made with Niigata Uonuma-grown Koshihikari rice and Ariake-sea nori, offered in 20+ varieties (e.g. mentaiko, salted salmon, chirimen-zansho, soy-marinated egg yolk) with fillings and toppings developed under a food researcher's supervision; sides include tonjiru (pork miso soup) and karaage fried chicken.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name "Haruchan" comes from the proprietress's mother's name, positioning the onigiri as a literal taste of her mother's cooking.
The interior deliberately uses shoji windows, wooden nameplate-style menus, and a hinoki counter to create a warm, distinctly Japanese, old-house feel just steps from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station.

caveats: Menu is centered on fish/meat/egg fillings (mentaiko, salmon, chirimen-zansho, soy-sauce egg yolk), plus tonjiru and karaage sides — no gluten-free or vegan claims were found anywhere in the sources checked (official listing, gnavi/Tabelog search snippets, local blog feature), so this is included as a mom-and-pop cultural/rice-craft pick rather than a dietary-safe pick. Several aggregator pages (Hot Pepper, Retty, Hitosara, Gnavi, Rakuten, Note.com) returned 403/blocked and could not be directly fetched; facts below rely on Tabelog EN listing, WebSearch snippets of those same pages, and the taitobase.com feature article snippet. Hours/closed-day status conflict slightly (one aggregator snippet says no fixed closed day; Instagram bio reportedly says irregular closures) so hours_status is marked irregular.
closed_days: No fixed closed day listed by most sources, but treat as irregular — confirm via Instagram/phone before visiting.
cuisine: Handmade onigiri (rice balls) specialty shop using Niigata Koshihikari rice and Ariake nori
cultural_comfort_note: A small, Japanese-only neighborhood onigiri counter with no English menu confirmed in any source, so some pointing/simple Japanese may be needed to order.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled); address is 東京都台東区浅草2-13-4, on Kokusai-dori roughly 1-3 minutes' walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, which itself sits well within 0.8km of Sensō-ji — falls inside the Asakusa target circle by address/landmark description.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation were found in any source. Fillings include soy-sauce-marinated egg yolk (soy sauce typically contains wheat) and sides include karaage (fried chicken, typically flour/wheat-battered) and tonjiru (miso soup, miso can contain wheat/barley depending on type) — meaning cross-contact with wheat is likely throughout the kitchen. Plain salted rice balls may be closer to gluten-free by ingredient list alone, but shared prep surfaces/utensils are not confirmed safe. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 10:00-22:00 daily (per multiple listing aggregators); some sources note irregular/no-fixed closed day (不定休) rather than a guaranteed daily schedule — call ahead to confirm.
name_ja: 浅草おにぎりはるちゃん
neighborhood: Asakusa, Taito-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or protocol was found in any source; the menu's karaage (fried chicken) and soy-sauce-based fillings suggest wheat is present in the kitchen, creating cross-contact risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One listed filling is 卵黄しょうゆ漬け (soy-sauce-marinated egg yolk), and standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat unless specifically labeled tamari/gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core menu is built around fish (salmon, mentaiko/cod roe), egg, and meat-based sides (tonjiru pork soup, karaage fried chicken), so shared rice, utensils, and cooking surfaces are not confirmed free of animal-product contact even for a plain rice ball.

seats_text: Counter seating for 7 plus table seating for 8 (one source described the tables as 2-seat tables x 4); eat-in and takeout both available.
vegan_detail: No vegan claims found. The menu is centered on fish and meat fillings (salted salmon, mentaiko/cod roe) and animal-based sides (tonjiru pork soup, karaage fried chicken); dashi/stock ingredients for the soup are not detailed but Japanese tonjiru conventionally uses katsuo (bonito) or niboshi dashi. A plain salt-only onigiri might be achievable by request, but no dedicated vegan option, ingredient list, or staff accommodation process was confirmed in any source.

lead: 麺屋 我ガ 渋谷二丁目中央店
name_ja: 麺屋 我ガ 渋谷二丁目中央店
cuisine: Tonkotsu ramen (Fukuoka-style pork bone broth, 21+ hour simmer, Ichiran-lineage recipe)
neighborhood: Shibuya Ward, Shibuya 2-chome (near Miyamasuzaka, between Shibuya and Omotesando stations)
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-8-8
geocode_note: Network geocoding disabled per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding. Multiple sources describe the shop as an 8-minute walk from both Shibuya and Omotesando stations, placing it in Shibuya 2-chome, well within the Shibuya Scramble target radius; address not independently geocoded here.
seats_text: Small shop with counter seating (some sources cite 12, one blog cites 13 counting a couple of table seats); layout includes wall-facing counter seats and a couple of 2-person tables, designed with solo diners in mind.
hours_raw: 10:00-22:30, no regular closed days
closed_days: None reported (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket vending machine typical of Ichiran-lineage shops, and no English menu or allergy/dietary accommodation has been confirmed by any source.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-noodle tonkotsu ramen shop; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or allergy menu was found in any source. The tonkotsu broth itself is pork-bone based, but the shop's tare (seasoning base) and the aged spicy chili sauce are not confirmed wheat-free, and shoyu-based seasonings common to this style typically contain wheat. Celiac diners should not consider this a safe option.
vegan_detail: Every listed dish, including the vegetable-topped versions (e.g. moyashi/bean-sprout ramen, negi ramen), is built on the same 21-hour-simmered pork bone (tonkotsu) broth — there is no vegetable or plant-based broth alternative mentioned in any source. The gyoza and most toppings (chashu pork, ajitama egg) are also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegan diners.

roles: Fukuoka-origin ramen group; Tokyo/Shibuya branch of Menya Gaga
bio.origin: Ogori City, Fukuoka Prefecture (original honten founded 2013)
bio.background: Menya Gaga began in Ogori, Fukuoka in 2013 after its founder learned the recipe directly from Ichiran's founders, Sadayuki and Keiko Nakahara, reportedly opening on the same site where an early-generation Ichiran shop had once stood. The group expanded within Fukuoka (Ogori, Kurume, Tenjin) and to Bangkok, Thailand, before opening this Shibuya 2-chome shop on February 2, 2026 as its first Tokyo location.
bio.specialty: Ajitama ramen (味玉らーめん) — tonkotsu ramen topped with a seasoned soft-boiled egg; the shop's best-selling item
bio.philosophy: The stated aim is to preserve and carry forward the pork-bone ramen flavor inherited from Ichiran's founders: a broth simmered over 21 hours for concentrated umami and a creamy, odor-free mouthfeel, paired with a distinctive aged spicy sauce blending chili peppers and Aomori garlic. The Shibuya shop's interior pairs counter seating aimed at solo diners with calligraphy of four-character philosophical phrases (e.g. 行雲流水, 'drifting like clouds, flowing like water') and original jazz playing in the background.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's Tokyo debut was widely reported as carrying on the taste directly transmitted by Ichiran founders Nakahara Sadayuki and Keiko; the Ogori honten dates to 2013 and the group also runs shops in Kurume, Tenjin, and Bangkok.

Coverage of the Shibuya opening describes handmade water gyoza made by a chef with Chinese dim sum credentials, and a soup 'simmered for 21 hours or more, pursuing a creamy mouthfeel without any odor while maintaining concentrated umami and sweetness from pork bone.'
A blog write-up of the Shibuya shop describes the interior's counter seating for solo diners, wall calligraphy of philosophical four-character phrases, and jazz playing in the background.

caveats: Several source pages (Tabelog Japanese page, official site, ramendb, Retty, note.com blog, ikemen3 blog) returned HTTP 403 when fetched directly; details on hours, seats, address, and background were reconstructed from search-result summaries and secondary blog/news coverage instead of direct page fetches. Seat count varies slightly between sources (12 vs 13). Payment method at the ticket vending machine (cash-only vs card-accepting) could not be confirmed. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found for this restaurant from any source — it is a standard wheat-noodle, pork-broth tonkotsu ramen shop.

address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 パラシュートランドビル1F フードコートGO-FUN内 鶏味座YATAI

roles: Food-court branch of the Torimikura (鶏味座) chicken-specialty chain
bio.background: 鶏味座YATAI is the Tokyo Dome City food-court (GO-FUN) branch of Torimikura (鶏味座), a chicken-focused restaurant brand operated by Will International (ウイル・インターナショナル), whose full-service flagship restaurant is in Aoyama, Minato-ku.
bio.specialty: 究極の親子丼 (Ultimate Oyako-don) — chicken lightly simmered in the shop's original warishita sauce and topped with a carefully finished, silky egg
bio.philosophy: Across its locations the brand's stated focus is on treating chicken and egg with particular care — the tare (simmering sauce) the chicken cooks in and the texture of the egg finish — alongside crispy karaage and craft beer, sold in a fast, counter-service format at this food-court location.
bio.origin: Part of the wider 鶏味座 (Torimikura) brand, whose original restaurant is in Aoyama, Minato-ku

anecdotes.text: The brand's oyako-don technique, as described for the flagship store, involves simmering Tokyo jidori chicken in a tare built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi, then topping it with egg folded to a silky finish — the same 'ultimate oyako-don' concept is sold at this Tokyo Dome City stall.

At the Tokyo Dome City food court, the shop is described as offering the 'ultimate oyako-don' (chicken lightly simmered in an original sauce, finished with carefully prepared egg), karaage, and craft beer.

caveats: Target metadata listed the area as Chiyoda-ku, but verified sources place this shop inside Tokyo Dome City at 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 (Bunkyo-ku) — still an easy walk from Suidobashi Station, so it satisfies the radius requirement even though the ward name differs. This is a fast, counter-service food-court stall (part of a multi-location chicken-specialty chain, not an independent business) rather than a sit-down restaurant, and no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found anywhere — it is being logged honestly as a standard omnivore chicken/oyakodon shop, not a dietary-specialist pick. cash_only, reservation_required and seats were not explicitly confirmed by any source; cash_only=false and reservation_required=false reflect the normal operating pattern of a mall food-court counter stall (card payment accepted, walk-up ordering, no reservations), not a directly sourced statement.
closed_days: None found — reported as open every day (年中無休); follows the general Tokyo Dome City / GO-FUN food-court operating schedule
cuisine: 親子丼・から揚げ・鶏料理
cultural_comfort_note: It's an order-at-counter food-court stall inside a major tourist complex (Tokyo Dome City), so basic Japanese or pointing at photos should be enough, though no English menu was confirmed by any source.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free option was mentioned in any source found. The karaage (fried chicken) is a standard Japanese-style deep-fried item, typically coated in wheat flour/starch, and the signature oyako-don's tare (simmering sauce) is a proprietary sauce that in standard Japanese cooking is soy-sauce based (soy sauce normally contains wheat) — no gluten-free alternative was identified. Treat as not celiac-safe absent direct confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 (daily, food-court hours)
neighborhood: 文京区後楽 (Tokyo Dome City / Suidōbashi)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature oyako-don is finished with the shop's original tare (simmering sauce); no gluten-free or wheat-free tare/soy sauce was mentioned in any source, and Japanese tare of this kind is typically soy-sauce based (soy sauce normally contains wheat).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The brand's signature broth/tare is described (for the technique used across its stores) as built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi, meaning fish stock underlies core menu items such as the oyako-don.

seats_text: Food-court counter stall with shared GO-FUN food-court seating; no dedicated seat count found in any source
geocode_note: Address confirmed via web search as inside Tokyo Dome City, 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 パラシュートランドビル1F フードコートGO-FUN — a short walk from Suidōbashi Station (well within 0.8km). lat/lng left null per instructions since network geocoding is blocked; downstream geocoding should use the address_ja above.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options were identified. The signature dish is chicken and egg based (oyako-don), and the brand's core tare/broth recipe is described as built on bonito, mackerel, and horse-mackerel dashi (fish stock), making it unsuitable for vegan diners. No plant-based menu items were found in any source.

lead: 東京ギョーザスタンドウーロン 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-36-14 KAWANO ANNEX 3F

bio.origin: Tachikawa (original shop), expanded to Tokyo Station Gransta, Nihonbashi, and Shinjuku
bio.specialty: Hand-wrapped pan-fried gyoza (焼餃子) and water-boiled gyoza (水餃子), including cilantro and shiso variations, paired with an original house craft beer brewed with oolong tea leaves
bio.philosophy: A 'neo machi-chuka' (new-style neighborhood Chinese) izakaya concept — casual town-Chinese cooking made approachable for a quick meal or a few after-work drinks
bio.background: Part of the Tokyo Gyoza Stand Oolong group, which began as a popular gyoza shop in Tachikawa and later expanded into Tokyo Station's Gransta mall and Nihonbashi before this Shinjuku 3-chome branch opened on April 30, 2026, one minute on foot from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit.

anecdotes.text: The gyoza recipes were developed in collaboration with a Michelin-experienced chef and a Chinese dim sum specialist, served alongside 'Uron Ale,' a house craft beer brewed using oolong tea leaves sourced from China's Fujian province.
The Shinjuku 3-chome branch opened April 30, 2026 as a 'neo machi-chuka' izakaya with pan-fried gyoza as its signature dish, also offering water-boiled, cilantro, and shiso gyoza varieties.

caveats: Newly opened (April 30, 2026) chain location of the Tokyo Gyoza Stand Oolong group; not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment. Gyoza wrappers and much of the menu are wheat-based, and no vegan dumpling or dedicated allergen accommodations were found in available sources.
closed_days: None found — appears to operate daily with no regular closed days
cuisine: 中華・ぎょうざ (Chinese / gyoza-izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, casual chain izakaya right by Shinjuku Station's southeast exit; ordering is straightforward with a simple, visual gyoza-and-beer menu, but no confirmed dedicated English menu.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked). Address places it one minute on foot from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit (新宿駅東南口), well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station (35.6896,139.7006).
gf_detail: This is a gyoza specialty restaurant — wrappers, dumpling fillings, noodle dishes (中華そば), and typical Chinese-izakaya seasonings (soy sauce, sauces) are wheat-based by nature. No gluten-free wrapper option, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners without prior direct confirmation from the restaurant.

neighborhood: 新宿 - 新宿駅東口 歌舞伎町
seats_text: Not published in available sources

lead: ファラフェルブラザーズ 渋谷パルコ店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町15-1 渋谷パルコ 7F

bio.origin: Israeli street food, brought to Japan
bio.background: Falafel Brothers was founded in 2017 by Israeli entrepreneur Amnon Agassy together with Japanese chef Daisuke Tsukahara. The first shop opened on Imoaraizaka in Roppongi, a neighborhood with a large foreign-resident community that already knew falafel, before the brand grew into a small Tokyo group with additional locations in Ebisu, Otemachi, Harajuku and the Shibuya PARCO branch (7th floor).
bio.specialty: Falafel pita wraps/sandwiches made from hand-formed chickpea falafel, plus rice-bowl plates and vegan sweets
bio.philosophy: Bringing casual, plant-based Israeli street food to Japan in an accessible, fully vegan form, using organic ingredients where possible.

anecdotes.text: The brand's first shop opened in 2017 on Roppongi's Imoaraizaka, a district chosen for its concentration of foreign residents already familiar with falafel, before the concept expanded toward a broader Japanese audience.
Falafel Brothers describes itself as bringing an easy, casual taste of Israeli food and vegan cooking to Japan.

caveats: Reported operating hours differ slightly across secondary sources (some list Mon-Thu 11:00-21:30/Fri-Sat 11:00-22:00, others Mon-Thu 11:00-21:00/Fri-Sun 11:00-22:00), likely because the shop follows Shibuya PARCO's own irregular seasonal hours — confirm before visiting. Gluten-free status is "options" only: the falafel itself is described as chickpea-based with no wheat-flour binder, and a rice-plate option is recommended for gluten-free diners, but sauce ingredients, shared-fryer status, and cross-contamination handling were not confirmed on any fetchable primary source, so celiac diners should double-check with staff. Seat count could not be confirmed.
closed_days: 不定休（渋谷PARCOの営業日・営業時間に準ずる）
cuisine: ファラフェル・イスラエル料理・ヴィーガン (Falafel / Israeli / Vegan)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside Shibuya PARCO, a major tourist-facing shopping complex, the shop is described as offering English-language menu support, so English-speaking visitors should find it easy to navigate.
gf_detail: The falafel itself is chickpea-based and made without a wheat-flour binder, and a gluten-free-friendly rice plate is offered as an alternative to the pita/bread sandwiches. However, the restaurant has not published confirmation about wheat in its sauces/dressings, shared-fryer use, or kitchen cross-contamination practices, so this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen — celiac and severely gluten-sensitive diners should confirm specifics with staff before ordering.
hours_raw: 月〜木 11:00-21:30、金・土 11:00-22:00（日曜は平日と同様の時間帯という情報もあり、渋谷PARCOの営業時間に準ずるため変動あり）
name_ja: ファラフェルブラザーズ 渋谷パルコ店
neighborhood: 渋谷/渋谷パルコ7F（渋谷スクランブル交差点より徒歩約4分）

positives.text: The entire menu is plant-based/vegan (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and the brand is described as using organic ingredients and being kosher-certified, reducing risk of hidden animal-derived ingredients such as dashi or bonito for vegan diners.
The falafel is chickpea-based with no wheat-flour binder, and the rice-plate menu option is noted as popular among gluten-free diners.

vegan_detail: The restaurant is a fully vegan concept: all menu items (falafel wraps/sandwiches, salad bowls, rice plates, sweets) are plant-based, with no meat, fish, dairy, egg, or honey. Sources describe the brand as using organic ingredients and holding kosher certification, which lowers the likelihood of hidden animal-derived additions like fish-based dashi, though exact sauce/dressing ingredient lists were not independently verified from a fetchable primary source.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町16-14 パティオI 1F

bio.background: BiOcafe describes itself as one of Japan's first organic cafe-restaurants built around 100% pesticide-free, chemical-fertilizer-free vegetables sourced from a small group of farmers chosen for sharing the owner's commitment to health and beauty. The concept is summed up on the official site as "食べてキレイになる" ("Eat to become beautiful").
bio.specialty: Gluten-free veggie burger — a bun made from rice flour and konjac flour (no wheat) filled with plant-based "veggie meat" and all plant-based sauces — alongside a monthly-changing gluten-free lunch course and gluten-free karaage.
bio.philosophy: Built around organic, additive-free vegetables and recipes reworked to be gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan where possible, so that health-conscious and diet-restricted guests can eat freely; the cafe also pours organic Italian wines chosen personally by the owner.

anecdotes.text: Lunch guests are served an all-you-can-eat salad bar of organic Bio vegetables together with homemade bread, soup, and detox water alongside the gluten-free/vegan main dishes.
Described in 2025 coverage as a cafe-and-bakery on Spain-zaka that's especially popular with vegan diners in Shibuya.

caveats: Hours details vary slightly by listing (the mid-afternoon "cafe time" window is given as either 15:30-18:00 or 11:00-17:00 in different sources), so treat the exact cafe-time boundary as approximate; the overall 11:00-23:00 span is consistent across sources. No source documents a dedicated gluten-free fryer/kitchen or explicit soy-sauce/dashi wheat policy — celiac-level diners should confirm cross-contact precautions directly with staff. Several source pages (Tabelog, biocafe.jp, Vegewel, Kumagai Corp brand page) returned 403 on direct fetch in this session, so facts from those domains are drawn from search-engine summaries of their content rather than a direct page fetch.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed day; open daily per available listings)
cuisine: Organic Cafe - Gluten-free, Vegan-friendly
cultural_comfort_note: A modern organic cafe in central Shibuya that draws international vegetarian/vegan visitors, but menus and service appear to be primarily in Japanese, so basic Japanese or translation help is useful.
gf_detail: Offers a monthly-changing dedicated gluten-free lunch plus standing gluten-free items such as a veggie burger with a bun made from rice flour and konjac flour (no wheat) and gluten-free karaage. However, the same lunch service includes a self-serve salad buffet with homemade (wheat) bread and soup in the same area, and no source confirms a dedicated fryer or full wheat-free kitchen separation, so this is well-suited to gluten-sensitive diners but celiac-level guests should confirm cross-contact handling directly with staff.
geocode_note: Address (Udagawa-cho 16-14, Shibuya-ku), a ~4-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station near Spain-zaka, is well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble reference point. Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled) — lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding.
neighborhood: Shibuya (Udagawa-cho / Spain-zaka), near Shibuya Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: The lunch service pairs gluten-free/vegan mains with a self-serve salad buffet that also includes homemade (wheat) bread and soup in the same buffet area, a potential cross-contact point for celiac-level guests.

positives.text: Vegetables used across the menu are 100% pesticide-free and grown without chemical fertilizer, sourced from farmers chosen for sharing the cafe's health-focused standards.

The gluten-free veggie burger replaces the wheat bun with one made from rice flour and konjac flour and uses only plant-based sauces and "veggie meat," making it fully suitable for a vegan diner avoiding wheat.
Runs a standing monthly-changing healthy lunch explicitly marketed as gluten-free, vegetarian, and vegan.

seats_text: About 30 seats
vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly dishes are available on request/menu, including a vegan veggie burger, brown rice pasta, and lasagna, built on the same organic, pesticide-free vegetable base used throughout the menu. No source directly confirms whether dashi/bonito stock is avoided across all dishes, so vegans should ask specifically which items are prepared without fish-derived dashi.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神南1-15-12 佐藤ビル2F

bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.background: Opened in 2009 in Shibuya's Jinnan neighborhood. According to ramen-industry coverage, the owner trained for years at AFURI and ZUND-BAR — ramen shops affiliated with the celebrated Nakamuraya lineage — before striking out on his own with a chemical-seasoning-free (無化調) shoyu ramen concept.
bio.specialty: Assari (light) and Kotteri (rich) shoyu ramen, and tsukemen with thick, chewy noodles — each built on a double stock combining a full day's worth of simmered pork bone, chicken, aromatic vegetables and black soybeans with a bonito and dried-sardine seafood stock.
bio.philosophy: "Gentle ramen for the body" (体に優しいらーめんを) — no MSG or artificial seasonings, relying on natural ingredients for umami.
roles: owner's wife: pastry chef (formerly at ZUND-BAR), makes the shop's desserts

anecdotes.text: The owner trained extensively at AFURI and ZUND-BAR, sister shops connected to Nakamuraya, developing his soup-making craft before opening Kugatsudo.
The owner's wife, previously a pastry chef at ZUND-BAR, hand-makes the shop's desserts, giving Kugatsudo its identity as a combined 'ramen and sweets' cafe.

caveats: Recent monthly hours-announcement posts on the official site (spanning Feb–June 2026) indicate the owner has been adjusting hours, possibly for health/recovery reasons; a June 20, 2026 note confirms the shop was still open until 21:00, but exact current-day hours should be checked on the official site or @Kugatsudo on X before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source — this is a traditional wheat-noodle, meat-and-fish-stock ramen shop, not suited to celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日)
cuisine: Ramen & Tsukemen
cultural_comfort_note: A small, independently run neighborhood ramen shop with a Japanese-language menu and no confirmed English signage or English-speaking staff in any source found.
gf_detail: The ramen and tsukemen use standard wheat noodles, and the shoyu (soy sauce) tare is soy-sauce based. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu option is mentioned by the shop or in any press coverage found — not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
geocode_note: Address is 神南1-15-12 (Jinnan 1-chome), immediately north of Shibuya Station and well within the Shibuya Scramble 0.8km target radius (roughly an 8-minute walk), consistent with the target's stated location.
hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 daily on days open (or until soup sells out); closed Sunday. Note: multiple 2026 announcements on the official site describe adjusted/shortened hours in some months — check the official site or X (@Kugatsudo) for the current schedule.
neighborhood: Jinnan, Shibuya
seats_text: Small counter/table seating in a bright, cafe-like interior (exact seat count not confirmed in sources).
vegan_detail: Both ramen options ("assari"/light and "kotteri"/rich) use a double stock — an animal stock of pork bone, chicken, aromatic vegetables and black soybeans, combined with a bonito (katsuo) and dried-sardine (niboshi) seafood stock. The tsukemen dipping sauce is similarly meat/fish-based. No vegetable-stock, plant-based broth, or vegan menu item is described in any source found — not suitable for vegan diners.

cuisine: Regional Japanese izakaya (郷土料理) housed in a Showa-era kominka (old private house); sashimi, jizake (local sake), kuroton (black pork) shabu-shabu, and a rotating menu of dishes sourced from across Japan's regions, including vegetable-forward items (corn tempura, Kyoto-style rolled egg, etc.)
neighborhood: 新宿西口

closed_days: Saturdays and Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood izakaya with an English-language Tabelog listing but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is a good idea.
vegan_detail: As a standard izakaya, most cooked dishes rely on bonito-based dashi and typical seasonings, and the menu centers on sashimi and pork shabu-shabu; some individual vegetable dishes (tempura, salads, tofu-based items) may be vegetable-only but are not confirmed vegan-prepared (broth, sauces, and shared fryers likely contain animal products). No dedicated vegan menu or vegan certification was found — best to ask staff dish-by-dish.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or celiac-safety information was found in any source. Standard izakaya risk factors apply: soy sauce (wheat), tempura batter (wheat, and likely a shared fryer), and dashi/sauces that may contain wheat-based ingredients. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-6321-4385) and confirm specific dishes and fryer use rather than assuming safety.
caveats: Secondary sources show some inconsistency in reported hours (a few listings mention evening-only or Monday-closed schedules that appear to describe other 十徳-brand branches, such as the separate 新宿本店 a few blocks away at 西新宿1-5-12). The hours given here reflect the two most consistent, cross-corroborated sources specific to this kominka location; calling ahead is recommended. No gluten-free or vegan-specific protocols could be confirmed from any source — flagged for a phone check before visiting.

roles: owner/operator under the 酒処十徳 (Sake-dokoro Juttoku) regional-izakaya brand
bio.background: The restaurant occupies a Showa-era kominka (old private house) in Nishi-Shinjuku, one block from Shinjuku Station's west exit, with its original early-Showa building fabric largely intact rather than gutted for a modern fit-out. It is one of several Tokyo branches run under the 十徳 (Juttoku) name, a group built around sourcing regional Japanese sake and countryside cooking.
bio.specialty: Kuroton (premium black pork) shabu-shabu and sashimi platters, served alongside a rotating lineup of dishes drawn from regional Japanese cuisine (郷土料理) — described in listings as a 'tour of Japan's local flavors.'
bio.philosophy: The brand's stated focus is on hunting down jizake (local, small-brewery sake) directly from breweries and tasting events, pairing it with regional home-style cooking, and extending that same care to the ingredients, cooking, room, and service — omotenashi hospitality built around sake discovery.

anecdotes.text: The cellar holds 70-plus types of sake, shochu, and umeshu, including sought-after labels like Dassai, Hiroki, and Jikon, sourced by staff visiting breweries and regional tasting events; brewers themselves are said to occasionally stop by the shop.
A food blogger described it as a quiet, easy-to-get-a-seat izakaya tucked on a back street near Shinjuku's west exit, good for a quick, unhurried drink.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町30-18 メイサ南平台1F

bio.origin: Nepal
bio.background: Opened in Shibuya's Sakuragaoka backstreet in 2010 by a Nepalese owner-chef known to regulars as Padam-san, Nepalico set out to serve genuine Nepali home cooking rather than a Japanese-ified curry-shop version, in a small dining room decorated with photos and crafts from Nepal.
bio.specialty: Dal Bhat — the everyday Nepali meal of dal (lentil soup), bhat (rice), tarkari (vegetable or meat curry) and achar (pickle); ordered at lunch it comes with free refills of the dal soup and rice.
bio.philosophy: To introduce ordinary Japanese diners — not just curry enthusiasts — to the real, comforting flavor of Nepali family cooking: mild, spiced with imported Nepali spices, and closer to a home-cooked set meal than a restaurant curry.

anecdotes.text: Owner Padam-san runs the restaurant with the aim of introducing everyday Japanese diners, not curry specialists, to authentic Nepali home cooking.
The lunchtime Dal Bhat set comes with free refills of the dal soup and a free rice size-up, plus a small salad and mini chai.

caveats: Gluten-free and vegan claims are inferred, not confirmed by the restaurant: no source found explicitly stating dedicated gluten-free procedures, a dedicated fryer, or whether ghee/dairy is used in the vegetarian curries. The vegetarian Dal Bhat set (dal soup, tarkari, two achar/pickles, rice) is naturally free of meat and of the obvious Japanese gluten carriers (soy sauce/dashi are not used in Nepali cooking), but the menu also includes wheat-based items (e.g., naan/momo-style dishes per menu listings), so cross-contact in a small shared kitchen cannot be ruled out. Diners with celiac disease should confirm preparation directly with staff.
closed_days: Sundays
cuisine: Nepalese (home-style Dal Bhat, curry, momo)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, independent Nepali-owned neighborhood restaurant popular with Japanese office workers at lunch; the menu and staff are geared to Japanese speakers, but the cuisine and owner are Nepali-immigrant background, so simple English or pointing at the menu photos should get by.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment); address confirms location in Sakuragaoka-cho, Shibuya, roughly a 4-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station's west/Inokashira exit, i.e. well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free protocols. The vegetarian Dal Bhat set (dal lentil soup, rice, vegetable tarkari, two achar pickles) is naturally free of wheat and does not rely on Japanese gluten carriers like soy sauce or dashi, since this is a Nepali kitchen, not a Japanese one. However, the broader menu includes wheat-containing items, and there is no stated dedicated fryer or confirmed avoidance of cross-contact in the shared kitchen — ask staff before ordering if you have celiac disease.
neighborhood: Sakuragaoka-cho, Shibuya (behind Cerulean Tower, near Nampeidai)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No published information on dedicated gluten-free prep areas or cross-contamination controls; ask staff directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No published information confirming whether the vegetarian Dal Bhat is prepared without ghee/dairy or shares cookware with meat curries; ask staff directly.

positives.text: The Dal Bhat vegetarian set (dal soup, tarkari, two achar, rice) replaces the meat curry with extra vegetable/pickle sides, and at lunch the dal soup and rice are refilled free of charge.

seats_text: Small dining room, around 26 seats (counter plus a few tables)
vegan_detail: A vegetarian Dal Bhat set (no meat curry, swapped for extra achar/pickles) is regularly offered at both lunch and dinner. It is not confirmed vegan: Nepali curries commonly use ghee (clarified butter) and dairy is common in Nepali cooking, and no source states the vegetarian set is dairy-free. Treat as vegetarian-friendly with vegan needing direct confirmation from staff.

bio.background: 麦ノ音 (Mugi no Oto) opened March 14, 2019 in Kabukicho, Shinjuku, as a live-music dining bar ("mixed restaurant") combining craft beer, natural wine, and house-made sausage. It shares its building/space concept with an attached reservation-only sushi counter (へいらっしゃい) and a concept cafe (ひみつきち屋さん).
bio.specialty: "Ratzi's Organic Sausage" — a handmade sausage sourced from Sausage Fest, a Bib Gourmand (Michelin Guide) restaurant in Kumamoto run by Czech chef Ladislav Hajžman, who spent eight years as manager of the Dutch embassy residence in Tokyo before turning to sausage-making. It is seasoned simply with just salt, pepper, garlic, and herbs, and served alongside smoked bacon and six rotating craft beer taps.
bio.philosophy: Owner Watanabe said he wanted to offer dining-out experiences unique to a restaurant setting — live musician performances, craft beer, and house-made sausage — and expressed hope that overseas visitors would enjoy it too.

anecdotes.text: The signature dish, Ratzi's Organic Sausage, comes from Kumamoto restaurant Sausage Fest, helmed by Czech chef Ladislav Hajžman, a former eight-year manager of the Dutch embassy residence in Tokyo turned sausage maker; the sausage is kept simple with only salt, pepper, garlic and herbs.
On opening, owner Watanabe said he wanted to create restaurant-only experiences — live music, craft beer and house-made sausage — and hoped overseas tourists would enjoy it as well.

caveats: This is a craft-beer, natural-wine and organic-sausage dining bar with live music in Kabukicho — a meat- and beer-centric venue, not a place marketed with any gluten-free or vegan accommodations; no such mentions turned up in any source checked. Sources also disagree slightly on Monday: one listing gives shortened Monday hours (18:00-23:00) while another labels Monday as the weekly closed day — confirm before a Monday visit. Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, the official site (mugioto.com), and several other listing pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so facts here are drawn from WebSearch result snippets of those pages (PR Times press release, TV Tokyo Plus food feature, Shinjuku Keizai news article, Hotpepper/Tabelog aggregation) rather than direct page fetches.
closed_days: Reported inconsistently: one source lists 毎週月曜日 (closed every Monday) while another gives shortened Monday hours (18:00-23:00) rather than a closure. Treat as effectively closed or very limited on Mondays; confirm directly.
cuisine: Bistro / dining bar — organic house-made sausages, craft beer (6 rotating taps), natural wine, with live musician performances
cultural_comfort_note: A modern Kabukicho dining bar geared toward adults with live music and online reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Ikyu); the owner has expressed hope that overseas visitors enjoy it, but no English menu or English-speaking-staff claim was confirmed, so basic Japanese or gestures may be needed.

roles: Italian restaurant, part of the AWkitchen family of vegetable-focused pasta restaurants
bio.background: AWkitchen began in 2004 in Omotesando/Aoyama as a restaurant built around eating vegetables. "Figlia" is Italian for "daughter," and this location — the brand's daughter outpost — opened on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square in 2019, moving the concept from its original Aoyama roots into the new landmark tower directly connected to Shibuya Station.
bio.origin: Omotesando/Aoyama, Tokyo (original AWkitchen concept, founded 2004)
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's guiding idea is pairing housemade fresh pasta with an abundance of vegetables sourced from a nationwide network of contracted farmers, changing seasonally so guests taste different vegetable combinations through the year.
bio.specialty: AW Farm Bagna Cauda (農園バーニャカウダ) — a signature dish of vegetables from the restaurant's partner farms served with a warm dipping sauce, paired with a rotating selection of handmade fresh pasta.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's farm bagna cauda has become widely shared on social media and is considered the signature draw of the Shibuya Scramble Square location.
AWkitchen's original Aoyama/Omotesando shop, the concept's birthplace, was known as a beloved 'restaurant where you eat vegetables' before the brand expanded to Shibuya, Ebisu and other locations.

caveats: Direct page fetches (Tabelog, official site, booking sites) were blocked by network/proxy policy in this environment (HTTP 403 on nearly every relevant domain); all facts below are drawn from search-result snippets of those same pages rather than full-page fetches, so menu-level and allergy-specific detail is thinner than usual. No gluten-free or vegan-specific claims could be confirmed from any source — this is a vegetable-forward Italian restaurant with wheat-based fresh pasta, not a GF/vegan-dedicated kitchen.

bio.origin: 文京区後楽（水道橋駅東口徒歩1分）
bio.background: Okkon is a small, deeply personal Hiroshima-okonomiyaki counter run by an owner who moonlights as a professional-wrestling mask craftsman and match referee. The basement shop, opposite the Tokyo Dome Hotel, doubles as a shrine to two passions: the walls and stairwell are covered in pro-wrestling posters and hand-made masks, and the room is decked out in Hiroshima Toyo Carp memorabilia, making it a magnet for Carp fans after a Tokyo Dome game.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built with cabbage, bean sprouts, pork and soba or udon noodles shipped in from Hiroshima, griddled crisp and finished with a thick sauce and a heavy scattering of scallions — the nikutama-soba (肉玉そば) and nikutama-udon (肉玉うどん), sometimes topped with squid tempura, are the most talked-about orders.
roles: pro-wrestling mask craftsman and referee (owner's side vocation)

anecdotes.text: Featured in the TV segment 'なりゆき街道旅' with actress Mayumi Wakamura visiting the Suidobashi shop to sample the Hiroshima okonomiyaki.
The owner is known in the pro-wrestling world as a mask-maker and referee, and the shop's decor and clientele reflect that double life alongside its Hiroshima Carp fandom.

caveats: The briefing area listed "千代田区後楽" but every source consistently places the shop at 東京都文京区後楽1-1-15 (Bunkyo-ku, not Chiyoda-ku) — this appears to be a minor error in the target brief, since Kōraku itself straddles the Bunkyo/Suidōbashi area and the shop is confirmed to be 1 minute on foot from JR Suidōbashi Station's east exit, well inside the 0.8km Suidōbashi radius. Several listing pages (official site, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Ekiten, Cookdoor) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so hours, seat count (53, per Gnavi-derived search snippet), and closed-day information are drawn from search-engine text extraction of those pages rather than a direct page read; treat seats and the exact closing time as reasonably confident but not fully primary-verified. No source of any kind mentions gluten-free or vegan accommodation — this is a standard wheat-batter/wheat-noodle Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop, so the no/no dietary rating is a straightforward inference from the dish composition, not a quote from the restaurant about allergies.
closed_days: No fixed closed day was found in any source; several directory listings show no closed-day entry, suggesting the shop may operate every day of the week, but this is unconfirmed.
cuisine: お好み焼き（広島焼き）— Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
cultural_comfort_note: A very local, character-driven okonomiyaki counter themed around pro wrestling and the Hiroshima Carp, with no English menu confirmed in any source, though it does appear on a couple of English-language booking listings.

No source mentions any gluten-free substitution, rice-noodle option, or celiac accommodation. This is not a suitable choice for a strict gluten-free or celiac diet.

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門2-10-5 1F

bio.origin: Founded in Akihabara, Tokyo in 2019 by Yoshiike, inspired by a personal trip to Finland.
bio.background: KIELO COFFEE began in Akihabara in 2019 after founder Yoshiike traveled to Finland and was struck by the Finnish sense of peace of mind, work-life balance, and the daily ritual of the coffee break. He felt Japan lacked the culture that produced that kind of happiness, and set out to build coffee shops that embody it. The brand has since grown to several Tokyo locations, including a dedicated vegan/gluten-free sister cafe (Be green by KIELO COFFEE) in Hongo. KIELO COFFEE ASAKUSA opened August 15, 2025, a short walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate, pairing the brand's Nordic sensibility (soft wood tones, a central communal table) with Japanese design touches like shoji screens.
bio.specialty: Light-roast, single-origin specialty coffee (roughly ten in-house-roasted origins) and a Nordic cinnamon roll, alongside carrot cake and other homemade pastries.
bio.philosophy: The name Kielo means 'lily of the valley' in Finnish, chosen to express the hope that happiness will return to everyday life; the brand's stated mission is to bring Finland's unhurried 'coffee break' culture into Japanese daily life.

anecdotes.text: Founder Yoshiike was drawn to Finland's sense of peace and quality of life during a visit there, and felt that the culture behind that happiness was exactly what Japan lacked — leading him to open KIELO's first shop in Akihabara in spring 2019.

caveats: Primarily a specialty-coffee and pastry cafe, not a dietary-dedicated kitchen — GF and vegan items are specific homemade sweets alongside a regular wheat/dairy pastry menu (e.g. the Nordic cinnamon roll), so cross-contact is not confirmed as controlled. Could not directly fetch the official site or Tabelog page (both returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch); all facts below are corroborated via web-search snippets of the official site, a local-news grand-opening release (Akiba Keizai), and independent blog coverage — none were invented.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (open daily); one press note mentioned irregular closures limited to its August 2025 opening month.
cuisine: Nordic-inspired specialty coffee and bakery cafe
cultural_comfort_note: Brand operates an English-language official site and an English Tabelog listing, and the cafe sits in the heart of the very tourist-facing Kaminarimon/Sensoji area.

gf_detail: Multiple sources (Akiba Keizai grand-opening release, independent cafe blogs) state KIELO COFFEE's homemade sweets lineup includes a gluten-free option made without wheat flour, alongside a vegan option — this appears to be a brand-wide practice also mentioned for the original Akihabara shop, not something confirmed specifically photographed/verified at the Asakusa branch. The same kitchen also bakes wheat-based items (e.g. the signature Nordic cinnamon roll), and no source describes separate GF prep equipment, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contact protocols — celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.

positives.text: Sweets are described as all homemade, with a gluten-free (no wheat flour) option and a vegan (no animal product) option specifically called out as part of the regular lineup — a deliberate dietary accommodation rather than an incidental one.

bio.background: 光醤 is one of a small cluster of Shibuya ramen shops (alongside らーめん金伝丸 and 男旭山) run by the same local operator out of the Dogenzaka/Shibuya Mark City area, rather than a national chain.
bio.specialty: Hakata-style tonkotsu-shoyu ramen (thin noodles, adjustable firmness) with unlimited free kaedama noodle refills
bio.philosophy: Build the bowl around genuinely good shoyu (soy sauce) rather than treating shoyu as an afterthought to the pork-bone broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, 光醤 (Hikari Hishio — 'light' + hishio, an old word for a fermented seasoning), is said to come from the owner being struck by how beautiful fresh soy sauce looked catching the light at a shoyu brewery, which inspired him to try to make what he considered Japan's best tonkotsu-shoyu ramen.

caveats: Tabelog carries two listings at this same address: the target ID 13069220 ("光醤"/Hikari Hishio) reads as the current, actively-reviewed listing (355+ reviews), while a separate, differently-named listing (13124685, "光醤 渋谷店") is explicitly marked [Closed] on Tabelog. Treated these as the same physical shop, with the target ID being the live listing. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source — this is a standard wheat-noodle, pork-bone-broth ramen shop, included here as a MOM_AND_POP / OMNI reference point rather than a dietary-safe pick.
