address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-2-63

bio.background: Busan is a newer concept from 株式会社のみもの (Nomimono Co., Ltd.), a Tokyo restaurant group best known for quirky specialty-noodle concepts such as "Curry is a Beverage" and "Why Put Chili Oil in Soba?". The shop opened on March 30, 2026 in Kanda-Ogawamachi, taking over the space that had previously housed the group's own "Why Put Chili Oil in Soba? Kanda-Ogawamachi" branch.
bio.specialty: Nikujiru udon (肉汁うどん) — a dipping-style udon that pairs smooth, firm Sanuki-style noodles with a hearty, vegetable- and pork-laden dipping broth in the Musashino udon tradition.
bio.philosophy: The name "Busan" (武讃) is a portmanteau of Musashino (武蔵野) and Sanuki (讃岐), reflecting the shop's premise of fusing Tokyo's rustic, home-style Musashino udon with the smoother, more refined noodle texture of Sanuki udon from Kagawa — an East-meets-West "hybrid udon" mashup.
roles: Udon specialty shop under the Nomimono restaurant group

anecdotes.text: For the two-day opening event on March 30-31, 2026, everyone who ordered a main noodle dish received a free deep-fried chikuwa (fish cake) tempura.

A diner described the noodles as slightly thin, soft Sanuki-style udon eaten in a hearty Musashino-style dipping broth, noting that the leftover udon-yu (noodle cooking water) is kombu (kelp) water, which tastes like kenchin-jiru when used to dilute the remaining broth.

One reviewer noted the dipping broth is so packed with pork and vegetables (eggplant, negi, shiitake) that there's barely room left to dip the noodles, and that a single thick "himokawa" flat noodle is included on top for fun.

caveats: Hours, seat count, and closed-days were sourced from Tabelog listing data surfaced via search (direct WebFetch to Tabelog and several news/blog pages returned 403 errors, so those pages could only be read via search-engine snippets rather than full-page fetch). Official restaurant website could not be confirmed — only the parent company's site (nomimono.co.jp) and its brand-listing pages were found, with no dedicated Busan page located. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; area confirmation relies on the address and known walking distances between Ogawamachi/Jimbocho and Suidobashi Station (roughly 700-900m by street route, so likely under 0.8km straight-line) rather than direct geocoding.
closed_days: None — open every day of the week (年中無休)
cuisine: Hybrid Udon (Musashino x Sanuki nikujiru/dipping udon)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at the entrance, and no English menu or signage has been reported by visitors.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address is 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-2-63, in the Ogawamachi/Jimbocho district of Chiyoda. Nearest stations reported are Ogawamachi Station (0 min walk), Jimbocho Station (3 min), Awajicho Station (3 min), and Shin-Ochanomizu Station (3 min); the walking route between Jimbocho Station and Suidōbashi Station is commonly cited as roughly 700-900m, and Ogawamachi 3-chome sits on the Suidōbashi-facing side of that stretch, so the address is judged to be within (or very close to) the 0.8km target radius of Suidōbashi Station.
gf_detail: The entire menu is built around udon, i.e., wheat-flour noodles (both the Sanuki-style noodle and the housemade Musashino-style flat "ichi-tanki" noodle), so there is no gluten-free noodle option. The dipping broth (tsuyu) is also almost certainly seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based, adding a second source of gluten. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found.

bio.background: Opened on March 16, 2015 by an owner couple who took over an existing izakaya space in the Nishi-Shinjuku backstreets, a few minutes' walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, and had it redesigned around reclaimed old-house timber.
bio.specialty: Shuzo teishoku (the daily changing set meal) - rotating home-style dishes such as crispy chicken nanban with housemade tartar sauce, a shirasu (whitebait) rice bowl, pork shabu-shabu, or salmon sashimi, served with miso soup and a choice of rice portion.
bio.philosophy: Built around the idea of 'banshaku to banmeshi' (an evening drink and an evening meal) - a small, unpretentious neighborhood counter where someone can drop in alone for a drink or settle in at a table with company.

anecdotes.text: The interior was redesigned by Nagano-based Sansui-sha using 'kobitsu' - reclaimed pillars and beams salvaged from century-old dismantled farmhouses - worked into the existing layout of the space to give the small room a warmer, wood-lined feel.

Tabelog reviewers describe it as 'one of the shops I want to become a regular at,' noting the shop is often full at dinner and praising the daily teishoku, the housemade tartar sauce on the chicken nanban, and the free choice of rice portion.

caveats: Payment methods (cash-only vs. card) could not be confirmed with certainty - several reviewers mention paying cash but no source explicitly rules out cards, so call ahead (03-3369-4722) to check. Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (roughly a dozen counter + table seats). No gluten-free or vegan information was found anywhere for this shop; it reads as a traditional teishoku-ya/izakaya (fried, breaded, dashi- and soy-based home cooking) with no indication of allergy-specific accommodation, so treat the GF/vegan ratings here as conservative defaults rather than a confirmed no.

cuisine: 居酒屋・和食（定食）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, Japanese-only neighborhood counter spot with no English menu or site found, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help when ordering.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi were mentioned in any source. The signature dishes (chicken nanban is breaded and deep-fried with a wheat-based tartar sauce; teishoku sets rely on standard soy sauce and dashi) point to wheat throughout the menu, with no evidence of substitutions. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from the shop by phone.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:30-23:00 (Mon-Fri); closed Sat, Sun, and public holidays
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (Shinjuku Nishiguchi), Shinjuku
seats_text: Small counter-plus-table izakaya; sources disagree on the exact count (roughly a dozen seats between counter and two table sections).

No vegetarian or vegan option, dashi substitution, or plant-based mention appears in any source, so it should be treated as not accommodating for vegan diners without calling ahead.

cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles) and tempura

Lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding); pin should be derived downstream from the address.
seats_text: One secondhand source cited roughly 70 seats for the shop, but this was not clearly confirmed as specific to the main store (honten) versus the family's branch location, so an exact count is not stated with confidence.

closed_days: Friday
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, tourist-frequented shop steps from Sensoji with an English-language Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.
vegan_detail: The signature tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is built on bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, per the restaurant's own description of its "trio" of broth, tempura, and noodles — this is an animal-based stock used throughout the menu. No vegan-adapted broth or dish was found advertised anywhere, so vegan and vegetarian diners should not expect accommodation without directly and skeptically confirming with staff.
gf_detail: No source confirms a 100%-buckwheat (juwari/生粉打ち) noodle, a gluten-free menu, or a dedicated gluten-free fryer here, despite specific searches for these terms. The restaurant markets its noodles as made from premium domestic (Shinshu-centered) soba flour, but does not state the buckwheat-to-wheat ratio, and traditional soba shops commonly use a wheat binder (nihachi-style) unless juwari is explicitly advertised. The tempura batter is standard wheat-flour batter, and the soy-based tsuyu is likely to contain wheat via shoyu, though this specific fact was not independently confirmed for this shop. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-3845-4500) or ask staff in person about the buckwheat ratio and kitchen handling before ordering — do not assume this is a safe gluten-free option.
caveats: Underlying facts were gathered via WebSearch result snippets (which quote and cite the source pages) because direct WebFetch access to tabelog.com, owariya-asakusa.com, e-asakusa.jp, retty.me, and even unrelated control domains (e.g. en.wikipedia.org) returned HTTP 403 in this environment — the outbound proxy's egress policy appears to be blocking these hosts at the CONNECT level, not the sites themselves. No dietary claim was invented; where confirmation was unavailable, the record says so explicitly rather than assuming the "GF" framing implied by the assignment brief.

bio.origin: Kaminarimon-dori, Asakusa, Taito-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: Founded in 1860 (Man'en 1, the last year of the Edo period) on Kaminarimon-dori near Sensoji Temple, Owariya has been run by the same family for six generations across more than 160 years, serving Asakusa locals and Sensoji pilgrims. According to the shop's own account, the name comes from Shinmon Tatsugoro — the Edo-era fire brigade chief who also served as a gatekeeper of Sensoji — who suggested a name evoking the vigorous 'spread of a shrimp's tail' when the founder left firefighting to open the restaurant. The shop displays a 'Sensoji Goyo' (Official Purveyor to Sensoji Temple) sign, reflecting its long-standing ties to the temple.
bio.specialty: Tempura soba (天ぷらそば) topped with two oversized shrimp tempura, fried in specially sourced sesame oil, so large they spill over the edge of the bowl
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its aim as 'a flavor you don't tire of even eating it every day,' built on what it calls a trio of essentials: a bonito-based tsuyu, shrimp tempura fried in specially ordered sesame oil, and firm noodles made from top-grade domestic (Shinshu-centered) buckwheat flour.

anecdotes.text: The novelist Nagai Kafu, a devoted Asakusa regular, is said to have eaten here every single day for a year. He always took the same reserved back seat, and the proprietress was instructed to serve him kashiwa nanban (chicken soba) without even taking his order once tea was poured. He ate in near-silence and left payment in coins lined up neatly along the edge of the table before leaving.

A photograph of Kafu eating at the restaurant — taken secretly by a fan, since the famously camera-shy writer disliked being photographed — was later given to the shop and still hangs inside as a memento of his patronage.

lead: 博多串焼き・野菜巻き工房 渋谷宮益坂のごりょんさん
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷1-6-3 ヴィラファーストビル渋谷 B1F

bio.background: Goryon-san is operated by Basix Co., Ltd., a Tokyo restaurant group led by representative director Hiroshi Iwasawa (known as "Gan-san") that also runs the atmospheric izakaya brands Teyan-tei, Jomon, and Meatman. The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka branch opened on April 19 in a deliberately low-visibility spot — reached via a stairway down to a basement level with no prominent signage — reflecting the group's stated approach of building each shop's individuality around food and hospitality rather than location, signage, or a manual-driven service style.
bio.origin: Hakata (Fukuoka) izakaya cuisine
bio.philosophy: The shop's name, "Goryon-san," is a term of affection used in Hakata for a household's proprietress — a woman admired for her charm, virtue, kindness, and cooking skill during Hakata festivals. The restaurant frames its hospitality and cooking around that warm, home-style image.
bio.specialty: Yasai-maki-gushi (野菜巻き串) — skewers of fresh vegetables wrapped in domestic pork belly and grilled; other signature items include steamed gyoza and Hakata motsunabe (offal hot pot).
roles: Operated by Basix Co., Ltd., under representative director Hiroshi Iwasawa

anecdotes.text: The shop was deliberately opened on a quiet side street with no signage, reached down a basement stairway, as part of parent company Basix's philosophy of pursuing each shop's individual appeal through food and hospitality rather than location or manuals.

caveats: Third-party listing pages (Tabelog, Retty, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, official owst.jp site, Yahoo Loco, Autoreserve, TableCheck) could not be directly fetched in this environment — outbound connections to those hosts were blocked at the network policy level (403 on CONNECT), so this record is built from WebSearch result snippets and one directly-fetched article (food-stadium.com), not full page reads. Business hours vary slightly between cached listings (17:00–23:30 vs 17:30–24:00); 17:00–23:30 daily with no regular closed day is used here as the more frequently attested figure. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found for this restaurant in any source — it is a meat-forward Hakata izakaya (pork-belly-wrapped vegetable skewers, gyoza, motsunabe) with no indication of dietary accommodation, so GF/vegan diners should not rely on this listing without calling ahead. Payment method (cash-only or cards) was not confirmed in any source.

There is no mention of a dedicated fryer, gluten-free soy sauce, or allergy protocol. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.

The rest of the menu (motsunabe offal hot pot, other pork/chicken skewers) is similarly meat-centered, and Japanese izakaya sauces/stocks routinely use bonito-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu, labeling, or options were found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-2-13 八千代ビル1F

bio.background: Opened in February 2024 a few minutes from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, run as one of roughly 100 restaurants produced by the Osaka-based SASAYA Holdings restaurant group.
bio.specialty: "Takaya soba" — dark, coarsely-ground hand-cut buckwheat noodles served with natural salt from the Shirakami mountain range in place of the usual dipping sauce, so the buckwheat's own flavor comes through.
bio.philosophy: Buckwheat is sourced by region and season for the hand-cut soba, paired with charcoal-grilled yakitori made from a rare chicken breed and a rotating list of 40-plus types of junmai sake.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature dish is a dark, coarsely-ground hand-cut soba eaten with Shirakami-range natural salt instead of tsuyu dipping sauce, letting diners taste the noodle itself.
Described as a hideaway sake bar ("隠れ家的日本酒酒場") built around hand-cut soba, seasonal small plates, and sake from across Japan.

caveats: Primary sources (Tabelog, official gorp.jp site, gnavi, hitosara) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so all facts below are drawn from Google/web-search result snippets that quote or summarize those pages, not from directly rendered pages. Facts are corroborated across multiple independent snippets (address, hours, seats, phone all repeated consistently), but hours in particular show minor variance between two search passes and should be treated as approximate. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists anywhere in the available sources — this is a standard izakaya serving wheat-blended handmade soba, charcoal-grilled chicken, and grilled fish, with no indication of a dedicated fryer, allergy protocol, or plant-based menu.

cuisine: Izakaya with handcrafted soba, charcoal-grilled yakitori, and sake

The hand-cut soba is not described as 十割 (100% buckwheat) in any source, so it should be assumed to contain wheat flour as a binder in the traditional nihachi style; the dipping sauce/seasonings are standard izakaya shoyu-based preparations. No mention of a dedicated fryer or any celiac-safe handling. Not recommended for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.

lead: フォーハノイ屋台33 ルミネエスト新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-38-1 ルミネエスト新宿店 8F

bio.origin: Vietnam (Hanoi street-food concept)
bio.background: Opened April 8, 2022 on the 8th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, billed by its operators as Japan's first restaurant built around a 33-variety pho menu. It's one location of a small Tokyo pho chain that also has branches in Nakano, Suginami and Kichijoji, and it sits about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's east side.
bio.specialty: 33 different pho varieties, plus a signature whole coconut ice cream served scooped from the shell (a mango version is also offered)
bio.philosophy: Furniture and decor were imported directly from Vietnam to recreate the bright, colorful feel of a Hanoi street stall (yatai) inside a Shinjuku department-store food floor, pairing typical pho and bun with com ga, coconut-sweet-potato curry, congee sets, banh mi and spring rolls.
roles: branch of a small multi-location Tokyo pho chain

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's most talked-about item is a whole coconut ice cream scooped out of the coconut shell, with a mango variation also on the menu.
Marketed at launch as Japan's first restaurant format centered on a 33-item pho noodle lineup.

caveats: This is a general Vietnamese restaurant, not a certified gluten-free or vegan kitchen. The vegetable-based pho and Tabelog's "vegan options" tag are encouraging signs, but no source confirms fish sauce (nuoc mam) is excluded from the broth itself, and wheat-containing items (banh mi, fried spring rolls) are made in the same kitchen. Celiac or strict-vegan diners should confirm broth ingredients with staff before ordering.
closed_days: None found — follows Lumine Est Shinjuku's building calendar rather than its own fixed closed day
cuisine: Vietnamese (ベトナム料理) — pho and street-food style dishes
cultural_comfort_note: A mall-floor restaurant right at Shinjuku Station with a Tabelog English listing and card/QR payment, so it's easy for travelers, though an on-site English menu is not confirmed.
geocode_note: Address places it on the 8th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly adjacent to Shinjuku Station's east side — well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station; lat/lng left null per instructions for downstream geocoding.
gf_detail: Pho itself is made from rice noodles, which are naturally wheat-free, and the menu includes 33 pho variations. However, no gluten-free certification, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free-soy-sauce statement was found; the same kitchen also produces wheat-based items (banh mi, fried spring rolls), so cross-contact risk for a strict celiac diet is unconfirmed and should be asked about directly.
hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (last order around 21:30), reported hours follow Lumine Est Shinjuku's building hours; one source lists 11:00–21:45 (L.O. 21:15) — minor variation across listings, not clearly day-specific

staff_allergy_handling.text: Restaurant is tagged on Tabelog for allergen accommodation, but the specific scope (which allergens, how requests are handled) was not confirmed from the sources available.

positives.text: A vegetable-based pho made without meat is offered as a regular menu item, positioned as a lighter option among the 33 pho varieties.

Condiments including chili sauce, herb vinegar and nuoc mam (fish sauce) are set out at the table for diners to add themselves rather than being mixed into dishes beforehand, giving diners some control over fish sauce.

seats_text: About 72 seats (table seating)
vegan_detail: Menu includes a vegetable-based pho made without meat, and Tabelog tags the restaurant as offering vegan options. Table condiments (nuoc mam fish sauce, chili sauce, herb vinegar) appear to be self-added rather than built into the broth, which helps, but no source confirms the vegetable pho's stock is entirely free of fish sauce, dashi, or other animal-derived seasoning, so vegan diners should double-check the broth base with staff.

lead: POTA PASTA 渋谷道玄坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-7 2F・3F

bio.chef_name: 江波戸千洋 (Ebato Chihiro), representative of parent company Echigoya Co.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo — opened 2016 next to Shibuya Mark City
bio.background: Before founding POTA PASTA, Ebato ran a Sanuki-udon specialty shop. He applied udon noodle-making techniques — 'fumikomi' (treading/kneading) and 'jukusei' (aging) — to durum wheat to develop a proprietary fresh pasta noodle, aiming to bring authentic hand-made-style pasta down to fast-food prices in a market he felt was stuck at roughly 1,000 yen per plate because dried pasta takes too long to cook for fast table turnover.
bio.specialty: Kiseki no Bolognese ("Miracle Bolognese") made with A4–A5 grade Japanese wagyu, and the shop's signature Ultimate Carbonara (究極のカルボナーラ) with a rich egg-and-cream sauce
bio.philosophy: Standardize every step — the fresh noodles and sauces are made off-site and flash-frozen, then simply boiled and combined to order — so that any staff member can consistently produce good pasta at very low prices (dishes historically started around 330–560 yen), making 'real' fresh pasta an everyday, fast-casual option near Shibuya Station.

anecdotes.text: Ebato developed the thick, chewy fresh pasta using know-how from his earlier Sanuki-udon shop, applying udon kneading and aging techniques to durum wheat as a proprietary product.

POTA PASTA was designed from the menu-development stage to fully standardize cooking so that any staff member makes the same dish — including a fully manualized method for the creamy egg-and-cream carbonara sauce.
The shop offers both thick and thin house-made noodles; the thick noodle is prized for its extra-chewy, edged mochi-mochi texture, the thin for how well it carries sauce.

caveats: Sources gave two slightly different hours formats for the same listing — a continuous 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30) and a split 11:30–16:00 / 17:00–22:00 lunch/dinner service; the split version is reflected here as it came from a more structured listing, but this could not be independently confirmed by direct page fetch (Tabelog, Gurunavi, and Hotpepper all returned 403 to automated fetch, so all details are drawn from search-result excerpts of those pages rather than the raw pages themselves). Payment method (cash vs. card) at the ticket vending machine could not be confirmed from any source, so cash_only is left as an unconfirmed default of false. An official brand site (potapasta.com) was referenced in search results but could not be directly fetched/verified, so official_url is left blank.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed days per multiple listings, though hours may vary by source)
cuisine: パスタ・生麺パスタ (fresh house-made pasta, fast-casual Italian-style)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine with photo menus and no reservations needed, so pointing and basic numbers get you through even without English, but don't expect English-speaking staff for detailed allergy questions.
gf_detail: All noodles are a proprietary fresh wheat pasta developed using Sanuki-udon kneading and aging techniques applied to durum wheat — this is a wheat-based product with no gluten-free noodle alternative found in any source. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or preparation process was mentioned anywhere. Not appropriate for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 17:00-22:00 (some listings show a continuous 11:30-22:00, L.O. 21:30); 無休 (open daily, no fixed closed days)
name_ja: POTA PASTA 渋谷道玄坂店（ポタ パスタ 渋谷道玄坂店）
neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya — about a 1-minute walk from Shibuya Station's Keio Inokashira Line central exit
seats_text: About 10 counter seats facing the street-side window

No vegan menu, plant-based substitution option, or vegan labeling was found in any source. A strict vegan would need to ask staff directly about a plain tomato-based option, with no guarantee of accommodation.

lead: カリーライス専門店エチオピア 本店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-10-6

bio.origin: Opened in 1988 (Showa 63) in Kanda-Jimbocho, one of Tokyo's most curry-competitive neighborhoods.
bio.background: Ethiopia started life in 1988 as a coffee-and-curry café. Its Ethiopian coffee blend proved so popular with customers that the shop took the name 'Ethiopia' for itself, even though the curry served is Indian-style rather than Ethiopian.
bio.specialty: Curry rice built on a housemade, flour-free roux — a large batch of celery-and-onion paste sautéed with spices and simmered for about three days until the vegetables melt down into a naturally thick, silky sauce, finished with 12 spices. Diners choose from chicken, beef, shrimp, bean, or vegetable curry, and can dial the spice level up in steps (famously as high as 70x), which has made it a rite of passage for Tokyo spice enthusiasts as well as a lunchtime staple for office workers and students in the Jimbocho/Kanda area.
bio.philosophy: Owner-chef Kenji Suzuki has kept the 1988 recipe unchanged, insisting on made-from-scratch spice blending and fresh vegetables rather than a store-bought curry roux — an unwavering commitment to not compromising on the curry's quality or flavor.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1988 as a coffee-and-curry café; its name comes from an Ethiopian coffee blend that was popular with early customers.
The curry base is a large batch of celery-and-onion paste cooked with spices and rested about three days, so the vegetables dissolve into a naturally thickened, flour-free roux.
Spice levels can be ordered far beyond standard, with regulars working their way up toward a legendary '70x' heat level over repeated visits.

caveats: Opening time is reported inconsistently across sources: most listings (Tabelog, HotPepper, Gnavi) and the pattern implied by the official site's store-info page state an 11:00 opening Mon–Sat / Sun-Holiday, while a couple of food-blog write-ups mention a 10:10 (weekday) / 10:00 (Sun-holiday) opening. The record below uses the more consistently corroborated 11:00 opening. Dietary information (flour-free roux, meat-stock-based curry base) comes from independent food-writer/blog descriptions, not an official allergen statement from the restaurant — diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should confirm directly with staff before ordering.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular closed days)
cuisine: Curry rice specialist — vegetable- and spice-forward Indian-style curry (カレーライス専門 / 野菜・スパイス)
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, very popular independent curry shop with a Japanese-language menu and no confirmed English-language support, but simple to navigate (order by curry type and spice level) and well used to first-time and international visitors given its fame.
gf_detail: Multiple independent food-writer accounts describe the housemade curry roux as prepared without wheat flour — it is thickened naturally by long-simmering a vegetable (celery, onion) and spice puree rather than using a flour-based roux, which is a positive sign for those avoiding wheat. However, this is not an official allergen disclosure from the restaurant: there is no confirmed information on whether other seasonings (e.g., soy sauce) contain wheat, and no published cross-contamination protocol. Those with celiac disease should ask staff directly before ordering.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is disabled in this environment; lat/lng are left null and should be derived downstream from address_ja. Address (東京都千代田区神田小川町3-10-6) and multiple sources describing the shop as 3–5 minutes' walk from Jimbocho Station and 5 minutes from Ochanomizu Station place it in the Kanda/Jimbocho cluster adjacent to Suidōbashi Station.
name_en: Curry Rice Specialist Ethiopia (Honten / Main Store)
name_ja: カリーライス専門店エチオピア 本店
neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho / Kanda-Ogawamachi, Chiyoda-ku — 3 min walk from Jimbocho Station

positives.text: The curry roux is made without wheat flour, thickened naturally from a long-simmered puree of celery, onion, and other vegetables with 12 spices rather than a store-bought roux.

seats_text: Roughly 41 seats total — counter seating on the 1st floor (~18 seats) and table seating on the 2nd floor (~23 seats).
vegan_detail: All standard curry varieties — including the vegetable curry (野菜カリー) and bean curry (豆カリー) — appear to share a common base built by simmering the vegetable-spice paste with meat stock/extract, according to multiple independent food-writer descriptions of the cooking process. No dedicated vegan menu item or plant-based-broth option has been identified, so this is not a safe choice for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-16-1 第三歯朶ビル1F

bio.background: Chef Kawase Aya trained at a culinary school in the United Kingdom, traveled extensively through France to eat and learn regional cooking, and later honed her craft at French restaurants associated with renowned sommelier Shinya Tasaki as well as at Hotel Okura Nihonbashi before opening her own bistro.
bio.specialty: Colorful, vegetable-forward seasonal French bistro cuisine, with sauces, salad dressings, bread and desserts all made in-house by the chef rather than bought in from suppliers.
bio.philosophy: Built around the idea that guests should be able to "eat plenty of vegetables" alongside carefully chosen wine, served with warm, unpretentious hospitality rather than formality.

anecdotes.text: The name "Coloris" plays on the French word for color/hue and on the chef's own name Aya (彩, which itself means "color"), reflecting the vividly colorful plates she creates.
Guests enter through an arched doorway into a small red-and-ivory dining room decorated like a storybook interior, which has made the bistro a popular choice for birthdays and anniversaries.

caveats: GF/vegan accommodation is not documented anywhere for this restaurant — it is a small, independent French bistro chosen for its vegetable-forward seasonal cooking, not a dedicated GF/vegan establishment. Sources are secondary aggregator sites (Tabelog, Hotpepper-derived search snippets, a fan blog) rather than directly fetched pages, since the official site and most booking/review platforms returned 403 errors to automated fetching; hours/address were cross-confirmed across multiple independent listings that agreed with each other. Reservation and cash-only fields are best-effort inferences (online booking exists via Ikyu/Ozmall/Hotpepper, suggesting card acceptance and non-mandatory reservations) rather than explicit statements.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (open on the day before a holiday; closed dates flexible for private party bookings)
cuisine: French Bistro, Seasonal Vegetables, Wine
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-language service has been documented; this is a small neighborhood French bistro run day-to-day in Japanese, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should be prepared to point at a menu or use a translation app, especially for allergy questions.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation area, or gluten-free statement has been documented. As a classic French bistro, dishes commonly involve roux-thickened sauces and house-baked bread served alongside courses, both of which typically contain wheat. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should call ahead (03-5389-1441) and confirm precautions directly with the chef before booking.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat and days before public holidays: Lunch 12:00-14:30 (food L.O. 12:30, drink L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (food L.O. 19:00, drink L.O. 20:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays (private party bookings can extend outside these hours).
name_ja: ビストロ コロリス
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku / Seibu Shinjuku Station area
seats_text: 22 seats
vegan_detail: Course menus are built around fish and meat mains with butter- and cream-based French sauces; a vegetable salad with house-made dressing typically appears as a starter, but no vegan menu, dedicated vegan course, or hidden-animal-ingredient disclosure (e.g., in stocks or sauces) has been documented. Vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly to discuss what, if anything, can be adapted.

lead: ラーメン二郎 新宿歌舞伎町店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町2-37-5 日新ビル1F

bio.background: Ramen Jiro traces its roots to a yatai (street stall) started around 1968 by Takami Yamada near Keio University in Mita, Minato-ku, which grew into the original honten (main shop) and, later, a network of independently run 'chokkei' (direct-lineage) branches trained in the founder's method. The Kabukicho shop opened on 1997-08-11 as a company-operated ('foods-kei') branch, and was uniquely permitted to keep the 'Jiro' name after the trademark was formally registered in 2003 (when other non-lineage branches had to drop it). In October 2016 it relocated to its current Kabukicho 2-chome address under new management and converted to full chokkei (direct-lineage) status, with an owner-chef trained in the Jiro method.
bio.origin: Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo (birthplace of the original Ramen Jiro honten)
bio.specialty: Jiro-style tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: thick, chewy flat noodles in a pork-bone and soy-sauce broth, topped with two slices of fatty chashu pork, garlic, and a towering mound of cabbage and bean sprouts, customizable via the shop's 'call' system (amount of garlic, vegetables, fat, and sauce strength).

anecdotes.text: Of the original company-run 'foods-kei' branches (which also included Shimbashi, Kanda, and Toranomon), the Kabukicho shop was the sole holdout allowed to keep operating under the 'Jiro' name after the 2003 trademark registration, until it converted to a true direct-lineage (chokkei) shop in 2016.
Staying open into the small hours (last order around 2:30am), the shop has become known as a late-night refuge in Kabukicho, drawing nightlife workers such as hosts and hostesses after their shifts.

caveats: This is a classic Jiro-style ramen shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations of any kind — noodles and tare are wheat-based and the broth is pork-bone with lard, so it is unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners. Portions are famously enormous, the shop uses a Japanese-only ticket-vending machine, and there is a specific ordering ritual ('call') that first-timers should research beforehand. Cash only; no reservations; counter seating only, often with a queue.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cuisine: Jiro-style tonkotsu-shoyu ramen (二郎系ラーメン)
cultural_comfort_note: Menu and ticket machine are Japanese-only and ordering follows an unwritten 'call' etiquette (specifying garlic/vegetable/fat amounts) that first-time and non-Japanese-speaking diners generally need to learn from a guide beforehand.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address is in Kabukicho 2-chome, Shinjuku; multiple sources place it roughly 1 minute on foot from Seibu-Shinjuku Station and about 5-7 minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station's east exit, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
gf_detail: Ramen is built entirely around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare; there is no gluten-free noodle substitute, no dedicated gluten-free prep area, and no indication the kitchen accommodates gluten-free requests. Not safe for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 11:30-翌2:30 (11:30am-2:30am next day), closed Wednesdays
name_ja: ラーメン二郎 新宿歌舞伎町店
neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho, Shinjuku)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/tare is described as a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone and soy-sauce) base; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based, and no wheat-free tamari alternative is mentioned anywhere.

seats_text: 14-seat counter
vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, finished with pork fatback/lard and topped with fatty chashu pork; there is no vegetable-broth or plant-based option. Only the vegetable toppings (cabbage, bean sprouts, garlic) are themselves plant-based, but the dish as served is not vegan or vegetarian.

lead: シャンティ スープカリー
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-7-4 渋谷第一ビル地下1階

bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido style, brought to Tokyo
bio.background: Founded by an owner surnamed Uemura, who traveled to Hokkaido to sample the region's soup curry, fell in love with the dish, and opened a Sapporo-style soup curry shop in Tokyo in November 2003 — reportedly the second such shop to expand from Sapporo into the Kanto region. The Shibuya branch later relocated a short distance within Dogenzaka (to the basement of the Shibuya Dai-ichi Building near Shibuya Mark City) after the building housing its original location was torn down for area redevelopment.
bio.philosophy: The name Shanti is Sanskrit for 'peace and tranquility.' The company's stated purpose is to make people around the world happy through a single bowl of soup curry.
bio.specialty: Chicken and vegetable soup curry — a broth built from 20-plus spices and medicinal herbs, enriched with pork back fat for body, served with meltingly tender slow-cooked chicken and colorful vegetables (taro, paprika, and others); spice level is adjustable across more than 40 steps, and a milder, clearer 'Saigon' broth is offered alongside the original.

anecdotes.text: Owner Uemura discovered soup curry on a trip to Hokkaido and was so captivated that he opened what is described as Tokyo's second Sapporo-style soup curry restaurant in November 2003.

caveats: This is a soup-curry chain (branches in Shibuya, Harajuku, Ikebukuro, Takadanobaba, Itabashi, Oyama, and Sapporo), not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free restaurant. No official allergen chart or gluten-free/vegan menu was found in available sources; dietary status below reflects general dish composition (broth-based curry with meat stock and pork back fat, vegetable-forward options available) rather than confirmed allergen-free preparation. Direct restaurant-site pages (shanticurry.com) and several review-aggregator pages (Tabelog detail page, Hitosara, Showmetalk, Okamooo, Chawanbushi, Enjoytokyo) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch in this session; all facts here are drawn from WebSearch result snippets of those and other reachable pages, cross-checked across multiple independent sources (Tabelog search snippet, Shibuyaku2shin local news blog, r.gnavi, and a 2025-dated Tabelog visit reference confirming the shop is still operating).
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cuisine: スープカレー・野菜料理
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and ordering is simplified by a numbered spice-level system, making it approachable for non-Japanese speakers, though there is no dedicated English allergy/ingredient guide.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Shibuya-ku, Dogenzaka 2-7-4 (basement of the Shibuya Dai-ichi Building), a short walk from Shibuya Station / Shibuya Mark City — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595, 139.7005). Not geocoded per instructions; lat/lng left null.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or allergen chart was found for this location. The curry soup itself is broth-based rather than roux-thickened, but seasonings likely include soy sauce (typically wheat-containing in Japan), and naan (wheat) is offered as a side alongside rice. No information was found on shared fryers, cross-contamination protocols, or a gluten-free-safe soup base. Guests needing strict gluten avoidance should ask staff directly about the specific soup and seasoning ingredients before ordering.
name_ja: シャンティ スープカリー（渋谷店）
neighborhood: 渋谷（道玄坂／渋谷マークシティ付近）

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No confirmation found on whether soy sauce or other seasonings used are wheat-free; standard Japanese shoyu (which contains wheat) is likely used unless confirmed otherwise by staff.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward curry options (e.g. taro, paprika, and other vegetables) exist alongside the signature chicken soup curry, and at least one review characterized the restaurant as offering 'a good choice for vegetarians and meat eaters.'

seats_text: 約39席（カウンター席あり、おひとり様可）
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The signature soups are built on chicken stock enriched with pork back fat, so the standard broth is not vegan. Some vegetable-heavy curry preparations exist, and the menu has been described as accommodating to vegetarians, but no vegan-specific broth, dashi-free option, or confirmation of animal-free stock was found. Vegans should ask staff directly whether any vegetable curry is made without meat stock, dashi, or animal fat.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-34-1 鳴海ビル1F

roles: カレー・洋食・デリのお店 / 夜はダイニングバー
bio.specialty: ビーフシチュー(beef stew) — 定番の人参とじゃがいも、大ぶりでじっくり煮込んだ牛肉がゴロゴロ入り、ワインでまろやかに仕立てたシチュー。ランチには喫茶店風の特製カレーライスやお弁当も人気。

anecdotes.text: 手描き風の看板に描かれたおじさんの似顔絵に惹かれて入店したという常連客のレビューがあり、個人経営らしい親しみやすい佇まいが伝わる。
ランチには手作りのお弁当もあり、テイクアウトにも対応していると紹介されている。
昼は喫茶店風の特製カレーライスを提供し、夜になるとバーに姿を変える二面性のある店として紹介されている。

caveats: The primary Tabelog listing could not be fetched directly (403 response), so identity, address, and hours were cross-verified via Gurunavi, Foursquare/LINE Place listings, and multiple blog/review posts referencing the same address and phone number (03-5226-0050). No source described any gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, preparation, or cross-contamination practices — this is a standard Western-style (yōshoku) curry/beef-stew deli and evening dining bar, so its base dishes (roux-based curry, stewed beef) should be assumed to contain wheat and animal-derived stock unless staff confirm otherwise. Seat count and precise weekday/weekend hour consistency could not be independently confirmed beyond a single directory snippet.
closed_days: 土日祝 (Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays)
cuisine: 洋食・カレー・ビーフシチュー・デリ (Yōshoku curry, beef stew, deli lunch / dining bar)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu, website, or English-language service was found in any source; expect a Japanese-only, locally-oriented small shop.
geocode_note: Address is 神田神保町2-34-1 (Narumi Building 1F), which multiple sources place about a 5-minute walk from Jimbocho Station — consistent with the task's stated location. Straight-line distance from Jimbocho Station to Suidōbashi Station is approximately 0.7km, so the restaurant falls near the edge of, but within, the 0.8km Suidōbashi target radius. Lat/lng left null per instructions for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free roux, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. This is a Western-style curry and beef-stew shop; curry roux and stew bases of this type are typically wheat-thickened and often use ordinary (wheat-containing) soy sauce. Celiac or gluten-avoidant diners should not assume safety here and would need to ask staff directly, though no evidence suggests staff are set up to accommodate such requests.
neighborhood: 神田神保町 (Kanda-Jimbocho), Chiyoda City — near Jimbocho Station
seats_text: Not confirmed by any source; described in reviews as a small, intimate shop.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were identified. The shop's known dishes (beef stew, curry with meat) are animal-based, and no source mentioned vegetable-only or dashi/bonito-free preparations. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct, in-person confirmation, and even then likely limited.

lead: 信州屋 新宿南口店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-18 ニューアサヒビル1F

roles: 立ち食い・着席兼用のそば専門店（チェーン店舗）
bio.background: 信州屋は、大正2年（1913年）創業の老舗企業クマガイコーポレーション株式会社が展開するそば専門店ブランドで、新宿南口店は新宿駅南口・新宿三丁目エリアの店舗。石臼で挽いた信州（長野）産そば粉を使い、押出式の製麺機で自家製麺を作り、注文ごとに茹でたてを提供する立ち食い・着席兼用スタイルの店。
bio.origin: 長野県（信州）産そば粉を使用
bio.specialty: 打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたてのもりそば／かけそばと、目の前で揚げるかき揚げ天ぷら。季節限定で根付き天然せりそばなどの限定メニューも登場する。
bio.philosophy: 「打ちたて、茹でたて、揚げたて」をモットーに、安心・安全でおいしいそばを、忙しいビジネスパーソンにも手早く提供する。器には有田焼（唐子文様）を用いるなど、立ち食いそばながら質にもこだわる。

anecdotes.text: 季節限定の「根付き天然せりそば」（鰹出汁の温つゆに天然の根付きせりと豚バラ肉）が、立ち食いそば屋の水準を超えていると評された。
冷しかき揚げそば（430円)など、コストパフォーマンスの良いメニューと、コシのある麺の食感が実食レビューで評価されている。

caveats: Confirmed as a real, currently-operating chain tachigui/casual soba shop (a 2001-opened branch of the Kumagai Corporation's Shinshuya brand, at its current 新宿3-35-18 address after an earlier relocation — an old pre-relocation Tabelog listing, ID 13191559, is a separate defunct page, not this one). Direct WebFetch to Tabelog and most aggregator pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all facts here come from WebSearch-surfaced excerpts of those pages (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Shinshu Nagano food guide, Kumagai Corporation brand page, and several dining blogs/reviews) rather than a full page fetch. Reported hours vary slightly by source (7:30 start is consistent; closing time ranges 23:00-23:30 depending on day/source) — treat exact closing minute with some caution. No source (official brand page, Tabelog, or otherwise) mentions any gluten-free or vegan accommodation; this is a standard wheat-blended tachigui soba shop with bonito-dashi tsuyu, not a dietary-focused restaurant.
closed_days: 無休（年末年始を除く可能性あり／No regular closed days reported; open daily)
cuisine: そば（打ちたて蕎麦・立ち食い／着席兼用）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a fast, order-at-the-register tachigui-style soba shop aimed at local office workers with no ticket machine (pay after ordering) and no confirmed English menu, so some basic Japanese or pointing at a photo menu will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free option or 100%-buckwheat (juwari) soba is mentioned by any source; the shop's own description only says the noodles are made in-house from stone-ground Shinshu soba flour via an extrusion noodle machine, with no claim of being wheat-free. Tempura (a signature item, fried fresh to order) is wheat-battered, and the tsuyu dipping sauce in standard tachigui soba shops of this type is soy-sauce (wheat-containing) and bonito-dashi based. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Multiple sources consistently place the shop 2-3 minutes' walk from the JR Shinjuku Station South Exit (and ~3-5 minutes from Tokyo Metro Shinjuku-sanchome Station), well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
hours_raw: 月〜金 7:30-23:30、土・日・祝 7:30-23:00（無休）※出典によりわずかに終了時刻の記載が異なる（23:00〜23:30の幅）
name_en: Shinshuya Shinjuku Minamiguchi-ten (Soba-dokoro Shinshuya, Shinjuku South Exit branch)
name_ja: そば処 信州屋 新宿南口店
neighborhood: 新宿駅南口 / 新宿区新宿3丁目

positives.text: Tempura and soba are made fresh to order in-house (stone-ground soba flour, extrusion noodle-making, boiled to order) rather than pre-made, per the operator's own description of its process.

seats_text: About 30 seats, mixing standing counters and regular seating
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or option is mentioned by any source found. As a standard soba-ya, the tsuyu (dipping/soup sauce) is very likely bonito (katsuo) dashi-based per typical practice at this style of shop, and no plant-based dashi alternative is advertised. Not confirmed vegan-friendly.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア12F

bio.origin: Mediterranean/Arabian cuisine, with a brand concept tracing food and drink history back to ancient Mesopotamia
bio.background: CARVAAN TOKYO is the Shibuya branch of the CARVAAN restaurant group (operated by FAR EAST Inc.), which also runs locations in Hanno and Yokohama. It opened in November 2019 on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, the skyscraper directly connected to Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Mediterranean/Arabian dishes such as hummus, falafel, merguez sausage, tagine, and makbous (spiced rice)
bio.philosophy: The CARVAAN brand concept traces the shared origins of food and drink to ancient Mesopotamia, where wheat cultivation, bread-baking and beer fermentation began, and frames the restaurant as a way to reflect that layered history and culture through Mediterranean and Arabian cooking adapted with umami for Japanese palates.
roles: Shibuya branch of the CARVAAN restaurant group

anecdotes.text: The interior is designed as 'an exotic, non-everyday Arabian space overlooking Tokyo's skyline,' with 13th-century Mamluk Sultan Baibars-style brass lamps hanging from the ceiling and antique decorative plates and wall lamps, evoking an Egyptian palace.
CARVAAN TOKYO opened November 1, 2019 inside the newly built Shibuya Scramble Square, introduced in the opening press release as a Mediterranean/Arabian restaurant.
Almost 90% of the menu is marked halal/Muslim-friendly, and reviewers have singled out dishes like the burger as standouts for Muslim diners.

caveats: Upscale price point (lunch course from roughly ¥4,950; dinner course from roughly ¥8,800; average dinner budget cited around ¥6,000-8,000), so this is not a budget/under-30-USD-per-person spot. No explicit gluten-free program, certification, or cross-contamination protocol was found in any source — GF diners should call ahead. This is a mall/skyscraper chain restaurant (part of the CARVAAN group with sister locations in Hanno and Yokohama), not an independent mom-and-pop shop. Several source pages (Tabelog, official site, foodre, Retty, hitosara, r.gnavi, tripnote, groovyjapan, veltra, being-happy, prtimes) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, so details below are drawn from search-engine snippets of those pages rather than full page fetches; core facts (address, hours, seats, halal status) were corroborated across multiple independent snippets.
closed_days: 無休 (no fixed regular closed day per available listings; may occasionally follow Shibuya Scramble Square facility holidays)
cuisine: Mediterranean / Arabian (halal, vegetable-and-spice-focused)
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available and the restaurant is Muslim-friendly certified by the Islamic Center of Japan, so staff are accustomed to international and tourist diners.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, certification, or cross-contamination protocol was found in any available source. Mediterranean/Arabian cuisine here includes wheat-based items (pita-style bread, grain-based sides), so gluten exposure is likely on several dishes even where they are vegan/halal. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should call ahead or ask staff in person to confirm ingredients and kitchen handling before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address places it inside Shibuya Scramble Square, the tower directly atop/adjacent to Shibuya Station, well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target coordinates (35.6595, 139.7005).
hours_raw: Weekdays: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Afternoon Tea 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Weekends & holidays: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Afternoon Tea 14:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). One later search snippet cited slightly different lunch hours of 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:30) daily with dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00) — treated as the same overall continuous 11:00-21:00 operating window. No regular closed day found in any source.
name_ja: カールヴァーン トウキョウ
neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷)

positives.text: CARVAAN TOKYO is certified as a Muslim-friendly shop by the Islamic Center of Japan; the kitchen uses naturally derived, halal-certified ingredients imported from around the world and states dishes contain no chemical seasonings, alcohol, or pork-derived ingredients.
Specific menu items are explicitly labeled vegan and halal, including the falafel, hummus and tahini-based dips, and a vegan version of the chicken curry.

seats_text: 80 seats total, including 62 table seats plus counter seating; no private rooms
vegan_detail: Select menu items are explicitly marked vegan and halal — including falafel, hummus, and tahini/harissa-style dips, plus a vegan version of the chicken curry — but the overall menu is meat-inclusive (lamb, chicken, merguez sausage, tagines), so it is not a vegan restaurant. Vegan diners should confirm dish-by-dish with staff. No mention of hidden dashi/bonito was found in any source; as this is Mediterranean/Arabian rather than Japanese cuisine, that specific risk is less likely but was not explicitly documented either way.

lead: いろり庵きらく そば 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-20（JR水道橋駅東口改札外直結）

roles: JR東日本クロスステーション フーズカンパニーが運営する立ち食いそばチェーン「いろり庵きらく」の水道橋店
bio.background: いろり庵きらくは、JR東日本グループのJR東日本クロスステーション フーズカンパニーが首都圏の駅構内・駅前で展開する立ち食いそばチェーン。水道橋店はJR水道橋駅東口改札外に直結する形で2022年7月18日にオープンした。
bio.specialty: 茹でたての生そばと、注文を受けてから揚げる自家製かき揚げなど各種天ぷらをのせたそば・うどん。
bio.philosophy: 生の蕎麦を店舗で都度茹で上げて提供するスタイルにこだわり、そばとの相性を追求した特製つゆを使う、というのがチェーン共通のコンセプトとして案内されている。

anecdotes.text: 水道橋店は、そばを自動で茹で上げてすすぐ「そばロボット」を導入したいろり庵きらくの5店舗目となった。1時間あたり約150食分の茹で上げが可能で、茹で時間は約100秒とされる。

caveats: Outbound WebFetch to nearly every relevant domain (tabelog.com, foods.jr-cross.co.jp, shop.jr-cross.co.jp, hatenablog, sobadb, hitosara, Yahoo Map) was blocked by the environment's outbound proxy at the CONNECT layer (confirmed via proxy status showing repeated connect_rejected/403 entries for these hosts, not specific to this session). All facts below come from WebSearch result snippets that themselves cite and quote these pages, cross-checked across multiple independent search queries and sources (JR East Cross official store listing, Tabelog, a dated Connected Robotics press release, a local opening-day blog, and JR East Cross's published allergen advisory PDF/page) for consistency (seat count, hours, address, and opening date each corroborated by 2+ independent sources). No direct page fetch was possible to quote exact original wording, so bio confidence is set to medium rather than high. This is a JR-station chain shop (not an artisanal/independent spot), included in the target list under a "十割そば" framing that could not be independently confirmed — the flour ratio of the noodle itself was not verifiable from available sources, and regardless, the allergen advisory confirms shared-pot boiling with wheat noodles and a wheat-containing soy sauce tsuyu, so it is not celiac-safe. No vegan menu items or plant-based broth were found in any source.
closed_days: 定休日なし（年中無休、駅の営業に準ずる）
cuisine: 立ち食いそば・うどん（天ぷらトッピング中心）
cultural_comfort_note: Order at a self-service ticket machine (typically Japanese-only or minimal English) and eat standing or at a quick counter; no table service and little English support should be expected.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address confirms the shop is physically attached to the east ticket gate of Suidōbashi Station (target landmark 35.7017,139.7539), so it is trivially within the 0.8km radius requirement.
gf_detail: Per the chain's own published allergen advisory, soba noodles are boiled in the same pot as udon and Chinese-style (ramen) noodles, both wheat-containing, so even a plain kake soba carries direct cross-contact. The tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is soy-sauce based and prepared in a shared kitchen alongside wheat, soy, and mackerel-containing items; the advisory explicitly warns that allergens not listed as direct ingredients can still contaminate dishes via shared equipment. The noodle's exact flour ratio (whether 100% buckwheat) could not be independently confirmed. Overall: not safe for celiac or severe wheat-allergic diners.
name_ja: いろり庵きらく そば 水道橋店
neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町（水道橋駅東口）

gf_cross_contamination.text: Udon and Chinese-style (wheat) noodles are boiled in the same pot as the soba noodles, so those with wheat allergy or celiac disease need to be careful even with a plain soba order.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsuyu, which contains soy sauce, is prepared in a shared kitchen alongside items containing wheat, soy, and mackerel; the chain's allergen chart notes that allergens not listed as direct ingredients can still be introduced via shared equipment/facilities.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain (excluding a couple of named branches) publishes a company-wide allergen chart covering its menu and directs customers with concerns to check it or ask staff.

seats_text: standing/counter seating, approximately 21 seats
vegan_detail: No vegan-labeled items or plant-based broth alternative were found in any available source. The chain's tsuyu is a conventional soy-sauce based broth typical of Japanese tachigui soba shops (traditionally dashi built on bonito/katsuobushi), and tempura batter typically contains egg. Without an on-site confirmed vegan option, treat as not vegan-friendly.

address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸2-7-3

bio.background: A small, family-run tonkatsu counter tucked in Asakusa's Hanakawado district, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station's north exit. The current generation keeps it a neighborhood institution: the owner and his son work the fryer while his wife runs the front of house, serving hearty set meals at old-school prices to a mostly local, regular clientele.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around specially made panko and carefully sourced pork, fried using a low-temperature technique meant to keep thick-cut cutlets tender all the way through rather than just crisp on the outside. Alongside the classic tonkatsu sauce, the shop offers its own sesame-paste dipping sauce as a second way to eat the cutlet.
bio.specialty: Thick-cut rosu katsu (pork loin cutlet) teishoku - a noticeably thick loin cutlet fried low and slow for tenderness, served with rice, miso soup, shredded cabbage, and pickles.

anecdotes.text: A 2010 visitor blog described leaving "fully satisfied" after the thick-cut loin cutlet set, crediting the shop's low-temperature frying technique for keeping the thick meat tender.
A 2021 review titled it a spot with 'satisfyingly thick tonkatsu' worth the visit at everyday prices.
A TripAdvisor reviewer called it 'really good Tonkatsu and well worth the money,' noting a generous portion for around ¥1,050-1,350 and that staff manage a little English with visitors.

caveats: Small independent tonkatsu shop, not a gluten-free or vegan destination — included for completeness/context only. Several detail sites (Tabelog, Retty, Yahoo Loco, LINE Place, TripAdvisor) blocked direct fetching in this environment, so hours, cash-only status, and reservation policy are drawn from secondary web-search snippets (Super Hotel Asakusa's local gourmet guide, asakusa-taiken.com, and older blog posts) rather than a primary page fetch, and should be treated as moderate- rather than high-confidence. Care was taken to avoid conflating this independent Hanakawado shop with the separate "とんかつ はせ川" chain (Ryogoku/Higashi-Ginza/Asakusabashi locations, different Tabelog listings) that shares the same name.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (日曜・祝日)
cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) and kushiage (fried skewers)
cultural_comfort_note: An old-school Asakusa counter shokudo with a Japanese-only feel — a review noted staff can manage a little English and gestures, but do not expect an English menu.
geocode_note: Do not geocode (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng set to null so the pin can be derived downstream from address_ja. Address 花川戸2-7-3 is confirmed to be about a 4-5 minute walk from Asakusa Station's Tobu-line north exit and close to Sensoji, well within the 0.8km Asakusa target radius.
gf_detail: Tonkatsu here is breaded in wheat panko and deep-fried to order; no gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free sauce option was found in any source. Traditional tonkatsu sauce and dipping sauces are also typically wheat/soy-based. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

neighborhood: Hanakawado, Asakusa

vegan_detail: The menu centers entirely on pork tonkatsu and fried pork/seafood skewers (kushiage); no vegetarian or vegan dishes, or accommodation for plant-based diners, were found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町6-20 パラシオンシブヤ1F（パラシオン渋谷1F）

bio.origin: エイジアキッチン（Asia Kitchen Co.）系列店
bio.background: 「成ル」はエイジアキッチンの第1号店として1999年に渋谷駅近くで開業したが、渋谷駅南口の再開発に伴い2013年8月に一度閉店。同年11月10日、渋谷・宇田川町（センター街裏手）に場所を移し、鉄板焼きと日本酒の店「ウダガワ 成ル」としてリニューアルオープンした。
bio.specialty: 名物のだし巻き玉子（卵焼き）。卵の種類・だしの濃さ（あっさり／甘め）・トッピングを選んで自分好みにカスタマイズできる一皿で、店の看板メニューとなっている。

anecdotes.text: エイジアキッチンの第1号店だった「成ル」は渋谷駅南口再開発のため2013年8月に閉店し、同年11月に宇田川町で鉄板焼き・日本酒の店として生まれ変わった。
名物のだし巻き玉子は卵の種類、だしの濃さ、トッピングを選んで自分好みに仕上げられる。

caveats: Standard izakaya/teppanyaki restaurant, not marketed as vegan or gluten-free friendly. No dedicated allergy-free menu, dedicated fryer, or alternative (wheat-free) soy sauce was found in any source. Signature dish (dashimaki tamago) is built on traditional dashi, which typically contains bonito (katsuobushi) and soy sauce — not vegan and likely not gluten-free by default. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-3461-1166) and confirm specifics, as no online source addresses allergy accommodation. Seat count (52) and detailed hours were only found via aggregator/booking-site listings rather than a single authoritative primary source, so treat as approximate. Restaurant is part of the Asia Kitchen group (not an independent mom-and-pop).
closed_days: Reported as largely no fixed weekly closed day (不定休), with New Year holidays (12/31–1/3) closed; sources disagree slightly on this, so treat closed-day info as approximate.
cuisine: 居酒屋・鉄板焼き（Izakaya / Teppanyaki）
cultural_comfort_note: Primarily Japanese-language listings and menu; an English Tabelog page exists but no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions; address confirms location in Udagawa-cho, Shibuya, a short walk from Shibuya Scramble, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce/dashi option is documented anywhere. As a traditional izakaya/teppanyaki restaurant, the soy sauce and dashi used in the signature dashimaki tamago and other dishes almost certainly contain wheat (regular shoyu), and the teppan grill is shared across all dishes, creating cross-contact risk. Anyone with celiac disease should call ahead and ask detailed questions before dining; do not assume any dish is safe by default.
hours_raw: 17:00-24:00 daily per most listings (one source lists 17:00-23:00); some sources note 不定休 (irregular closures) with New Year holidays (12/31-1/3) off.
neighborhood: 渋谷（センター街・公園通り沿い、宇田川町）
seats_text: Counter seating around the teppan grill plus table/tatami seating, roughly 52 seats total (figure from aggregator listings, not independently confirmed by the restaurant).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based accommodation is documented. The signature dashimaki tamago is an egg dish made with traditional dashi (typically bonito/katsuobushi-based), and the broader izakaya/teppanyaki menu centers on meat and seafood. No source mentions a vegan or vegetarian option, kombu-only dashi, or substitutions. Strict vegans should not assume any accommodation without calling ahead.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-8-12 2F

bio.origin: Nepal
bio.background: Opened in July 2021 in Nishikanda, between Suidobashi and Jimbocho stations. The owner-chef is from Nepal and trained at Musashiya, a well-known old-school yōshoku (Western-style) restaurant in Shimbashi, before striking out on his own with this eight-seat counter shop.
bio.specialty: Omurice — a two-egg omelette laid over butter-scented chicken rice, served with a side of napolitan pasta and salad.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name, Yōinshoku, is a coined word blending yōshoku (Western food) and inshoku (food and drink). It carries forward the butter-forward, showa-era yōshoku style learned at Musashiya while adding the chef's own playful, spice-leaning touches, such as a curry-spiked dry-curry omurice.

anecdotes.text: "ネパール人が営むユニークな洋食店。新橋のむさしやチルドレンですが、メガ盛りもあり、いかにも学生やリーマン受けしそう！" (A unique yōshoku shop run by a Nepali chef — a 'child' of Shimbashi's Musashiya, with hefty portions that appeal to students and office workers.)
A blog visit describes the butter aroma as reminiscent of Musashiya, while noting the omurice and chicken rice have become their own distinct dish under this chef.

caveats: Payment methods (cash-only vs. card/PayPay) could not be confirmed from available sources; cash_only is a low-confidence default of false. No English menu or official website was found — Instagram is the closest thing to an official presence. This is a classic yōshoku/omurice-curry-napolitan counter shop with no vegan or gluten-free accommodations identified in any source.
closed_days: 土曜・祝日 (Saturdays and public holidays)
cuisine: 洋食・オムライス (Yōshoku / Western-style Japanese food, omurice specialist)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat local counter shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho popular with students and office workers; no English menu or English-speaking-staff information was found, so be ready to order in Japanese or by pointing at photos.

gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodations were mentioned in any available source. The menu is built around wheat-heavy yōshoku staples — napolitan pasta, curry with a flour-based roux, and likely breaded items typical of this style — making it unsuitable for a gluten-free or celiac diet as currently documented.

cuisine: うどん (Udon)

seats_text: approx. 128 seats (per aggregator listings; not independently confirmed on an official page)
closed_days: 無休 (open every day)
cultural_comfort_note: An old-school, mostly local udon shop near Shinjuku Station West Exit; a Tabelog English page and delivery-app listing exist, but basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.
vegan_detail: A traditional udon specialist known for meat-topped and dashi-based bowls (pork, beef, miso-braised, salad udon with mayonnaise-based dressing). No vegan-specific menu, plant-based dashi, or vegetarian labeling was found in any source; the broth is presumed to be conventional bonito/katsuo-based dashi as is standard for this style of shop.
gf_detail: The restaurant's identity is built around hand-made wheat udon noodles, freshly pressed twice daily at their own nearby noodle factory — there is no gluten-free noodle substitute mentioned anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) and wheat-based dashi/seasonings are used throughout the menu. Not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Sangokuichi traces back to 1954, when founder Hashimoto Ken went independent and opened a small (about 6-tsubo) shop near Shinjuku's east exit, first selling bags, then converting it into a ramen shop the following year. In 1965 the shop changed business again to become an udon restaurant. The name '三国一' ("best of the three kingdoms") comes from a trip: returning by shinkansen from a Sanuki-udon tasting tour in Shikoku, the founder saw Mt. Fuji through the train window — a peak visible from three provinces (Shizuoka, Kanagawa, Yamanashi) — and named the shop after it. The company now runs three shops around Shinjuku Station (east exit, west exit, and the Island Tower location), all still hand-making their own noodles.
bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo (founded 1954/opened as udon shop in 1965)
bio.specialty: Salad udon (サラダうどん) — cold udon topped with fresh vegetables, tuna or meat, and a house-made mayonnaise-based dressing
bio.philosophy: Everything made in-house: noodles are hand-pressed twice daily at the company's own nearby noodle-making facility and delivered fresh, and the dressing, mayonnaise, and dashi used across the menu are all made from scratch on site.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the founder spotting Mt. Fuji — visible across three provinces — from a shinkansen window while returning from a Sanuki-udon eating trip in Shikoku.

Salad udon, now a common menu item at udon shops across Japan, is said to have originated here roughly 40 years ago when the previous-generation owner invented it during a health-food-conscious era; it became the shop's signature best-seller.
A food writer compared the shop's cult following and hearty, no-frills style to Ramen Jiro, calling it 'the Jiro of udon.'

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, Yahoo Map, tablemark.co.jp, shinjuku.mypl.net, retty.me, and several other domains was blocked by the environment's outbound proxy (403 on CONNECT). All facts here were obtained via the WebSearch tool, which synthesizes answers from fetched page content and cites source URLs; none were invented. Seat count (128) and exact hours/closed-days come from secondary aggregator listings (Tabelog/Gnavi-derived search snippets), not a directly-fetched official page, so treat as reasonably reliable but not primary-source-verified. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this restaurant in any source found — it is a conventional, wheat-noodle-centric old-school udon shop; GF/vegan assessment is therefore based on the absence of any such offering rather than an explicit disclaimer from the restaurant.

bio.background: SHARK COFFEE opened in April 2023 on the Shibuya Park-dori side near Shibuya PARCO, LINE CUBE SHIBUYA and Yoyogi Park, inside a ground-floor space (Shibuya Homes 1F) that gives it an unusually spacious, sofa-lined layout for central Shibuya.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (opened 2023)
bio.philosophy: The café's stated concept is "からだにいいものを気軽に" — enjoying things that are good for the body, casually. It pairs specialty coffee (a single organic house blend, 100% organic beans) with sweets and light food curated by an organic consultant to be gluten-free, largely vegan and additive-free.
bio.specialty: Vegan carrot cake (made with rice flour), vegan coconut-raspberry pie, gluten-free melon pan, and the house organic-bean specialty coffee and plant-milk lattes.

anecdotes.text: The café is dog-friendly throughout the space except the sofa seating, and has become known among Tokyo cafe-hoppers as a rare large, calm, pet-welcoming spot in the middle of Shibuya.
Its drinks lean fully organic — including a honey-free chai latte and kombucha — alongside the standard organic-bean coffee menu.

caveats: Menu is not entirely vegan (e.g., Greek yogurt containing dairy appears on the menu), so vegan diners should still check individual items. No explicit dedicated-fryer or wheat-free-kitchen certification was found for gluten-free items, so celiac-level cross-contamination assurance could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: None stated (定休日なし — no fixed closed days)
cuisine: ヴィーガンカフェ・オーガニックコーヒー (vegan/gluten-free-friendly café, organic specialty coffee)
cultural_comfort_note: No confirmed English menu, but ordering is straightforward café-style (point at displayed cakes/drinks) and payment is cashless card/QR only, which is easy for foreign visitors.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address places it on Shibuya Park-dori near Shibuya PARCO, well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble (35.6595,139.7005).
gf_detail: Sweets and light food are curated by an organic consultant specifically to be gluten-free, additive-free and mostly vegan, including gluten-free cakes, pizza, hot dogs, curry bread, rice-flour carrot cake, and gluten-free melon pan. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, dedicated fryer, or formal cross-contamination protocol was documented in any source found, so those with celiac disease should confirm specific items with staff before ordering.

However, the menu is not exclusively vegan — items such as Greek yogurt (dairy) also appear — so vegan diners should check individual dishes rather than assume the whole menu is animal-free.

bio.background: Toriichizu is a nationwide Japanese chicken-focused izakaya chain operated by FS.shake Co., Ltd., with roughly 100 branches across Japan; this branch sits two minutes on foot from Suidōbashi Station's east exit in the Jimbōchō/Kanda-Misakichō office district.
bio.specialty: Bone-in chicken (骨付鳥) marinated in a house recipe and finished with a proprietary tare sauce (spice level selectable from mild to extra-hot), and a mizutaki chicken hot pot slow-simmered over 8+ hours from chicken bones with no added MSG.

anecdotes.text: The chain's signature kawa-kushi (grilled chicken-skin skewers) are dusted table-side with a 'secret spicy powder' unique to Toriichizu.

caveats: This is a large nationwide chain izakaya (approx. 100 locations), not an independent or GF/vegan-focused establishment. No gluten-free or vegan menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy protocol could be confirmed from official or listing sources; assessment is based on standard izakaya/yakitori-chain cooking practices (soy-based tare, shared fryer, chicken-bone broth) rather than a direct statement from the restaurant.
closed_days: None advertised (year-round/無休), except closed January 1 for New Year's; reduced hours Dec 29-31 and Jan 2-3.
cuisine: 焼き鳥・水炊き (Yakitori & Mizutaki chicken hot pot) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-language chain izakaya with picture-menu ordering typical of large casual chains; no English menu, vegan signage, or foreign-visitor accommodation was found in any listing.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address and listings place it 2 minutes on foot from Suidōbashi Station's east exit, within the target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is advertised. The signature bone-in chicken and grilled skewers are finished with a proprietary tare sauce, which in standard izakaya cooking is soy-sauce based and contains wheat. The karaage (fried chicken) is coated in a spiced flour breading and cooked in a shared fryer alongside other fried items, creating cross-contamination risk. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature mizutaki hot pot broth is slow-simmered from chicken bones for 8+ hours; no vegetable or plant-based broth alternative is listed.

No plant-based dashi, broth, or dish substitutions were found in any official or listing source.

lead: すごい煮干ラーメン凪 新宿ゴールデン街店本館
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-1-10 2F（新宿ゴールデン街内）

bio.origin: Founded 2004 in a tiny 4-tsubo, 8-seat rented space in Shinjuku Golden Gai
bio.background: Ramen Nagi began in 2004 when founder Satoshi Ikuta and ramen-loving friends started serving noodles once a week out of a borrowed, tiny Golden Gai space with no money, no signature flavor, and no customers. The shop's name, Nagi (凪, meaning a windless calm sea), was chosen because the founders felt they had nothing to start with and wanted to create their own wind from a calm sea. Word of mouth grew the weekly pop-up into a full-time shop, and lines eventually stretched over 100 people deep outside this same Golden Gai alley. This location is the original honkan (main store) where the entire Nagi niboshi-ramen chain began, and it has since grown into a chain of 40+ locations in Japan and dozens more overseas.
bio.specialty: Sugoi Niboshi Ramen — a intensely rich broth built from dried niboshi (baby sardines/anchovies), sourced from producers across Japan, combined with an original wide "ittan-men" noodle and a spiced sardine-based tare aged for several days.
bio.philosophy: Nagi's ethos is built around pushing niboshi (dried fish) ramen to its most concentrated, umami-heavy extreme, and around the shop's scrappy, community-built origin story in Golden Gai's tiny alleys.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was chosen at a low point: with no money, no signature taste, and no customers, the founders decided to make their own "wind" from Nagi, a windless calm sea.
In 2015, this Golden Gai shop briefly collaborated with insect-food advocate Yuta Shinohara on a limited 100-bowl "cricket ramen," using dried cricket for dashi alongside the usual niboshi.

caveats: This is a conventional niboshi (dried-fish) ramen shop, not a gluten-free or vegan-focused establishment; it is included here only for completeness of the area sweep. Noodles are wheat-based and the broth is built on dried anchovy/sardine (niboshi) stock, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu items were found in any source checked.
closed_days: None — open 365 days a year
cuisine: ラーメン（煮干し）
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, tourist-visited Golden Gai landmark with an English Tabelog listing and ticket-vending-machine ordering, but menus and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle or menu option was found in any source. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the tare (seasoning sauce) is a spiced sardine/soy-based blend that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. No mention of a dedicated allergy-safe fryer or gluten-free accommodation exists in the sources reviewed, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.

No plant-based broth, noodle, or topping alternative was found in any source checked.
