bio.origin: 西浅草（浅草橋の名店「串衛門」で修行）
bio.background: 夫婦二人で営む串揚げ専門店。ご主人が浅草橋の人気串揚げ店「串衛門」で串揚げの味に感銘を受け、その技術のもとで修行を積んだのち、より多くの人にその魅力を伝えたいという思いから西浅草に自分たちの店を構えた。
bio.specialty: 串揚げ10串コース ― 旬の魚介・肉・野菜をバランスよく揚げた10本の串を、タルタルソースなど10種類のオリジナルソースで味わう
bio.philosophy: 専門店であっても堅苦しくなく、気軽に楽しんでほしいという想いで営業している

anecdotes.text: 店主は浅草橋の専門店「串衛門」で食べた串揚げに感銘を受け、同店で修行を積んだ後に独立して西浅草に開店した。
専門店とはいえ夫婦2人で営む店で、気軽に楽しんでほしいというのが店主たちの想い。

caveats: This is a traditional kushiage (deep-fried skewer) specialty restaurant with no confirmed gluten-free or vegan accommodations. Skewers are coated in wheat-based batter/panko and fried; dipping sauces (including oroshi ponzu) are soy-sauce based. No dedicated fryer, allergy menu, or gluten-free/vegan options were found in any source. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free/vegan diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant by phone (Japanese only).
closed_days: 月曜日 (Mondays)
cuisine: 串揚げ・揚げ物 (Kushiage / deep-fried skewers)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, intimate husband-and-wife counter restaurant in a quiet residential corner of Nishi-Asakusa; expect Japanese-only signage and menus with limited English support, though online reservation platforms (Tabelog, AutoReserve) allow bookings.
geocode_note: Address (西浅草3-27-13) is approximately 450-500m from Sensoji, about a 5-minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station Exit A2 — confirmed within the 0.8km Sensoji radius via nearby-block reference coordinates (35.716284, 139.791880) obtained from map listings, not by direct geocoding. lat/lng left null per instructions.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, allergy menu, or dedicated fryer were found in any source. All skewers are coated in a wheat-flour/panko batter and deep-fried together in shared oil; the tartar and oroshi-ponzu sauces used across the 10-sauce tasting are soy-sauce (wheat) based. This restaurant is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
neighborhood: 西浅草（浅草・つくばエクスプレス浅草駅近く）

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's signature sauce lineup includes oroshi ponzu, a soy-sauce-based dressing, alongside original tartar sauces used across its 10-sauce tasting.

seats_text: Small counter with a few additional table seats, run personally by the owner couple; exact seat count not published.
vegan_detail: Menu is overwhelmingly meat- and seafood-based (shrimp, clams, crab, wagyu beef, pork belly, chicken tsukune); no vegan-labeled dishes or accommodations were found. Even skewers featuring vegetables are typically wrapped with or served alongside meat (e.g., asparagus wrapped in meat) and fried in the same shared oil as animal products. Sauces are not confirmed dashi/bonito-free. Not recommended for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-17-4

bio.origin: Nagasaki
bio.specialty: Kyushu-style (Nagahama/Hakata) tonkotsu ramen served only on weekday lunches, made with a pork-head-based broth continually replenished (yobimodashi method) alongside a spine/knuckle stock for a rich, emulsified, lightly funky ('kusa-uma') pork-bone flavor without excess gaminess
bio.philosophy: A jingisukan (spiced, herb-marinated grilled lamb) specialist by night who uses the quiet weekday lunch hours to recreate the nostalgic roadside tonkotsu ramen of his Nagasaki youth
bio.background: 国際秘羊館 opened in Shibuya's Dogenzaka/Shinsen area in June 2025 as a 'creative jingisukan' restaurant serving herb- and spice-marinated grilled lamb (its name is a playful pun on 秘宝館, a type of curio museum). Starting February 2026, the jingisukan chef, Yahiro, began offering a separate weekday-only lunch menu of old-fashioned Kyushu tonkotsu ramen, recreating the Nagahama-style ramen-stall bowls he remembers eating growing up in Nagasaki.

anecdotes.text: The lunch ramen was born as a side project by the jingisukan chef: he set out to reproduce the funky-but-delicious ('kusa-uma') pork-bone ramen from old Nagahama ramen stalls, simmering a pork-head-based stock and continually replenishing it with backbone/knuckle stock (a traditional 'yobimodashi' method) so the broth turns rich and lightly emulsified without becoming too gamey.
Reviewers describe being surprised that a jingisukan specialty shop could turn out ramen of this quality, calling it a genuine hidden find rather than a side-business afterthought.

caveats: This spot is primarily a "creative jingisukan" (spiced grilled lamb) restaurant in the evenings; the Hakata/Nagahama-style tonkotsu ramen that matches this app's ramen listing is served only on weekday lunches (11:00-14:00, started Feb 2026). It is not gluten-free or vegan-friendly: wheat noodles and a pork-bone broth run through the ramen, and the dinner menu is meat-centric lamb BBQ. Full street-address banchi (1-17-4) and hours were reconstructed from aggregator listings and blog/news coverage rather than the restaurant's own site, since Tabelog and the restaurant's likely pages could not be fetched directly in this environment (network-blocked); cross-checked across multiple independent sources for consistency.
closed_days: Sundays; also closed Dec 31 - Jan 3
cuisine: Ramen (Kyushu/Hakata-Nagahama style tonkotsu, weekday lunch only) at a restaurant that serves creative jingisukan (grilled lamb) for dinner
cultural_comfort_note: A very local, characterful spot (playful pun name, jingisukan-by-night/ramen-by-day setup) with no confirmed English menu or signage, so some comfort navigating Japanese-only ordering is helpful.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, with a soy-sauce-based tare typical of the style; no gluten-free noodle, broth, or preparation alternative is mentioned anywhere in coverage of this restaurant, and the kitchen is not set up to avoid wheat cross-contact.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment). Address confirmed via multiple independent listings as 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-17-4 (Dogenzaka, Shibuya), described as roughly 2 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station and 4-6 minutes from Shinsen Station — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble anchor point.
hours_raw: Weekday lunch (ramen only): 11:00-14:00. Weekday dinner (jingisukan): 16:00-04:00. Saturday: 16:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:00), jingisukan only, no lunch ramen service. Closed Sundays and Dec 31 - Jan 3.
neighborhood: Shinsen / Dogenzaka, Shibuya
seats_text: Counter and table seating across two floors: roughly 8 counter seats plus 5 box seats on the ground floor, and about 25 seats upstairs (~38 total).
vegan_detail: The lunch ramen is built entirely around a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and the dinner menu centers on grilled lamb (jingisukan) with dishes like lamb tartare/sashimi; no plant-based broth, noodle, or protein alternative is mentioned anywhere in available coverage.

lead: シズラー新宿東宝ビル店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-19-1 新宿東宝ビル1F（〒160-0021）

roles: Salad bar & grill restaurant, part of the Sizzler chain (operated in Japan by Royal Holdings Co., Ltd.)
bio.origin: Sizzler is a U.S.-founded steak/salad-bar chain; this branch is run by Japanese operator Royal Holdings.
bio.background: Sizzler Shinjuku Toho Building opened June 3, 2025 on the 1st floor of the Shinjuku Toho Building — the same tower that houses Hotel Gracery Shinjuku and the Godzilla Head, just outside Kabukicho. It was Sizzler's first brand-new store opening in five years and became its 11th location in Japan. A breakfast buffet service for both hotel guests and walk-in diners was added starting June 4, 2025.
bio.specialty: The Sizzler Salad Bar: an all-you-can-eat, self-serve spread said to include 70+ items — seasonal vegetables, house-made deli salads, soups, fruit, pasta, taco fixings, and mini desserts — served alongside grilled steak and chicken mains.

anecdotes.text: This was Sizzler's first new store opening in five years and its 11th location in Japan, opening June 3, 2025 in Shinjuku.

Press coverage described the new Shinjuku Toho Building location as offering a calm, natural-wood interior with sofa seating and a terrace, aimed at both hotel guests of Gracery Shinjuku and outside visitors.

A visitor blog noted that, despite expecting hotel-buffet-style lines, the salad bar was surprisingly uncrowded, with mostly female diners lingering over the salad bar to chat — describing it as popular for girls'-night-out style visits.

caveats: Facts below rely mostly on the official Sizzler site, press releases (Royal Holdings/Fujita Kanko), news coverage, and one visitor blog — Tabelog and several other pages (japanrestaurant.net, findmeglutenfree, Tripadvisor, ameblo, framilife) could not be directly fetched in this session due to network/proxy blocks, so those points are relayed only via search-engine summaries, not primary-source text I read myself; treat gluten-free/vegan specifics as directional, not verified for this exact branch. The target brief lists the area as "西新宿" but the confirmed address (歌舞伎町1-19-1, inside the Shinjuku Toho Building / Hotel Gracery Shinjuku) is actually just east of Shinjuku Station near Kabukicho — still comfortably within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark (~3 min walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station, ~5 min from JR Shinjuku Station East Exit), just not literally "West Shinjuku." No closed-day information was found in any source; it appears to operate daily. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding).
closed_days: Not found in any source consulted; the restaurant appears to operate every day of the week (no closed-day notice on the official site or in press coverage).
cuisine: American-style salad bar & grill (buffet-style salad bar with 70+ vegetable/fruit/deli items, plus grilled steak and chicken mains)
cultural_comfort_note: A major hotel-based chain restaurant (inside Hotel Gracery Shinjuku) catering heavily to domestic and international/tourist guests, with online reservation booking and a self-explanatory buffet format that needs little Japanese to navigate.
geocode_note: Address confirmed via official site and press releases as 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-19-1, Shinjuku Toho Building 1F (inside/adjacent to Hotel Gracery Shinjuku, near the Godzilla Head landmark). Multiple sources state it is roughly a 3-minute walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station and a 5-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station East Exit, which places it well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station landmark radius used for this app. lat/lng left null per instructions — no network geocoding performed.
gf_detail: The all-you-can-eat salad bar (70+ items: seasonal vegetables, fruit, deli salads, soups, pasta, taco fixings) offers many naturally gluten-free choices to build your own plate, and general reporting on the Sizzler Japan chain (not confirmed for this specific branch) describes an allergen information sheet covering Japan's 28 legally designated allergens, available from staff on request, plus other branches reportedly labeling allergens near the buffet. However, wheat-containing items are also on the bar and grill menu (croutons, pasta, curry roux, tacos, breaded/fried items), buffet serving utensils are shared across dishes, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep area has been confirmed for this location. Celiac diners should ask staff directly for the current allergen list before eating.
hours_raw: Breakfast buffet 6:30–10:30 (L.O. 10:00); regular hours 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30), daily.
name_ja: シズラー新宿東宝ビル店
neighborhood: 歌舞伎町・新宿東宝ビル内（ホテルグレイスリー新宿1F）, 新宿区 — near JR Shinjuku Station East Exit / Seibu-Shinjuku Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: The salad bar is a shared self-serve buffet where guests use common serving utensils across all dishes, so cross-contact between gluten-containing and gluten-free items is possible even though many items are naturally gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soups, curries, and dressings on the buffet may contain dashi, meat stock, or dairy, and are served from the same buffet line as vegetable dishes, so strict vegans should check ingredients item by item rather than assume the whole bar is plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: General reporting on the Sizzler Japan chain describes staff providing an allergen-removal menu and a list covering 25+ designated allergen items on request, though this describes chain-wide practice rather than a confirmation specific to the Shinjuku Toho Building branch.

A Tripadvisor review of a different Tokyo Sizzler branch (Setagaya) reported staff bringing an allergy-information folder when asked, suggesting this is a chain-standard practice, though not independently confirmed for this Shinjuku location.

positives.text: The salad bar is described as offering 70+ items of fresh seasonal vegetables, fruit, and made-to-order components, giving diners with dietary restrictions substantial control over what goes on their own plate.

seats_text: 180 seats total (146 indoor + 34 on the terrace)
vegan_detail: The buffet salad bar has many vegetable-, fruit-, and legume-based items that can be combined into a plant-based plate, and taco/rice/pasta stations allow build-your-own combinations. However, soups, curries, dressings, and some prepared sides commonly use dashi (fish stock), meat stock, or dairy, and no dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or vegan-specific preparation has been confirmed for this branch. The restaurant's core menu is a steak-and-grill concept, so the strongest vegan options are limited to what can be assembled at the salad bar itself.

lead: ベーカリー サンチノ 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア 1F（エキュート エディション 渋谷内）

bio.background: Santino is a rice-flour-bread concept produced by Akimasa Sugikubo, the owner-chef of the well-known Tomigaya/Yoyogi-Koen bakery "365日" (365 Nichi, opened 2013) and head of Ultra Kitchen Inc. This Shibuya branch is inside the ecute EDITION food hall on the 1st floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, which opened November 1, 2019.
bio.specialty: コシヒカリ生食パン — a very moist, chewy "nama" (soft-crust) milk bread made from a blend of Sado Island (Niigata) Koshihikari rice flour and Kumamoto rice flour.
bio.philosophy: The name "Santino" evokes the idea of "[someone]'s hometown" / a producer's home region — the shop's concept centers on tracing where its rice flour and other ingredients come from, spotlighting specific regional producers through bread.

anecdotes.text: The dough's moist, chewy texture comes from blending Sado-Island (Niigata) Koshihikari rice flour with Kumamoto rice flour; a small amount of wheat gluten is mixed in to help the dough rise, so the shop is described as 'not completely gluten-free' — recommended as a gentle entry point for people new to rice-flour bread rather than as a celiac-safe option.
Santino is produced by Akimasa Sugikubo, the chef behind the popular Yoyogi/Tomigaya bakery '365日'.

caveats: Marketed as a rice-flour ("米粉") bread specialist, but multiple independent sources report the dough intentionally includes a small amount of wheat gluten (roughly 20% of the flour blend) for texture and rise — it is explicitly described as "not completely gluten-free" and is not safe for celiac disease or strict gluten avoidance. Vegan status of individual items (eggs/dairy/butter) was not confirmed beyond a general note that some enriched items (e.g. croissants) are butter-based.
closed_days: 無休（渋谷スクランブルスクエアの休館日に準ずる／No fixed closed day — follows Shibuya Scramble Square's facility calendar）
cuisine: 米粉パン・ベーカリー（Rice-flour bread bakery）
cultural_comfort_note: Self-service bakery counter (pick tray/tongs, pay at register) inside a major train-station shopping complex — pointing and paying works fine without Japanese, but ingredient and allergy details are likely Japanese-only, so ask staff directly if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding unavailable in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address and multiple sources confirm the shop is on the 1st floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, which is directly connected to Shibuya Station (渋谷駅直結) — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble Square center point.
gf_detail: Despite the "rice-flour bread" branding, the dough is a blend of rice flour (Niigata Sado-Island Koshihikari + Kumamoto rice flour) with a small amount of wheat gluten deliberately added — reported at roughly 20% of the flour blend — to help the dough rise and hold texture. Food blogs covering the shop explicitly state it is "not completely gluten-free" (完全グルテンフリーではありません). This is a normal working bakery (not a dedicated gluten-free facility), so cross-contact with wheat-based products is also expected. Not appropriate for celiac disease or strict gluten avoidance.
hours_raw: 10:00〜21:00（施設営業時間に準ずる）／10:00-21:00 daily, following the mall's operating hours
name_ja: ベーカリー サンチノ 渋谷店
neighborhood: Shibuya (Shibuya Scramble Square 1F, directly connected to Shibuya Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bread's dough is a blend of rice flour and a small amount of intentionally-added wheat gluten (~20% of the flour blend) for texture and rise — this is a general-purpose bakery producing rice-and-wheat-gluten breads together, not a dedicated gluten-free facility, and it is explicitly described as 'not completely gluten-free.'

positives.text: The shop is well regarded as an approachable option for people curious about rice-flour bread's moist texture, though coverage is careful to note it is not a strict gluten-free product.

seats_text: No dedicated seating confirmed — appears to be a take-out-focused bakery counter inside the ecute EDITION Shibuya food hall; no eat-in area mentioned in available sources.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan line or vegan-labeled items were found in available sources. General coverage of Santino's pastry case notes butter-rich items such as croissants, implying dairy is used in at least part of the lineup. Whether the signature rice-flour breads themselves contain eggs/dairy was not confirmed — ask staff before purchasing if avoiding animal products.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田錦町3-22 テラススクエア2F

bio.background: GARB pintino is one location in the GARB series of casual French restaurants run by the Osaka-founded Balnibarbi restaurant group. The Jimbocho branch relocated in 2015 into the Terrace Square building in Kanda-Nishikicho (its original site had been near Tokyo Tower), taking over a bright, open space with 6-meter ceilings that today mixes a bar counter, lounge seating, private rooms and a terrace.
bio.philosophy: The stated concept is to let guests "casually enjoy food and drink in their own style, without pretension" — a relaxed take on French cooking built around seasonal, domestically sourced meat, vegetables and fish chosen for freshness and origin, finished with French technique.

caveats: Primary Tabelog and official-site pages could not be fetched directly (blocked by network policy/403s); all facts below are corroborated across multiple secondary listing sites (Balnibarbi corporate page, Gnavi, Ikyu, aggregated search of Tabelog/official site content) rather than a single direct fetch, so treat exact hours/seat figures as reported-consistent rather than primary-source-verified. No confirmed vegan or gluten-free menu; this is a mainstream French bistro that will take allergy requests by advance phone call only, with no evidence of dedicated GF/vegan preparation.
closed_days: Sundays; dinner also closed on Saturdays (lunch only on Sat); dinner service reported open on public holidays
cuisine: French (casual French bistro)
cultural_comfort_note: All located information is in Japanese with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so it is best to call ahead in Japanese (or have someone call) to discuss any allergy or dietary needs before booking.
geocode_note: Address (Kanda-Nishikicho 3-22, Terrace Square) is roughly 0.7km from Suidōbashi Station and about 2 minutes' walk from Jimbocho Station, placing it within the target Jimbocho/Takebashi area and within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation process was found. As a French bistro, standard dishes are likely to include wheat-based elements (bread, roux/sauces, pastry, breading). The restaurant's general practice is that guests with allergies or ingredients they cannot eat should call in advance so the kitchen can review options; no information on a dedicated fryer or wheat-free alternatives was found. Celiac diners should treat this as a call-ahead-and-verify situation, not a gluten-free-safe kitchen.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (food LO 14:00); Dinner 17:30-23:00 (food LO 20:30, drink LO 22:30). Sat: Lunch only 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:00); dinner closed. Sun: closed. Dinner reported open on public holidays.
name_ja: GARB pintino（ガーブ ピンティーノ）
neighborhood: Kanda-Nishikicho, near Jimbocho/Takebashi, Chiyoda City

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guidance associated with the restaurant's booking listings states that customers with food allergies or ingredients they cannot eat should call the restaurant in advance so staff can confirm what can be accommodated.

positives.text: Reports associated with the restaurant's listings note that when guests informed the restaurant of allergies or disliked ingredients ahead of time, the accommodation was handled attentively.

seats_text: 96 seats total (86 indoor plus a 10-seat terrace)
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific process was confirmed. French bistro cooking of this style typically relies on butter, cream, and meat- or fish-based stocks, and no evidence of plant-based substitutions or a vegan track was found in available sources. Vegan diners would need to call ahead to ask what, if anything, the kitchen can adapt.

lead: ディヴァーノ ワインダイニング 新宿
name_ja: ディヴァーノ ワインダイニング 新宿
cuisine: Italian / French-leaning wine dining with natural wine (vin nature) pairing; seasonal-ingredient dishes, beef red-wine stew and game (gibier) specialties
neighborhood: Shinjuku Sanchome, Shinjuku
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-31-1 大伸第2ビル 5F
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirmed as 1-minute walk from Shinjuku-Sanchome Station (well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station 35.6896,139.7006).
seats_text: 34 seats total: sofa seating (8-seat table x1, 4-seat table x1, 2-seat tables x5), a private room (up to 6), and a low counter (6 seats). Whole-venue buyout up to 34 seated / 50 standing.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri: 18:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30); Sat, Sun, holidays: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: Japanese-language phone/web reservation system with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.
gf_detail: No gluten-free-specific information (dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, wheat in dashi/tempura, or cross-contamination protocols) was found in any source consulted. The menu leans Italian/French with seasonal-ingredient plates and meat/game mains, which may involve wheat-based pasta, roux, or breading. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten intolerance should call ahead and confirm specific dishes before dining; this cannot be recommended as a safe celiac choice without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based tasting course, or vegan-specific accommodations were mentioned in any source found. The restaurant's identity centers on natural-wine pairing with seasonal-ingredient cooking that includes a signature beef red-wine stew and game (gibier) dishes, i.e. it reads as a meat-forward wine-dining spot rather than a vegetable-forward one. Vegan or vegetarian diners should call ahead to ask what can be modified.
caveats: Sources describe this as a natural-wine-focused Italian/French dining spot with beef and game specialties; no source found supports a vegetable-forward or vegan/gluten-free-oriented identity, so gf_confidence and vegan_status are marked 'ask' rather than assumed favorable. Address, hours, phone, and seating were corroborated via search-engine summaries of Tabelog, the official site, and Hot Pepper listings after direct WebFetch to tabelog.com, divano.owst.jp, hitosara.com, ikyu.com, ozmall.co.jp, tiny-corp.com, and favy.jp all returned HTTP 403 (network/proxy blocked those hosts in this environment); no claim below rests on invented data, only on search-result text drawn from those pages.

bio.background: The restaurant's owner holds a sommelier qualification and personally selects the natural-wine (vin nature) list served at the restaurant, from beginner-friendly bottles to rare, sought-after labels for enthusiasts.
bio.specialty: Beef braised in red wine (牛肉の赤ワイン煮込み) and game (gibier) dishes, paired with the sommelier's natural-wine selections
bio.philosophy: The name 'Divano' means 'sofa' in Italian, reflecting the restaurant's casual-yet-refined all-sofa-seating concept; the food and wine program is built around pairing seasonal, carefully sourced ingredients with organically grown, naturally vinified wines chosen personally by the owner-sommelier.

anecdotes.text: オーナーソムリエが自ら厳選したこだわりのナチュラルワインは、ビギナーから上級者までが満足できるとっておきのものを豊富にご用意しており、オーガニック（有機栽培）のブドウを使い、ワインの醸造においても自然な製法にこだわった生産者のワインです。(The owner-sommelier personally selects an extensive range of prized natural wines, satisfying beginners and connoisseurs alike, from producers who use organically grown grapes and commit to natural winemaking methods.)
「ディヴァーノ(Divano)」はイタリア語でソファーという意味。グリーン/レッド/ブラックなどカラフルなデザインのソファーがあり、白を基調とした内装。('Divano' is Italian for sofa; the restaurant has colorful green/red/black sofa seating in a white-toned interior.)

lead: ミラン・ナタラジ 渋谷店
name_ja: ミラン・ナタラジ 渋谷店
cuisine: Natural Indian vegetarian cuisine — organic-farm vegetable and legume curries, soy-meat curries, and dals; no meat, fish, chemical seasonings, preservatives, or artificial additives
neighborhood: 渋谷（神南・公園通り、渋谷駅徒歩3分）
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神南1-22-7 岩本ビル3F
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions; address confirmed as Jinnan 1-chome, Shibuya-ku, ~2-3 min walk from Shibuya Station (Tokyu Toyoko Line exit 7 per Vegewel), well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.
seats_text: About 60 seats total; private/party bookings available for 30-60 guests seated (up to 80 standing)
hours_raw: 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30; takeout/delivery accepted until 22:30), year-round except Dec 30 16:00 - Jan 1
closed_days: No regular closed day (open year-round); closed only for New Year's (Dec 30 16:00 – Jan 1)
cultural_comfort_note: Menus are presented in Japanese and English with pictogram labeling for vegan/vegetarian and allergen (wheat, egg, dairy, nuts) status, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to navigate.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is vegetarian by design (no meat or fish is used anywhere in the kitchen), and a substantial vegan-labeled selection is available including soy-meat curries and a vegan naan made with komatsuna greens (no egg or dairy). Note that some standard menu items do contain dairy (ghee, cream, yogurt) per traditional Indian preparation, so vegan diners should check pictogram labels or ask staff for the vegan-marked dishes specifically. No katsuo/bonito dashi is used since the cuisine is Indian-style, not Japanese-style broth-based.
gf_detail: The soy-meat (大豆ミート) Nataraj curry is described by the restaurant/reviewers as completely gluten-free, and rice can be substituted with brown rice; menus use pictograms to flag wheat-containing dishes. However, the popular naan is made with natural yeast and domestic (regular) wheat flour, so it is NOT gluten-free, and no dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free kitchen zone is documented — curries are simmered in a shared kitchen where wheat-based naan dough is also handled. Celiac-level diners should confirm cross-contact directly with staff before ordering; this is a solid options-friendly spot, not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen.

bio.origin: Founded by Kanhaiyalal N. Buddhadev (originally from Gujarat, India) together with his Japanese wife Masako Saikawa
bio.background: Nataraj opened Japan's first vegetarian Indian restaurant in Takadanobaba in September 1989, founded by a Gujarati-born vegetarian who moved to Tokyo and wanted to introduce authentic vegetarian Indian cooking to Japan. The chain grew to locations in Ogikubo (1990), Minami-Aoyama (2000), Ginza (2002), and the Shibuya branch (Milan Nataraj), which opened in September 2015 a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Vegetable and soy-meat curries built from organically farmed produce, served with naan baked in an Indian clay tandoor oven imported from India; signature vegan naan is kneaded with komatsuna greens.
bio.philosophy: Nataraj cooks all curries from vegetables, legumes, and plant proteins with no chemical seasonings, preservatives, artificial colorings, or additives, aiming to bring genuinely natural, additive-free vegetarian food to Japan.

anecdotes.text: The company runs its own organic farms in Minami-Boso (Chiba) and Akeno (Yamanashi), growing the vegetables used seasonally in its restaurants without pesticides or herbicides, using only organic fertilizer.
Nataraj is credited as the restaurant that started Japan's vegetarian curry movement, tracing back to its 1989 Takadanobaba opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or fryer is documented; naan made with regular wheat flour is prepared in the same kitchen as the curries, so cross-contact is possible for wheat-sensitive/celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most curries are dairy-based per traditional Indian recipes (ghee, cream, yogurt), so vegan diners must rely on menu pictogram labeling or ask staff to identify the vegan-safe dishes rather than assuming the whole menu is vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Menu items are labeled in both Japanese and English with pictograms indicating vegan status and allergens (wheat, egg, dairy, nuts), letting diners self-screen dishes at a glance.

positives.text: The soy-meat Nataraj curry is completely gluten-free, and rice can be substituted with brown rice.
All curries are prepared vegetarian, using vegetables, legumes, and soy meat with no meat or fish, and no chemical seasonings, preservatives, or artificial additives.
The restaurant sources vegetables from its own pesticide-free organic farms in Chiba and Yamanashi.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official site, or Hotpepper pages (all returned HTTP 403 in this environment); all facts were sourced via WebSearch result summaries citing those pages plus a third-party recipe/culture site (manpuku-veggie.com) for founder/farm history. Hours and closed-days figures come from aggregated search summaries rather than a directly fetched primary page, so should be spot-checked before publishing if precision is critical. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (geocoding disabled); address_ja is the authoritative location signal.

lead: 寿司の惑星 水道橋
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-17-5 稲葉ビル 1F

bio.background: 寿司の惑星 (SUSHI PLANET) is a small Tokyo sushi-and-tempura izakaya chain; the Suidobashi shop opened in May 2025 as a sister location to the group's original Musashi-Kosugi restaurant. It sits just south of Suidobashi Station, near Tokyo Dome.
bio.origin: Part of a small independent chain (Musashi-Kosugi honten + Suidobashi)
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri made with akazu (red vinegar) shari, with pieces starting around ¥75, alongside made-to-order tempura
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as bringing old-school Edomae tradition — red-vinegar rice, fish chosen with the toppings in mind — to a casual, affordable izakaya setting rather than a formal sushi-ya.

anecdotes.text: Tempura here goes beyond the usual shrimp and vegetables to include playful items like cheese-stuffed sausage, simmered daikon, and even a 'dessert tempura.'
Tuna and white fish are sourced fresh daily, and every piece of sushi uses akazu red-vinegar shari for a more traditional Edomae flavor.

caveats: Primary source pages (Tabelog, the official reloadedge.com site, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Menu-Tokyo, Autoreserve) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so details below are drawn from search-engine-indexed excerpts of those pages rather than a full direct read. No source found mentions gluten-free or vegan accommodations of any kind — this is a conventional Edomae sushi/tempura izakaya, and the gf/vegan fields reflect the absence of any such offering rather than a confirmed negative from an allergy-specific inquiry. Seat count and exact closed-day policy could not be pinned to a single authoritative figure (multiple sources describe flexible/irregular closures rather than a fixed weekly day off).
closed_days: 不定休 (no fixed weekly closed day; closures are irregular, e.g. around New Year — sources did not specify exact dates)
cuisine: 江戸前寿司・天ぷら（大衆寿司酒場／izakaya-style Edomae sushi and tempura）
cultural_comfort_note: Menu and service appear to be Japanese-language first (izakaya-style operation); a Tabelog English listing exists but no English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce (tamari), or dedicated fryer is mentioned in any source reviewed. As a standard Edomae sushi and tempura izakaya, the tempura batter is presumably wheat-flour based and shared with other fried items, and standard shoyu (which typically contains wheat) is the likely condiment. Celiac diners should not consider this GF-safe without asking staff directly, and language may be a barrier.

The restaurant's identity centers on fresh tuna and white fish nigiri and tempura (likely egg-based batter); traditional izakaya cooking of this kind typically relies on katsuo (bonito) dashi in broths and sauces, which is not vegan. No sources indicate any vegetarian or vegan-friendly items.

lead: タムジャイサムゴー 新宿中央通り店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-28-16 新宿コルネ坂詰ビル 1F・2F

bio.background: TamJai SamGor (譚仔三哥) is a Hong Kong rice-noodle (mixian) soup chain. It traces back to the original Tam Jai Yunnan Mixian founded in Hong Kong in 1996 by two brothers; after a 2008 family split, the third brother ("SamGor"/三哥) and sixth brother kept operating their branches as TamJai SamGor, developing it into one of Hong Kong's most popular noodle-soup chains (three-time Michelin Bib Gourmand recipient there). Both the original Tam Jai and TamJai SamGor were later acquired by Japan's Toridoll Holdings, which brought TamJai SamGor to Japan for the first time, opening this Shinjuku Chuo-dori shop as the brand's Japan debut store on March 31, 2022.
bio.origin: Hong Kong
bio.philosophy: Build a bowl from rice noodles, a choice of original soup bases, ten spice levels, and a wide range of fresh vegetable and protein toppings — the store markets over a million possible combinations, with an emphasis on the signature flavor profile described as "fragrant, numbing, spicy, hot, fresh" (香麻辛辣鮮).
bio.specialty: Customizable 米線 (mixian) rice-noodle soup — choose soup base, spice level (1-10), and toppings; a vegetable-topped version with noodles reduced or swapped for lettuce is offered as a lighter option.
roles: Japan flagship/debut location of the Hong Kong chain, operated in Japan by Toridoll Holdings

anecdotes.text: This Shinjuku Chuo-dori shop was TamJai SamGor's first store in Japan, opening March 31, 2022, billed as the Hong Kong No.1-popularity noodle brand's Japan landing.

TamJai SamGor split from the original Tam Jai Yunnan Mixian in a 2008 family divide between the founding brothers; both chains were eventually bought by Japan's Toridoll Holdings, which unified them under Tam Jai International.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog, official tjsamgor.jp, or several other pages (persistent 403s from the fetch tool in this environment); all facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages plus PR Times, Shinjuku Keizai, Chinese-language Wikipedia on the parent brand, and delivery-platform listings (Uber Eats). Seat count is inconsistently reported (62 vs. 72 across sources) so left unconfirmed. No source gives a first-hand statement from the restaurant about celiac/allergy protocol, dedicated fryer, or soy-sauce wheat content — gluten-free confidence reflects that the noodle itself is rice-based and heavily marketed as gluten-free, not that the full kitchen/menu is confirmed gluten-free-safe. Vegan status is unconfirmed; menu clearly includes meat/fish items (pork wontons, clams, fish balls, fried chicken wings) suggesting broths are not vegan by default.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no fixed closed days reported)
cuisine: Hong Kong rice-noodle soup (米線/mixian) — gluten-free rice noodles, customizable spice and toppings
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering means choosing a soup base, spice level, and toppings from a large combination menu, which is easier with a little Japanese or a translation app even though the concept (build-your-own noodle soup) is intuitive.
gf_detail: The signature 米線 (mixian) noodles are made only from rice and water and are marketed by the brand and delivery platforms explicitly as gluten-free, so the core noodle dish is a strong option for gluten-avoiders. However, the menu also includes clearly wheat-containing items (pork wontons, likely-battered/fried chicken wings, etc.) prepared in the same kitchen, and no source confirms a dedicated gluten-free fryer, gluten-free-certified soup bases, or wheat-free soy sauce — soup bases and seasonings have not been confirmed free of wheat-derived soy sauce. Treat as noodle-safe but not confirmed celiac-safe; ask staff about specific soup-base ingredients if you have celiac disease.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null intentionally. Address (Shinjuku 3-28-16) places this shop in Shinjuku San-chome, adjacent to the Isetan/Shinjuku-dori area and a short walk from both Shinjuku-sanchome Station and JR Shinjuku Station's east side, consistent with the target area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen/fryer is confirmed; the menu includes wheat-containing items (e.g. pork wontons) made in the same kitchen as the rice-noodle soups.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu toppings include pork wontons, clams, and (per delivery listings) fish balls and fried chicken wings, indicating soup bases and shared prep are not run as a vegan kitchen even when a vegetable-topped noodle bowl is ordered.

positives.text: The signature 米線 (mixian) rice noodles are made from just rice and water, so they are naturally gluten-free — this is called out directly in the store's own delivery-platform listing title.
A vegetable-forward option exists: diners can reduce the noodle portion or substitute lettuce for noodles, topped with a variety of fresh vegetables.

seats_text: Reports vary (roughly 60-72 seats across two floors, 1F and 2F); exact seating not confirmed from an authoritative source.
vegan_detail: No source confirms a vegan menu item, vegan broth, or vegan certification. Toppings and soup bases lean meat/seafood (pork wontons, clams, fish balls per delivery listings, chicken-wing side dish), and Hong Kong mixian soup bases typically use meat or bone stock, though this specific shop's stock ingredients were not directly confirmed. A vegetable-topped noodle bowl (noodles reduced or swapped for lettuce) is available, but it is not confirmed that the soup base itself is meat/dashi-free. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about the soup base ingredients.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-1 渋谷岩崎ビル3F

bio.chef_name: 保坂 翔馬 (Hosaka Shoma), listed as head chef on Hitosara
bio.origin: Opened June 20, 2020 by AP Company (operator of the Tsukada Farm / 塚田農場 izakaya chain) as a flagship 'jidoriya' concept store in Shibuya
bio.background: Jidoriya Tsukada is a flagship redefinition of AP Company's long-running Tsukada Farm chicken-izakaya brand, launched under the concept '違いのわかる大人のための塚田農場' (Tsukada Farm for adults who can tell the difference). The company positioned Shibuya as its model district, opening this store alongside several other 'Tsukada'-branded chicken specialists in the neighborhood.
bio.specialty: Miyazaki Jitokko (みやさき地頭鶏) and Kurosatsuma (黒さつま鶏) jidori chicken — breeds said to make up roughly 1% of chicken eaten in Japan — served as charcoal-grilled skewers, chicken sashimi and tataki (breast/thigh), and a chicken-bone-broth hot pot (shabu-suki), with rare cuts featured alongside the standard grilled repertoire
bio.philosophy: The chickens are raised on the company's own farms for two to three times longer than standard broilers, with an emphasis on low-stress rearing to bring out deeper umami and sweetness in the meat; the restaurant pairs bold charcoal-grilling with low-temperature cooking and raw preparations to showcase the ingredient from multiple angles rather than treating it as a single-dish izakaya staple.

anecdotes.text: AP Company's press release describes the store as a strategic flagship ('戦略拠点') built around a U-shaped open counter where guests watch the charcoal grilling up close, intended to move the chicken-izakaya format upmarket for an adult clientele.

Coverage of AP Company's Shibuya expansion notes the company clustered two Tsukada Farm locations and five 'Tsukada'-branded specialty spinoffs (including this one) in Shibuya as a proving ground for the rebrand.

caveats: Chicken-specialty restaurant (jidori/local chicken) with no vegan menu and no confirmed gluten-free protocol; suitable for omnivore diners only. Chain-operated flagship store (AP Company / Tsukada Farm group), not an independent mom-and-pop. Hours reconstructed from Tabelog and secondary listing data since several primary pages (official site, Hitosara, Retty) returned 403/blocked during verification and could not be directly fetched — cross-checked against multiple independent search snippets for consistency. Seat count and chef name come from secondary aggregator listings (Yahoo Loco/Hitosara search snippets), not a directly fetched primary page, so treat as indicative rather than certain.
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular closures, e.g. Jan 4–5）
cuisine: Premium jidori (local chicken) specialist — charcoal-grilled skewers, rare cuts, chicken sashimi/tataki, and chicken-bone-broth hot pot
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional Japanese izakaya-style course dining with Japanese-language menus and booking systems (Ikyu, Ebica, Hitosara); a Tabelog English page exists but no English-speaking staff or English menu was confirmed.

bio.background: Yoriai is a teishoku (set-meal) restaurant a few minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station, known for hearty, generously portioned lunch sets and a lively, welcoming proprietor who serves diners at the counter.
bio.specialty: Fresh horse mackerel (aji-fry) set meal made with domestic mackerel shipped daily from Toyosu market, served with rice from an Akita farm (one free refill, or a large portion on request).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant offers more than 10 varieties of hearty Japanese set meals, with rice from an Akita farm that diners can have refilled once for free or upgraded to a large serving.
Its most popular set meal is fried horse mackerel (aji-fry) made with fish shipped fresh from Toyosu market.

A visiting food blogger described the yuzu-pepper fried chicken (yūrinji) set as juicy karaage topped with plenty of scallion and a well-balanced sweet-savory sauce, served by an energetically welcoming owner.

caveats: Dietary accommodations were not confirmed directly with the restaurant. This is a traditional fried-food/meat-and-fish teishoku shop with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan menu options; not recommended for celiac or strict vegan diners without advance inquiry.
closed_days: Sundays
cuisine: Traditional Japanese teishoku (set-meal) cafeteria — fried fish/meat sets including aji-fry, karaage, and yūrinji chicken, served with Akita-grown rice
cultural_comfort_note: A small local counter-style set-meal shop with no evidence of an English menu; pointing at photos of dishes or using a translation app is recommended.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled). Address and multiple sources place it roughly 3-5 minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station's East Exit, well within the 0.8km target radius.

No gluten-free menu items, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodations were found in official or review sources.

vegan_detail: Menu is built around fried fish and meat set meals (horse mackerel aji-fry, chicken karaage, yūrinji chicken, menchi-katsu); no vegetarian or vegan set meals were identified in available sources, and dashi/sauces likely contain bonito/fish-derived ingredients.

bio.background: A charcoal-grill set-meal restaurant (定食屋) in Nishi-Shinjuku, operated by Humax Corporation, specializing in yakizakana (grilled fish) teishoku for lunch and izakaya-style charcoal grilling at night.
bio.specialty: Toro-saba shioyaki teishoku (fatty mackerel salt-grilled set meal) and salmon belly (sake harasu) grilled over charcoal

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was selected for Tabelog's 'Teishoku Hyakumeiten' (Top 100 Set-Meal Restaurants) list in 2021, recognized for its thick-cut, charcoal-grilled fish set meals such as fatty mackerel and salmon belly.

Lunch specials in the ¥680-¥980 range feature grilled fish that bloggers describe as thick, juicy, and freshly grilled to order, including toro-saba, sake harasu, aji, shima-hokke, toro-nishin, sanma, and gin-zake depending on season.

caveats: This is a traditional charcoal-grilled fish/izakaya set-meal restaurant with no dedicated vegan or gluten-free program found in any source. No mentions of allergy accommodation, dedicated fryer, or GF/vegan menu items were found in Tabelog, Gurunavi, or food-blog coverage. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should not rely on this listing without calling ahead in Japanese to confirm ingredients (soy sauce, dashi) — treat gf_confidence/vegan_status as 'no' based on absence of evidence, not a confirmed negative.

lead: 豆腐料理 空野 渋谷
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町4-17 チェリーガーデン1F

bio.background: Sorano is a tofu-specialty restaurant operated by FOODGATE Co., Ltd., a Tokyo restaurant group that also runs 'Gohan-ya Ichishin Daikanyama' and 'Udon Yamanaga.' The Shibuya shop (previously known as 'Sora no Niwa') sits beside the JR tracks a short walk from Shibuya Station's south exit, and is one of several sister branches (Ebisu, Osaka-Minamisenba) built around the same house-made-tofu concept.
bio.origin: Part of a small Tokyo/Osaka tofu-restaurant group (FOODGATE Co., Ltd.), not a single-founder shop.
bio.specialty: Sorano Tofu (空野豆腐) — silken tofu coagulated tableside from unadulterated soy milk (no additives or chemical coagulants), made from organic Hokkaido Tokachi soybeans pressed to a rich 14-degree soy-milk density; also known for kinu-age (delicately fried silken tofu) and fresh yuba (tofu skin) sashimi.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated priority is tofu made without additives or coagulants, pairing traditional tofu-making knowledge with newly created tofu-centered dishes rather than treating tofu as a side note to a standard izakaya menu.

anecdotes.text: The tableside tofu order sets over about twenty minutes into a dense, creamy curd with a deeper soybean flavor than supermarket tofu — reviewers recommend ordering it first thing.
One diner called it 'the most memorable restaurant in Tokyo,' praising the calm, unhurried atmosphere built around tofu craftsmanship.
The interior features a small water feature with swimming carp and a mezzanine of semi-private seats, giving the space a modern-wa, unhurried feel distinct from typical Shibuya dining.

caveats: Aggregator sites (TripAdvisor) tag this restaurant as having 'vegan' and 'gluten-free' options, but firsthand reviews are much more cautious: one diner reported that a menu item marked 'vegetarian' was initially confirmed vegan by staff but, on double-checking, actually contained chicken — so dish labels should not be trusted without verbally confirming each item. The menu also includes a 'wheat gluten skewers' (fu) dish offered as a vegan option, which by definition is NOT gluten-free. No evidence of a dedicated gluten-free protocol, tamari substitution, or dedicated fryer was found. Treat as a tofu-forward omnivore restaurant with some vegetarian/vegan dishes, not a dedicated vegan or GF venue.
closed_days: Year-end/New Year (Dec 31–Jan 5) and Obon (Aug 13–15)
cuisine: 豆腐料理（豆腐専門・和食）
cultural_comfort_note: Reviewers report staff who speak English and an English-language menu is provided, making ordering and asking about dietary needs manageable for non-Japanese speakers.
geocode_note: Address (Sakuragaokacho 4-17, Shibuya-ku) and multiple sources confirming a ~5-minute walk from Shibuya Station's JR south exit place this well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble; lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found. The restaurant's standard soy sauce (used e.g. with the fresh tofu-skin sashimi) is ordinary Japanese shoyu, which typically contains wheat. One vegan-friendly menu item is explicitly 'wheat gluten skewers' (fu), which contains gluten and is unsuitable for celiac diners despite being plant-based. TripAdvisor lists a generic 'gluten-free options' tag but no restaurant-sourced detail backs this up, and no mention of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls was found. Celiac or severe gluten-avoidant diners should ask staff directly about tamari availability and preparation methods for each dish rather than relying on menu tags.
name_ja: 豆腐料理 空野 渋谷
neighborhood: 桜丘（渋谷駅より徒歩約5分）

gf_cross_contamination.text: A 'wheat gluten skewers' dish (made from fu, i.e. wheat gluten) is offered as a vegan-friendly menu item — it is plant-based but contains gluten, so it is not a safe option for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners despite appearing on 'vegan' recommendation lists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Fresh tofu-skin sashimi is served with standard soy sauce (shoyu), wasabi, and sudachi — no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A reviewer asked staff whether a 'vegetarian'-marked menu item was vegan; staff initially said yes, but on double-checking it turned out the dish actually contained chicken — indicating menu dietary labels are not fully reliable and should be verbally reconfirmed per dish.

positives.text: Reviewers describe a 'fantastic selection of vegan and vegetarian food' with staff willing to identify vegan-suitable dishes when asked, and an English menu that makes communicating dietary needs easier.
Core tofu dishes — cold silken tofu, fresh tofu-skin sashimi, and tofu gelato — are naturally vegan and were specifically praised by vegan diners on abillion.

seats_text: Approx. 95 seats, including semi-private mezzanine seating and private rooms for 4, 6, 8, and 10–20 guests.
vegan_detail: Vegan diners have several genuinely plant-based options — cold silken tofu, fresh tofu-skin (yuba) sashimi, and tofu gelato all reviewed favorably by vegan reviewers — plus a wheat-gluten (fu) skewer dish. However, most of the broader menu (tempura, carpaccio, salads, and other izakaya-style dishes) is not vegetarian, dashi/bonito use elsewhere on the menu is unconfirmed, and one review found a 'vegetarian'-labeled dish actually contained chicken. Vegan guests should verbally confirm each dish with staff rather than trusting menu markings, and expect portions of the vegan items to be small.

cuisine: つけ麺（濃厚豚骨魚介）
neighborhood: 千代田区 水道橋駅 4分
vegan_detail: The signature tsukemen broth is built on pork bone (tonkotsu) simmered with animal fats plus a seafood dashi of bonito flakes, sardine flakes, and kombu — no vegan or vegetarian version is offered, and the broth is not adaptable to remove animal ingredients.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen specialist — the dipping noodles are wheat-based (no rice or gluten-free noodle alternative found), and the soy-based tare/seasoning almost certainly contains wheat as is standard for shoyu. No gluten-free menu, allergen substitution, or dedicated preparation was found in any source checked; celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not eat here.

closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly closed day)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, order-and-queue tsukemen counter shop with Japanese-only ambiance typical of Tokyo ramen culture; no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, though ordering is simple and picture-menu-friendly.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodation could be confirmed anywhere; this listing is included only for general dietary context, and celiac/vegan diners should avoid it.

bio.origin: Kyoto, born 1978
bio.background: Yudai Tsujita worked at an izakaya after high school, opened his own izakaya at age 22, later sold it, then trained in ramen-making from around age 23 before opening the original Tsujita ramen shop in Kanda-Ochanomizu in 2005 at age 26.
bio.specialty: 濃厚特製つけ麺 (Rich Special Tsukemen) — thick wheat noodles dipped in a rich broth combining pork-bone tonkotsu with bonito, sardine, and kombu seafood dashi, topped with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, bamboo shoots, and nori.
bio.philosophy: Tsujita is widely regarded as the pioneer of 'noukou' (rich) tonkotsu-seafood tsukemen in Tokyo, built on an exacting selection of dozens of ingredients and a continual pursuit of a better bowl even after growing into a multi-branch brand (expanding to Osaka, Kanagawa, Saitama, Fukuoka, and even Los Angeles).

anecdotes.text: Tsujita created its own 'tsukemen etiquette': eat the noodles plain at first, add sudachi citrus at the one-third mark, then black shichimi pepper for the final third, finishing with a soup-splitting (wari-soup) to enjoy the broth on its own.
The shop opened in Kanda-Ochanomizu in 2005 and is credited with helping ignite Tokyo's modern tsukemen boom before growing into a multi-city, multi-branch operation.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿4-2-7 Ciel新宿 1階D

bio.background: シンちゃんラーメン is part of Tokyo's "chan-kei" (ちゃん系) ramen genre — a family of small counter shops each named after a person's nickname (Shin-chan, Haru-chan, Ecchan, etc.) associated with the ramen company 株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン (Nagi Spirits Japan), which also runs the well-known "Nagi" niboshi ramen chain. The Shinjuku 4-chome shop opened around June 2022, a one-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station and about three minutes from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, tucked in a back alley away from the main Shinjuku bustle.
bio.specialty: Chuka soba (中華そば) — thin, flat house-made noodles in a pork-based clear broth (豚清湯スープ) with a soy-forward but light-colored tare, piled high with thick, freshly hand-sliced chashu, menma, and scallion. The chilled dipping-noodle version, mori chuka with extra chashu (もりチャーシュー), is also a signature order.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated approach is to avoid gimmicks and keep things fundamentally simple — a pork-based broth and honest, straightforward ingredients rather than a complicated recipe, which is characteristic of the back-to-basics "chan-kei" style.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is sliced fresh to order at a live kitchen counter in front of guests, so each bowl gets pork that was just cut rather than pre-sliced and held.
The soup is served piping hot and filled to the brim, with thick-cut, tender chashu, menma, and scallion on flat, chewy house-made noodles; free rice and pickled cucumber are offered on the side.
シンちゃんラーメン belongs to the same "chan-kei" family of shops (e.g. Haru-chan Ramen, which has been listed in the Michelin guide) run by 株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン, the company behind the niboshi ramen chain "Nagi."

caveats: Standard pork-based ramen counter shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation confirmed anywhere in sourcing — noodles, tare, and broth all contain wheat and animal product by default, and no source mentions substitutions, allergy protocols, or a dedicated fryer/pot. Hours are reported consistently across sources as weekday 11:00-23:00 / weekend & holiday 11:00-21:00, but one older source claimed 24-hour weekend service and closed-day info is described as irregular (不定休), so hours should be treated as approximate. No confirmed independent official website was found — the listed URL is a small-business homepage-builder page, not a verified brand site. Part of a multi-shop "chan-kei" network tied to a mid-size ramen company (株式会社凪スピリッツジャパン), so not a true single-owner mom-and-pop despite the small 15-seat counter footprint.
closed_days: 不定休（No fixed closed day; irregular closures reported）
cuisine: ラーメン・中華そば (Ramen, chuka soba, freshly hand-cut chashu)
cultural_comfort_note: A small Japanese-language counter ramen shop with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the ticket/menu) will help.
geocode_note: Address (東京都新宿区新宿4-2-7) and multiple independent sources place the shop a 1-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station and ~3 minutes from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark (35.6896,139.7006); lat/lng left null per instructions for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options or accommodations are mentioned anywhere in sourcing. This is a standard shoyu-leaning chuka soba shop: house-made wheat noodles are the entire point of the dish, and the soup is finished with a soy-based tare (sources describe a "light-colored but distinctly soy-flavored" broth), which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing shoyu rather than tamari. No dedicated gluten-free noodle, broth, or fryer was found, and celiac diners should assume this is not a safe option.
hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00-23:00、土・日・祝 11:00-21:00（不定休あり）※一部情報源では金・土24時間営業との記載もあり、要確認
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)

vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are indicated. The broth is a pork-based clear soup (豚清湯スープ) and the signature dishes are built around thick chashu pork; there is no mention of a plant-based broth, noodle, or topping substitution in any source, so vegan diners should assume there is nothing suitable here.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-37-12

bio.background: Opened in November 2005 on Denbouin-dori (伝法院通り), a shopping street near Sensoji Temple in Asakusa. It is an independently run, hand-made udon shop (operated as Yugen-gaisha Udon Kanoya / 有限会社饂飩叶屋), just a few minutes' walk from Sensoji.
bio.specialty: Hand-made udon noodles with a firm, springy 'tsuru-shiko' (つるしこ) texture in the Sanuki style, served hot or cold across roughly twenty menu variations, including tempura-topped bowls and set meals.
bio.philosophy: The dashi broth is built from scratch using Rishiri kelp, quality bonito flakes (katsuobushi), and dried sardines (iriko), with no added chemical seasoning (no MSG), aiming for a refined, Kansai-influenced depth of flavor rather than a shortcut broth.

anecdotes.text: Located on Denbouin-dori a few minutes' walk from Sensoji Temple, the shop is known as a popular, often-queuing udon spot offering around twenty types of hot and cold udon plus tempura-topped bowls.
The restaurant opened in November 2005 and makes Sanuki-style firm udon, with dashi from Rishiri kombu and quality bonito/iriko, without chemical seasoning, for a refined Kansai-style flavor.

caveats: Hours and seat counts vary slightly across directory sources (e.g., 11:00 vs 11:30 opening; seat counts reported anywhere from ~9 (counter+table on 1F) to 26 total including 2F). Direct site fetches (Tabelog, Denbouin-dori, Asakusa-navi, Hotpepper, Retty, Hitosara, gnavi) were blocked by network restrictions in this environment, so details are drawn from search-engine summaries of those pages rather than direct page reads — corroborated across multiple independent listings. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation was found in any source; treat this as a standard wheat-udon restaurant with a fish-based (bonito/iriko) dashi, not suitable for strict celiac or vegan diets.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cuisine: Udon (hand-made noodles, Sanuki-style with Kansai-influenced dashi)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, independently-run udon shop on a busy tourist street near Sensoji; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though its location on a heavily-touristed street near the temple means simple ordering is generally manageable with pointing/photos.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address places it on Denbouin-dori in Asakusa 1-chome, about a 4-minute walk from Asakusa Station and well within 0.8km of Sensoji Temple (35.7148, 139.7967).
gf_detail: Serves hand-made wheat-flour udon noodles (Sanuki-style, firm 'tsuru-shiko' texture) — udon is inherently wheat-based, and no gluten-free noodle alternative, gluten-free menu, or dedicated prep process is mentioned in any source. The dashi/tsuyu broth is a traditional Japanese preparation that would conventionally use wheat-containing soy sauce. No allergy protocol or cross-contamination information could be confirmed. Not recommended for celiac or strictly gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house dashi is made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and iriko (dried sardines) alongside kombu, and is used as the base for the broth across the menu; no plant-based dashi option was found.

positives.text: The broth is made from scratch with Rishiri kelp, bonito, and dried sardines, with no added chemical seasoning (no MSG) — a plus for diners avoiding additives, though it is not a GF/vegan accommodation.

seats_text: Seat counts vary by source: roughly counter + table seating on the first floor plus additional seating on a second floor; one source reports about 26 seats total, another describes counter (~4-5) and table seats (~2-4 tables) on the first floor alone. Exact current count unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: The dashi broth is built from Rishiri kombu combined with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and iriko (dried sardines) — a fish-based broth used throughout the menu — and no plant-based dashi or vegan option was found in any source. Tempura and other toppings likely also include animal ingredients. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: まぜそば 渋谷 チョップス
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町26-3 サンルイビル1F

bio.origin: Yoyogi, Tokyo (opened as a second location of the Yoyogi original)
bio.background: Chops grew out of Yoyogi's iekei ramen shop 'Samurai' (侍), which opened the original 'Mazesoba Tamani Ramen Chops' in Yoyogi in December 2021. The Shibuya branch — a one-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A3 exit, right by Marui and the scramble crossing — opened in 2022 as a second location, reinterpreting Jiro-inspired mazesoba for a busy Shibuya crowd.
bio.specialty: Mazesoba (soy-based or spicy 'karami'), Jiro-inspired but built to be lighter than a full Jiro bowl, topped with garlic, chicken oil (鶏油) and, at the table, a seasoned crunchy fried-batter topping called 'Chops Bomb' for a mid-bowl texture and flavor change.
bio.philosophy: Take the heavy, garlic-forward DNA of Jiro-style ramen and adapt it into a bowl light enough to work as an everyday, quick meal in the middle of Shibuya rather than an occasional indulgence.
roles: second location of Yoyogi's 'Samurai' iekei ramen group

anecdotes.text: Covered on SUSURU TV (episode 2402) trying the mazesoba in a large ('大') portion, describing it as a 'gutsy' new Shibuya opening.
A food blogger described it as 'Jiro-style but not too heavy — a bowl that works in Shibuya,' noting it channels Jiro's context while dialing back the weight to suit an urban lunch/dinner crowd.

caveats: Direct WebFetch of most secondary sources (Tabelog, Retty, meqqe, lit.link, ramendb, ramen-engineer, susurulab, ikkos-films, demae-can) returned HTTP 403 in this environment; facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that explicitly cite and summarize those same pages (address, phone, hours, station access, payment, opening history, menu/topping details). No shop statement on gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found anywhere in available sources — gf/vegan fields are inferred from the dish type (Jiro-inspired mazesoba: wheat noodles, soy-based tare, pork fat, chicken oil, garlic) rather than from an explicit denial by the shop, so treat as 'not accommodating' rather than 'confirmed unsafe.' Seat count could not be confirmed from any source.

cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a touch-panel food-ticket machine with photo-labeled menu items, so a first-timer can point-and-order, though the garlic/oil/topping customization prompts are Japanese-only.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/mazesoba noodles and a soy-sauce-based (shoyu) tare are used throughout the menu; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe process was mentioned in any source. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

lead: チャルモゴッソヨ 東京ドームシティ店
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 東京ドームシティ 黄色いビル2階 FOOD STADIUM TOKYO

bio.origin: 韓国料理チェーン（ワールドリカーインポーターズ株式会社／ZATOグループ運営）
bio.background: 「チャルモゴッソヨ（잘 먹었어요）」は韓国語で「よく食べました」という意味。2025年11月13日、東京ドームシティ内のフードホール「FOOD STADIUM TOKYO」内にオープンした韓国酒場で、「映え×健康志向の韓国ダイニング」をコンセプトに掲げている。
bio.specialty: サムギョプサル（豚バラ肉の炭火焼き）とビビンバ
bio.philosophy: 「よく食べました」という店名の通り、写真映えする韓国料理と、マッコリやチャミスルなどの韓国のお酒を気軽に楽しめる“韓国酒場”として、東京ドームシティを訪れる人々に賑やかな食体験を届けることを目指している。

anecdotes.text: 店名の由来は韓国語で「よく食べました」を意味する「잘 먹었어요」。定番のサムギョプサルやビビンバ、韓国のお酒（マッコリ、チャミスルなど）とともに、SNS映えする“推しカラードリンク”などフォトジェニックなメニューも提供している。
2025年11月13日オープン。ランチ限定のサムギョプサル定食（1,080円）は、ご飯・チャプチェ・キムチ・ごぼう・ナムル・サンチュ・韓国のりが付く構成。

caveats: This is a food-court-style stall inside the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall at Tokyo Dome City, sharing communal seating (~480 seats) with other vendors rather than having its own dining room. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (menu callouts, ingredient disclosures, or allergy protocols) could be found from any source, so GF/vegan ratings below default to the cautious "ask" level rather than being confirmed safe or unsafe. Direct fetching of tabelog.com, zato.co.jp, prtimes.jp, tokyo-dome.co.jp, and several blog URLs was blocked by network policy in this environment (all returned 403/connect_rejected at the proxy layer, not site-specific blocks — even google.com failed the same way), so this record relies on web-search result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than full-page fetches. Facts below are taken only from what appeared in those search snippets.

Celiac or strict gluten-avoiding diners should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering; this cannot be confirmed safe.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-8-5 中川ビル2F

bio.origin: Okinawa
bio.background: Gusuku is a small owner-run Okinawan izakaya tucked in a zakkyo (mixed-use) building in Shinjuku Sanchome. It is run by a master known to regulars as "Miyachan," who is originally from Okinawa and, according to reviews, previously trained as a French chef before opening this Okinawan-focused spot.
bio.specialty: Goat sashimi (yagi-sashi), a rare specialty even among Okinawan restaurants in Tokyo, served when stock allows; also yagi-nabe (goat hotpot with goat offal), mimigā (pig ear), and champuru stir-fries.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant aims to bring "uchinaa taimu" (Okinawan time) — a slow, unhurried pace — into the middle of fast-moving Shinjuku, giving guests a calmer, more intimate izakaya experience than typical Tokyo Okinawan restaurants.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the food as curing their "Okinawa-loss" (homesickness for Okinawa), calling it authentic, home-region-quality cooking.
Unlike many bustling Okinawan izakaya in Tokyo, reviewers note Gusuku is quiet enough to relax and enjoy a slow-paced meal.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodations, allergy protocols, or dedicated-fryer information were found in any available source. This is a traditional Okinawan izakaya built around goat meat, pork, and seafood dishes with shoyu- and dashi-based seasoning, so it is not a safe or suitable pick for strict celiac or vegan diners despite meeting the geographic criteria. Reported hours and closed-day details vary slightly across secondary listing sites (Tabelog, Retty, aumo, Gnavi); some list year-round operation ("無休") while at least one source suggests irregular closures. The Tabelog and restaurant's own blog pages returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, so details below are drawn from search-engine summaries of those and other secondary sites (Retty, aumo, Gnavi, Yahoo Map, AutoReserve) rather than direct page reads; core facts (address, phone, hours, goat-sashimi specialty, owner nickname) were corroborated across multiple independent sources.
closed_days: No fixed closed day reported (multiple sources say open year-round / "無休"); one source suggests occasional irregular closures.
cuisine: Okinawan (Ryukyu) izakaya cuisine, notable for goat sashimi (yagi-sashi)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local, Japanese-language izakaya with karaoke and no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should expect a fully local experience.
geocode_note: Address is 東京都新宿区新宿3-8-5 中川ビル2F, reported as a 1-minute walk from Exit C4 of Shinjuku Sanchome Station (roughly 205m), placing it within the broader Shinjuku Station area; lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions for downstream geocoding.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source. As a traditional Okinawan izakaya, dishes are very likely to use wheat-containing shoyu and dashi bases, and the menu includes fried items; goat sashimi itself may be simple but is typically served with dipping sauces/condiments that could contain wheat. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-18-6 102

bio.background: Chef-owner Katsuya Otsuka went to France at 24, spending roughly two and a half years training in Paris (including Michelin-starred kitchens) before finishing with six months on Corsica. Back in Tokyo he refined his craft at respected houses such as Roquabrousse in Aoyama, becoming a head chef at 29 and opening his own restaurant, Ludique, in Asakusa's Kannonura backstreets in 2016.
bio.origin: Japan (trained in Paris and Corsica, France)
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name is French for "playful," and that spirit shows in Otsuka's inventive starters, but the cooking underneath is described as solid, classical French technique rather than overwrought or experimental.
bio.specialty: Foie gras monaka — a crisp wafer 'sandwich' amuse-bouche filled with smooth foie gras mousse, layered with apple compote and yuzu jam, served since the restaurant's opening and considered its signature bite.

anecdotes.text: Ludique opened in 2016 in Asakusa's quieter Kannonura (behind Sensoji) area as a small, counter-driven French restaurant with 15 seats, 7 of them facing the open kitchen.
The foie gras monaka amuse has been on the menu since day one and is what many guests remember most.
Otsuka trained roughly three years in Michelin-starred kitchens in Paris and Corsica before working at Roquabrousse in Aoyama, Tokyo.

caveats: Classical French fine-dining restaurant centered on foie gras and course menus; no evidence of any gluten-free or vegan menu, policy, or dedicated preparation was found in any source consulted (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Hitosara, gnavi editorial, Eatpia). GF/vegan ratings below are precautionary defaults, not confirmations from the restaurant. No independent official restaurant website was found — only an Instagram account and third-party reservation listings (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Ikyu, TableCheck, OZmall).
closed_days: 水曜日定休（月1回不定休あり） — closed Wednesdays, plus one additional irregular closure per month
cuisine: フレンチ・フランス料理（Classical French, course-driven, foie gras signature)
cultural_comfort_note: A small (15-seat) chef's-counter French restaurant run day-to-day in Japanese with course-menu dining etiquette; no English menu or English-speaking-service confirmation was found, so first-time foreign visitors should expect a primarily Japanese experience and may want to reserve via Tabelog's English booking page.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found in any source. This is a classical French course restaurant, a cuisine style that routinely relies on wheat (bread service, roux- and flour-thickened sauces, pastry elements, breaded/battered preparations), so hidden gluten exposure is likely without prior arrangement. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking to confirm what, if anything, can be modified — this listing cannot promise safety.
neighborhood: 台東区浅草（観音裏）／浅草駅から徒歩約5分
