The restaurant's identity and signature dish (foie gras monaka) are built around foie gras and other animal-based French classics (game/jibier dishes are also mentioned), so it should not be presented as vegan-friendly without direct confirmation from the restaurant.

lead: 大勝軒まるいち 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-20-11 宇田川ビル 1F・B1F

bio.background: Opened in Kita-Akabane in July 2007, just four months after Kazuo Yamagishi's legendary Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken — the shop widely credited with popularizing tsukemen (dipping noodles) — closed its doors. The founder trained directly under Yamagishi (sources describe him as one of Yamagishi's very last apprentices) and opened this shop to carry the technique forward; the chain has since grown to a handful of Tokyo locations including this Shibuya branch.
bio.origin: Direct lineage of Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken, the birthplace of modern tsukemen under 'ramen god' Kazuo Yamagishi
bio.specialty: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) — thick, handmade wheat noodles made from flour, water, egg and kansui, served alongside a rich dipping broth simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, vegetables and dried fish, topped with grilled chashu pork and a soft-boiled egg
bio.philosophy: Preserving and carrying forward the noodle-making technique and hospitality spirit of the original Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken after its 2007 closure.

anecdotes.text: The shop's first location opened in Kita-Akabane in July 2007, only four months after Kazuo Yamagishi's original Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken closed, founded by one of his last apprentices to keep the recipe and spirit alive.

The Shibuya branch first opened in March 2016 nearby, closed briefly in January 2017 for a relocation, then reopened later that year at the Udagawa Building spot it occupies today near Shibuya Hikarie.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle tsukemen/ramen shop, not a dietary-specialty spot. No gluten-free noodle or vegan broth option was found in any source; celiac and vegan diners should not rely on this shop without calling ahead, and even then the kitchen is not set up for either diet.
closed_days: None found — appears to operate year-round (年中無休)
cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン (Tsukemen / dipping-noodle ramen specialist — shio, miso and shoyu-style tsukemen)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic no-frills Tokyo tsukemen counter shop with no confirmed English menu — expect to order in Japanese or by pointing, and know upfront that the broth and noodles are not gluten-free or vegan.
gf_detail: The noodles are handmade in-house from wheat flour, water, egg and kansui (lye water) — there is no gluten-free noodle alternative. The dipping broth and any shoyu-based tare almost certainly contain wheat-derived soy sauce, typical of this style of tsukemen. No gluten-free menu, preparation area, or accommodation was found in any source.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding unavailable in this environment); address is corroborated by Tabelog, the official site, and Hot Pepper Gourmet, and is on Shibuya 2-chome near Miyamasuzaka/Shibuya Hikarie, within easy walking distance of Shibuya Scramble crossing.
hours_raw: 10:00-22:30, 年中無休 (open every day of the year)
name_ja: 大勝軒まるいち 渋谷店
neighborhood: 渋谷（宮益坂・渋谷ヒカリエ周辺）

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are handmade from wheat flour, water, egg, and kansui (lye water) — this is a traditional wheat-based tsukemen shop with no gluten-free noodle alternative mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, vegetables, and dried fish, and standard bowls come topped with pork chashu and a soft-boiled egg — there is no vegan broth or protein option described.

seats_text: About 23 seats total, split between counter seating on the ground floor and additional seating in the basement (B1F).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, broth, or protein substitute was found. The dipping broth (the core of every tsukemen bowl) is made from pork bone, chicken carcass, and dried fish stock; standard toppings include pork chashu and a seasoned soft-boiled egg. This is not a vegan-friendly kitchen.

lead: 石釜 ベイクブレッド 茶房 タムタム
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-9 1F

bio.origin: Tokyo (born December 3, 1957)
bio.background: The building has housed a family-run tea house (kissaten) since 1973. Tamura Nobuyuki naturally took over the family business and gradually shifted its focus to coffee and new food offerings. When the building needed reconstruction due to age, he closed for about a year, then partnered with a designer to rebuild the space and rethink the menu, reopening on September 20, 2014 as the modern stone-oven café "石釜bake bread 茶房 TAM TAM."
bio.specialty: 石釜焼きホットケーキ — a very thick stone-oven-baked hotcake/pancake, crisp on the outside and fluffy inside from the oven's radiant heat; also stone-oven French toast and stone-oven curry.
bio.philosophy: Business consultant Isao Endo and Hitotsubashi University emeritus professor Ikujiro Nonaka featured Tamura and TAM TAM's stone-oven hotcake as a case study in Endo's book on how an everyday dish like the pancake can become the basis of a distinctive, thriving business.

anecdotes.text: Management consultant Isao Endo, motivated after a well-known Kanda pancake shop closed in 2012, went searching for outstanding pancake shops nationwide; this led him to profile Tamura Nobuyuki and TAM TAM's stone-oven hotcake alongside Hitotsubashi professor Ikujiro Nonaka in his book on business lessons from pancakes.

The café occupies a tea house that has operated at the same Jimbocho spot since 1973; after the building was rebuilt, the owner took about a year off before reopening the space in its current stone-oven concept form on September 20, 2014.

caveats: Direct fetches of Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Hitosara, Retty, and several blog pages returned HTTP 403 (bot-blocked); facts below are drawn from search-result snippets/aggregations of those pages plus the Toyokeizai feature, not from full page fetches. No page found any gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient substitution, or allergy-accommodation information for this shop — this is a wheat/egg/dairy-centric bakery-café (stone-oven bread, hotcakes, French toast), so GF/vegan accessibility is assessed as low by default absence rather than a confirmed negative statement from the shop. Reported hours vary slightly between sources (e.g., Sunday close listed as either 21:30); the most detailed, internally consistent version (weekday/Saturday 11:00–22:30, Sunday/holiday 12:00–21:30) was used. Seat count is reported as either 30 or 32 depending on source; cash-only status was not confirmed by any source (assumed card-accepting is NOT confirmed either — treat as unknown).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular/occasional closures, no fixed weekly closed day found)
cuisine: カフェ（石窯焼きパン・ホットケーキ / stone-oven baked bread and pancakes）
cultural_comfort_note: No source indicates an English menu, English-speaking staff, or English signage; this reads as a traditional Japanese neighborhood café experience.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-9 is corroborated by multiple sources as roughly a 1-minute walk from Jimbocho Station Exit A7, which places it well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station (~0.7km straight-line) — inside the target area.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, flour substitution, or dedicated gluten-free preparation was found in any source. The entire menu is built around wheat: stone-oven baked bread, toast, and the signature thick hotcakes/pancakes. In a bakery environment centered on wheat flour and a shared stone oven, cross-contamination risk for celiac diners would be very high. Not recommended for strict gluten-free/celiac needs.
name_en: Ishigama Bake Bread Sabō Tamu Tamu (TAM TAM)
name_ja: 石釜 ベイクブレッド 茶房 タムタム
neighborhood: 神保町
seats_text: Approximately 32 seats (one source lists 30).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu items, egg/dairy substitutions, or plant-based alternatives were found in any source. The signature hotcake, French toast, and stone-oven curry all appear to be conventional egg-, butter-, and milk-based preparations. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

address_ja: 〒160-0023 東京都新宿区西新宿1-5-1（小田急ハルク 1階）

bio.origin: Yotsuya Iga-cho, Tokyo (original 1903 stall location)
bio.background: Founded in Meiji 36 (1903) by Kenichi Ishinabe as a tempura stall (yatai) in Yotsuya Iga-cho, Tokyo. The business later grew into a full restaurant behind the Mitsukoshi department store in Shinjuku, and was rebuilt in its current Nishi-Shinjuku location, inside the Odakyu Halc building near Shinjuku Station's west exit, when the building was completed in 1961.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style (traditional Tokyo) tempura, served as a fixed course meal (course-only menu, no a la carte)
bio.philosophy: A family business now in its fourth generation, committed to preserving the techniques and style of traditional Edomae tempura passed down since the restaurant's founding over 120 years ago.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant traces its roots to a tempura yatai (street stall) opened in 1903 by founder Kenichi Ishinabe in Yotsuya; the current premises were completed in 1961 after the postwar redevelopment that produced the Odakyu Halc building.
Today the restaurant is run by fourth-generation owner Jin Ishinabe, continuing over a century of family-run Edomae tempura tradition just steps from Shinjuku Station's west exit.

caveats: No evidence of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found on the restaurant's own site or listing pages. This is a traditional, century-old Edomae tempura restaurant serving a fixed course menu (wheat-flour batter, likely bonito-based dashi and standard soy sauce); it is included in the sweep result set but does not meet dedicated GF/vegan criteria based on available sources. Direct WebFetch of several source pages (official site, Ikyu, Retty, Gnavi, Ekiten, Localplace) was blocked by network policy in this environment; facts below are drawn from WebSearch result summaries of those same pages, and some details (exact Sunday hours, precise Wednesday closure pattern) vary slightly between sources and could not be cross-verified against the raw page text.
closed_days: Wednesdays (reported by some sources as specifically the 2nd, 4th, and 5th Wednesdays of the month)
cuisine: 天ぷら (Tempura, Edomae-style, course-only)
cultural_comfort_note: A formal, century-old course-only tempura restaurant with private tatami seating; no English menu or English-language service was confirmed in available sources, so some Japanese ability or reservation assistance is recommended.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour batter, or allergy-accommodation information was found for this restaurant. As a traditional Edomae tempura specialist, the batter is presumed wheat-flour based and the dipping sauces (tentsuyu) and dashi are presumed to use standard soy sauce (which typically contains wheat) and bonito-based stock; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free options were mentioned in any source reviewed. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation by phone.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:15), Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00), Monday-Saturday; one source describes Sundays/holidays as dinner-only (~17:00-21:30). Closed Wednesdays, with at least one source specifying only the 2nd, 4th, and 5th Wednesdays of the month are closed. Course-menu only (no a la carte).
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (Shinjuku Station West Exit, inside Odakyu Halc)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No product-level confirmation was found on whether the tentsuyu dipping sauce uses standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce or a gluten-free alternative; as a traditional Edomae tempura restaurant it should be assumed to use standard soy sauce unless confirmed otherwise by phone.

seats_text: 29 seats, including two counter areas and a private tatami room seating up to 6
vegan_detail: The restaurant is a fixed-course tempura specialist featuring shrimp, fish, and other seafood tempura, with dashi presumed to be bonito (katsuobushi) based per standard Edomae tempura practice. No vegan course, substitution option, or plant-based menu was found in any source reviewed. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 韓国料理 Korean Dining 彩
address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸2-11-15

bio.background: A small, family-run Korean home-cooking (韓国家庭料理) restaurant tucked into a quiet residential corner of Hanakawado, a few minutes' walk from Asakusa Station, known among neighborhood regulars for hearty, vegetable-forward meals.
bio.specialty: 生野菜のせビビンバ (fresh vegetable bibimbap) and 石焼きビビンバ (sizzling stone-pot bibimbap)
bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes carefully selected ingredients, unhurried preparation, and cooking technique built up over years of experience, aiming to give guests a relaxed, home-style dining experience.

anecdotes.text: Local Asakusa guides recommend it specifically for its generous, vegetable-packed bibimbap lunch set.

caveats: Traditional Korean home-cooking menu (bulgogi, sundubu jjigae, budae jjigae, cheese dakgalbi) alongside a vegetable-rich fresh bibimbap. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan accommodations, allergy protocol, or English menu were confirmed on any source checked — good for a vegetable-forward Korean meal, but celiac/vegan diners should confirm ingredients (gochujang, broths) with staff directly, likely in Japanese.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜定休)
cuisine: Korean home cooking (韓国家庭料理) — bibimbap, jjigae hot pots, bulgogi
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood Korean home-cooking spot with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — basic Japanese or pointing at the handwritten/blackboard menu will help.
geocode_note: Address (Hanakawado 2-chome, Taito-ku) is consistently described across sources as ~5 minutes' walk from Asakusa Station (Ginza Line) and near Hanakawado Park, placing it well within 0.8km of Sensōji Temple (Asakusa). Coordinates left null per instructions; geocoding to be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol was mentioned on any source checked. Korean seasonings used across the menu (gochujang, soy-based sauces) are typically wheat-based, and dishes like jjigae hot pots and cheese dakgalbi likely involve shared cooking surfaces/pots. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering; nothing here should be assumed safe without confirmation.
name_ja: 韓国料理 Korean Dining 彩（だいにんぐ彩）
neighborhood: 浅草・花川戸 (Asakusa / Hanakawado), Taito-ku
seats_text: 25 seats; private group bookings (貸切) possible
vegan_detail: The restaurant offers a fresh vegetable bibimbap (生野菜のせビビンバ), but this is a general Korean home-cooking menu, not a vegan or vegetarian establishment — it also serves bulgogi, sundubu jjigae, budae jjigae, samgyetang, and cheese dakgalbi. Jjigae/soup bases and bibimbap toppings likely include meat, egg, or seafood-derived stock unless specifically requested otherwise, and no vegan-specific accommodation is advertised.

lead: 渋谷横丁 北海道食市
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神宮前6丁目20番地 RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK South 1F（渋谷横丁内）

bio.origin: Hokkaido regional food concept, part of the Shibuya Yokocho food-alley complex
bio.background: This stall sits inside Shibuya Yokocho, a roughly 100-meter indoor alley of about 19 stalls on the south side of RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, built by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho to recreate the atmosphere of a regional Japanese yokocho drinking alley, bringing craftspeople and specialties from Hokkaido down to Kyushu and Okinawa together in one place. This particular stall is dedicated to Hokkaido, and specifically Tokachi-area, food and drink culture.
bio.specialty: Sapporo-style miso ramen, Hokkaido seafood rice bowl (kaisendon), jingisukan (grilled seasoned lamb), Tokachi-style pork bowl (butadon), and Tokachi gyoza dumplings
roles: Hokkaido specialty izakaya stall within a multi-region yokocho food hall

caveats: This is a Hokkaido regional izakaya/food-hall stall (ramen, gyoza, jingisukan, seafood/pork bowls) with no evidence anywhere of gluten-free or vegan accommodation; menu is wheat- and animal-protein-heavy. Seats and precise founding story could not be confirmed because several source pages (hamakura-style.com, harao.tokyo, hitosara.com, the official shibuya-yokocho.com site) returned 403 errors and could not be fetched directly; facts here rely on search-result snippets of those and other pages (Tabelog, Mitsui Shopping Park official listing).
closed_days: No regular closed day stated (operates daily; hours vary by day of week)
cuisine: Hokkaido regional izakaya - miso ramen, seafood donburi, jingisukan, Tokachi pork bowl and gyoza
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, all-Japanese-style yokocho izakaya stall inside a busy Shibuya food hall; no confirmed English menu or staff English support was found, so basic Japanese food vocabulary helps.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null. Address confirms location inside RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK South 1F, Jingumae 6-chome, Shibuya-ku — a well-known complex roughly a 2-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station and well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble crossing.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found in any source. The menu is centered on wheat-based items — ramen noodles, gyoza dumplings — plus soy-sauce-seasoned Hokkaido izakaya dishes (jingisukan, donburi), and Japanese soy sauce/shoyu typically contains wheat. No dedicated fryer, wheat-free noodle option, or allergy protocol is mentioned anywhere.
hours_raw: 月〜土・祝日 11:00〜翌5:00、日 11:00〜23:00（営業時間は予告なく変更となる場合がございます）
name_ja: 渋谷横丁 北海道食市
neighborhood: Jingumae / RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, Shibuya
vegan_detail: No vegan option found in any source. The menu is meat- and seafood-driven (grilled lamb jingisukan, pork bowl, Hokkaido seafood donburi, chicken dishes), and Hokkaido izakaya broths/sauces commonly use dashi or bonito-based seasoning. No plant-based menu items or substitutions are indicated anywhere.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-19-9 MMビル1F

bio.background: A small, no-frills Taiwanese home-cooking (台湾家庭料理) restaurant just steps from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, identifiable by its green exterior and red Chinese lanterns. It has a lived-in, casual izakaya feel with Chinese-language chatter that regulars say makes it feel like a trip to Taiwan itself, and it gets especially busy after events let out at nearby Tokyo Dome.
bio.specialty: Water-boiled dumplings (水餃子) with a soft, springy skin flavored with chives, ginger, and sesame oil, and meatballs (肉団子) with a fine-textured, juicy filling studded with scallop for extra umami; crispy pan-fried gyoza are also a favorite.

anecdotes.text: JR水道橋駅西口の改札を出てすぐのところにある台湾家庭料理店で、緑の外壁に紅い中国提灯が特徴。中国語が飛び交い、台湾にいるような雰囲気がある。
水餃子は皮柔らかめでツルした食感、ニラと生姜、ごま油が効いている。肉団子はきめ細かな生地で肉汁たっぷり、貝柱がコロコロ入り旨味たっぷり(2025年2月の口コミ)。
東京ドームでのイベント後に賑わう、水道橋駅から徒歩数分の台湾料理店。

caveats: No official website found; confirmed via Tabelog, restaurant aggregator listings (Retty, Gurunavi, Hitosara, Hot Pepper), and blog/review snippets accessed via web search since direct page fetches were blocked (403) by most listing sites. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere for this restaurant — it is an ordinary Taiwanese home-cooking izakaya with no dietary-accommodation marketing; GF/vegan diners should assume standard risk (wheat-based soy sauce, shared fryers, dashi/meat stock likely in many dishes) and ask staff directly, ideally with Japanese-language help.
closed_days: Monday (月曜日)
cuisine: Taiwanese cuisine (台湾料理・台湾家庭料理)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, cash-only neighborhood spot with no English menu or online booking evident; basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will be needed to order and to ask about ingredients.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address confirmed as 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-19-9, a roughly 2-4 minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit per multiple sources, well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free options are mentioned in any source. As a standard Taiwanese eatery, dishes such as gyoza/dumpling wrappers, soy sauce, and fried items should be assumed to contain wheat; cross-contamination risk is likely high given the small, busy kitchen. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff (Japanese needed).
neighborhood: 水道橋駅・千代田区神田三崎町
seats_text: Not specified in available sources (small, casual izakaya-style seating)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options, labeling, or accommodations are mentioned in any source. This is a meat- and seafood-heavy home-style Taiwanese menu (pork dumplings, meatballs with scallop, etc.); dishes very likely use meat/bonito-based stocks throughout. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners without extensive kitchen customization, which was not indicated as available.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿5-17-6 三光町ハイム B1F

bio.background: The restaurant is run as a small family business; the owner's husband is French, and the French home cooking she learned for her own family became the basis of the restaurant's menu.
bio.specialty: Duck confit (鴨のコンフィ) — fittingly, "canard" is French for duck, the restaurant's namesake
bio.philosophy: Casual, unpretentious French home cooking (家庭料理) in generous portions, drawing on recipes from across France's regions, served in a relaxed bistro atmosphere rather than a formal grand-maison setting.

anecdotes.text: Housed in a basement space down a Shinjuku alley, the restaurant is described as a hidden bistro-style spot about five minutes' walk from Isetan Shinjuku, popular for its hearty, home-style French cooking and warm, homey atmosphere — including for birthday celebrations.
A blogger described CANARD as a Shinjuku-Sanchome spot with generous French home cooking and a friendly, chatty madame running the room.

caveats: Full menus and allergy/dietary details could not be directly fetched (official site, Gorp, Hotpepper, and Yelp pages returned 403 errors in this environment); information here is drawn from search-result summaries of those pages and from Tabelog's listing. Hours, seat count, and address are corroborated across multiple sources but should be reconfirmed by phone (03-3200-0706) before visiting, especially for dietary accommodations, which this small home-style French bistro shows no evidence of formally offering.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (月曜日・火曜日)
cuisine: フレンチ（フランス家庭料理）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, 19-seat neighborhood bistro run by a Japanese owner with a French husband; no English menu or English-language service was confirmed, so basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation process is mentioned in any available source. As traditional French home cooking, the menu (duck confit, meat and fish mains, cream/butter-based sauces, bread service) relies heavily on wheat — sauces are typically roux-thickened and bread accompanies meals — so cross-contact and hidden wheat risk should be assumed high. Celiac diners should not rely on this restaurant without calling ahead, and even then should expect limited or no safe options.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（Shinjuku Sanchome）
seats_text: 19 seats, including counter seating
vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat and fish (duck confit is the namesake dish — "canard" means duck in French) with butter- and cream-based French sauces; no vegan, vegetarian, or plant-based options, and no mention of dashi/bonito use since this is French rather than Japanese cuisine, but animal-based stocks and butter are standard throughout. No vegan accommodation was found in any available source.

lead: しゃぶしゃぶ れたす 渋谷センター街店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町28-2 ニュー渋谷ビル2階

bio.origin: Part of the "Shabu-shabu Retasu" chain, which opened its first shop in Nakameguro, Tokyo in November 2017 before expanding to Shibuya, Sapporo, Musashi-Kosugi and Susukino.
bio.background: Shabu-shabu Retasu built its concept around "one person, one pot" (一人一鍋) — rather than sharing a communal hot pot, every guest gets their own individual pot so they can pick their own broth, dipping sauce, and pace. The Shibuya Center-gai branch sits two minutes from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit in a wood-toned dining room.
bio.specialty: Individually-portioned shabu-shabu built around fresh vegetables sourced directly from contract farms (around 20 varieties) paired with aged beef tongue, finished with a choice of noodles including a gluten-free Chinese-noodle option.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's stated commitment is to a "drinkable dashi" — an additive-free stock made from five kinds of carefully selected bonito flakes — paired with eight broth styles and eight dipping sauces so each diner can compose the meal to their own taste rather than following a fixed course.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant describes offering 8 types of broth and roughly 50 menu items centered on fresh vegetables, with finishing options spanning Chinese noodles, a gluten-free Chinese noodle, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto.

The concept page frames the restaurant's core promise as an additive-free, bonito-based "drinkable" dashi made from five selected types of katsuobushi, alongside 20 varieties of vegetables from contract farms.

caveats: This is a chain restaurant (not an independent mom-and-pop shop) — Nakameguro is the flagship, with branches in Shibuya, Sapporo, Musashi-Kosugi and Susukino. Confirmed facts (address, hours, seats, GF noodle option, bonito-based dashi) come from Tabelog/Hot Pepper/official-site search snippets, since the official site and Tabelog blocked direct page fetches (403) — only search-engine summaries of those pages could be retrieved, not the raw HTML. No page could be fetched to confirm whether the dipping sauces (tare) are wheat-free, or whether any of the 8 broths is dashi-free/vegan; treat gf_confidence and vegan_status accordingly as conservative estimates pending an in-person check.
closed_days: No regular closed day found — search results describe it as open every day (全日) of the week.
cuisine: Shabu-shabu (individual-pot hot pot with farm-direct vegetables and aged beef tongue)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern casual chain restaurant popular with young, mixed-language Shibuya crowds; basic Japanese or pointing at the picture-based menu should be enough, though no English menu was confirmed.
geocode_note: Address (東京都渋谷区宇田川町28-2, New Shibuya Building 2F) and multiple sources place it ~2 minutes' walk from JR Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, in Center-gai — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing landmark.
gf_detail: The finishing-course menu explicitly lists a gluten-free Chinese noodle alongside the regular Chinese noodle, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto options, so a specific GF starch option exists. However, no source confirms whether the 8 dipping sauces (tare) or any of the 8 broths are made with wheat-free soy sauce, and no allergy/cross-contamination policy could be found. Each guest's shabu-shabu is cooked in their own individual pot (not a shared communal pot), which may reduce cross-contact risk versus a shared hot pot, but this was not explicitly stated as an allergy safeguard by the restaurant. Celiac diners should confirm tare/broth ingredients with staff before ordering.
hours_raw: 12:00-22:00 daily (L.O. food 21:30, last entry 21:00)
name_ja: しゃぶしゃぶ れたす 渋谷センター街店
neighborhood: 渋谷 宇田川町（センター街）

gf_cross_contamination.text: Each guest is served their own individual shabu-shabu pot rather than sharing one communal pot with the table, which is the core '一人一鍋' (one person, one pot) concept of the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant's signature dashi is described as an additive-free stock made from five kinds of selected bonito (katsuobushi) flakes, used across its broths.

positives.text: A gluten-free Chinese noodle is offered as one of the finishing-course choices, alongside regular noodles, pho, egg porridge, and cheese risotto.

seats_text: Around 50 seats total, with tables sized for 2, 4, 6, or 8 guests; the whole restaurant can be booked for private parties up to 50 people.
vegan_detail: The signature dashi across the restaurant's broths is bonito(katsuobushi)-based, not plant-based, and the specialty dish (aged beef tongue) is meat. No source confirms a dashi-free or fully vegetable-based broth among the 8 options, or a vegan-labeled tare. Vegetables are a major focus (about 20 farm-direct varieties, sold on a fresh vegetable platter) and could be eaten raw/plain, but a vegan diner would need to ask staff which broth (if any) and sauces avoid fish and animal products.

lead: 炭火串焼ヒヨク之トリ 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-15-11 西角ビル

bio.origin: Second branch of the Azabu Juban original, which has repeatedly been named to Tabelog's Top 100 Yakitori Restaurants in Japan (2018, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2024).
bio.background: Hiyoku no Tori opened its Suidobashi branch in July 2020 as the second location of its acclaimed Azabu Juban yakitori restaurant, bringing the same charcoal-grilling craft to diners near Tokyo Dome.
bio.specialty: Kōshū Shingen-dori (a prized branded chicken from Yamanashi) grilled skewer-by-skewer over top-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal; the restaurant has also run limited menus featuring the rare "Tokyo Shamo" heritage game fowl.
bio.philosophy: Skilled grillers cook each skewer individually over binchotan to bring out the chicken's full flavor, pairing the food with original sours (including a whole-lemon sour) designed to complement grilled chicken; a strong ventilation system is emphasized so guests don't leave smelling of smoke.

anecdotes.text: For its first anniversary, the Suidobashi branch ran a limited membership/course offering that let guests try both the rare 'Tokyo Shamo' heritage chicken and its usual Kōshū Shingen-dori side by side.

The Azabu Juban flagship has been selected for Tabelog's 'Top 100 Yakitori Restaurants' list multiple years running (2018, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2024), and the Suidobashi branch was opened to bring that same grilling craft near Tokyo Dome.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source; this is a traditional charcoal yakitori (chicken skewer) restaurant. Seat count could not be confirmed from independent sources (only general descriptions of counter and table seating). Hours/closed-day information reflects a mix of sources and a note that the shop moved to irregular (不定休) closures at some point, so calling ahead is recommended.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); some listings note Sunday as a typical off day, but sources indicate the shop shifted to irregular closed days — confirm before visiting.
cuisine: Yakitori (charcoal-grilled chicken skewers)
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood yakitori counter aimed mostly at local and Japanese-speaking diners, with menus and staff interaction primarily in Japanese, though online reservation platforms (Hotpepper, Ikyu, Autoreserve) offer some English-adjacent booking support.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address places it on Hakusan-dori just south of Tokyo Dome, about a 2-minute walk from the west exit of JR Suidobashi Station — well within the Suidōbashi Station target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. Traditional yakitori tare (glaze) is soy-sauce based and contains wheat; even shio (salt-only) skewers share the same binchotan grill as tare-basted skewers, so cross-contact is likely. Diners with celiac disease should not assume safety here without calling ahead and should expect no dedicated gluten-free accommodation.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:20); Sat/Sun/Holidays: 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:20). Some sources note a shift to irregular (不定休) closed days — confirm current schedule before visiting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All skewers, whether salt (shio) or sauce (tare) based, are grilled on the same shared binchotan charcoal grill, so cross-contact between wheat-containing tare and other skewers is likely; no dedicated gluten-free grilling process was found in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's tare glaze for yakitori is a traditional soy-sauce-based sauce, which in standard Japanese yakitori preparation contains wheat.

positives.text: The restaurant highlights a strong ventilation/smoke-extraction system for the dining room, though this addresses odor/comfort rather than food allergens.

seats_text: Counter seating (L-shaped counter) plus table seating, including tables for groups of 4; exact total seat count not confirmed by any source.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling found. This is a chicken-skewer specialty restaurant; the menu is meat-centric, and no confirmation of vegan-suitable side dishes, dashi-free preparations, or bonito/katsuo-free options was found in any source.

bio.background: D.I.Y. SALAD & DELICATESSEN is a made-to-order chopped-salad and deli counter tucked into the basement food floor of LUMINE 1 at Shinjuku Station, run by the ADemotion delicatessen brand group (Cardenas Co.). It was built around the idea of letting time-pressed Shinjuku office workers and shoppers assemble their own bowl — base, protein, vegetables, toppings and dressing — rather than choosing from a fixed menu.
bio.specialty: The 'Super D.I.Y. Special' build-your-own chopped salad, piled with a large volume of vegetables and a choice of meat or deli toppings.
bio.philosophy: Positioned as a health-conscious, nutrient-dense alternative to typical station fast food — the shop markets its food as gluten-free-friendly, refined-sugar-free and additive-free, aimed at health-minded women and commuters wanting a customizable, vegetable-forward meal on the go.

anecdotes.text: A Shinjuku-area lifestyle magazine profiled it as a hugely popular, nutrient-packed salad specialty shop inside LUMINE Shinjuku, highlighting its healthy positioning and popularity with local shoppers.
A food blogger who visited for lunch described it as a chopped-salad specialist inside LUMINE Shinjuku offering a substantial vegetable-based lunch with build-your-own toppings and dressings.

caveats: Most details (hours, address, dietary framing) come from aggregated search-result summaries of Tabelog, LUMINE, and food-media pages rather than a full direct fetch of each page (direct page fetches were blocked in this environment), so treat fine print (exact seat count, precise dressing ingredients, card brands accepted) as unconfirmed until checked in person or by phone (03-6304-5774). No page directly itemized whether soy sauce/dashi or specific dressings contain wheat or fish-derived ingredients — ask staff before ordering if celiac or strictly vegan.
closed_days: Irregular — follows LUMINE Shinjuku (LUMINE 1) building holiday schedule, not fixed weekly closures
cuisine: Customizable Salad, Delicatessen, Vegetables
cultural_comfort_note: A mall food-hall counter inside LUMINE Shinjuku with an English-language Tabelog listing and simple point-and-build ordering, so basic English/pointing should get you through, though menu boards and dressing ingredient details are likely Japanese-only.
gf_detail: The shop is marketed (via its operator's brand page and a Shinjuku wheat/dairy/egg-free dining roundup) as offering gluten-free, refined-sugar-free and additive-free items, and the build-your-own format lets diners skip bread, croutons or breaded toppings. However, this is a shared deli counter also handling meat, bread-adjacent items and prepared dressings, and no source itemizes which dressings contain soy sauce or other wheat-derived ingredients, or describes dedicated equipment/cross-contact protocols. Celiac diners should ask staff directly which toppings and dressings are wheat-free before ordering.
hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 daily (hours follow LUMINE Shinjuku LUMINE 1's operating hours; closures are irregular and follow the building's holiday schedule)
name_ja: D.I.Y. salad & delicatessen（ディーアイワイ サラダ アンド デリカテッセン）
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (LUMINE 1 B2F, directly at Shinjuku Station south side)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source describes a dedicated gluten-free prep area; the counter also prepares meat and other deli items alongside salads, so cross-contact for gluten and other allergens has not been independently confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Store listings note the shop accommodates vegan diners and allergy-related requests alongside its gluten-free-friendly, additive-free positioning, but do not describe a formal allergy protocol.

positives.text: Marketed as offering gluten-free, refined-sugar-free and additive-free food and drink options for health-conscious customers.
The build-your-own ('D.I.Y.') format lets diners choose every topping and dressing themselves, making it easier to avoid specific ingredients compared to a fixed-menu restaurant.

seats_text: Counter-style seating only (exact seat count not published); takeout is also offered for eating elsewhere in or around Shinjuku Station.

Exact dressing recipes (e.g., whether any contain honey, dashi, or other animal-derived ingredients) are not published online, so vegan diners should confirm dressing ingredients with staff at the counter.

lead: ラーメン 龍の家 新宿小滝橋通り店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-4-5 藤野ビル1F

bio.origin: Kurume, Fukuoka — birthplace of tonkotsu ramen
bio.background: Ramen Tatsunoya (龍の家) was founded in Kurume, Fukuoka in 1999 by staff who had trained at Hakata's famed Ippudo. The Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori branch began life in September 2007 as a stall in the Tobu Department Store Ikebukuro ramen food court before relocating to its current roadside spot on Otakibashi-dori on July 11, 2009 — the brand's first standalone Kanto shop. The chain has since grown to about a dozen locations across Kyushu, Tokyo, and overseas, and this Shinjuku shop sits among the neighborhood's ramen heavyweights (Ramen Jiro, Ichiran, Menya Musashi, Mongoru Tanmen Nakamoto).
bio.specialty: Tsukemen motsu — offal (motsu) dipping noodles in a pork-bone base, an item exclusive to this Tokyo shop — alongside the shop's core tonkotsu ramen made from pork bones simmered into a rich, mild-flavored soup.
bio.philosophy: Rather than following the traditional Kurume 'yobimodoshi' method of continuously topping up the tonkotsu stock, Tatsunoya uses a 'torikiri' approach, cooking down and using up each day's broth in full. The brand describes its style as 'Neo Kurume ramen' — noodles 'born in Hakata, raised in Kurume.'

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori shop opened in its current location on July 11, 2009, after starting as a 2007 department-store food-court stall in Ikebukuro.
Tatsunoya was founded in Kurume 26-27 years ago by ramen makers who trained at Hakata's Ippudo, and has grown to roughly 12 shops across Kyushu, Tokyo and overseas.
The Otakibashi-dori shop is described as expressing 'the spirit of Kurume, without resting on tradition' through its signature tsukemen.

roles: Tonkotsu ramen and tsukemen specialist, first Kanto branch of the Kurume-founded Tatsunoya chain

caveats: Hours could not be independently confirmed via direct page fetch (only search-result snippets were accessible); several secondary sources give slightly different closing times (22:00 vs 23:30 vs midnight) though all agree on an 11:00 open and a weekly Tuesday closure. Building name in the address also appears variously transliterated (藤野ビル / 富士野ビル) across listings.
closed_days: Tuesdays (open on public holidays)
cuisine: Tonkotsu ramen and tsukemen with pork offal (Kurume/Kyushu-style)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine typical of Japanese ramen shops, but the location is well used to international visitors and often has lines of inbound tourists.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; lat/lng left null. Address (Nishi-Shinjuku 7-4-5) and multiple sources confirming a 5-8 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station place it well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol is mentioned in any source. Noodles are standard wheat noodles and the tonkotsu broth/shoyu-based tare very likely contain wheat (soy sauce). Not a safe choice for celiac or wheat-avoidant diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, closed Tuesdays (open on public holidays); some secondary listings give a later close (up to 23:30 or midnight) so exact closing time should be confirmed on arrival

neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku / West Shinjuku)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tare/seasoning is shoyu (soy-sauce) based tonkotsu, which in standard Japanese ramen preparation contains wheat; no wheat-free tare or noodle alternative is documented for this shop.

seats_text: Reported at roughly 18 seats total (counter plus table seating); one source instead lists 9 counter seats and 2 tables, so the exact split is uncertain but overall size is small/casual.
vegan_detail: Every broth on the menu is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, and the shop's signature dish is offal (motsu) tsukemen. No plant-based broth, vegetarian, or vegan menu items are mentioned in any source found.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-19-6 津田ソシアルビル1F

bio.origin: 東京都生まれ (Tokyo)
bio.background: Katsumi was founded in 1976 (Showa 51) by the current owner's father as a neighborhood kappo restaurant near Dogenzaka-ue. Tsuda Katsuyuki, born in 1975, grew up watching his father cook and naturally set his sights on becoming a chef himself. He trained at Kaiseki Kawabata and Hanagoyomi in Roppongi before joining the family restaurant, and became the second-generation owner in 2009 when his father retired.
bio.specialty: きんき煮付 (simmered kinki/thornhead rockfish) — the restaurant's most popular dish, prepared in an old-fashioned, gently sweet-savory style; the lunchtime oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) is also a well-known specialty.
bio.philosophy: Tsuda goes to Tsukiji himself every morning to hand-select fish, and the kitchen emphasizes simple, ingredient-forward washoku built on traditional kappo fundamentals rather than novelty.

anecdotes.text: 津田勝幸は1975年に東京都生まれ。料理人をしている父親の後ろ姿を見て育ち、自然と料理の道を志した。懐石川端と六本木の花ごよみにて修業した後、実家である季節割烹 勝味に入店した。2009年に父親である先代の隠居と共に二代目店主に就任した。
毎日店主が築地に出向き、目利きを働かせて仕入れた鮮度の良い魚を6〜7品盛り合わせた豪華な一皿を提供している。一番人気は「きんき煮付」で、昔ながらの優しい味が名物料理となっている。
昭和51年創業の歴史ある味を800円から提供 — restaurant opened in Showa 51 (1976) and is known for its long-standing, affordable lunch dishes.

caveats: This is a traditional seafood-focused kappo restaurant with no confirmed vegan or gluten-free accommodations found in any source; the signature dish is a simmered fish and the kitchen relies on daily fresh fish from the market, so it is not a good fit for strict vegan or celiac-level gluten-free diners. Direct-fetch of the restaurant's own site (kappou-katsumi.com / katsumi-shibuya.com) and Tabelog returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all facts below were corroborated via search-engine snippets of those pages plus independent listings (Hitosara, Hot Pepper) rather than a full page read; core facts (address, hours, chef history, seat count) were each corroborated across at least two independent sources.
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日 (Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays)
cuisine: 日本料理・季節割烹（旬の魚介中心の家庭的な和食）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood kappo restaurant run by a Japanese-speaking family; no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found, so first-timers should expect a Japanese-only experience.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions; lat/lng left null. Address (東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-19-6) and multiple sources describing it as 6-8 minutes' walk from JR/Ginza-line Shibuya Station and 5 minutes from Keio Inokashira-line Shinsen Station place it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free labeling, or dedicated preparation was mentioned in any source found. As a traditional kappo restaurant, dishes very likely use standard shoyu (contains wheat), dashi (often bonito-based, sometimes with mirin/soy), and possibly tempura or breaded items, with no indication of a gluten-free alternative or dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should not assume safety and would need to ask directly (in Japanese) before visiting.
neighborhood: 道玄坂 / 神泉 (Dogenzaka / Shinsen, Shibuya)
seats_text: 総席数45席（Lカウンター、テーブル、座敷あり／宴会最大着席20名）
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-friendly claim found in any source. The restaurant's identity centers on daily fresh fish from the market (sashimi platters, simmered kinki, seafood-based izakaya-style dishes), and dashi in traditional kappo cooking is typically bonito (katsuo)-based, so hidden animal-derived ingredients should be assumed in soups, simmered dishes, and sauces unless a diner confirms otherwise directly with staff.

lead: 渋谷西村フルーツパーラー 道玄坂店

bio.background: Nishimura Fruit Parlor traces back to 1910 (Meiji 43), when it began as a high-end fruit shop in Kagomachi (present-day Koishikawa, Bunkyo Ward). The Dogenzaka shop opened in 1935, and the fruit parlor on its 2nd floor was added the following year, 1936. Today the 1st floor houses the flagship fruit shop selling premium seasonal fruit for gifts and personal use, while the 2nd-floor parlor serves fruit parfaits and fruit sandwiches, one minute's walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, just past the Scramble Crossing.
bio.origin: Kagomachi (Koishikawa), Tokyo, 1910; Dogenzaka location since 1935
bio.philosophy: The kitchen prizes each fruit at its seasonal peak, changing the menu roughly seven times a year so parfaits and sandwiches always feature fruit at its best.
bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit sandwich (フルーツサンド) and fruit parfaits built around whichever fruits are in peak season
roles: Long-established family fruit merchant and parlor

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1910 as a high-end fruit shop; the Dogenzaka store opened in 1935 with the parlor added in 1936. The menu is changed about seven times a year to serve fruit at its most delicious moment.
The 'Special Seasonal Fruit Sandwich,' combining Tochiotome strawberries with mixed fruit across two sandwich types, is a signature item at roughly 1,880 yen.

caveats: The official Tabelog and shop-owned pages (tabelog.com, snfruits.com, shibuyadogenzaka.com) returned 403 Forbidden on direct fetch, so details are corroborated via web search summaries of those and third-party aggregator pages (Gnavi, Navitime, Rurubu, Retty, Entabe, Loveretro, Tripadvisor) rather than direct page reads. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan program was found anywhere; this is a mainstream fruit-dessert parlor, not a diet-specialized spot, so celiac/vegan diners should verify specifics with staff before ordering.

cuisine: フルーツパーラー・パフェ・フルーツサンド (fruit parlor: parfaits and fruit sandwiches)
cultural_comfort_note: English menus are available and the spot is a well-known tourist stop right off Shibuya Scramble Crossing, but staff English is limited, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodation was found. The signature fruit sandwiches are made with regular wheat bread, and parfaits often include cake, cookie, or wafer elements; menu allergen labels for several items list 小麦 (wheat) alongside 乳・卵・アーモンド (dairy, egg, almond). Not celiac-safe without direct staff consultation, and no dedicated gluten-free process or fryer was documented.

Address and multiple sources place the shop one minute's walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, immediately past the Scramble Crossing — well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble (35.6595,139.7005) target radius.

lead: 讃岐うどん大使 水道橋麺通団
address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷1-13-2

roles: Managed by Shinji Yamanaka (山中信二), a native of Kagawa Prefecture and former member of the original "Mentsudan" (麺通団) group
bio.background: Suidobashi Mentsudan opened in July 2009 as the second Tokyo branch of the Mentsudan lineage — the group whose 1990s guerrilla udon-hunting guidebooks (published out of Kagawa Prefecture) is credited with sparking Tokyo's Sanuki udon boom. The manager, a Kagawa native, worked at the original Nishi-Shinjuku "Tokyo Mentsudan" before opening "Kichijoji Mentsudan" in 2007 and then this Suidobashi shop, located in the business district across from Tokyo Dome City, in 2009.
bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (the birthplace of Sanuki udon)
bio.specialty: Kake udon finished with iriko (dried-sardine) dashi poured from a self-serve tap on the wall, and the shop's popular mentai kama-tama udon (mentaiko and raw egg tossed with hot noodles).
bio.philosophy: Bring the real, no-frills self-service Sanuki udon experience to Tokyo — order your noodles, help yourself to tempura and sides from the counter, and pay at the register at the end, just as at the honke (originator) shops in Kagawa.

anecdotes.text: Diners pour their own iriko dashi from a tap mounted on the wall before eating, part of the shop's deliberately rustic, self-service Sanuki-style setup.

The shop is the second Tokyo offshoot of the original "Mentsudan" group, opened in July 2009 by a Kagawa-native manager who had previously run the Nishi-Shinjuku Tokyo Mentsudan and the Kichijoji branch.

caveats: Hours could not be independently confirmed by direct page fetch (Tabelog and the shop's own channels returned 403 to automated fetch); the 10:00-15:00 lunch hours are corroborated by multiple independent search citations of the Tabelog listing, but an older (c.2009-era) blog cited 10:00-23:30, which may be outdated. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this shop — it is a traditional wheat-based Sanuki udon specialist with a fish-based (iriko) dashi, included here for completeness/transparency rather than as a GF/vegan recommendation.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular/no fixed closed day)
cuisine: うどん（讃岐うどん / Sanuki udon, self-service style）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a self-service Sanuki udon shop: order and receive your noodles first, help yourself to tempura and side dishes from the counter, then pay at the register — the multi-step flow can be confusing on a first visit, but pointing at the picture menu works fine.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour Sanuki udon specialist — the noodles themselves are made from wheat, and no gluten-free noodle, rice-noodle substitute, or allergen menu was found in any source. Tempura sides (self-served from a shared counter) are fried in a shared fryer alongside wheat-battered items, and shoyu-based dipping sauces are standard. No dedicated fryer, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or staff allergy protocol was found. Not suitable for a celiac or wheat-allergic diner.

Dry/tossed styles like bukkake or zaru with soy-based tare might reduce but not eliminate animal-derived ingredients (shoyu-based tare can itself contain bonito extract), and this was not confirmed either way — not recommended for a strict vegan diner without asking staff directly (Japanese-only environment).

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-12-23 松沢ビル2F

roles: Kushiyaki (grilled skewer) izakaya specializing in Oyama-dori chicken
bio.background: A small, second-floor hideaway a few minutes from Shinjuku Station's west exit, done up in a Japanese folk-craft (mingei) style with a counter plus a few tables — the kind of unassuming local spot regulars duck into after work.
bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Oyama-dori (大山鶏) chicken sashimi served with wasabi, alongside skewers such as breast with wasabi, plum-shiso, cheese, or yuzu-kosho, plus thigh, negima, tsukune, and skin — grilled using a combination of charcoal and electric heat to bring out the chicken's flavor.
bio.philosophy: Built around a single high-quality chicken breed (Oyama-dori) and careful skewer-by-skewer grilling rather than a sprawling menu.

anecdotes.text: Skewers start from around 150 yen — inexpensive for the Shinjuku area — with a typical dinner running about 4,000-4,999 yen.
The restaurant is known for its chicken sashimi from Oyama-dori chicken, dressed simply with wasabi, as its signature dish.

caveats: This is a meat-focused (chicken) kushiyaki izakaya, not a vegan or dedicated gluten-free establishment — no vegan menu items were found, and there is no confirmed gluten-free protocol (tare sauce is soy-sauce/wheat-based; tsukune may contain wheat-based binder; shared charcoal/electric grill). GF diners could ask for shio (salt) preparations to avoid tare, but cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Sources gave slightly inconsistent wording on the Monday closure rule (some list it as a plain weekly off-day, others specify "closed Sundays and any Monday that is a public holiday" implying Monday is normally open) — reflected here as a regular Mon-Sat schedule with Sunday plus holiday-Mondays off.
closed_days: Sundays; also Mondays that fall on a public holiday
cuisine: Kushiyaki (grilled chicken skewers) / Izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A small local izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and no evidence of English-speaking service, though its Tabelog listing does have an English page for browsing before you go.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is disabled in this environment; lat/lng left null. Address confirmed as 東京都新宿区西新宿7-12-23 松沢ビル2F (Matsuzawa Building, 2F), Nishi-Shinjuku 7-chome. Multiple sources give distances of ~190m (3 min) from Shinjuku Nishiguchi (west exit area), ~370m (5 min) from JR Shinjuku Station, and ~380m (5 min) from Seibu-Shinjuku Station — all well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free protocol or menu was found. As a tare-based kushiyaki shop, the standard basting sauce is soy-sauce based (contains wheat), and tsukune (chicken meatballs) commonly use a wheat-based binder. Ordering skewers shio (salt-grilled, no tare) would reduce but not eliminate wheat exposure, since the same charcoal/electric grill and tare-brushing tools are shared across items. Celiac-level diners should ask staff directly and treat this as a use-caution, not a confirmed-safe, option.
hours_raw: Regularly Mon-Sat 17:30-23:30 (L.O. food 22:30, drinks 23:00); closed Sundays, and closed on Mondays that fall on a public holiday
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (West Shinjuku), near Shinjuku Station west exit / Seibu-Shinjuku Station
seats_text: Small counter plus a few tables, roughly 16 seats total
vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around Oyama-dori chicken (sashimi and grilled skewers); no vegetarian or vegan dishes were identified in any source. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-21-8

bio.background: A neighborhood soba shop in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, founded in 1950. It closed temporarily for seismic reinforcement and renovation work and reopened in March 2026, continuing to serve the same backstreet clientele it always has.
bio.specialty: Hamaguri seiro — clam dipping soba with plump, briny hamaguri clams. Originally offered only in spring, it proved so popular that it became a year-round fixture. Seasonal specialties include kaki nanban (oyster soba) in winter and nishin soba (herring soba).
bio.philosophy: A longtime regular describes the shop's ethos as unpretentious: soba here is everyday comfort food, best enjoyed simply — soba shochu cut with soba-yu (noodle cooking water) alongside a few small dishes, finished with a plate of noodles, rather than a ceremonious affair.

anecdotes.text: A writer recalls being taken to Benten by his grandfather and describes it as a tiny, spotlessly kept shop seating only about a dozen people, where the ritual is soba-yu-wari (soba shochu diluted with the noodles' cooking water) with small dishes before the soba itself — soba, he writes, 'shouldn't be eaten pretentiously, just as everyday comfort food.'
The clam dipping soba (hamaguri seiro) began as a spring-only special but became so popular with regulars that it is now served year-round.
Founded in 1950, the shop closed for seismic reinforcement and renovation work and reopened on March 5, 2026.

caveats: Reopened in March 2026 after seismic-retrofit/renovation work; some recent reports cite "open 11:30 until sold out" rather than the long-standing fixed weekly schedule, so hours may still be settling — call ahead. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented anywhere; this is a traditional wheat-based soba-ya with bonito-dashi broths, not a suitable choice for strict celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: Wednesday (regular closing day per long-standing schedule; possibly adjusted after March 2026 renovation — unconfirmed)
cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles) — Edo-style neighborhood soba-ya, seasonal specialties like hamaguri (clam) and kaki (oyster) soba
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local soba-ya with no confirmed English menu and cash-only payment — an authentic neighborhood experience rather than a tourist-oriented one.
geocode_note: Address (浅草3-21-8) places it in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, well within 0.8km walking distance of Sensōji Temple, matching the target area.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodles or dedicated preparation are mentioned anywhere. This is a traditional Edo-style soba-ya; the noodles are standard soba (not confirmed as 100%/juwari buckwheat), the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce based, and the menu includes tempura. No source mentions a gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation — not a safe choice for strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun 11:30–21:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Wednesdays. Note: following the March 2026 renovation/reopening, some recent listings describe hours simply as "11:30 until sold out," so the fixed closing times above may no longer be exact.
neighborhood: Asakusa Kannon-ura (Yanagi-dori), Asakusa 3-chome
seats_text: Roughly 27 seats total, split across a small counter, table seating, and a raised tatami area
vegan_detail: The menu centers on seafood and meat: clam (hamaguri) soba, oyster (kaki) nanban, herring (nishin) soba, chicken-based mujina soba, and pork katsu dishes. Broths are bonito (katsuobushi) dashi-based. No vegan options, substitutions, or dashi alternatives are mentioned in any source.

lead: チャオタイ 渋谷道玄坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-9-10 K&Kビル2F

bio.origin: Founded in 1993 in Yokohama's Isezaki-chō district; the Shibuya Dōgenzaka shop is one of several branches the group now runs across the Kanto region.
bio.background: Chao Thai opened in Yokohama in 1993, at a time when Thai food was still largely unfamiliar in Japan, and became one of the pioneering authentic Thai restaurants in the Kanto region. It has since grown into a small group of Thai restaurants (Yokohama, Ginza, Shibuya Dogenzaka, Shibuya Axsh and others).
bio.philosophy: The restaurant aims to serve Thai food authentic enough that Thai customers themselves recognize it as 'the real thing,' with Thai cooks preparing the food in a spacious dining room used for everything from casual meals to parties and banquets.
bio.specialty: Pad Thai (including an egg/shrimp version and a vegetable-forward version) and the multi-course 'Chao Thai Course,' plus Tom Yum Kung, green curry, and papaya salad (som tam).

anecdotes.text: Chao Thai holds Thai Select certification, a mark awarded by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce to restaurants outside Thailand that meet its standards for authentic Thai cuisine.

caveats: Seat count and a few operational details vary slightly across secondary listing sites (e.g., ぐるなび lists ~80 seats for this branch, while some aggregator summaries appear to mix in figures from the separate Shibuya Axsh location). No official gluten-free or vegan menu/protocol was found for this restaurant — the entries below reflect general Thai-cuisine caveats (fish sauce, oyster sauce, shrimp paste, possible wheat in soy sauce) rather than confirmed restaurant policy. Direct Tabelog/official-site fetches returned 403s in this environment; facts are drawn from search-result snippets of those and other secondary pages (ぐるなび, ホットペッパー, タイ・セレクト, etc.) rather than full page reads.
closed_days: None stated — open year-round with no fixed weekly closed day (定休日なし)
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known Shibuya Thai restaurant with an English-language Tabelog listing and delivery presence, so some English/tourist accommodation is likely, though staff English ability isn't independently confirmed.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, allergy chart, or celiac-safety protocol was found. As a Thai kitchen, many dishes use rice noodles (e.g., pad thai) which are naturally wheat-free, but soy sauce and other seasonings used in Thai-Japanese cooking can contain wheat, and there is no confirmation of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Anyone with celiac disease should ask staff directly about specific dishes and sauces before ordering.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); no fixed closed day
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked); address_ja captured in full for downstream geocoding. Dogenzaka 2-chome is a short walk (well under 0.8km) from Shibuya Scramble, so the target-area requirement is satisfied by address/landmark proximity.

Vegans should ask staff to confirm that fish sauce, oyster sauce, and shrimp paste are excluded from a specific dish before ordering.

bio.philosophy: Good ingredients are only the starting point — what matters is how they are used to make guests happy.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is built around fish delivered directly from the market and seasonal vegetables, prepared with the philosophy that 'good ingredients are just the starting point — what matters is how we can make our customers happy,' according to owner-chef Kase Masanari.

caveats: No confirmed information on gluten-free or vegan accommodations was found; this is a traditional seafood-focused kappo/izakaya, not a dietary-specialist restaurant. English menu/support and formal reservation requirements were not confirmed — a Japanese-language phone call or Hot Pepper/Gurunavi booking is recommended.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-15-15 東宝観光ビル2F

bio.background: The owner was born and raised on Aogashima, a remote volcanic island in the Izu chain with a population of roughly 160 people. The restaurant opened in Nishi-Shinjuku on March 24, 2014, bringing island food and drink to the mainland for people who cannot easily make the difficult boat/helicopter trip to Aogashima itself.
bio.origin: Aogashima, Izu Islands, Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Faithfully reproduces Aogashima and wider Izu/Ogasawara island home cooking in Tokyo, using seafood, vegetables, salt and shochu sourced from the islands rather than mainland substitutes.
bio.specialty: Aogashima and Izu-island regional dishes such as simmered sea turtle, muro-aji (mackerel scad) fish cutlet, and ashitaba (island celery) with tuna mayonnaise, alongside the island's rare 'ao-chu' shochu.

anecdotes.text: The owner's family runs a minshuku (guesthouse) back on Aogashima and is also involved in producing the island's rare 'ao-chu' shochu, which the restaurant pours in Tokyo.

The restaurant is described as a way to experience Aogashima's isolated island culture and cuisine without leaving West Shinjuku, complete with an aerial photo of the island marking the building entrance.

caveats: This is a traditional Japanese izakaya specializing in Aogashima/Izu-island home cooking (seafood, fried items, soy-sauce-based seasoning). No gluten-free or vegan accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocols could be confirmed in any source despite targeted searching — treat as not dietary-restriction-friendly unless confirmed directly with the restaurant before visiting.
closed_days: Sunday (holiday hours: please inquire directly)
cuisine: Aogashima/Izu-island regional home cooking (郷土料理), izakaya style
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally-run izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and standard izakaya service style; English support is not documented, so some Japanese phrasebook or translation help is useful.

The menu includes breaded, deep-fried items (e.g. muro-aji fish cutlet) and relies on standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-23:30; closed Sunday (holiday hours vary, inquire directly)
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku, near Seibu Shinjuku Station
seats_text: 26 seats (6-seat counter, table seating, and a tatami room)

Not recommended for vegan diners.

lead: クンバ ドゥ ファラフェル
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神泉町23-1 MEビル1F

bio.background: Shibuya's Shinsen neighborhood tucked-away vegan falafel specialty shop; the storefront is unmarked and covered in ivy, giving it a hideaway feel that regulars find largely by word of mouth rather than signage.
bio.specialty: Falafel pita sandwich — housemade pita packed with freshly fried chickpea falafel, roasted eggplant, hummus, and a generous mix of fresh vegetables, arranged so the cross-section looks like a bouquet of flowers.

anecdotes.text: サンドイッチはまるで野菜のブーケのように彩りよく盛り付けられ、見た目は華やかだが食べ応えも十分とレポートされている。
看板もなく蔦に覆われた外観で、知らないと店だと気づかずに通り過ぎてしまうほどの隠れ家的な店構えと紹介されている。
ファラフェルはひよこ豆を主原料とした中東風コロッケで、100%植物性・低カロリーのヴィーガンフードとして紹介されている。

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official pages, or several blog/vegan-directory pages (all returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch); facts below are drawn from Google/web search result snippets that themselves quote or summarize those pages, cross-checked across multiple independent listings (Tabelog, Vegewel, halalgourmet.jp, tokyosandwich.com, Retty, NAVITIME). No page could be directly confirmed to state explicit gluten cross-contamination/frying-oil practices, so gf_confidence is conservatively set to "ask" even though the falafel itself (chickpea-based) is inherently gluten-free — it is normally served in wheat pita, and a no-bread plate/salad option exists per one search summary but was not independently confirmed on a fetched page. One search snippet gave postal code 151-0045 vs. the more consistent 150-0045 (NAVITIME, Vegewel) for the same 神泉町23-1 address — 150-0045 is almost certainly correct and used here. No dedicated official restaurant website was found; an Instagram handle (@kuumbadufalafel) appears to be associated with the shop but was not verifiable via direct fetch, so official_url is left blank. Owner/chef name and any stated restaurant philosophy were not found in available sources.

cuisine: ファラフェル・中東料理（ヴィーガン）
cultural_comfort_note: The shop has no visible signage and an ivy-covered, easy-to-miss storefront, so finding it and navigating the menu likely requires some comfort reading Japanese or asking staff, even though the concept (falafel sandwiches) is internationally familiar.

gf_detail: Falafel itself is chickpea-based and naturally gluten-free, but the signature dish is a sandwich served in housemade wheat pita bread. Search summaries mention a falafel plate (salad, grilled vegetables, fava-bean dumplings) as a menu option that could avoid the pita, but this was not independently confirmed, and no source addresses shared fryer oil, cross-contact during pita baking, or wheat in the sauces. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-6416-8396) and confirm specifics before relying on this as a safe gluten-free option.

As a Middle Eastern concept (not Japanese), there is low inherent risk of hidden dashi/bonito, though this was not explicitly confirmed by the restaurant itself.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-26 CK126 1F

bio.background: atacu cafe opened in 2023 on a quiet street near the Jimbocho intersection. It is run solo by owner Ataku Tomohiko (安宅智彦) — the café's name is widely understood to derive from his surname. He has a varied food-industry background spanning Japanese cuisine and pastry work, and handles cooking, sweets, and coffee himself.
bio.philosophy: The café centers on vegetable-forward, colorful dishes made with fresh produce, paired with a rotating case of seasonal handmade desserts. The weekly-changing lunch set (soup, a side, dessert, and coffee) is designed to keep the short daily menu from feeling repetitive.
bio.specialty: 旅するチーズタコライス ("Traveling Cheese Tacoraise") — a taco rice bowl served with four original sauces meant to evoke different countries: Japanese teriyaki, Korean gochujang, French Caesar, and Indian curry.

anecdotes.text: A local Tokyo walking-guide feature describes the shop's simple, clean storefront near the Jimbocho intersection and profiles the signature four-sauce 'Traveling Cheese Tacoraise,' plus its generous, colorful salad bowls and dessert case.
The proprietor is identified as 安宅智彦 (Ataku Tomohiko), who runs the small counter-and-table café alone.

caveats: No source found confirming dedicated vegan or gluten-free preparation, a dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific protocols — salad bowls and vegetable-forward dishes are the draw, but sauces (teriyaki, curry, Caesar) likely contain wheat/soy sauce and possibly dairy or fish-derived ingredients. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead or ask staff directly before visiting. Owner operates solo, so a phone/Instagram DM in advance is sensible generally.

cuisine: Café — creative vegetable-forward salad bowls, taco rice, curry, and seasonal handmade desserts with coffee
cultural_comfort_note: A small, solo-owner neighborhood café that likely operates with a Japanese-only menu and no English signage, but the simple counter/case ordering style is easy to navigate by pointing even without Japanese fluency.

The signature tacoraise and curry dishes use teriyaki, gochujang, Caesar, and curry sauces that commonly contain wheat-based soy sauce or roux, and shared kitchen equipment should be assumed. Ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering if you have celiac disease.
