lead: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 神保町店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-7 三光堂ビル1F

roles: Stone-mill soba makers (in-house team, part of the Sagatani/Sagataniグループ chain operated by 株式会社フォーユー / Foryou Group)
bio.background: This branch is one of many locations of the 十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷（さがたに） chain, operated by 株式会社フォーユー (Foryou Group), which runs stone-mill soba shops across Tokyo (Jimbocho, Kanda-Ogawamachi, Shibuya, Hamamatsucho, Tamachi, Shinjuku, Ariake and more). No single founder or personal-history narrative was found for this branch specifically — the story that is documented is the company's production method.
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated approach is 挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて — freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried. Whole buckwheat groats are ground on a stone mill inside the shop (visible near the entrance), and the flour is used the same day to make 十割そば (soba made from 100% buckwheat flour, with no wheat used as a binder), aiming to serve high-quality soba at everyday, affordable prices rather than as a luxury item.
bio.specialty: 十割そば (jūwari soba) — soba noodles made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat binder, milled in-house daily; served as simple mori-soba or with tempura/kakiage toppings.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer described being impressed less by refinement than by the value: a two-tone tokumori soba for 680 yen and a plain mori-soba for 400 yen, milled in-house on a stone mill, calling the pricing 'humbling' for what you get.

caveats: This is a multi-location chain operated by 株式会社フォーユー (Foryou Group) — not an independent single-owner mom-and-pop shop, despite the target listing's framing. The soba itself is genuinely wheat-free (十割そば), which is the basis for the GF category tag, but the kitchen is not a dedicated gluten-free facility: it also fries wheat-battered tempura, and no shop-specific confirmation was found that the dipping sauce (tsuyu) uses wheat-free soy sauce, so gf_confidence is set to "options" rather than "dedicated" or "high". No vegan-specific accommodations were found; standard soba tsuyu in Japan typically contains katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. lat/lng are null per instructions (no geocoding performed) — address and "2-minute walk from Jimbocho Station" place it within about 0.7-0.8km of Suidōbashi Station, satisfying the target-area requirement, but this was estimated from stated walking distance, not geocoded coordinates.
closed_days: None found — appears to operate daily (weekday and weekend/holiday hours both listed, no closed-day mentioned in any source)
cuisine: 十割蕎麦（立ち食い／セルフ式そば専門店）
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language self-service ticket machine near the entrance (pay by cash or transit IC card such as Suica); no English menu was confirmed, so a little Japanese or menu-reading confidence helps.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null (network geocoding disabled in this environment, per instructions). Address (千代田区神田神保町1-7) and multiple sources' "1-2 minute walk from Jimbōchō Station A7 exit" place the spot within roughly 0.7-0.8km of Suidōbashi Station, satisfying the target-area requirement, though this is a walking-distance estimate rather than a geocoded confirmation.
gf_detail: The soba noodles are 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder — stone-milled in-house daily, so the plain soba itself is inherently wheat-free. However, this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: tempura and kakiage toppings are coated in wheat-flour batter and fried in the same kitchen, so cross-contact is possible for anyone ordering those toppings or sharing fryer equipment. No shop-specific information was found confirming whether the tsuyu (dipping sauce) uses a wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with regular wheat-containing shoyu. Diners with celiac disease or a severe wheat allergy should order plain soba without tempura and ask staff directly about the tsuyu before ordering.
name_ja: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 神保町店（十割蕎麦 嵯峨谷 神保町店）
neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町（神保町駅より徒歩1〜2分）

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and kakiage items are coated in wheat-flour batter and cooked in the same in-house kitchen as the soba; there is no evidence of a separate gluten-free fryer or prep area.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No shop-specific confirmation was found on whether the dipping sauce (tsuyu) uses a wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is typically made with regular wheat-containing shoyu, so this should not be assumed safe without asking staff.

positives.text: The soba noodles are genuinely 十割そば — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder — which is unusual even among specialty soba shops, and the buckwheat is stone-milled in-house daily for freshness.

seats_text: 21 counter seats + 18 table seats (39 total, per third-party listings)
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu items, vegan dashi, or vegan accommodations were found in any source. Traditional Japanese soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, and no shop-specific evidence suggests this location deviates from that norm, so the standard menu should be assumed non-vegan unless confirmed otherwise with staff.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区（水道橋駅前、白山通り沿い）※正確な丁目・番地は確認できませんでした

bio.background: Despite its old-fashioned Japanese name ("kissa-dō"), this is actually a newer, bright Taiwanese-style tea café tucked on the second floor of a building near Suidōbashi Station, just off Hakusan-dori.
bio.specialty: Fruit tea and juice series, especially the mango jasmine tea, with customizable sweetness and ice levels

anecdotes.text: A reviewer called it 'a bright, clean Taiwanese tea café' with counter seating that looks out over Hakusan-dori while calm piano music plays, though they felt the chairs could be more comfortable.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog page (blocked to automated fetch); all facts are drawn from search-engine snippets of Tabelog and a customer review page, cross-checked across multiple independent queries for consistency. Exact chome/banchi street address could not be located in any available source — only "Chiyoda-ku, 2-min walk from Suidōbashi Station, facing Hakusan-dori, 2nd floor" is confirmed. No closed-day was found in any source, so hours_week assumes daily 11:00-19:30 with closed_days marked unconfirmed. No gluten-free or vegan-specific claims were found anywhere; the drink-based menu (fruit tea, juice) is inherently free of obvious wheat/animal ingredients but this was not independently confirmed by the venue. No official website was found. One automated search summary repeatedly asserted the Tabelog listing was in "on-hold/suspended" (掲載保留) status, but no title or snippet across many searches actually carried a 【閉店】/【移転】/保留 marker (unlike comparably-searched closed shops, whose titles did carry such markers) — this claim appears to be a summarizer artifact rather than a grounded fact, so closed_or_on_hold is left false, but this should be re-verified before publishing if possible.
closed_days: Not confirmed in available sources
cuisine: Sweets/Tea Room/Fruit Tea (Taiwanese-style)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small counter-order tea café with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) will help when ordering.
geocode_note: lat/lng left null per instructions. Confirmed location signals: Chiyoda-ku, Suidōbashi Station 2-minute walk, 2nd floor, facing Hakusan-dori — consistent with the required Suidōbashi Station radius, but no precise chome/banchi was found so the downstream geocode from address_ja will be approximate (station-level).
gf_detail: No gluten-free information, cross-contamination policy, or allergen menu was found in any source. The signature drinks (fruit tea, fruit/mango jasmine tea, juice) do not obviously contain wheat, but this was not confirmed by the venue, and nothing is known about syrups, toppings, or any food items that might be served alongside the drinks. Ask staff directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Small counter seating overlooking Hakusan-dori (exact count not confirmed)
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling or menu information was found. The core drink menu (fruit tea, mango jasmine tea, juice) appears plant-based on its face, but milk-tea variants, honey-based sweeteners, or dairy toppings were not ruled out, and nothing about kitchen practices is confirmed. Ask staff before ordering if avoiding animal products is essential.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町28-3 A2ビル地下1階(B1F)

bio.origin: South Korea
bio.background: Hanuri Shibuya Honten is a Korean yakiniku and traditional Korean restaurant tucked in the basement of the A2 Building on Bunkamura-dori, right next to Shibuya 109 and Mega Donki, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station. It has been certified an "Excellent Korean Restaurant" (優秀韓食レストラン) by Korea's Korean Food Foundation in a joint inspection with the Hattori Nutrition College, a certification given to restaurants judged to serve authentic, high-quality Korean cuisine.
bio.specialty: Kuroge wagyu beef toro yukhoe (Korean-style raw beef tartare), hand-cut naengmyeon (cold buckwheat noodles) in beef-bone broth, and the restaurant's original nakkopse — a spicy hot pot of octopus, beef tripe and shrimp.
bio.philosophy: Government-certified commitment to authentic, traditional Korean cooking rather than a Japanese-style adaptation, served in an accessible yakiniku/izakaya format with private and semi-private rooms.
roles: Korean yakiniku and traditional Korean restaurant

anecdotes.text: The restaurant holds the Korean government's "Excellent Korean Restaurant" certification, awarded after an on-site audit by the Korean Food Foundation and Hattori Nutrition College for authenticity of its Korean cuisine.
Its original nakkopse (spicy octopus, tripe and shrimp hot pot) has been noted by fans of the TV series Solitary Gourmet (Kodoku no Gurume).
The house special seasoned sauce (yangnyeom) along with free-refill grilled kimchi, negi salad, house miso and wrapping vegetables are highlighted as signature touches for the samgyeopsal.

caveats: This is a Korean yakiniku/izakaya restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan specialty venue. No restaurant-specific statements about allergen handling, dedicated fryers, or ingredient sourcing (e.g., whether soy sauce/gochujang used contain wheat) were found in any source fetched; the gf/vegan notes below reflect the general nature of the menu, not confirmed restaurant policy. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-6433-7466) to confirm. Two Hanuri locations exist in Shibuya (this "Honten" at Udagawa-cho 28-3, and a separate smaller "Hanuri Shibuya-ten" on Dogenzaka) — this record is for the Udagawa-cho Honten matching the target Tabelog listing and address.
closed_days: None confirmed — reported as open every day of the year, including New Year holidays (年中無休)
cuisine: Korean yakiniku (Korean BBQ) and traditional Korean cuisine
cultural_comfort_note: Very close to Shibuya Station with an English Tabelog listing and online reservation platforms (Hot Pepper), but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff — expect basic accommodation for non-Japanese speakers.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address (Shibuya, Udagawa-cho 28-3, A2 Building B1F, beside Shibuya 109) is a 1-2 minute walk from Shibuya Station / Shibuya Scramble, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free protocol was found in any source. As a traditional Korean yakiniku restaurant, sauces and marinades (ganjang/soy sauce, gochujang, house yangnyeom sauce) commonly contain wheat, and shared grills mean cross-contact with wheat-containing marinades is likely. Grilled meats ordered plain and unmarinaded, plus simple vegetable sides, may be the safest bets, but this must be confirmed with staff — celiac-safety is not established.
hours_raw: 11:30-24:00, continuous service (no afternoon break); food last order 23:00, drink last order 23:30. Happy hour on drinks (draft beer, highball, lemon sour, ¥329) daily from lunch until 19:00. Open every day, including New Year holidays.
neighborhood: Shibuya, Udagawa-cho

positives.text: Vegetable-forward sides — grilled kimchi, negi (scallion) salad, house miso and lettuce/perilla wrapping leaves for samgyeopsal — are described as free refills, giving vegetarian/vegan diners extra plant-based options to fill up on alongside the meat menu.

seats_text: Approximately 62 seats total, including table seating plus several private/semi-private rooms (partitioned or fully enclosed) accommodating 2-12 guests each; banquet/buyout capacity up to about 80 seated guests.
vegan_detail: The menu is centered on grilled meats (samgyeopsal, wagyu yukhoe, galbi) and meat/seafood hot pots (nakkopse), so it is not vegan-oriented. Refillable vegetable sides (salad, grilled kimchi, wrapping vegetables) offer some plant-based bulk, but broths, house sauces, and banchan may contain fish-based dashi, fish sauce, or animal products not confirmed vegan. Vegan diners should ask staff item-by-item rather than assume any dish is vegan.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-32

bio.origin: Founded 1959 (Showa 34) in Kanda-Jimbocho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: The shop traces back to founder Saburo Miyata, born in the Taisho era, whose family had earlier sold daigaku-imo (candied sweet potato) — which is why the shop was named 'Imoya' ('potato shop') even though it switched to serving tempura. The original Jimbocho honten (main shop) that Miyata opened in 1959 became a neighborhood institution, packing lunchtime lines of publishing-house editors, salarymen and students onto its famous 7-seat counter before it permanently closed on March 31, 2018. This location (listed on Tabelog as a currently-operating branch distinct from the closed honten) carries the same name, format and recipes forward.
bio.specialty: Ebi Ten Teishoku (shrimp tempura set meal) — a fixed tempura set of shrimp, squid, kisu (sillago), pumpkin and shungiku (chrysanthemum greens) served with rice and miso soup
bio.philosophy: No-frills, fast-fried tempura at old-Tokyo teishoku-ya prices, served at a worn wooden counter with a deliberately retro Showa-era feel rather than a polished dining-out experience

anecdotes.text: Founder Miyata's family had sold daigaku-imo (candied sweet potato) before he opened the tempura shop in 1959 — the shop kept the 'imo' (potato) name even after the switch to tempura.

The original honten ran for roughly 60 years on just 7 counter seats, with lines forming at lunchtime, before closing for good on March 31, 2018; several branch shops (noren-wake) continued the recipes afterward.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to the Tabelog page and most blogs returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all facts below were gathered via search-engine result snippets (not a direct page fetch) and cross-checked across multiple independent secondary sources. Note there are two Tabelog listings for "いもや" in Jimbocho: this target (ID 13131365, sometimes machine-translated as "Imoya Annex") is not flagged as closed, while a separate listing (ID 13000413, "いもや 本店") is explicitly marked 閉店/[Closed] — the original honten shut permanently on March 31, 2018. Reported operating hours vary slightly between sources (e.g. weekday close at 14:00 vs 14:15, evening close at 18:00 vs 18:15); as a tiny 7-seat counter shop it may also close early when ingredients sell out. No restaurant-specific statements about allergen handling, gluten-free accommodation, or vegan preparation were found in any source consulted — the GF/vegan assessment below is based on the standard composition of a classic Tokyo tempura teishoku (wheat batter, dashi-based tentsuyu/miso soup), not a confirmed statement from the restaurant.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays; some Saturdays (reported as 1st/3rd/5th Saturday) also closed — treat as irregular and confirm before visiting
cuisine: 天ぷら (Tempura, teishoku/set-meal style)
cultural_comfort_note: A small old-school Japanese counter shop with a short, simple two-item menu (no confirmed English signage), so ordering is easy even without Japanese, but don't expect English-language allergy conversation.
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-batter tempura counter: the fixed teishoku set is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce and miso soup are dashi-based and very likely contain wheat soy sauce. No gluten-free preparation, alternative batter, or dedicated fryer is mentioned in any source found — not celiac-safe.
geocode_note: lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed); address_ja above is the full address for downstream geocoding. Location (Kanda-Jimbocho 1-chome, near the Jimbocho intersection) is roughly 0.7 km from Suidobashi Station, within the 0.8 km target radius.
hours_raw: Reported as Mon–Fri approx. 11:30–14:15 & 17:00–18:15 (one source lists 11:30–14:00 & 17:00–18:00); Sat approx. 11:30–14:15, lunch only, no dinner; closed Sun, public holidays, and some Saturdays. Sources disagree slightly on exact minutes — call ahead or arrive early since this tiny counter can sell out.
neighborhood: 神保町 (Jimbocho), Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: 7-seat counter
vegan_detail: The fixed tempura set includes shrimp and squid, and the accompanying miso soup and tentsuyu dipping sauce are dashi-based (traditionally bonito/katsuo). No vegan or vegetable-only option, and no vegan accommodation, was found mentioned in any source — not suitable for vegan diners as currently understood.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア12F

bio.origin: Sister restaurant of 玉笑 (Tamawarai), a soba restaurant in Jingūmae regularly listed in the Michelin Guide Tokyo
bio.background: 半笑 (Hanwarai) is a more casual sister concept of the Michelin-listed soba restaurant 玉笑 (Tamawarai) in Jingūmae, opened on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square. The interior, designed by architect Kengo Kuma with earthen walls by a master plasterer, is built to feel like a hidden back-alley soba shop tucked inside a Shibuya skyscraper.
bio.specialty: 名物 天おろしそば (tempura oroshi soba, about ¥2,200) — grated daikon soba topped with shrimp tempura served dramatically balanced on the bowl's lid. The noodles are a ni-hachi (2:8) blend made from pesticide-free buckwheat sourced from Hokkaido, Ibaraki, and Nagano, stone-ground with some husk left in for a robust flavor.
bio.philosophy: Carries forward Tamawarai's soba craftsmanship in a more accessible, everyday format, while trying to accommodate a wider range of guests — including adding a vegan shojin soba option to the menu.
roles: soba restaurant, sister shop of Michelin-listed Tamawarai

anecdotes.text: The vegan soba's broth is made from kombu and dried shiitake mushroom rather than bonito flakes, giving a gentle but deep umami flavor without any animal products.
Despite sitting on the 12th floor of a shopping-complex skyscraper, the roughly 12-seat counter space is designed to feel like a quiet, hidden back-alley soba shop.

The soba is stone-ground from pesticide-free buckwheat grown in Hokkaido, Ibaraki, and Nagano, with the shrimp-tempura oroshi soba served with the tempura balanced on the bowl's lid as the signature presentation.

caveats: The soba noodles are a ni-hachi (2:8) blend containing wheat flour as a binder (tsunagi) — this is NOT a gluten-free noodle regardless of sauce ingredients, so gf_confidence is set to "no" for celiac safety. A vegan shojin soba with kombu/dried-shiitake dashi (no bonito) is confirmed as a menu option, but most of the menu (including the tsuyu dipping sauce for the signature dish) is bonito-based. Could not directly fetch the Tabelog page or the restaurant's own hanwarai.base.shop site (both returned 403); all facts below are drawn from WebSearch snippets of secondary sources (soba blogs, the Shibuya Scramble Square shop listing, a magazine feature, and directory sites) rather than a directly fetched primary page, so treat address/hours as high-confidence but not primary-source-verified.
closed_days: 不定休（渋谷スクランブルスクエアの営業日に準ずる） — irregular closures that follow the Shibuya Scramble Square building's own schedule
cuisine: Soba (pesticide-free buckwheat, ni-hachi 2:8 blend)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside the very international Shibuya Scramble Square complex and welcoming to solo diners, but the restaurant itself is a traditional Japanese soba counter with no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or pointing/translation help is useful.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null. Address places it inside Shibuya Scramble Square, directly at the Shibuya Scramble Square landmark coordinates, well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble target radius.
gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are a ni-hachi (2:8) blend — 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour used as a binder (tsunagi) — confirmed by multiple soba-specialist sources describing the noodle-making. This means the noodles contain wheat/gluten by default; no 100%-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option was found in any source. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) for the signature dish is also soy-sauce based (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, bonito), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No information was found on a dedicated gluten-free fryer or gluten-free noodle preparation area. Not safe for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners as currently documented.
neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷スクランブルスクエア12F, Shibuya Scramble Square 12F)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba is made as a ni-hachi (2:8) blend, meaning wheat flour is mixed directly into the noodle dough as a binder — this is a structural gluten content issue, not just a cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi/tsuyu for the signature dish is described as bonito-based with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.
soy_sauce_wheat.source: WebSearch synthesis, no single primary source page successfully fetched

positives.text: A vegan shojin soba is offered with a broth made from kombu and dried shiitake mushroom instead of bonito flakes, explicitly to accommodate diverse dietary needs.

seats_text: Approximately 12 seats, mostly counter seating
vegan_detail: A dedicated vegan shojin soba is offered as a menu item, with a broth made from kombu (kelp) and dried shiitake mushroom rather than bonito/katsuobushi, giving a plant-based umami base. However, this appears to be one specific menu item rather than a vegan-adapted full menu — most other dishes (including the signature tempura oroshi soba) use a bonito-based dashi/tsuyu. No information found on whether shared cooking equipment (e.g., the same tempura fryer or soba-boiling water) is used between the vegan dish and animal-based dishes.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-1-13

bio.background: A casual, quick-service soba and udon shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho known locally not just for its noodles but for its rice-bowl (donburi) side menu.
bio.specialty: Donburi rice bowls served alongside soba/udon — katsudon (pork cutlet bowl), curry rice, oyakodon (chicken and egg bowl), toridon (chicken bowl), and tenjū (tempura rice bowl)

anecdotes.text: Rocket News24's long-running 'standing soba wandering' column profiled this shop specifically because its donburi rice bowls were unusually good for a soba shop, and interviewed the owner about why he puts so much effort into the rice-bowl side of the menu rather than just the noodles.

caveats: Sources disagree slightly on weekday closing time (11:00-21:00 per Tabelog/most listings vs. 11:00-22:00 per one aggregator) — used the majority-sourced 21:00 close. Could not directly load Tabelog, Gnavi, HotPepper, or Yelp pages (403 blocked); all facts drawn from search-engine summaries of those and other aggregator pages (sobadb, ekiten, navitime, jalan, gourmet-true, Yelp). No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists in any source — this is a standard Japanese soba/udon/donburi shop with no dietary accommodations documented.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day found (open 7 days); likely closed only around New Year holidays per one listing.
cuisine: Soba, Udon (donburi/rice-bowl sides)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, no-frills quick-service noodle shop with no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff — comfortable for those with basic Japanese or pointing at photos.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked). Address 東京都千代田区西神田2-1-13 is in Nishi-Kanda, Chiyoda-ku; multiple independent sources describe it as a 3-5 minute walk from both Suidōbashi Station and Jimbōchō Station, placing it well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark.
gf_detail: This is a traditional soba/udon shop; soba noodles are typically wheat-blended (not gluten-free) and the tsuyu dipping sauce/broth is made with standard wheat-based soy sauce. No source mentions gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or any gluten-aware preparation — assume standard wheat exposure throughout, including in the tempura used for tenjū bowls.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 11:00-21:00 | Sat: 11:30-21:00 | Sun/Holidays: 11:30-17:00 (one secondary aggregator lists weekday close as 22:00 instead of 21:00)
neighborhood: 千代田区神保町・水道橋 (Nishi-Kanda, near Jimbōchō/Suidōbashi stations)
seats_text: 36 seats, table seating only (no counter seats)
vegan_detail: Menu is built around donburi (katsudon, oyakodon, curry rice, toridon, tenjū) and soba/udon in what is standard bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi broth. No vegan menu items, plant-based dashi, or vegan accommodations are documented in any source — assume hidden animal-derived dashi throughout.

address_ja: 東京都台東区松が谷3-1-12

bio.origin: Kappabashi kitchenware district, Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Sensing Touch of Earth opened on Kappabashi Dōgu-gai (the famous kitchenware-supply street) in Asakusa as a specialty coffee and organic matcha café, with an interior-design showroom on the second floor. It is operated by Budscene Inc. (founded 2009), whose representative is Yūichi Namiki; the space itself was designed by a firm that normally builds music studios and soundproof rooms, which is why acoustics, lighting, and fixtures were treated with unusual care for a café.
bio.specialty: Hand-poured specialty coffee (French press) from Foret Coffee, a self-roaster in Shinano Town, Nagano Prefecture, using single-origin beans from Ethiopia, Guatemala and Brazil, plus matcha drinks made by a barista whisking organic matcha to order one cup at a time.
bio.philosophy: The café is built around three ideas — pursuit of a high-quality space, a sense of appropriateness, and food/drink craft — aiming to be a place where visitors experience the room itself (calm jazz and classical music through professional audio equipment) rather than just grabbing a coffee.

anecdotes.text: The shop names its French-press coffee flights "morning," "afternoon," and "evening," choosing beans and roast profiles to suit the time of day they're meant to be drunk.
Seasonal limited drinks such as a matcha-and-strawberry koji (rice-malt) latte are released periodically using the café's organic matcha.

caveats: No page found makes an explicit gluten-free or vegan claim; this is a specialty-coffee/matcha café rather than a food-allergy-focused venue, so GF/vegan status is inferred from the naturally simple beverage menu (coffee, matcha) rather than confirmed policy. Craft beer and baked sweets on the menu are not confirmed gluten-free. Plant-milk availability for lattes was not confirmed on any fetched page.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, announced via the café's social media/Instagram)
cuisine: Specialty Cafe - organic matcha lattes, specialty coffee, peaceful space
cultural_comfort_note: The café draws many foreign visitors and has an English name and Tabelog English listing, but no source confirms an English menu or fluent English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese greetings will help.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer is described anywhere. Plain specialty coffee (French press/espresso) and hand-whisked matcha are naturally wheat-free, but baked goods, affogato toppings, and the craft beer on the menu are not gluten-free, and no source addresses cross-contamination. Celiac guests should confirm with staff before ordering beyond plain coffee or matcha.
name_ja: Sensing Touch of Earth（センシング・タッチ・オブ・アース）
neighborhood: 浅草・合羽橋 (Kappabashi), Asakusa, Taito-ku

positives.text: Plain hand-whisked organic matcha and French-press specialty coffee are simple, wheat-free beverages with no obvious hidden gluten source.

seats_text: 42 seats inside plus 2 outdoor terrace seats; a private theater-style room upstairs can also be reserved separately for groups.
vegan_detail: No page confirms plant-milk options (soy/oat/almond) for the matcha lattes or espresso drinks, and no vegan-labeled food items were found. Matcha and black coffee are inherently plant-based, but lattes may default to dairy milk unless a substitute is requested and confirmed available. Baked sweets, affogato, and ice cream are not confirmed dairy-free.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町5-20 道玄坂サンクスビル B1F

bio.origin: Thailand (via a Shibuya Thai-pub group with a sister branch in Ebisu)
bio.background: CONROW is a Thai food pub tucked in the basement of a building on the Dogenzaka hill above Shibuya, near Shinsen Station. It positions itself less like a typical formal Thai restaurant and more like an easygoing Thai-style pub, serving reasonably priced small plates alongside Thai beer, whisky, and cocktails so groups can share several dishes over drinks. It has a sister location, CONROW Ebisu.
bio.specialty: Gai Yang (Thai-style roasted/grilled chicken) is repeatedly singled out by reviewers as the standout dish, alongside Som Tam (green papaya salad), Pad Thai, Tom Yum seafood soup, and a Thai-style pizza topped with cilantro and cheese.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's own description emphasizes affordable small-plate portions so diners can sample a wide range of authentic Thai dishes in one sitting, in a stylish, pub-like setting that intentionally breaks from typical Thai-restaurant decor.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described the Gai Yang as tender and well-seasoned with crispy skin, and praised the staff's lively, upbeat energy during a dinner visit.
A review on Thai Hyper (thaich.net) highlighted that all the staff were personable and made for a pleasant dining experience.

caveats: No source found addressing gluten-free or vegan accommodations specifically; Thai cuisine here commonly relies on fish sauce, shrimp paste, and possibly wheat-containing soy/oyster sauces, so celiac and vegan diners should confirm directly with staff before ordering. Hours and closing-day details vary slightly between listing sites, so it is worth confirming current hours before visiting.
closed_days: No regular closed day found in available listings (possible irregular closures, e.g. New Year holidays, not confirmed).
cuisine: Thai cuisine (タイ料理) — Thai food pub / izakaya style
cultural_comfort_note: Menu, listings, and service appear to be primarily in Japanese with no confirmed English-language menu or support, so some Japanese ability or translation help is useful.
gf_detail: No source mentions gluten-free options, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce. As a Thai pub menu, dishes like Pad Thai and stir-fries likely use soy sauce and oyster sauce (both typically wheat-containing in Japan), and fried items are not confirmed to use a separate fryer. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm ingredients directly rather than assume any dish is safe.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Multiple independent listings place the restaurant in Maruyama-cho on the Dogenzaka side of Shibuya, roughly 3 minutes on foot from Shinsen Station and about 5-8 minutes on foot from Shibuya Station/Shibuya Scramble Crossing, which is comfortably within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Food L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:30-23:30 (Food L.O. 22:30, Drink L.O. 23:00). Sat/Sun/Holidays: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Food L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00-23:30 (Food L.O. 22:30, Drink L.O. 23:00). (One older listing showed a shorter dinner window ending 22:00; the 23:30 close appears in more recent listings.)
name_ja: CONROW 渋谷道玄坂（コンロウ）
neighborhood: 道玄坂・円山町 (Dogenzaka / Maruyama-cho), near Shinsen Station, Shibuya
seats_text: Small basement space with a mix of counter and table seating; multiple sources note it is compact and can fill up, so reservations are recommended especially for groups.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source. Thai cuisine at this pub-style restaurant relies heavily on fish sauce (nam pla) and likely shrimp paste across curries, salads, and stir-fries; vegan diners should not assume any dish is plant-based without asking staff directly, and there is no confirmation of a katsuo/bonito-free option for broths.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-7-5 東海西神田マンション1F

bio.background: Opened in 2006 near Suidobashi Station, the restaurant is run by an owner-chef who spent years working across a wide range of culinary genres before settling on a Mediterranean-rooted Italian style for this shop, described in local write-ups as a neighborhood casual Italian bar (bal) rather than a formal ristorante.
bio.specialty: Sardine (iwashi) dishes are the house's most popular seafood item, alongside lamb chops and a signature oversized hamburg steak (特大ハンバーグ); the kitchen blends genres freely, pairing Mediterranean technique with flavors adjusted to Japanese palates.
bio.philosophy: The chef is described as carefully sourcing ingredients and serving only dishes he is personally satisfied with, fusing 'wa' (Japanese) taste sensibilities into an Italian-Mediterranean menu -- reflected in the restaurant's name, which combines 和 (wa, Japanese) and 伊 (i, Italy).

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer notes that the space previously housed an unrelated curry shop also called 'Waiwai,' before the current Italian restaurant of a similar-sounding name opened here.
A Suidobashi-area walking-guide feature describes the current lunch service as generously portioned and health-conscious, highlighting the owner's attentive, warm service toward guests.

caveats: No official gluten-free or vegan program was found for this restaurant in any source checked (official site, Tabelog, gnavi, favy, ekiten, hitosara -- several of which returned 403/blocked to direct fetch, so information below is drawn from search-indexed content of those pages). This is a standard Italian/Mediterranean-Japanese fusion restaurant built around pasta, bread, seafood (notably sardines), lamb chops, and a signature hamburg steak -- there is no evidence of a gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or vegan menu. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should call ahead (03-3239-0725) and confirm specifics directly; do not rely on this listing alone for safety-critical decisions. Several listing sites (wai-wai-tei.jp, favy.jp, foodre.jp, hitosara.com, ekiten.jp, retty.me) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch and were only accessible via search-engine snippets, so some details (hours, seats, payment) are corroborated across multiple independent snippet sources but could not be directly re-verified page-by-page.
closed_days: Sunday (irregular/occasional additional closures; Sunday reservations considered case-by-case on request)
cuisine: Casual Italian / Mediterranean-Japanese fusion (izakaya-style "Italian bar") -- known for sardine (iwashi) dishes, lamb chops, a signature oversized hamburg steak, and a 30-40 label wine list
cultural_comfort_note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; this reads as a neighborhood spot oriented toward Japanese-speaking regulars, so first-timers should be prepared to order in Japanese or use a translation app.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac-specific protocol is documented anywhere. As an Italian restaurant, pasta and bread (wheat) are core menu items, and Japanese-Italian fusion sauces commonly rely on soy sauce (wheat) and possibly dashi. There is no indication the kitchen is set up to prevent cross-contamination for celiac-level sensitivity. Diners must call ahead and discuss specific dishes/ingredients directly with staff before assuming safety.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled per policy); address is 千代田区西神田2-7-5, which multiple sources describe as a 5-minute walk from both Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations -- placing it within the Suidobashi Station (35.7017,139.7539) 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat Lunch 11:30-16:00 (LO 15:00); Mon-Fri Dinner 17:30-24:00 (LO 23:00); Sat Dinner 17:30-23:00 (LO 22:00); Closed Sunday, with occasional irregular closures and Sunday reservations available on request.
neighborhood: Nishikanda (西神田), Jimbocho/Suidobashi border, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: 17 seats total: 9 at the counter, 8 at tables, plus additional outdoor terrace seating (1-8 seats, seasonal); no private rooms.
vegan_detail: The menu is centered on fish (especially sardines), lamb, and a signature hamburg steak (beef/meat-based), so it is not vegan-friendly by design. No dedicated vegan menu or plant-based options were found in any source. Some pasta or vegetable side dishes could potentially be adapted on request, but sauces may contain butter, cream, meat/fish stock, or dashi -- none of this is confirmed. Vegan diners should call ahead to ask what, if anything, can be modified.

lead: 地鶏 海鮮 十割そば だししゃぶ 日日
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町2-39-6 江口ビル1F

bio.background: Nichinichi is a late-night soba specialist sitting at the seam between Kabukicho's entertainment district and its hotel quarter, staying open from 4pm until 4am to serve the neighborhood's night-shift crowd, drinkers, and after-hours diners.
bio.specialty: Juwari soba (100% stone-ground buckwheat noodles, made without wheat flour binder, blended with a fragrant dark buckwheat flour) served alongside their signature Satsuma Tsuyu Shabu (dashi-based dipping shabu-shabu) and Hakata jidori free-range chicken dishes.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its menu around stone-milled buckwheat and carefully prepared bonito-based dashi, pairing rustic soba craft with izakaya-style chicken, seafood, and hot pot for a menu meant to carry a night out from early evening into the early morning.

anecdotes.text: Described as a night-focused soba restaurant standing at the boundary of Kabukicho's entertainment area and its hotel district, known for its Satsuma Tsuyu Shabu made with rich dashi broth.

caveats: Could not access the Tabelog page directly or the restaurant's official site (both returned 403); details compiled from search-result summaries of blog posts, Hotpepper, and other listing sites. No explicit allergy-handling, dedicated-fryer, or wheat-free-soy-sauce information was found — celiac diners should confirm the tsuyu/soy sauce and dashi ingredients directly with staff before ordering.
closed_days: None confirmed — described as open year-round (年中無休), with possible irregular closures around New Year and summer holidays.
cuisine: そば・鶏料理 (Jidori soba, seafood, and dashi shabu-shabu)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-language accommodation was confirmed in available sources, so Japanese-language ability or a translation app will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: The soba itself is juwari (十割) — 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour used as a binder — which is unusual and noteworthy for celiac diners. However, no information was found confirming whether the dipping tsuyu, dashi shabu broth, or soy sauce used are wheat-free, and cross-contamination in a kitchen that also prepares tempura-style and other wheat-containing dishes has not been confirmed. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free protocol, so diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu/soy sauce ingredients and shared cooking equipment before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null. Address places it in Kagabuki-cho, Shinjuku, roughly a 4-minute walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station and about a 6-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, i.e. within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: 16:00～翌4:00（料理L.O. 翌3:00、ドリンクL.O. 翌3:30）年中無休（年末年始・夏季休暇あり）
name_en: Jidori Kaisen Juwari Soba Dashi Shabu Nichinichi (Nichinichi Shinjuku Honten)
name_ja: 地鶏 海鮮 十割そば だししゃぶ 日日 新宿本店
neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho), Shinjuku — near Seibu-Shinjuku Station

positives.text: The signature soba is juwari (十割そば) — 100% buckwheat made with stone-ground flour and no wheat flour binder — which is a naturally wheat-free noodle base, unlike standard soba blended with wheat flour.

seats_text: About 30 seats total, including counter and table seating, with a private-room option available for parties of up to 20.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-and-seafood-focused izakaya-soba restaurant: jidori (free-range chicken) dishes and seafood (海鮮) are core menu items, and the signature "dashi shabu" and soba tsuyu are dashi-based, which in Japanese cooking is almost always made with bonito (katsuobushi), a hidden animal ingredient. No vegan-specific menu, substitutions, or plant-based dashi option was found in available sources.

lead: 純天然だしラーメン アートマサシヤ
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区南平台町2-8 日興パレス南平台アヅマ 1F

bio.origin: Okinawa (previous shop) / Tokyo (current location)
bio.background: Kanegae was a longtime staff member/executive at the Tokyo select-shop apparel company BEAMS before leaving the fashion industry to teach himself ramen-making.
bio.specialty: Junten-nen dashi ramen — a completely no-chemical-seasoning (無化調), 100% natural-ingredient ramen built on a seafood-and-chicken dashi (bonito, dried sardine, chicken) finished with a house-rendered lard-and-sesame aroma oil and a hint of rayu chili heat.
bio.philosophy: A conviction that food made purely from natural ingredients, without chemical additives, nourishes both body and spirit — he wanted to spread that idea after leaving the apparel world.

anecdotes.text: Kanegae first opened a ramen shop called Shima Ramen Masashiya in Naha, Okinawa in 2008, naming it 'Masashi' after the owner of a guesthouse (minshuku) he loved staying at during his time in Okinawa.

In 2011 he and his wife moved the business to Tokyo, opening in the current Minamidaira, Shibuya location under the name Art Masashiya — the 'Art' chosen in tribute to Art Coffee Salon, the kissaten (retro coffee shop) that had operated in the same space for more than 20 years before them.
The shop keeps much of the original kissaten's interior intact, giving the ramen counter a nostalgic, Showa-era coffee-shop atmosphere rather than a typical ramen-shop look.

caveats: No source found describing gluten-free noodles, allergy protocols, or wheat-free alternatives — this appears to be a standard wheat-noodle, no-chemical-additive (無添加) ramen shop, not a gluten-free-adapted one despite the working title. The broth is fish- and chicken-based (bonito/dried sardine/chicken dashi), so it is not vegan-friendly. Not verified via Tabelog directly (site was unreachable in this session); identity, address, hours and story were corroborated across multiple independent Japanese sources (BEAMS company blog, Tabelog Magazine, Maduro Online, and local business listings), with 2024–2025 references confirming it is still operating.
closed_days: 水曜日 (Wednesday)
cuisine: Ramen (natural/no-chemical-additive dashi broth)
cultural_comfort_note: A hidden, retro-café-turned-ramen-counter with a cash-only Japanese ticket vending machine and no English menu, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps, though the counter staff are described as friendly to newcomers.
geocode_note: Not geocoded in this environment; lat/lng left null. Address confirmed as Minamidaira-cho, Shibuya-ku, roughly a 7-minute walk from Shinsen Station and about a 9–12 minute walk from Shibuya Station, per multiple independent listings — consistent with the Shibuya Scramble target radius.
gf_detail: No source (Tabelog reviews, food blogs, magazine features, or business listings) mentions gluten-free noodles, a wheat-free alternative, or any celiac/allergy accommodation. The ramen appears to use standard wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing. Despite being marketed as "純天然だし" (pure natural dashi) and 無添加 (no chemical additives), that branding refers to avoiding MSG/artificial seasoning, not to being gluten-free. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners without direct confirmation from the shop.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun, and holidays/pre-holidays: 11:30-15:00. Closed Wednesdays.
name_ja: 純天然だしラーメン アートマサシヤ
neighborhood: Minamidaira-cho, Shibuya (near Shinsen Station / Shibuya Station)
seats_text: Small counter-and-table ramen shop, about 15 seats (roughly 7 at the counter, the rest at tables), in a converted retro kissaten space.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is built from seafood and chicken dashi (bonito flakes / katsuobushi and dried sardines / niboshi, plus chicken), topped with a house-rendered lard-based aroma oil — not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based broth or noodle option is mentioned in any available source.

cuisine: 創作イタリアン（炭火焼き・スパイス使い）/ Creative Italian, charcoal-grilled
neighborhood: 千代田区三崎町（水道橋駅より徒歩3分）
address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-2-15 大場ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (network geocoding blocked in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based pin placement.
seats_text: About 42 seats total, including live-kitchen counter seating (from 1 guest) and two private/semi-private basement rooms (6-15 guests and 8-12 guests).
hours_raw: Lunch Mon-Fri 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner Mon-Thu & Sat 18:00-21:30 (L.O.), Fri 18:00-22:00 (L.O.); Sunday/holidays normally closed but can open irregularly for pre-booked parties of 10+.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (irregular exceptions for reserved groups of 10 or more)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-speaking staff could be confirmed online; phone reservations (03-6661-2150) and ordering are expected to be in Japanese, so a translation app is recommended.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, wheat-free pasta/bread substitution, or dedicated fryer was found on the restaurant's own pages or major booking sites. The kitchen is pasta- and bread-forward (Italian) and uses a shared charcoal grill and open counter kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Sauces lean on imported spices/herbs/citrus rather than Japanese soy-based sauces, which may reduce (but not eliminate) hidden-wheat risk from shoyu/dashi common at Japanese-style restaurants; this was not explicitly confirmed either way. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm specific dishes and cooking surfaces.
vegan_detail: GAZZO's identity is built around a butcher-shop/charcoal-grilled-meat concept ('spice x butcher's'), with organic vegetables used as accompaniments rather than as a standalone vegan menu. No vegan-specific dishes, plant-based substitutions, or confirmation of meat-stock-free sauces were found. Vegan diners should call ahead to ask which vegetable dishes can be made without meat, butter, or animal-based stock.

bio.chef_name: "Tecchan" (てっちゃん), referred to on the restaurant's own materials as the 'good-mood chef'
bio.background: The chef trained originally as a dolce (pastry) craftsman before shifting to charcoal-grilled Italian cooking, which shows up in the restaurant's habit of presenting handmade cakes to guests celebrating birthdays and anniversaries.
bio.specialty: Whole cuts of carefully selected meat (from contracted domestic farms) grilled simply over charcoal and finished with dozens of imported spices, herbs, and citrus, alongside organic-vegetable side dishes.
bio.philosophy: The stated house motto is 'Umai! ga seikai' ("Delicious is the only right answer") — cooking meat simply over charcoal to let the ingredient quality speak, rather than heavy technique or formal fine-dining pretension.

anecdotes.text: The chef, a former pastry craftsman, personally makes and presents handmade cakes to guests dining for a birthday or anniversary.
The restaurant frames its cooking as 'spice x butcher's,' grilling whole cuts of contract-farm meat over charcoal and finishing them with spices, herbs, and citrus gathered from around the world.

positives.text: The kitchen sources organic vegetables and uses fresh, traceable contract-farm meat, and cooking relies mainly on charcoal grilling plus imported spices/herbs/citrus rather than heavy sauces, which may make it easier to identify and discuss individual allergens with staff.

caveats: Primary source pages (gazzo.jp, Tabelog, Gurunavi, Hot Pepper, Ikyu, Hitosara, Retty) all returned HTTP 403 when fetched directly in this environment (network policy blocked those hosts); all facts above come from search-engine-indexed summaries of those same pages rather than direct page reads, so exact wording/details should be reconfirmed before publishing. The task brief listed the neighborhood as 千代田区神田猿楽町, but every source instead gives the address as 東京都千代田区三崎町2-2-15 (大場ビル1F) — both are within the same 'Suidobashi Station, 3-min walk' claim and the address is well within 0.8km of Suidobashi Station, so it still satisfies the target-area requirement; the neighborhood label was corrected to 三崎町. No vegan or gluten-free specialization, dedicated fryer, or explicit allergy protocol was found anywhere for this restaurant — it appears to be a standard (non-dietary-focused) creative Italian/charcoal-grill restaurant, so it is listed as OMNI with 'ask' confidence on both GF and vegan, not as a recommended safe pick for celiac or strict vegan diners without a confirming phone call.

address_ja: 〒160-0023 東京都新宿区西新宿7丁目21−3 西新宿大京ビル1階

bio.origin: Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo
bio.background: The shop traces its lineage to Kashiwagi Fujiya (柏木ふじ家), a Nishi-Shinjuku soba shop founded in 1933. After that original shop closed, its fourth-generation successor — who had trained at Tokyo shops recognized with a Michelin Bib Gourmand, including Soba Tajima in Hiroo and Honjinbo in Shinbashi — opened this reincarnation, Teuchi Soba Fujiya, in December 2019, later moving to its current Nishi-Shinjuku Daikyo Building location to refine the hand-made soba further.
bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba, 100% buckwheat noodles), hand-ground on a custom stone mill and hand-cut without machines, served alongside freshly fried tempura.
bio.philosophy: Everything from milling the domestically-sourced buckwheat on a stone mill to hand-cutting the noodles is done in-house without machines, and noodles are boiled to order just before serving for the best texture and aroma.

anecdotes.text: The owner personally hand-grinds buckwheat with a special stone mill and hand-cuts every noodle without machines, a discipline carried over from training at Michelin Bib Gourmand-listed Tokyo soba shops.

The restaurant is the fourth-generation continuation of the old Nishi-Shinjuku shop Kashiwagi Fujiya (founded 1933); the current owner reopened it under the new name in December 2019 after training at Soba Tajima (Hiroo) and Honjinbo (Shinbashi).

caveats: Located at Nishi-Shinjuku Daikyo Building, about a 1-2 minute walk from Tokyo Metro Nishi-Shinjuku Station (Marunouchi Line) Exit 1, but roughly 800m-1km on foot from JR Shinjuku Station itself (multiple sources cite 8-12 minutes) — near the edge of the target radius. Closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays; lunch service can end earlier than the posted time if the soba sells out. GF/vegan assessment is based on general published menu descriptions (availability of 十割/juwari soba), not an explicit written allergy policy from the restaurant — always confirm in person.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦・十割蕎麦・天ぷら (Handmade soba — including 100% buckwheat juwari soba — and tempura)
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional local soba craftsman's shop with an all-Japanese menu and no confirmed English signage or menu, though modern cashless payment (card, e-money, QR pay) is accepted.
gf_detail: The shop offers 十割そば (juwari, 100% buckwheat noodles with no wheat flour in the noodle itself) as an upgrade from its standard 二八そば (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat blend), so a wheat-free noodle option exists. However, no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, fryer, or boiling water is documented; the shop also serves wheat-battered tempura and likely uses standard soy-sauce-based tsuyu, and both juwari and nihachi noodles are hand-made in the same space. This is not confirmed celiac-safe — cross-contact risk is real and unmanaged as far as published sources show; ask staff directly before ordering.
hours_raw: 11:30〜売り切れまで（14時前後）、17:30〜21:00 L.O.（土曜・日曜・祝日休み）
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku / West Shinjuku), adjacent to Tokyo Metro Nishi-Shinjuku Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's menu structure offers a 100% buckwheat (juwari) noodle alongside its standard wheat-blended (nihachi) noodle, but no dedicated gluten-free prep area, fryer, or cooking water is documented, and tempura is wheat-battered.

positives.text: A wheat-free (100% buckwheat) noodle option is a standing menu item rather than a special request, which is a meaningful GF-aware baseline compared to shops offering only wheat-blended soba.

seats_text: Approximately 29 seats across counter and table seating, plus a private room for up to 8 guests.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, dashi ingredients, or vegetarian accommodations are documented for this shop. Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is conventionally bonito/katsuobushi dashi-based, and nothing in available sources indicates a plant-based alternative here.
geocode_note: Do not geocode here — address_ja is 〒160-0023 東京都新宿区西新宿7丁目21−3 西新宿大京ビル1F. This sits right by Tokyo Metro Nishi-Shinjuku Station (Marunouchi Line) Exit 1 (~1-2 min walk, on Ome Kaido/青梅街道), which multiple sources place roughly 800m-1km on foot (8-12 minutes) from JR Shinjuku Station proper — close to the edge of the 0.8km target radius from the Shinjuku Station anchor point given.

lead: ターリー屋 渋谷並木橋店
name_ja: ターリー屋 渋谷並木橋店
cuisine: Indian Curry with Naan (インドカレー / インド定食)
neighborhood: 渋谷 並木橋交差点
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-14-4 足立ビル1F
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirmed as Shibuya 3-14-4, at the Namikibashi intersection on Meiji-dori, a 5-8 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station (Shin-Minamiguchi/East exits), well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing.
seats_text: Small counter-style shop; secondary sources give conflicting seat counts (roughly 15-20 seats reported, one listing claims 36), so an exact figure could not be confirmed.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular closed days)
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-run chain Indian-curry shop primarily oriented to local diners; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service for this branch, though the picture-friendly curry-and-naan set format is easy to point-and-order even without Japanese.
vegan_detail: The set-meal format lets you pick 2 curries from a rotating list that typically includes a vegetable curry alongside chicken, keema, and mutton options, but the vegetable curry is not confirmed to be free of ghee, butter, or cream, which are common in Japanized Indian curry gravies. No dedicated vegan menu, labeling, or preparation process was found for this location, so vegan diners should ask staff about dairy/ghee in the specific curry before ordering.
gf_detail: The signature format centers on wheat naan baked fresh in-house with free refills, but rice can be substituted as the base, which removes the naan itself from the meal. However, no information was found confirming the curries are made without a wheat-based roux/thickener or wheat-containing soy sauce, and the open kitchen actively rolls and bakes wheat naan throughout service, creating a real flour cross-contamination risk. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free process, so this is not a safe default choice for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.

roles: chain restaurant / branch of a Tokyo-founded Indian curry chain
bio.background: Thali-ya (ターリー屋) is one of Japan's largest Indian-curry chains, launched in 2002 in Tokyo and since expanded to roughly 44 shops across Tokyo, Kanagawa, Chiba, and Osaka. This Shibuya branch sits right at the Namikibashi intersection on Meiji-dori, on the walk from Shibuya Station toward Ebisu.
bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2002)
bio.specialty: The ターリー屋定食 (Thali-ya set meal): a choice of two curries served with tandoori items and freshly baked naan (with free refills) or rice, in generous, filling portions.
bio.philosophy: The chain built its identity by moving away from the idea that Indian food in Japan means only 'curry rice,' instead creating a distinct 'Indian teishoku (set meal)' category — one-plate meals meant to fill you up, with unlimited naan refills and free large-portion rice.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger described the shop's cramped, narrow interior as part of its charm, and singled out the tandoori set meal as tasting notably good.
The shop is noted for a dedicated takeout counter where customers can order curry-and-naan or curry-and-rice bento boxes directly from outside.

caveats: Primary sources (Tabelog official site, thali-ya.com) were unreachable during this session due to a live outbound-fetch outage affecting essentially all external hosts (confirmed via repeated 403s including on google.com); all facts here come from WebSearch result snippets that quote or summarize those same primary pages, plus secondary aggregators (Retty, gnavi, blogs). Seat count, exact card-payment policy for this specific branch, and any gluten-free/vegan-specific handling were not independently confirmed and should be treated as unverified — call ahead (03-3406-7882) to confirm dietary details before visiting.

lead: 立ち食い蕎麦 噛む。
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-48-1

roles: Standing soba shop specializing in extra-thick, chewy noodles
bio.background: Opened May 7, 2025 on Hakusan-dori between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, the shop quickly drew attention from Tokyo's tachigui-soba (standing soba) enthusiast community for noodles far thicker and chewier than the typical standing-soba norm.
bio.specialty: Meat dipping soba (nikutsuke soba) — choice of beef, pork, or chicken piled over extra-thick 'wasshiwashi' noodles, alongside tempura soba and a limited-time soba breakfast (6:00-9:00).
bio.philosophy: The shop's own name, Kamu (嚙む, "to chew"), states its concept outright: noodles thick and firm enough that eating them means real chewing rather than the usual soba slurp, which reviewers say releases more of the buckwheat's aroma and flavor with each bite.

anecdotes.text: The regular price for the meat dipping soba is 980 yen; for the opening days of May 7-10, 2025 it was offered at a 300-yen discount (680 yen).
Reviewers describe the noodles as 'wasshiwashi' style, thick with strong chew, calling it true to the shop's chew-focused name.
The interior is a chic, black-toned, clean design meant to feel calm enough that women dining alone are comfortable there.

caveats: Sources disagree slightly on the exact street address (2-48-1 vs 2-48-2 Kanda-Jimbocho) and on weekly closed days (most agree it is open daily/無休, one aggregator claimed Sunday closure); this record follows the majority-agreeing sources (2-48-1, open daily with shorter Sunday hours). No dedicated GF or vegan information was found for this shop specifically — the gf/vegan assessment below is based on it being a conventional wheat-noodle standing-soba shop with a meat-centric specialty, not on any explicit allergen statement from the shop.
closed_days: 無休 (no fixed closed day; open every day, with shorter hours on Saturday and Sunday)
cuisine: Standing soba (tachigui soba) — extra-thick "wasshiwashi" chewy noodles, meat dipping soba (beef/pork/chicken), hot and cold soba, tempura soba, onigiri/inari sides, limited-time breakfast soba
cultural_comfort_note: Standing-only counter shop that orders via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (shokken), typical of tachigui-soba stalls, with no English menu reported.
gf_detail: This is a conventional standing-soba shop, not a gluten-free or buckwheat-only (juwari) specialist — its signature noodle is explicitly described as an extra-thick, wheat-blended "wasshiwashi" style, and traditional soba tsuyu/dipping sauce at this type of shop is wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free noodle, sauce, or dedicated-fryer accommodation was found in any source. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
name_ja: 立ち食い蕎麦 噛む。
neighborhood: Kanda-Jimbocho, between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations along Hakusan-dori
seats_text: Counter-only standing seating in a compact space; no fixed seat count reported by any source.

lead: 玄蕎麦しんがり 浅草本店
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-19-8

bio.origin: 浅草・新仲見世通り（浅草寺近く）で営業する玄蕎麦専門店。埼玉県草加市にもう1店舗を構える小規模2店舗展開。
bio.background: 浅草寺に近い新仲見世通り沿いで営業する玄蕎麦（皮ごと挽いた蕎麦粉を使う蕎麦）の専門店。株式会社ココチーノが運営し、埼玉県草加市にもう一店舗を持つ小規模な2店舗展開。
bio.specialty: 完全十割玄蕎麦 — つなぎ粉を一切使わず、国産玄蕎麦粉100%だけで打った蕎麦。新潟県・長野県の契約農家から仕入れた在来種の玄そばを店主自身が石臼で挽き、注文後にその都度製麺するという打ちたてにこだわる。
bio.philosophy: つなぎを使わない完全十割にこだわり、蕎麦粉を自家製粉、麺は注文が入ってから打つ。蕎麦つゆに添える辛味大根も店主自らが栽培したものを使うなど、自家生産・自家製粉・打ちたてを軸にした蕎麦作りを掲げている。
roles: 蕎麦専門店（十割玄蕎麦・天ぷら）

anecdotes.text: 店主自ら新潟県・長野県の契約農家から仕入れた在来種のみの玄そばを石臼で挽き、蕎麦つゆに添える辛味大根も自家栽培している。
その時期最高の国内産玄蕎麦粉を100%使い、注文後に製麺する完全十割蕎麦を、浅草寺・浅草駅近くの新仲見世通り沿いで通し営業（10:00〜22:00）で提供している。

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to tabelog.com and most restaurant/blog domains (cococino.co.jp, asakusa-shinnaka.com, asakusa-navi.jp, fasma.jp, momoti.com) was blocked at the network/proxy level in this environment (403 policy denial on CONNECT), so all facts here come from WebSearch result snippets that quote/summarize those pages rather than from directly fetched page bodies. Multiple independent listing sources (Tabelog, the operating company's own site, the Shin-Nakamise shopping street association, tourism guides) consistently corroborate the address, hours, and 100%-buckwheat (juwari/genzoba) composition, so confidence in the core identity/location/hours facts is good. Gluten-free and vegan assessments are inferential from menu composition (tempura, wagyu, shrimp, horse sashimi on the menu; no wheat-binder in the soba itself) since no page explicitly addresses allergen protocols, dedicated fryers, or wheat-free soy sauce — celiac-level diners should confirm directly with the restaurant.

positives.text: The base soba noodle is a genuine 100% buckwheat (juwari) product milled in-house from single-origin buckwheat and cut fresh per order, rather than a machine-made 2:8 blend that always contains wheat.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町15-3 グランエストビル2階

bio.background: A medicinal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) specialty restaurant in Shibuya's Maruyama-cho nightlife district. The kitchen simmers its own stock in-house for about 8 hours from a pork-bone and chicken-carcass base with vegetables, without additives, artificial coloring, or chemical seasoning, then builds two broths from it: a mild white (白湯) broth and a numbing-spicy mala broth infused with 26 kinds of Chinese medicinal herbs. Vegetables used are stated to be domestically sourced (Japan-grown), and the restaurant keeps 30-plus vegetable varieties in stock so a single hot pot meal can cover a day's worth of vegetables.
bio.specialty: Herbal medicinal hot pot (薬膳火鍋) built on an 8-hour pork-and-chicken bone stock infused with 26 Chinese herbs, served as a choice of mild white broth or numbing mala broth.
bio.philosophy: Positions itself around 医食同源-style thinking — food as medicine — aiming to let guests eat a hot pot that is genuinely nourishing for the body rather than just spicy or indulgent, explaining each herb used as the pot is prepared tableside.
roles: Medicinal hot pot specialty restaurant (薬膳火鍋専門店)

anecdotes.text: A local food feature described late nights in Shinsen as good value for hot pot, noting the bold fragrance of Chinese medicinal herbs and urging readers to experience "the real thing" (本物を体感せよ) at 老麻火鍋房.

caveats: No confirmed vegan or gluten-free menu was found. Both signature broths are built on a pork-bone/chicken-carcass stock, so the base soup itself is not vegan or vegetarian regardless of which vegetables are added. No information was found confirming that dipping sauces, seasonings (e.g., soy sauce, doubanjiang-type pastes), or the broth seasoning are wheat-free, and no allergy-handling policy was published. Guests with celiac disease or who need certified vegan food should contact the restaurant directly (03-6455-3500) before visiting. Hours/seat figures reflect the most recent listings found across the official site and portal sites and were consistent across sources, but were not independently re-verified via a live phone call.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, certification, or explicit wheat-free claim was found. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the composition of the dipping sauce, broth seasonings, and any shared cooking surfaces before ordering.

Over 30 vegetable varieties are kept in stock and could be ordered as ingredients, but no vegetable-only or vegan broth option, and no vegan menu, was found in any source checked.

bio.background: Formerly operating under the name "なぎさ食堂" (Nagisa Shokudo), the spot rebranded as "ちょいバル Bocado" while staying at the same back-alley address near Suidobashi Station. It runs a dual concept: a fish-set-meal lunch counter by day and a Spanish-tapas-leaning fish bar by night.
bio.specialty: Daily rotating grilled fish teishoku with barley rice at lunch; at night, seared bonito (かつおのたたき), grilled mackerel with salt, mushroom ajillo, and fried horse mackerel (アジフライ) with plum-paste tartar sauce.

anecdotes.text: During lunch the restaurant serves a different grilled fish with barley rice and a daily miso soup as a healthy set meal, with free large portions; in the evening it becomes a casual bar with glasses of wine from 380 yen alongside seasonal carpaccio and fresh fish dishes.

caveats: Could not fetch the primary Tabelog and official-site pages directly (blocked by network policy in this environment); all facts below are drawn from search-engine snippets of Tabelog, the official site (sabatetu.gorp.jp), Gurunavi, Yahoo Loco, and Hot Pepper rather than a full page read, so menu/hours detail may be incomplete or slightly dated. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation information was found anywhere — this is a conventional seafood/tapas izakaya with no dietary-focus signaling.

lead: 博多もつ鍋 前田屋 新宿東口店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3丁目23-12 パンドラビル3F

bio.background: Maedaya is a motsunabe (beef offal hot pot) specialist that built a devoted following in Hakata, Fukuoka, known there for long queues outside its shops. In February 2026 the brand opened its first-ever Tokyo location, this branch, just steps from Shinjuku Station's east side.
bio.origin: Hakata, Fukuoka
bio.specialty: Motsunabe hot pot made with hand-selected A5-grade Japanese wagyu beef offal in a light, refined miso-based broth, finished with garlic chives and cabbage from contracted farms; the shop is also known for a sesame-marinated kanpachi (yellowtail) starter.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps the motsunabe broth intentionally light and clean so the natural sweetness and umami of the carefully chosen offal comes through on its own, rather than being masked by a heavy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku Higashiguchi branch marks Maedaya's first-ever Tokyo location, following years of popularity and queues at its original Hakata, Fukuoka shops.

caveats: This is a meat-and-offal-centric hot pot restaurant (beef/pork motsu, miso/soy-based broth, wheat noodle finish) with no confirmed vegan or gluten-free accommodations found in any source. Not a suitable pick for celiac or vegan diners; included here mainly for completeness/comparison rather than as a safe recommendation. Seat count and precise closed-day policy were not independently confirmable beyond aggregated listing snippets (direct fetch of Tabelog, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, Retty, Yahoo Map, and the official site all returned HTTP 403 in this environment; facts below come from WebSearch result summaries of those pages plus the Fukuoka Leap-Up news article and the brand's own mail-order product allergen labels).

The broth is soy-sauce/miso based, and motsunabe meals are traditionally finished with wheat-based chanpon noodles. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct kitchen confirmation.

bio.origin: Iwate Prefecture (owner's home region)
bio.background: The owner trained at the now-closed soba-kappō restaurant Akasaka Takegami before opening this Asakusa shop, cooking with ingredients, buckwheat, and sake brought in from his native Iwate Prefecture.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba made from Iwate buckwheat flour (the shop keeps two different batches on hand, so the character of the noodles varies by the day), served alongside Iwate regional home-style dishes.
bio.philosophy: A soba-kappō approach that pairs quietly excellent noodles with a changing lineup of Iwate country cooking and Iwate sake, in a small, low-key backstreet setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Kuwasaru, comes from an Iwate dialect expression meaning roughly 'the chopsticks keep moving' — a nod to food you can't stop eating.
The owner hails from Iwate and trained at the since-closed soba-kappō restaurant Akasaka Takegami before striking out on his own in the Asakusa backstreets.
Two different lots of Iwate buckwheat flour (including grain from the Yabukawa area) are kept in rotation, so the soba's flavor and texture shift from day to day.

caveats: Business-hours sources disagree slightly on closing time (some list 12:00-22:00, others 12:00-23:00 with food/drink last order at 22:00) — table above uses the more commonly cited 12:00-22:00 with Wednesday closures. No source confirms card acceptance vs. cash-only, so this should be treated as unverified. No source explicitly states whether the soba is 100% buckwheat (jūwari) or a wheat-blended (nihachi) style, so gluten content of the noodles themselves is unconfirmed either way. No dedicated official website was found, only Facebook (facebook.com/kuwasaru.asakusa) and X (@KuwasaruAsakusa) accounts.

lead: スタミナラーメン 鬼山 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-6 山本ビル

bio.origin: Opened June 26, 2025 by 株式会社INGS (INGS Inc.), the company behind the popular light-broth ramen chain らぁ麺はやし田; this is a rare foray into rich, stamina-style ramen for the company.
bio.background: The shop is an homage to "すず鬼" (Suzuki-Oni), the famed late-night stamina ramen served at 鶏こく中華すず喜 near Mitaka Station, created by that shop's owner. Suzuki-Oni itself is a hybrid dish combining toppings from Chiba's cult "Arilan Ramen," a soup style modeled on Chiba/Uchibo's regional "Takeoka-style" ramen, and Jiro-inspired thick noodles.
bio.specialty: Stamina Ramen (garlic, pork backfat, and ginger) — also served as spicy stamina ramen, stamina abura-soba (oil noodles), and stamina tsukemen (dipping noodles), priced roughly ¥950-¥1,000.
roles: Operated by 株式会社INGS (multi-brand ramen operator)

anecdotes.text: The recipe combines three distinct regional Chiba/Tokyo ramen traditions into one bowl: Arilan Ramen's toppings, Takeoka-style soup, and Jiro-style noodles — a tribute to Suzuki-Oni in Mitaka.
INGS describes the shop as a departure from its usual light-broth ramen lineup, built specifically around a heavier garlic/backfat/ginger "stamina" style.

caveats: This is a garlic/pork-backfat "stamina ramen" specialty shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations found in any source; noodles are wheat-based and the tare/toppings are pork- and soy-sauce-based. Included for completeness in the target-area sweep, not as a GF/vegan pick.

lead: 新潟カツ丼タレカツ 神保町本店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-8-9 立川Bビル1F

roles: Original flagship shop of the Tarekatsu chain (opened June 2007)
bio.origin: Niigata City, Japan — home of the tare-katsudon style
bio.background: Tarekatsu's cooking style traces back to Niigata City, where in the early Showa era Michitaro Komatsu, founder of the original Niigata shop Tonkatsu Taro, began dipping thin, freshly-fried pork cutlets in a sweet-savory soy sauce tare instead of folding them into egg as in Tokyo-style katsudon. That regional dish became a Niigata soul food, and this Jimbocho shop — opened in June 2007 as the very first Tarekatsu location — brought it to Tokyo, a few minutes' walk from Suidobashi Station.
bio.specialty: Yasai hire-katsudon (vegetable and fillet-cutlet rice bowl), the shop's most popular dish, alongside the classic tare-katsudon and the double-tier nidan-mori katsudon
bio.philosophy: Pork cutlets are pounded thin before frying so they stay light and easy to bite through, then dipped whole in a sweet-soy tare rather than being simmered in egg — the defining trait of Niigata-style katsudon as opposed to the Tokyo style.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers originally from Niigata comment that the tare seasoning and frying at this Tokyo shop live up to the hometown version ("新潟人も認める美味さ").
The vegetable fillet-katsu bowl (yasai hire-katsudon) is noted as the shop's most popular menu item.

caveats: This is a traditional Niigata-style pork-cutlet (katsudon) specialty shop, not a vegan/gluten-free-oriented restaurant — flagged here mainly so the app can correctly warn celiac/vegan diners away rather than recommend it. Payment-method confirmation (card/QR accepted) comes from a sister Tarekatsu location's listing, not this exact shop, so treat cash_only as a reasonable inference rather than a confirmed fact for this address. No official English-language site or menu was found.
closed_days: なし — open every day of the week, no listed closed days
cuisine: 和食 (Japanese) — Niigata-style tare-katsudon (soy-tare pork cutlet rice bowl)
cultural_comfort_note: A small 12-seat counter-only local katsudon shop with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff — comfortable for diners who can manage basic Japanese ordering.
gf_detail: The signature dish is a pork cutlet coated in wheat-flour panko breading and deep-fried, then dipped in a soy-sauce-based tare sauce — both the breading and the tare introduce wheat/gluten. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated fryer was found in any source; this is not a suitable choice for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区西神田2-8-9) and multiple independent sources place the shop 3 minutes' walk from Suidobashi Station's east exit along Hakusan-dori toward the Jimbocho intersection — well within the 0.8km Suidobashi Station target radius. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should use the address_ja.
