No vegan-adapted dish or vegan accommodation was found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-6-2 栄ビル4F

bio.chef_name: 古井繁規 (romanization varies across sources: "Shigeki Furui" appears most often, also seen as "Shigehiro Furui")
bio.background: Chef Furui trained in Italy's Piedmont region, then spent nine years as head chef of Osteria Oriera in Shinjuku Sanchome, where the restaurant earned a Tabelog Award eight years running. He went on to lead Ristorante Pegaso in Minami-Aoyama for two years before opening Il Lato as his own restaurant in February 2021.
bio.origin: Piedmont, Italy (culinary training)
bio.specialty: Seafood-driven Italian courses built on ingredients from Toyosu Market — handmade pasta alongside precision-grilled fish and meat.
bio.philosophy: The name "Il Lato" means "side" or "facet" in Italian, reflecting the chef's approach of drawing out the many different sides and expressions of each seasonal ingredient.

anecdotes.text: Chef Furui led Osteria Oriera to a Tabelog Award for eight consecutive years before striking out on his own to open Il Lato in February 2021.
The restaurant's name, meaning "side" in Italian, is meant to capture the chef's philosophy of revealing the many facets of seasonal ingredients through his cooking.

caveats: Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (figures range from about 12 to 19 total seats), so seats is left null. Chef's given-name romanization also varies by source (Shigeki/Shigehiro Furui). No dedicated official restaurant website was found — only third-party reservation/media listings (Tabelog, Hitosara, Ikyu, Gurunavi, TableCheck, gourmet-calendar). No page explicitly discussing gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found; this is an omakase-style seafood/meat-focused Italian restaurant with handmade pasta as a signature, so dietary flexibility is unconfirmed and should be discussed directly with the restaurant in Japanese in advance. Cash-only status is unconfirmed (set to false as a reasonable default given use of online reservation platforms, but not independently verified).
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus irregular additional closures (不定休)
cuisine: イタリアン (Italian, seafood-focused omakase courses)
cultural_comfort_note: Reservations run through Japanese-language platforms (Ikyu, Gurunavi, Hitosara, TableCheck) and a Japanese phone line, with no evidence of an English menu, so diners without Japanese may want a hotel concierge or reservation site to book ahead.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented in any source found. The restaurant's signature offerings include handmade wheat pasta, and seafood dishes are likely to use soy sauce (shoyu, typically wheat-containing) and dashi-based sauces. Because this is a small open-kitchen omakase restaurant, cross-contamination risk for a shared kitchen is likely but unconfirmed. Celiac diners should call ahead in Japanese to discuss specific needs before booking; do not assume any dish is safe without direct confirmation from the chef.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
seats_text: Small open-kitchen restaurant with counter seating facing the chef plus a few table seats; exact seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (roughly 12-19 total).
geocode_note: Address (東京都新宿区新宿3-6-2 栄ビル4F) confirmed via multiple listings as approximately 1-minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station and about 3-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed in this environment); pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. The restaurant is built around an omakase-style course centered on seafood, meat, handmade pasta, and dashi/bonito-based sauces, which is structurally difficult to adapt to a fully plant-based meal. Vegan diners should not expect standard accommodation and would need to arrange a heavily customized meal in advance by direct, specific request — success is not guaranteed.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-24-6

bio.chef_name: 坂本良太郎 (Sakamoto Ryōtarō) — 3rd generation chef
bio.origin: Founded 1941 by 坂本友治郎 (Sakamoto Yūjirō), who trained in French cuisine at the Sannō Hotel before opening this Western-style (yōshoku) restaurant in Asakusa.
bio.background: Grill Grand opened in 1941, when Asakusa's Kannonura backstreet was a lively hanamachi (geisha district); the restaurant became a favorite of geisha and often catered Western dishes for the neighborhood's ryōtei (traditional restaurants). It has stayed in the same family for three generations: the founder's grandson, Ryōtarō, now cooks while his older brother, Shōichi, and their parents (2nd generation) run the front of house.
bio.specialty: Beef stew simmered for hours until fork-tender, and the signature 特製オムライス (tokusei omuraisu) — omurice finished with a choice of the house demi-glace or a ketchup sauce. Their crab cream croquette has won gold seven years running in the Croquette Grand Prix's cream category.
bio.philosophy: The demi-glace sauce is treated as the soul of the kitchen — simmered for about two weeks from beef tendon, beef and chicken bones, aromatic vegetables, tomato and red wine to build layers of umami; the beef for the stew is braised roughly seven hours until it falls apart.

anecdotes.text: As teenagers, the two brothers who now run the restaurant had a good-natured cabbage-shredding contest in the kitchen; their father (the 2nd generation owner) judged that the elder brother, Shōichi, lacked a cook's touch, which is when Shōichi decided he'd focus on managing the restaurant instead, leaving the cooking to his brother Ryōtarō.
The restaurant is a well-known local landmark 'just behind Sensō-ji' and has been recommended in the Michelin Guide Tokyo Bib Gourmand selection.

roles: Front of house/management: Sakamoto Shōichi and 2nd-generation parents

caveats: Task brief listed the address as 台東区浅草3-21-8, but every independently verified source (Tabelog, Michelin Guide, Google/local listings) gives 東京都台東区浅草3-24-6 — used the verified address. Seat count could not be confirmed from any fetched source (WebFetch on Tabelog and most blog/listing sites returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so verification relied on WebSearch result snippets rather than full page fetches). Hours have minor discrepancies between sources: most consistently report Tue-Sat 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00), but a couple of older listings show slightly longer weekday dinner hours (until 21:30) — used the more consistently reported figures. No gluten-free or vegan information exists anywhere for this restaurant; it is a traditional 80+ year old yōshoku (Japanese-Western) restaurant built around wheat-flour demi-glace roux, breaded/fried croquettes, and egg-based omurice, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners without extensive modification, which the kitchen shows no evidence of offering.
closed_days: 日曜日・月曜日 (Sunday and Monday)
cuisine: 洋食（フランス料理仕込みのビーフシチュー・オムライス・カニクリームコロッケなど）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local yōshoku restaurant with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — order in Japanese or with a translation app, though its Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition means occasional non-Japanese visitors are not unusual.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodation could be found anywhere. The signature dishes are built on wheat: the house demi-glace sauce is a classic French-style roux-based sauce simmered about two weeks, the crab cream croquette is breaded and deep-fried (shared fryer, no indication of a dedicated fryer), and omurice/beef stew are served with this same wheat-thickened sauce. Not celiac-safe.
hours_raw: 火〜土 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00)。日・月定休。※一部の古い情報源では平日夜21:30までとの記載もあり、来店前の電話確認を推奨。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or fryer is indicated anywhere; the crab cream croquette (a breaded, deep-fried item) is one of the restaurant's signature dishes, implying a shared fryer used for wheat-breaded foods.

seats_text: Not confirmed by any source; described in blog visits as a small, intimate neighborhood restaurant.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, labeling, or accommodation found. The restaurant's identity is built around meat- and dairy-heavy French-yōshoku technique: the demi-glace sauce base uses beef tendon, beef bones and chicken bones; the beef stew is slow-braised beef; omurice contains egg and butter; the crab cream croquette contains dairy cream, crab, and egg-based breading. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: 黄金の塩らぁ麺 ドゥエ イタリアン
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-2-3 東急プラザ渋谷 6F

bio.origin: Main branch (honten) is in Ichigaya, Chiyoda-ku; this is the Tokyu Plaza Shibuya branch of a small Tokyo ramen chain
bio.background: Chef Kazuo Ishizuka ran six Italian restaurants over roughly 25 years before meeting legendary ramen craftsman Minoru Sano (佐野実), which led him to cross over into ramen and fuse his Italian training with the craft.
bio.specialty: Ramen Fromage (らぁ麺フロマージュ) — noodles finished with a rich, Gorgonzola-style cream cheese, plus the signature golden salt ramen (黄金の塩らぁ麺) built on a chicken-bone broth enriched with kelp and scallop dashi. Menus also mix in Italian touches like prosciutto (生ハムフロマージュ).
bio.philosophy: A self-described 'Cheese × Ramen' fusion concept — treating each bowl like a compressed Italian-inflected full course, only possible from a chef who has worked both sides of Italian cooking and ramen craft.

anecdotes.text: Ishizuka previously operated six Italian restaurants before meeting ramen master Minoru Sano and pivoting to create his own ramen brand blending Italian and ramen technique.
The Due Italian brand's Ichigaya honten was selected as a Michelin Guide Tokyo Bib Gourmand pick and has appeared in the Michelin Guide Tokyo for three consecutive years.

caveats: This is a branch of a small multi-location Tokyo ramen chain (Due Italian), not a standalone independent shop — confirm current branch status directly since chain locations occasionally rebrand or close (e.g. the Kichijoji branch renamed). Its 'healthy noodle' is marketed with the word gluten-free but per menu descriptions is actually a half-wheat-flour / half-konjac blend, so it still contains wheat — this is NOT safe for celiac diners despite the marketing language, and this record reflects that correction rather than the shop's own claim. The broth is chicken-bone (kelp/scallop) based and dishes feature cheese, cream, and prosciutto, so there is no vegan pathway. Payment-method and precise reservation-policy details are inferred from aggregated listings rather than a single confirmed page; verify by phone before a special-diet visit. Hours reflect the most recently published weekday/weekend schedule and should be reconfirmed close to visit date.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closure; follows Tokyu Plaza Shibuya's own holiday/closure schedule (the mall rarely closes)
cuisine: Italian-fusion ramen (chicken-based golden salt/soy ramen topped with cream cheese, Gorgonzola, and prosciutto)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside the international Tokyu Plaza Shibuya mall right at Shibuya Scramble, with photo-driven ramen menus that are easy to point-and-order from, but no confirmed English-speaking staff or English-language menu.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-2-3 (Tokyu Plaza Shibuya), 6F — this building sits directly on the Shibuya Scramble crossing, well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should resolve from address_ja.
gf_detail: Do not rely on this shop for a celiac-safe meal. It offers a 'healthy noodle' swap advertised with gluten-free language, but per menu sourcing it is actually a half wheat-flour / half konjac-noodle blend — it still contains wheat gluten. The soy-sauce ramen (醤油らぁ麺) uses conventional wheat-based shoyu, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, separate fryer, or allergen protocol. Someone with celiac disease or a severe wheat allergy should treat this as an unsafe option.
name_en: Due Italian – Tokyu Plaza Shibuya (Ougon no Shio Ramen)
name_ja: 黄金の塩らぁ麺 ドゥエ イタリアン 東急プラザ渋谷店
neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (inside Tokyu Plaza Shibuya, 6F, at Shibuya Scramble)

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's 'healthy noodle' is marketed with gluten-free language but is actually a half wheat-flour / half konjac-noodle blend, meaning it still contains gluten and is unsafe for celiac diners despite the framing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 醤油らぁ麺 (soy-sauce ramen) is made with conventional shoyu, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature golden broth is built from chicken bones (鶏ガラ) simmered with kelp and scallop, and dishes are finished with cream cheese, Gorgonzola, and prosciutto — there is no vegan or vegetarian broth option.

positives.text: A noodle-swap option (part-konjac) is available for those wanting a lower-carbohydrate bowl, though it is not a genuine gluten-free option.

seats_text: Approximately 17 seats, non-smoking
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house broth is chicken-bone based with kelp/scallop dashi, and the signature dishes are built around dairy (cream cheese, Gorgonzola) and cured pork (prosciutto). No plant-based broth or protein alternative is documented.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-16-1 ニュー駿河台ビル1F

bio.background: Maruka was opened in 2003 by a Kagawa-prefecture (Takamatsu) native who left a career in advertising/sales-promotion planning to become an udon craftsman. Before opening his own shop he trained at Yamakoshi, a Kagawa institution often credited as one of the birthplaces of kamaage-style udon.
bio.origin: Kagawa Prefecture (Takamatsu), Japan
bio.philosophy: To bring Kagawa's everyday soul food — Sanuki udon as it is actually eaten at home, not a showcase dish — to Tokyo, with every bowl timed and boiled so it reaches the customer at its freshly-made peak.
bio.specialty: Hand-made Sanuki udon, especially kamaage-style noodles served straight from the cooking water, alongside classics like kake and niku (meat) udon and hand-fried toppings such as chikuwa and chicken tempura.

anecdotes.text: The shop is often mistaken for a decades-old institution, but it was actually founded about 20 years ago (2003) by a former ad-agency employee who quit his corporate job to pursue udon full time.

The owner calculates the timing of every order — how much noodle dough to boil and for how long — so that each bowl is served at its ideal just-cooked moment, treating this attentive service as part of recreating Kagawa's 'everyday udon' experience.
Before opening Maruka, the Takamatsu-born owner trained at Yamakoshi, a Kagawa shop regarded as one of the originators of kamaage-style udon.

caveats: Not a gluten-free or vegan-focused restaurant — it is a classic hand-made Sanuki wheat-udon specialist. Included for its extremely high local reputation, but celiac/wheat-avoidant and vegan diners should look elsewhere; strict vegans and anyone avoiding gluten/wheat cannot safely eat the signature dish here.
closed_days: Sundays, national holidays, and New Year's holidays; may also close early on any day once the day's noodles sell out
cuisine: Sanuki Udon (Local Specialty)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood udon shop with no English menu, no online reservations, and frequent lines out the door — come ready to queue and point at the menu.
gf_detail: This is a specialist wheat-udon shop — the noodles themselves are made from wheat flour with no gluten-free alternative offered. Popular toppings (chicken tempura, chikuwa fish-cake tempura) are deep-fried, almost certainly in a shared fryer with wheat-battered items, and the broth is a soy-sauce-based dashi (Japanese soy sauce is typically wheat-containing). No gluten-free accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00-16:00, 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:30); Sat 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:30); closed Sun, national holidays, and New Year's; may close earlier on any day if the noodles sell out
neighborhood: Kanda-Ogawamachi, Jimbocho, Chiyoda City
geocode_note: Address is Kanda-Ogawamachi 3-16-1, Chiyoda-ku — search-tool distance data places it about 0.24km from Jimbocho Station and roughly 0.80km from Suidōbashi Station, i.e. right at the edge of but within the Suidōbashi target radius; the block sits between Jimbocho, Ochanomizu, and Suidōbashi stations in the same dense central-Tokyo cluster.
seats_text: About 26 seats (two 9-seat tables and two 4-seat tables)
vegan_detail: The signature broth is an iriko (dried sardine)/mixed dried-fish dashi with kombu, so the base soup is not vegan. Popular toppings such as chicken tempura and chikuwa (fish cake) tempura are also animal-derived. No plain-dashi or vegetable-broth alternative was found in any source, so this shop cannot be recommended for vegan diners.

lead: ラーメン まこと屋
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町33-10 J+Rサイド Jビル1階（〒150-0042）

bio.origin: Osaka (founded in Fukushima-ku, Osaka, October 11, 1999)
bio.background: Ramen Makotoya is a beef-bone (gyukotsu) ramen chain that grew out of a single shop opened in Fukushima-ku, Osaka in 1999 by Makoto Food Service Co. The company has since expanded to roughly 100 locations in Japan and abroad. The Shibuya Center-gai shop opened in February 2024 as the chain's push into the Kanto region, reportedly around its 81st store, with a stated goal of 200 locations by 2030.
bio.specialty: 牛骨白湯ラーメン (creamy beef-bone tonkotsu-style ramen) — each store simmers roughly 30kg of beef bones (about six 5kg bones) per batch in large pressure pots to make the signature milky broth. The Shibuya branch also has a store-limited Taiwan-style abura soba (mazesoba).
bio.philosophy: The chain's catchphrases are "a ramen shop that makes families happy" and "you can see smiles through the steam" — the company emphasizes handmade preparation and mostly domestic (Japanese-sourced) ingredients rather than a dietary-focused approach.

anecdotes.text: The shop's spicy miso condiment is a proprietary blend of several spices and fruit juices aged into house miso, and its garlic oil is fried in three separate stages at different times and temperatures before being blended together.

The Shibuya Center-gai location opened February 23, 2024, becoming one of the chain's Kanto-region outposts as the Osaka-born chain expanded eastward, and offers a store-exclusive Taiwan-style mazesoba not found at other branches.

roles: 24-hour beef-bone ramen chain shop (Kanto flagship branch)

caveats: This is a mainstream 24-hour beef-bone ramen chain shop, not a vegan/vegetarian or gluten-free-focused restaurant — no plant-based broth or gluten-free noodle option was found. Included for completeness/logistics (central Shibuya, 24-hour access, English/Chinese touch-panel ordering) but celiac and vegan diners should treat it as a fallback, not a safe choice, and confirm any allergy concerns with staff via the in-store allergen chart.
closed_days: None — open 365 days a year
cuisine: Ramen (beef-bone/gyukotsu tonkotsu-style broth; multiple soup and topping variations), 24-hour operation
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via an outdoor touch-panel ticket machine that supports English and Chinese, and the shop accepts credit cards and QR-code payments, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking visitors.
geocode_note: Address (Udagawa-cho, Shibuya) and multiple sources place the shop about 480m from Shibuya Station and directly on/near Shibuya Center-gai, well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble landmark; lat/lng left null per instructions for downstream geocoding.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option or dedicated wheat-free menu was found. The noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the signature beef-bone broth and shoyu-based seasonings are prepared in a shared kitchen alongside wheat products. The only reduced-carb option found ("糖質オフ麺") is a low-sugar noodle made by combining starch with regular wheat flour — it is not gluten-free. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 24時間営業・年中無休 (24-hour operation, open every day of the year)
name_ja: ラーメンまこと屋 渋谷センター街本店
neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya (Shibuya Center-gai, ~3-5 min walk from Shibuya Station)

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu displays a QR code linking to a full allergen chart covering the eight major mandatory allergens (egg, dairy, wheat, buckwheat, peanut, shrimp, crab, walnut) plus soy, beef, chicken and pork, though a blogger who visited with an egg/dairy-allergic child still recommended confirming specifics with staff in person before ordering.

positives.text: Provides an in-store QR-code allergen chart at the table/register covering major allergens rather than leaving customers to guess ingredients.

seats_text: 18 counter seats, no smoking anywhere in the shop
vegan_detail: The signature and default broths are all beef-bone (gyukotsu) based, and other soup variants use seafood/beef combinations — no plant-based or vegetable broth option was identified. Shoyu-based seasonings likely include bonito/fish-derived dashi components typical of Japanese ramen. Not suitable for vegans; no vegetarian broth alternative was found in available sources.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町2-2-9

bio.chef_name: Kazuo Tomiyama (owner), with son Toshiyuki also active in the shop
bio.origin: Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: The shop stands on a spot that had housed a soba restaurant called Kagaya from the late Meiji into early Taisho era. Futaba's predecessor owner trained at Kokura-an, a soba house in Nihonbashi Hon-cho, then bought out Kagaya in Taisho 5 (1916) and reopened it under the new name Futaba (冨多葉) in May of Taisho 6 (1917). The restaurant has continued on the same spot for more than a century, including rebuilding after the shop was lost in the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and again in air raids during World War II.
bio.specialty: Katsudon (pork cutlet rice bowl) alongside classic soba and udon; close to half of today's customers order the ¥980 katsudon rather than soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop traces back to a soba restaurant called Kagaya on this spot from the late Meiji into early Taisho era; Futaba's predecessor owner trained at Kokura-an in Nihonbashi Honcho before buying Kagaya in Taisho 5 (1916) and reopening it as Futaba in May of Taisho 6 (1917).
The restaurant has kept operating at the same location through more than a century, including rebuilding after the shop was destroyed in the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake and in WWII air raids.

Current owner Kazuo Tomiyama served for many years in the local Kanda-Sarugakucho neighborhood association (youth section, crime-prevention section, vice-chair), and his son Toshiyuki has taken on a vice-chair role in the association's youth section as well, keeping the shop closely tied to the surrounding community.

Although it is a century-old soba shop, close to half of today's customers order the katsudon rather than soba, and the ¥980 katsudon is often singled out for its careful cooking and old-fashioned, retro flavor.

caveats: Traditional soba/katsudon shop with no gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations found in any source. The menu centers on soba/udon in a likely bonito-based dashi, breaded fried tempura and katsudon (breaded pork cutlet), all of which typically involve wheat and animal-derived stock in standard Japanese preparation; no source found describes allergen-free options or dedicated equipment. Payment methods, exact seat count, and any English-language support are unconfirmed by available sources — call ahead (03-3291-4887) for dietary questions. Two secondary sources gave slightly different closing times (17:00 vs 19:00); the more frequently repeated figure (17:00) was used.
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日 (Closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays)
cuisine: そば・カツ丼 (soba noodles and katsudon)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a very traditional, local soba-ya with no English menu or signage indicated in any source found — expect an all-Japanese ordering experience typical of an old neighborhood shop.
geocode_note: Address (神田猿楽町2-2-9, Chiyoda-ku) is described by multiple sources as roughly midway between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, about a 7-minute walk (~550m) from JR Suidobashi Station and a 6-minute walk from Jimbocho Station, confirming it falls within the 0.8km target radius of Suidōbashi Station. Lat/lng left null per instructions; no geocoding performed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodations are mentioned in any source. As a traditional soba-ya, the menu is built around soba/udon noodles, tempura, and katsudon (breaded, fried pork cutlet) — dishes that in standard Japanese preparation typically involve wheat (in noodles, tempura batter, and soy-sauce-based tsuyu) and are not celiac-safe without direct confirmation from the shop.
hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜17:00、土日祝定休 (Mon-Fri 11:00-17:00; closed Sat, Sun, and public holidays)
neighborhood: 神田猿楽町（神保町駅・水道橋駅間）、千代田区
seats_text: Small, old-fashioned shop with roughly 8 counter seats (including individual partitioned counter spots for solo diners) plus 3 tables and a larger raised tatami-style seat in back.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian dishes are identified in any source. The menu centers on pork katsudon and soba/udon served in what is typically a bonito (katsuobushi)-based dashi at traditional soba shops of this style — no plant-based options or dashi substitutions are mentioned anywhere found.

lead: ベーカリー&レストラン 沢村 新宿
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区千駄ヶ谷5-24-55 NEWoMan新宿2F エキソト フードホール

bio.background: SAWAMURA began in 2009 at Harunire Terrace in Karuizawa, Nagano — a resort town known for its streams and forest greenery, which inspired the shop's name ("a village by a stream, surrounded by greenery"). The brand grew from its original Karuizawa bakery into a small group of bakery-restaurants, including this Shinjuku branch inside NEWoMan, directly connected to Shinjuku Station's new south exit.
bio.origin: Karuizawa, Nagano Prefecture (founded 2009)
bio.specialty: Naturally leavened bread made from over 30 domestic and imported flour varieties, slow-fermented at low temperature using four house-cultivated sourdough starters — including standards like campagne and croissant — served alongside a restaurant menu pairing bread with meat, fish, and vegetable dishes and pasta.
bio.philosophy: A bread-first philosophy built around natural yeast fermentation, carefully selected flours (plus ingredients like Cambodian Kampot salt and Amami-island cane sugar), and close attention to dough temperature, humidity, and water hardness to bring out the full flavor of the wheat.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku shop is a Tokyo branch of the original, highly popular Karuizawa bakery; its natural-yeast bread draws lines by around 9am for the limited morning bakery selection.
SAWAMURA is featured in a guide to Tokyo restaurants with ties to Nagano Prefecture, reflecting its Karuizawa roots.

caveats: This is fundamentally a wheat bakery (natural-yeast bread is the signature product), so it carries high flour cross-contamination risk and is not suitable for strict celiac/gluten-free needs. No dedicated vegan menu was confirmed; vegan-curious diners would need to ask staff directly. Seat count and precise chef attribution could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: 無休 (open every day of the year, per official/aggregator listings)
cuisine: Naturally-leavened (sourdough) bakery and European-influenced bistro restaurant
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside NEWoMan Shinjuku directly by Shinjuku Station's new south exit, a modern station complex used heavily by tourists, so some English signage/support can be expected, though service is primarily in Japanese.
gf_detail: This shop's identity is built entirely around wheat bread — natural sourdough made from 30+ flour varieties is the signature product, and flour/bread is used throughout both the bakery counter and the restaurant kitchen. No gluten-free bread, menu section, or dedicated gluten-free preparation process was found in any source. Given open bakery displays and shared kitchen equipment, airborne flour and cross-contact risk would be high. Not recommended for diners who need strict gluten-free/celiac-safe food.

bio.background: otaco opened in September 2010 in Asakusa's Kannon-ura backstreets, along Kototoi-dori just north of Sensoji Temple. The shop's name comes from the nickname of the owner's young son.
bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo (opened September 2010)
bio.specialty: Rice-flour chiffon cake — a plain version plus cocoa and rotating seasonal flavors, sold as a whole cake or by the single slice for eating on the go
bio.philosophy: Marketed as "anshin sweets" (reassuring, safe sweets) meant to be enjoyed every day without worry — made with body-friendly ingredients (unrefined kibi sugar, domestic rapeseed oil, fresh Okukuji eggs) and no wheat flour or baking powder anywhere in the kitchen, letting the rice flour's naturally moist, mochi-like texture and gentle sweetness stand on its own.

anecdotes.text: Every cake is handmade one at a time in the shop's kitchen by the owner, known to regulars as "otaco mama."
The shop's name otaco comes from the pet nickname of the owner's son.

caveats: This is a small takeout-only cake stand, not a sit-down restaurant — there is no seating, no savory menu, and only one product line (chiffon cake). The plain/standard cakes contain egg, milk, and condensed milk, so they are not vegan or dairy-free. Reported as cash-only; confirm current payment options by phone (03-6458-1375) before visiting.
closed_days: Sunday and Monday (regular weekly closure); shop also closes for the day whenever cakes sell out, and posts occasional irregular extra closures on its own calendar
cuisine: Gluten-free rice-flour chiffon cake specialty shop (takeout)
cultural_comfort_note: It's a simple point-and-pay takeout counter selling one type of product, so basic greetings and numbers are enough to order even without shared language.

gf_detail: No wheat flour is kept anywhere in the shop, and no baking powder is used — every chiffon cake is made from a 100% domestic rice-flour batter that is leavened solely by whipped egg foam, so there is no shared-kitchen wheat cross-contact for the cakes themselves. This is a single-product cake specialist, not a full-meal venue, so there is nothing else on offer to worry about (no soy sauce, dashi, or fried items to check).

positives.text: Highlighted by gluten-free-focused bloggers as a reassuring option for people with wheat allergies because the entire kitchen is flour-free rather than just offering a token gluten-free item.

bio.background: Founded in 1957 in Shibuya's Dogen-zaka district, the shop was built around a simple idea: eel (unagi), long considered a luxury food in Japan, should be something ordinary people could enjoy casually and affordably. Instead of the formal, pricier kabayaki-box style served at many unagi restaurants, the founders built the menu around charcoal-grilled eel skewers — served almost like yakitori — so diners could order a few at a time.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Kushiyaki eel skewers grilling nearly every part of the fish over Kishu binchotan charcoal (roughly 400°C) — including kurikara (a coiled belly-meat skewer), hire (fin), tsumaki, tanshaku, kimo (liver), kabuto (head), baramimi, and rebaa. The kurikara skewer is said to have originated at this shop.
bio.philosophy: Keep unagi cuisine unpretentious and accessible — grill it simply over binchotan charcoal, sell it by the skewer rather than only as a formal set meal, and let regulars drop in without a reservation the way they would at a neighborhood yakitori counter.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant serves eight distinct cuts of eel skewer — kurikara, hire, tsumaki, tanshaku, kimo, kabuto, baramimi, and rebaa — grilled over Kishu binchotan charcoal, using nearly the whole fish rather than only the fillet.
Kurikara, a skewer made by coiling belly meat, is said to have originated at this shop and remains one of its signature orders.
The shop opened in 1957 with the concept of making eel, then seen as a luxury item, accessible to everyone through skewer-style service.

caveats: Most primary source pages (Tabelog, official site, gurunavi, hotpepper, retty, navitime) returned HTTP 403 under this session's egress policy and could not be fetched directly; all facts here come from search-result summaries of those pages (consistent across multiple independent listings) rather than direct page reads. No restaurant-specific statement was found confirming or denying wheat content in the eel tare, use of a dedicated fryer, or any vegan/gluten-free accommodation — the menu is built entirely around eel and grilled skewers, with no indication of a wheat-free tare or plant-based option.

Celiac or wheat-allergic diners should assume the tare and any accompanying items contain wheat and should not rely on this restaurant without asking staff directly.

bio.origin: 宮城県 (Miyagi Prefecture) oyster producers
bio.background: A direct-from-producer seafood izakaya (産直市場) near Suidōbashi Station specializing in oysters sourced straight from growers in Miyagi, with additional fresh catches from Hokkaido and Iwate.
bio.specialty: 殻付き牡蠣 (shell-on grilled oysters) from Miyagi, served alongside fresh sashimi platters (tuna, toro-salmon, scallop) and a lunchtime 牡蠣三昧定食 (oyster feast set meal).
bio.philosophy: Sources oysters directly from Miyagi producers and only serves oysters that meet the prefecture's strict raw-consumption ('生食用') quality standards, prized for their rich flavor and large meat.

anecdotes.text: 名物の殻付かきは、潮流と森の恵みが育む宮城の豊かな海で育った牡蠣は、味が濃厚で身も大きいのが特徴。宮城県は牡蠣の名産地のため厳しい規格基準が設けられており、その基準をクリアした質のよい『生食用』の牡蠣だけを提供しています。

roles: Water-dumpling specialty restaurant, Shinjuku East Exit branch of the Gyopao (餃包) chain
bio.background: Gyopao's water-dumpling recipe traces back to the founder's Taiwanese father and Kumamoto-born mother — the dumplings blend the flavor of his father's home-town xiaolongbao with his mother's Kumamoto dago-jiru broth. The brand's direct roots go back to a dumpling restaurant the father opened in 2004. After the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake pushed the company to the brink of closing almost all of its shops, the family opened the original Gyopao in Roppongi as a last stand, betting everything on water dumplings alone. This Shinjuku East Exit branch opened in July 2024 as a station-front sister location, directly connected to the Shinjuku Subnade underground arcade, about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's East Exit.
bio.origin: Roppongi, Tokyo (original Gyopao location); this is the Shinjuku East Exit branch
bio.specialty: 肉汁水餃子 (juicy meat-filled water dumplings) — thin-skinned boiled dumplings that burst with broth similar to xiaolongbao, served with broths such as chicken paitan or hot-pot style preparations
bio.philosophy: The company deliberately narrowed its menu to water dumplings only, dropping the pan-fried gyoza that once made up 80% of sales in order to become a true specialist. The menu is kept largely unchanged over time, with an emphasis on in-restaurant hospitality and service that customers can't get from delivery.

anecdotes.text: After the 2011 earthquake put the company on the verge of bankruptcy, closing nearly every other shop, the founder staked everything on opening the original water-dumpling specialty shop in Roppongi.
The company chose to stop selling pan-fried gyoza — which made up 80% of sales — in order to commit fully to water dumplings as a specialist shop.

caveats: Meat-dumpling specialty izakaya; no gluten-free or vegan menu items, ingredient disclosures, or allergy-handling information were found in any available source (official site, Tabelog, Gnavi, Hotpepper). Dietary ratings here reflect absence of accommodation, not a confirmed lack of any option — travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should not rely on this spot and should contact the restaurant directly if considering it.

roles: Chain tsukemen (dipping noodle) specialty restaurant, one of ~40+ nationwide branches
bio.background: Mita Seimenjo was founded in 2008 at its original honten in Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo, as a dedicated tsukemen (dipping noodle) shop. It grew into one of the drivers of Tokyo's 'rich pork-bone and seafood' tsukemen boom, expanding to roughly 40+ locations nationwide plus delivery service across many prefectures by the 2020s. This Suidobashi/Tokyo Dome-mae location is one of those branch shops, a short walk from Tokyo Dome and Suidobashi Station.
bio.origin: Mita, Minato-ku, Tokyo (original 2008 honten)
bio.specialty: Noko Tonkotsu Gyokai Tsukemen (濃厚豚骨魚介つけ麺) — a rich pork-bone-and-seafood dipping noodle bowl served with the chain's proprietary thick square-cut wheat noodle
bio.philosophy: The chain's stated goal is 'to make tsukemen an everyday, familiar food' (つけ麺を身近な日常食にする), pursued through a three-part broth built from animal stock (pork bone and chicken), vegetable stock, and a blended fish dashi (katsuobushi, mackerel, and flying-fish stock).

anecdotes.text: At the original Mita honten, a single small noodle-making machine kept on the third floor — the source of the shop's name ('seimen' = noodle-making) — was used for the trial-and-error process that produced the chain's signature rich pork-bone-and-seafood tsukemen recipe.

The chain blends animal broth (pork bone/chicken), plant-based vegetable stock, and a three-way fish dashi (katsuobushi, sababushi, agobushi) into its signature tsukemen soup, paired with an original thick 'kaku straight No. 12' noodle.

bio.background: Established as a specialty chicken restaurant on Meiji-dori near Namiki Bridge, a short walk from Shibuya Station's east/new south exit. The shop occupies a two-story standalone building and has built its reputation in Shibuya as a go-to spot for oyakodon lovers, drawing lines at lunch and being featured on television for its egg dish.
bio.specialty: Oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) made with Chiba-raised free-range 'Akadori' chicken and eggs from Iwai, Ibaraki. The rice bowl is built in two stages — a fluffy egg layer topped with a second, deep-orange, extra-rich yolk — giving the dish its signature glossy, creamy texture.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to sourcing specific, traceable ingredients (named chicken breed and named egg producer) rather than generic supermarket chicken and eggs, applied consistently across both the lunchtime oyakodon and the evening charcoal-grilled yakitori menu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's rich, creamy egg oyakodon has been featured on television programs and is repeatedly named among Shibuya's top oyakodon spots by local food bloggers and 'best of' round-ups.

Diners describe the oyakodon as deceptively simple-looking but built around a deliberate two-step egg technique (fluffy whites first, rich yolk poured on top) that produces its trademark glossy, golden appearance.

caveats: Multiple secondary listings (Hotpepper, Gnavi, Ekiten) show slightly differing hours between lunch/dinner windows and reference a separate 'はし田屋 渋谷店' branch near the east exit as well as this 本店 — the address and Tabelog ID used here (渋谷3-15-4, ID 13005353) match specifically to 本店. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation could be confirmed from any source; treat both diet fields as precautionary defaults rather than confirmed restrictions.

lead: 台南担仔麺 水道橋本店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-1-13 十勝ビル2F

bio.origin: Tainan, Taiwan (dish origin); restaurant founded in Tokyo in 1986
bio.background: Opened in 1986 near Suidobashi Station, this restaurant was one of the first in Tokyo to specialize in Tainan-style dan zai noodles at a time when authentic regional Taiwanese cooking was still rare in Japan. Four decades later it remains one of the longest-running Taiwanese specialty restaurants in the Suidobashi/Jimbocho area.
bio.specialty: 担仔麺 (dan zai noodles) — a small bowl of clear pork broth topped with slow-simmered minced pork and cilantro, traditionally paired with 魯肉飯 (braised pork rice) and other Tainan-style small plates.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant recreates the feel of a Tainan night market, decorating the dining room with lanterns and serving dishes in small, shareable portions in the way dan zai noodles are traditionally eaten alongside several other small plates in Tainan.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as offering a night-market atmosphere with lanterns, serving small-plate Tainan cuisine meant to be shared the way locals in Tainan eat dan zai noodles alongside several other dishes.

Now in its fifth decade of business, the Suidobashi location is noted as one of Tokyo's original Taiwanese restaurants, credited with introducing authentic Tainan flavors to Japan when such regional Taiwanese cooking was still uncommon.

caveats: This is a traditional pork-based Taiwanese noodle specialist (dan zai noodles built on a meat broth and minced pork, with wheat noodles and standard Japanese soy sauce). No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, ingredient disclosure, or allergy protocol was found on the official site, Tabelog, or other listings/reviews searched. Not recommended for celiac or strict vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff (Japanese only, phone 03-3263-4530). Payment-method and reservation-requirement details could not be confirmed from available sources and are best-guess defaults.

bio.origin: D.K International Co., Ltd. (headquartered in Osaka), represented by Masahiro Iwatani, opened veJin as a dedicated 100%-plant-based ramen concept inside FOOD HALL BLAST! TOKYO on November 22, 2023.
bio.background: veJin was developed in partnership with Fuji Oil Co., using Fuji Oil's soy-processing technology to create "Prime Soy Meat" for the chashu topping and a plant-based dashi built on Fuji Oil's proprietary MIRACORE ingredient platform, so the shop could offer a rich, tonkotsu-style white broth without any animal products.
bio.specialty: Vege Shiro (white broth) ramen in shoyu, shio, and miso styles, topped with soy-meat chashu and burdock-root chips, plus vegan gyoza and a chashu rice bowl.
bio.philosophy: The stated concept is "kind to the earth and body" (地球とカラダにやさしい) — built to widen ramen options for people who avoid meat, fish, eggs, or dairy, and to introduce Japan's ramen culture to a global, plant-based-curious audience.
roles: operating company: D.K International Co., Ltd.

anecdotes.text: veJin was founded with the aim to "expand food choices for people who cannot eat animal-derived dishes for various reasons, let them enjoy ramen, a Japanese food culture, and share it with the world."
The shop uses Fuji Oil's newly developed "Prime Soy Meat," engineered to combine meat-like fiber and chew with a smooth mouthfeel that was previously hard to achieve in plant-based chashu.

caveats: Seat count and card/QR payment specifics were not confirmed in available sources; assumed non-cash-only based on typical food-hall norms, not a direct statement. No gluten-free noodle option or dedicated gluten-free protocol was found in any source — treat as not celiac-safe. Hours reflect the most recent listing found (11:00-23:00, Sunday 11:00-21:00, no regular closed days aside from New Year's) but were not independently reconfirmed on Tabelog directly due to a fetch block; sourced via secondary aggregator (EnjoyTokyo/Time Out search snippet).
closed_days: なし（年末年始を除く） — No regular closed days, except New Year's holidays.
cuisine: ビーガンラーメン
cultural_comfort_note: A modern food-hall ramen counter aimed partly at an international, plant-based-curious crowd, with an English Tabelog listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff.
geocode_note: Address (代々木2-7-2, Shibuya Ward) is inside FOOD HALL BLAST! TOKYO, described in multiple sources as roughly a 3-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's South Exit — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle option or dedicated gluten-free process was found in any source. The ramen noodles are standard wheat noodles, and no source states the shoyu/soup bases are wheat-free. This is a vegan-focused shop, not a gluten-free one — not confirmed safe for celiac diners.
neighborhood: 新宿南口（代々木）／フードホール ブラスト トウキョウ

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's entire menu is 100%-plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy are used in any dish or broth on site, which removes animal-product cross-contact for vegan diners (though wheat allergens are not addressed).

positives.text: Broths, chashu (soy meat), and toppings are all formulated to be free of meat, fish, egg, and dairy as the shop's core concept, rather than as a special off-menu accommodation.

vegan_detail: The shop is fully plant-based: soup bases (shoyu, shio, miso, white "vege shiro" broth), soy-meat chashu, burdock-root chips, gyoza, and rice-bowl toppings all avoid meat, fish, eggs, and dairy as the shop's core concept — this is a dedicated vegan ramen kitchen, not a vegan-option add-on. No mention of bonito/katsuo dashi anywhere in sourced material.

bio.background: Sakura is a small, old-fashioned drinking spot tucked in a back alley near Shinsen Station, run by two women. The proprietress (女将) cooks a home-style menu that changes daily, paired with a notably deep sake list.
bio.specialty: Daily-changing home-style izakaya dishes (女将の手料理) paired with an extensive Japanese sake selection

anecdotes.text: 毎日変わる女将の手料理と豊富なお酒を楽しめる、昔ながらの呑み屋 (an old-fashioned tavern where you can enjoy the proprietress's daily-changing home cooking alongside a wide sake selection)
Described as run by two women in a Shibuya back alley, known for its impressive sake lineup alongside simple appetizers

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog page (blocked by network policy); identity, address, and hours were corroborated across Tabelog search-snippet content, Hotpepper, and Ekiten listings that agree on the same address and hours. No official restaurant website found. No dietary/allergy-specific sourcing exists for this spot (traditional home-style izakaya, not vegan/GF-focused) — gluten-free and vegan status are unconfirmed and should be treated as "ask staff directly." Diners with celiac disease should call ahead and confirm ingredients directly — this is not a place with confirmed GF safety measures.

No source confirmed plant-based dashi, ingredient transparency, or a vegan-friendly menu — vegan diners should ask staff directly before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-12-8 US水道橋ビル1F

bio.background: Igaya is a small, independent seafood izakaya near Suidōbashi Station whose owner personally goes to the fish market to select the day's stock, with a stated commitment to serving only fish that was still alive that same morning.
bio.specialty: Set course built around same-day sashimi, plus grilled and simmered (nimono) preparations of fresh fish
bio.philosophy: A guarantee of same-morning-caught fish as the foundation of every dish, from sashimi to grilled and simmered preparations.

anecdotes.text: 店主のこだわり: the restaurant's core promise is that all fish served was alive that very morning, with the owner sourcing the catch himself at the market and offering sashimi, grilled fish, and simmered fish alongside other appetizers.

caveats: Direct page fetches of the restaurant's own site and major aggregators (Tabelog, Hotpepper, Gurunavi, Hitosara) returned 403 errors in this environment; all facts below are drawn from search-engine snippets of those same pages, so wording is paraphrased rather than quoted verbatim. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation information was found anywhere — this is a conventional fresh-fish izakaya, not a diet-specialized restaurant, so GF/vegan travelers should ask staff directly and expect limited options (soy sauce, dashi, and sauces are standard Japanese, likely wheat- and bonito-based, with no confirmed alternative).
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: Fresh Seafood Izakaya - Live morning fish, sashimi, grilled & simmered fish preparations
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood seafood izakaya near Suidōbashi Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and a translation app for allergy questions) will help.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区三崎町2-12-8, US水道橋ビル1F) places it about a 1-minute walk from the East Exit of JR Suidōbashi Station, well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark. Lat/lng left null per instructions — no geocoding performed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found. Plain sashimi is naturally gluten-free, but grilled and simmered (nimono) dishes are likely finished with standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and the kitchen is not GF-dedicated. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about soy sauce and sauce ingredients before ordering.

neighborhood: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku (Kanda-Misakichō)
seats_text: Approximately 30 seats, including horigotatsu (sunken kotatsu) seating
vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian options identified; the entire menu is built around fresh whole fish (sashimi, grilled, simmered), and dashi/stock is presumed to be fish- or bonito-based as is standard for this style of izakaya. Not suitable for vegan diners.

This is a classic French bistro serving course menus that typically rely on wheat-based elements (bread service, roux-thickened sauces, pastry desserts); nothing confirms whether the kitchen can safely adapt dishes for celiac diners. Must call ahead and ask directly before booking.

Diners with vegan needs would need to call ahead to ask if the kitchen can substitute meat/fish courses; nothing confirms it can.

bio.background: Bistro Kojiya is a small, casual French bistro tucked in a quiet alley off the main Shinjuku streets, close to Shinjuku-Sanchome Station. According to its booking-site listings, the chef trained at well-known restaurants, including establishments overseas that hold Michelin stars, before opening this intimate 16-seat spot.
bio.specialty: A monthly-changing, seasonal chef's-choice (omakase) French course, often featuring a double-main format with both a meat and a fish course; the April-May 2026 menu, for example, ran caprese, turnip velouté, salmon, duck breast, and cherry pie for dessert.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its menu around seasonal ingredients, refreshing the full course lineup roughly once a month rather than running a static menu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as an upscale-casual bistro in a quiet Shinjuku alleyway, popular for occasions like women's lunch parties, pairing its course menu with an all-you-can-drink selection of around 10-15 wines chosen by the chef.
A visitor blog post recounts a women's-group lunch course with a free-flow wine option at Bistro Kojiya in Shinjuku-Sanchome.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-14-9 ソシアル道玄坂1F

bio.origin: Hyogo Prefecture
bio.background: According to blog accounts, the owner once served as an assistant priest at his family's temple in Hyogo before spending about a decade working the kitchen of a dining bar in Takadanobaba, Tokyo. He then taught himself how to build ramen soup from scratch before opening this shop with his wife in November 2003.
bio.specialty: Additive-free (無化調) shoyu ramen built on a double soup of pork bone and fish, with a lesser-known off-menu shio (salt) version also available.
bio.philosophy: The shop keeps things deliberately small and simple: a three-item menu, lunch-only hours, and closing the moment the soup runs out rather than stretching it to serve more bowls.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run as a husband-and-wife team from a 10-seat counter, open only for a roughly four-hour lunch window and closing for about a month each summer because of the heat.

Despite having no flashy exterior or trendy gimmicks, the shop has kept a loyal following in Shibuya's competitive ramen scene for more than twenty years on the strength of its soup alone, and was named to the Tabelog Ramen TOKYO 100 in 2025.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation of any kind; included only for completeness of the area's ramen coverage. Not recommended for celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日、夏季に約1ヶ月の長期休業あり (Closed Sundays and public holidays; also closes for roughly a month each summer)
cuisine: ラーメン（無化調・醤油／塩）
cultural_comfort_note: The menu is Japanese-only and limited to just three simple items (ramen, ramen with egg, ramen with roast pork), and it's cash-only counter seating with no reservations, but ordering is straightforward since choices are minimal.
geocode_note: Address (道玄坂1-14-9) places the shop in Dogenzaka, Shibuya, within a few hundred meters of Shibuya Station and the Scramble crossing, and near Shibuya Mark City as specified — well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius. Not geocoded per instructions; lat/lng left null.
gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour ramen noodles with no gluten-free substitute offered. The shoyu-based broth almost certainly uses conventional wheat-containing soy sauce, and no gluten-free or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source. Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:30〜15:30（スープが終わり次第終了）日・祝定休、夏季休業あり
neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂（渋谷マークシティ近辺）
seats_text: Counter seating, 10 seats
vegan_detail: Broth is made from pork bone and fish (double soup), and there are no vegetarian or vegan menu options. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

lead: 熊魚菴 たん熊北店 東京ドームホテル店
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 東京ドームホテル 4F

roles: Kyoto-cuisine, tempura, teppanyaki and sushi restaurant inside Tokyo Dome Hotel, operated by Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise Co., Ltd.
bio.background: Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise traces its lineage to a small counter-style kappo restaurant called Tankuma, opened in 1928 on the banks of Kyoto's Takase River by Kumasaburo Kurisu. The restaurant's fuller name, Kumagyoan (熊魚菴), was bestowed later by Heiko Hirata, then head priest of Kyoto's Tenryu-ji temple, drawing on a classical Chinese phrase for rare delicacies. The present operating company, Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise Co., Ltd., was established in May 1993 and now runs a group of Kyoto-cuisine restaurants nationwide; the Tokyo Dome Hotel branch is one of its Tokyo locations, occupying a large fourth-floor dining floor with separate Kyoto-cuisine, tempura, teppanyaki and sushi kitchens, plus private rooms including a tea room, and views over the Tokyo Dome.
bio.origin: Kyoto
bio.specialty: A5 Kobe beef teppanyaki courses, and a softshell-turtle (suppon) kaiseki course served as nabe, chawanmushi, tempura and rice porridge, offered in a strictly limited run of about 10 servings a day.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name Kumagyoan (熊魚菴) was given by Heiko Hirata, head priest of Kyoto's Tenryu-ji temple, based on a classical Chinese expression for the finest delicacies.
The Tokyo Dome Hotel branch offers a suppon (softshell turtle) kaiseki course — served as a hot pot, chawanmushi, tempura and rice porridge — limited to about 10 servings per day.

caveats: This is one Tokyo branch of a nationwide restaurant group (18 locations), not an independent shop; it is a distinct company (Yugyoan Tankuma Kitamise Co., Ltd., est. 1993) from the similarly named "Honke Tankuma" (tankuma.jp) — both trace back to the same 1928 Kyoto founder lineage but are now separate businesses, so care was taken to source facts about this specific branch/company. Tabelog and the official yugyoan.co.jp / tokyodome-hotels.co.jp pages returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so identity, address, hours and history were corroborated via search-engine-indexed content from those same official/reservation-platform pages (ikyu, TableCheck, Tokyo Dome City, hotel site) rather than direct page fetches. No GF-specific or vegan-specific menu was found in any source; allergy accommodation appears to be handled case-by-case by phone rather than via a dedicated menu, per the reservation-platform's standard allergy notice for this restaurant.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (open every day, year-round, per listed hours) — occasional irregular closures possible but none reported.
cuisine: Kyoto kaiseki / tempura / teppanyaki / sushi
cultural_comfort_note: As a large international hotel restaurant (Tokyo Dome Hotel) with English-language pages on the hotel's own site and English-language online reservation via TableCheck, it is set up to serve English-speaking guests, though allergy/dietary discussion is directed to a phone call rather than handled online.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu was found. The restaurant's own allergy notice (via its reservation platform) states that guests with food allergies, including wheat, must call the restaurant directly rather than note it in an online booking comment field; it explicitly warns that dishes are cooked in a shared kitchen using shared cooking and washing equipment, so trace cross-contamination is possible, that its dishes are not a complete allergy-accommodation menu, and that the restaurant may decline to serve guests with severe allergy symptoms for safety reasons. As a traditional Kyoto-kaiseki/tempura restaurant, tempura batter is standard wheat flour and soy sauce used in dashi and sauces is very likely wheat-based unless specifically substituted — celiac guests should call ahead and confirm case-by-case rather than assume any dish is safe.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null. Location confirmed by address and multiple independent sources as inside Tokyo Dome Hotel, a 1-2 minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station East Exit / Toei Mita Line Suidōbashi Station Exit A2 — well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station target-area center (35.7017, 139.7539).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dishes are prepared in a kitchen shared across the restaurant's full menu, using shared cooking and washing equipment, so trace amounts of allergens (including wheat) may be present even in dishes without wheat as a listed ingredient.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with food allergies or disliked ingredients (seafood, meat, dairy, buckwheat, wheat, etc.) are asked to contact the restaurant directly by phone rather than note it in the online reservation comment field, so staff can discuss options before the visit.
staff_allergy_handling.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant states its dishes are not a complete allergy-accommodation menu and that it may decline to serve guests with severe allergy symptoms, for the guest's own safety.
staff_allergy_handling.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

positives.text: The restaurant proactively publishes an allergy-handling policy and invites direct phone consultation before the visit, rather than ignoring the topic.
positives.source: restaurant allergy notice, as indexed via reservation platform (yugyoan-tokyo / TableCheck listing)

seats_text: Large hotel dining floor seating roughly 200 across separate Kyoto-cuisine, tempura, teppanyaki and sushi sections, plus 7 private rooms including a tea room.
vegan_detail: No vegan or shōjin-style menu was found for this branch. As a formal Kyoto-kaiseki, tempura and teppanyaki restaurant, dashi (stock) is very likely made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and other fish/meat-based elements are standard across courses (teppanyaki is built around Kobe beef; a suppon/softshell-turtle course is a headline dish). Vegan diners should call ahead and discuss substitutions case-by-case; a fully plant-based meal cannot be assumed available.

lead: 小松庵総本家 新宿高島屋店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区千駄ヶ谷5丁目24番2号 タカシマヤタイムズスクエア13F

bio.origin: Komagome, Tokyo (founded 1922)
bio.background: A century-old Tokyo soba specialist that began as a small shop opened in 1922 (Taisho 11) in Komagome, Toshima Ward, by founder Komatsu Tsuruichi and his wife. The shop weathered the Great Kanto Earthquake the following year, and over three generations of the Komatsu family it grew from a single neighborhood store into a group of soba houses anchored in department stores and terminal stations across Tokyo — including Ginza, Marunouchi, and this Shinjuku Takashimaya branch, which opened in 1996 alongside Takashimaya Times Square itself, two minutes from JR Shinjuku Station's South Exit.
bio.specialty: 生粉打ち三昧 (kikouchi-zanmai) — a set pairing both cold and hot handmade soba, made with 100% domestically grown buckwheat flour and no wheat flour binder, served with a sesame dipping sauce and tempura.
bio.philosophy: "The pursuit of delicious food, and the training of true craftsmen" — the company's founding motto, which it upholds through its own soba-training school (蕎学舎, Kyogakusha), opened in Komagome in 2010 to teach Edo-style soba-making to new generations of in-house noodle artisans.
roles: Department-store soba restaurant with private dining rooms

anecdotes.text: Komatsuan Sohonke was founded in 1922 in Komagome, Tokyo, by Komatsu Tsuruichi and his wife; the shop was hit by the Great Kanto Earthquake the very next year and rebuilt from there.

The Shinjuku Takashimaya location's private rooms were designed around the third-generation owner's idea that 'gathering inside a cave-like space creates a warm, convivial atmosphere' — finished with chic earthen-toned walls and chandeliers overlooking the Shinjuku night skyline.

All soba served at Komatsuan Sohonke locations is 生粉打ち (kikouchi): handmade using only domestically grown, in-house-milled buckwheat flour, with no wheat flour used as a binder — a practice maintained since the company's founding.

In 2010 the company opened its own training facility, 蕎学舎 (Kyogakusha), in Komagome to teach Edo-style soba-making, reflecting its founding motto of pursuing delicious food and training true craftsmen. The company marked its 100th anniversary in 2022.

caveats: The signature noodles are 100% buckwheat with no wheat flour binder (生粉打ち), but the dipping sauce (tsuyu) and tempura at Japanese soba restaurants typically involve wheat-based soy sauce and wheat-flour batter, and no dedicated gluten-free or vegan protocol was confirmed for this location. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should ask staff directly about tsuyu ingredients, tempura frying oil, and boiling-water cross-contact before ordering.
closed_days: Irregular closures, following Takashimaya department store's own holiday schedule (no fixed weekly closed day).
cuisine: Soba (traditional handmade buckwheat noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a major department store two minutes from Shinjuku Station's South Exit, a setting popular with tourists and visited internationally per review sites; basic Japanese or menu photos should suffice, though English-language menu support is not confirmed.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Takashimaya Times Square 13F, Sendagaya 5-24-2, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo — about a 2-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's South Exit, well within the Shinjuku Station target radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding); the pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: Komatsuan's noodles are made via 生粉打ち (kikouchi): handmade with 100% domestically grown buckwheat flour and no wheat flour binder, confirmed via the brand's official site and store-news pages, and stated to be practiced at all of its locations. This is a genuine, company-wide GF-relevant feature of the noodles themselves. However, it is standard practice across the whole chain rather than a dedicated celiac-safe program: the dipping sauce (tsuyu) and tempura batter at traditional soba restaurants typically use wheat-containing soy sauce and wheat flour respectively, and no information was found confirming a dedicated fryer, separate boiling water, or celiac-level cross-contact protocol at this specific branch. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff about the tsuyu and tempura before ordering.
hours_raw: Mon–Thu 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Fri–Sat 11:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); Sun/Holidays 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed irregularly, following Takashimaya department store's holiday schedule.
name_ja: 小松庵総本家 新宿高島屋店
neighborhood: Shinjuku (Takashimaya Times Square, Sendagaya, Shibuya-ku) — 2 min walk from JR Shinjuku Station South Exit

positives.text: Noodles are handmade daily using 100% domestically grown buckwheat flour with no wheat flour used as a binder (生粉打ち / kikouchi), a practice the company states it maintains across all of its locations since its founding.

seats_text: Exact seat count not confirmed in available sources. The location includes regular table/counter dining plus cave-themed private and semi-private rooms usable for group or celebratory dining, per the restaurant's own site.
vegan_detail: Traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is typically made with bonito-based (katsuobushi) dashi; no vegan-specific menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan protocol was found for this location in the sources reviewed. Vegans should ask staff directly about the dashi and sauce ingredients before ordering, as most dishes likely are not vegan by default.

address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草1-3-14

bio.background: Hirayama Shu trained at the celebrated Ryōgoku soba restaurant Hosokawa (ほそ川) before honing his skills further and opening his own shop in Nishi-Asakusa in November 2021.
bio.specialty: Juwari soba — handmade noodles made from 100% stone-ground buckwheat with no wheat flour binder, cut fresh daily.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to "freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled": whole buckwheat groats brought in from Fukui are hulled and stone-ground each morning, then immediately kneaded and hand-cut without any resting or aging period, to keep the buckwheat's aroma and flavor at their peak.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in November 2021, founded by a soba craftsman who trained at the renowned Hosokawa in Ryōgoku before striking out on his own near Tawaramachi Station.
Buckwheat groats are peeled and ground on a stone mill fresh every morning, then kneaded and cut immediately — the dough is never left to age, in pursuit of the purest buckwheat taste and aroma.

caveats: Restaurant explicitly states it cannot modify its menus for gluten-free or vegetarian/vegan/halal needs — dinner courses use wheat-containing soy sauce and include tempura, and the broth/dashi contains animal products. Gluten-avoiders are advised to reserve à la carte and self-select dishes, but this is guest-managed risk, not a verified allergy-safe accommodation, so celiac diners should be very cautious. Some secondary sources give slightly different dinner start times (17:30 vs 18:00); confirm directly before visiting. lat/lng were not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled) — pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday
cuisine: Soba (100% buckwheat, juwari, hand-cut, freshly stone-milled daily)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains an English-language site (asakusa-hirayama.com/en) and is bookable via English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck, byfood), but menus and in-person service are primarily Japanese-oriented.

gf_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat flour binder, but the dipping sauce/tsuyu and most course dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce, and courses include tempura. The restaurant has stated it is unable to modify its set courses to accommodate gluten allergies or gluten-free requests; guests wanting to avoid wheat are advised to reserve à la carte and choose dishes themselves — there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation or verified allergy-safe process, so this is not suitable for guests requiring strict celiac-level safety without further direct confirmation from the restaurant.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-15 福田ビル2F

bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.background: A family-run soba noodle shop founded in 1952 on a narrow backstreet off Dogen-zaka, just a few minutes from Shibuya Station. Over more than 70 years it has watched the neighborhood transform around it, staying a steady lunchtime fixture for office workers, longtime regulars, and soba enthusiasts. Guests climb a staircase to reach a bright, unexpectedly spacious dining room with a small garden (tsuboniwa) visible through the windows — often described as a quiet oasis just off the busy Dogen-zaka strip.
bio.specialty: Stone-ground (ishiusu-biki), fragrant thin soba noodles, served in dishes such as kakiage-soba (tempura fritter soba) and seasonal specials like cold eggplant soba (hiyashi-nasu soba) and 'su-gomori soba' served heaped in a large bowl.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen favors fresh, seasonal ingredients without excess — aiming for clean, straightforward, health-conscious soba rather than heavy or elaborate preparations.

anecdotes.text: Described by local food bloggers as a hidden gem down a Dogen-zaka side street, where portions are generous and toppings (like the kakiage tempura) are notably large.
Reviewers note the dining room's calm, dignified atmosphere — a bright room with a garden view reached via a staircase, a contrast to the gritty street below.
Called a beloved old-school soba spot that has drawn a cross-generational local following for decades.

caveats: Payment methods (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from available sources. Operating hours are reported inconsistently across listings — some show a lunch-and-dinner schedule while a more recent summary states evening service ended around April 2024, leaving lunch-only hours; the lunch-only version is used here as the likely current schedule, but this should be reconfirmed before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source; this is a traditional soba shop, not marketed toward those diets.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: Soba (traditional buckwheat noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, Japanese-language-first neighborhood soba shop with no indication of an English menu, though it has been covered in a few English-language Tokyo food blogs for visitors comfortable ordering in Japanese or by pointing at a menu.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment). Address (Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya) is a short walk from Shibuya Station/Shibuya Scramble, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soba option was mentioned in any source. As a traditional soba shop, the noodles are very likely a wheat-buckwheat blend (not 100% juwari buckwheat), and the dipping sauce/broth (tsuyu) is a dashi-and-shoyu (soy sauce, wheat-containing) base per menu descriptions. Tempura is fried in shared oil alongside other items. Celiac diners should not consider this a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff, which could not be verified here.
hours_raw: Most sources report lunch service 11:00-14:30 (or in some listings 11:00-15:30), closed Sundays and public holidays. One synthesis of listings suggests evening service (previously roughly 17:00-20:30/22:00 on weekdays per Hot Pepper/Jalan) was discontinued around April 2024, leaving lunch-only hours; this could not be independently confirmed and sources disagree.
neighborhood: Dogen-zaka, Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hot soba is served in a dashi- and shoyu-based broth, and cold soba comes with a shoyu-based dipping sauce (tsuyu) — both traditional preparations that contain wheat via soy sauce.

positives.text: Menu includes vegetable tempura, cold eggplant (nasu), and natto dishes alongside the standard soba, giving some plant-forward options on the menu (though broths are traditionally dashi-based).

seats_text: No official seat count found; described by visitors as a bright, spacious dining room upstairs with one large communal table plus several smaller 2-4 person tables.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu or accommodations were found. The standard tsuyu/broth used for hot and cold soba is dashi-based (typically bonito/katsuo), which is not vegan. Vegetable tempura, cold eggplant, and natto dishes appear on the menu and could suit vegetarians, but none were confirmed as prepared with a vegan (non-bonito) broth or separate fryer oil.

lead: 満点籠 まんてんろう
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-11-2 帝都御茶ノ水ビル1F

bio.origin: Shanghai, China
bio.background: The dim sum chef is from Shanghai and previously trained/worked at a well-known Tokyo hotel kitchen before opening this shop in Kanda Ogawamachi in 2026.
bio.specialty: Hand-wrapped xiaolongbao (soup dumplings) made to order in four varieties (plain, shiitake, shrimp, crab roe), plus pan-fried shengjianbao and scallion-oil noodles.
bio.philosophy: Dumplings are wrapped only after an order comes in and steamed immediately on high heat, a technique meant to keep moisture from softening the wrapper while sealing in the meat broth; the kitchen also emphasizes domestically sourced (Japan-grown) ingredients for the fillings.

anecdotes.text: Because the xiaolongbao are wrapped to order and steamed the moment they're formed, the skin stays taut rather than becoming soggy, and the broth trapped inside the filling stays hot and juicy when the dumpling is bitten into.
The Shanghai-born owner-chef also serves shengjianbao (pan-fried soup dumplings) and scallion-oil noodles alongside the signature xiaolongbao.

caveats: Newly opened (2026), meat/seafood-centric Shanghainese dim sum shop; no vegan or gluten-free dishes were identified. The signature xiaolongbao and shengjianbao wrappers are wheat-based by definition, so this is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. Closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cuisine: 中華・小籠包・上海料理 (Shanghainese Chinese, xiaolongbao specialty)
cultural_comfort_note: No evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; this reads as a standard local neighborhood restaurant best visited with a translation app or this guide's help.
gf_detail: The restaurant's entire identity centers on wheat-wrapped dumplings (xiaolongbao and pan-fried shengjianbao), so wheat is unavoidable in the signature dishes. Standard soy sauce dipping condiments used with xiaolongbao in this style of cooking typically contain wheat as well. No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated cooking equipment was mentioned in any source reviewed.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is disabled; lat/lng are left null for downstream address-based placement. Based on the address (Kanda Ogawamachi 3-chome, Chiyoda-ku) and multiple sources describing the shop as a roughly 5-minute walk from Ogawamachi, Shin-Ochanomizu, and Jimbocho stations, it sits in the Suidōbashi/Jimbocho/Ogawamachi cluster, an estimated 700-800m from Suidōbashi Station — within the target radius, though close to its edge.

Broths and sauces in this style of Shanghainese cooking commonly use animal-based stock.

lead: ごちそう焼むすび おにまる 西新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿3-1-1 西新宿玉枝ビル1階（〒160-0023）

bio.background: Onimaru is a specialty "gochisou yaki musubi" (deluxe grilled rice ball) chain. The Nishi-Shinjuku shop, which opened April 21, 2024, was the brand's first Tokyo location, chosen for its spot a short walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit.
bio.origin: Aichi-based rice-ball chain expanding nationally (Nagoya area origin), with this shop marking its Tokyo debut.
bio.specialty: Gochisou yaki musubi — a bincho-charcoal grilled rice ball stuffed with a fluffy, housemade rolled omelet (tamagoyaki).
bio.philosophy: Built around a hand-selected large-grain rice variety called "Koshihougyoku," cooked in a large kettle for texture, then finished by grilling over bincho charcoal for fragrance; the shop offers roughly 20 rotating rice-ball varieties made fresh through the day, aimed at quick takeout for commuters (weekday opening from 7:30am).
roles: takeout counter with limited outdoor bench seating

anecdotes.text: Opened April 21, 2024 as the Onimaru brand's first Tokyo store, located near Shinjuku Station's west exit.
The shop stocks about 20 varieties of rice balls, including a salmon musubi made with a seven-type dashi blend and a tuna mayo finished with a traditional simmered (kaeshi) soy sauce.
The signature item is a grilled rice ball filled with a fluffy, housemade rolled omelet (tamagoyaki).

caveats: This is a takeout-focused rice-ball counter, not a sit-down restaurant; seating is limited to a few outdoor benches. No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, labeling, or allergen protocol was found on any source consulted — assessments below are inferred from ingredient descriptions (dashi, soy sauce, mayo, meat/fish fillings), not from an official allergy statement.
closed_days: None found — reported as open every day of the year (年中無休)
cuisine: おにぎり・焼きおむすび (specialty grilled rice balls / onigiri)
cultural_comfort_note: A simple walk-up takeout counter with a visible display case of rice balls, so ordering by pointing works fine even without Japanese, but no English menu or GF/vegan signage was found.
gf_detail: Rice itself is naturally gluten-free, but many of the signature items use a soy-sauce-based glaze (e.g., the "kaeshi shoyu" tuna mayo and several other flavors), and standard Japanese soy sauce/kaeshi is typically wheat-based; mayo-based fillings in Japan (commonly Kewpie brand) may also contain barley. No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or preparation protocol was found in any source, and there is a tempura shrimp item ("海老天むす") suggesting a shared fryer. Celiac travelers should ask staff directly about ingredients per item rather than assume any flavor is safe.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address places the shop on Nishi-Shinjuku 3-chome, reported as about a 5-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.

seats_text: Primarily a takeout counter; a few outdoor bench seats are available for eating on the spot, but no formal seat count was found.
