The great majority of the roughly 20 daily rice-ball flavors contain fish (salmon, katsuobushi/bonito dashi, tuna, shirasu), meat (pork), egg, cheese, or mayonnaise. A plain salted rice ball is implied to exist as a base option (separate from the ~20 specialty flavors), which could be vegan, but this was not confirmed as dashi-free or explicitly marketed as vegan by the shop.

lead: 鶏そば・ラーメン Tonari
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町14-3 日興パレスビル渋谷 1階

roles: Head chef trained in traditional Japanese cuisine
bio.background: Tonari is a small counter ramen shop in the back streets of Shinsen (Shibuya), run by the same operator behind a neighboring oyster bar. The head chef, trained in Japanese cuisine, spent time studying light, clear-broth ramen shops around Tokyo before building the restaurant's signature chicken soba.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids synthetic seasoning entirely. The base broth simmers chicken bones, gizzards, and skin from Misakura-branded chicken with kombu dashi and a light touch of garlic, ginger, and scallion for about two hours, finished with a whisper of bonito. The tare is made with 'Shichifuku,' said to be Japan's only certified organic white soy sauce (shiro shoyu), built up with dried shiitake mushroom and dried shrimp using a technique borrowed from soba-dipping-sauce making rather than chemical dashi powder.
bio.specialty: White-soy-sauce chicken soba (concentrated chicken broth with a pale, clear tare) and an oyster ramen made with oyster broth and oyster soy sauce, topped with oyster meat and oyster wontons — drawing ingredients from the operator's sister oyster bar next door. The menu also rotates in tsukemen, abura-soba, maze-soba, and weekly specials built around other proteins such as red sea bream or sweet shrimp.

anecdotes.text: The head chef, trained in Japanese cuisine, researched light and clear broth ramen shops across Tokyo before developing Tonari's signature chicken soba.

The chicken soba's clear broth is simmered for two hours from Misakura chicken bones, gizzards and skin with kombu dashi, finished with a subtle bonito note, and paired with a tare made from Japan's only certified organic white soy sauce, 'Shichifuku,' enriched with dried shiitake and dried shrimp instead of chemical seasoning.

Alongside the chicken soba, Tonari serves an oyster ramen combining oyster broth and oyster soy sauce with oyster meat and oyster wontons, using ingredients shared with the operator's neighboring oyster bar.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop, not a gluten-free or vegan specialist. Broths are chicken- and bonito-based and the tare is a wheat-containing white soy sauce, so there is no evidence of a safe option for celiac or strict vegan diners. Hours, cash/card policy, and seat count are drawn from aggregated listings rather than a directly-fetched official page (most primary listing pages returned 403 errors during verification), so diners should double check same-day hours before visiting, especially since the shop is known to close early once the soup sells out.

cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat neighborhood ramen counter with a Japanese-language menu and no indication of English signage or staff — approachable if you can point at menu items, but not set up for non-Japanese-speaking tourists.

gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen shop with no mention of gluten-free noodles or a separate preparation area. The signature tare is made from "Shichifuku" white soy sauce (shiro shoyu), a wheat-based seasoning, and the broth is finished with bonito. No gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or allergy protocol were found in any source.

bio.background: Garden Terrace is the ground-floor lobby lounge of the Tokyo Dome Hotel, a large international hotel adjacent to Tokyo Dome and directly by Suidobashi Station, which opened in June 2000 as part of the Tokyo Dome City entertainment complex.
bio.philosophy: Designed around an 'urban oasis' concept, the lounge is a light-filled, water-and-greenery-bordered space where guests can relax over tea and cake while watching the courtyard fountain through floor-to-ceiling windows.
bio.specialty: Seasonal whole cakes and afternoon tea sets made by the hotel's pastry team, also available for takeout

anecdotes.text: For the hotel's 26th anniversary, the pastry team created a special anniversary cake called "Jardin de Fruits" (ジャルダン・ド・フリュイ).

This is a general hotel lobby café/patisserie lounge with no confirmed dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu; treat dietary confidence as unconfirmed rather than assured. lead: 北海道名物らー麺 えびそば 一幻 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7丁目8-2 福八ビル1F

bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido (founded 2003)
bio.background: Ebisoba Ichigen began in Sapporo in 2003, founded by Masahiko Maeda after training at other ramen shops. He built the concept around a shrimp-based ramen unlike the miso/shio/shoyu norm of Hokkaido ramen, and the brand has since expanded to New Chitose Airport, Kansai, and Tokyo (including this Shinjuku shop, 3 minutes' walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station).
bio.specialty: Ebisoba (shrimp ramen) in shio, miso, or shoyu tare, with a choice of thick or thin noodles and three broth-intensity levels (そのまま/ほどほど/味わい)
bio.philosophy: The shop's identity is built entirely around extracting maximum flavor from shrimp: heads are simmered for hours, then finished with a house-made shrimp oil and back fat stir-fried in a wok to build a rich, aromatic shrimp broth.

anecdotes.text: Every day, large quantities of sweet-shrimp heads are slow-simmered in big stockpots; the extracted umami is then finished with a proprietary shrimp oil and back fat stir-fried in a Chinese wok to create the shop's signature rich shrimp broth.

caveats: Standard wheat-noodle, pork/shrimp-based ramen shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations identified; included in the dataset as a verified spot but not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. Weekly closed-day status is ambiguous across sources (official site says irregular/no fixed closed day; some secondary listings say Wednesday) — confirm by phone before a special trip.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-7-13

roles: small family-run izakaya / postwar food stall lineage
bio.background: Shōchan started as one of the yatai (open-air food stalls) that lined Asakusa's postwar Hoppy Street, opening in 1951 (Shōwa 26). It became a fixed shopfront roughly four decades later but kept its stall-style, half-outdoor seating and its original simmering pot of beef stew, topped up continuously ever since (a "tsugitashi" nikomi sauce).
bio.specialty: Gyu-nikomi — beef tendon, konnyaku (devil's tongue jelly) and tofu slow-simmered for hours in a sweet-savory, soy-based sauce that has been continuously replenished since the shop opened; also served over rice as gyu-meshi.
bio.philosophy: An old-school Asakusa drinking-stall spirit: cheap, simple, unpretentious dishes (many under ¥500) meant to be eaten quickly at the counter or an outdoor tent table while drinking Hoppy, with the same pot of sauce carried forward for decades rather than remade from scratch.

anecdotes.text: David Bowie is said to have visited the shop incognito, and locals still say 'if you come to Hoppy Street, go to Shōchan.'
Regarded as 'the originator of Asakusa's nikomi (beef stew)' on Hoppy Street, selected among Tabelog's top-100 izakaya lists.

caveats: Traditional beef-tendon stew izakaya with no indication of gluten-free or vegan accommodation anywhere in the sourcing; the signature dish and its sauce are meat-based and almost certainly soy-sauce-based (soy sauce typically contains wheat in Japan), so this listing is included for completeness/context only, not as a safe GF or vegan option. Menu/hours are informal and can shift with weather since the shop still operates in a semi-outdoor, stall-like style.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (regular closed days), though sources note the shop sometimes opens on off-days and hours can shift with weather since it retains a stall-style operation.
cuisine: Izakaya - Gyunikomi (beef tendon/beef stew), traditional Asakusa drinking-stall food
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, tourist-visited stop on Hoppy Street with an English-language Tabelog listing and reportedly an English menu, but it is an old-school local stall where Japanese is the working language and no dietary accommodation should be assumed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere in the sourcing. The signature nikomi stew is simmered in a soy-based sauce (Japanese soy sauce/shoyu almost always contains wheat) that has reportedly been continuously topped up since 1951, meaning any wheat content is baked into the base and cannot be removed per-order. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

Not suitable for vegan diners.

bio.background: Gashira Shibuya bills itself as a "fish shop with a chef in it" (料理人のいる魚屋) — a seafood-focused izakaya tucked on a side street off Dogenzaka, a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station. Fish bought fresh each morning is displayed at the counter and the menu changes daily depending on what came in.
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.specialty: Chef's daily 5-piece sashimi selection (刺身5点盛り) made from whatever fish was landed that morning
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself less as a conventional restaurant and more as a fishmonger's counter where a chef prepares the day's catch to order, letting the seasonal fish itself set the menu rather than a fixed menu of dishes.

anecdotes.text: Local blog coverage describes it as a restaurant serving delicious fish tucked quietly away on a small side street off Dogenzaka.
Listing copy notes the shop takes pride in offering fresh fish, including varieties not commonly found at other restaurants, at reasonable prices.

caveats: This is a standard seafood izakaya with no dedicated vegan or gluten-free program identified in any source — no menu labeling, allergy protocol, or dedicated equipment was found. Hours listings are inconsistent across aggregators (Hotpepper's own site-specific listing shows Mon-Fri 18:30-22:30 / Sat 18:00-22:30 / closed Sun & public holidays, which is used here as the primary source, but a lunch service of 12:00-14:00 and a later 17:00-24:00 evening window also appear on some directory sites — likely stale/legacy data). Confirm current hours by phone (03-3477-1515) before visiting. Payment info (cards accepted, e-money not accepted, PayPay/Rakuten Pay accepted) is sourced from aggregated listing data, not a directly fetched official page, since most restaurant-review sites (Tabelog, Hotpepper, Retty, Gnavi, Hitosara) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetches in this session; findings rely on search-result snippets of those pages rather than full page fetches.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cuisine: Seafood / Izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A local Japanese-style seafood izakaya near Shibuya Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — diners with dietary restrictions should be prepared to explain them in Japanese or via translation.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. As a traditional Japanese seafood izakaya, dishes very likely use wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi, and the menu includes fried items (tempura-style preparations are typical of this genre) with no indication of a dedicated fryer. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm ingredient-by-ingredient; this is not a restaurant that can be assumed safe without direct staff confirmation.
neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used or available; as a conventional izakaya it should be assumed standard wheat-containing shoyu is used throughout unless staff confirm otherwise.

seats_text: Approximately 60 seats total, including semi-private and private rooms (rooms for 2, 4, 6, and 10-20 people) on the 2nd floor.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. This is a fish-centric seafood izakaya where the core concept is fresh daily catch prepared by a chef at the counter; dashi and other stocks should be assumed to contain bonito/fish unless confirmed otherwise. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners.

lead: 北ノ醤油チーホー 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-16-10

bio.background: Opened March 25, 2026 as the first physical restaurant produced by SUSURU, one of Japan's best-known ramen YouTubers, whose channel is built around eating a bowl of ramen every single day. The concept reportedly grew out of a ramen event he took part in at Tokyo Racecourse in February 2026. The shop is run by the company andfoods, which also operates the ramen shop "Ore no Ikiru Michi." SUSURU had turned down repeated offers to open his own restaurant before finally committing to this one, and the shop drew lines of 50+ people from its opening day.
bio.specialty: 北ノ醤油ラーメン (Kita no Shoyu Ramen) — a Sapporo-style bowl with thick, wavy noodles in a rich soy-sauce broth, topped with garlic-fried minced pork and bean sprouts stir-fried in an iron pan for a toasty, addictive aroma, finished with pork back fat for sweetness.
bio.philosophy: A YouTuber's personal, hands-on take on "serious" shoyu ramen — bringing the intensity and obsessive daily ramen-eating culture of his channel into a physical shop rather than opening a generic tie-in restaurant.

anecdotes.text: SUSURU had repeatedly turned down offers over the years to open his own restaurant before finally deciding to open this one.
The shop's opening day drew a queue of more than 50 people.

caveats: This is a hype/YouTuber-produced shoyu ramen shop, not a dietary-focused restaurant — included in the dataset primarily as a mainstream comparison point, not a safe GF/vegan pick. No source mentions gluten-free noodles, allergen substitutions, or vegan preparation; the broth and toppings are pork-based (minced pork, back fat, likely chashu) and the noodles/soy sauce are standard wheat-based. Payment method (cash-only vs. card/IC) was not confirmed by any source, so cash_only is a best-guess default of false, not a verified fact. No confirmed official restaurant website was found (only third-party blogs, Tabelog, a ramen database, and SNS account references), so official_url is left blank.
closed_days: None found — sources describe it as open daily (定休日:なし), though one blog referenced irregular closures (不定休); treat holiday hours as unconfirmed.
cuisine: ラーメン・醤油系 (Shoyu ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: A hype local ramen counter with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — expect to order in Japanese and likely wait in line, since the shop is a social-media/YouTuber sensation.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options are mentioned anywhere. The signature bowl uses standard wheat ramen noodles (thick, wavy Sapporo-style) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based broth, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. No dedicated fryer, allergen menu, or gluten-free substitution was found in any source — assume this is not celiac-safe.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, no listed closed days (定休日:なし) per most sources; one source referenced irregular holidays (不定休)

vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is mentioned. The signature ramen is pork-based (minced pork stir-fried with garlic, pork back fat for richness), and no plant-based broth or noodle option was found in any source.

roles: Neo-yatai (food stall) dining complex produced by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, the Tokyo hospitality group behind Ebisu Yokocho and Shibuya Yokocho
bio.background: Opened August 1, 2022, on the former site of the long-loved Shinjuku fruit stand 'Hyakkaen,' just steps from Shinjuku Station's east exit. It was built as part of a 'station-front yatai revival' project by producer Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, aiming to recreate the casual, open-air food-stall culture that once stood in front of Japanese train stations before it disappeared amid Tokyo's redevelopment.
bio.origin: Tokyo (Shinjuku), part of a Tokyo/Saitama/Chiba/Guam-wide chain of 'yokocho' and yatai dining complexes produced by the same company
bio.specialty: Asabiki (morning-fresh) yakitori from the in-house stall Hotei, served alongside shellfish from Kaidō, wagyu yakiniku from Shinjuku-en, and spicy noodles from Karamen — four stalls under one open-sided roof.
bio.philosophy: Revive the informal 'ekimae yatai' (station-front stall) drinking culture of old Japan, giving travelers and commuters a retro, Showa-era open-air spot to gather for a quick skewer and a drink.

anecdotes.text: The complex was built on the site of the long-running Shinjuku fruit shop Hyakkaen, reusing the location's history as a neighborhood gathering point.
Described in the press as a retro, smoking-allowed Showa-style izakaya that transformed the well-known former fruit-shop site into a stall-lined drinking spot.
The complex groups four independent stall concepts — shellfish stall Kaidō, wagyu yakiniku stall Shinjuku-en, spicy noodle stall Karamen, and morning-fresh yakitori stall Hotei — under one roof.

caveats: This is a corporately produced multi-stall "neo-yatai" complex (four stalls: Kaidō, Shinjuku-en, Karamen, Hotei) run by Hamakura-teki Shoten Seisakusho, not an independently owned single-owner shop — so it is not truly "mom-and-pop" despite the casual yatai/stall framing; classified here as OMNI. It sits in Shinjuku 3-chome near the JR Shinjuku Station east exit, not in Nishi-Shinjuku (west exit) as the target brief stated — this was verified against multiple independent listings. Individual stalls within the complex (e.g., Hotei) also have their own separate Tabelog pages; this record covers the umbrella "屋台苑" listing only. Hours, closed-day, and payment-method details vary across secondary sources and could not be independently confirmed from a primary page (Tabelog and the official producer page both returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch), so treat hours/payment info as indicative rather than certain — call ahead to confirm.
closed_days: None reported (open daily, no fixed closed day per most sources) — one secondary source mentioned Wednesday closures, unconfirmed
cuisine: Yakitori, yakiniku, shellfish and ramen yatai stalls (izakaya complex)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, smoking-permitted retro-Showa izakaya complex aimed at a local/Japanese crowd; no English menu or English-speaking-staff confirmation was found, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu board will help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address confirmed as Tokyo, Shinjuku-ku, Shinjuku 3-chome 24-5, described consistently across sources as ~30 seconds' walk from JR Shinjuku Station's east exit — well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark coordinate.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found for this complex. As a yakitori/yakiniku/izakaya venue, tare glazing sauces and shoyu (both typically wheat-based) are standard preparation, and no gluten-free protocol or dedicated cooking equipment has been documented. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.

neighborhood: Shinjuku 3-chome, near JR Shinjuku Station East Exit
seats_text: Approximately 142 seats across the complex's four stalls, including open-air ground-floor seating and second-floor seating
vegan_detail: This is a meat- and seafood-centered yakitori/yakiniku/shellfish complex; no vegan menu, plant-based options, or dashi/bonito-free preparations were found in any source. Standard izakaya dashi (typically bonito/fish-based) should be assumed unless a stall confirms otherwise.

bio.background: RICE HACK Gluten-free Bakery was produced by Setsuko Otsuka, known in Japan as "米粉の母" (the "Mother of Rice Flour"), a leading figure in the country's rice-flour industry. The bakery opened in the Jingumae/Omotesando area of Shibuya on November 12, 2022, under the company Rice Terrace Inc. (株式会社ライステラス), which also runs the adjoining Onden House café and rice-flour cooking classes.
bio.specialty: 100% gluten-free rice-flour baguette and curry bread (カレーパン), plus rice-flour pizza — the vegan marinara pizza is frequently singled out by reviewers.
bio.philosophy: Otsuka's push to perfect gluten-free rice-flour bread grew out of a desire to let families eat the same bread together even when one member has a wheat allergy or celiac disease; years of development went into rice-flour bread that stays moist rather than the dry, crumbly texture typical of gluten-free bread.

anecdotes.text: Otsuka's motivation traced back to a family in which one child could eat bread while a younger sibling with a wheat allergy could not — she wanted to make it possible for the whole family to eat the same bread together, and after years of research completed a 100% gluten-free rice-flour bread.

The bakery's rice-flour bread is described as moist and soft rather than dry and hard even after cooling — a common complaint about gluten-free bread — and many first-time customers reportedly become repeat buyers on the spot.

caveats: Located in Jingumae 5-chome near Meiji-Jingumae Station / Cat Street (Harajuku-Omotesando area). Walking-distance web sources place it roughly 750m-950m from Shibuya Scramble Crossing, which is right at the edge of the app's 0.8km target radius — straight-line distance (not confirmed here since geocoding is disabled in this environment) should be checked downstream to confirm it clears the radius. Hours are noted by third-party sources as somewhat irregular (single-staff operation, closes early when sold out), so treat listed hours as approximate and it's wise to check their Instagram (@yeswehackrice) before visiting. It is takeout-only with no in-house seating (a separate adjoining café allows eating on-site with a drink purchase). Not all products are vegan — some contain egg or dairy — so vegan diners should ask about specific items.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cuisine: Gluten-free rice-flour bakery (bread, baguette, curry bread, pizza)
cultural_comfort_note: Featured in several English-language gluten-free/celiac travel guides and popular with overseas visitors, but the bakery operates primarily in Japanese with no confirmed English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: Dedicated 100% gluten-free bakery — all breads, baguettes, curry bread, and pizza are made with Japanese rice flour instead of wheat flour, developed specifically so people with celiac disease or wheat allergy can eat safely alongside family members without allergies. No wheat-flour products are baked on site. Specific allergen details for individual items (egg, dairy, soy sauce ingredients) are not documented in available sources, so celiac visitors should still confirm with staff for any item with a filling or glaze.
geocode_note: Do not geocode here (blocked in this environment) — address is 神宮前5-16-5, near Meiji-Jingumae Station (about 5-8 min / ~400-650m walk) and Cat Street (3 min walk). Web sources estimate roughly 750m-950m walking distance to Shibuya Scramble Crossing, which is close to but not clearly inside the 0.8km target radius; downstream geocoding should verify.

gf_cross_contamination.text: RICE HACK is a bakery dedicated to 100% gluten-free rice-flour products; no wheat flour is used in its breads, which were developed specifically so people with celiac disease or wheat allergy can eat safely.

positives.text: Reviewers on international gluten-free travel platforms describe it as a must-visit bakery in Tokyo for people with celiac disease, highlighting the rice-flour baguette and a vegan marinara pizza.
Listed as a dedicated gluten-free bakery on multiple English-language celiac/gluten-free directories.

seats_text: Takeout only — no in-house seating at the bakery counter itself; an adjoining café allows eating on site if you purchase a drink.

No documented use of hidden dashi/katsuo (bonito) was found, but this has not been explicitly confirmed for every item.

roles: Founder: Taira Tatsu (平辰), originally from Sado Island, Niigata
bio.background: This exact location is the birthplace of the entire Shoya izakaya chain. Founder Taira Tatsu began with a small yakitori stall near Ikegami Honmon-ji temple in 1968; as that business grew, he opened the first-ever "Shoya" here in Kanda-Misakicho in 1973 (Showa 48), a one-minute walk from Suidobashi Station. A plaque still hangs at the entrance marking it as the chain's "birthplace" (発祥の地).
bio.specialty: Generous fresh sashimi platters, the restaurant's signature since it opened, plus the clam butter (あさりバター) dish that Shoya itself originated
bio.philosophy: Conceived as "an oasis in the urban desert" — home-style cooking and service meant to feel wrapped in maternal warmth, prepared by hand by resident chefs (itamae)
bio.origin: Kanda-Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku — founding location of the nationwide Shoya chain, opened in 1973

anecdotes.text: A sign at the entrance reads: "Founded in Showa 48 (1973), Shoya took its first breath in this place. We continue to ask for your patronage, together with our main store, into the future" — commemorating the site as the chain's birthplace.

caveats: This is a corporate chain izakaya (operated by Daisho Corporation), not an independent mom-and-pop shop — but this specific branch is historically significant as the literal founding location of the entire Shoya chain (opened 1973), which is why it carries the "本家" (head/original store) designation. Note on identity: the exact Tabelog URL given in the brief (ID 13093068, titled "庄や 本家店") currently shows Tabelog's "掲載保留" (listing on hold / unconfirmed) status, likely because Tabelog consolidated/re-registered this location under a newer listing, "庄や 水道橋店" (https://tabelog.com/tokyo/A1310/A131003/13199799/), at the identical address and phone number (03-6380-9311). Multiple independent, currently-live sources (official Daisyo brand site, Hotpepper, Jalan, Rakuten Gnavi, Navitime) confirm the restaurant is actively operating today at this address with current hours, so closed_or_on_hold is set to false based on the underlying business, not the single stale Tabelog page ID. No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, allergy protocol, or cross-contamination information was found in any source checked; this is a standard, unmodified izakaya menu.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open every day, no scheduled closures)
cuisine: Izakaya — fresh sashimi and chef-prepared (itamae) home-style dishes; the founding restaurant of the nationwide Shoya chain, in continuous operation since 1973
cultural_comfort_note: A large, well-known nationwide chain right by the station exit with picture-menu ordering and card payment, but no confirmed English menu, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol was found for this location. As a standard izakaya it relies on wheat-based soy sauce, tempura batter, and other wheat-containing seasonings across most cooked dishes, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high; celiac diners would need to ask staff directly and should not assume safety.

lead: アイスの沼 浅草 伝法院通り店

bio.background: Aisu no Numa ("Ice Cream Swamp") is the first directly-operated retail shop opened by Rex Very, a company that has long wholesaled premium gelato to restaurants and retailers across Japan. The Denboin-dori shop opened on 18 July 2025, with an interior that blends traditional Japanese motifs with a modern, playful look to suit its location on one of Asakusa's classic shopping streets.
bio.origin: First flagship store of gelato wholesaler Rex Very, opened July 2025 on Denboin-dori, Asakusa.
bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is "nani yori mo sozai" (ingredients above all) — building each gelato flavor around the character of a specific, often regional, ingredient rather than standard mix formulas.
bio.specialty: Rotating lineup of 40+ gelato and ice flavors built around single ingredients, including a Super Premium strawberry gelato made with 60% Fukuoka Amaou strawberry juice and pulp, and a peanut gelato made with premium Yachimata-grown peanuts from Chiba.
roles: flagship/direct-operated store of a gelato wholesaler

anecdotes.text: The shop's concept is described as wanting customers to "fall into a swamp" (numaru) of outstanding ice cream, built entirely around maximizing the quality of its ingredients.

Signature items include a Super Premium Amaou strawberry gelato (¥800, limited quantity) made with 60% strawberry juice/pulp from Fukuoka, and a Premium peanut gelato (¥650) using Chiba's Yachimata-grown peanuts.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, the shop's own site/Instagram, or several local blog write-ups (all returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch); facts above are drawn from search-engine snippets/summaries of PR Times, EnjoyTokyo, and Straight Press coverage plus Tabelog's listing title. No vegan or gluten-free claims were found anywhere for this shop — dietary fields reflect that absence, not a confirmed \"no.\" Seat count and exact closed-day policy are weakly sourced (a single aggregator snippet) and should be treated as approximate.

gf_detail: No gluten-free claims, allergen list, or wheat-avoidance information were found for this shop in any source checked. As a gelato/ice specialty shop, base gelato is often naturally free of wheat, but the 40+ flavor lineup likely includes cookie, wafer, or biscuit mix-ins and cones, and scoops are typically served from open, shared cases with shared scoops — a cross-contamination risk for celiac diners. Nothing confirms a dedicated gluten-free process. Ask staff which specific flavors and cones are wheat-free before ordering.
hours_raw: Weekdays 9:30-17:00; Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 9:30-18:00 (per opening announcement); closed-day policy not confirmed.

bio.origin: Chef reported to hail from Nagasaki, Kyushu
bio.background: Opened in Udagawa-cho, Shibuya in October 2024 as the sister restaurant of the Shoto-area izakaya "Michi Nakimichi" (ミチナキミチ). The space, a former café, was DIY-renovated into a green-and-wood natural-toned izakaya & bar spanning the 3rd and 4th floors of the Shin Tokyo Building, functioning like a café by day for lunch and transforming into a full Asian-style izakaya by night.
bio.specialty: Avocado and tuna yukhoe (avocado to maguro no yukke) and herb-grilled chicken are cited as signature dishes; a cream-sauce salmon steak is also frequently mentioned.
bio.philosophy: Homemade, herb-forward cooking that blends Japanese technique with Asian/ethnic flavors (described locally as "和なエスニック料理"), built around fresh vegetables and seafood, with an emphasis on visually striking plating and Kyushu-sourced ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe it as a hidden-gem Asian creative izakaya near Shibuya Station, with an Instagram-worthy DIY green-and-wood interior and standout dishes like the avocado-tuna yukhoe and herb-grilled chicken.
Described as a sister restaurant to the popular izakaya Michi Nakimichi, run by a Kyushu (Nagasaki)-born izakaya cook who applies local Kyushu ingredients to a Japanese-inflected ethnic-food concept.

caveats: Sources are mostly aggregator/reservation sites and social-media blog posts rather than a fetchable official website (Tabelog, Instagram, Retty, Gnavi, Yahoo Map, and other listing pages all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so details are drawn from web-search summaries of those pages). Reported hours differ slightly between sources (11:30-16:00/16:00-23:00 vs. 12:00-16:00/16:00-23:30); the 11:30/16:00/23:00 schedule (attributed to the restaurant's own reservation/Tabelog listing) is used here. Floor listed as "3F/4F" in some sources vs "1F bar / 2F izakaya" in another — likely a mix-up with the sister restaurant; the 3F/4F address is used since it appears in the majority of sources alongside the same building name. No vegan- or gluten-free-specific information was found anywhere; this is a general Asian-fusion izakaya, not a dietary-specialty restaurant, so GF/vegan fields reflect an "ask staff" default rather than confirmed accommodation. Seat count could not be confirmed from any accessible source.

Signature dishes are meat- and seafood-based (avocado-tuna yukhoe, herb-grilled chicken, cream-sauce salmon steak), and as a Japanese-Asian izakaya, hidden dashi/katsuo (bonito) in sauces and broths is likely. Vegetable-forward small plates may exist given the "homemade herb" cooking style, but this could not be confirmed. Vegans should ask staff about ingredients dish-by-dish.

lead: 炭火焼干物定食 しんぱち食堂
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町3-7-14 たかのビル1F

bio.origin: Chain brand (57+ locations nationwide as of 2026), now part of the Skylark Group restaurant company; this Suidobashi branch opened around April 2020.
bio.background: Shinpachi Shokudo is a Japanese set-meal (teishoku) chain built around charcoal-grilled dried fish (himono). The brand's stated mission is to "restore and evolve Japan's everyday food culture," pairing carefully sourced grilled fish with attention to rice quality, house-made dashi-based miso soup, and pickles from established, traditional producers.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled dried fish set meals — the menu features more than 20 kinds of grilled fish (salmon, mackerel, sardine, aji, etc.), grilled to order on a proprietary charcoal grilling setup rather than pre-cooked, alongside a signature unagi (eel) donburi made with eel from a hundred-year-old wholesaler.
bio.philosophy: "Protecting a value that is one of a kind" — fish is grilled to order on an in-house-developed charcoal grill after the order is placed, with sourcing regions for the dried fish carefully chosen; the brand frames itself as reviving traditional Japanese home-style cooking (gohan, miso shiru, tsukemono) at accessible prices.
roles: Japanese set-meal (teishoku) chain restaurant, counter/table seating arranged in a U-shape around an open kitchen

caveats: This is a large nationwide teishoku chain (57+ locations, part of Skylark Group), not a gluten-free or vegan specialty spot and not a small independent mom-and-pop shop. No allergy menu, dedicated gluten-free options, or vegan accommodations were found in any source. Direct fetches of Tabelog and several store-info pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment; facts below are drawn from WebSearch result summaries of gnavi, hitosara, navitime, the official shinpachi-shokudo.com site, and the Skylark Group 2026 press release, cross-checked across multiple independent listings for consistency.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）— no regular closed day found across multiple listings
cuisine: 日本料理（炭火焼干物、定食）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, high-turnover Japanese set-meal chain with picture-menu teishoku boards; basic Japanese or pointing at menu photos is enough to order, but no English menu was confirmed.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Address and multiple map listings (Navitime, Yahoo Map, Goo Map) place the restaurant roughly 0.2km / ~1-3 min walk from JR Suidōbashi Station west exit, well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or dedicated preparation process was found in any source for this location or the chain generally. The teishoku format bundles grilled fish with miso soup, pickles, and often a dipping sauce; Japanese soy sauce and some pickles/marinades commonly contain wheat, and nothing indicates ingredient substitution or cross-contact controls. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten avoidance without direct, in-person confirmation from staff, and even then reliability is uncertain given the absence of any published allergen information.
name_en: Sumibiyaki Himono Teishoku Shinpachi Shokudo (Suidōbashi branch)
name_ja: 炭火焼干物定食 しんぱち食堂 水道橋店
neighborhood: 神田三崎町（千代田区）、水道橋駅西口近く

positives.text: Dried fish is grilled to order over charcoal rather than fried, and the menu emphasizes simple preparations (salt-grilled or dashi-based) rather than heavily sauced dishes, which may simplify identifying lower-risk items — though this has not been confirmed with staff.

seats_text: Seat count not published in available sources; described as counter and table seating arranged in a U-shape around the open kitchen.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around grilled dried fish, and the miso soup is made with traditional Japanese dashi, which is typically bonito (katsuobushi)-based and not vegan. No vegetarian or vegan set meal, dashi substitution, or plant-based option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners as currently documented.

lead: すっぽん 田一（でんいち）
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区代々木2-15-12 クランツ南新宿1F

bio.background: A specialty Japanese course restaurant built entirely around suppon (soft-shell snapping turtle), located steps from Minami-Shinjuku Station on the south side of Shinjuku Station.
bio.specialty: すっぽん革命コース (Suppon Kakumei course) — kuzu-starch-fried turtle karaage, liver sashimi salad, chawanmushi, suppon hot pot, and a finishing rice porridge (zōsui)
bio.philosophy: The restaurant sources 'Hagakure suppon' from farms across Japan that the owners visited personally, raised without pesticides or herbicides in EM-bacteria-treated water and fed only pesticide-free vegetable feed — quality said to command roughly double the price of standard farmed suppon.

anecdotes.text: Its signature sourcing story: turtles are raised in specially selected farms using microorganism (EM)-treated water and pesticide-free feed, described by the restaurant as yielding a noticeably higher-grade 'Hagakure suppon' than typical farmed turtle.

Course descriptions note the karaage (fried turtle) is coated in kuzu (arrowroot) starch rather than a standard batter, served alongside liver sashimi, chawanmushi, hot pot and a closing rice porridge as part of the multi-course meal.

caveats: This is a high-end specialty suppon (soft-shell turtle) restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan establishment. Every course is built around turtle meat, egg-based chawanmushi, and traditional dashi/soy-based hot pot broth. Dietary details below are inferred from published course descriptions, not from direct restaurant confirmation of allergen handling — call ahead to confirm before booking if you have celiac disease or are strictly vegan.
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日)
cuisine: すっぽん料理・日本料理 (Soft-shell turtle course cuisine, Japanese haute cuisine)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, reservation-only Japanese course restaurant with semi-private table rooms; expect Japanese-language service and no English menu, so it helps to confirm dietary needs by phone (or with a Japanese-speaking helper) before your visit.
geocode_note: Address places it in Yoyogi 2-chome, Shibuya-ku, directly adjacent to Minami-Shinjuku Station and referred to in multiple listings as the "新宿南口店" (Shinjuku South Exit branch) — well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station. Per instructions, lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or wheat-free accommodation is advertised. The signature karaage (fried turtle) is coated in kuzu (arrowroot) starch rather than wheat flour, which is a favorable sign, but the course also includes hot pot and dipping preparations typical of Japanese haute cuisine that commonly use wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) and bonito-based dashi; no statement about gluten-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination handling was found. Diners needing strict gluten avoidance should call ahead and confirm specifics with staff.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Dinner 17:00-22:00. Sat & holidays: Lunch 12:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00 (Saturday/holiday lunch reservations must be made by 18:00 the previous day). Closed Sundays.
name_ja: すっぽん 田一（でんいち）
neighborhood: 南新宿 (Minami-Shinjuku)
seats_text: 26 seats total, including 5 semi-private table rooms (partitioned, seating 10-16 combined), plus counter and pair seating
vegan_detail: This is a specialty suppon (soft-shell turtle) restaurant — every course centers on turtle meat (hot pot, karaage, liver sashimi) alongside egg-based chawanmushi and dashi-based broths. There is no vegetarian or vegan course offered, and nothing in the published menus suggests any plant-based option exists.

cuisine: 居酒屋・季節魚（Izakaya / seasonal fish and sake)
neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Asakusa Kannonura)
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF preparation protocol was found in any source. This is a traditional seasonal-fish izakaya built around simmered, grilled and dried fish dishes with soy-sauce-based seasoning, so wheat (via shoyu) should be assumed present in most cooked dishes and glazes. Some plain grilled or raw fish items may be naturally lower-risk, but nothing has been verified as celiac-safe — ask staff carefully about individual dishes and sauces before ordering.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is oriented around daily fresh wild fish, homemade dried fish (自家製干物), and pure rice sake pairings; roughly 35 dishes are offered and fish dominates, with fish-based dashi assumed throughout. No vegan dishes or plant-based accommodations were found in available sources — not a suitable choice for vegan diners.
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-34-3
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address is in the Kannonura backstreets directly behind Sensoji Temple, well within easy walking distance of Sensoji — consistent with multiple sources describing it as located in the 観音裏 (Kannonura) area.
seats_text: Small counter-style izakaya: about 7 counter seats plus a 4-person raised seating area (roughly 11 seats total, per secondary sources).
closed_days: Monday (open if Monday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional counter-seat izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking service — best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or using a translation app; reservations are recommended given the very limited seating.

bio.background: Opened in 2017 in the Kannonura backstreets behind Sensoji Temple, the shop quickly built a following among Tokyo food and sake enthusiasts and went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing.
bio.specialty: Homemade dried fish (自家製干物) and daily fresh wild fish dishes paired with pure rice (junmai) sake served at room temperature or gently warmed.
bio.philosophy: The name "ずぶ六" is an old Edo-period term for someone who is thoroughly, happily drunk — the shop's aim is for the food and sake to be good enough that guests end up exactly that way.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, 'ずぶ六,' is an old term for someone completely drunk — reflecting the shop's goal of food and sake good enough to leave guests wonderfully so.
The owner commutes in from Saitama and personally selects the day's fish at Adachi Market before service, then prepares roughly 35 dishes a day including handmade dried fish.
The shop pours only junmai (pure rice) sake — about 10 served chilled and 15 at room temperature or warmed — in 90/120/150ml measures so guests can taste widely.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog or several Japanese food-guide pages (blocked by the network proxy with 403 errors), so this record relies on web-search snippets and secondary write-ups (Sapporo Beer blog, a sake-focused blog, Asakusa tourism site, dancyu) rather than primary-page fetches. Core facts (address, hours, closed day, cash-only, seat count, opening year, Michelin Bib Gourmand status) were each corroborated by at least two independent sources. No information specific to gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found anywhere — this appears to be a conventional fish-and-sake izakaya with no dietary-accommodation focus.

lead: 元祖油堂 宮益坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-19-15

bio.origin: Ganso Aburado is a Tokyo-born abura soba (oil noodle / brothless ramen) chain run by Gift Holdings, with branches across Shibuya, Ebisu, Shinjuku, Kitasenju, Tamachi, Kashiwa, Sendai and Yokohama; this Miyamasuzaka branch opened October 23, 2025 right by Shibuya Station.
bio.background: Part of a specialty abura-soba brand built around a custom house noodle and a secret sweet-savory soy tare finished with flavored oil.
bio.specialty: Abura soba (oil noodles) tossed table-side in the house tare and seasoned oil, topped with egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese, with a large 225g noodle portion as the standard size.
bio.philosophy: The brand's stated approach is to let diners build their own bowl: a custom-developed wheat noodle for smooth texture and chew, paired with a rich soy-based tare and flavored oil, then finished at the table with a wide array of condiments so each customer can adjust the flavor to their own taste.
roles: Abura soba (oil noodle) specialty restaurant chain location

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger wrote that compared to other oil-soba shops this location isn't overly greasy, with a mild soy-sauce flavor that lets the richness of the egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese come through, paired with thick, chewy noodles.
Another visitor noted the shop offers a large (225g) noodle portion, an 880-yen bowl with a drink bar included, and more than ten table-side flavor-changing condiments, popular with younger diners.
A local blogger described the Miyamasuzaka branch as having a stylish, women-friendly interior.

caveats: This is a wheat-noodle abura-soba (oiled ramen) chain shop, not a place set up for gluten-free or vegan diners; noodles, soy-based tare, and broth are all standard wheat/animal-based, so it is included here only as a verified, currently-operating spot within the target radius, not as a GF/vegan recommendation.
closed_days: None listed — open every day of the week (no regular closed days).
cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば (Abura soba / oil noodles, brothless ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: Casual counter-seat noodle shop with a picture-friendly menu and table condiments to self-customize the noodles; a little Japanese (or pointing) helps for ordering toppings and mix-in flavors, but it's an easy walk-in experience.
gf_detail: The dish is inherently wheat-based: the house noodle is a custom wheat noodle and the tare (sauce) is soy-sauce based, both containing gluten. The chain's own allergen chart lists wheat (小麦) as present in the noodle dish along with egg and dairy. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated gluten-free process is documented, so this is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
name_ja: 元祖油堂 宮益坂店
neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka), Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain's official allergy information chart lists wheat (小麦), egg, and dairy as allergens present in the oil-noodle dish, consistent with the soy-based tare and standard wheat noodle used chain-wide.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes a printed allergen chart per menu item, noting that sensitivity varies by individual and the chart is meant as a reference rather than a guarantee against reactions.

seats_text: 18 counter seats
vegan_detail: The standard bowl uses a chicken/pork-style soy tare and toppings such as egg yolk and Parmesan-style cheese; no vegan or vegetarian broth, noodle, or topping option is documented on the official site or in reviews, so this is not a vegan-friendly choice as served.
geocode_note: Address 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-19-15 (Miyamasuzaka) is described in multiple sources as a 1-minute walk from Shibuya Station's B4 exit, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595,139.7005). Network geocoding is blocked in this environment; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-16-10

roles: founder/owner (name not disclosed in available sources)
bio.background: Opened in June 2020 in a renovated old Japanese house (kominka) near Suidobashi Station, HATONOMORI was launched after its founder happened to taste fresh, never-frozen mutton in Nagano Prefecture and wanted to bring that experience to Tokyo diners.
bio.origin: Iida City, Nagano Prefecture (source of the fresh mutton and the secret tare recipe)
bio.philosophy: The shop argues that mutton's reputation for a strong, unpleasant smell comes from frozen meat; fresh mutton shipped directly (never frozen) has a delicate flavor, soft texture, and natural sweetness that doesn't need to be masked with rice or heavy seasoning.
bio.specialty: 生マトンジンギスカン — fresh (never-frozen) mutton and lamb grilled tableside on a jingisukan dome, dipped in a secret tare sauce that is a reproduction of a famous restaurant's recipe from Iida, Nagano.

anecdotes.text: The founder was captivated by mutton eaten by chance in Nagano and decided to open a Tokyo restaurant built entirely around fresh, never-frozen mutton shipped directly from Iida City, launching HATONOMORI in a renovated old folk house near Suidobashi Station in June 2020.
A reviewer described the lamb as tender and delicious eaten rare, though found the accompanying garlic-forward tare sauce strong enough to overshadow the meat's quality.

caveats: Site was verified via WebSearch result snippets and cached page summaries; direct WebFetch of tabelog.com, hitosara.com, and the restaurant's own gorp.jp site was blocked (HTTP 403) by the environment's outbound proxy for these hosts, so no page was read in full — only aggregated search-result text. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (sauce ingredients, allergen policy, cross-contact practices) was found in any source; this is a meat-centric jingisukan restaurant with an unusually short dinner-only service window (17:00-19:00) and no lunch or Sunday/holiday service outside private bookings, so advance reservation is strongly advisable even though not confirmed as strictly required.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（予約による貸切対応は除く）
cuisine: ジンギスカン（生マトン・ラム専門 / Grilled fresh mutton & lamb, Genghis Khan style）
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu, English signage, or English-speaking-staff mentions were found in any source, so expect a Japanese-language-only experience.
gf_detail: The menu centers on grilled fresh mutton and lamb served with a proprietary secret tare dipping sauce; no gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or allergen disclosure was found in any source. Japanese tare/dipping sauces are typically soy-sauce (shoyu) based and commonly contain wheat, and the tabletop grill is shared across all diners' orders, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Celiac diners should call ahead to ask specifically about the tare's ingredients and any shared cooking surfaces before dining.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都千代田区三崎町2-16-10 by multiple independent sources (Yahoo Loco, hitosara, search aggregation of the official site), consistently described as a 3-4 minute walk from JR Chuo Line / Toei Mita Line Suidobashi Station — well within the 0.8km target radius of Suidōbashi Station (35.7017,139.7539). Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding of the address is required.
hours_raw: 月～土 ディナー 17:00～19:00（L.O.18:00、ドリンクL.O.18:30）。日曜日・祝日は予約による貸切・イベント対応のみ。

neighborhood: 水道橋（水道橋駅より徒歩3〜4分）
seats_text: 1st floor: long counter seating (renovated old Japanese house); 2nd floor: table seating for 2-4 people plus a private room for up to 4; private/group bookings for 20+ available by reservation.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated fresh-mutton-and-lamb grill restaurant built entirely around meat; no vegetarian or vegan dish, side, or menu accommodation was found in any source reviewed.

lead: てっちゃん 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-34-13 三経ビル7階

bio.background: Tetchan Shinjuku is a Showa-retro-styled meat izakaya (肉酒場) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, built around carefully prepped horumon (beef/pork offal), grilled beef tongue and Genghis Khan-style grilled lamb, served in a nostalgic, unpretentious drinking-den atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Fresh horumon (offal) that is carefully cleaned and prepped before slow simmering into kushi-nikomi (skewer stew); steam-grilled Genghis Khan lamb cooked with beer; pepper-crusted grilled beef tongue.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes thorough, careful preparation of offal to draw out its natural flavor, paired with a deliberately old-fashioned, low-cost izakaya feel so regulars can linger over cheap drinks in a retro Showa-era setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a 2-hour all-you-can-drink plan for ¥777 and glasses of alcohol priced under ¥200 regardless of how many are ordered, positioned as cheaper than a convenience store and one of the lowest prices in the area.

caveats: Business-detail fields (address, hours, seats) were corroborated across the Hotpepper Gourmet listing, Jalan, and Navitime, which broadly agreed. No source contained any vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, or food-allergy information for this specific restaurant — the dietary assessment below is inferred conservatively from the fact that the menu is entirely offal/meat-centric (horumon, beef tongue, Genghis Khan lamb) with tare/shoyu-based sauces and stews, not from any explicit statement by the restaurant. Payment method (card vs. cash-only) and exact independence/chain status could not be positively confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / open every day, no regular closed day）
cuisine: 居酒屋・肉料理 (izakaya / meat & offal grill)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-language service was confirmed in any source, so this reads as a standard Japanese-language neighborhood izakaya best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese or by pointing at photos.
geocode_note: Address (新宿区新宿3-34-13) is described by multiple sources as roughly 1 minute's walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit A5 and about 5 minutes' walk from JR Shinjuku Station's central ticket gate, placing it comfortably within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station. lat/lng left null per instructions (geocoding derived downstream from address_ja).
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation was found in any source. The menu centers on horumon (offal) skewer-stew, tare-glazed Genghis Khan lamb, and grilled beef tongue — dishes that typically rely on shoyu- and tare-based sauces (soy sauce commonly contains wheat) and shared grill/stew equipment, so cross-contact risk should be assumed high and celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
name_ja: てっちゃん 新宿店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku
seats_text: Approx. 60 seats (per Hotpepper Gourmet listing); private/group buyout available for up to 50 guests.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu, options, or accommodations were found in any source. The restaurant's entire identity and signature dishes are built around animal offal, beef tongue, and lamb, with no evidence of a vegetable-forward alternative; assume this is not workable for a vegan diner without prior direct arrangement with the restaurant.

lead: ビストロ デ マニラ

bio.origin: フィリピン
bio.background: フィリピン出身のシェフが浅草3丁目交差点近くで営む、東京では珍しいフィリピン料理専門の洋食店。深夜から早朝まで営業しており、遅い時間の食事を求める客に本場の味を提供している。
bio.specialty: アドボ（豚肉または鶏肉を酢と醤油ベースのたれで甘酸っぱく煮込んだフィリピンの国民食）
roles: シェフ（フィリピン出身）

anecdotes.text: アドボは酸っぱさが控えめに調整されており、日本人客にも食べやすい味わいで、肉が非常に柔らかいと評判。
深夜から早朝まで営業する珍しい業態で、深夜料金のメニュー設定がある。

caveats: This is a niche Filipino-comfort-food late-night bistro, not a restaurant that markets itself toward gluten-free or vegan diners. No gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient disclosure, or allergy protocol could be confirmed from any source; the signature dish (adobo) is soy-sauce and meat based. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should treat this as a low-confidence match and inquire directly (Japanese-language phone call likely needed) before visiting.

cultural_comfort_note: Small, independent, cash-only late-night spot with no confirmed English menu or website beyond a Facebook page — phone reservations and ordering are likely Japanese-only, so bring basic Japanese or a translation app.

Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from staff in Japanese.

Vegan diners would need to ask staff directly in Japanese and should not assume any dish is vegan by default.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-26-21 東急ステイ渋谷新南口1F

bio.background: TENCUPS opened on the ground floor of the Tokyu Stay Shibuya Shin-Minamiguchi hotel as a café-by-day, cocktail-lounge-by-night concept. Its name is drawn from the tarot card "Ten of Cups," which symbolizes harmony and connection between people.
bio.specialty: Hand-made bakery items and café drinks (including a vegan banana cake and pandan latte) by day; Asian fusion small plates and natural-wine cocktails as a music lounge from evening.
bio.philosophy: Built around the "Ten of Cups" idea of nurturing connection — through loved ones, music, and food that engages the senses — the space is meant to be a gathering point for people to share culture and build relationships, not just a place to eat.

anecdotes.text: TENCUPS takes its name and concept from the tarot card "Ten of Cups," aiming to be a place where food, music, and human connection intertwine across all times of day.
The café offers hand-made bakery items and original café drinks from morning, transitioning into a music lounge with cocktails and Asian fusion cuisine from 6pm, with DJ and live events at night.

caveats: Hours and closed-day details vary slightly across secondary listings (some show irregular holidays instead of a fixed Monday closure); the Monday-closed, Tue-Sun schedule is corroborated by multiple Tabelog user reviews and is used here as the best-supported version. No source could be directly fetched (network policy blocked most Japanese restaurant/booking sites); all facts are drawn from search-engine snippets of those pages rather than full page reads, so some menu/allergy detail could not be independently verified.
closed_days: 月曜日 (closed Mondays)
cuisine: Café / Asian-inspired fusion cuisine and cocktail lounge
cultural_comfort_note: Set inside an international hotel (Tokyu Stay) near Shibuya Station, so basic English is likely manageable, but detailed allergy conversations should be confirmed in simple Japanese or via translation app.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, rice-flour options, or allergy accommodations are mentioned in any source found. The menu centers on hand-made bakery items (croissants, cookies, cake) which are typically wheat-based, and the evening Asian fusion dishes may use soy sauce or other wheat-containing condiments. Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without direct confirmation from staff, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation area or fryer was identified.
neighborhood: Shibuya Shin-Minamiguchi (新南口), Shibuya

positives.text: A vegan banana cake is listed as a standing menu item alongside items like pandan latte, indicating at least some intentional plant-based options are offered rather than being incidental.

seats_text: Approximately 50 seats (counter, table, and shared seating)
vegan_detail: A vegan banana cake is confirmed as a standing menu item, showing some plant-based options exist, but there is no evidence of a broader vegan menu or systematic labeling. The evening Asian fusion dishes and cocktail-lounge menu likely include animal-derived ingredients (dashi, fish sauce, honey, dairy) that are not called out as avoidable, so vegan diners should ask about individual dishes beyond the known vegan items.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町16-10

bio.background: The shop is a continuation of "油そば 大黒屋" (Abura Soba Daikokuya), which originally operated under the JR train tracks near Shibuya Station. When that spot had to relocate due to the Shibuya Station redevelopment, the owners reopened nearby in Sakuragaoka-cho as まぜそば七, carrying the original recipe forward under a new name and sign marked with the character 七 (seven).
bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (formerly a guardrail/underpass stall near Shibuya Station)
bio.specialty: Abura-soba / mazesoba (oil-based mixed noodles) topped with charcoal-grilled chashu pork
bio.philosophy: The shop is built around balancing three housemade elements: an aromatic oil made from garlic and ten kinds of aromatic vegetables, a shoyu-based tare aged for about a week, and a small amount of soup stirred together with the noodles. Their housemade rayu (chili oil) uses seven kinds of spices, echoing the shop's name.

anecdotes.text: ガード下の【油そば 大黒屋】が移転し、現在の『七』が目印の【まぜそば七】に生まれ変わりました。もともとは渋谷駅ガード下にて営業しており、移転して『まぜそば七』としてオープンした。
自家製香味油に厳選した唐辛子など七種類のスパイスを加えて作った自家製のラー油を使用。にんにくや10種類の香味野菜からつくった自家製香味油・1週間寝かせて作った醤油ダレ・少量のスープのバランスにこだわった油そばが特徴。

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle mazesoba/abura-soba specialist with no confirmed gluten-free or vegan accommodations. Included in the dataset as a genuine local spot, but celiac and vegan diners should not eat here without independently confirming ingredients — the noodles themselves are wheat-based and the tare is a shoyu (soy sauce) sauce, both of which contain gluten by default, and the broth/toppings are meat-based.
closed_days: No fixed closed days found (定休日なし) based on listing data.
cuisine: まぜそば・油そば (Mazesoba / abura-soba — oil-based and Taiwan-style mixed noodle specialist)
cultural_comfort_note: A small local counter-only noodle shop near Shibuya Station; expect a Japanese-language menu and a fast-paced, order-at-counter local ramen-shop atmosphere rather than English-friendly tourist service.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options were found. The noodles are standard wheat chuka-men and the signature tare is a shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) base aged about a week; no dedicated gluten-free noodle, sauce substitution, or allergen protocol is mentioned in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners without further direct confirmation from the shop, which is unlikely given the single-recipe, counter-only format.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Multiple independent listings (Tabelog, Hotpepper, official site) place the shop at 東京都渋谷区桜丘町16-10, described consistently as a 2-3 minute walk from Shibuya Station, which is well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target-area center.
hours_raw: 11:30-23:30, no fixed closed days (定休日なし)
neighborhood: Sakuragaoka-cho, Shibuya (2-3 min walk from Shibuya Station south/west exit)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is a shoyu (soy sauce)-based sauce aged for about a week; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari or GF alternative is mentioned in any source.

seats_text: 13 seats at the counter
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu options were found. The shop's signature dish is topped with charcoal-grilled pork chashu, and the aromatic oil and tare are built around meat/umami-forward flavor with no indication of a plant-based broth or hidden-dashi-free option.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-16-8 ハギワラビル1F

bio.origin: Suidōbashi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: The Suidōbashi shop is the original flagship of Torisoba Kaguraya, which opened in the early 2010s near JR/Toei Suidōbashi Station and later moved a short distance to its current building (near Exit A6) around 2019. It has since grown into a small tori-paitan (chicken white broth) ramen specialist group with several branches around Tokyo (including Shinjuku and Uguisudani).
bio.specialty: Nōjuku Torisoba (濃熟鶏そば) — a rich, collagen-laden golden chicken paitan broth simmered slowly with chicken bones and vegetables; the menu also includes a lighter clear-broth Assari Torisoba, a flying-fish (tobiuo) shoyu chicken soba, a tomato-salt chicken soba, and tsukemen dipping noodles.
bio.philosophy: Long, careful simmering of chicken and vegetables to extract maximum umami and collagen into a silky, not-too-heavy paitan broth, offered alongside lighter and flavor-variant versions so diners can choose their preferred richness.

anecdotes.text: The Suidōbashi shop is described as the honten (main/original shop) of the Kaguraya group, situated about a 1-3 minute walk from Suidōbashi Station's A6 exit, with the group's history page noting its growth from this single location into a multi-branch chain.
A food blogger described the Nōjuku Torisoba as a 'silky, deeply matured chicken soba' prized for its umami-rich collagen broth.

caveats: This is a conventional chicken-broth (tori paitan) ramen shop, not a vegan or gluten-free specialist — no dietary-accommodation information (dedicated fryer, alternative noodles, allergy protocol) was found in any source checked. Wheat-based ramen noodles and shoyu-based tare are standard here; broth is chicken-bone based throughout the menu, so there is no vegan option. Hours are reported slightly inconsistently across sources (11:00-22:30 vs 11:00-23:00 close); payment method (cash vs. card/IC) could not be confirmed from available sources. Official site (torisoba-kaguraya.com) and Tabelog returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, so details are drawn from search-engine-cached snippets of those and third-party aggregator/blog pages rather than direct page reads.
closed_days: None reported (無休 — open every day)
cultural_comfort_note: A standard Japanese neighborhood ramen shop near a busy station exit; expect a Japanese-language menu and typical counter-ramen ordering etiquette, with no confirmed English support.
cuisine: Ramen — tori paitan (rich chicken broth) ramen and tsukemen
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations found. The shop serves standard wheat ramen noodles, and its signature broths are seasoned with shoyu (soy sauce, e.g. a flying-fish/tobiuo shoyu tare) which in Japan is almost always wheat-based unless specified otherwise. No mention of gluten-free noodles, tamari, or an allergy protocol was found in any source; not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based pin placement. Multiple independent sources (search-cached official site, local ramen blogs, aggregator listings) agree the shop sits about 1-3 minutes' walk from Suidōbashi Station's A6 exit, comfortably within the 0.8km Suidōbashi target radius.
hours_raw: Reported as 11:00-22:30 daily by one aggregated source describing the main shop; another source lists 11:00-23:00 weekdays/Saturday and 11:00-23:00 Sunday for what appears to be the same shop at a prior address. No closed days (無休) reported by any source.
neighborhood: 水道橋 / Suidōbashi
seats_text: Small counter-focused shop: about 8 counter seats plus two 4-seat tables (roughly 16 seats total).
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option found. Every headline menu item (Assari Torisoba, Nōjuku Torisoba, tobiuo-shoyu chicken soba, tomato-salt chicken soba, tsukemen) is built on a chicken-bone (paitan) broth, so there is no plant-based broth alternative described anywhere in the sources checked.

lead: 麺堂にしき 新宿歌舞伎町店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-11-3 九石新歌舞伎町ビル1F

bio.background: 麺堂にしき is a small duck-broth ramen shop chain that began at a Kanda-Ogawamachi location before opening this Kabukicho branch as its second shop (2号店) on March 17, 2021. In spring 2025 the Kabukicho shop's lease ended and the team temporarily relocated operations to a new address on Shokuan-dori (Hyakunincho), opening a sister store there in April 2025. The Kabukicho location itself was revived and reopened at the same Kabukicho address on August 27, 2025, and has been operating there since, with customer reviews confirmed as recently as January 2026.
bio.origin: Kanda-Ogawamachi (original shop), expanded to Shinjuku Kabukicho in 2021
bio.specialty: 鴨だし醤油ラーメン (duck dashi shoyu ramen) — a soy-sauce ramen built on a broth that draws out the umami of duck and seafood, paired with distinctive toppings; the shop uses fine, straight, low-hydration noodles from Mikawaya Seimen for a chewy bite, along with thick-sliced chashu. A 山椒ラーメン (Sichuan pepper) variation is also offered.

anecdotes.text: When the original Kabukicho shop closed in spring 2025 after its lease ended, the owner posted on the shop's X/Twitter account: "材料が無くなったので営業終了させていただきました…来てくださった沢山のお客様のおかげでこの場所で4年続けることが出来ました 本当にありがとうございます" ("We've closed because we ran out of ingredients... thanks to the many customers who came, we were able to keep this location going for 4 years. Thank you truly.") — before relaunching nearby.

caveats: Hours/closed-day data conflict across sources: older listings (from the shop's original 2021 opening period) show 11:00-15:00/17:00-23:30 with Tuesday closed, but the most recent and mutually-consistent sources (Tabelog-derived search data, ramendb, mashup-kabukicho, HAMONI) all agree on 18:00-13:00(next day), open every day — this record uses the latter as current. The shop closed in spring 2025 when its lease ended, briefly operated only from a relocated sister store on Shokuan-dori (Hyakunincho), then reopened at this same Kabukicho address on 2025-08-27; a review dated January 2026 confirms it is currently open. Payment method (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from any source — cash_only is a best-effort default (false), not a sourced fact. No official standalone website was found; Instagram is the closest confirmed official channel. This is a wheat-noodle, duck/seafood-dashi ramen shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations found in any source.
closed_days: なし（年中無休 / no fixed closed day, per current listings）
cuisine: ラーメン（鴨出汁・醤油）/ Ramen (duck dashi, shoyu)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-speaking-staff signage was found in any source, so expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of a small Kabukicho ramen counter.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is disabled; lat/lng left null intentionally. Address confirmed identically across Tabelog-derived search results, ramendb, mashup-kabukicho, and HAMONI as 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-11-3 九石新歌舞伎町ビル1F. Multiple sources state the shop is a ~3-minute walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station, which is itself immediately adjacent to JR Shinjuku Station, placing the address well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station target-area landmark (35.6896,139.7006).
gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop: noodles are fine straight low-hydration wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, and the tare is a duck-dashi shoyu (soy sauce), which conventionally contains wheat. No source mentions a gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated gluten-free menu, or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 18:00〜翌13:00、年中無休（食べログ等、直近の複数情報源より）。開店当初（2021年）の一部情報源には11:00-15:00/17:00-23:30・火曜定休の記載もあるが、現行の情報とは一致せず古い可能性が高い。
name_ja: 麺堂にしき 新宿歌舞伎町店
neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 (Kabukicho), near Seibu-Shinjuku Station
