address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-7-21

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo
bio.background: Founded by owner Nishibori-san as a takeout-focused specialty shop; the shop is credited as the originator of Asakusa's now-popular charcoal-grilled eel rice ball (unagi yaki-onigiri).
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled domestic eel rice ball (国産鰻の焼きおにぎり)
bio.philosophy: Serve quality domestic eel (mainly from Miyazaki) at prices well below typical unagi specialty restaurants, so more people can enjoy it casually while walking around Asakusa.

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder, Nishibori-san, came up with the idea of an eel-topped grilled rice ball so people could enjoy domestic eel more casually; he chose the Asakusa location for its warm, friendly shitamachi (old-downtown) character and the crowds that gather there.
The menu was developed together with a veteran eel craftsman, using domestic eel (mainly from Miyazaki) and Tochigi-grown Koshihikari rice, carefully grilled over high-heat binchotan charcoal.

caveats: Hours found across sources were inconsistent (variants included 9:30-17:30 / 10:00-17:00 on weekdays and 9:30-18:20 / 10:00-18:15 on weekends); Wednesday closure was consistent across sources. As a small-batch takeout shop it also may sell out and close early on busy days. Official site (unana.jp) and Tabelog page could not be directly fetched (403) — details are drawn from search-indexed content of those and several secondary Japanese sources (san-tatsu.jp, kumin.news, NAVITIME, Retty/Hotpepper aggregated data). No restaurant-specific allergy or gluten-free statement was found anywhere; the GF assessment below is a cautious inference from the standard kabayaki (soy-sauce glaze) preparation method, not a sourced claim from the shop itself.
closed_days: Wednesdays (may also close early when sold out)
cuisine: うなぎ（Unagi eel specialty, takeout-focused）
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is in Japanese, sometimes via a numbered ticket system when queues form, with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff — a translation app or pointing at photos will help.
gf_detail: This is a traditional kabayaki-style eel shop: the eel rice balls, liver skewers, and rice bowls are all basted with a soy-sauce based sweet glaze (tare) while grilling. No gluten-free or wheat-free tare, dedicated allergy menu, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Assume wheat exposure through the glaze and confirm directly with staff before ordering if you have celiac disease.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null (network geocoding is blocked in this environment); downstream geocoding should use address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-7-21.
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri approx. 9:30–17:30, Sat/Sun/Holidays approx. 9:30–18:20; closed Wednesdays. (Some sources list slightly different times, e.g. 10:00–17:00 weekdays / 10:00–18:15 weekends — treat as approximate and expect early sell-out closures.)
neighborhood: Asakusa, near Hanayashiki amusement park
seats_text: Primarily a takeout/walk-up counter; a few nearby eat-in spots exist with a view of the Hanayashiki roller coaster, plus weekend/holiday-only tatami seating for about 12 people on the 2nd floor.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated eel (unagi) specialty shop — every dish centers on grilled eel (rice balls, liver skewers, kabayaki, and unaju rice bowls). There are no vegan or vegetarian menu items.

address_ja: 〒150-0043 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-12 道玄坂通 1F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2016)
bio.background: Part of the WithGreen salad bowl chain, founded in Tokyo in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi (武文智洋) and grown to roughly 54 locations nationwide by its 10th anniversary in 2026. This Dogenzaka-dori shop opened on August 24, 2023 as the chain's 22nd store overall and its 3rd in the Shibuya area, occupying the 1st floor of the Dogenzaka-dori mixed-use building about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A2 exit.
bio.specialty: Gizzard salad (砂肝サラダ) - the chain's best-selling bowl, alongside build-your-own salad bowls with a choice of over 10 dressings
bio.philosophy: Built around the slogan '一杯で主食になるサラダ' (a salad that becomes your whole meal in one bowl) - domestically grown Japanese vegetables (used exclusively except in cases like typhoon-related shortages) are combined with a customer's choice of protein and dressing so a salad can stand in as a full lunch rather than a side dish.
roles: Fast-casual, made-to-order salad bowl counter (order at register, eat-in or takeout)

anecdotes.text: WithGreen's concept is a salad that becomes your main meal in one bowl, founded in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi and grown to roughly 54 locations nationwide by its 10th anniversary in 2026.

The Dogenzaka-dori branch opened August 24, 2023 as the chain's 22nd store and its 3rd in the Shibuya area, on the 1st floor of the Dogenzaka-dori building about 2 minutes from Shibuya Station's A2 exit.
The chain's signature gizzard salad (砂肝サラダ) has sold over 1 million bowls company-wide since launch.

caveats: This is a fast-casual chain location (not an independent mom-and-pop shop). Store-specific celiac/allergen verification (e.g., a confirmed dedicated allergen-free prep process) was not found; guidance below is based on the chain's general allergen disclosures rather than this branch specifically. Confirm any dietary need with staff before ordering.
closed_days: No regular closed days were found in available sources; listings show it operating daily 10:00-21:00.
cuisine: Salad bowls, healthy lunch sets, build-your-own bowl with vegetable/protein/dressing customization
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is counter-service with a build-your-own format (pick base, toppings, dressing) shown on Japanese menu boards; basic Japanese or pointing at photos will get you through, no confirmed English menu.
geocode_note: Address (渋谷区道玄坂2-25-12) places the shop on Dogenzaka-dori, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's A2 exit, well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble landmark. Per instructions, lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: Bowls are vegetable-forward and customizable (choose base, toppings, protein, dressing), so a wheat-free combination is plausible, but the chain's own allergen chart lists wheat, soy, and apple as allergens found in the finishing sauces/dressings poured over the salads (後かけのタレ), and the chain notes possible cross-contamination and advises asking staff about honey/allergen content per item. No dedicated gluten-free prep line or fryer was confirmed. Celiac diners should ask staff which specific dressings are wheat-free before ordering.
hours_raw: 10:00-21:00 daily (eat-in last order 20:30)
neighborhood: Shibuya Dogenzaka (渋谷道玄坂通), near Shinsen Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: The chain's allergen chart flags wheat, soy, and apple as allergens present in the finishing sauces/dressings, and notes possible cross-contamination between menu items; customers are advised to check with in-store staff for specifics.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several of the chain's dressings are soy/wheat-based finishing sauces (後かけのタレ) that are called out specifically as containing wheat and soy allergens.

vegan_cross_contact.text: At least one dressing (a lemon-based dressing) is labeled vegetarian/vegan-compatible on the chain's menu at other WithGreen branches (Kagurazaka, Aoyama); it is not confirmed whether this exact labeling is posted at the Dogenzaka-dori branch.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain directs customers with allergen concerns, including questions about honey use and potential cross-contamination, to ask in-store staff directly rather than relying solely on printed charts.

positives.text: Salads are built to order from a wide choice of over 10 dressings and toppings, using domestically grown Japanese vegetables.

seats_text: 12 seats, counter/eat-in style
vegan_detail: A vegetable-only bowl can likely be assembled by skipping the default meat/gizzard/chicken topping and choosing a plant-based dressing (a lemon dressing is marked vegetarian/vegan-compatible on the chain's menu at some branches), but standard bowls default to animal protein, and dashi/animal-stock use in dressings is not fully confirmed. Ask staff to confirm a specific bowl is fully vegan before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町1-2-4 冨田屋ビル1階

bio.background: Hirano-san started out running a mobile kitchen-car (food truck) specializing in mackerel dishes, drawn to the close, direct customer interaction that street food allows. He decided to go independent during the COVID-19 pandemic, saying he was partly inspired to start his own business after watching the Korean drama "Itaewon Class" (梨泰院クラス). In July 2023 he opened a fixed shop in Jimbocho/Kanda-Sarugakucho.
bio.specialty: Saba no kuro-ni (鯖の黒煮) — mackerel simmered for two days in a tamari-based sauce until the bones soften enough to eat whole.
bio.philosophy: Carried over from his food-truck days: keep the counter small and the connection with customers close, building the menu around a slow-cooked signature dish rather than a large fixed menu.

anecdotes.text: When the shop first opened it sold only about 35 meals a day; business picked up after Hirano-san began offering a daily fried-horse-mackerel (aji fry) special alongside the mackerel stew. The menu has since grown to roughly 15 items centered on chicken and seafood.

caveats: This is a fish/meat-centered teishoku (set-meal) shop with no gluten-free or vegan accommodations found anywhere in public sources — best suited to omnivore diners, not GF/vegan travelers. Payment method (cash-only vs. card/e-money accepted) could not be confirmed from any source. Some listings (e.g. Hot Pepper Gourmet, which categorizes a same-named "食堂 新" as an izakaya) show an expanded schedule of 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-22:00 with Saturdays sometimes open and reservations for evening service; this conflicts with the Tabelog-derived, multiply-corroborated lunch-only schedule (Mon-Fri 11:00-14:30, closed Sat/Sun/holidays) used here as the primary record. It's possible the shop added dinner/izakaya service after the original lunch-only opening, or that the Hot Pepper listing is a related-but-distinct entity at a different address — this should be reconfirmed directly before a weekend/evening visit.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays (Saturday is occasionally open — best to call ahead: 03-5577-7576)
cuisine: Fresh seafood teishoku (Japanese set meals) — signature slow-simmered mackerel (saba no kuroni), fried horse mackerel (aji fry), and rotating chicken/seafood obanzai sides; lunch only
cultural_comfort_note: A small, fast-paced counter-seat neighborhood shokudo near Jimbocho Station catering mostly to local office workers and students; expect a Japanese-only menu and no particular English support.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Kanda-Sarugakucho (神田猿楽町) 1-2-4, Chiyoda-ku, Tomitaya Building 1F — about a 4-minute walk from Jimbocho Station and well within walking distance of Suidobashi Station. Per instructions, lat/lng are left null (no geocoding performed); pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or accommodations were found in any source. The signature mackerel stew is simmered in a tamari-based soy sauce (たまり醤油) rather than standard koikuchi shoyu, but this has not been confirmed wheat-free and is not marketed as a GF measure. The menu also includes breaded, deep-fried items (aji fry / fried horse mackerel), which virtually guarantees wheat exposure and shared-fryer cross-contact. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 月〜金 11:00〜14:30 (L.O.) ／ 土・日・祝 定休 (Hot Pepper's listing separately shows 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-22:00 with Saturdays sometimes open — unreconciled, see caveats)
neighborhood: Jimbocho / Kanda-Sarugakucho (神田猿楽町), Chiyoda-ku — near Suidobashi

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature saba kuro-ni is simmered for two days in a tamari-based soy sauce (たまり醤油) rather than standard shoyu; this has not been confirmed as wheat-free by the restaurant and should not be assumed safe for celiac diners.

seats_text: Small counter-style seating; exact seat count not published. One diner review notes a narrow entrance with steps down to the counter area.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian offerings were found — the menu centers entirely on mackerel, horse mackerel, chicken, and other seafood dishes, with dashi/fish-based sauces used throughout (e.g., the tamari-based simmering liquid for the signature dish). Not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-19-2 第1セイコービル1F

bio.background: Opened in 1971 under the original name "Ishikarigawa Ramen," one of the early Tokyo shops to introduce Hokkaido-style miso ramen to the city. The shop was rebranded to its current name, 十味や, in 2002.
bio.specialty: 濃厚合わせ味噌ラーメン (rich combined-miso ramen), alongside white miso, red miso, and spicy miso variations, served with house-made wavy noodles.
bio.philosophy: The name "十味" ("ten flavors") comes from an original ten-ingredient spice blend the shop developed itself — including chili, white sesame, Sichuan pepper, garlic, ginger, and dried shrimp — used to season its miso ramen and give it a distinctive, layered flavor.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name '十味' comes from a self-developed ten-ingredient spice blend (chili, white sesame, Sichuan pepper, garlic, ginger, dried shrimp, and others) used to season the ramen.
A local ramen writer described it as a miso ramen shop 'loved for 53 years' that regulars can eat every day, noting the free large-noodle portions and brisk counter service.
A food writer's essay on the shop is titled 'Miso ramen you can eat every day / Shinjuku west exit's Tomiya,' describing it as a neighborhood staple near Shinjuku's west exit.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle miso ramen shop with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation in any source found; included for completeness/context only, not recommended for celiac or strict vegan diners.
closed_days: None — open every day of the year (無休), including holidays.

cultural_comfort_note: Orders are placed at a Japanese-only ticket vending machine and paid in cash, with no English menu identified, though the simple ramen-counter format is easy to follow even without fluency.

Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町30 SHIBUYAHILLS B1F

bio.background: Chef Tomohiro Kumagai trained for about ten years at Towers Restaurant Cucagne inside the Cerulean Tower Tokyu Hotel in Shibuya, working his way up from banquet cooking to the hotel's signature French dining room 150 meters above the street. In 2021 he won the Grand Prize at the Rougié Recipe Competition and placed second at the APGF U-29 French Cuisine Competition, and in 2022 he was selected as one of 16 rising 'focus chefs' for Diner's Club France Restaurant Week, Japan's largest French food event. He opened C'est ouf in June 2025 together with owners Kenta and Kota Sanda, the brothers behind the Shibuya izakaya Otonage and the shochu bar Achoo, in the same basement as their daytime cafe Cherry Pick Hills.
bio.philosophy: Bringing hotel-trained French technique down to street level: instead of a fixed tasting course, Kumagai serves his cooking as a la carte small plates at a standing counter built around an open kitchen, paired with natural wine, so guests can drop in without a reservation and eat and drink as much or as little as they like.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, C'est ouf, is French slang for 'amazing!' or 'crazy!' — a nod to the surprise of finding serious hotel-caliber French cooking inside an unpretentious, reservation-free standing bar.

C'est ouf opened on June 29, 2025 in the basement of the same Sakuragaoka-cho building as Cherry Pick Hills, the daytime cafe also run by the Sanda brothers, who are known in Shibuya for the izakaya Otonage and shochu bar Achoo.

Kumagai spent roughly ten years cooking at Towers Restaurant Cucagne in the Cerulean Tower Tokyu Hotel before going independent, and has placed in national French-cuisine competitions including the Rougié Recipe Competition and APGF U-29.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated preparation area was found in any available source. This is a general modern French bistro (standing-counter style, natural wine, chef-driven a la carte plates), not a restaurant marketed toward dietary restrictions — GF/vegan diners must ask staff directly (likely in Japanese) and should not assume safety, especially for celiac-level gluten avoidance. No confirmed English menu or official website; hours are consistent daily but closures are irregular and announced only on Instagram.

Ask staff directly before ordering; do not assume any dish is safe without confirmation.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-7-14 YS西神田ビル1F

bio.chef_name: ザーチイ夫妻 (Za Chi couple)
bio.origin: Myanmar-born couple; independently opened this shop in September 2018 as a sister branch of the 35+-year Roppongi dumpling institution "肉汁水餃子 餃包" (Gyopao), where they had previously worked lunch service.
bio.background: Before striking out on their own, the couple worked the lunch shift at Gyopao's Roppongi crossing flagship. Wanting to take on the challenge of running their own restaurant at least once in their lives, and believing the Gyopao-style dumpling deserved a wider audience, they invested roughly 8 million yen of their own money to open the Suidobashi shop.
bio.specialty: Juicy pan-fried gyoza (焼き餃子) made with 100% Kagoshima black pork, alongside home-style Chinese dishes like lu rou fan (braised pork rice), oil-poached chicken, and mapo tofu.
bio.philosophy: To bring the Gyopao dumpling tradition beyond Roppongi and prove, through their own shop, that an immigrant-run neighborhood restaurant could succeed on its own terms.

anecdotes.text: Sales rose steadily from the September 2018 opening through November, dipped in December, then recovered from January 2019 and hit their original target by April 2019 -- recouping the roughly 8 million yen initial investment in about a year.

caveats: Not a gluten-free or vegan-focused restaurant -- it is an omnivorous Chinese dumpling shop. Wheat-wrapped fried gyoza and wheat-based seasonings are central to the menu, and no gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source. Included for completeness/local-color value, but should be flagged to celiac and vegan diners as effectively not suitable rather than recommended.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; possible closure over New Year holidays) -- no fixed weekly closed day found
cuisine: 中華・餃子・点心 (Chinese, dumplings/gyoza and home-style Chinese dishes)
cultural_comfort_note: A local, Japanese-language neighborhood restaurant with online booking via major Japanese platforms (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gurunavi); no confirmed English menu was found, though it does appear on Tabelog's English-language listing.
gf_detail: The signature dish is pan-fried gyoza in a wheat wrapper, and Chinese-style seasoning (wheat-based soy sauce, likely shared fry surfaces for fried items like the gyoza and oil-poached chicken) is standard across the menu. No gluten-free wrapper, dedicated fryer, or gluten-free menu was mentioned in any source checked. This is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding is blocked in this environment); address places it about a 3-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station and 5 minutes from Jimbocho Station, well within the 0.8km target radius of Suidobashi Station.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri & day before holidays: 11:00-15:30, 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:30, drink L.O. 21:30). Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:00-16:59, 17:00-20:00 (food L.O. 19:00, drink L.O. 20:00).

seats_text: About 30 seats

Not recommended for vegan diners without direct confirmation from staff.

lead: 茶庭 然花抄院 渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-21-1 渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs 5F

bio.origin: 京都・室町 (Kyoto Muromachi)
bio.background: 然花抄院（Zen Kashoin）は、大正創業のカステラ専門店・長崎堂が2009年に立ち上げた京都発の菓子・茶ブランド。京都室町本店は築300年の京町家を改装した店舗で、渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店は2012年4月オープンのブランド初の東京店。
bio.specialty: 『然』カステラ ― 京都で丹波黒豆を食べて育った鶏の卵黄のみを使い、紙焼きで毎日店内工房から届けられる出来立てのカステラ
bio.philosophy: 「花を愛でる心でお客様をもてなす」という京都本店由来のおもてなしの精神を、渋谷の茶庭でも継承している。

anecdotes.text: 渋谷ヒカリエ ShinQs店は2012年4月にオープンした、然花抄院ブランド初の東京出店で、京都の風情を楽しめる茶庭として展開している。
看板商品の『然』カステラは卵黄のみを使用し、卵・砂糖・小麦粉・牛乳・水飴で作られる紙焼きカステラ。

caveats: Corporate confectionery/tea cafe chain (not GF- or vegan-focused); signature items contain wheat, egg, and dairy with no confirmed gluten-free or vegan menu/protocols found.
closed_days: 無休（渋谷ヒカリエの営業日に準ずる）— no fixed weekly closed day; follows the Shibuya Hikarie building's calendar.
cuisine: 甘味処・日本茶カフェ（和菓子・抹茶・わらび餅）
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a major Shibuya department store (ShinQs at Hikarie) directly connected to Shibuya Station, with an English-language Tabelog listing and heavy tourist foot traffic.
gf_detail: The signature "然" castella contains wheat flour, egg, and milk and is baked fresh in-house daily; no gluten-free menu items, substitutions, or allergy protocols were found in any source. Plain matcha usucha is naturally wheat-free, but no dedicated gluten-free preparation area was confirmed and cross-contact risk in the shared confectionery kitchen is unaddressed — not recommended for celiac diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-22-12 新宿サンパークビル本館5F

bio.origin: Founded in 1949 (Showa 24) by Kobayashi Heizo, whose family shop name was 'Hayashiya.' He later reversed the characters of his given name Heizo (平三) to create 'Sanpei,' the name behind the restaurant's signature Sanpei Rice.
bio.background: Hayashiya is a long-running yoshoku (Japanese-Western) diner in a Shinjuku Station-adjacent building, preserving the look and menu style of a Showa-era department store dining hall, with counter seats overlooking the Kabukicho streets below.
bio.specialty: Omurice (omelette rice) and the revived 'Sanpei Rice' -- ketchup rice topped with a breaded pork cutlet folded in egg and finished with demi-glace sauce.
bio.philosophy: Keeps a full roster of old-fashioned yoshoku dishes -- omurice, beef stew, hamburger steak, and even offal stew rarely found at Western-style restaurants -- alive in something close to their original postwar form.

anecdotes.text: Because the founder's name (Heizo, reversed to 'Sanpei') coincidentally matched that of the famous rakugo comedian Hayashiya Shohei (林家三平), the restaurant asked him to appear in a commercial with the catchphrase 'cheap, delicious, so sorry about that' -- and he became a regular customer afterward.
The signature Sanpei Rice -- katsu folded in egg over ketchup rice with demi-glace -- was revived on the menu after a 45-year absence.

caveats: Classic Showa-era yoshoku diner with no vegan or gluten-free accommodations found in any source -- omurice, katsu, and demi-glace sauces are all wheat- and egg/meat-based. Payment method (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from available sources; call ahead to check. Exact seating count and precise closing/last-order details vary slightly across listings.
closed_days: None listed -- open year-round (年中無休) according to multiple listings.
cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese-Western), omurice / omelette rice, Showa-retro diner
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-language official presence was found, so pointing at photos of dishes (omurice, Sanpei Rice) may help non-Japanese-speaking visitors order.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free options were found in any source. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes: omurice (rice wrapped in egg, but sauces built on wheat roux/demi-glace), breaded pork cutlets (wheat flour and panko breading), and soy-sauce-based preparations. Not recommended for celiac diners.

address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草2-7-8

bio.origin: New York, USA (owner-chef ran a Japanese restaurant there for 33 years before returning to Japan)
bio.background: The owner spent 33 years cooking at a Japanese restaurant in New York before returning to Japan and opening this small tempura counter in the Kappabashi tool-town district of Nishi-Asakusa in March 2011 — the same month as the Great East Japan Earthquake, a dramatic start for the new shop. He and his wife, together with their three daughters, have run the counter as a family ever since.
bio.specialty: Seasonal vegetable and fish tempura fried in taihaku gomayu (太白胡麻油), unroasted white sesame oil
bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built around taihaku gomayu — a refined, unroasted sesame oil the owner wanted to fry with in New York but couldn't justify importing from Japan at the time; opening the shop in Asakusa finally let him cook with the oil the way he'd always intended.
roles: wife and three daughters help run the counter

anecdotes.text: 天ぷら福岡（From New York）は、ニューヨークで33年間日本食レストランで腕を磨いた店主が、2011年3月に浅草かっぱ橋に開店した天ぷら専門店です。開店予定のその時に東日本大震災が日本を襲い、それからはご夫婦と娘さん3人で力を合わせて営業を続けてきました。
太白胡麻油を使用しており、ニューヨーク時代は日本からわざわざ持っていかなければならずコスト的に合わなかったため、浅草に来てようやく使用できるようになった。
英語が堪能なご夫婦であることから、店内には外国人客も多く訪れており、ザンビア、ドイツ、オーストラリアなどからのお客様も来店している。
夜のコースはジャズミュージシャンにちなんだボブコース、コルトレインコース、マイルスコースの3種類があり、落ち着いたジャズが流れる天ぷら屋として知られる。

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodation, dedicated fryer, or dashi substitution is documented anywhere for this restaurant — treat GF/vegan claims as unconfirmed (assume standard wheat-based tempura batter and soy-based dipping sauce). Lunch service (12:00-14:30) does not accept reservations (walk-in only); dinner is course-only (Bob/Coltrane/Miles courses) and reservations are advisable given the 6-seat counter. Sources note "other irregular closures" beyond the standard Mon/Tue closed days, so hours should be reconfirmed before visiting. Payment method (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday (plus occasional unscheduled irregular closures)
cuisine: Tempura (seasonal vegetables and fish)
cultural_comfort_note: The owner and his wife are fluent English speakers who ran a restaurant in New York for over 30 years and now regularly host international guests from around the world.

Not verified safe for celiac diners — no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

positives.text: The English-speaking owner and his wife ran a Japanese restaurant in New York for 33 years and are experienced at hosting international guests, including visitors from Zambia, Germany, and Australia.

lead: 渋谷 和牛焼肉 USHIHACHI 極
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-3-1 渋谷駅前ビル 4F

bio.background: USHIHACHI 極 (Kiwami) is the premium-line Shibuya branch of the USHIHACHI wagyu yakiniku brand (operated under GYRO HOLDINGS, which also runs a sister branch in Ueno). The restaurant buys whole, carefully selected kuroge (black-haired) Japanese wagyu cattle rather than pre-cut wholesale meat, using the full animal to keep quality high while controlling cost.
bio.specialty: Toro-cut wagyu seared for three seconds on one side, USHIHACHI-style raw beef yukhoe, and salt-grilled ox tongue
bio.philosophy: The 'whole-animal purchase' model is presented as the restaurant's core idea: buying a full animal and using every cut lets the kitchen serve rare and premium parts that are hard to find elsewhere, at a price meant for everyday dining rather than only special occasions.
roles: Yakiniku restaurant (part of the USHIHACHI/GYRO HOLDINGS group)

anecdotes.text: The signature three-second-seared toro cut is marketed as having a repeat-order rate said to exceed 90% among diners who try it.

caveats: No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu was found for this restaurant in any source consulted; it is a mainstream wagyu yakiniku restaurant, not a dietary-specialty spot. GF/vegan guidance below is based on general yakiniku-format reasoning (shared grill, soy-sauce-based tare) rather than any restaurant statement about allergens — celiac and vegan diners should call ahead (03-3464-0929) to confirm before visiting. Exact total seat count and per-person budget were not confirmed from available sources.

The shared grill and shared kitchen mean cross-contact with wheat-containing sauces is likely; celiac diners should call ahead and clarify carefully rather than assume safety.

lead: 宮崎風土 くわんね
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区代々木2-2-1 新宿サザンテラス「新宿みやざき館KONNE」2階

bio.background: Opened April 28, 2018 as the restaurant corner of the Miyazaki Prefecture antenna shop 'Shinjuku Miyazaki-kan KONNE' inside Shinjuku Southern Terrace, right by Shinjuku Station's South/New South Exit. It is operated by A Pei Company, which also runs the nationwide Tsukada Farm izakaya chain.
bio.origin: Miyazaki Prefecture, Kyushu
bio.specialty: Chicken nanban — Miyazaki-style fried chicken in sweet vinegar with tartar sauce
bio.philosophy: The name 'Kuwanne' is a warm Miyazaki dialect phrase meaning roughly 'please, eat up' — chosen to express a wish for guests to enjoy sun-blessed Miyazaki ingredients in a good mood.

anecdotes.text: Manager Honma Yuta, then 31, had spent six and a half years working at the Tsukada Farm chicken chain before being put in charge of this new-concept restaurant. He kept refining the chicken nanban recipe until it won the gold award in the Chicken Nanban category at the 11th Japan Karaage Grand Prix in April 2020.
The restaurant's interior is finished with cedar wood from Obi, Miyazaki, to bring the scent and feel of the prefecture's forests into the dining room.

caveats: Postal address is in Shibuya-ku (Yoyogi 2-2-1), but the restaurant occupies the 2nd floor of the Southern Terrace / Shinjuku Miyazaki-kan KONNE building, directly at Shinjuku Station's South/New South Exit (about 1 minute on foot) — well within the target radius of Shinjuku Station. This is a conventional Miyazaki regional-cuisine izakaya (fried chicken nanban, jidori charcoal-grilled chicken, dashi/miso-based hiyajiru) with no evidence anywhere of gluten-free or vegan accommodation; treat gf_confidence/vegan_status as conservative defaults from menu composition, not from a confirmed kitchen policy.
closed_days: No regular weekly closure (open daily); closed around New Year and occasionally follows the KONNE building's own holiday schedule
cuisine: Miyazaki regional specialty cuisine (宮崎県郷土料理) — chicken nanban, jidori charcoal-grilled chicken, hiyajiru cold soup
cultural_comfort_note: Sits inside a prefectural antenna-shop dining hall in the high-traffic Southern Terrace complex next to Shinjuku Station, so it is used to casual and tourist foot traffic, but no English menu or allergy-communication support was confirmed in any source.
gf_detail: Signature dishes (chicken nanban, karaage) are wheat-battered and deep-fried, and the menu is built on traditional Miyazaki miso- and soy-based preparations (hiyajiru cold soup, tare sauces) that are almost certainly made with ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce/miso. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free option was mentioned by any source found — not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person kitchen confirmation.
geocode_note: Address is technically Shibuya-ku Yoyogi 2-2-1, but the venue is inside the Southern Terrace / KONNE building directly adjoining Shinjuku Station's South/New South Exit (~1 min walk per multiple sources), placing it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
name_ja: 宮崎風土 くわんね
neighborhood: 新宿駅南口 (Shinjuku Station South Exit)
seats_text: 36 seats (10 counter, 26 table)
vegan_detail: Menu centers on Miyazaki jidori chicken (grilled and fried), chicken nanban, katsuo (bonito) rice bowls, and hiyajiru cold soup made with niboshi (dried sardines) and miso-based dashi. No vegan dishes, substitutions, or dashi alternatives were mentioned in any source.

bio.background: Opened in 1952 in Shibuya's old Hyakken-dana entertainment alley just off Dogenzaka, this family-run noodle shop has served the same unfussy soy-sauce ramen recipe for more than 70 years, outlasting the neighborhood's transformation from the postwar era into modern Shibuya. The current tile-fronted building dates to 1985, replacing the original wooden storefront.
bio.specialty: Moyashi wantan-men (bean sprout wonton ramen) - plump, generously filled wontons and crisp bean sprouts in a soy-based pork-bone and seafood broth topped with fragrant charred scallions; it reportedly makes up 70-80% of all orders.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen deliberately avoids trends and sticks to a classic soy-based broth, which longtime fans describe as comforting and unchanging - 'like plain rice' - through the decades.

anecdotes.text: Regulars sometimes order just the wontons on their own, without noodles, in a large portion - a sign of how central the filling is to the shop's identity.
The shop has been named among Ramen Walker's best ramen shops in Tokyo and has been featured in television specials about long-loved neighborhood noodle shops.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found for this shop in any source consulted; it is a traditional wheat-noodle ramen shop and should not be presented as GF/vegan-safe. Exact total seat count and precise Tabelog star rating were not independently re-verified beyond search snippets since direct Tabelog page fetch returned 403.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cuisine: ラーメン・町中華（中華そば／醤油ベース豚骨魚介スープ、1952年創業）
cultural_comfort_note: A classic, cash-only Showa-era neighborhood noodle shop with counter seating and no confirmed English menu - approachable with a little Japanese or pointing at the (mostly noodle) menu.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya-ku, Dogenzaka 2-chome, off Hyakken-dana street) places it well within walking distance of Shibuya Scramble crossing; lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions since network geocoding is blocked in this environment.
gf_detail: Standard wheat-flour ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu, contains wheat) based pork-bone and seafood broth; no gluten-free noodle substitution or dedicated gluten-free preparation was mentioned in any source. Gyoza (pan-fried dumplings) also contain wheat wrappers. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:30-20:30, closed Wednesdays
neighborhood: 渋谷・道玄坂（百軒店）／神泉

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing; no wheat-free tamari or gluten-free soy sauce option was found in any source.

seats_text: Ground floor: counter seating only, 7 seats; second floor has additional table seating (exact upstairs count not confirmed).
vegan_detail: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood (gyokai) based, with pork char siu, gyoza, and other meat-based toppings standard across the menu; no vegetarian or vegan menu items or substitutions were found in any source consulted.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-8-9 立川Aビル1F

bio.chef_name: Vel Karunamoorthy (ヴェル・カルナモルティ)
bio.origin: Sri Lanka (born on a tea plantation, associated with the Nuwara Eliya tea-growing region)
bio.background: Ceylon Drop was opened as a Sri Lankan tea specialty shop near Suidobashi/Jimbocho by an owner who grew up on a tea estate in Sri Lanka. The shop imports single-origin, Sri Lanka Tea Board-certified teas through a shortened supply chain directly from auction-participating estates, alongside serving home-style Sri Lankan food through lunch, tea time, and dinner.
bio.specialty: The Sri Lanka Plate — turmeric rice served with a chicken curry and a separate vegetable curry, plus mizuna greens and pol sambol (coconut relish), finished with a complimentary cup of single-origin Ceylon tea and a sweet dessert such as kiri pani.
bio.philosophy: Bring the freshest possible Ceylon tea and authentic Sri Lankan home cooking to Tokyo by sourcing directly from tea estates and auction-linked distributors, minimizing the distance between plantation and cup.

anecdotes.text: 店主はスリランカの茶園生まれで、現地の紅茶文化を紅茶ファンに直接届けることをコンセプトにしている（スリランカ政府紅茶庁認定の紅茶を最小限の流通ルートで仕入れ）。
店長のヴェル・カルナモルティさんがスリランカから直接仕入れした紅茶を販売し、ランチ・ティータイム・ディナーで本格的なスリランカ料理も提供している。

caveats: Hours found via two slightly different secondary sources; the fuller version (Tue-Fri 11:30-22:00, Sat/Sun/Holiday 11:30-20:30, closed Mon) is used here but should be reconfirmed. Tabelog page itself could not be fetched directly (network block); all facts sourced from search-engine snippets of the official site, food blogs, and aggregator listings, not full-page reads. No seat count, reservation policy, or payment-method confirmation was found. No explicit statement exists about gluten-free or vegan practices — the categorization here is inferred cautiously from menu composition (rice + vegetable curry available; wheat-based paratha/roti also on menu; pol sambol traditionally may include dried fish) rather than from any staff/allergy statement, so a diner must ask in person.
closed_days: 月曜日 (Monday)
cuisine: Sri Lankan (tea specialty shop with Sri Lankan curry meals)
cultural_comfort_note: A small independent Japanese-run/Sri Lankan-owner shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service, though the cuisine itself (rice and curry) is easy to point at.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked). Address is 千代田区西神田2-8-9, which multiple independent sources (Tabelog area listing, blogs) describe as a roughly 4-5 minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station, placing it well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi Station target radius.
gf_detail: Sri Lankan curries here are rice-based (turmeric rice) which is naturally gluten-free, but the menu also includes clearly wheat-containing items (paratha, egg roti), and there is no statement anywhere found about a gluten-free menu, dedicated cookware, or cross-contamination controls. Sauces/curries may use soy sauce or other wheat-derived seasonings in ways not disclosed. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should ask staff directly about specific curry ingredients and preparation before ordering.
neighborhood: 千代田区西神田 (Suidōbashi/Jimbōchō area)

positives.text: The standard curry set (Sri Lanka Plate) pairs a chicken curry with a separate vegetable curry on the same plate, giving those avoiding meat a plant-based curry option without a special request.

vegan_detail: A vegetable curry is part of the standard lunch/dinner plate alongside the chicken curry, and dal-style vegetable curries are common in Sri Lankan cooking, but no page found explicitly labels any dish as vegan. Pol sambol (the coconut relish typically served alongside the curries) traditionally can include dried Maldive fish flakes in Sri Lankan cooking, which would not be vegan; whether this shop's version is fish-free was not confirmed in any source. Vegetarians/vegans should ask staff to confirm the vegetable curry and pol sambol are made without fish, shrimp paste, or ghee/butter.

lead: 小料理屋 わるくない
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-11-6 エクレ新宿 3F

bio.background: Opened in 2020, this small counter-only Japanese eatery near Shinjuku Sanchome Station was built around an L-shaped counter in a clean, simply refined space meant to give adults a calm, unhurried spot to enjoy seasonal Japanese cooking and sake.
bio.specialty: Simmered and carefully prepared fish dishes, handmade otsumami (small appetizers), a dashi-rich tamagoyaki (Japanese rolled omelet), and potato salad, alongside seasonal vegetables sourced from contracted farms.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant is built around a comforting, unpretentious washoku (Japanese home-style cooking) concept, pairing seasonal dishes with seasonal Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The name わるくない ('not bad') expresses the owner's hope that every guest leaves the meal feeling it was more than satisfying — genuinely 'not bad at all.'
The chef's carefully simmered fish and handmade appetizers are paired with seasonal vegetables from contracted farms, served at an L-shaped counter alongside a rotating selection of Japanese sake.

caveats: Address (Shinjuku 3-11-6) is a ~2-minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station, which itself sits roughly 0.7-0.8km from Shinjuku Station — borderline but not clearly outside the target radius, so treated as within area. No dedicated vegan or gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source; this is a standard fish-and-dashi-centered small Japanese restaurant, not a diet-specialty spot. Payment method (cash-only vs. cards) and exact seat count were not confirmed by any fetched source — only "L-shaped counter seating" is documented. Hours differ slightly between sources (one lists close at 23:30, another at 24:00 with food L.O. 23:00 / drinks L.O. 23:30); the fuller last-order version is used here.

cultural_comfort_note: A small, local counter-only izakaya-style restaurant with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so some Japanese ability (or a translation app) will help when ordering.

Celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should ask the chef directly about ingredients dish-by-dish before ordering — no dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol was found in any source.

Some seasonal vegetable dishes from contracted farms exist on the menu, but none were documented as prepared vegan (dashi-free).

No confirmation found on whether dashi/bonito-based stock is used elsewhere on the menu; vegan diners should verify specific dishes with staff.

roles: cafe founded by owners with a family background in ryokan/inn hospitality
bio.background: According to the cafe's own concept page, the name and philosophy of SANSOU CAFE trace back to the founders' family history running a mountain inn (ryokan) and hotel business; the cafe carries forward the predecessor's guiding idea of wanting to give guests 'a time to truly relax from the heart.'
bio.specialty: Koso genmai (enzyme-fermented brown rice) served as the centerpiece of the menu, alongside gluten-free sweets and a vegan curry.
bio.philosophy: The cafe describes its cooking as 'kihonshoku' (basic, foundational eating) — using seasonal ingredients and carefully chosen seasonings, minimizing additives, and favoring easy-to-digest preparations built around enzyme brown rice so that both body and mind can settle naturally.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's name and hospitality concept were chosen to carry on a predecessor's belief, from the family's ryokan/hotel background, in creating a genuine space to relax from the heart through food.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Extent of allergy accommodation can vary by situation, so guests with concerns are advised to consult staff directly when visiting.

positives.text: The cafe minimizes food additives and trans fats and builds its menu around easily digestible dishes centered on enzyme brown rice.

caveats: Direct WebFetch to sansoucafe.com, its /concept/ page, Tabelog, and Hotpepper all returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so address/hours/story details were reconstructed from web-search result snippets citing those same domains, cross-checked across at least two independent searches for consistency (address and hours matched exactly across separate queries). Seat count, phone number, payment method (cash vs. card), and precise reservation policy could not be confirmed from any accessible source and are left null/best-guess-conservative. GF/vegan safety specifics (dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce, cross-contact protocol) were not found and are left empty rather than assumed.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-6-1

bio.background: Founded in 1963 (Showa 38) next to what is now Shibuya Mark City, a two-minute walk from Shibuya Station. A casual, long-running yakitori and unagi izakaya now led by a third-generation proprietress referred to locally as the shop's 'kanban okami' (signboard proprietress), who moves between the kitchen and the floor running the business.
bio.specialty: Its signature dish is 'o-gushi yakitori' (large-skewer yakitori): a single oversized skewer stacked in five layers — thigh meat, scallion, shishito pepper, and breast meat — basted in the shop's own sauce and grilled over charcoal.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames itself as preserving 'taishu no aji' — the everyday, unpretentious taste of the common people — keeping portions large and prices accessible rather than chasing refinement.

anecdotes.text: The basting sauce (tare) has never been fully replaced since the restaurant opened in 1963 — it is only topped up. With over 1,000 skewers grilled a day, the shop's own materials estimate that roughly 21.9 million skewers' worth of chicken essence has enriched the sauce over more than 60 years, giving it its deep, savory character.
A feature on the shop describes its five-layer o-gushi yakitori and the continuously-replenished tare as the reason the tavern has comforted generations of Shibuya regulars.

In March 2026 the company opened a takeout stand at Tokyo Dome offering its signature large-skewer yakitori and heritage sauce to baseball spectators, extending the brand beyond its original Shibuya shop.

caveats: The name given in the task brief ("鳥武 本店") does not match the restaurant actually found at the supplied Tabelog URL, which is 鳥竹総本店 (Toritake Sohonten) — almost certainly a transcription mix-up between the kanji 竹 and 武. All facts below were verified for 鳥竹総本店, the real, currently operating restaurant at that Tabelog URL and address, which does match the stated founding year (1963) and location (adjacent to Shibuya Mark City, ~1-2 min walk from Shibuya Station). No gluten-free or vegan accommodation, allergen list, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source; this is a traditional shoyu/tare-based yakitori and unagi specialty shop, not oriented toward GF or vegan diners. Payment method (cash-only status) could not be confirmed from available sources and is a default assumption.
closed_days: None — open every day (無休)
cuisine: Yakitori (charcoal-grilled chicken skewers) and unagi (grilled eel) specialty izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A classic, order-as-you-go Japanese izakaya where menus and service are primarily in Japanese, though it has been drawing more international visitors in recent years.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-6-1, immediately next to Shibuya Mark City and about a 1-2 minute walk from Shibuya Station — well within the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble target radius. Lat/lng left null per the no-geocoding policy; pin to be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: This is a traditional yakitori/unagi shop whose signature dish is basted in a shoyu-based tare that has been continuously topped up (never fully replaced) since 1963 and used across the shared grill and sauce pot for essentially all skewer and unagi orders — wheat-based soy sauce contact is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated prep/fryer setup is advertised in any source found.
neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (next to Shibuya Mark City)
seats_text: Large multi-floor izakaya seating, roughly 120 seats.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on charcoal-grilled chicken skewers and unagi (eel) glazed in a shared savory sauce; there is no vegetarian or vegan menu, and the sauce and broths are meat/fish-derived. Not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: スパイスカリーはちわん
address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-5-2 Tasビル 1F

bio.background: Hachiwan is a spice-curry specialty shop whose Jimbocho branch opened on November 18, 2025, roughly midway between Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations (about a 4-minute walk from Jimbocho Station's Exit A2). Coverage of the opening describes it as produced by a chef with a background at a members-only yakiniku restaurant in Nishi-Azabu, who set out to build a genuinely wheat-free curry using Japanese pantry staples instead of a flour-based roux. It is the second Hachiwan location, alongside a sister shop at Toyosu Senkyakubanrai.
bio.specialty: A rotating lineup of three curries a day (single or two-curry combination), built on Japanese dashi rather than a wheat-flour roux — for example a mackerel-and-bonito-dashi coconut curry — served with a self-serve buffet of seasonal side dishes.
bio.philosophy: No wheat flour in the curry base: the shop leans on vegetables, spices, and Japanese dashi/miso to build depth and umami, aiming for what it calls '本格' (authentic, proper) curry that happens to be gluten-free rather than a diet product.
roles: Second location of the Hachiwan brand (sister shop at Toyosu Senkyakubanrai)

anecdotes.text: A diner posted about trying the mackerel-and-bonito-dashi coconut curry with the free side-dish buffet, calling it a clear hit and predicting the shop would become popular.
A food blogger's write-up on a Jimbocho-area curry crawl centers on the generous self-serve side-dish buffet as the highlight of a visit to this branch.

caveats: Direct fetches of the primary sources (Tabelog, official site, note.com, Retty, Yahoo Map, AutoReserve) all returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so this record is compiled from web-search result snippets/excerpts of those pages rather than full page reads. Closed-day details conflict slightly across sources (Saturday/Sunday/holidays vs. Sunday-only) and should be double-checked. Seat count, dedicated-fryer status, soy sauce composition, and whether a dashi-free vegan curry option exists on any given day are not documented in the sources found — worth confirming directly before publishing as celiac-safe.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays (one source instead lists Sunday only as closed — unconfirmed which is current)
cuisine: Gluten-free Japanese-style spice curry with self-serve side-dish (副菜) buffet
cultural_comfort_note: A small lunch-only curry counter aimed at the local Jimbocho office crowd, with no confirmed English menu, English signage, or online reservation in English — expect Japanese-only ordering.
gf_detail: The shop's central concept, repeated across its own site and food-media coverage, is curry made without wheat flour — using vegetable and spice umami plus Japanese dashi (mackerel, bonito) and miso instead of a flour-thickened roux, which directly addresses the main wheat source in most Japanese curry. However, whether the soy sauce used contains wheat, whether any fried items share oil/a fryer with wheat-containing foods, and general kitchen cross-contamination protocols are not documented in the sources available — celiac diners should confirm these specifics with staff before ordering, especially regarding the rotating side-dish buffet, whose ingredients change daily and are not itemized online.

bio.origin: Shinjuku Sanchome, Tokyo — opened its first shop on February 4, 1975
bio.specialty: Kushi-age (deep-fried skewers) fried one at a time to order at an open counter, using an original fine-panko coating and a secret in-house dipping sauce; roughly 40 vegetable varieties (asparagus, garlic, etc.) among about 150 total ingredients offered.
bio.philosophy: An all-you-can-eat 'until you say stop' course format built around a teppan-style open counter, where the cook fries and serves each skewer individually in front of the guest rather than batch-frying.
bio.background: Founded in 1975 as a single Shinjuku Sanchome counter shop, Tatsukichi grew into a small local kushi-age specialty group (Shinjuku honten, Akihabara, Hibiya, and — opened for its 2025 50th anniversary — the 'Happourou' branch back at its original Shinjuku Sanchome site) while keeping its original recipes. In April 2024 the operating company was acquired as a subsidiary of restaurant group TRIPLETS Inc.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant opened its first location on February 4, 1975 (Showa 50) and marked its 50th anniversary in 2025 by opening a new 'Happourou' branch back at the original Shinjuku Sanchome site.
Kushi-age is fried and served skewer by skewer right at the counter in front of guests, continuing until the customer says 'stop.'
In April 2024, TRIPLETS Inc. made the company operating Shinjuku Tatsukichi's three kushi-age shops in Tokyo a subsidiary.

caveats: Tabelog, official site, and several aggregator pages (Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, TableCheck) all returned HTTP 403 to direct page fetch in this environment, so details below are drawn from search-engine result snippets of those same pages rather than full page fetches — treat address/hours/phone as reasonably solid (corroborated across multiple independent snippets) but treat allergy-handling and vegan/GF specifics as lower-confidence since no page explicitly discusses celiac or vegan accommodation for this restaurant. No dedicated GF or vegan menu was found; this is a traditional deep-fried-skewer shop with breaded (wheat panko) coating shared across a single fryer for all ingredient types, so it is not celiac-safe and only cautiously vegan-workable.

The composition of the restaurant's secret house dipping sauce is not published and likely contains wheat-based soy sauce or Worcestershire-style sauce. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushi-age skewers — meat, seafood, and vegetable alike — are coated with the restaurant's original panko breadcrumb and fried in the same oil at the open counter, so there is no separate gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable skewers are fried in the same shared oil, at the same counter, as the meat and seafood skewers.

positives.text: Roughly 40 of the restaurant's approximately 150 skewer ingredients are vegetables (e.g. asparagus, garlic), a wider vegetable selection than at most kushi-age shops.

seats_text: Counter seating only (all-counter layout); exact seat count not published in available sources.
vegan_detail: About 40 of the roughly 150 skewer ingredients are vegetables (asparagus, garlic, etc.), giving vegans real menu choices, but they are breaded and fried in the same shared oil as meat and seafood skewers, and the ingredients of the house dipping sauce are not published, so animal-derived seasoning (e.g. dashi/bonito) and oil cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Best to tell staff about dietary restrictions when seated and ask specifically which skewers and the sauce are free of animal products.

cuisine: 焼き鳥・鶏料理（希少部位おまかせ／穴場名店）
neighborhood: 道玄坂／渋谷
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-6-8 2F
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked). Address corroborated across multiple independent sources (official site listing, Rakuten Gnavi, Ikyu) as 道玄坂2-6-8; restaurant is repeatedly described as roughly a 4-5 minute walk from Shibuya Station, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble (35.6595,139.7005). One secondary source listed the floor as "B1・2F" rather than just "2F" — treat exact floor as unconfirmed but the location itself is well corroborated.
seats_text: Small, intimate 10-seat counter on the second floor, away from the street noise of central Shibuya
closed_days: 日曜日 (Sundays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small reservation-only omakase counter with no fixed menu and no confirmed English support — a little Japanese (or a hotel/concierge booking) will make the visit much smoother.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-omakase-only counter: there is no printed menu, the course is built entirely around rare cuts of chicken (breast, thigh, drumstick, knee meat, etc.) served with fruit-based sauces (e.g. green apple, mango), and it traditionally closes with a minced-meat rice dish (soboro gohan). No vegetarian or vegan course, substitution policy, or plant-based option was found in any source, and the omakase-only, chef's-choice format leaves little room for a meaningful vegan meal here.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated-equipment information was found for this restaurant in any source checked. It is a traditional yakitori counter serving an omakase course of skewers and sauces that in Japanese yakitori commonly include wheat-based soy sauce (tare) alongside salt (shio) preparations, but this restaurant's specific sauce composition (including the fruit-based glazes reviewers mention) was not documented in gluten terms. Because the course is fixed and chef-directed with no printed menu, a celiac diner would need to contact the restaurant directly in advance to discuss ingredients and cross-contact — this cannot be confirmed as safe from available sources.

bio.background: According to multiple independent reviews, the owner-chef of Daraku has an unusual background outside the yakitori trade — he previously worked designing accessories before opening this counter in Shibuya.
bio.specialty: An omakase-only course built around rare, hard-to-find cuts of chicken (e.g. knee meat, drumstick) sourced without loyalty to a single chicken brand, selecting whichever region's bird best suits a given part; skewers are paired with unusual sauces such as green apple and mango, and the course traditionally closes with a soboro (minced chicken) rice dish.
bio.philosophy: There is no printed menu — the chef serves whatever cut is best that day in a chef's-choice sequence until the guest says stop, and he pairs the meal with sake and wine chosen purely for how well they suit the food rather than by brand name.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe Daraku as a hideaway in the middle of Shibuya where a rare chicken cut you can't get elsewhere is always on offer, served from a quiet, second-floor counter away from the crowds.

The owner-chef, who has an unusual prior career designing accessories, chooses chicken by part rather than by brand, seeking out whichever producer's bird is best suited to each specific cut, and pairs the food with carefully chosen sake and wine rather than famous-name bottles.

caveats: This is fundamentally a chicken-focused yakitori omakase counter, not a GF- or vegan-oriented restaurant — it is included here as a notable, hard-to-find independent (mom-and-pop) spot rather than for dietary accommodation. No source consulted (the official site returned a 403 block; search snippets and several aggregator/blog pages were used instead) documented any gluten-free or vegan policy, so gf_confidence and vegan_status reflect an absence of evidence rather than a confirmed negative. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should contact the restaurant directly before booking. Floor listed as 2F per the most direct-looking source, though one aggregator listed 'B1・2F' — worth reconfirming at booking.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-36-6 大安ビル2F

roles: Owner: Yasuda Shin'ichi (安田眞一), a certified senior sommelier
bio.background: Opened in 2001 just outside Shinjuku Station's east/central-east exit as a hand-made soba specialty restaurant. The shop mills its own domestic (Hokkaido) buckwheat in-house with a stone mill, then hand-kneads, hand-rolls and hand-cuts the noodles fresh each day, keeping the wheat-flour binder to roughly 20% to preserve the buckwheat's natural aroma and flavor.
bio.philosophy: Minimal binder, all-manual noodle-making (hand-kneaded/hand-rolled/hand-cut daily) to let the stone-milled buckwheat's own flavor come through, paired with seasonal Japanese small dishes and a sommelier-selected list of wine and sake.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut stone-milled soba (seiro / inaka seiro / tenpura seiro), served alongside dishes such as duck confit and binchotan-charcoal-grilled jidori chicken.

anecdotes.text: Just inside the entrance on the right you can watch the buckwheat being stone-milled in-house, with the soba-making station further back where the noodles are hand-kneaded, hand-rolled and hand-cut every day.
Positioned as a spot for relaxed dinners or business entertaining just steps from Shinjuku Station's southeast exit, known for dishes like Omi duck confit alongside its soba.

caveats: Primary source pages (Tabelog, official site, gourmet listings) returned 403/could not be directly fetched in this environment (network policy blocks these hosts), so details are drawn from search-engine result summaries of those pages rather than raw page text — treat exact hours and seat counts as approximate and confirm by phone (03-3352-5113) before visiting. This is a traditional hand-cut soba specialty restaurant, not a dedicated gluten-free or vegan establishment: the noodles use a ~20% wheat-flour binder (not 100% buckwheat/juwari) and the kitchen features duck, chicken and traditional bonito-based dashi, so it is not celiac-safe and not vegan. Payment method acceptance (cards vs cash-only) was not independently confirmed.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open year-round) except New Year's and the summer Obon period; Monday lunch service is closed (dinner only on Mondays).
cuisine: 蕎麦 (soba - hand-cut buckwheat noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: An upscale, Japanese-oriented soba-and-sake restaurant with counter, table and tatami-room seating and online booking, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking service — comfortable for visitors with some Japanese or a translation app, less so for zero-Japanese diners with dietary questions.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address 東京都新宿区新宿3-36-6 大安ビル2F is confirmed by multiple independent listings as a 1-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's east/central-east exit, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius (35.6896,139.7006).
gf_detail: The soba here is nihachi-style: stone-milled domestic buckwheat hand-kneaded with roughly 20% wheat flour as binder — not 100% buckwheat (juwari), so the noodles themselves contain gluten. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) and the tempura batter used for tenpura seiro are standard preparations that typically rely on wheat-based ingredients. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe accommodations were found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 平日11:30-14:00(L.O.)・17:00-22:00(L.O.)、土曜12:00-22:00(L.O.)、日祝~21:30(L.O.)、月曜は17:00-22:00(L.O.)のみ（ランチ休み）。年中無休（年始・夏期旧盆を除く）。※情報源により細部の表記に差異あり、来店前に要確認。
neighborhood: 新宿東口 (Shinjuku East Exit)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are made with a stone-milled buckwheat dough that includes roughly 20% wheat flour as a binder, so all standard soba served here contains gluten — there is no all-buckwheat (juwari) or gluten-free noodle option identified.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional soba specialty shop, the tsuyu dipping sauce follows standard shoyu-based preparation; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free tsuyu alternative was mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu includes duck confit and charcoal-grilled chicken, and traditional soba tsuyu is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi; no vegan menu, plant-based dashi, or vegan labeling was found.

seats_text: Roughly 88 seats across counter, table and tatami-room seating (approx. 40 counter / 26 table / 22 tatami per listings) — figure not independently verified via a directly fetched primary source.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba specialty restaurant, not a vegan or vegetarian-focused establishment. The menu features duck confit, binchotan-charcoal-grilled chicken, tamagoyaki (egg), and a sommelier-curated wine/sake list; traditional soba tsuyu is conventionally made with bonito (katsuo) dashi. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan labeling was found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1-15-3

bio.origin: Opened in Asakusa in 2025 by Sumapura, a company based in Kimitsu City, Chiba Prefecture that runs a multi-floor "Japanese culture experience" venue.
bio.background: The mochi shop occupies the ground floor of a multi-story building in the heart of Asakusa devoted to hands-on Japanese culture: mochi pounding on the 1st floor, a spot to eat mochi with matcha while viewing art on the 2nd floor, and calligraphy, tea ceremony and a sake bar on the floors above.
bio.specialty: "Living mochi" (生きてる餅) — rice pounded fresh daily in a traditional wooden mortar and mallet, served plain or with a choice of up to about ten toppings such as kinako (roasted soybean flour), sweet red bean paste, kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), matcha, grated daikon, and creative options like caramel nuts.
bio.philosophy: Built around letting visitors experience mochi-making as a living craft rather than just buying a packaged sweet — a hands-on mochi-pounding experience (about 1,000 yen per person, includes kinako mochi) is offered alongside the shop counter.
roles: mochi shop / wagashi counter within a Japanese-culture experience venue

anecdotes.text: A reviewer who visited on April 5, 2026 described enjoying freshly pounded mochi served in zenzai (sweet red bean soup).
A blogger described it as a "living mochi" specialty shop where visitors can also try mochi-pounding themselves.

caveats: Network restrictions in this environment blocked direct fetches of tabelog.com and the official hanbei.ltd pages (403 at the network gateway), so details below are drawn from search-engine summaries of those pages plus secondary sources (Asakusa local news site, blog reviews), not from directly rendered primary-source HTML. Seating and cash-only policy for the mochi counter specifically are not confirmed. Vegan status is inferred from listed ingredients (rice, soybean flour, red bean, sugar) rather than an explicit vegan claim from the operator — confirm no animal-derived ingredients (e.g., gelatin, lard in anko) in person. The shop can sell out and close before the posted closing time.
closed_days: Wednesday (水曜日)
cuisine: Mochi, traditional Japanese sweets (wagashi)
cultural_comfort_note: The shop has a full English-language website and English-language Tabelog listing, so English-speaking visitors can look up menu and toppings in advance even without staff English ability confirmed.
gf_detail: The mochi itself is not gluten-free: according to the shop's own product description, a small amount of premium Japanese wheat flour is mixed in to help the mochi keep its soft texture until the next day. This makes the core product unsuitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners; no dedicated gluten-free line, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocol was found.
hours_raw: 9:00〜17:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：水曜日 — "9:00-17:00, closes early once sold out; closed Wednesdays"
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa), Taito City — near Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station and Sensō-ji

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information found on cross-contamination handling; the shop is not positioned as gluten-free and mixes wheat flour directly into the mochi dough.

seats_text: Primarily a takeout/experience mochi counter on the 1st floor; a related tea room area on an upper floor of the same building reportedly seats around 20, but seating specific to the mochi shop itself is not confirmed.
vegan_detail: No explicit vegan claim was found from the operator. The core ingredients described (mochi rice, small amount of wheat flour, kinako/roasted soybean flour, red bean paste, kuromitsu black sugar syrup, matcha, grated daikon) are plant-based on their face, but red bean paste (anko) can sometimes include non-vegan additives depending on preparation, and this has not been confirmed either way. Treat as unconfirmed rather than verified vegan.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-22-13 東マイアミビル1F

bio.background: A small stand-style Turkish kebab counter just steps from Shibuya Hikarie, near Shibuya's east exit / Miyamasuzaka. It keeps long, no-day-off hours (8am to 11pm) and runs mostly as a grab-and-go takeout spot for the after-work and late-night crowd.
bio.specialty: ケバブ丼 (kebab rice bowl) — grilled chicken and/or beef kebab served over rice with cabbage, tomato, and a spiced mayo-ketchup sauce

caveats: Small independent takeout kebab stand, not a vegan/GF specialty spot. No vegan protein option exists (chicken/beef/mix only), and gluten-free safety is unconfirmed — the rice-bowl option skips the pita bread but is prepared at the same small counter as the bread and wrap items, with no stated allergen protocol. Treat as a backup/novelty option for GF or vegan diners rather than a primary recommendation, and ask staff directly about ingredients if you have celiac disease.
closed_days: Open year-round (無休) except the New Year holiday period, roughly Dec 27 – Jan 5
cuisine: トルコ料理・ケバブ (Turkish / kebab)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is simple — point at a photo menu and pick your meat (chicken/beef/mix) and sauce (mild/hot/yogurt/teriyaki) — but this is a small local stand where staff may not speak much English, so a few basic Japanese words help.
geocode_note: Address (東京都渋谷区渋谷2-22-13 東マイアミビル1F) places the shop immediately beside Shibuya Hikarie, roughly 500-600m from Shibuya Scramble crossing — well within the 0.8km Shibuya target radius. lat/lng left null per instructions; geocoding is derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: Menu is pita sandwich, tortilla wrap, or ケバブ丼 (kebab-over-rice bowl) — all ¥600. The rice bowl skips the wheat pita/tortilla, but no source confirms a wheat-free sauce recipe, dedicated prep surface, or fryer at this small stand, so cross-contact with the bread-based items is likely. Not verified safe for celiac disease — ask staff directly.
neighborhood: 渋谷 渋谷（ヒカリエ前）
seats_text: Small stand-style shop with counter service near Shibuya Hikarie; primarily a takeout/street-food operation with little to no formal seating found in sources.
vegan_detail: Meat options are limited to chicken, beef, or a mix — no tofu, seitan, or vegetable-only kebab was found in the menu, reviews, or shop listings, so there is no vegan protein option here.

lead: 肉丸酒場 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-19-7 大庄ビル3F

bio.background: A private-room izakaya near Suidobashi Station built around Brazilian-style churrasco (charcoal-grilled skewered meat) crossed with Japanese meat-sushi (肉寿司) small plates, aimed at group parties and company banquets rather than solo dining.
bio.specialty: Seared meat sushi (炙り肉寿司), roast beef, and charcoal-grilled churrasco skewers, often served as part of all-you-can-eat/all-you-can-drink course menus.

caveats: This is a meat-centric Brazilian churrasco/izakaya concept, not a vegan or gluten-free specialist. No restaurant-sourced statement on allergen handling, dedicated fryers, or a wheat-free soy sauce alternative was found in any of the sources checked (Tabelog, official site link, Hot Pepper, Yahoo Loco, gorp.jp aggregator, general web search) — several of those pages returned 403 to direct fetch, so details here are drawn from search-result summaries citing those pages rather than a full page read. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should call ahead (03-6272-5268) before booking.
closed_days: Monday (exceptionally open in March, April, December, and during Tokyo Dome event days)
cuisine: Churrasco & Meat bar - Brazilian-style grilled meat
cultural_comfort_note: The Tabelog listing has an English reservation page, but the venue's own site, menu, and in-restaurant service appear to be Japanese-only, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null); address confirms the venue is about a 1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, well within the 0.8km Suidobashi radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer statement was found. As a churrasco/izakaya serving meat sushi (vinegared rice, likely soy-based tare/sauces) and grilled skewers, wheat exposure via soy sauce and shared marinades/grills should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise; not suitable for celiac-level strictness without calling ahead.
hours_raw: 火～日 16:00～24:00（料理L.O. 23:30、ドリンクL.O. 23:30）／月曜定休（3月・4月・12月、東京ドームイベント開催時は営業）
name_ja: 肉丸酒場 水道橋店
neighborhood: 千代田区水道橋（神田三崎町）
seats_text: 84 seats total, all in private rooms; private buyout available for 50-90 guests, sofa seating also available
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based substitution options were found. The restaurant's identity is entirely meat-focused (churrasco skewers, meat sushi, roast beef, often as all-you-can-eat courses), so it is not a realistic option for vegan diners.

lead: 萬馬軒 新宿西口本店
address_ja: 〒160-0023 東京都新宿区西新宿7-1-3 加藤ビル 1F

roles: founder: Eiji Hori (1988, original Meguro shop)
current operator: Garden Group Co., Ltd. (since Nov 2025 acquisition)
bio.background: Manbaken is a Tokyo miso-ramen specialist tracing back to 1988, when it was founded in Meguro by Eiji Hori. Over more than three decades the brand built a following for its rich pork-and-chicken broth blended with a secret miso tare. In autumn 2025 the then four-store chain was acquired by restaurant operator Garden Group, which set out to preserve the original recipe while professionalizing operations and growing the brand. The Nishi-Shinjuku Honten location, opened February 13, 2026 a few minutes from Shinjuku Station's west exit, was launched as the brand's flagship 'next chapter' store — its fifth location overall and third in the Shinjuku area.
bio.origin: Meguro, Tokyo (1988)
bio.specialty: 濃厚味噌らーめん (rich miso ramen) — pork-bone and chicken broth emulsified with a secret blended miso tare, served with thick domestic wheat noodles (from Mikawaya Seimen). The signature bowl is the Negi Miso Chashu Men, topped with thick-cut braised chashu pork.
bio.philosophy: Under its current operator, the stated aim is to carry forward more than 30 years of miso-ramen recipe and technique while improving store operations, with a mid-term goal of becoming the leading station-front miso-ramen brand across greater Tokyo.

anecdotes.text: On its February 13, 2026 opening day, the Nishi-Shinjuku Honten offered its miso ramen at a special opening price of 600 yen (tax included).

The store was billed by its operator as the flagship marking 'the next chapter of the Manbaken brand' — the brand's fifth location and the third in the Shinjuku area, positioned to catch business, tourist, and inbound demand near Shinjuku Station.
Manbaken was acquired by Garden Group (TSE: 274A) in a cash deal of about 200 million yen, taking over operation of the brand's four existing directly-run stores from November 1, 2025.

caveats: Network policy blocked direct WebFetch of tabelog.com, manbaken.jp, and all blog/press sources during this verification (proxy returned 403 "policy denial" on every external host tried), so all facts below come from WebSearch result snippets/summaries rather than direct page fetches — treat with the corresponding caution. IMPORTANT NAME/URL MISMATCH: the Tabelog URL supplied in the brief (.../13291666/) actually resolves to a sister location named "萬馬軒 新宿西口店" (opened Nov 2023), NOT "新宿西口本店" as targeted. There is a separate, distinctly-numbered Tabelog listing for "萬馬軒 新宿西口本店" (ID 13317757, opened 2026-02-13) at the same building (西新宿7-1-3 加藤ビル) which matches the target name exactly; this record documents that store (13317757) and reports its corrected tabelog_url rather than the one given in the brief. Also note: this is a corporately-run chain (acquired by Garden Group, JASDAQ 274A, in 2025) expanding toward 30 locations — not a small independent operation — and no gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found in any source; it is a standard pork/chicken-bone miso ramen shop.
closed_days: No regular closed day (open daily); official notice that irregular/temporary closures may occur
cuisine: 味噌ラーメン (Miso Ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: A short walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit in a high-traffic tourist/business corridor its operator explicitly targeted for inbound visitors, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found.

Geocoding was not performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream derivation from address_ja.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles (thick domestic wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen) and a soy-sauce-based secret miso tare; no gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF preparation area, or allergen-friendly menu was mentioned in any source found. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free/celiac diet.
