name_ja: 萬馬軒 新宿西口本店
neighborhood: Shinjuku Nishiguchi (Nishi-Shinjuku), Shinjuku Station area

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The miso tare/soup base is described as combining pork-bone and chicken stock with a secret soy-sauce blend, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned.

seats_text: 20 seats total (8 counter seats, 12 table seats across three 4-person tables)
vegan_detail: Broth is built on pork bone and chicken stock, and the signature bowls are topped with thick-cut chashu (braised pork); no plant-based broth, vegetarian, or vegan menu option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners as currently documented.

lead: ひょうたんなべ 雷門店
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1丁目2-9（〒111-0032）

bio.background: A homemade-oden izakaya tucked 30 seconds' walk from Asakusa's Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate) — one of the neighborhood's most recognizable landmarks. The kitchen builds its oden broth from Rishiri kelp, bonito, and chicken, simmering more than 30 kinds of ingredients including handmade tsumire (fish balls) and satsuma-age (fried fish cake) alongside oden staples like daikon, potato, egg, and hanpen.
bio.specialty: Tomato-cheese oden — a dashi-soaked tomato topped with melted cheese for a milky twist on the classic hotpot — served alongside sashimi, ankimo (monkfish liver), and tempura.
bio.philosophy: Built around a slow-simmered, kombu-and-bonito dashi and a large rotating lineup of oden ingredients, paired with a curated selection of Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits just 30 seconds on foot from Kaminarimon, the iconic gate at the entrance to Asakusa's Sensoji temple complex, making it a popular stop for visitors exploring the area.
Its oden broth is described as kombu- and bonito/chicken-based with over 30 types of ingredients, including handmade tsumire and satsuma-age, plus a signature tomato-cheese oden.

caveats: Direct WebFetch to hyotannabe.com, Tabelog, and other listing pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment; details below are drawn from search-engine summaries of those same pages (official site, Tabelog EN, Ekiten, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Gorp) cross-checked for consistency across sources. Saturday opening time shows minor variance across sources (13:00 vs 14:00) — 14:00 was the more frequently cited figure and is used here. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation, allergy menu, or dedicated-fryer statement was found on any source; the oden broth is bonito/chicken-and-kombu based (not vegan) and traditional shoyu-based seasoning is standard for this style of oden (likely contains wheat), but this was not explicitly confirmed on any fetched source.
closed_days: None found — open every day, year-round (no fixed closed day identified across sources).
cuisine: Oden izakaya (homemade dashi-based Japanese hotpot / tavern)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood oden izakaya aimed primarily at Japanese-speaking guests; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found beyond automated translation on the Tabelog English listing.
geocode_note: Lat/lng intentionally left null (no network geocoding in this environment). Address 東京都台東区浅草1丁目2-9 is corroborated across multiple listings as approximately 30 seconds on foot from Kaminarimon Gate, placing it well within the 0.8km radius of the Sensoji/Kaminarimon target-area landmark (35.7148, 139.7967).
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found on the official site, Tabelog, or other listings. The oden broth and general Japanese-izakaya seasoning conventions typically rely on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and the menu also includes tempura, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high; celiac diners should not treat this as a safe option without asking staff directly, and even then confirmation may be difficult given no confirmed English support.
name_ja: ひょうたんなべ 雷門店
neighborhood: Asakusa / Kaminarimon (浅草雷門)
seats_text: Approximately 35 seats across counter, tatami floor seating, and horigotatsu (sunken-floor) tables; no private rooms; fully non-smoking indoors.
vegan_detail: The signature dashi is built on bonito (katsuobushi) and chicken stock, and most oden ingredients are fish-based (tsumire, satsuma-age, hanpen) or meat-based. No vegan menu, plant-based dashi option, or vegan labeling was found on any source, so this is not a viable option for vegan diners as currently documented.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-25-10 ベニー清建ビル1F

bio.origin: Turkey
bio.background: A small Turkish kebab shop on Dogenzaka run by a Turkish owner-chef, cooking in the casual style of a Turkish neighborhood lokanta (corner eatery) rather than a formal restaurant menu.
bio.specialty: Kebab plates and kebab sandwiches (chicken, lamb, beef), Iskender kebab, and baklava
bio.philosophy: Bring the everyday flavors of a Turkish street-corner lokanta to Shibuya, with a bilingual menu so both locals and visitors can order comfortably.

anecdotes.text: Kebab varieties are created by a Turkish chef with the taste of a Turkish street-corner cafeteria (lokanta), and the shop uses a different type of bread for its kebabs than other kebab spots in Tokyo.
The restaurant is run by a sole owner-chef described as extremely friendly with great service; because the shop is narrow, only the rear seats can accommodate groups larger than four.

caveats: Seat count, exact gluten-free safety practices (cross-contact, fryer sharing), and the full vegan status of individual sauces/dips could not be confirmed from available sources — pages for the official site, Tabelog, and several review aggregators returned access errors, so this record relies on search-result snippets rather than full page fetches. Confirm dietary details by phone or in person before visiting.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cuisine: トルコ料理・ケバブ (Turkish cuisine / kebab)
cultural_comfort_note: The menu is bilingual in English and Japanese and staff can communicate in English, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to order and ask questions.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification was found. Kebab plates (grilled meat, rice, salad) could work for a gluten-free diet if the pita/flatbread wrap is skipped, but marinades, sauces, and whether the grill/prep area avoids cross-contact with bread have not been confirmed. Ask staff directly (English is spoken) before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirms the shop is on Dogenzaka roughly a 3-minute walk from Shibuya Station, well within the Shibuya Scramble 0.8km radius.
neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂

positives.text: The menu has both English and Japanese, and the staff can speak English, so ordering and asking about ingredients is not a problem.
The restaurant is certified halal and also lists vegetarian dishes such as salads and dips alongside its kebab-focused menu.

seats_text: Small, narrow shop with counter and a few tables; seats toward the rear can fit groups larger than four, other seating is for two — exact seat count not published.
vegan_detail: Vegetarian dishes (salads and dips) are offered alongside the halal kebab menu, but Turkish dips/sauces often contain yogurt, and no source confirmed which specific dishes are fully vegan (dairy- and honey-free). Ask staff before ordering if strict vegan avoidance is required.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町一丁目4-6 愛光舎ビル1階

bio.background: BRISK STAND is a Japanese gourmet-burger brand built around its original "kitta yatsu" (the cut one) burger; after winning the Gourmet Burger Japan Championship 2025 it expanded rapidly nationwide. The Suidobashi shop (BRISKSTAND SUIDOBASHI, subtitled "Burger & Bar") opened July 26, 2025, a 1-2 minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station near Tokyo Dome, and was reported as roughly the brand's 14th store.
bio.origin: Japan (nationwide chain, Tokyo branch)
bio.philosophy: The brand's catchphrase is "和食にかぶりつけ" ("bite into Japanese cuisine") — burgers built with a Japanese culinary sensibility, using whole-grain-flour buns and domestic beef, in a wood-toned "wa-modern" interior.
bio.specialty: "切ったやつ" (Kitta Yatsu, "the cut one") — a 100% Japanese-beef patty burger that is cut and double-seared on a hot griddle so the meat's juices soak into the cut surfaces of the bun and patty, finished with house-made sweet stewed onions.

anecdotes.text: BRISK STAND won first place in the JAPAN BURGER CHAMPIONSHIP 2025 gourmet burger competition before opening this Suidobashi location.
The Suidobashi shop opened as roughly the 14th BRISK STAND location, styled as a 'wa-modern' space blending the calm of a traditional restaurant with the casualness of a burger stand.

caveats: Gluten-free and vegan/vegetarian accommodations are not published anywhere found: buns are made from whole-grain wheat flour and every headline burger uses a 100% Japanese-beef patty, so this spot is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners based on available sourcing. Some address/hours details were cross-confirmed via search-result snippets (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, PR Times) rather than a directly fetched page, since several source pages returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; no fixed weekly closed day, may close early if sold out)
cuisine: ハンバーガー・グルメバーガー (gourmet burger)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern Western-format burger-and-bar concept with an English brand name and picture-friendly menu, so ordering is straightforward even with minimal Japanese, though no confirmed English-language menu was found.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null); address corroborated across Tabelog, Hot Pepper, and the brand's opening press release, all placing the shop about 1-2 minutes' walk from JR Suidobashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free bun or dedicated wheat-free option is documented. The signature buns are described as made from whole-grain (unbleached) wheat flour, and no allergen/gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination information was found in any sourced material. Treat as not celiac-safe.
name_ja: ブリスクスタンド水道橋（BRISKSTAND SUIDOBASHI）
neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Kanda Misakicho), Suidobashi, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Counter seating for about 20; no private rooms.
vegan_detail: No vegetarian or plant-based patty/burger option was found; every burger described uses a 100% Japanese-beef patty. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available sourcing.

lead: 俺の割烹 炉ばた 新宿
address_ja: 東京都新宿区歌舞伎町1-6-3 石塚ビル6F（〒160-0021）

roles: Robatayaki izakaya operated by 俺の株式会社 (Ore no Co., Ltd.)
bio.background: Occupies the Ishizuka Building space in Kabukicho that was previously home to the game-meat izakaya 炉とマタギ (Ro to Matagi). That shop closed for renovation in spring 2025, and the space reopened on June 25, 2025 as 俺の炉ばた under the 俺の ("Ore no") restaurant group. On January 14, 2026 it was folded into the group's 俺の割烹 (Ore no Kappo) line and renamed 俺の割烹 炉ばた 新宿, its current name.
bio.origin: Part of 俺の株式会社, a Tokyo restaurant group whose model is built around staffing kitchens with chefs trained in high-end/Michelin-caliber settings and selling their cooking at a fraction of typical fine-dining prices.
bio.specialty: Charcoal robatayaki of game meat (jibier) — Ezo venison, wild boar, duck and lamb, including a four-game-meat platter (肉四種盛り合わせ) — cooked live at two grill stations, an open-hearth 原始焼き and a mesh-grill 網焼き, for a smoky, tableside-theater style of service.
bio.philosophy: The 俺の group's stated approach is to put skilled cooks on ingredient-forward menus with a very high food-cost ratio (reportedly over 60%), covering the economics through fast table turnover rather than high per-dish margins.

anecdotes.text: The location reopened in the Ishizuka Building in Kabukicho on June 25, 2025 as "Ore no Robata Shinjuku" after previously operating as 炉とマタギ.

The shop is grouped under 俺の株式会社's 俺の炉ばた concept, described as the group's first Japanese-style (和) concept in 11 years, combining a primitive open-hearth grill and a mesh grill so guests can watch high-grade seafood and game meat cook.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, the official Ore no site, HotPepper, Yahoo Map, Navitime, and Jalan pages returned HTTP 403 (blocked by session egress policy), so facts below were gathered via web-search snippets that quote/summarize those same pages rather than a raw page fetch. Details converge consistently across Tabelog, the official Oreno.co.jp page, Yahoo Map, Navitime, and Jalan, giving reasonable confidence, but no gluten-free/vegan-specific statement from the restaurant itself was found in any source — the GF/vegan ratings below are inferred from the cuisine type (meat- and dashi-centric robatayaki), not from an explicit allergen policy, so they should be treated as cautious defaults pending direct confirmation with the restaurant.
closed_days: None found — reported as open every day (定休日なし)
cuisine: Robatayaki (炉ばた焼き) — charcoal-grilled game meat (jibier) and seafood izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: Has an English-language Tabelog listing and online table booking, and sits in a heavily tourist-trafficked part of Kabukicho, but no English menu or bilingual-staff claim was found, so a few basic Japanese food/allergy words will help.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer information, or wheat-free statement was found in any source. As a robatayaki izakaya, tare/glaze sauces, ponzu, and dipping sauces typically used on grilled skewers and hotpot items are usually soy-sauce (wheat) based, and shared grill surfaces are likely used across meat, seafood, and any breaded items. Celiac diners should ask staff in detail about individual dish sauces before ordering.
name_ja: 俺の割烹 炉ばた 新宿
neighborhood: 歌舞伎町 Kabukicho, Shinjuku
seats_text: Mix of semi-private rooms (wood/lattice-screened), fully private rooms, table, box, and counter seating; exact seat count not published in available sources.
vegan_detail: Menu is centered on grilled game meat and seafood; no vegan menu, dashi ingredients, or plant-based accommodation statement was found. Robatayaki restaurants of this type often grill some vegetables, but that has not been confirmed here, and traditional broths/hotpot bases in this style of cooking commonly use bonito (katsuobushi) or other animal-based dashi. Vegan diners should ask in detail before visiting.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-35-9 早苗マンション1F

bio.origin: Asakusa, Tokyo — in business roughly 46 years as of sources found
bio.specialty: Tori kamameshi (chicken clay-pot rice, one of 11 kamameshi varieties on the menu), yakitori, and kamo nabe (duck hot pot) with a housemade ponzu and katsuobushi-based broth
bio.philosophy: A nostalgic, old-Tokyo neighborhood eatery in the backstreets behind Sensoji (the 'Kannon-ura' district) where regulars return as much to see the owner and his wife as for the food.

anecdotes.text: 鳥せんは浅草で30年以上続く老舎で、人情味あふれる店主と奥さんの人柄が温かく、お二人に会いに来店する常連のお客様も多い。
自慢の鶻鳩や常連のお客様に人気の釜めしはもちろん、焼鳥やカジキマグロ大トロステーキなどの焼物も多数ご用意されております。人気の釜めしは全部で11種類で、特に人気な釜めしはプリップリの鳥をふんだんに使った鳥釜めしです。

caveats: Important discrepancy: the seed brief described this target as a "karaage specialist - ginger-based fried chicken, 40+ years experience," which matches the online retail/wholesale seasoned-meat business asakusa-torisen.com (a similarly-named but distinct company selling packaged karaage). However, the supplied Tabelog URL (ID 13082586) actually resolves to a different, separately-run establishment: 鳥せん (Torisen), a ~46-year-old couple-run izakaya in the Kannon-ura backstreets of Asakusa specializing in yakitori, chicken kamameshi (clay-pot rice), and duck hot pot — not a karaage specialist. I verified and reported on the actual Tabelog-listed restaurant rather than inventing a match to the karaage description. Direct WebFetch of tabelog.com, asakusa-torisen.com, hotpepper.jp, and gnavi.co.jp all returned 403 (blocked at the network/gateway level in this environment); all facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries that cite and quote those pages, since direct page fetches were not obtainable. No vegan or gluten-free information was found for this restaurant in any source — it is a traditional meat-and-fish izakaya with no indication of dietary accommodation.
closed_days: Monday (if Monday is a public holiday, closed the following Tuesday instead)
cuisine: Yakitori (grilled chicken skewers), chicken kamameshi (clay-pot rice, 11 varieties), and kamo nabe (duck hot pot) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school neighborhood izakaya run by a local couple with a regulars-first atmosphere; no evidence of an English menu or foreign-visitor-oriented service, so basic Japanese or a translation app is recommended.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding blocked in this environment). Address confirmed as 東京都台東区浅草3-35-9 via Gurunavi/Tabelog listings; multiple sources describe the location as being in the "観音裏" (Kannon-ura) backstreet district directly behind Sensoji's main hall, which places it well within the 0.8km Sensoji target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergy-specific information were found in any source. The confirmed menu is built around yakitori with soy-sauce-based tare glaze, kamameshi, and duck nabe served with housemade ponzu — tare and ponzu in traditional Japanese cooking are typically soy-sauce-based and therefore contain wheat, and there is no indication this kitchen offers a wheat-free alternative. Treat as not gluten-free safe without direct confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 火〜日・祝日・祝前日 17:00〜22:00（料理L.O. 22:00、ドリンクL.O. 22:00）、月曜定休（月曜が祝日の場合は翌火曜定休）
neighborhood: Kannon-ura, Asakusa, Taito, Tokyo
seats_text: Sources vary somewhat: counter seating (roughly a dozen seats) plus tatami-style zashiki rooms accommodating groups up to around 14; exact configuration unconfirmed.
vegan_detail: No vegan options or accommodations were found in any source. The confirmed specialties (yakitori, chicken kamameshi, duck nabe) are entirely meat-based, and the duck hot pot broth is explicitly katsuobushi (dried bonito) based — meaning even the soup base is not vegan/vegetarian. Not recommended for vegan diners.

lead: 旭川成吉思汗 大黒屋 新宿駅東口店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3丁目24-2 新宿アオキビル10階

bio.origin: Asahikawa, Hokkaido
bio.background: Daikokuya Jingisukan began in Asahikawa, Hokkaido in 2002 and became a queue-forming local favorite there before opening its first Tokyo branch just outside Shinjuku Station's East Exit on November 21, 2024, on the 10th floor of the Shinjuku Aoki Building. The interior incorporates Ainu-inspired patterns and offers window seats with night views over Shinjuku.
bio.specialty: Fresh, never-frozen raw lamb (生ラム) grilled Jingisukan-style on a domed hot plate, especially the shoulder loin (肩ロース); popular sides include a crunchy daikon-and-almond salad and Korean-style cold noodles (冷麺).
bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes air-flown, genuinely fresh (non-frozen) lamb, kept in a dedicated lamb refrigerator held below 0°C, as a point of difference from most Jingisukan restaurants that use frozen meat.
roles: Jingisukan (Hokkaido-style grilled lamb) restaurant, Tokyo branch of an Asahikawa-founded chain

anecdotes.text: A blogger visited with family, including a Hokkaido-native mother curious about the restaurant, and found the raw lamb shoulder loin excellent along with the cold noodles, ramen salad, and daikon salad, describing the restaurant as clean and delicious.

caveats: This is a meat-focused Jingisukan (grilled Hokkaido lamb) restaurant, not a dietary-specialty venue. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu, allergy chart, or English-language accommodation was found in any source consulted. The chain's signature dipping sauce (tare) is soy-sauce based and contains wheat, and items like cold noodles use wheat noodles. Several key pages (official site, Tabelog details, Hot Pepper, Gnavi) returned 403/blocked on direct fetch, so this record relies on search-engine-indexed snippets of those same pages rather than full direct reads; core facts (existence, address, hours, opening date, cuisine) are corroborated across multiple independent listing sites.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day (open all 7 days); irregular closures reported around the New Year holidays (Dec 31–Jan 3) and occasional single dates such as Nov 10 — confirm current closures before visiting.
cuisine: ジンギスカン (Jingisukan) — Hokkaido-style grilled raw lamb
cultural_comfort_note: A modern chain restaurant with online table booking, but no confirmed English menu, multilingual staff, or dietary/allergy signage was found, so non-Japanese-speaking diners with allergies should bring a written allergy card.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null); address confirms location is roughly a 3-minute walk from Shinjuku Station East Exit, well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station (35.6896,139.7006).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or allergen chart was found. The chain's signature tare (dipping sauce) is soy-sauce-based; a retail version of the same "Daikokuya-supervised" Jingisukan sauce lists soy sauce and wheat among its ingredients, so the standard dipping sauce is not gluten-free. Menu items such as cold noodles (冷麺) and possibly the tsukune (meatballs) likely contain wheat. Plain salt-grilled lamb and vegetables ordered without the tare may be lower-risk, but this restaurant uses a shared open grill with no confirmed dedicated gluten-free cooking area, so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should call ahead (03-5990-2927) and ask specifically about the tare ingredients and grill-sharing practices before eating.
name_en: Asahikawa Jingisukan Daikokuya Shinjuku Station East Exit
name_ja: 旭川成吉思汗 大黒屋 新宿駅東口店
neighborhood: 新宿駅東口 Shinjuku Station East Exit

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The retail 'Daikokuya-supervised' Jingisukan sauce (same brand/recipe lineage as the restaurant's tare), made by Sorachi, lists ingredients including soy sauce (国内製造), apple, sugar, hydrolyzed protein, onion, rice fermentation seasoning, vinegar, spices, sake, salt, kelp extract, bonito extract, and states it contains wheat, soy, chicken, pork, and apple.

seats_text: Counter seats and table seats, including window seats with night views over Shinjuku; exact seat count not published in sources found.
vegan_detail: This is a lamb-grill restaurant with no vegan menu found. Side dishes such as a daikon-and-almond salad, grilled vegetables, and ramen/cold-noodle salads exist, but sauces and dressings likely include bonito (kelp/bonito extract appears in the chain's sauce ingredients) and other animal-derived seasonings, so even the vegetable sides are not confirmed vegan-safe. Vegan diners would need to ask staff in detail about each dressing/sauce and should expect very limited, unconfirmed options in a restaurant built around meat grilling.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草1丁目37-6 ライオンズマンション雷門103

bio.origin: Second branch ("ふたつめ" = "the second one") of 茶々工房, a Japanese-tea sweets shop originally founded in 2003 in Takadanobaba; this Asakusa location opened in 2022.
bio.background: Run by an individual craftsman (surname Aizawa per press coverage) who hand-built the extruding machine used to press the mont blanc to order, spending roughly six months developing it — the mechanical core is off-the-shelf but he built the wooden housing and hand-painted the shop's logo on it himself.
bio.specialty: 生搾り抹茶モンブラン (fresh hand-pressed matcha mont blanc), extruded to order into a waffle cone with mascarpone cream, red beans (azuki), matcha sauce, amber sugar and lemon marmalade.
bio.philosophy: Sources matcha exclusively from Hattori Farm in Shizuoka, an organic, pesticide-free grower chosen because it shades its tea bushes with solar panels as an energy-conscious growing method; sources chestnuts from a grower in Kasama, Ibaraki, introduced through a relative of the owner and chosen partly because that farm also provides employment support for people with disabilities.

anecdotes.text: The machine used to press each mont blanc to order was hand-built by the shop's owner over about six months; the mechanism itself is off-the-shelf but he built the wooden casing and painted the shop's logo onto it himself.

All matcha used in the shop comes from a single organic, pesticide-free farm (Hattori Farm) in Shizuoka, chosen in part because the farm uses solar panels to shade its tea as an energy-efficient growing method.
The chestnuts come from a grower in Kasama, Ibaraki, introduced to the owner through a relative; that farm was selected partly because it also offers employment support to people with disabilities.
The fresh-pressed matcha mont blanc became a social-media sensation, drawing queues of both local and international visitors even on weekdays.

caveats: Sources disagree on whether this shop has a fixed weekly day off: Tabelog lists Monday closures, while several aggregator pages (Yahoo Map, gourmet-calendar roundups) describe it as open daily with no regular holiday — confirm before visiting. It is a small, takeout-focused matcha-mont-blanc specialty counter; reports on in-store seating are inconsistent (most say takeout-only, a couple mention a small 2-person table), so treat seating as unreliable. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source: the signature mont blanc is built on a wheat waffle cone with mascarpone (dairy) cream, and this is a single-item dessert specialist, not a restaurant with substitution options.
closed_days: Monday (per Tabelog); note some other sources report it as open daily with no fixed closed day — unconfirmed.
cultural_comfort_note: A compact, likely Japanese-only takeout counter, but it's a well-known social-media destination that regularly draws foreign tourists, so simple pointing/photo-menu ordering for its one signature item should be manageable.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address corroborated by multiple independent sources as 東京都台東区浅草1丁目37-6, described consistently as ~3 minutes' walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6, placing it well within the Sensōji/Asakusa target radius.
gf_detail: The signature and effectively only product is a mont blanc built on a wheat waffle cone with cake/cream components; no gluten-free version, alternative base, ingredient allergen labeling, or dedicated preparation area was mentioned in any source found. Treat as not celiac-safe.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (multiple sources agree on these hours); closed-day status conflicts across sources — see caveats.
neighborhood: 台東区浅草 (Asakusa, Taito-ku) — approx. 3-minute walk from Asakusa Station Exit 6
seats_text: Reports are inconsistent — most describe it as a takeout-only counter with no seating; a couple of sources mention a small 2-person table inside. Not reliably confirmed either way.
vegan_detail: The mont blanc contains mascarpone cream (dairy) and other non-vegan components (likely eggs/dairy in the cake/waffle base); no vegan version, substitution, or plant-based alternative was mentioned in any source found.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町15-1 渋谷パルコ B1F

bio.background: QUATTRO LABO is a music café and bar produced by CLUB QUATTRO, the Shibuya live-music venue operator. It originally opened in 2014 inside Kichijoji PARCO, then closed there in August 2019 and reopened in November 2019 in the basement of Shibuya PARCO, bringing along its collection of more than 5,000 analog records and CDs and a handmade large speaker system.
bio.specialty: Lemon Rice, a limited-quantity signature dish created in collaboration with curry devotee Yuki Komiyama of Hohdiiran
bio.philosophy: By day it runs as a casual café; by night it becomes a dedicated music bar, serving food and drinks while classic analog records play through a custom high-end sound system spanning 1960s-70s rock, soul, jazz, roots music, and city pop.
roles: Music café & bar produced by CLUB QUATTRO

anecdotes.text: The venue originally opened in 2014 at Kichijoji PARCO before relocating to the basement of Shibuya PARCO in November 2019, bringing its collection of over 5,000 analog records and CDs.
The Lemon Rice, a limited-quantity signature dish, was produced by curry enthusiast Yuki Komiyama of Hohdiiran.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information could be found for this spot in any source searched (official site, Tabelog, press coverage, or blogs). It is a general café/music-bar menu built around items like curry and the signature Lemon Rice, which typically involve wheat-based roux/soy sauce; dietary accommodation is unconfirmed either way, so celiac/vegan diners should contact the venue directly before visiting.
closed_days: Open year-round, following Shibuya PARCO's operating calendar (no regular weekly closed day found).
cuisine: Café / Music Bar
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside a major Shibuya shopping complex frequented by tourists, but no English menu or English-language service was confirmed in any source.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on the official site, Tabelog, or press coverage. The menu centers on curry-style dishes (e.g., the signature Lemon Rice) which commonly use wheat-containing roux and soy sauce in Japan; assume wheat exposure unless confirmed otherwise by staff at time of visit.

neighborhood: 渋谷 (渋谷PARCO地下1階, Shibuya Station area)

vegan_detail: No vegan menu, labeling, or ingredient breakdown (e.g., dashi/katsuo/bonito use) was found on the official site, Tabelog, or press coverage. Menu appears to be a standard omnivore café/bar offering (curry, Lemon Rice, drinks); vegan diners should confirm ingredients with staff before ordering.

lead: 花とごはんとクリームソーダ なぎさ食堂
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 東京ドームシティ ミーツポート

bio.background: Opened in December 2024 in the Meets Port area of Tokyo Dome City, directly in front of Tokyo Dome City Hall, a short walk from Suidobashi Station.
bio.specialty: Photogenic cream sodas in a range of colors (including a pure-white version and a deep-indigo version, plus an adults-only version with alcohol), rice plates paired with fish or meat mains such as acqua pazza, and an afternoon tea set served with a drink.
bio.philosophy: A flower-filled, photogenic and retro concept space designed as much for taking pictures as for eating — every corner of the shop, including a second-floor terrace, is treated as a photo spot, tying into Tokyo's cafe culture around fan-activity (oshikatsu) outings.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's afternoon tea set, served amid a flower-filled dining room and priced with a drink included, is highlighted as a bookable elegant afternoon-tea experience at Tokyo Dome City.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, official site, Hotpepper, gnavi, or hitosara pages (all returned HTTP 403 in this environment); facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets citing those pages, not full page fetches, so treat with medium confidence. Address places this spot in Bunkyo-ku (文京区), not Chiyoda-ku as given in the task's area label — confirmed via multiple independent listings — but it is a ~3-minute walk from Suidōbashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius, so it qualifies. Business hours are ambiguous: most sources (incl. the official Tokyo Dome City gourmet guide) list 11:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30), but one search result referenced a change to 11:30-19:00 (L.O. 18:30) "from May 1" with no year specified — hours may also extend during Tokyo Dome events. No fixed weekly closed day was found for this location, but isolated irregular closures (e.g., specific dates in May) were referenced. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information, allergy protocol, or dedicated-fryer information was found for this venue in any source searched.

The menu centers on rice plates with fish or meat (e.g., acqua pazza) and sauces that commonly rely on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi in Japanese cafe cooking; celiac diners should ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contamination before ordering.

seats_text: Not published in any source found.

Dishes are fish/meat-centric (rice plates, acqua pazza) and likely use dashi/bonito-based stocks common in Japanese cafe cooking; the cream sodas may be vegan but ingredients (e.g., cream/syrups) were not confirmed. Vegan diners should ask staff directly.

name_ja: This is Crêpe（ディスイズクレープ）
cuisine: クレープ・カフェ (French-style crêpe and coffee shop)
neighborhood: 新宿 / 新宿御苑前・新宿三丁目周辺
vegan_detail: No vegan menu items were identified. The shop's signature item is a butter-forward crêpe built around Échiré dairy butter, and standard French crêpe batter typically contains egg and milk; no egg-free or dairy-free alternative is advertised on any source checked.
gf_detail: The crêpe batter itself is made from carefully sourced French wheat flour — described repeatedly across sources as a core selling point ("French premium wheat") — so gluten is baked into the base product, not just a cross-contact risk. No gluten-free batter or dedicated GF item was found. Not suitable for celiac guests.
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿2-7-1 不二川ビル1F
geocode_note: Address is 2-7-1 Shinjuku (Shinjuku-ku). Multiple sources describe the shop as a 2-minute walk from Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line "Shinjuku Gyoemmae" Station Exit 1, and a 4-minute walk from "Shinjuku Sanchome" Station Exit C4. Independently verified routed walking distance from JR Shinjuku Station to Shinjuku Gyoemmae Station is ~820m (11 min), so the shop sits at or just inside the edge of the 0.8km radius from Shinjuku Station — not clearly outside it.
seats_text: Seating capacity not confirmed in available sources; the shop reads as a small counter/takeout-oriented crêpe stand rather than a full sit-down café.
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A compact Japanese specialty crêpe counter with no English menu confirmed — pointing at the photo menu and basic greetings should be enough to order.

bio.background: This is Crêpe is a small crêpe-and-coffee specialty shop near Shinjuku Gyoen, built around a single hero product: a "Sugar Butter" crêpe that showcases the dough itself rather than piling on toppings.
bio.specialty: Sugar Butter crêpe made with French wheat flour and Échiré butter, paired with specialty coffee (beans sourced from origins such as Ethiopia and Kenya)
bio.philosophy: The shop's own framing (via its Instagram bio and store description) is built around "the highest-quality crêpes, the highest-quality coffee, communication full of smiles, and a comfortable atmosphere in the store" — an emphasis on letting a carefully made, chewy French-flour dough be delicious on its own, without needing heavy toppings.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its signature style as "生地だけで美味しいもちもちSugar Butter" — a chewy Sugar Butter crêpe that's delicious on the strength of the dough alone.
In 2024 the shop added the "imako" mobile payment/ordering option, reported as part of the brand's push to make ordering more convenient for customers near Shinjuku Gyoen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not applicable as a cross-contact question in the usual sense — the standard crêpe batter is itself made with French wheat flour, so gluten is a base ingredient rather than an incidental contaminant, and no gluten-free batter option was found.

caveats: No source was directly fetchable via automated browser (Tabelog, official Instagram, Wolt, Retty, PR Times, and autoreserve all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch); all facts above are drawn from search-engine result snippets that quote/summarize those pages, not from direct page reads. Distance from Shinjuku Station is close to the 0.8km cutoff (borderline, not clearly outside) rather than comfortably inside it — flagging for reviewer judgment. This is a standard-wheat, butter-and-likely-egg crêpe shop with no vegan or gluten-free accommodation found; it should not be marketed as GF/vegan-friendly beyond an honest omnivore/mom-and-pop listing.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-8-4

bio.origin: Founded in Showa 48 (1973) by a family originally from Nagano Prefecture.
bio.background: Lodge Akaishi opened in Asakusa's 観音裏 (Kannon-ura) backstreets in 1973. The name comes from Mt. Akaishi (赤石岳), a peak in the Southern Alps visible from the founders' hometown in Nagano — hence the log-cabin, mountain-lodge look of the wood-paneled interior. Because Asakusa was historically a lively entertainment and hanamachi district, the shop originally stayed open into the early morning hours to serve the neighborhood's night-shift workers; today it still runs from morning until the middle of the night, so its clientele shifts through the day from local residents having a leisurely breakfast or lunch, to people getting off work late at night, to taxi drivers taking a break before dawn.
bio.specialty: Napolitan spaghetti (ナポリタン) and its ebi sando / ebi katsu sandwich (fried shrimp sandwich), widely cited by food writers as among the best in Tokyo.
roles: kissaten (retro Japanese-style coffee shop / yoshoku diner)

anecdotes.text: 店名は創業者である現マスターのお父さんと叔父さんが長野県の出身であったことから、赤石岳を由来としています。 (The shop's name comes from Mt. Akaishi, since the founders — the current master's father and uncle — were from Nagano.)
深夜まで営業しているのは、浅草がかつて花街として深夜まで賑わっていた名残りだと言われている。 (It stays open late as a holdover from Asakusa's days as a bustling hanamachi entertainment district.)
東京で最も美味しいエビフライサンドを食べるならココ！ (If you want the best fried-shrimp sandwich in Tokyo, this is the place.)

caveats: Old-school Showa-era yoshoku kissaten (Western-style diner/coffee shop), not GF- or vegan-focused; signature dishes (napolitan pasta, breaded fried-shrimp sandwich) are wheat-based and no gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found in any source. Some sources give slightly different weekday closing time and seat count (35 vs 36); minor hours discrepancies between search snippets could not be fully reconciled without a direct site fetch (Tabelog and most official-style pages returned 403 to automated fetch).
closed_days: Monday (月曜日)
cuisine: 洋食・喫茶 (Western-style diner / retro coffee shop)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic Showa-era neighborhood kissaten with no indication of English menus or English-speaking staff, though it is well covered by Japanese food blogs and appears in the Tabelog 100 kissaten list.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options or accommodations found. Signature dishes are wheat-based: napolitan is wheat pasta, and the famous ebi sando is breaded, deep-fried shrimp on wheat bread. No mention of gluten-free substitutions or dedicated frying equipment. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address captured from search-indexed listings (Rakuten Gnavi, Ekiten) as 東京都台東区浅草3-8-4. The shop is explicitly located in the 観音裏 (behind Sensō-ji) backstreets of Asakusa, consistent with the target area.
neighborhood: 浅草 観音裏 (Asakusa, Kannon-ura)
seats_text: Approximately 36 seats (sources vary between 35 and 36; roughly 30 table seats plus 5-6 counter seats)
vegan_detail: No vegan options or accommodations found. This is a classic yoshoku (Western-style) kissaten built around eggs, dairy, meat, and shrimp (its two signature items — napolitan with meat/vegetables in an egg-and-butter-friendly kitchen, and a deep-fried shrimp sandwich — are both non-vegan). No mention of a vegetable-only menu or dashi/bonito-free preparation.

lead: 秀吉 神保町水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-48-1（1・2F）

bio.background: This is a branch of the OC Group's "Hideyoshi" yakitori/kushi-katsu izakaya chain, one of several Hideyoshi locations around Tokyo (this one straddling Jimbocho and Suidobashi).
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori (skewers priced from around 80 yen) alongside a "baka-mori" (oversized-portion) series of karaage fried chicken and kushi-katsu.
bio.philosophy: The stated concept, repeated across listing sites, is speed, low price, and taste ("早い・安い・美味い") — high-volume, value-priced grilled and fried chicken rather than a chef-driven or artisanal approach.
roles: Yakitori/kushi-katsu izakaya, part of a multi-location chain

anecdotes.text: Uses binchotan charcoal for the yakitori and an original sauce blended from eight seasonings and vegetables for some dishes.
Known among reviewers for very large karaage portions — one reviewer described sharing yakitori, a big karaage, and a large salad with drinks for about ¥4,000 for two people.

caveats: Business-hours details vary slightly between listing sites (some show Sat/Sun as 12:00–23:00, others as 17:00–24:00); the shop is confirmed 不定休 (irregular closures), so hours should be checked before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan menu, labeling, or accommodation was found in any source — this is a standard chicken-focused izakaya (grilled skewers with soy-based tare, fried karaage and kushi-katsu). Street number appears as both 2-48-1 and 2-48-3 across different directories, though all agree on the same building/block near Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly closed day found)
cuisine: 焼き鳥・串カツ (Yakitori / grilled chicken skewers and deep-fried skewers izakaya)
cultural_comfort_note: A local Japanese-language chain izakaya with no evidence of an English menu, so pointing at photos on the menu or using a translation app is recommended.
geocode_note: Address block (神田神保町2-48) is consistently placed about 2–4 minutes' walk from Jimbocho Station and 4–6 minutes from Suidobashi Station across multiple directories, comfortably within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark.
gf_detail: Yakitori tare (glaze) and the karaage/kushi-katsu batter are wheat-based soy-sauce/flour preparations typical of this style of izakaya; no gluten-free menu, substitutions, or dedicated-fryer claims were found in any source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 月〜金: ランチ 11:30-14:00／ディナー 17:00-24:00。土・日・祝: 一部情報源では12:00-23:00、別の情報源では17:00-24:00と表記が分かれる。不定休。（Source listings disagree slightly on weekend hours — confirm directly before visiting.）
name_ja: 秀吉 神保町水道橋店
neighborhood: 神保町 (Jimbocho), Chiyoda-ku

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kushi-katsu and karaage are deep-fried items on the menu alongside grilled skewers; no separate/dedicated fryer for allergen-free items was mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's own original sauce and yakitori tare are described as blended seasoning sauces typical of this izakaya style, which in Japan are standardly soy-sauce (wheat-containing) based; no wheat-free tare or shoyu was mentioned.

seats_text: Group seating available for up to 60 people, including a private second-floor room that holds up to 40
vegan_detail: The menu is chicken-centric (yakitori, karaage, kushi-katsu) with no vegetarian or vegan dishes mentioned in any source found; sauces/tare are not confirmed dashi- or bonito-free. Not recommended for vegan diners without direct advance confirmation from the restaurant.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-23-2 イワヲビル 1F・2F（〒150-0043）

roles: Second restaurant opened (July 2000) by Asia Kitchen Co., a small independent Shibuya izakaya group founded by restaurateur Eiji Yoshizaki (also behind sister shops Naru, Sanchaki and Sanjuugoban)
bio.background: Iwao occupies a wooden house said to be around 70-80 years old that originally housed a tailor's shop, tucked down a back alley off Dogenzaka a few minutes from Shibuya Station. The building was renovated into a two-floor 'hideaway' izakaya, and as of 2025 the shop was marking roughly 25 years in business.
bio.specialty: Fresh sashimi moriawase (assorted raw fish platter) and roast Miyazaki 'Ozaki' wagyu beef
bio.philosophy: Japanese home-style dishes reworked with the kitchen's own original arrangements, built around fresh fish and quality-sourced ingredients (vegetables, fish and meat delivered directly from Kawada Farm), served in a quiet old-house setting meant to feel like a true neighborhood hideaway rather than a tourist-facing izakaya.

anecdotes.text: The building was originally a Western-style tailor's shop before being converted into the current izakaya.
Iwao is the second restaurant of Asia Kitchen Co., opened in July 2000, following the group's first shop Naru (1999).
A black-vinegar (kurozu) cocktail is a particular favorite among women who visit the bar.

caveats: Could not directly load Tabelog, official site, Retty, gnavi, Hotpepper or Miil pages (all returned HTTP 403 through the fetch tool/proxy); all facts below are drawn from web-search result summaries that quoted or paraphrased those same pages, cross-checked across at least two independent listings (Tabelog search snippet, Hotpepper, r.gnavi, Retty, Asia Kitchen official site, foosta-career.com, food-stadium.com). No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this shop anywhere in the sources found — it is a conventional sake-focused izakaya (sashimi, wagyu roast beef, tempura-style dishes), so GF/vegan ratings below default to the cautious end. Exact seat count and definitive closed-day policy vary slightly by source (most say 年中無休/no regular closed day); Friday/Saturday hours run later (until ~1:00) than the rest of the week. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding blocked) — address_ja is the authoritative location string for downstream geocoding.
closed_days: None found — most listings state 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular closed day)
cuisine: Japanese izakaya / sake bar — creative Japanese cuisine centered on fresh sashimi and grilled/roasted dishes, with a large rotating sake selection
cultural_comfort_note: This is a low-profile, locals'-favorite hideaway (the entrance is easy to miss) with a Japanese-language menu and no signs of English service, so non-Japanese-speaking diners should expect to lean on a translation app or a Japanese-speaking companion.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is mentioned anywhere in available sources. As a standard izakaya, dishes almost certainly rely on wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi, and some items appear to be tempura-style fried; there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce option. Celiac or strict gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without asking staff directly (in Japanese) about individual dishes, and should treat this as a high-risk venue absent that confirmation.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu & Sun: 17:30–24:00 (food/drink last order 23:30). Fri, Sat & days before public holidays: 17:30–1:00 (last order 24:30). Reported as open year-round with no fixed closed day.
neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station Hachiko exit and near Shinsen Station)
seats_text: Counter seating spread across two floors, plus tatami-style semi-private booths for 2-4 guests, larger table seating, and party/private rooms reported to hold up to roughly 17-20 guests for groups.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. The kitchen's signature items are fresh-fish sashimi and roast wagyu beef, and traditional izakaya dashi is typically bonito (katsuobushi)-based, so hidden animal-derived stock is likely throughout the menu even in vegetable-forward dishes. Not recommended for vegan diners without a detailed conversation with staff about specific dishes.

lead: つけそば 神田 勝本
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町1-2-4

bio.background: Kanda Katsumoto is the second shop opened by chef Matsumura Yasushi, who spent 36 years working in French cuisine, including a stint as executive chef at the former ANA Crown Plaza Hotel Kyoto, before shifting his career to ramen so he could serve carefully made food at an accessible price. He first opened Chukasoba Katsumoto in Suidobashi in 2015, then Kanda Katsumoto in nearby Jimbocho, and later Ginza Hachigo, all under what is now known as the Katsumoto Group.
bio.specialty: Kiyoshiru tsukesoba (清湯つけそば) — a clear niboshi-and-chicken dipping broth served alongside two kinds of noodles, thin and flat, blended together in one bowl.
bio.philosophy: Matsumura applies decades of French fine-dining technique — layered stocks, precise seasoning — to a bowl of noodles he keeps affordably priced, wanting more people to be able to enjoy carefully crafted food.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's operating company went bankrupt in March 2022 amid pandemic-era losses, but all three Katsumoto Group shops, including Kanda Katsumoto, reopened within about two months under a new operator, Food Operation Japan Co.
Kanda Katsumoto was selected for the Tabelog Ramen TOKYO 'Best 100' list two years running, and it appeared again on the 2025 edition of the list.

caveats: Payment methods (cash vs. IC card/card) at the ticket machine could not be confirmed from available sources. Hours/closures fluctuate — Monday closures are irregular (不定休) and announced via the shop's social media, and the shop only began opening Sundays as of late December 2025, so treat hours as indicative rather than fixed. The operating company (株式会社勝本) went bankrupt in March 2022; the shop reopened about two months later under new operator Food Operation Japan Co. and has continued operating since (reselected for Tabelog's Ramen 100 in 2025). No gluten-free or vegan accommodation of any kind was found in any source — this is a standard wheat-noodle, fish/chicken-broth tsukemen specialist with no dietary-accommodation program.
closed_days: Monday closures are irregular (月曜不定休) and announced via the shop's social media (@kanda_katsumoto); otherwise open daily as of late 2025 when Sunday service was added.
cuisine: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) specialist — clear niboshi (dried sardine), chicken, and dried-fish stock broth with two blended noodle types
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at a small counter with no English menu reported, though the Katsumoto Group's official website has an English-language page.
geocode_note: Address (東京都千代田区神田猿楽町1-2-4, Kanda Sarugakucho) places the shop roughly 400-600m from Suidōbashi Station and about 300-400m from Jimbocho Station, both well within the 0.8km target radius of Suidōbashi Station. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (network geocoding disabled in this environment); pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: The noodles are wheat-based (a blend of two wheat-noodle types is the shop's signature), and the tare/broth is soy-sauce (shoyu) based, per the menu name 清湯（しょうゆ）つけそば. No gluten-free noodle substitution, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 10:00-18:00, Sun 10:00-17:00, or until the broth sells out for the day; Monday closures are irregular (不定休) — check the shop's social media (@kanda_katsumoto) before visiting. Sunday service was only added starting December 21, 2025 (previously closed Sundays).
name_ja: つけそば 神田 勝本
neighborhood: Jimbocho / Kanda Sarugakucho (神保町/神田猿楽町)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth's tare is a shoyu (soy sauce) base, per the menu item 清湯（しょうゆ）つけそば, served with wheat noodles; no gluten-free noodle alternative is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built from niboshi (dried sardines), other dried-fish stocks, and chicken (including Nagoya Cochin), with no vegetable-only broth option documented.

seats_text: Counter seating, about 13 seats
vegan_detail: The shop's only broth is fish- and chicken-based (niboshi, other dried fish, and multiple chicken varieties including Nagoya Cochin for flavor adjustment), with wheat noodles as the base product. No vegetable broth, plant-based, or vegan option was found in any source. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

lead: つけ麍・らーめん 波海拉
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿4-1-9 新宿ユースビル 8F

bio.background: Opened July 30, 2025 on the 8th floor of the Shinjuku Youth Building (新宿ユースビル PAX), about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's south/southeast exit. The shop is run by 株式会社トータルウェル (Total Well Corporation), a restaurant-development company, under an official licensing agreement with 舎鈴 (Sharin) — the sister brand of the celebrated Tokyo Station tsukemen shop 六厘舎 (Rokurinsha) — allowing it to use Rokurinsha-lineage recipes and techniques.
bio.specialty: Tsukemen with a pork-bone and seafood (gyokai) blended dipping broth, served in a 'separate plate' style with chashu, menma, ajitama (seasoned egg), and nori plated apart from the noodles; also a dried-sardine-scented dark-soy-sauce ramen with curly noodles. Core menu: Tsukemen Iki, Tantan Tsukemen Homura, and Ramen Shinobi.
bio.philosophy: The name 波海拉 (Namiura) draws on Katsushika Hokusai's ukiyo-e print 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa' from his Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji series, evoking a 'wave' of concentrated broth flavor.
roles: operator: 株式会社トータルウェル (Total Well Corporation)

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was inspired by Hokusai's 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa.'
Namiura operates under a licensing agreement with 舎鈴, sister restaurant of the famous Tokyo Station tsukemen shop 六厘舎 (Rokurinsha), using its recipes and methods.
Ingredients (chashu, menma, ajitama, nori) are served on a separate plate rather than atop the noodles, a signature presentation touch.

caveats: Direct WebFetch of Tabelog and most blog/review pages returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so hours, address, and seating details are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages (kanko-shinjuku.jp tourism-association article, ramendb, and related blogs) rather than direct page reads. Payment method (cash vs card/IC) and exact seat count could not be independently confirmed from a fetched primary source — seat count (~55) and cashless acceptance are commonly reported for this shop but unverified firsthand. No official standalone website was found; Instagram/X accounts serve as the shop's official online presence. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information exists for this restaurant in any source — it is a standard pork-bone/seafood broth ramen-tsukemen shop with no dietary accommodations mentioned.

cultural_comfort_note: A standard Japanese-language neighborhood ramen/tsukemen shop near Shinjuku Station; no confirmed English menu, English signage, or English-speaking-staff reports found.

lead: WE ARE THE FARM 渋谷
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区松濤1-28-11 Pigeon松濤高田ビル1F

bio.origin: Own farm in Sakura City, Chiba Prefecture
bio.background: WE ARE THE FARM Shibuya (formerly STAND BY FARM Shoto) is a "farm-to-table" vegetable bar run by a group that also operates its own organic farm in Sakura, Chiba. The restaurant cultivates roughly 150 heirloom ("koteishu"/fixed-seed) vegetable varieties without pesticides or chemical fertilizers, and vegetables are said to be harvested each morning and brought straight to the Shibuya restaurant.
bio.specialty: Field-style teppanyaki bagna cauda (畑の鉄板焼きバーニャカウダ) — a hot-plate bagna cauda piled with freshly harvested heirloom vegetables and a house-made dip
bio.philosophy: The concept centers on showcasing rare, disappearing heirloom vegetable varieties that farmers have preserved for generations but that have fallen out of commercial cultivation due to difficult growing conditions and short shelf life, paired with a low-waste ethos.

anecdotes.text: Their Antioxidant Hot Pot Course uses a "veggie broth" made from vegetable peels and scraps combined with Japanese dashi, filled with seasonal organic vegetables, as a way of using the whole vegetable rather than discarding trimmings.
anecdotes.source: Web search aggregation of official/restaurant descriptions

anecdotes.text: The restaurant offers a limited-quantity gluten-free, vegan, and low-sugar tart from tart artist Akira Yamamoto, and has published guidance for gluten-free dining at the Shibuya location.

caveats: Primary sources (Tabelog, official site, Vegewel, Hachidory, blog) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment; all facts below come from web-search result summaries of those same pages, so wording/details could not be independently cross-checked against the raw page text. No confirmation of a dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce; the vegan course reportedly must be reserved in advance because the regular menu contains many animal-derived items, and a hot-pot dish combines vegetable broth with Japanese dashi (dashi typically contains bonito/katsuo unless specified otherwise). Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm specifics by phone before visiting.
closed_days: Monday
cuisine: Vegetable dishes — organic heirloom vegetables (farm-to-table vegetable bar, Italian-leaning small plates)
cultural_comfort_note: The official site publishes an English-language version and offers 24-hour online booking, suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese-speaking guests, though on-site English fluency of staff is unconfirmed.

gf_detail: The restaurant has published its own blog post on enjoying gluten-free dining at this Shibuya location and offers a limited-quantity gluten-free (also vegan, low-sugar) tart from tart artist Akira Yamamoto. However, no source confirms the full menu is wheat-free, that soy sauce used is wheat-free, or that a dedicated gluten-free fryer/prep area exists — the vegetable-forward menu likely has some naturally GF dishes but cross-contamination and hidden-wheat risk (e.g., in sauces, tempura-style items) should be confirmed directly with staff, especially for celiac-level strictness.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A hot-pot dish combines a vegetable-scrap "veggie broth" with Japanese dashi, which commonly includes bonito (katsuobushi) unless a plant-based dashi is specifically requested.
