positives.text: All vegetables are grown on the group's own organic farm in Sakura, Chiba, without pesticides or chemical fertilizers, and are marketed as harvested and delivered fresh each morning.
positives.source: Web search summary of official site / restaurant guides

seats_text: Total of 54 seats
vegan_detail: A vegan course exists but reportedly should be reserved in advance, since the regular menu features many animal-derived dishes. One signature hot-pot item mixes vegetable-scrap broth with Japanese dashi, which typically contains bonito unless a plant-based version is requested — diners wanting a strictly vegan meal should specifically order the vegan course and confirm dashi/bonito exclusions rather than order freely from the general menu.

lead: ソウルフードインディア 水道橋
address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷1-21-7 1F

bio.origin: Mumbai, India
bio.background: Chef Rachman trained at a high-end hotel in Mumbai before coming to Japan, and has been the head chef at this Suidōbashi shop for roughly a decade.
bio.specialty: North Indian-style curry with rich, deeply spiced gravies, served with naan baked in a traditional Indian clay tandoor over charcoal.
bio.philosophy: Home-style North Indian cooking made accessible and affordable near the station, with charcoal-fired tandoor naan as the signature touch.

anecdotes.text: Main chef Rachman honed his skills at a luxury hotel in Mumbai and has run the kitchen at this shop for about 10 years.
All naan is baked to order in an Indian clay tandoor fired with charcoal, giving it a distinctive smoky aroma.

caveats: Address and hours corroborated by multiple independent listing sites (HotPepper, Gurunavi, Ameba blog aggregation) via search; the restaurant's own site (soulfoodindia.com / soulfoodindia.owst.jp) and Tabelog could not be directly fetched in this environment (blocked by network policy), so those facts rest on search-engine summaries of those pages rather than a direct page fetch. No explicit gluten-free or vegan cross-contamination statement was found from the restaurant itself; dietary fields are marked conservatively as "ask".
closed_days: 年中無休（年末年始を除く）／ No regular closed day, closed only around New Year's
cuisine: North Indian curry (北インドカレー), vegetarian-friendly options
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, station-front curry shop with an Indian chef and a simple photo/English-friendly menu; basic Japanese or English food words are enough to order comfortably.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or explicit allergy protocol is advertised. Indian-style curries are typically thickened with onion/spice bases rather than wheat roux, so a rice-based curry (skipping the naan, which clearly contains wheat and is baked in the same tandoor as other breads) may be workable, but this has not been confirmed by the restaurant and should be checked with staff, especially regarding shared prep surfaces and any wheat-containing thickeners or pre-made sauces.
hours_raw: 11:00～22:30（料理L.O. 22:00、ドリンクL.O. 22:00）、年中無休（年末年始を除く）
name_ja: ソウルフードインディア 水道橋店
neighborhood: 本郷／水道橋 (Bunkyō-ku Hongō, 1 min walk from Suidōbashi Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No specific gluten cross-contamination statement was found; naan and other wheat items are prepared in the same kitchen and tandoor as curries.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No specific vegan cross-contact statement found; curries and naan may be finished with ghee, butter, or yogurt-based dough unless requested otherwise.

positives.text: A vegetable curry (ベジタブルカレー, mild) is offered as a standing option on both the lunch and dinner menus, served with naan or rice.

seats_text: 21 seats
vegan_detail: A meatless vegetable curry is listed on the regular menu, so a vegetarian meal is straightforward, but full vegan status is not confirmed: North Indian curries at shops like this are often finished with ghee (clarified butter) or a dab of cream/yogurt, and naan dough commonly includes dairy or yogurt, so vegan diners should confirm with staff whether the vegetable curry and any bread can be made without dairy.

lead: スタイルズケイクス&カンパニー
name_ja: スタイルズケイクス＆カンパニー
cuisine: Cake/Tart Boutique (takeout-only tart, pie, and baked-goods shop)
neighborhood: Kanda-Ogawamachi, near Jimbōchō Station (Chiyoda-ku)
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-16-5 第二岩崎ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Multiple sources place the shop 2 minutes on foot from Jimbōchō Station Exit A5 in Kanda-Ogawamachi 3-chome, which is estimated to fall within ~0.6-0.8km of Suidōbashi Station, satisfying the target-area requirement; downstream geocoding of address_ja should confirm precisely.
seats_text: Takeout-only boutique; no customer seating on site.
hours_raw: Reported as approximately 12:00-19:00 Monday-Friday and 12:00-17:00 Saturday, closed Sundays and public holidays; frequently sells out and closes early. Same-day telephone hold requests (取り置き) are taken roughly 9:00-10:30. Sources disagree somewhat on exact weekday closures (a few mention additional irregular closures), so hours should be treated as approximate.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (confirmed by multiple sources); Saturday hours are shortened and additional irregular closures are reported by some sources
cultural_comfort_note: Same-day items are announced overnight on the shop's Japanese-language Facebook page and popular tarts sell out via a Japanese-only morning phone hold line, so visiting without Japanese help or a big appetite for uncertainty is risky.
vegan_detail: This is a butter-and-cream French-style tart/pie shop (fruit tarts, mont blanc, banana cream tart, fig clafoutis) — dairy and eggs are core to nearly every item, and no vegan-adapted recipes or vegan claims were found.
gf_detail: The regular tart and pie lineup uses standard wheat-flour pastry crusts. Separately, the shop sells a wheat-free 米粉の焼きドーナツ (baked rice-flour donut, ~¥250) mentioned in blog/menu coverage — but no source describes a dedicated gluten-free process, separate equipment, or cross-contamination controls, and it is baked in the same small kitchen as the wheat-based tarts. Celiac diners should ask directly about cross-contact before relying on this item.

bio.chef_name: 岩崎修 (Iwasaki Osamu) and his wife 百合子 (Yuriko)
bio.origin: Kanda, Tokyo (both are Kanda locals)
bio.background: The couple ran a café in the Kanda neighborhood for about 15 years and also did food-related publishing work before closing the café. They then opened Style's Cakes & Co. as a scaled-down, tart-focused shop suited to their own pace of life.
bio.specialty: Fruit tarts with a thick, crunchy, well-browned crust, especially the seasonal fresh fruit tart and the French chestnut mont blanc tart; also known for a banana cream tart and a fig clafoutis pie.
bio.philosophy: To make handmade tarts "身の丈にあった形で" (in a form that fits their own scale/means) rather than running a larger café operation.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the tart crust as thick, crunchy and fragrant with very fresh fruit, and note that popular flavors like the mont blanc tart routinely sell out via the morning telephone hold line.
A blogger described the shop as delivering a sense of happiness/emotion through its tarts, listing the fresh fruit tart, mont blanc, and other seasonal offerings.

positives.text: Alongside its wheat-flour tarts, the shop offers a baked rice-flour donut (米粉の焼きドーナツ) as a wheat-free option on some days.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to tabelog.com, the shop's official site (stylescakes.com), and most blog/review domains was blocked by this session's network egress policy (403 at the proxy level), so all facts here are drawn from WebSearch-returned snippets of those pages rather than full page fetches. Hours/closed-day details conflict somewhat across sources (some say Mon-Fri+Sat-short, others mention Wed or additional irregular closures); treat hours_week as an approximation and confirm by phone (03-3291-6910) before a visit. Gluten-free and vegan claims are limited to what secondary sources mention (one wheat-free rice-flour donut item); no direct allergen-protocol statement from the shop itself was found.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-18-10 大野ビル2号館 2F

bio.origin: Shibuya, Tokyo (originally opened in Uchisaiwaicho/Shimbashi in July 2021)
bio.background: Owner-chef Kazusei Sato first encountered Singaporean food as a university student, backpacking mainly through Southeast Asia as well as Europe and Africa. After graduating he took a sales job but left after about a year, then worked across a range of restaurant genres — Italian, a bakery, a teishoku diner, izakaya, and a yakitori shop — to build hands-on cooking experience. He opened his first Singaporean-Chinese restaurant in Shimbashi/Uchisaiwaicho in July 2021, then relocated it to its current Shibuya site (a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station) on April 7, 2022. A second, smaller location called 獅天鶏飯ハナレ opened nearby in spring 2024.
bio.specialty: 究極の海南鶏飯 (Hainanese chicken rice) — low-temperature-cooked chicken breast served with jasmine rice cooked in chicken fat/stock, with complimentary rice refills.
bio.philosophy: Sato aims to convey Singapore's multi-ethnic food culture through spicing (cumin, fish sauce/nampla) alongside Chinese-style fried and stir-fried dishes, and leans on Shibuya's location by buying vegetables from producer markets to fold Japanese seasonal produce into the small-plate menu.

anecdotes.text: 店長で調理担当の佐藤一聖さんが海南鶏飯に対する強い思い入れを持ち、シンガポール料理店での経験をベースに立ち上げた。大学生の頃バックパッカーとして東南アジアを中心に世界を巡る中でシンガポール料理に出会った。
新橋発のシンガポール料理店として1年足らずでオープンから渋谷へ移転し、人気店となった。

caveats: No dedicated website was located; hours/menu details were pieced together from Tabelog, gourmet blogs, and directory sites rather than a single authoritative source. Closed days are described as irregular (不定休), so hours should be reconfirmed on Instagram (@shiten_k_han) before visiting. No evidence of any gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found — this is a chicken/pork-centric Singaporean-Chinese menu.
closed_days: 不定休（インスタグラム @shiten_k_han で最新情報を要確認）
cuisine: シンガポール料理・海南鶏飯（シンガポール×中華の酒場）
cultural_comfort_note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff was found in sources; expect a Japanese-language, locals-oriented izakaya-style experience near Shibuya Station.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, allergen labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source. The signature Hainanese chicken rice is typically served with soy-sauce-based dips/sauces (dark and light soy sauce both contain wheat), and the kitchen also does Chinese-style frying and stir-frying, making cross-contact likely. Not recommended for celiac-level gluten-free needs without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions; lat/lng left null. Address places it on Meiji-dori in Shibuya 3-chome, a stated 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station, consistent with the Shibuya Scramble target radius.
neighborhood: 渋谷
seats_text: 23席（カウンター7席、テーブル16席）
vegan_detail: The menu is built around chicken (chicken-fat-cooked rice, chicken broth) and pork (bak kut teh) dishes typical of Singaporean-Chinese cuisine; no vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or mention of avoiding fish sauce/oyster sauce/dashi was found in any source. Not a suitable choice for vegan diners based on available information.

lead: かのや 新宿西口店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-16-11

bio.background: Kanoya (かのや) is a small operator of stand-and-eat (tachigui) noodle counters clustered around Shinjuku — the Nishiguchi (west exit) and Tonan-guchi (south-east exit) shops — plus a Shimbashi location, all making their own fresh soba and thick Sanuki-style udon in house rather than buying pre-made noodles.
bio.specialty: Tempura udon — a thick hand-made Sanuki-style udon topped with a large shrimp tempura — is the counter's most recommended order; kakiage soba, made with a sweet-onion kakiage, is another regular favorite.
bio.philosophy: The shop sources its flours deliberately for each noodle: buckwheat for the soba and Hokkaido wheat for the multi-hydration Sanuki-style udon. Soba is served in a dark Kanto-style broth and udon in a lighter Kansai-style broth as the default pairing, but customers can ask staff to swap the broth style.

anecdotes.text: Kanoya runs a handful of self-made-noodle tachigui soba shops around Shinjuku (Nishiguchi and Tonan-guchi) and Shimbashi, each turning out its own fresh soba and thick udon.
Soba is standard with a Kanto-style (dark) broth and udon with a Kansai-style (light) broth, but the broth style can be requested to change.
The shop's most recommended dish is the tempura udon with a generously sized shrimp tempura on top; the kakiage udon, with its sweet-onion kakiage, is also popular.

caveats: Direct WebFetch/curl access to Tabelog, Hotpepper, and the shop's own sites was blocked by an environment-wide network outage during verification (repeated 403s on CONNECT to essentially all external hosts, including plain google.com). All facts below come from WebSearch result snippets that quote/summarize those same real pages (Tabelog, Hotpepper, k-trust-kanoya.com, kanoya-g.jp, soba-machichuka blog, udon-repo.net), not from independently fetched full pages, so treat sourcing as slightly less rigorous than a direct fetch. No page found any mention of gluten-free or vegan accommodation for this shop — it is a traditional tachigui soba/udon counter with no dietary-restriction focus, so GF/vegan claims here are inferred from standard soba-shop practice (wheat-containing shoyu-based dashi, mixed-flour soba, shared tempura fryer), not from an explicit shop statement.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular/occasional closures; otherwise open daily per listed hours) — some directory listings instead show 無休 (no regular closed day); exact policy not confirmed beyond these secondary listings.
cuisine: そば・うどん (Soba/Udon) — stand-up (tachigui) style
cultural_comfort_note: Order via a Japanese-only ticket machine (食券機) and hand the ticket to counter staff — no English menu was found, so a little Japanese (or menu photos) helps.
geocode_note: Multiple independent listings (Tabelog, Hotpepper, NAVITIME, Yahoo Map) agree on 東京都新宿区西新宿1-16-11, described as a 2-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit — well within the Shinjuku Station (35.6896,139.7006) 0.8km target radius. Network geocoding was not performed per instructions; lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding.
gf_detail: No gluten-free option or accommodation is advertised anywhere for this shop. It is a standard tachigui soba/udon counter: the udon is 100% wheat, the soba is a blended-flour soba (not guaranteed juwari/100%-buckwheat), and the tsuyu (dipping broth) is a classic katsuobushi-and-shoyu based sauce, so wheat is present via the soy sauce as well as the noodles themselves. Tempura items (shrimp tempura, kakiage) are almost certainly fried in a shared fryer used for all breaded items. This is not a suitable choice for celiac or strict gluten-avoidant diners.
name_ja: かのや 新宿西口店
neighborhood: 新宿西口 (Shinjuku Nishiguchi)
seats_text: Roughly 26 seats — a mix of standing/counter and some table seating (reviewers note it's roomier inside than the stand-up exterior suggests).
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling was found. As a traditional soba/udon tachigui shop, the standard tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito) based, and tempura toppings include shrimp and other non-vegan items. There is no indication of a kombu-only or plant-based dashi option, so vegan diners should assume hidden fish stock in the broth even for a plain zaru/mori order.

lead: 炙り味噌らーめん 麺匠 真武咲弥
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-10-3

bio.origin: Sapporo, Hokkaido (the shop's miso-ramen style and secret tare originate from Sapporo; this is the Shibuya branch of the Samurai Noodle company)
bio.background: Owner/company president Okumura Munehiro developed a wheat allergy himself, which pushed him to create ramen that anyone could eat 'regardless of religion, constitution, or dietary belief' -- leading to the shop's gluten-free brown-rice noodle and vegan ramen lines alongside the standard menu. An earlier profile of the company also recounts Okumura rebuilding from an approximately 8-million-yen bankruptcy, working as a truck driver to save capital before eventually opening the ramen business that grew into today's shop.
bio.specialty: Aburi Miso Ramen (炙り味噌ラーメン) -- a secret miso tare blended from thirteen ingredients, seared (aburi) over high heat, then combined with a clear (chintan) soup for a charred, aromatic, umami-rich bowl in a Sapporo-style stir-fried-vegetable tradition.
bio.philosophy: Making a bowl of ramen that anyone can eat, whatever their religion, allergy, or food philosophy -- the stated reason for offering dedicated gluten-free brown-rice noodles and a full vegan ramen line alongside the standard wheat-noodle, non-vegan menu.

anecdotes.text: 奥村社長は「生まれて初めてラーメンを食べられて、感動したと言ってくれる人もいる。多くの人に届けばうれしい」と話している (Okumura has said some customers tell him it was the first time in their life they were able to eat ramen at all, and were moved -- he hopes the food reaches more people like them).

Company/founder background piece describing Okumura's roughly 8-million-yen bankruptcy, his period working as a truck driver to rebuild capital, and the eventual founding of the Samurai Noodle aburi-miso ramen business.

caveats: Hours vary noticeably by day and by source (compare NAVITIME's per-day breakdown vs. other aggregator listings); confirm current hours before a late-night visit. Payment methods (cash vs. card at the food-ticket vending machine) could not be independently confirmed. Whether a dedicated fryer is used for the gluten-free karaage, and full detail on cross-contact handling in the shared kitchen, were not confirmed from available sources -- call ahead (03-6416-3778) if celiac-level strictness is required.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular/no fixed closed day reported)
cuisine: ラーメン（炙り味噌ラーメン / Aburi Miso Ramen）
cultural_comfort_note: A food-ticket vending-machine ramen shop typical of Japan, but the operator publishes an English-language site and the shop is well known among Tokyo's gluten-free/vegan community, so basic English or pointing at photos should get you through ordering.
gf_detail: Offers a dedicated gluten-free menu built on brown-rice (genmai) noodles sourced from a pesticide/chemical-fertilizer-free producer (Miyauchi-sha) in Unnan, Shimane -- e.g. "Aburi GF Genmai Noodle" ramen (from roughly ¥1,250-1,600 depending on flavor). The shop also developed a gluten-free karaage (fried chicken) and places wheat-free soy sauce at every table specifically as an allergy accommodation. However, this is a shared kitchen that also cooks standard wheat-noodle ramen and other wheat-containing items, and no source confirms a fryer used exclusively for gluten-free items, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out for celiac-level sensitivity -- ask staff about current allergy protocols.
hours_raw: Sources differ slightly; NAVITIME lists: Mon 11:00-翌2:30, Tue/Wed/Thu/Sun 11:00-翌4:30, Fri/Sat 11:00-翌6:30. Other aggregators give slightly different late-night cutoffs (e.g. Sun-Thu to 翌25:00/翌4:00-ish, Fri-Sat/pre-holiday to 翌7:00). Closed days reported as 不定休 (irregular).
name_en: Aburi Miso Ramen Mensho Shinbu Sakiya (Shibuya)
name_ja: 炙り味噌らーめん 麺匠 真武咲弥 渋谷店
neighborhood: Dogenzaka 2-chome, Shibuya (about 5 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source confirms separate cooking equipment/water for gluten-free noodles versus the shop's standard wheat noodles; the kitchen prepares both on the same menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 各席に小麦不使用のしょうゆを置くなどアレルギーに配慮 (Wheat-free soy sauce is placed at every table as an allergy accommodation).

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner developed the gluten-free noodle, gluten-free karaage, and vegan ramen lines specifically so customers with wheat allergies or dietary restrictions could eat there; some customers have told him it was the first ramen they were ever able to eat.

positives.text: Dedicated gluten-free brown-rice (genmai) noodle option made from noodles sourced from a pesticide/chemical-fertilizer-free producer in Shimane Prefecture; a blogger specializing in gluten-free living in Japan wrote it up as a standout GF ramen find in Shibuya.
Four flavors of dedicated vegan ramen (miso, spicy miso, shoyu, shio) at a uniform price, documented by a vegan-focused dining blog.

seats_text: Approximately 28 seats across counter and table seating on the 1st and 2nd floors.
vegan_detail: A dedicated vegan ramen line is offered in four flavors -- miso, spicy miso (kara-miso), shoyu, and shio -- at a uniform price (around ¥1,250, or about ¥1,550 with the gluten-free brown-rice noodle upgrade). This sits alongside the shop's standard, non-vegan menu (which includes pork and other animal-derived ingredients), so it is not an all-vegan restaurant -- diners must specifically order from the vegan menu. No source directly confirmed the vegan broth's full ingredient list (e.g. whether it is purely kombu/vegetable-based with zero risk of shared-broth cross-contact), so very strict vegans should ask staff to confirm preparation.

lead: 神田神保町 天麩羅 はちまき
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-19

bio.origin: Founded near Kanda Station, Chiyoda City, Tokyo; relocated to its current Jimbocho (Suzuran-dori) location in 1945 after the original shop was destroyed in WWII air raids
bio.background: Founded in Showa 6 (1931) by Aoki Torakichi, who had trained at the kappo restaurant "Uoyasu" in Kanda. When Uoyasu closed following the Great Kanto Earthquake, Aoki went independent and opened his own tempura shop near Kanda Station. That original shop burned down in the Tokyo air raids of WWII, and from 1945 the restaurant has operated continuously at its present site on Suzuran-dori in Jimbocho.
bio.specialty: 穴子海老天丼 (Anago-Ebi Tendon) - a rice bowl topped with two pieces of shrimp tempura, one whole conger eel (anago) tempura, and two pieces of seasonal vegetable tempura, served with miso soup and dressed in a long-simmered tare sauce built on bonito and kelp dashi.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name, "Hachimaki" (headband), comes from words the founder's mentor at Uoyasu gave him when he went independent: that he must work his whole life with a hachimaki tied on, forever training and studying his craft - a philosophy of lifelong dedication that the restaurant has kept as its guiding ethos for over 90 years.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was a favorite haunt of Showa-era literary and stage giants, including mystery writer Edogawa Ranpo, novelist Ibuse Masuji, playwright Hojo Hideji, and actor Sano Shuji, who all frequented the shop.
Edogawa Ranpo was especially fond of the anago-ebi tendon, the dish credited with hooking generations of regulars and still the restaurant's signature order today.

caveats: Traditional Edo-style tempura counter: batter is wheat-flour based and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is soy-sauce based, with no gluten-free or vegan accommodation documented anywhere. Lunch tendon is very affordable (roughly ¥800-1,500) but the evening course ("すずらんコース") runs ¥5,000-5,999, so overall price point varies a lot between lunch and dinner. Credit cards (VISA) are accepted only on purchases over ¥5,000; smaller tabs, especially at lunch, are often cash or PayPay only.
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesday)
cuisine: 天ぷら（江戸前天丼・穴子海老天丼）/ Edo-style tempura, tempura rice bowls
cultural_comfort_note: A 90+ year old, mostly Japanese-language neighborhood tempura counter near Jimbocho Station; there is no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so celiac and vegan diners should be prepared to explain dietary needs in Japanese or with a translation card.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-19 on Suzuran-dori, about 3-5 minutes on foot from both Jimbocho Station (Hanzomon/Toei Shinjuku lines) and Suidobashi Station, placing it within roughly 0.7km of the Suidobashi Station target center. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; geocoding from address_ja is to be handled downstream.
gf_detail: All tempura here is prepared with a traditional wheat-flour batter, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is a classic soy-sauce-based sauce with bonito/kelp dashi - not wheat-free. No source (official site, Tabelog, or food blogs) mentions a gluten-free batter, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free tare. This is not a safe choice for a celiac diner without extensive advance discussion with staff, and likely not accommodable at all given the shop's traditional, unchanged recipe.
name_ja: 神田神保町 天麩羅 はちまき
neighborhood: 千代田区神田神保町（すずらん通り沿い）/ Jimbocho, Chiyoda City
seats_text: 33 seats total (counter and table seating)
vegan_detail: The signature dishes center on anago (conger eel) and ebi (shrimp) tempura, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is built on bonito (katsuo) and kelp dashi. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based tare, or kombu-only dashi option are mentioned in any source - this is a seafood-centered tempura restaurant with no documented vegan pathway.

address_ja: 東京都台東区花川戸1-10-3

bio.specialty: Onigirazu (hand-formed, mold-free rice sandwiches) and onigiri — the shop is a small specialty stand dedicated entirely to these two rice-ball formats.

caveats: No dedicated gluten-free or vegan menu/certification could be confirmed from any source. This is a traditional Japanese onigiri/onigirazu takeout stand — fillings observed in public listings and social media (spam, tuna, eel/unagi) are not vegan, and typical Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, tsukudani-style glazes) usually contain wheat, though this shop's exact recipes were not documented in any source found. Diners with celiac disease or severe allergies should not rely on this listing and must ask staff directly in Japanese before ordering. No seating count, price range, or payment-method details could be independently verified.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (火・水定休)
cuisine: Onigirazu (rice sandwich) and onigiri specialty shop
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood takeout stand with no evidence of English menus or English-speaking staff; bring a translation app or point at photos.
gf_detail: No allergen or gluten-free information is published by the shop. Plain rice and nori are naturally gluten-free, but common onigiri/onigirazu fillings and glazes in Japan (soy sauce, tsukudani, teriyaki-style sauces) typically contain wheat, and this shop's specific recipes, sauce ingredients, and cross-contamination practices were not documented in any source found. Diners must ask staff directly about individual filling ingredients before ordering; no dedicated gluten-free process or fryer was confirmed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address confirmed as 台東区花川戸1-10-3, roughly 30 seconds' walk from Tobu Asakusa Station's north exit, which is immediately adjacent to Sensō-ji/Asakusa — well within the 0.8km Sensōji target radius.
hours_raw: 7:30〜16:00頃（売り切れ次第終了）、定休日：火曜・水曜
neighborhood: Asakusa (Hanakawado), Taito-ku
seats_text: Not confirmed by any source; the shop appears to be a small takeout-focused stand rather than a sit-down restaurant, but no explicit seating count was found.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options are advertised. Publicly visible fillings/products referenced in listings and social media include eel (unagi), spam, and tuna, indicating the shop's offerings are not vegan by default. No plant-based onigiri/onigirazu options were confirmed in any source, and typical Japanese seasonings may contain hidden dashi (bonito) even in vegetable-appearing fillings. Diners must ask staff directly.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町3-12 モノステップ2 1F

bio.background: Owner Nukui Hiroshi spent roughly ten years training at ramen shops around Tokyo before going independent, opening his first shop in Setagaya's Kamimachi neighborhood in July 2010. He relocated to the current Udagawa-cho, Shibuya location in November 2014, where he now runs the small counter-only shop.
bio.specialty: Tsukemen: chewy medium-thick noodles made with Hokkaido wheat flour and Okinawan salt, served with a rich dipping broth that blends pork-bone stock with a cold-brewed bonito, mackerel, and dried-sardine seafood stock, rested overnight and reduced for concentrated depth.
bio.philosophy: Aims for a thick, deeply savory dipping broth that is intentionally restrained on sweetness and acidity, so it works even for diners who dislike sweeter or sharper tsukemen styles.

anecdotes.text: Rice and a raw egg are offered free with meals, letting diners make tamago-kake-gohan (egg over rice) or stir the egg into their noodles or broth as they like.
The shop also serves craft beer, positioning itself as a relaxed 'ra-nomi' (ramen + drinking) spot to pair the rich tsukemen with a beer.
At lunchtime the shop has offered a limited Jiro-inspired ramen known as 'Nukaji-ro' as a special item.

caveats: This is a traditional wheat-noodle, pork-bone/seafood-dashi tsukemen shop with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation — included in the sweep for area/verification purposes but not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. Hours vary somewhat by source and the shop may close early if sold out, so hours_week is a best reconstruction from secondary listings, not a directly confirmed Tabelog fetch (Tabelog blocked direct access; used indexed search results instead).
closed_days: Sunday (定休日: 日曜日)
cuisine: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) / Ramen
cultural_comfort_note: Japanese-language, cash-only counter shop with no confirmed English menu or signage — bring a translation app or be ready to point at photos.
gf_detail: Noodles are made from wheat flour (Hokkaido-produced) and the broth is a conventional pork-bone and bonito/mackerel/dried-sardine seafood stock finished with soy sauce (shoyu) — standard wheat-based tsukemen. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or allergy menu was found in any source; this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Udagawa-cho, Shibuya-ku, roughly a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding from address_ja required.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: lunch 11:00–16:00, dinner 18:00–21:00 (some listings show 18:00–22:00); Sat/holidays: 11:00–17:00 (lunch only, no dinner service); closed Sundays. May close early ("売り切れ次第終了") if the broth sells out.
neighborhood: Udagawa-cho, Shibuya

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen noodles are made with wheat flour and the dipping broth is a conventional soy-sauce-seasoned pork-bone and seafood stock — no gluten-free noodle or broth alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dipping broth combines pork-bone stock with bonito, mackerel, and dried-sardine (niboshi) fish stock, so both the tsukemen and ramen broths are animal-based with no vegan version noted.

seats_text: Small counter seating for about 8 guests
vegan_detail: Both ramen and tsukemen broths are built on pork bone plus bonito/mackerel/dried-sardine fish stock (katsuobushi, sababushi, niboshi), and the free egg/rice service is not vegan either. No vegan broth, noodle, or dish substitution was found in any source — not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-22-17 sequence SUIDOBASHI 1F

bio.background: KANPAI TERRACE is the all-day dining restaurant on the ground floor of sequence SUIDOBASHI, a lifestyle hotel run by Mitsui Fudosan Hotel Management, one minute from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit. It opened alongside the hotel and underwent a full renovation of its dining room and riverside terrace, reopening on March 1, 2024, with a further refresh of the terrace zone (a daytime co-working terrace that turns into an evening beer garden) in mid-2025.
bio.specialty: Truffle and pancetta carbonara finished tableside with a giant wheel of cheese

anecdotes.text: The terrace, which runs along the Kanda River, was redesigned with bench seating, tables, and a green-roofed area, and relaunched with a special reopening course menu in March 2024.
In 2025 the terrace was reworked again to function as a 'working terrace' during the day and a beer garden in the evening, with the lunch menu also renewed.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (menu, staff protocol, or dedicated fryer) was found on any source consulted; the kitchen appears to be a standard Italian all-day dining menu built around wheat pasta, pizza, bread, and dairy, so this listing relies on direct staff inquiry rather than confirmed accommodations. Full restaurant pages (Tabelog, Ikyu, OZmall, Gnavi, official hotel site) returned 403 errors when fetched directly in this environment; all facts below come from WebSearch result summaries of those pages rather than direct page reads, so some granular details (exact phone number, full course pricing) could not be independently confirmed beyond the search snippets.
closed_days: None stated — open every day (無休)
cuisine: Italian Dining Bar / All-Day Dining
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside an international lifestyle hotel a minute from Suidobashi Station, with an English-language Tabelog listing, suggesting reasonable comfort for non-Japanese-speaking guests, though this was not independently confirmed via direct menu access.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding blocked in this environment); address captured as 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-22-17 sequence SUIDOBASHI 1F. Multiple independent sources describe the restaurant as 30 seconds to 1 minute on foot from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, placing it well within 0.8km of Suidōbashi Station (35.7017, 139.7539).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, gluten-free labeling, or dedicated-fryer information was found on any source. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes (carbonara and other cream pastas, pizza, croque monsieur, bread-based breakfast items), so a celiac or strictly gluten-free diner should not assume safety and must ask staff directly about ingredients and cross-contact before ordering.
hours_raw: BREAKFAST 7:00-12:00 (L.O. 11:30) / LUNCH 11:30-14:00 / CAFE TIME 14:00-17:00 / BAR & DINNER 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); overall open 7:00-23:00 daily, no closed days

Signature items are dairy- and meat-forward (cream-based carbonara with cheese, eggs benedict, meat platters like Angus beef tagliata), and hidden animal-derived ingredients (butter, cream, cheese, egg, possibly anchovy/dashi in sauces) should be assumed unless staff confirm otherwise. Plant-based diners should ask about substitutions before ordering.

lead: サラダボウル専門店 ウィズグリーン 新宿三丁目店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-1-17 山本ビル1F

bio.origin: WithGreen was founded in Tokyo in 2016 by CEO Tomohiro Takefumi, on the idea that a salad bowl could be a full meal on its own rather than a side dish.
bio.background: WithGreen is a made-to-order salad bowl chain that has grown from its 2016 Tokyo debut to roughly 40+ locations nationwide, including this Shinjuku Sanchome branch a one-minute walk from the station's A2 exit.
bio.specialty: The Original Salad — a build-your-own bowl where diners pick from whichever vegetables are in peak season, sourced from contracted farms across Japan, plus a rotating seasonal salad introduced every two months.
bio.philosophy: The shop's guiding idea is that a salad should be substantial enough to be your main meal ("主食になるサラダボウル"), built around domestically grown, in-season produce rather than imported filler.
roles: Salad bowl specialty chain, Shinjuku Sanchome branch

anecdotes.text: Since its founding, aside from exceptional circumstances like typhoons or natural disasters, WithGreen has made a point of using only domestically grown Japanese vegetables, changing its seasonal limited salad every two months to showcase Japan's four seasons.
WithGreen has continued opening new locations across Japan, expanding well past its original Tokyo shops into cities including Nagoya and Osaka.

caveats: Sources conflict on payment method: one aggregated listing indicated electronic payment (PayPay) is accepted, while another (likely reflecting Tabelog's own payment-method field) states cards and e-money are both not accepted, implying cash only — treated as cash_only here but this should be reconfirmed on-site. No dedicated gluten-free labeling, allergy chart, or cross-contamination statement was found for this location; GF and vegan guidance below is inferred from general menu customization, not a documented allergy policy. Direct page fetches (Tabelog, official withgreen.club, gnavi, aumo, hitosara, mamaco, Yahoo Map) returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all facts are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than full page reads.
closed_days: Open year-round except during the New Year's holiday period (年末年始のみ休み)
cuisine: Salad bowl specialty shop — customizable vegetable salad bowls built from seasonal, mostly domestically-grown produce, with optional chicken/protein toppings and a rotating seasonal salad
cultural_comfort_note: A fast-casual, order-at-counter salad shop where you point to and select ingredients, so basic Japanese or pointing at the visual menu is enough even without fluent Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer, or allergy chart was found for this location. Bowls are built to order from raw/grilled vegetables, but pre-made dressings, sauces, and any breaded or soy-sauce-based toppings could contain wheat; celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should ask staff to check individual dressing and sauce ingredients before ordering.
hours_raw: 11:00〜20:00（年中無休、年末年始を除く）
name_ja: サラダボウル専門店 ウィズグリーン 新宿三丁目店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目

positives.text: The shop offers a vegetarian/vegan-compatible dressing option (e.g. a lemon-based dressing), and diners can build a fully vegetable bowl by choosing their own toppings.

seats_text: 16 seats
vegan_detail: Diners can build a vegetable-only bowl themselves by choosing toppings, and at least one dressing (a lemon-based dressing) is marked as vegetarian/vegan-compatible. However, most standard menu bowls (e.g. Spicy Chicken Cobb Salad, sesame chicken salad) are built around chicken, and whether other dressings/sauces contain dashi, bonito, or other animal-derived ingredients is not disclosed anywhere found — vegan diners should confirm sauce and dressing ingredients with staff before ordering.

bio.origin: Nagano (Shinshū) soba tradition, opened in Shibuya's Dogenzaka in 1986
bio.background: Sobadokoro Shinshuya (そば処 信州屋) opened on Dogenzaka in Shibuya in 1986 as a standing/fast soba-and-tempura counter aimed at busy office workers a minute's walk from Shibuya Station. It is operated by a local Tokyo restaurant company with roots going back over a century in neighborhood eateries, and Shinshuya carries on that tradition with a stone-mill-ground Shinshū (Nagano) buckwheat flour milled and made into noodles in house.
bio.specialty: Freshly hand-made soba noodles topped with tempura that is fried to order at the counter as customers watch; the shop's motto is 打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて ("freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried"). A popular time-limited morning set (until around 11am) pairs a bowl of mori or kake soba with rice and a raw egg, which regulars mix with the tempura dipping sauce and tenkasu (tempura scraps).
bio.philosophy: 打ちたて、茹でたて、揚げたて — an emphasis on serving everything made fresh to order, quickly and at very low prices, for time-pressed commuters near Shibuya Station.

anecdotes.text: The soba is served in hand-painted Arita-yaki porcelain bowls decorated with the traditional 唐子 (karako) pattern, while everything else at the shop is prepared in house.
The shop runs a budget morning set (soba + rice + raw egg) available until around 11am, popular with local office workers who mix the egg into the tempura dipping sauce or over rice with tenkasu.

caveats: Secondary sources give slightly conflicting weekend closing times (some list Sat/Sun both to 23:00, others list Sun/holiday closing at 22:00 with Sat matching weekdays); the schedule here reflects the most commonly repeated version. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found in any source — this is a traditional wheat-blended soba/tempura counter, not a GF or vegan-oriented restaurant, so it is included here as a MOM_AND_POP local institution rather than for dietary specialization. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should not rely on this spot without asking staff directly (Japanese needed).
closed_days: None stated — reported as 無休 (open every day of the year)
cuisine: そば・天ぷら (standing/fast soba noodles and tempura)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional standing soba counter where an attendant takes your order verbally (no ticket machine, and menus/interactions are primarily in Japanese), so some Japanese ability or pointing/translation help is useful.
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address (道玄坂2-6-4, one minute from Shibuya Station) places it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target landmark.
gf_detail: No source indicates this shop offers gluten-free accommodation. The soba is not advertised as 十割 (100% buckwheat); as a standard machine/hand-made soba counter it should be assumed to use a wheat-flour blend in the noodles. The tempura is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter, and traditional soba tsuyu (dipping sauce) is soy-sauce based and typically contains wheat. No mention of a dedicated gluten-free fryer, GF noodles, or allergy menu was found in any reviewed source. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 平日 7:30〜23:00、土日 9:00〜23:00（一部情報源では日・祝は9:00〜22:00）、無休 — Weekdays 7:30am-11pm, weekends from 9am (closing time reported as either 11pm or 10pm on Sunday/holidays depending on source); open every day.
name_ja: そば処 信州屋 渋谷店
neighborhood: Dogenzaka, Shibuya (道玄坂, 渋谷) — one minute on foot from Shibuya Station
seats_text: About 10 counter seats plus additional standing-room space at the counter
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or accommodation was found. As a traditional soba shop, the tsuyu (dipping/soup broth) should be assumed to use standard katsuobushi (bonito) dashi unless a customer confirms otherwise with staff, and the menu includes egg, fried tofu (kitsune), and other animal-derived items alongside soba. No source confirms a vegan broth or dedicated vegan options.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-13-1 田辺ビル1階

roles: Owner-chef trained at Ryōgoku's Michelin-listed soba restaurant Hosokawa (ほそ川)
bio.background: The chef trained at Ryōgoku's renowned soba restaurant Hosokawa, itself a Michelin-listed shop, before opening Rokujoan near Suidōbashi Station in November 2021. Within about a year of opening, Rokujoan itself earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing.
bio.specialty: Stone-mill ground, hand-cut soba, including a coarsely-milled nihachi (buckwheat/wheat blend) noodle, alongside a shrimp tempura rice bowl (ebi tendon).

anecdotes.text: One food blogger highlighted a summer dish of cold soba with Tokushima-grown sudachi citrus, myoga, and scallion, describing the citrus's refined acidity working well against the sharp bite of the aromatic vegetables.

caveats: Traditional neighborhood soba restaurant recognized with a Michelin Bib Gourmand; no gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source — the signature noodle is a wheat-containing nihachi (二八) blend rather than 100%-buckwheat juwari soba, and the tsuyu/dashi tradition here is bonito-based. Seating capacity, English-language support, and payment methods were not confirmed in available sources (Tabelog's own page could not be fetched directly due to a 403 block; details below are drawn from Gnavi, blog reviews, and search-indexed listings).
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cuisine: Handmade soba (Michelin Bib Gourmand)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, traditional local soba shop with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is recommended.
gf_detail: Traditional soba shop; the signature noodle found in menu listings is a coarsely-milled nihachi (二八) soba, which by definition includes wheat flour as a binder (roughly 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) rather than a 100%-buckwheat (juwari) noodle. The menu also features tempura (e.g., a shrimp tempura rice bowl), which is wheat-battered and typically fried in shared oil. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was reported in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Multiple independent listings (Gnavi, blog reviews, search-indexed directories) give the address as Chiyoda-ku, Misakicho 2-13-1, Tanabe Building 1F, and describe it as a 3-minute walk from Suidōbashi Station — well within the 0.8km target radius.
neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidōbashi)

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu listings show a coarsely-milled nihachi (二八) soba — a wheat/buckwheat blend, not 100%-buckwheat — plus a shrimp tempura rice bowl with a wheat-flour batter, indicating standard shared-kitchen preparation rather than gluten-free handling.

seats_text: Seating capacity not specified in available sources; it is a small, counter-oriented handmade-soba shop.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-izakaya menu built around a bonito (katsuobushi) dashi tradition for the tsuyu dipping sauce, plus shrimp tempura and other seafood dishes. No vegan-specific dishes, dashi substitutions, or plant-based menu options were reported in any source.

lead: らーめん 鴨to葱 イートルミネ新宿店
address_ja: 〒160-0022 東京都新宿区新宿3-38-2 EATo(イイトルミネ)新宿 B1F

bio.origin: The original shop opened in Okachimachi (Ueno), Tokyo, under the JR railway overpass in the Ameyoko area; this Shinjuku branch (inside JR Shinjuku Station's EATo Lumine complex) opened in April 2024 as part of the brand's expansion.
bio.specialty: 鴨ラーメン (duck ramen) — a clear (清湯) broth made from only three ingredients: crossbred duck, green onion (negi), and water, slow-simmered for nearly two days.
bio.philosophy: Founder Amado Masayasu has said: "We make our soup using only duck, green onions, and water. We slowly simmer carefully selected crossbred duck for nearly two days on low heat. The duck's natural umami is extracted, and the green onion adds to the depth of flavor. Since we don't use chemical seasonings (no added MSG), achieving that balance is difficult."
bio.background: Kamo to Negi built its reputation at its original Okachimachi counter shop, known for lines of roughly 40 customers before opening on weekends, and for rotating the variety/origin of its green onions monthly (diners often choose 2 of 3 types on offer). It has since expanded to several Tokyo locations (Shinjuku, Shibuya, Asakusa, Sugamo) and Umeda in Osaka, with the Shinjuku Station branch offering a quick counter-service format for commuters.

anecdotes.text: On weekends, roughly 40 customers line up outside the original Okachimachi shop before it opens.
The shop's soup is made from just duck, negi, and water, simmered nearly two days, with no chemical seasoning added, per the founder.

caveats: Most source pages (Tabelog, official kamotonegi.com store page, several ramen blogs) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets that quote/summarize those same pages (Tabelog EN listing, official kamotonegi.com/en, ramendb, san-tatsu, favy, ramental.net, and the brand's own X/Twitter announcement of an hours change). The target name given was "イートルミネ新宿店" but every independent source confirms the correct katakana is "イイトルミネ新宿店" (EATo Lumine) — same Shinjuku Station location, just a rendering difference. No source discusses gluten-free or vegan accommodation for this shop; the broth is entirely duck-based (not vegan) and standard wheat ramen noodles/likely wheat-containing shoyu tare are used (not gluten-free), so this listing is included as a standard omnivore ramen spot rather than a GF/vegan pick. Cash-only status was not explicitly confirmed by any source; marked as accepting cards based on it being a staffed counter inside a large modern train-station commercial facility (EATo Lumine), which is the norm for such venues, but this is an inference rather than a sourced fact.
closed_days: 無休 (open every day, including holidays)
cuisine: 鴨ラーメン専門店 — 鴨(合鴨)・葱・水のみで炊いた清湯スープの鴨らーめん、鴨汁つけそば (Duck-ramen specialty shop; clear broth made from only duck, green onion, and water)
cultural_comfort_note: A fast-turnover station counter shop (14 seats) with an official English-language brand page, so basic English/picture menu support is likely, but expect a Japanese-first, ticket-and-counter ramen-shop experience with little English conversation.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options are indicated by any source. This is a standard ramen shop: wheat noodles are used, and the seasoning tare is very likely soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which typically contains wheat. No mention anywhere of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or allergen-safe handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Address places the shop inside EATo Lumine (B1F), a commercial facility built into/adjoining the ticket-gate area of JR Shinjuku Station itself — this is essentially at the Shinjuku Station landmark coordinate, well within the 0.8km radius.
hours_raw: 7:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), 年中無休 (open daily, no closed days)
name_en: Ramen Kamo to Negi - EATo Lumine Shinjuku
name_ja: らーめん 鴨to葱 イイトルミネ新宿店
neighborhood: 新宿駅構内 (JR Shinjuku Station, EATo Lumine B1F) / 新宿区新宿
seats_text: 14 counter seats
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly: the signature broth is made purely from duck, green onion, and water (no vegetable/kombu-only broth offered), so it contains meat-derived stock by design. No plant-based or vegetarian noodle-soup option is mentioned by any source.

lead: ヴィーガン スシ トウキョウ (Vegan Sushi Tokyo)
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区松濤1-25-6 パークサイド松涛103

bio.chef_name: Kitchen supervised by vegetable-sushi pioneer Akira Tanimizu (per secondary press coverage)
bio.origin: Founded by a small team including Kazue Maeda and Shu Kudo, first opened June 2024 near Shibuya; moved to a permanent home in Shōtō (near Shinsen Station) December 1, 2025
bio.background: Opened as Japan's first specialized vegan sushi restaurant. Maeda, who became vegan several years earlier out of concern for animal welfare and the environment, and co-founder Shu Kudo built the restaurant around the idea that visiting vegans should not have to choose between sightseeing and eating well in Japan.
bio.specialty: Slow-cooked eggplant nigiri and plant-based gunkan (mock ikura, vegan egg) built entirely from vegetables and plant proteins
bio.philosophy: Rather than imitate seafood, the kitchen leans into vegetables' own umami and sweetness, using ingredients like miso and sesame for depth instead of fish-based dashi or bonito.

anecdotes.text: Co-founder Kazue Maeda became vegan out of concern for animal welfare and the environment; she and fellow founder Shu Kudo say they started the restaurant so that 'vegans can enjoy Japan 100%' instead of having to compromise between sightseeing plans and meals.

During development the team debated whether plant-based sushi should imitate seafood or build its own sensory language, ultimately choosing to amplify vegetables' natural umami and sweetness with miso and sesame rather than mimic fish.

caveats: Tabelog and the restaurant's own site returned 403/blocked to direct fetch in this environment, so this record is built from search-engine summaries of secondary sources (aggregators, press, blogs) rather than a direct read of the primary pages. The restaurant relocated from an original Dōgenzaka location (open since June 2024, lunch-focused) to this Shōtō/Shinsen address on Dec 1, 2025; some cited menu/price/payment details (e.g., the ~¥3,000–5,000 set pricing, card-only payment) come from reviews of the earlier location and may not be fully current for the new address. Hours and closed-day information conflict slightly across sources (some show lunch+dinner, others dinner-only 18:00–22:00, some note "irregular holidays") — confirm current hours/closed days directly with the restaurant or its TableCheck page before visiting. No explicit statement about gluten-free protocols (dedicated fryer, wheat-free soy sauce) for this location was found in any source.
closed_days: No single fixed weekly closed day confirmed across sources; some describe daily dinner service, others note irregular holidays — verify current status before visiting.
cuisine: Vegan Sushi
cultural_comfort_note: Bookings run through an English-language TableCheck reservation page and the restaurant is regularly covered by English-language Tokyo food/travel blogs, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking visitors to book and dine.
gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free equipment, a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce, or a dedicated fryer at this restaurant. Being 100% vegan removes animal-based risks, but the menu includes items like tempura-style "fried shrimp" and sushi typically finished with soy sauce, which in Japan is usually wheat-based unless specified otherwise — celiac diners should confirm ingredients directly with staff before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; lat/lng left null. Address captured as reported by secondary sources (restaurant listing aggregators) for the Shōtō/Shinsen location that opened Dec 1, 2025.
hours_raw: Reported as 18:00–22:00 daily (dinner, reservation-based) at the current Shōtō address since its Dec 1, 2025 opening; some sources also list lunch hours (approx. 11:00–15:30). Treat with caution — see caveats.
name_ja: ヴィーガン スシ トウキョウ
neighborhood: 神泉 (Shinsen) / 松濤 (Shōtō), Shibuya

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen and menu are vegan — no fish, meat, egg, or dairy is used anywhere on the menu, so there is no risk of cross-contact with animal products for vegan diners.

positives.text: Described by reviewers as Japan's first specialized vegan sushi restaurant, built specifically so vegan visitors don't have to compromise on traditional sushi experiences.

seats_text: Small, intimate space (exact seat count not published in available sources)
vegan_detail: Fully vegan menu — all sushi, sides, and desserts are plant-based, using vegetable and soy/plant-protein substitutes (e.g., mock ikura, vegan egg gunkan, slow-cooked eggplant nigiri) with no fish, meat, dairy, or fish-based dashi/bonito anywhere on the menu.

lead: ベジフォンデュ 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町12-7 渋谷エメラルドビル401（住所は二次情報源による未確定情報）
caveats: This listing could be confirmed to exist (matching name, category, and Shibuya location) via its Tabelog "en" listing URL and an independent Tripadvisor listing ("VEGI FONDUE SHIBUYA"), but every attempt to actually open the Tabelog page, the Tripadvisor page, or any blog/press coverage was blocked (HTTP 403) in this environment, including via a text-proxy fallback. No independently fetched page content was obtained, so hours, seat count, price, menu specifics, dietary safety practices, and current-operating status are UNCONFIRMED — they are not included or are left as honest unknowns rather than invented. The absence of a "【閉店】" (closed) marker in the restaurant's title across many repeated search-engine snippets (unlike comparable Shibuya listings that do show this marker when defunct, e.g. "【閉店】beacon") is weak circumstantial evidence the listing is still active, but this is not a confirmed fact. address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-7-11 北村ビル1F

roles: dessert flagship of the Musashi-Koyama patisserie "patisserie de bon cœur"
bio.background: DIXANS (written "10 DIXANS", read ディゾン) is the flagship café of the Musashi-Koyama pâtisserie "patisserie de bon cœur." It was opened to mark the pâtisserie's 10th anniversary — the name is a play on the French "dix ans," meaning "ten years."
bio.origin: Parent pâtisserie based in Musashi-Koyama; this café location sits in Nishi-Kanda, a short walk from Suidōbashi Station.
bio.specialty: Thick-cut toast (roughly 4cm) with a crisp, caramelized crust and a soft, pillowy crumb, served alongside cakes supplied by patisserie de bon cœur such as the Gâteau Pistache Fraise and Mont Blanc.
bio.philosophy: The toast's unusual thickness is a deliberate design choice: when a loaf is halved to share between two guests, both resulting slices come from the center of the loaf, so no one is stuck with the drier end piece.

anecdotes.text: The signature toast was not on the original menu — it was added after the café's team encountered a brown-sugar toast in Kichijōji and later worked with the pastry chefs of patisserie de bon cœur to develop their own version, refining the exact height so that halving a loaf gives every guest an identical, crust-free center cut.

caveats: No official-site fetch was possible (dizon.foodre.jp and several blogs returned 403 to automated fetch), so hours, seat count, and story details are drawn from Tabelog, the Chiyoda ward tourism site, and secondary blogs/search snippets rather than the restaurant's own site directly. No gluten-free or vegan menu, ingredient list, or allergy protocol was found in any source — this is a conventional wheat-and-dairy European-style café (toast, butter, patisserie cakes), not a place with confirmed allergy accommodations. Seat count (~30) is an approximate figure aggregated from visitor blog descriptions, not an official figure.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day; check the café's social media before visiting.
cuisine: Stylish European-influenced dessert café — signature thick-cut toast and patisserie cakes, coffee/latte program; Tabelog Café 100 (Tokyo East) 2025 selection
cultural_comfort_note: It's walk-in/first-come seating with a Japanese-language menu of toast, coffee, and cakes — simple to order with basic Japanese or by pointing at the display case; no confirmed English menu or signage.
geocode_note: Address confirmed via the Chiyoda ward tourism site, Tabelog, and multiple independent blogs as 東京都千代田区西神田2-7-11 北村ビル1F, described consistently as about a 5-minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station — within the 0.8km target radius of that landmark. Lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
gf_detail: The café's signature item is thick-cut wheat toast, and its cakes come from a conventional pâtisserie (patisserie de bon cœur). No gluten-free bread, dessert, or dedicated allergen-handling protocol was mentioned in any source reviewed (Tabelog, Chiyoda tourism page, cafe blogs). Not appropriate for celiac guests without direct confirmation from staff, and likely not accommodating given the wheat-centric concept.

lead: 京町恋しぐれ 新宿 本館
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-27-10 武蔵野会館6F

bio.background: 京町恋しぐれ 新宿 本館 is a large Kyoto-themed izakaya operated by the DD Holdings (Diamond Dining) restaurant group. The interior recreates a Kyoto machiya townscape inside a Shinjuku building, with a simulated river, stone paths, and several private-room styles, aiming to give guests "the feeling of a school trip to Kyoto" two minutes from JR Shinjuku Station.
bio.origin: Kyoto-style izakaya concept, Shinjuku, Tokyo (DD Holdings group restaurant)
bio.specialty: Obanzai (Kyoto home-style vegetable dishes) alongside namafu (wheat gluten cake) and yuba (tofu skin), served with a curated selection of Japanese sake, in Kyoto-townscape-themed private dining rooms.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes its aim as letting guests experience Kyoto's inherited food culture and flavors "with all five senses" without leaving Shinjuku.

anecdotes.text: 「京の町屋をモチーフにした店内には斬新な「和」の空間が特徴で、川のせせらぎや石畳など、古都の風情を感じながら...食文化とこだわりの地酒を心ゆくまで味わえます」(The interior, modeled on a Kyoto machiya, features an innovative 'wa' space with the sound of a flowing river and stone paths, letting guests savor the food culture and carefully chosen local sake amid the atmosphere of the old capital.)

「JR新宿駅から徒歩2分で、古き良き京都の町並みを再現した店内で、新宿にいながら、修学旅行で京都を訪れたような気分で楽しめる」(Two minutes on foot from JR Shinjuku Station, the interior recreates an old Kyoto townscape, letting guests feel as if on a school trip to Kyoto while still in Shinjuku.)

caveats: This is a large chain izakaya (part of the DD Holdings/Diamond Dining restaurant group), not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free establishment. Its signature 生麩 (namafu) dish is wheat gluten, i.e. NOT gluten-free. No vegan or GF-specific menu, allergy protocol, or dedicated fryer information was found in any source; celiac or vegan diners should not rely on this listing without asking staff directly, and it may not belong in a curated GF/vegan app.

cuisine: Kyoto-style izakaya (京風居酒屋) — obanzai, namafu (wheat gluten), yuba (tofu skin), and sake-focused menu; not a vegetarian or shojin restaurant despite some Kyoto vegetable dishes.
cultural_comfort_note: Large tourist-accessible chain izakaya with online reservation systems (Hotpepper, Tabelog English page) and a Tabelog English listing, but no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or a translation app is helpful.

Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, detailed confirmation from staff on-site.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神宮前6丁目20-10 RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK サウス 3F（VEGEGO MIYASHITA PARK）

bio.origin: VEGEGO is a Korean home-cooking chain brand operated by Tokyo restaurant company Kichiri Holdings; this branch sits in the food court of RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK, about a two-minute walk from Shibuya Station.
bio.background: The Miyashita Park location is one of roughly 20 VEGEGO branches nationwide. The Korean name for the concept, オヌレシクタン ('today's menu'), reflects the brand's set-meal format built around colorful, vegetable-heavy Korean home cooking rather than a single owner-chef's story.
bio.specialty: VEGEGO special stone-bowl bibimbap topped with six kinds of housemade namul (seasoned vegetable sides).
bio.philosophy: The brand's tagline is built around the idea that health and beauty come from enjoying delicious food every day, aiming to help diners eat more vegetables through housemade namul and mul-kimchi rather than through restrictive eating.
roles: Korean home-cooking food-court restaurant, part of the VEGEGO chain

anecdotes.text: A visitor blogging about the Shibuya branch described the stone-bowl bibimbap and Korean spread as feeling like a quick trip to Seoul.
A Tokyo dining column recommended the branch as a place to enjoy Korean home cooking as an affordable set meal near Shibuya Station.

caveats: Despite the "Vege" branding, this is an omnivorous Korean home-cooking chain (part of Kichiri Holdings, ~20 locations nationwide) — not a vegan or gluten-free-dedicated restaurant. It emphasizes vegetable-forward bibimbap and namul sides, but the wider menu is meat-heavy (yangnyeom fried chicken, cheese dakgalbi) and no source confirms a vegan or gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or allergy protocol. Seat count and exact reservation/payment policy could not be confirmed from public sources.
closed_days: No regular weekly closed days found; the store follows RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK facility operating days.
cuisine: 韓国料理（野菜中心・ビビンバ／Korean home cooking, bibimbap-focused）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual food-court counter inside RAYARD MIYASHITA PARK with picture-menu ordering, manageable for visitors with minimal Japanese, though no English-language allergy support is confirmed.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient labeling, or fryer information was found in any source. As standard Korean home cooking, the kitchen almost certainly uses soy sauce and gochujang (both traditionally wheat-based) and serves breaded/fried items like yangnyeom chicken, all prepared in a shared food-court kitchen with no mention of gluten-free protocols. Cross-contamination risk should be assumed; not suitable to rely on for celiac-level safety without asking staff directly (in Japanese) and getting specific reassurance.

Standard Korean pantry items like kimchi, gochujang, and broths frequently contain fish sauce, anchovy stock, or other animal-derived ingredients, and none of the available sources rule this out dish-by-dish, so a fully vegan order cannot be confirmed without asking staff on site.

The restaurant has a calm atmosphere with a warm wooden interior, typical of a neighborhood yakitori counter.
bio.specialty: Soy-sauce marinated liver skewer (レバー醤油漬け) — prized by reviewers for its creamy, melting texture and rich flavor.

A popular menu item is the liver soy-sauce marinade, which has a creamy, melting texture and rich flavor.

bio.background: Shinjuku Buan is run by M's Dining Co. (株式会社エムズダイニング), a small Tokyo hospitality group that operates a handful of Japanese-wine-centered bistros around the city (including sister shops in Kagurazaka and Nishi-Ogikubo), of which this Shinjuku Gyoenmae location is one.
bio.specialty: Casual French- and Italian-leaning bistro cooking built on morning-picked, pesticide-free vegetables from a contracted farm on the Miura Peninsula (Kanagawa) and fish landed daily at Sukumo Port in Kochi Prefecture; a lunchtime quiche is called out by name in diner reviews.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is to cook casual French and Italian dishes using Japanese ingredients and seasonings specifically to pair with Japanese wine, aiming to feel like an approachable, walk-in 'wine dining' spot rather than a formal restaurant — the walk-in wine cellar (around 300 varieties, 1,000+ bottles sourced from producers across Japan) is central to that identity.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer's visit is titled "キッシュが美味しい、葡庵のランチ" ("The quiche is delicious — lunch at Buan"), calling out the quiche as a lunch highlight.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan menu, protocol, or dedicated-fryer information is documented anywhere for this restaurant — it is a Japanese-ingredient French/Italian wine bar, not a diet-specialized spot. Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (50 vs 51). Confirm dietary accommodations and current hours directly by phone (03-3351-0141) before visiting.

No dedicated fryer or gluten-free prep area is documented. Diners with celiac disease should call ahead (03-3351-0141) to discuss specific ingredients before visiting.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-36-2

bio.background: 徳太樓 (Tokutaro) was founded in 1903 (Meiji 36) in Asakusa. Per multiple secondary sources, the founder came from a lower-ranking samurai family of the Akimoto clan from Tatebayashi domain, and after the Meiji Restoration trained in confectionery-making at a shop called Utaro, which had ties to the well-known Eitaro Sohonten confectioner in Nihonbashi. The shop has continued in the same neighborhood, in what locals call "oku-Asakusa" (the quieter area behind Senso-ji), ever since.
bio.specialty: Kintsuba — a rectangular sweet of simmered Tokachi (Hokkaido) azuki beans, cooked down and skimmed to a lightly sweetened filling, then hand-coated with a wheat-flour batter applied with a spatula (rather than dipped, as most kintsuba makers do) and baked so all six sides brown evenly on a copper griddle. The technique gives Tokutaro's kintsuba an unusually thick, chewy skin compared to other shops' versions. The shop reportedly makes over 1,000 pieces a day, and recommends eating them within about three days.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately old-fashioned, hand-technique approach — most notably the spatula-applied (rather than dipped) batter coating — that the shop has kept as a point of difference from other Tokyo kintsuba makers since the Meiji era.

anecdotes.text: A Tokyo Shimbun feature describes Tokutaro as a hidden, old-established kintsuba shop found in "oku-Asakusa," the quieter district behind Senso-ji, away from the main tourist crowds.
A gift-guide piece describes Tokutaro's kintsuba as a treasured hand-me-down gift within Asakusa's traditional hanamachi (geisha district) community.

caveats: Network geocoding is disabled in this environment (lat/lng left null by design). Additionally, this session's outbound WebFetch/curl access was globally blocked by the egress proxy for the entire research window (confirmed by testing unrelated control domains like wikipedia.org and google.com, which also returned 403), so no page could be directly fetched this session — every fact here comes from WebSearch result snippets/summaries across ~10 independent secondary sources (Tokyo Shimbun, shinise.tv, sushi-blog, asakusakanko.com, allabout.co.jp, etc.) rather than a single authoritative page (Tabelog listing itself and the official site tokutarou.shop could not be directly fetched). Hours (Mon-Fri 10:00-18:00, Sat 10:00-17:00, closed Sun/holidays) and the address (Asakusa 3-36-2) recur consistently across sources, giving reasonable confidence, but seat count, cash/card policy, and reservation policy were not found anywhere and are best-guess defaults. Distance to Senso-ji was judged qualitatively (not geocoded) from repeated descriptions of the shop as being in "oku-Asakusa," "behind Senso-ji," and 4-10 minutes on foot from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station / Ginza-line Asakusa Station / Tawaramachi Station — consistent with, but not certain to be inside, the 0.8km radius; treat the pin as approximate once geocoded downstream from the address.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: 和菓子・きんつば (traditional Japanese confectionery, kintsuba specialist)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-established neighborhood wagashi shop off the main tourist route in "oku-Asakusa," with no evidence of English signage, menus, or English-speaking staff in any source — expect a Japanese-only, locals'-shop experience.
gf_detail: The signature and dominant product, kintsuba, is defined by a wheat-flour (komugiko) batter that is hand-applied with a spatula to the surface of the azuki filling and then baked — wheat is intrinsic to the recipe, not an optional ingredient. No source mentions any gluten-free item, dedicated gluten-free process, or cross-contamination protocol. This is not a safe or accommodating choice for celiac or gluten-free diners.

Multiple independent sources place the shop in "oku-Asakusa," described as "behind Senso-ji" (浅草寺裏手の奥浅草), and give walking times of about 4-10 minutes from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station, Ginza-line Tawaramachi Station, and Ginza-line Asakusa Station — all of which are themselves within a few minutes of Senso-ji. This is consistent with, though not verified to be strictly inside, an 0.8km straight-line radius of Senso-ji (35.7148,139.7967); it is not clearly outside that radius, so found=true per instructions.

lead: 肉うどん 肉めし 甚三 渋谷本店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町20-3 アパホテル渋谷道玄坂上南館 1階

bio.origin: Kagawa-style (Sanuki) udon craft brought to Tokyo
bio.specialty: Niku udon (meat udon) and niku meshi (meat rice) — housemade Sanuki udon noodles in a dried-anchovy dashi broth, topped with pork
bio.philosophy: Source key ingredients — wheat flour, soy sauce, dashi kelp and sugar — directly from Kagawa and the Setouchi region to recreate an authentic Sanuki udon experience in Tokyo, paired with Chiba-sourced pork for the signature meat dishes.
bio.background: Jinza is a small Tokyo restaurant group built around bringing genuine Sanuki (Kagawa-style) udon to Tokyo. It now has several locations — Daimon, Shimbashi, Kanda, Takadanobaba and Shibuya — with the original Daimon shop repeatedly named to Tabelog's Top 100 restaurant list. The Shibuya (Dogenzaka-ue) shop opened on April 3, 2025, a short walk from Shinsen Station.

anecdotes.text: The shop sources its udon wheat flour, soy sauce, and dashi ingredients directly from Kagawa and the Setouchi region, using thick kelp from Naruto and specialty sugar from the long-established Kagawa maker Baikodo; the pork used in the meat udon comes from Chiba Prefecture.
After opening on Dogenzaka in Shibuya on April 3, 2025, the local food blog Shibukichi described the shop's dashi-rich broth and chewy, freshly-made udon as sheer bliss.

caveats: This is a meat-focused Sanuki udon specialty restaurant, not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free establishment. Wheat udon noodles, wheat-based shoyu, and an iriko (dried anchovy) dashi broth make it unsuitable for celiac or strict vegan diners; recorded for cultural/informational context only.
closed_days: No regular closed days (open year-round / 年中無休), though service can end early on any given day once the day's udon supply runs out (玉切れ次第終了).
cuisine: Sanuki Udon with meat, Japanese noodles
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket machine, payment is cash-only, and there is no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff.
gf_detail: All noodles are housemade Sanuki-style udon milled from Kagawa wheat flour — there is no gluten-free noodle alternative. The soy sauce and dashi are also sourced from Kagawa/Setouchi producers using traditional wheat-based shoyu. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate fryer, or celiac-safe protocol is indicated; not recommended for diners with celiac disease.
geocode_note: Address is in Shibuya-ku Maruyama-cho, on Dogenzaka-ue, about a 3-minute walk from Shinsen Station and roughly 0.6-0.8 km from Shibuya Scramble crossing — within the target Shibuya area. No network geocoding was performed (per instructions); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding.
