vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric restaurant (niku udon / niku meshi featuring Chiba-sourced pork) built on an iriko (dried-anchovy) dashi broth. No vegetarian or vegan menu items are indicated, and the shared dashi base is fish-derived throughout the udon line.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町1-4-26

bio.background: 七転八起 (Shichiten Hakki) opened on July 8, 2025, at a site that had previously housed a shop called "すする担々麺." It is run by the company 株式会社MINORU, which also operates the ramen shop 麺やすする near Gakugei University station.
bio.specialty: 七転八起麺 — a bowl built on a triple stock of chicken, pork, and seafood, poured over thick, chewy straight noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, topped with sliced pork, stir-fried bean sprouts, and naruto fish cake. Spice level is adjustable from 1 to 15.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name is a Japanese idiom meaning "fall down seven times, get up eight" — a nod to resilience — and its own tagline frames the bowl as a 辛旨×肉盛りの勝負ラーメン, a spicy, savory, meat-piled "fighting ramen" meant to be a test of appetite as much as a meal.

anecdotes.text: A local ramen blogger visiting near opening described it as a shop that seems poised to become a spot foreign visitors gravitate to, given its bold flavor concept and central Suidobashi location.

caveats: No vegan or gluten-free accommodation was found in any source (official site, Tabelog, or blog reviews) — this is a conventional meat-and-seafood-based ramen/tsukemen shop with wheat noodles. Payment methods (card/e-money acceptance) were not confirmed by any source, so cash_only is a best-guess default rather than a verified fact. Reservation policy was not explicitly stated but walk-in ramen-counter service is standard for this shop type.
closed_days: No regular closed day; closed only for New Year's holidays
cuisine: ラーメン・つけ麺（鶏・豚・魚介のトリプルスープ、辛旨系）Ramen / tsukemen — spicy chicken-pork-seafood triple broth
cultural_comfort_note: A standard counter-and-table ramen shop where you order at the counter or a ticket-style menu with numbered spice levels — pointing and basic greetings are enough, no English required.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature bowl uses thick straight wheat noodles from Mikawaya Seimen, and the broth/tare is a miso-based triple stock (chicken, pork, seafood) typical of shops that use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso. No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe preparation is mentioned anywhere.
geocode_note: Not geocoded — network geocoding is disabled in this environment. Address (東京都千代田区神田三崎町1-4-26) and multiple sources referring to the shop by the name "水道橋" (Suidobashi) place it directly adjacent to Suidobashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
neighborhood: 神田三崎町 / 水道橋駅前 (Kanda-Misakicho / in front of Suidobashi Station)
seats_text: 19 seats (7 counter, two 2-person tables, two 4-person tables)
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The base broth is explicitly a chicken, pork, and seafood triple stock, and the shop's core concept is a "meat-piled" (肉盛り) spicy ramen with pork and stock-derived umami throughout — no plant-based broth or noodle option is offered.

lead: ストロベリーフィールズ
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿6-2-16

bio.background: Fruits Shop Kanno is a fruit shop that opened shortly after World War II in Nishi-Shinjuku. In 1995 the shop was renovated and the owning family added an attached café, Strawberry Fields, so customers could sit down and eat the shop's fruit rather than just buy it to take home. The shop is now run by the third generation of the family.
bio.specialty: Fruit sandwich (furutsu sando) — thick-cut fresh fruit (strawberry, orange and pineapple, or grapefruit, banana and kiwi) layered with lightly sweetened whipped cream between soft white bread.
bio.philosophy: The third-generation owner selects seasonal fruit each morning at Tokyo's Ota wholesale market, and the café keeps its cream light and only lightly sweetened so the fruit itself stays the star of the dish rather than being buried in sugar and cream.
roles: fruit shop and attached café, family-run across three generations

anecdotes.text: The fruit sandwich is deliberately built with less cream and less sugar than typical versions so the fruit's own sweetness comes through; it is generously portioned (comes with a drink, plus banana and a small salad on the side) and is popular enough that it often sells out by mid-to-late afternoon.

The café was added onto the pre-existing fruit shop in a 1995 renovation, and the shop is described as having opened not long after the war; it is currently run by the third-generation owner who personally selects the fruit at Ota Market.
Bread is described as soft/fluffy (ふわふわ), with fruit sandwiches offered in two set combinations (strawberry-orange-pineapple, or grapefruit-banana-kiwi).

caveats: Cannot confirm gluten-free or vegan options exist here — no source mentions either. The signature fruit sandwich uses regular wheat bread and dairy whipped cream, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners as-is; treat this as a mainstream fruit-focused café, not a dietary-restriction-friendly spot. Hours found conflicting across sources (10:00-18:00 vs 10:00-15:00/15:30); went with the figure repeated most consistently (10:00-18:00, closed Sat/Sun/holidays) but this should be phone-verified before a visitor relies on it. Distance from Shinjuku Station is reported as roughly a 10-12 minute walk (about 1-2 minutes from Nishi-Shinjuku Metro Station specifically), which is close to but may exceed a strict 0.8km straight-line radius from JR Shinjuku Station; included given the target's own area label of "Nishi-Shinjuku / near Nishi-Shinjuku Station."
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日 (Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays)
cuisine: Fruit cafe / fruit parlor attached to a fruit shop — signature fruit sandwiches (furutsu sando), fruit parfaits, fruit juice, plus light meal items (pilaf, curry).
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only, old-school neighborhood fruit-shop café with no English menu or website found — expect to point at the display case or use simple Japanese/gestures to order.
geocode_note: Address is 東京都新宿区西新宿6-2-16. Multiple independent sources describe it as 1-2 minutes' walk from Tokyo Metro Nishi-Shinjuku Station and roughly a 10-12 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's west exit. Per instructions, lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja; straight-line distance from Shinjuku Station is plausibly near/within 0.8km but not independently verified by coordinates.
gf_detail: No source mentions gluten-free options, alternative bread, or allergen accommodations. The signature fruit sandwich is made with standard wheat bread (食パン). No indication of gluten-free bread, dedicated prep area, or allergen menu. Should be treated as not gluten-free.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (most consistently reported); one source states 10:00-15:00/15:30 with fruit sandwiches often selling out by afternoon regardless. Closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays.
name_ja: ストロベリーフィールズ（フルーツショップカンノ）
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku), Shinjuku City
seats_text: Small café seating attached to the fruit shop; exact seat count not found in available sources.
vegan_detail: No vegan mentions found. The signature fruit sandwich uses dairy whipped cream with no stated dairy-free alternative. Fresh fruit items (parfaits, juice) may be plant-based by nature but this is not confirmed as an intentional vegan offering, and no vegan-labeled menu items were found.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-7-13

bio.origin: 1945年（昭和20年）創業
bio.background: 浅草花月堂本店は戦後間もない昭和20年（1945年）創業の老舗菓子店で、浅草寺本堂左側、西参道商店街の入口という参拝客の目に留まる場所に店を構えている。看板商品のジャンボめろんぱんは通常の3倍の時間をかけて発酵させた生地を使い、外はサクサク、中はふんわりとした食感に仕上げている。
bio.specialty: ジャンボめろんぱん（Jumbo Melon Pan）— 手のひらサイズの大きな菓子パンで、外側はカリッと香ばしく焼き上げられ、中はふんわり軽い食感。1個250円前後で、本店では1日約3,000個、週末には2,000個以上が売れる人気商品。
roles: 和菓子店・メロンパン専門店

anecdotes.text: 2012年頃から浅草を案内するバスガイドたちの間で口コミが広がり、ツアーガイドの休憩スポットとして利用されたことがきっかけで店の知名度が一気に高まったと伝えられている。
看板商品のジャンボめろんぱんは通常のメロンパンより3倍長く生地を発酵させることにこだわっており、その製法が外カリ中ふわの食感を生んでいる。

caveats: Tabelog and the shop's official site returned network-policy 403 errors when fetched directly in this environment (confirmed via repeated WebFetch/curl failures against tabelog.com and asakusa-kagetudo.com), so this record is built from WebSearch result snippets that quote and summarize those and other independent Japanese travel/food sites (jalan, san-tatsu, sakuran-sanpo, hotpepper, navitime), cross-checked for consistency across multiple sources. Exact seat count, full ingredient list, and any allergen/cross-contamination details were not found in any source — treat GF/vegan info here as "no confirmed accommodation," not a confirmed absence of wheat/egg/dairy.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休、売り切れ次第閉店）
cuisine: 甘味処・メロンパン・カフェ
cultural_comfort_note: A famous walk-up bakery counter right beside Sensoji — you point at the jumbo melon pan in the case and pay cash, no Japanese conversation required, though don't expect much English beyond basic ordering.
gf_detail: The signature jumbo melon pan is a wheat-flour yeasted bread with a sugar-cookie crust; no gluten-free bread, flour substitution, or dedicated GF preparation was mentioned in any source. Not appropriate for celiac diners — this is a wheat bakery through and through.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled per instructions); lat/lng left null. hours_raw: 9:00〜16:30（売り切れ次第閉店）、年中無休

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷3-15-2 住報渋谷ビル 1F

bio.background: Part of DineVita Group, a Tokyo restaurant company that also runs ramen and yakitori shops around the city. This is a dedicated Khao Man Gai (Thai poached-chicken rice) counter a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's new south exit; a sister delivery-only outlet was added on Uber Eats/Demae-kan/menu platforms starting in 2021.
bio.origin: Thailand
bio.specialty: Khao Man Gai — jasmine rice cooked in chicken broth, topped with poached chicken and served with a fermented dipping sauce
bio.philosophy: Operator DineVita Group frames its broader mission as moving diners from mere satisfaction to genuine 'delight' in the dining-out experience.
roles: part of the DineVita Group restaurant company

anecdotes.text: The house sauce is described as a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce, made with specially selected domestic herb-fed chicken.

Some food media market the shop's cooking as carrying on the style of Bangkok's Michelin-recognized 'Pink' Khao Man Gai (Go-Ang Pratunam Chicken Rice); independent blog research suggests any formal franchise tie to that Bangkok original was severed years ago, so this lineage should be read as marketing framing rather than a confirmed corporate link.

caveats: Gluten-free and vegan accommodation are unconfirmed — no dedicated GF/vegan menu, allergy protocol, or staff-handling information was found in any source; ask staff directly before ordering if you have celiac disease, a wheat allergy, or eat vegan. Payment is cash or PayPay only — credit cards and other e-money are reported as not accepted. Sources give slightly conflicting closed-day info (some say irregular closures, others cite only the New Year holiday), so hours_status/closed_days should be treated as approximate. Direct page fetches to Tabelog and the official site were blocked by network policy in this environment (403 at the proxy level, not a site-specific block); all facts below come from search-engine result snippets/summaries of those and other pages, not from directly fetched page content, so treat address/hours/phone as reasonably but not fully verified — a periodic recheck via Tabelog is recommended.
closed_days: Reported as irregular (不定休) by some listings; multiple sources confirm closure Dec 31–Jan 1 (New Year holidays).
cuisine: Thai — Khao Man Gai (Hainanese-style poached chicken rice) specialist
cultural_comfort_note: No confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found; a fast-casual counter shop, so basic Japanese or a translation app is recommended for allergy questions.
geocode_note: Not geocoded — network geocoding is disabled in this environment (lat/lng left null). Address and multiple independent sources place it about a 4-5 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station's new south exit, which is well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing target point.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol was found in any source. The signature dipping sauce is described as a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce — soy sauce commonly contains wheat in Japan/Thailand unless specified otherwise, and no gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned. The chicken itself is poached rather than fried, but rice, broth, and soup seasoning ingredients are not detailed publicly. Ask staff directly about the sauce and broth ingredients before ordering if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.

neighborhood: 渋谷（渋谷駅周辺） — Shibuya, near Shibuya Station new south exit

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The special dipping sauce is a fermented blend of Thai miso, soy sauce, and black vinegar soy sauce; soy sauce used in Japan typically contains wheat unless a store specifies otherwise, which was not found for this shop.

vegan_detail: The menu centers on poached chicken served over chicken-broth-cooked rice, so there is no naturally vegan version of the signature dish. Sauce and soup ingredients (which may include fish sauce, chicken fat/broth, or other animal products typical of Khao Man Gai) are not detailed in any available source, and no vegan option or substitution has been confirmed. Ask staff directly before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町2-16（〒101-0051）

bio.origin: Part of an integrated meat business rooted in Takasaki, Gunma — the company runs its own meat processing/wholesale operation (Takasaki Shokuniku Center) and sells directly through its own restaurants, cutting out middlemen.
bio.background: Jimbocho Shokuniku Center is a yakiniku/hormone (offal) specialist built on a 'processing to plate' model: meat and organ meats are delivered fresh each morning from the company's own supply chain and served the same day, which the restaurant uses to justify unusually low prices for grilled offal in central Tokyo.
bio.specialty: Jōshū Tonton (上州とことん) — the company's own proprietary pork breed (a crossbreed raised in the Annaka/Haruna area of Gunma) — served alongside morning-delivered beef liver (rebā) and heart (hatsu); the lunchtime all-you-can-eat course lets diners choose from beef/pork cuts plus liver and heart.
bio.philosophy: Keep organ meat fresh enough to be odor-free by controlling the whole chain from processing to grill, and price it so grilled offal is an everyday lunch rather than a special occasion.

anecdotes.text: A June 2026 feature described the lunch all-you-can-eat (45 minutes, about ¥1,250, including rice/soup/onion salad) and reported that regulars in the know go straight for the liver and heart sets, praising the morning-delivered offal as tender and free of the usual gaminess.
A visitor blog described the ¥1,000 all-you-can-eat yakiniku lunch, noting the fresh, odor-free liver and heart and the popularity of the onion salad and soup refills.

caveats: This is a meat-focused yakiniku/hormone (offal) restaurant, not a gluten-free or vegan destination — flagged here for completeness/contrast rather than as a recommended pick for those diets. Note: the same company operates several branches (Ueno, Shimokitazawa, Akabane) with inconsistent track records — the Shimokitazawa branch closed and reopened and closed again between 2023-2025 — but all evidence found for this specific Honten (main store, Jinbocho) location, including a June 2026 feature article, indicates it is open and operating normally. Lat/lng were not geocoded per instructions; distance to Suidōbashi Station was estimated from station coordinates and is approximate.
closed_days: Year-end/New Year holidays (年末年始). On the 1st of each month, only lunch service runs — dinner is closed for inventory (棚卸).
cuisine: Yakiniku & Hormone - integrated processing for fresh meat
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu was confirmed in available sources; the lunch all-you-can-eat is ordered via simple lettered sets (A/B/C), which makes pointing-and-ordering manageable even with minimal Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a standard yakiniku/hormone restaurant with no gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation confirmed anywhere in available sources. Some cuts (e.g. liver, heart, momo) are offered "shio" (salt) rather than in tare sauce, which would avoid the soy-sauce-based marinade, but tare (used on other cuts) is typically wheat-containing soy sauce, and meats are grilled on shared grills alongside sauced items, so cross-contact is likely. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about tare ingredients and request shio-only items grilled away from sauced cuts; nothing here should be assumed safe without asking.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri & day before public holidays: 11:30-15:00, 17:00-23:30. Sat/Sun/Public holidays: 11:30-23:30. On the 1st of each month, lunch only (dinner closed for inventory).
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address is 神田神保町2-16, immediately at/near Jinbocho Station (approx. 1-2 min walk per multiple sources), which is roughly 0.7km from Suidōbashi Station (35.7017,139.7539) — within the 0.8km target radius.
name_ja: 卸）神保町食肉センター 本店
neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（Jinbocho）

gf_cross_contamination.text: Meat is cooked on shared tabletop yakiniku grills alongside items brushed with soy-sauce-based tare, creating likely cross-contact for gluten-sensitive/celiac diners; no dedicated gluten-free grill or preparation area was found in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's tare-marinated cuts follow standard yakiniku practice using soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based unless specified otherwise; no wheat-free soy sauce or tamari alternative was mentioned in any source.

positives.text: Some cuts (liver, heart, some pork/beef) are offered plain with salt (shio) rather than tare, which structurally avoids the soy-sauce marinade if requested.

seats_text: 36 seats
vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric yakiniku/hormone all-you-can-eat restaurant (beef, pork, liver, heart) with no vegan dishes identified in any source. Rice and soup sides are not confirmed vegan and, given the restaurant's meat-broth-forward menu, likely contain animal-derived stock/dashi. Not recommended for vegan diners.

lead: 松太郎 新宿小滝橋通り店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-7-28 第一山本ビル1F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan
bio.background: Matsutaro (松太郎) is a ramen-specialty spin-off brand launched by Matsuya Foods Holdings — the major company behind the nationwide Matsuya gyudon chain. This Nishi-Shinjuku (Kotakibashi-dori) shop, which opened July 30, 2025, is the brand's very first location, converted from a former Matsuya beef-bowl outlet on the same street.
bio.specialty: Shoyu ramen (680 yen), made with a tare blended from four different soy sauces for a light but full-bodied flavor; also offers a shio (salt) ramen seasoned with kombu extract and a hidden seafood dashi.
bio.philosophy: The brand's stated concept is to be affordable and "never boring no matter how many times you eat it" — aiming to be everyone's everyday dining table ("みんなの食卓でありたい").

anecdotes.text: Coverage of the opening framed it as Matsuya Foods placing its first-ever ramen shop into one of Shinjuku's most competitive ramen districts, betting on a simple 680-yen bowl to stand out.
The shop later added a morning ramen service, a detail local ramen bloggers noted as a change from its original hours.

caveats: This is a corporate chain ramen shop (Matsuya Foods' new sub-brand), not an independent or dietary-focused restaurant. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were identified in any source; noodles and soy-sauce tare are wheat-based and broths use animal-derived dashi. Exact current hours could not be independently fetched (site blocked WebFetch); hours reflect the most recent search-aggregated snippet, which differs from the shop's original opening-day hours, so on-the-day confirmation is advisable.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed day)
cuisine: Ramen
cultural_comfort_note: A standard Japanese chain ramen counter with no indication of English menus or foreign-visitor-oriented service — expect Japanese-only ordering via counter/ticket-style service typical of Matsuya-group shops.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address is 東京都新宿区西新宿7-7-28, reported as roughly a 4-6 minute walk from Seibu-Shinjuku Station and about an 8-9 minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, placing it within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options identified. The ramen noodles are standard wheat noodles, and the signature shoyu ramen's tare is a blend of four soy sauces (typically wheat-containing). No gluten-free menu, substitution, or dedicated preparation process was mentioned in any source found.
hours_raw: 7:00-23:00（L.O.22:30）※開業時は11:00-0:00（LO23:30）として案内されていたが、朝ラーメン開始後に変更された模様

neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku / Seibu-Shinjuku (西武新宿)

address_ja: 東京都台東区雷門2-3-12

bio.background: Housed in a renovated 50-year-old traditional Japanese building (kominka) near Kaminarimon, blending old wood-and-tile character with a cleaned-up modern dining room; the second floor looks out over the old Edo-style street below.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori made from branded free-range chicken from Iwate Prefecture, paired with Kyoto-style oden simmered in a broth of Hokkaido Hidaka kelp and chicken bones.
bio.philosophy: Built around slow-made, comforting flavors: the oden broth is drawn out gently over time from kelp and chicken bones so the daikon and other ingredients soak up a mild, homely taste, while the yakitori relies on the quality of Iwate's free-range birds rather than heavy seasoning.

anecdotes.text: The oden here is simmered in a special dashi carefully drawn out over low heat from Hokkaido Hidaka kelp and chicken bones, giving the daikon and other ingredients a gentle, comforting flavor.

The restaurant occupies a renovated 50-year-old house near Kaminarimon, combining traditional Japanese atmosphere with a modern, clean dining space, with the second floor offering a view over the old street below.

caveats: Hours, seating, and menu details were pieced together from aggregator listings (Rakuten Gurunavi, Enjoy Tokyo, Retty/search snippets) rather than a directly fetched official page, since the official site and Tabelog both returned 403 errors to automated fetch; core facts (address, cuisine, building story, seat count) were corroborated across at least two independent sources. No dietary-accommodation claims (gluten-free/vegan) could be found anywhere, so those fields reflect an honest absence of information rather than a confirmed 'no.'
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed day, per aggregator listings)
cuisine: Yakitori (charcoal-grilled skewers using Iwate-prefecture branded free-range chicken), Kyoto-style oden, izakaya/Japanese comfort food
cultural_comfort_note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking service was found; expect a Japanese-language izakaya experience, though its location steps from Kaminarimon means some tourist familiarity is likely.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated-fryer information could be found. As a yakitori and oden restaurant, tare (grilling sauce) and oden broth very likely contain wheat-based soy sauce; shio (salt-only) skewers may reduce wheat exposure for individual skewers but this is not confirmed by the restaurant. Celiac diners should not assume safety and would need to ask staff directly (in Japanese) about ingredients.

bio.background: Owner Nishi Masahiro spent about 25 years based in Italy, working in manufacturing-related businesses (interior design, apparel, bags) in Milan and extending his activities to New York and Ireland. After returning to Japan he opened Panzerotteria in the residential Shoto neighborhood of Shibuya in April 2012, introducing panzerotti — Italian fried, stuffed pizza dough — a dish essentially unknown in Japan at the time.
bio.specialty: Panzerotti, Italian fried and stuffed pizza dough, served in roughly 40 varieties
bio.philosophy: Presents panzerotti, which in Italy is typically eaten as fast food on the street, as a proper sit-down dish at the table; the restaurant fries its dough in domestically produced cottonseed oil, chosen for its quality and vitamin E content.

anecdotes.text: Nishi Masahiro's 25 years living and working in Italy (Milan, with activity also in New York and Ireland) led him to open Panzerotteria in Shoto in April 2012, the first restaurant of its kind focused on panzerotti in Japan.

In 2014 the shop expanded with 'Panzerotteria Factory,' a second location with restaurant attached in Osaka, and began mail-order and wholesale sales of its fried pizza, later also running a Makuake crowdfunding campaign for frozen panzerotti.

caveats: Primary sources (official site panzerotteria.com, Tabelog, Ikyu, Hitosara, Cotton Oils Guide, Foodre, Bunkamura-dori shoplist) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch in this environment, so facts here are drawn from WebSearch result summaries of those same pages rather than raw page text — treat address/hours/bio as reasonably reliable but not independently re-verified by direct fetch. bio.origin: Tokyo
bio.background: Owner Oyama Mafumi was a devoted regular at the legendary Tsuta Coffee (蔦珈琲店) in Aoyama during his student years, visiting almost daily for about four years, then trained under its master for four more years before opening his own shop, 眞踏珈琲店, in Kanda-Ogawamachi in 2016. He built the shop's long counter specifically to recreate the counter conversations with the master that had drawn him to Tsuta Coffee.
bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin coffee (Brazil Santos No.2) hand-dripped through a cloth nel filter, plus a 15-hour cold-drip iced coffee rested overnight before serving; the curry rice, noted for its fruit acidity and cheese richness, has also been recognized in the Kanda Curry Grand Prix.
bio.philosophy: A 'Heisei-era kissaten' meant to carry forward the DNA of old Showa-era coffee shops: unhurried counter conversation, self-roasted coffee, and a room lined with thousands of secondhand books for customers to read.

anecdotes.text: "Even with the same beans, you can create different flavors by making changes to the grinding and extraction methods," the owner has said of his approach to coffee.
On praise for his curry: "Even if customers compliment me on the curry, since this area is full of famous curry restaurants, I can only apologize" — a nod to Kanda's reputation as a curry district.

caveats: Traditional independent kissaten (coffee/curry/book cafe); no vegan or gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy accommodation was found in any source. Curry rice and the croque monsieur sandwich both contain wheat and dairy. Do not rely on this listing for celiac or vegan needs.
closed_days: None found — open every day of the year (no fixed regular closed day reported by the shop or listings).
cuisine: 喫茶店・コーヒー (kissaten / coffee shop, curry rice, sandwiches, desserts)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic, book-lined neighborhood kissaten with no indication of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only ordering experience.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options, labeling, or cross-contamination information were found anywhere. The signature curry rice and the "Rucola in the Sea" croque monsieur are both wheat-based (roux and bread respectively), and there is no evidence of soy-sauce wheat content, fryer dedication, or any gluten-free alternative being offered.

bio.background: Asakusa Natsuno is a small izakaya on Asakusa's Hoppy Street (ホッピー通り), open since around 2014-2015. Before running the shop, the owner worked at a ramen restaurant, and he applies ramen-style soup-making technique to the restaurant's signature beef tendon stew.
bio.philosophy: The owner spends a full day preparing the beef tendon stew, carefully trimming excess fat and using only Japanese beef tendon, balancing several different cuts in the pot to give the finished dish varied texture alongside its sweet-savory broth.
bio.specialty: 牛すじ煮込み (gyusuji nikomi) - slow-simmered beef tendon and tofu stew

anecdotes.text: The owner's tendon stew contains only beef tendon and tofu; he removes excess fat during preparation and the process takes a full day, an approach he brought over from his prior experience making ramen soup.

address_ja: 〒150-0001 東京都渋谷区神宮前6-19-17 GEMS神宮前 8F
geocode_note: Address (Jingumae 6-19-17, GEMS Jingumae Bldg, 8F) is described consistently across sources as a ~4-minute walk from Shibuya Station's B1 exit, which places it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble crossing landmark.
seats_text: 44 seats total, with private/semi-private rooms available; groups of 40+ can book the whole restaurant as a private charter.
closed_days: No fixed closed day found — listings show it open every day including holidays and the day before holidays.
cultural_comfort_note: It's a self-serve skewer-and-broth format (pick a soup, then grab skewers yourself from a shelf) with signage and staff interaction primarily in Japanese, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the picture menu) goes a long way.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based broth, or dedicated vegan options were found in any source. The restaurant is built around a mostly meat-forward self-serve skewer bar (thick-cut meats plus vegetables) with a choice of 白湯 (herbal white broth) or 麻辣 (mala spicy) soup; neither soup's stock composition is disclosed anywhere found, so whether either broth is animal-based (common for both styles in China) is unconfirmed. Vegans should ask staff directly about the broth base before ordering.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation info was found in any source (official site, Tabelog, Hot Pepper, or press coverage). The format is a self-serve buffet-style skewer shelf with 100+ types of skewered ingredients shared among all diners, plus a mala broth that in Sichuan-style cooking commonly includes soy-based seasonings and doubanjiang (fermented bean paste, sometimes containing wheat) — meaning cross-contact risk in the shared soup and skewer selection cannot be ruled out. Not verifiable as celiac-safe; diners with celiac disease should call ahead and ask specifically about broth ingredients and cross-contact before relying on this restaurant.

bio.origin: Chengdu, China
bio.background: This Shibuya branch is reported to be the first Japan location of 七婆串串香 (Qipo Chuanchuanxiang), a Chinese chuan-chuan-xiang (skewer hot pot) chain that originated in Chengdu and has grown to over a thousand locations across mainland China (sources cite figures ranging from roughly 1,100 to 1,800 stores). It opened in the GEMS Jingumae building near Miyamasuzaka, a short walk from Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Self-serve chuan chuan xiang (skewered hot pot): diners choose a 白湯 (mellow herbal white broth) or 麻辣 (numbing-spicy mala, in four spice levels) soup, then help themselves to over 100 kinds of skewered meats and vegetables from a self-service shelf to cook tableside.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to Tabelog, the official site, Hot Pepper, and several other listing pages was blocked in this environment (proxy returned 403/policy-denial on those hosts), so this record is built entirely from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those pages rather than full page fetches. Payment method (cash-only status) was not confirmed by any source and is a best-guess default given it's a modern building tenant. No gluten-free/vegan/allergy-specific statements were found anywhere; the corresponding fields reflect that absence rather than a confirmed policy.

lead: 豚ノヴァ零-zero- 神田水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-8-9 ウインド水道橋ビル 1F

bio.origin: 国分寺本店（豚NOVA）の2号店として2025年3月8日にオープン
bio.background: 「豚ノヴァ 零-zero- 神田水道橋店」は、国分寺で人気の二郎系ラーメン店「豚NOVA（豚ノヴァ）」の2号店として、JR水道橋駅・神保町駅の間、水道橋駅東口徒歩5分の場所に2025年3月8日にグランドオープンした。国分寺本店とは異なり、通常のラーメンは提供せず「汁なし（マゼソバ寄りの二郎インスパイア）」専門の一杯に特化している。店主のGo氏は埼玉の人気二郎系店「富士丸 東浦和店」や「D麺」で修行した経歴を持つ。
bio.specialty: 汁なし（二郎インスパイア系の油そば・まぜそば）。丼全体を混ぜると小麦の甘みを含んだ香りが立ち上る、汁なし専用に配合・形状を変えた自家製麺が看板。
bio.philosophy: 経営上の数字よりも味への一切の妥協のなさを優先しており、一杯あたりの原価率は60%を超えるという。国分寺本店の味をベースにしながら、カエシ・豚・トッピングを細かく調整し、神田水道橋店ならではの独自の味わいに仕上げている。

anecdotes.text: 汁なし専用の麺は、汁なしのために小麦の配合や形状を変えたもので、丼全体をかき混ぜた時に小麦の甘みを含んだ香りがフワッと立ち上り、ムチっとした食感に密度の高い小麦感が味わえます。二郎系の名店で修行をしたGo店主が作り上げた、こだわりの麺です。
豚ノヴァは、経営的な数値を度外視して味に対して一切の妥協なく提供をしており、一杯の原価率は60%を超えており、これは最高の原材料で最高の味を提供するためです。
店主は埼玉の人気二郎系店「富士丸 東浦和店」と「D麺」で修行した。
2025年3月8日、国分寺に続く2号店として神田水道橋にグランドオープンした（水道橋駅徒歩5分）。

caveats: This is a wheat-noodle Jiro-inspired "shirunashi" (soupless mazesoba-style) ramen specialty shop — it is not gluten-free or vegan focused and is included only as a general OMNI dining option. Payment methods (cash vs. card/e-money) at the ticket machine could not be confirmed from available sources, so cash_only is a best-guess default of false rather than a verified fact. The shop's entire menu centers on a custom wheat-blend noodle specifically engineered for the "shirunashi" (soupless) format — wheat is the signature ingredient, not something worked around. No mention anywhere of gluten-free noodles, alternative flour, or allergy accommodation; the soy-based tare (カエシ) used for seasoning almost certainly contains wheat as is standard for Jiro-style shops. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.

bio.background: Yuzuan is a nationwide sushi-and-shabu-shabu buffet brand run by Monogatari Corporation. This Nishi-Shinjuku 'Ogake' branch opened on June 25, 2025, roughly the chain's 106th location, about a two-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, in a stretch of Nishi-Shinjuku where the company also opened a sister yakiniku location the same day.
bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat course pairing conveyor-style nigiri and rolls (30+ varieties) with shabu-shabu hot pot, meats and seasonal vegetables, ordered from the table via touchscreen tablets so food comes straight to your seat.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described treating themselves to a solo year-end dinner at Yuzuan on a roughly ¥5,000 budget and being so satisfied with the sushi-and-shabu-shabu spread that they lost track of time.

caveats: This is a large all-you-can-eat chain restaurant (sushi + shabu-shabu), not a vegan or gluten-free specialist. The parent company publishes a chainwide allergen chart, but no dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or vegan menu/substitutions were confirmed in available sources. Celiac and strict-vegan diners should call ahead or check the allergen chart in person before ordering.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures) — no fixed weekly closing day was confirmed; check the official site for holiday notices.
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is done via tableside touchscreen tablets with photo menus, which makes navigating the all-you-can-eat course manageable with minimal Japanese, though an English-language menu was not confirmed.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (lat/lng left null per instructions). The address (西新宿7-1-1, Shinjuku Kaleidoscope Building 6F) is reported as about a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's west exit, well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius.
gf_detail: Sushi rice vinegar and dipping soy sauce (typically wheat-based in Japan), plus likely tempura/breaded items on the all-you-can-eat menu, mean most dishes are not gluten-free by default. The parent company (Monogatari Corporation) publishes a chainwide allergen chart flagging wheat and six other major allergens per menu item, and states that items not on the chart can be investigated on request (about a two-week turnaround), but no dedicated gluten-free fryer, GF soy sauce, or GF-specific course was found. Cross-contact risk in the shared kitchen should be assumed high; celiac diners should review the allergen chart and speak with staff directly before eating.

bio.background: MATCHA TIMES is a Japanese matcha-drink specialty brand that opened its Asakusa location on December 16, 2025, tucked on the 2nd floor of a residential backstreet a five-minute walk from Sensoji's Kaminarimon gate and two minutes from Tawaramachi Station. Press coverage describes it as a deliberately low-key 'hideaway' offering an escape from the tourist bustle around the temple.
bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (brand also has other Tokyo locations such as Shibuya and Shinjuku)
bio.specialty: Build-your-own matcha latte — customers choose the matcha grade, intensity, sweetness, milk, flavor (options include kuromitsu, kinako, houjicha, melon, mango, strawberry, chocolate, yuzu) and toppings (warabimochi, anko, cream); also known for its handmade 'demekin taiyaki' filled with matcha custard.
bio.philosophy: The brand states it wants to honor the spirit of traditional tea ceremony and the beauty of matcha while reinterpreting it, not as something formal, but in a form that fits naturally into modern daily life.

anecdotes.text: MATCHA TIMES sources its matcha directly from five long-established manufacturers, each with over 100 years of history, to ensure high, consistent quality across its customizable drink menu.
The Asakusa shop was deliberately sited away from the main tourist strip near Kaminarimon, on a quiet residential street up a flight of stairs, to create a calmer 'hideaway' matcha experience.

positives.text: Core matcha lattes are built from matcha powder, milk, and naturally gluten-free flavor add-ins like kuromitsu, kinako, and houjicha, with no dashi or soy-sauce-based savory dishes on the menu to introduce hidden wheat or fish stock.

bio.background: 庵狐（あんこ）は三宿・恵比寿・代官山・円山町（神泉）に店舗を構える小規模な居酒屋グループで、円山町店は渋谷区円山町の加藤ビル1階、神泉駅から徒歩2〜3分の場所にある。
bio.specialty: 地鶏（じどり）と鮮魚を炭火で焼く料理、日本酒・焼酎に合わせた一品料理
bio.philosophy: 毎朝仕入れる新鮮な地鶏と、産地直送の魚介・野菜を使い、気取らない居酒屋料理を日本酒・焼酎とともに提供するという方針が複数の掲載情報から窺える。

anecdotes.text: 検索結果によれば、庵狐グループは全国の港（特に築地）から直接仕入れる魚介類を看板に、日本酒のラインナップと合わせて提供しているとされる（店舗横断的な特徴として言及されており、円山町店固有の逸話としての確認はできていない）。

caveats: Primary listing pages (Tabelog, official site grill-anko.jp, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Retty, Yahoo Map) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; all facts below are drawn from search-engine snippets of those pages, which is a weaker source than a direct page fetch. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation, allergy protocol, or dedicated-fryer information was found anywhere in available sources — this is an ordinary seafood/chicken izakaya with no confirmed dietary-special-needs program, so GF/vegan diners should treat it as an "ask staff, verify in person" spot only, not a vetted safe option.

cultural_comfort_note: This is a local neighborhood izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff in any source found, so expect a Japanese-only experience.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区三崎町2-11-13 東洋ビル1F

bio.background: A small, single-location pork rice bowl (butadon) counter shop a couple of minutes' walk from JR Suidobashi Station, in a neighborhood dense with university campuses (Nihon University's Suidobashi campus is nearby). The shop is known locally as a hearty, budget lunch spot for students and workers, and has built something of a reputation in Tokyo's "big-portion" (dekamori) dining scene for its oversized bowls and an eating-challenge menu.
bio.specialty: Butadon (pork rice bowl) served over a large base of rice, with a choice of four house sauces — teriyaki garlic, teriyaki ginger, spicy soy sauce, or salt-ginger.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a standing eating challenge: finish an oversized 1.5kg pork bowl together with three bowls of miso soup within 30 minutes to win a free bowl coupon, and food blogs describe it as a well-known stop on Tokyo's 'big-eater' circuit.
A visitor review describes the room as crowded with students, with counter-only seating and a no-frills, local-diner feel.

caveats: Could not directly fetch the Tabelog or official site pages (both returned access errors); all details are corroborated via search-indexed aggregator listings (Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Hitosara, travel blogs) rather than a single primary source, so hours in particular should be reconfirmed before visiting — two different snapshots of the schedule were found and the more detailed/likely-current one was used. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation was found anywhere; this is a conventional pork-bowl shop, not a dietary-aware venue.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cuisine: 丼・ご飯もの（豚丼専門店 / pork rice bowl specialty shop）
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only, counter-seating student-diner with a Japanese-only menu and no English service found in any source, so some Japanese or confident pointing/gesturing will be needed to order.

gf_detail: This is a soy-sauce-based pork bowl shop — the four house sauces (teriyaki garlic, teriyaki ginger, spicy soy sauce, salt-ginger) are built on standard Japanese shoyu, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu, sauce substitution, or dedicated preparation process was mentioned in any source found. Not a safe choice for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.

roles: run by two women who operate the counter and kitchen
bio.background: Shinjuku Soba Kura (信州そば処 新宿そば蔵) is the Shinjuku Sanchome branch of Sobakura, a small two-shop Shinshu (Nagano)-style soba group that also runs a location in Komazawa. The Shinjuku Sanchome shop opened in January 2004, in a cozy, old-house-style interior with a nostalgic standing-soba-bar feel.
bio.origin: Nagano Prefecture (Shinshu) buckwheat and local sake, delivered fresh daily
bio.philosophy: The group's stated aim, per its official site, is '本場信州のそばを味わう喜び' — the joy of tasting authentic Shinshu soba — built around buckwheat noodles and sake sourced fresh from Nagano every day.
bio.specialty: 錦そば (Nishiki Soba), plus shiso (青じそ) flavored variations and Shinshu-style mori soba

anecdotes.text: The shop is run day-to-day by two women in a small counter-and-table space (about 12 seats) with a casual, nostalgic standing-soba-shop atmosphere; regulars praise the firm, fresh-tasting Nagano buckwheat noodles and the crisp vegetable and shrimp tempura, alongside snacks like nozawana pickles and horse sashimi (basashi) to go with the local sake.
Weekday moriSoba runs around ¥550 and a half-rice teishoku set around ¥935, reflecting the shop's casual, affordable pricing.

caveats: Gluten-free and vegan suitability are not addressed anywhere in the sources found; the assessment below is inferred from standard soba-shop practice (wheat-blended noodles unless stated otherwise, wheat-based soy sauce, bonito-based dashi) rather than a direct statement from the restaurant, so celiac/strict-vegan diners should not treat this as confirmed-safe. Card/cash payment policy was not confirmed in any source. The official group site (sobakura.com) did not resolve via direct fetch in this environment; its content used here comes from a cached search-engine excerpt of that page, not a first-hand page load, so treat those specific details (Jan 2004 opening, tagline) as slightly lower confidence than the address/hours, which were corroborated independently across Gnavi and Tsuku2 listings.
closed_days: Sundays (closed every Sunday)
cuisine: そば・日本酒 (Soba noodles and Japanese sake)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual, locally-run soba stand with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; some Japanese ability (or pointing at the tabelog/menu photos) will help when ordering.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都新宿区新宿3-1-32 (〒160-0022), consistently reported across independent listings as roughly 2-3 minutes' walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius. No network geocoding was performed per instructions; lat/lng are left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.
gf_detail: No source confirms the noodles are 十割 (100% buckwheat, jūwari); as a traditional Shinshu soba shop with no gluten-free claims found, the soba is presumed to be a standard wheat-flour blend (nihachi-style), and the menu includes tempura and soy sauce-based dipping sauce (tsuyu), both typically wheat-containing. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate boiling water, or dedicated fryer was mentioned anywhere. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
name_ja: 新宿そば蔵（信州そば処 新宿そば蔵）
neighborhood: Shinjuku-sanchome, Shinjuku

No vegan options, substitutions, or dashi alternatives were mentioned in any source reviewed.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神山町10-8

bio.background: Opened in 1988 (Showa 63) on a quiet backstreet of Shibuya's Kamiyama-cho, a few minutes' walk from Shinsen Station, deliberately set apart from the bustle of central Shibuya. The shop was previously known as "清山" (Seizan) before taking the name 神山 (Kamiyama).
bio.specialty: Hand-worked soba noodles, plus sobagaki (soba-flour dumplings) and a seasonal tempura assortment
bio.philosophy: A craftsmanship-first approach carried over from training at Takeyabu, one of Tokyo's most celebrated soba houses: domestic buckwheat is stone-ground fresh each morning and the dough is hand-worked to order, with the buckwheat variety changing by season.

anecdotes.text: The shop's soba craftsman trained at Takeyabu, a nationally known soba house, before opening this hideaway shop on a Shibuya backstreet.
Behind a glass wall, diners can watch the craftsman turn stone-ground buckwheat flour into noodles in one continuous, practiced motion.
Buckwheat is stone-ground on site each morning and mixed with spring water said to be drawn from the Haruna mountain area, used for both the noodles and the dashi.

caveats: Restaurant pages on tabelog.com and the shop's own official site could not be directly fetched in this environment (network policy blocked those hosts), so this record is built from search-engine snippets of secondary sources (food blogs, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Pasaporte) rather than a direct page fetch. Lunch (12:00-14:00 weekdays, 12:00-20:00 Sat) appears to be walk-in; dinner is course-only (approx. ¥5,500/7,700/11,000) and requires advance reservation by the day before, minimum 2 people. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation was mentioned in any source found — treat GF/vegan info here as an honest "not accommodating" assessment based on absence of evidence for a traditional bonito-dashi soba-ya, not a confirmed denial from the shop itself.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cuisine: 蕎麦 / Soba (traditional hand-worked buckwheat noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood soba-ya with no confirmed English menu or online booking; dinner reservations are made by phone in Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a traditional Edo-style soba-ya, not a gluten-free-focused kitchen. Some descriptions reference stone-ground buckwheat prepared fresh daily, but there is no confirmation the noodles are consistently 十割 (100% buckwheat, wheat-free) rather than the common 二八 blend (buckwheat plus wheat flour as binder), and the dipping sauce/dashi (tsuyu) follows the standard soy-sauce-and-bonito-dashi style typical of old-line soba shops, which normally contains wheat. Tempura is also on the menu, fried in shared oil with a wheat-flour batter. No gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any available source, so celiac or strictly gluten-free diners should not assume safety here without calling ahead and asking directly.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 12:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); Sat 12:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); closed Sun and public holidays. Dinner is course-only and reservation-required by the previous day, minimum 2 guests.
neighborhood: 神泉・神山町 (Shinsen / Kamiyama-cho, Shibuya-ku)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area, protocol, or wheat-free noodle guarantee has been documented; this is a shared traditional kitchen that also fries wheat-battered tempura.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce and dashi follow standard Edo-style soba preparation (bonito dashi with soy sauce); no wheat-free tamari or gluten-free sauce alternative was mentioned in any source found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is made with dried bonito (katsuobushi), and the menu includes tempura and sashimi, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan cooking; no plant-based dashi option was found in any source.

positives.text: Uses domestically grown buckwheat, stone-ground on the premises each morning for freshly milled noodles.

seats_text: Roughly 44 seats: basement floor has a 14-seat counter plus 12 seats across tatami private rooms; 1st floor has 18 table seats.
vegan_detail: This is a classic soba-ya built around bonito (katsuo) dashi, with tempura and sashimi also on the menu, so it is not vegan-oriented. Even a plain zaru soba would typically be served with a bonito-based dipping sauce. No plant-based dashi or vegan menu option was found in any source reviewed.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-5-3 高山ランド会館4F

bio.origin: Tokyo (small restaurant group with a second location in Tachikawa)
bio.background: 農家の台所 ("Farmer's Kitchen") sources its vegetables directly from roughly 40 contracted farmers around Japan under the concept 「人×素材」("People × Ingredients"). The Shinjuku Sanchome branch has been operating for around 17 years and reports having served over a million customers, built around its all-you-can-eat vegetable salad bar.
bio.specialty: The all-you-can-eat salad bar (20+ types of vegetables daily) sourced straight from contract farms, served alongside a bagna cauda-style dip and grilled/simmered mains such as salt-koji chicken thigh and grilled salmon harasu.
bio.philosophy: Direct-from-farmer sourcing and simple, hand-prepared cooking that lets the natural sweetness and character of seasonal vegetables speak for themselves.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers repeatedly single out the salad bar's rare vegetable varieties as noticeably sweeter and fresher-tasting than typical produce.
Described in a 2025 write-up as a bright, popular spot with a strong lunch salad-bar following.

caveats: Two Tokyo locations exist (Shinjuku Sanchome and Tachikawa Takashimaya) so this is a small chain, not a one-off independent shop. Gluten-free and vegan claims are moderate: only the lunch curry is confirmed vegan among set meals, and no source confirms wheat-free soy sauce, dashi, or a dedicated fryer — celiac diners should ask staff directly. Seat count could not be independently confirmed; only the horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) section's ~20-person capacity was found. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding); address is well within Shinjuku Station's 0.8km radius (adjoins Shinjuku-sanchome Station, ~5 min walk from JR Shinjuku's east exit).
closed_days: Open daily (無休), except New Year holidays
cuisine: Vegetable-forward Japanese izakaya/dining (野菜料理), farm-direct produce with grilled/simmered mains
cultural_comfort_note: Listed on English-facing tourist/booking platforms (Tabelog EN, byFood) and buffet-style ordering is easy to navigate visually, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification was found. The vegetable salad bar itself is naturally produce-based, but no source confirms whether shared dressings, soy sauce, dashi, or fried items are wheat-free, nor whether a dedicated fryer is used. WE TABLE tags the restaurant as accommodating gluten-free requests, but specifics were not documented — celiac diners should confirm details with staff before ordering.
name_ja: 農家の台所 新宿三丁目店
neighborhood: Shinjuku Sanchome

staff_allergy_handling.text: The miso soup included in lunch sets is made with fish dashi, but it can be requested to be left off the set when ordering.

positives.text: Only the curry among the lunch set options is vegan-compatible.
All toppings on the salad bar use no animal-derived ingredients, so vegan diners can eat freely from the salad bar alone.
Tagged on WE TABLE as accommodating allergy, vegan, halal, and gluten-free needs.

seats_text: Has a horigotatsu (sunken-kotatsu) seating section that can hold up to about 20 guests; total restaurant capacity not confirmed.
vegan_detail: Among the lunch set menu, only the curry is confirmed vegan; the miso soup that otherwise accompanies lunch sets contains fish dashi but can be omitted on request. The all-you-can-eat salad bar's toppings are all free of animal-derived ingredients, making it a reliable vegan option on its own. Other mains (grilled meats, fish) are not vegan.

address_ja: 東京都台東区西浅草2-25-15

bio.specialty: Three hand-cut soba noodle styles served side by side: a thin 'tsurutsuru hosogiri' (smooth, easy-swallowing thin cut), a nutty 'mochimochi arabiki' (coarse-ground cut), and an ultra-thick 'mogumogu futoi' (太打ち) noodle roughly 1cm wide, marketed as among the thickest hand-cut soba in Tokyo -- cut so wide that a single serving can contain as few as about 8 strands.
bio.philosophy: The shop varies the ratio of buckwheat to wheat binder to match each cut's texture: roughly 90% buckwheat for the thin noodle (smoother throat-feel), a 100%-buckwheat (juwari) blend for the medium noodle, and about 95% buckwheat for the ultra-thick noodle, using buckwheat flour sourced from various regions of Japan.

anecdotes.text: The 太打ち (ultra-thick) noodle is cut so wide that a single serving of soba contains only around 8 strands -- an oddity that has made it a minor food-blog phenomenon in Asakusa, often described as 'Japan's thickest soba'.
The restaurant offers three distinct hand-cut noodle styles (thin/coarse/ultra-thick) at different buckwheat ratios, and has been included in Tabelog's Top 100 Soba list.

caveats: Hours, seating layout, and noodle-ratio details are drawn from multiple independent food-blog/aggregator sources (Tabelog, nihon-soba.jp, food.onarimon.jp, momoti.com, otonano-shumatsu.com) that broadly agree, rather than from an official restaurant website (none could be confirmed). GF/vegan assessments are inferred from general soba-shop practice (wheat-based tsuyu, shared kitchen/boiling water, bonito dashi) since no page specific to this shop addresses allergen handling, dedicated equipment, or cross-contamination directly -- diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm directly with staff. Exact walking distance to Sensoji was estimated from the Nishi-Asakusa 2-chome address and proximity to Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station (~1-2 min walk per sources), not from live geocoding.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday (open on national holidays; closed the following weekday instead)
cuisine: Soba (hand-cut, three noodle-thickness varieties: thin, coarse-ground, and ultra-thick 太打ち)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional neighborhood soba-ya with no confirmed English menu or online reservation system -- some basic Japanese (or pointing at the Tabelog photos) will help when ordering.
gf_detail: This is a traditional hand-cut soba-ya, not a gluten-free-focused establishment. Of its three noodle cuts, the medium ('yaya futomen') noodle is reportedly made juwari-style (十割, 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat binder), while the thin noodle uses roughly 90% buckwheat/10% wheat and the ultra-thick noodle about 95% buckwheat/5% wheat -- so two of the three cuts do contain some wheat flour. No source confirms dedicated gluten-free boiling water, separate prep equipment, or a wheat-free dipping sauce; standard soba tsuyu is soy-sauce (shoyu) based and typically contains wheat. Diners with celiac disease should not assume any cut is safe without asking staff directly, given the shared kitchen and likely shared boiling water across noodle types.
neighborhood: Nishi-Asakusa, Taito, Tokyo
seats_text: About 22 seats, table seating only (three 4-person tables and one 6-person table); no counter or private tatami room.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu, labeling, or accommodation was found for this shop. As a traditional soba-ya, its dipping sauce (tsuyu) almost certainly rests on a katsuobushi (bonito) dashi with a soy-sauce-based kaeshi, which is standard practice for hand-cut soba shops of this type and is not vegan. No source confirms a kombu-only or vegetable-broth alternative.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都台東区西浅草2-25-15, about a 1-2 minute walk from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station per multiple sources -- that station sits roughly 500-600m from Sensoji, placing the restaurant within the 0.8km Sensoji radius. Network geocoding was not performed (blocked in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja.

lead: 餃子 小籠包 福包酒場 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1丁目7-10 新大宗渋谷道玄坂一丁目ビル1F

roles: 特級点心師 (senior dim sum chef, unnamed) prepares the dumplings
bio.background: Fupao Sakaba (福包酒場) is a casual offshoot of the same operators' upscale restaurant, 中国家庭料理 香園 (Chinese home cooking restaurant Kōen); the brand was created to bring the same dim-sum techniques and recipes to a lower price point. It has since grown into a multi-location Chinese-izakaya chain with branches across Tokyo (Roppongi, Shibuya, Ikebukuro), Kanagawa, Osaka, Sendai and other cities, and is actively recruiting franchisees. This Shibuya branch sits in the middle of Shibuya Chuo-gai on Dogenzaka, about a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's south exit / Inokashira Line west exit.
bio.specialty: 5-color soup dumplings (5色小籠包) — five hand-made xiaolongbao, each wrapped in a differently colored, differently flavored skin, served in a bamboo steamer that releases a cloud of steam when opened; listed as the shop's most popular item, especially with women.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant describes itself as committed to its ingredients, cooking methods, dining space and hospitality ("食材や調理法、空間から接客まで、お客様をおもてなしすることにこだわっています"), aiming to entertain guests visually as well as through flavor.

anecdotes.text: Monday–Friday 4–6pm the restaurant runs a 60-minute all-you-can-drink deal on sour cocktails for 1 yen, letting guests choose two flavors from the menu.
The five-color xiaolongbao are prized as much for the burst of steam and color when the steamer basket is opened as for their flavor, making them a popular photo moment.

caveats: This is a casual Chinese-izakaya chain (multiple branches nationwide), not a GF/vegan-focused establishment. No gluten-free or vegan menu, substitution, or allergy-handling information was found in any source, and the signature dishes (gyoza, xiaolongbao) use wheat-flour wrappers with meat fillings — treat as unsuitable for celiac/strict-vegan diners absent direct confirmation from staff. Phone number varies slightly between sources (050-1480-0251 vs 050-5492-2050), likely reflecting different listing-service reservation lines rather than the restaurant's own line. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding should resolve the address above, which sits on Dogenzaka 1-chome, ~250-400m from Shibuya Scramble crossing, within the target radius.
closed_days: None — open every day of the week (年中無休), no regular closed days found in any source.
cuisine: Chinese dumplings / dim sum izakaya (gyoza, xiaolongbao, Chinese home cooking)
cultural_comfort_note: No confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; ordering by pointing at photos or basic Japanese should be sufficient at this casual izakaya.
geocode_note: Address (東京都渋谷区道玄坂1丁目7-10) confirmed across Gnavi, Hot Pepper, and Yahoo Map listings; multiple sources describe it as a 2-minute walk from Shibuya Station's south exit / Inokashira Line west exit, placing it well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target point. Not geocoded in this pass (network geocoding blocked) — lat/lng left null for downstream resolution from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free substitutions, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source. The shop's signature items (gyoza, xiaolongbao) use standard wheat-flour dumpling wrappers, and as a general Chinese-izakaya menu it almost certainly relies on wheat-containing soy sauce and other seasonings; no evidence of any gluten-free accommodation was found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
name_ja: 餃子 小籠包 福包酒場 渋谷店
neighborhood: 道玄坂 (Dogenzaka), Shibuya — near Shinsen Station
