address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷1-33-7

bio.background: Originally a standard stand-up (tachigui) soba counter on Hakusan-dori between Suidobashi and Korakuen near Tokyo Dome, the shop reinvented itself around early 2020 into a dipping-soba (tsukesoba) specialist built around a single signature bowl of beef tsukesoba.
bio.specialty: Gyuniku (beef) tsukesoba — thick, firm soba noodles served alongside a rich, chili-oil-spiked dipping broth topped with simmered beef; other versions include nori, grated yam (tororo), and duck.

anecdotes.text: The shop shifted from a generic stand-up soba counter to a dedicated tsukesoba specialist focused on hearty beef soba made with deliberately hard, thick noodles.
Featured as a weekday lunch pick for the Hongo/Suidobashi office-worker crowd, ordered via a ticket vending machine at the counter.

caveats: Verified ward is Bunkyo-ku (Hongo), not Chiyoda-ku as originally briefed — but the address is only a 2-3 minute walk from Suidobashi Station, so it still sits within the Suidobashi target radius. This is a conventional (non-GF, non-vegan) beef-tsukesoba counter with no dietary accommodations found in any source; included as a general omnivore option near the station only.
closed_days: Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
cuisine: Soba (Tsukesoba - Dipping Noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine at the counter (PayPay QR accepted as an alternative), with no indication of an English menu — comfortable for diners with basic Japanese or point-and-order confidence.
geocode_note: Address (本郷1-33-7) is on Hakusan-dori between Suidobashi and Korakuen, reported as roughly a 1-3 minute walk from Suidobashi Station (Toei Mita Line A-exit / JR east exit) — well within the 0.8km Suidobashi Station target radius. Ward is Bunkyo-ku, not Chiyoda-ku. lat/lng left null per instructions (no geocoding performed).
gf_detail: No gluten-free noodle, sauce, or accommodation is advertised anywhere. This is a conventional soba counter using standard (wheat-buckwheat blend, not 十割/juwari) noodles, and its signature dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce-based, which in Japan is almost always wheat-containing — not appropriate for celiac-level gluten-free needs. No allergy information was found on Tabelog or any secondary source.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri) 11:00-17:00; closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays
neighborhood: Suidobashi, Bunkyo-ku
seats_text: About 6 chair seats at the counter
vegan_detail: The menu revolves around beef and duck tsukesoba with soy/dashi-based dipping broths; no vegan-labeled dishes, plant-based broth, or dashi substitution were found in any source. Even the vegetable-leaning options (nori soba, tororo/yam soba) are served with the same soy-based dipping sauce, which likely contains bonito-derived dashi.

lead: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 新宿京王モール店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-1 京王モールB4区画（京王モール地下、新宿駅西口）

roles: Branch of the Sagatani (嵯峨谷) 100%-buckwheat soba chain
bio.background: Sagatani was launched in Tokyo in 2011 by operator For You Co., Ltd. (株式会社フォーユー) to bring authentic 100%-buckwheat 'juwari' soba — normally the domain of pricier specialty soba-ya — into the cheap, fast tachigui (stand-and-eat) soba format. The chain grew to roughly 8-9 locations across central Tokyo. This Shinjuku Keio Mall branch sits underground in the Keio Mall shopping arcade just off Shinjuku Station's west exit and opened in December 2021.
bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (chain founded 2011)
bio.specialty: 十割そば (juwari soba) — noodles made from 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat used as a binder, milled fresh in-house each day on an in-store electric stone mill.
bio.philosophy: The chain's motto is '挽きたて・打ちたて・茹でたて・揚げたて' (freshly milled, freshly made, freshly boiled, freshly fried): buckwheat is stone-ground on site and the flour's water content is adjusted daily to match the weather and season, aiming to deliver specialty-shop soba quality at stand-and-eat prices and speed.

anecdotes.text: A Tabelog reviewer titled their post '嵯峨谷が新宿に戻ってきたヨ♪ (Sagatani is back in Shinjuku!)', celebrating the brand's return to a Shinjuku storefront with this opening.
Another reviewer calls it their 'go-to spot when the weather turns cold' (寒くなった時の定番), describing it as a reliable, comforting choice near the station.

caveats: This is a branch of the Sagatani chain (not an independent shop) inside the Keio Mall underground concourse at Shinjuku Station's west exit. It is not a certified or dedicated gluten-free establishment — the soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder, but the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is a standard Japanese preparation that very likely uses wheat-containing soy sauce, and tempura items are conventional wheat-battered and fried, likely in a shared fryer. No restaurant-specific allergen protocol or ingredient breakdown was found in available sources, so celiac-level safety cannot be confirmed — ask staff and consider ordering plain soba (mori/zaru) without tempura or sauce-heavy dishes if avoiding wheat strictly. The broth is fish-based (kombu + bonito/katsuo), so it is not vegan.
closed_days: No regular weekly closure found; as a shop inside the Keio Mall it follows the mall's own opening calendar and would only close on the mall's occasional maintenance/holiday closure days (not separately documented).
cuisine: Soba (100% buckwheat / juwari soba specialty), fast-casual tachigui-style
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (食券制) — cash or transit IC card — typical of fast soba counters, so a little Japanese or willingness to point at machine buttons/photos helps.
gf_detail: The soba noodles are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat flour used as a binder, which is unusual for soba (most soba blends in wheat flour) and a genuine plus for wheat-avoidant diners. However, the tsuyu dipping sauce is a standard Japanese preparation and Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat as well as soybeans, so the sauce very likely contains wheat; tempura toppings use ordinary wheat-flour batter and are fried in what is likely a shared fryer. No dedicated gluten-free menu, ingredient list, or allergen protocol for this location was found, so this cannot be confirmed safe for celiac disease — order plain soba and ask staff about the sauce and frying oil if strict avoidance is required.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Address places the shop inside the Keio Mall underground concourse at Nishi-Shinjuku 1-1, directly adjacent to JR Shinjuku Station's west exit (also ~1 min walk from Toei Oedo Line Shinjuku-nishiguchi Station Exit D3), well within the Shinjuku Station target radius.
name_en: Juwari Soba Sagatani — Shinjuku Keio Mall
name_ja: 十割蕎麦 さがたに 新宿京王モール店（嵯峨谷）
neighborhood: Shinjuku Station West Exit / Keio Mall (西新宿)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), the base of soba tsuyu, is typically brewed from wheat and soybeans together — this is general Japanese food-labeling guidance, not a statement specific to this shop's own recipe, but it applies to conventional tsuyu of the kind this chain serves.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The chain's broth is described as made with kombu and a blend of dried bonito (katsuobushi) stocks for both the cold and hot tsuyu, meaning the soup base is fish-derived and not vegan.

positives.text: Noodles are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat used as a binder, milled fresh in-store daily on an electric stone mill — a genuine plus versus ordinary soba, which typically blends in wheat flour.

seats_text: Counter/stand-style wall-facing seating; exact seat count not published.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-focused: the standard tsuyu/broth for both hot and cold soba is made with kombu and bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, so the default dipping sauce and hot soup contain fish. No plant-based dashi or dedicated vegan menu was found for this location. The soba noodles themselves are just buckwheat and water (no egg), but they are normally served with the fish-based sauce/broth, and tempura batter/toppings are not confirmed vegan either.

lead: 麺屋武蔵 武骨外伝
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-8-5

bio.background: Menya Musashi (麺屋武蔵) is a Tokyo ramen company founded in 1996 in Aoyama by Yamada Yuu, part of the influential wave of "1996-generation" Tokyo ramen shops credited with reshaping the genre. Bukotsu Gaiden ("the rugged sequel") is one of the company's Shibuya spin-off/sister brands, opened in the mid-2000s a short walk from Shibuya Station, and it has become one of the busiest tsukemen (dipping noodle) specialists in the neighborhood, known for very generous, freely-refillable noodle portions.
bio.specialty: Noukou Gaiden Kuro Tsukemen (rich black dipping noodles) — thick, chewy noodles served cold alongside a choice of three dipping broths: black (finished with a special soy-based tare), red (finished with a spicy miso paste), and plain white (no added paste), each available in a standard or richer katsuobushi-enhanced version.
bio.philosophy: Menya Musashi's founding concept was to run a ramen shop with the warmth and hospitality of a good izakaya, on the belief that the whole dining experience — not just the taste of the broth — is what makes a bowl of ramen genuinely satisfying.
roles: Shibuya branch of the Menya Musashi ramen group, specializing in tsukemen

anecdotes.text: The shop offers three colors of dipping sauce — white, black, and red — each built on a shared chicken-and-pork-bone base with dried sardine and bonito, letting diners compare three distinct flavor profiles in one visit.
A regular customer described it as the reward at the end of a long workday, praising the volume of noodles (up to 1kg free) and the richness of the black dipping broth topped with a soft-boiled egg.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations were found for this particular Shibuya branch. The chain's separately-marketed plant-based/vegan ramen (e.g. at the Shinjuku flagship) is not confirmed to be offered here. Seat count, hours, and payment details are aggregated from multiple secondary listings (Tabelog, HotPepper, gnavi, Foursquare, official storelist page) since direct page fetches were blocked in this environment; core facts (address, hours, three-broth tsukemen concept) were corroborated across at least two independent sources.
closed_days: None — open every day of the year (無休)
cuisine: つけ麺 (Tsukemen) — signature three-flavor dipping noodles (black/red/white broth)
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-only seating with a Japanese-language ticket machine and no reservations; the three broth colors (white/black/red) are easy to identify visually even without fluent Japanese, but ordering noodle amount/toppings may require pointing or basic phrases.
gf_detail: Standard wheat noodles are used with no gluten-free noodle alternative found. The broths are built on chicken/pork-bone stock with dried sardine (niboshi) and bonito (katsuobushi), and the tare/dipping pastes (soy-based black, miso-based red) are traditional wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated gluten-free process, allergen menu, or cross-contamination protocol was found for this location — not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:30, no closed days (無休)
name_ja: 麺屋武蔵 武骨外伝

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The black-style dipping broth is finished with a special soy-sauce-based tare (paste), a wheat-containing seasoning typical of shoyu-style ramen.

seats_text: Approximately 19 counter seats
vegan_detail: Broths are made from chicken/pork-bone stock combined with dried sardine (niboshi) and bonito flakes (katsuobushi) — not vegetarian or vegan. No plant-based tsukemen option was found advertised for this specific Shibuya branch (the company does sell a separate plant-based ramen line at other locations, but it is not confirmed here).
geocode_note: Geocoding not performed per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng set to null. Address 道玄坂2-8-5 is corroborated by multiple sources as roughly 2-5 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station, placing it well within 0.8km of Shibuya Scramble.

lead: らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-31-5 新宿ペガサス館102

bio.origin: Flagship ("honten") shop of the Hayashida ramen brand, run by the INGS restaurant group, located a one-minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station; the brand has since expanded to other Tokyo locations such as Ikebukuro.
bio.background: Hayashida built its name on a clear, richly flavored chicken "seiyu" (clear consommé-style) broth. Whole chicken and duck are simmered from early morning for hours, with the cooks carefully skimming off scum (aku) so the finished soup stays clean and translucent rather than cloudy.
bio.specialty: Tokusei shoyu ramen (特製醤油らぁ麺) — a soy-sauce ramen built on the shop's signature clear chicken-and-duck broth, served alongside a salt (shio) version and tsukemen.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen centers the bowl on pure chicken umami, using duck to add a rounder, sweeter backbone to the broth, then balances that roundness with a crisp, well-defined tare (seasoning sauce) so the soup doesn't taste flat.

anecdotes.text: Duck and whole chicken are used generously from early morning and simmered for a long time, with the cooks skimming the scum thoroughly to produce a highly refined, clear soup.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated blog/site summaries for らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店)

anecdotes.text: Bowls are pre-warmed before the soup and noodles are added, a small technique meant to keep the ramen from cooling too quickly once served.
anecdotes.source: https://www.google.com/search (aggregated blog/site summaries for らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店)

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku honten is known as a perpetually busy, queue-forming shop near Shinjuku Sanchome Station.

caveats: Payment methods (cash vs. card/IC) could not be independently confirmed from available sources — this is a ticket-vending-machine (食券制) ramen counter, which in central Tokyo increasingly but not universally accepts IC/electronic payment; treat cash_only as an estimate, not a confirmed fact. Seat count varies slightly between sources (15 vs 16). No gluten-free or vegan information exists for this shop in any source found — it is a conventional wheat-noodle, chicken/duck-broth ramen shop and is included here only as a standard/omnivore reference point, not as a GF/vegan-safe option.
closed_days: None listed (無休 / open every day); may close earlier than the posted closing time if the soup sells out.
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏そば・鶏清湯スープ）
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine typical of Tokyo ramen shops, so simple Japanese/pointing gets you through, but there's no need for fluent Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop: the noodles are wheat-based and the tare/seasoning for both the shoyu and shio versions relies on soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute, allergy-friendly menu, or dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination information was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null for downstream address-based geocoding. Address (新宿3-31-5, Shinjuku Ward) and cited access info (1-minute walk from Shinjuku Sanchome Station's A5 exit; 3-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station's central east exit) confirm the location is well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station (35.6896, 139.7006).
hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (無休 / no fixed closed day); may close early ("早仕舞い") if the soup runs out before closing time.
name_ja: らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku 3-chome)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu (soy sauce) ramen's tare is a standard Japanese soy-sauce-based seasoning; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari or GF alternative was mentioned in any source.

seats_text: Small counter-only shop; sources cite roughly 15-16 counter seats.
vegan_detail: The broth is a duck-and-chicken-bone clear consommé ("鶏清湯"), which is central to the shop's identity and offered in every ramen bowl. No vegetarian or vegan broth alternative, plant-based noodle, or meat-free menu item was found in any source, and additional non-vegan ingredients (e.g., chashu pork/chicken) are standard toppings. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

lead: ヴィーガン居酒屋 真さか
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町15-1 渋谷PARCO B1F

bio.origin: Opened in November 2019 when Shibuya PARCO reopened after renovation; a second location opened in Kyoto (Horikawa-Takatsuji) in December 2024.
bio.background: Run by Bird Feather Nobu Co. (株式会社バードフェザーノブ), the shop set out to recreate a classic, everyday Japanese izakaya — complete with a Showa-retro noren curtain entrance — using only plant-based ingredients throughout the kitchen.
bio.philosophy: The team wants vegan food to feel like normal, affordable izakaya fare rather than a special or expensive category, so that vegan and non-vegan customers can sit down and eat the same dishes together. In interviews the shop describes wanting to subvert the assumption that vegan cooking is difficult or costly to access, and to patiently walk first-time or unfamiliar customers through what 'vegan' actually means on the menu.
bio.specialty: Soy-meat karaage (plant-based 'fried chicken') — crispy outside and moist inside — served with a choice of about seven sauces, alongside vegan gyoza and lemon sours.
roles: Vegan izakaya restaurant, part of a small two-location group (Tokyo + Kyoto)

anecdotes.text: The restaurant keeps a retro Showa-era izakaya look with a noren curtain entrance, and draws daily interest from both regular Japanese customers and international visitors curious about vegan food.

Staff are described as taking care to walk unfamiliar or skeptical customers through the menu, addressing the common feeling that vegan food is 'unfamiliar' or 'looks difficult,' rather than assuming everyone already understands veganism.
The shop's stated goal is to 'subvert expectations in a good way,' building an izakaya where vegans and non-vegans can enjoy the same affordable, everyday dishes together.

closed_days: Officially "irregular" (不定休) — closures follow Shibuya PARCO's own building holidays, which are rare, so it is effectively open every day of the week during mall hours.
cuisine: Vegan Izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: It sits inside the very tourist-heavy Shibuya PARCO complex and multiple write-ups mention international visitors dining here, but no confirmed English-language menu was found, so basic Japanese/pointing may still be needed.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as Shibuya PARCO B1F, Udagawa-cho, Shibuya — roughly 400-500m from the Shibuya Scramble crossing, well within the 0.8km target radius. Lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or wheat-free certification was found. As a full-menu izakaya it very likely uses standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce in marinades and dipping sauces, and the signature soy-meat karaage is deep-fried, so cross-contact with wheat-based batter/flour in the fryer is probable. The restaurant is listed on WE TABLE, a Tokyo directory of allergy-conscious dining spots, suggesting some willingness to discuss allergens, but no specific celiac-safety statement (dedicated fryer, wheat-free tamari, etc.) was confirmed. Contact the restaurant directly before relying on this for celiac-level gluten avoidance.
hours_raw: 12:00-22:00 daily (Lunch 12:00-15:00 / Dinner 15:00-22:00; Last order food 21:00, drinks 21:30)
name_ja: ヴィーガン居酒屋 真さか
neighborhood: 渋谷 (Shibuya) — Udagawa-cho, inside Shibuya PARCO

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish on the menu is made using only plant-derived ingredients — the kitchen does not use meat, fish, dairy, or eggs at all, which removes the risk of animal-product cross-contact that vegan diners would face in a mixed kitchen.

positives.text: The restaurant is listed on WE TABLE, a Tokyo guide specifically curating restaurants that accommodate food allergies and special diets.
Also listed on Vegewel, a dedicated vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free restaurant directory for Japan.

seats_text: About 20 seats (table seating)
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based menu (20+ dishes always available) — soy-meat karaage, vegan gyoza, and other classic izakaya small plates, all made without meat, fish, dairy, or eggs. As a 100%-vegan kitchen there is no hidden dashi/katsuobushi (bonito) risk from the standard menu.

cuisine: スパイスカレー・ワインバル (Spice curry and wine bar)
neighborhood: 水道橋 / 東京ドームシティ (Suidobashi / Tokyo Dome City), Bunkyo-ku
address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 東京ドームシティ 黄色いビル2階 FOOD STADIUM TOKYO内
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked). Address places it inside Tokyo Dome City's "yellow building", 2F, within the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall — multiple sources describe it as a 3-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit, well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark.
seats_text: 24-seat dedicated island counter inside the FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall
hours_raw: 11:00〜23:00（FOOD L.O. 22:00／DRINK L.O. 22:30）。年中無休。12月31日のみ翌2:00まで延長営業、1月1日は通常通り営業。
closed_days: 無休 (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering and menu appear Japanese-only in available sources; however it sits inside Tokyo Dome City's food hall, a major tourist-trafficked complex, so staff are likely used to some non-Japanese-speaking visitors even though this isn't confirmed.
vegan_detail: The three rotating signature curries are all meat-based — lamb & spinach keema, chicken-wing and hazelnut butter chicken, and Ibaraki-grown Sanemon-pork vindaloo — served with jasmine rice, three types of spice achar pickles, a spiced egg, and herb salad. No vegetable-only or plant-based curry option, and no vegan menu, was found in the official site, press materials, or blog coverage reviewed. Hidden animal ingredients (butter, dairy in the butter-chicken curry, egg) are explicitly present, so this is not vegan-friendly based on current information.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free labeling, or allergen chart was found in the official site, opening press release, or blog reviews checked. As a "spice curry" concept the sauces may or may not use a wheat-flour roux (unconfirmed either way), and the achar pickles, spiced egg preparation, and any soy sauce used are not documented for wheat content. Celiac diners should ask staff directly about roux composition and cross-contact before ordering — this cannot be confirmed as safe from available sources.

bio.origin: Tokyo (opened by the Tokyo-based MARUGO restaurant group)
bio.background: MARUGO S opened December 9, 2025 as the MARUGO group's first food-hall concept store, inside the newly built FOOD STADIUM TOKYO on the 2nd floor of Tokyo Dome City's 'yellow building,' a few minutes from JR Suidobashi Station. The name's 'S' is said to stand for spice, stadium, start, standing, and special. Rather than a normal shop, it runs a dedicated 24-seat island counter within the shared food hall, where staff handle the order-to-serve process directly.
bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating spice curry, served single or as a three-way combination plate (1,800 yen) — lamb & spinach keema, chicken-wing and hazelnut butter chicken, and Ibaraki Sanemon-pork vindaloo — with jasmine rice, three types of spice achar pickles, a spiced egg, and herb salad.
bio.philosophy: Builds on the MARUGO group's French-Italian bistro roots while layering in Asian and Cajun spices and herbs for bar-style food meant to pair with wine, beer, and highballs; the group is known for taking care with slow-prepared venison and lamb.

anecdotes.text: A blog reviewer described MARUGO S as a hidden-gem seating option inside the often fully-booked FOOD STADIUM TOKYO food hall, noting the three-way curry combo's flavor is approachable and not aggressively spiced, making it easy to eat.

The shop is also known for a delicate 'snow' dessert — yogurt-based shaved ice with fresh berries, pistachio sauce, and espuma — that reportedly must be eaten within about five minutes before it melts.

caveats: No official allergen chart, gluten-free menu, or vegan menu was located for MARUGO S in the Tabelog listing, official site, opening press release, or blog coverage reviewed. Menu is described as rotating monthly, so specific dishes may change. Direct Tabelog/official-site page fetches were blocked by network policy in this environment (403), so details rely on search-engine synthesis of Tabelog, the MARUGO S official site, Tokyo Dome City's shop guide, the group's opening press release (atpress/newscast), and one independent blog review — cross-checked across multiple of those sources for the address, hours, and menu facts reported here.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-1-4 京王百貨店新宿店8F

bio.origin: Nikko, Tochigi (spring water and soybean sourcing)
bio.background: Tofu Ryori Kichiza is a tofu/yuba specialty restaurant located on the 8th-floor dining floor of Keio Department Store's Shinjuku store, part of a small group of "Kichiza" tofu restaurants (a sister location operates in Kioicho) run under Create Restaurants Holdings. The kitchen builds its cooking around Nikko tofu, made with domestic soybeans and spring water associated with Nikko, a region historically known for clean mountain water used in tofu-making.
bio.specialty: Nikko tofu (日光豆冨) and kumiage yuba (fresh-scooped tofu skin), served as part of multi-course kaiseki-style set menus
bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes seasonal ingredients prepared with care around its signature Nikko-water tofu and yuba, in a traditional Japanese dining setting with private-room options.

anecdotes.text: Course menus feature tofu prepared multiple ways alongside dishes such as dobin-mushi (steamed savory custard/soup), tuna and yuba sashimi, fried yuba rolls, and beef grilled on a magnolia leaf, with staff noted by reviewers as attentive and dressed in traditional attire.
The clientele is reported to be predominantly women, reflecting its popularity as a refined lunch/dinner destination inside the department store.

caveats: Menu is tofu/yuba-centric but not vegetarian: reported course dishes include tuna & yuba sashimi and beef grilled on magnolia leaf, so this is not a vegan or shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) restaurant despite the tofu specialty framing. No source found confirming gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce; standard Japanese kaiseki cooking typically uses wheat-containing shoyu and bonito-based dashi unless stated otherwise. Seat count, precise L.O. time (21:00 vs 20:30 cited inconsistently across secondary sources), and reservation policy came from aggregated web-search snippets rather than a single directly fetched primary page, since the official site, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, and Create Restaurants pages all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch. Address, phone, and general hours were corroborated across multiple independent search snippets.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day found; follows Keio Department Store Shinjuku's own operating calendar (department stores typically close only on rare occasions such as New Year's Day) — confirm before visiting.
cuisine: Tofu and yuba (tofu-skin) cuisine — traditional Japanese kaiseki-style tofu specialty restaurant, using Nikko-sourced tofu and spring water, with course menus that also include some fish and meat dishes
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Japanese department-store restaurant where menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese, though the Tabelog listing has an English page and picture-menu courses should be navigable for non-Japanese speakers with some help.
gf_detail: No source confirms gluten-free accommodation. As a traditional Japanese kaiseki-style restaurant, dishes very likely rely on standard shoyu (soy sauce, which typically contains wheat) and dashi that may include bonito/other seasonings; fried yuba items imply a shared fryer of unconfirmed dedicated status. Tofu itself is naturally gluten-free, but sauces, dips, and cooking liquids are the main risk points — celiac diners should ask staff directly about ingredients before ordering, ideally in Japanese or with a translated allergy card.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. reported as 21:00 in some sources, 20:30 in the Keio Department Store floor guide), daily, aligned with department-store operating hours

positives.text: Core ingredient (Nikko tofu/yuba) is naturally gluten-free and plant-based, giving a strong base to work from if staff confirm sauce and dashi ingredients.
