Vegans could likely request tofu/yuba-only a la carte items, but dashi used in cooking liquids commonly contains bonito (katsuobushi) in traditional Japanese kaiseki cooking, and this has not been confirmed either way for this restaurant — ask staff directly about dashi and sauce ingredients.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区円山町13-9 メゾン若林B103

bio.background: A small basement set-meal shop near Shinsen Station that changes character through the day: at lunchtime it runs as a slightly more considered teishoku-ya, and by evening the mood shifts into a calm, low-key drinking spot for the neighborhood.
bio.specialty: Daily-changing teishoku built around a simple grilled or simmered main — such as tsuke-yaki salmon — served with about seven vegetable-forward side dishes, rice, and miso soup; an omakase course expands this to seven small dishes plus a main, rice, and soup.
bio.philosophy: Rather than a fixed menu, the kitchen rotates through home-style Japanese dishes each day, with particular attention to the small vegetable side dishes that accompany the main plate.

anecdotes.text: A local food blogger titled their review of the shop "the meticulous cooking makes you want to sit up straighter out of respect," describing the care put into the small side dishes.
A dining writeup described it as a hidden local favorite near Shinsen worth discovering for its teishoku sets.

caveats: No documented gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found; this is a traditional Japanese set-meal diner using standard soy sauce and bonito-based dashi. Small discrepancies exist across secondary sources on exact dinner start time (18:30 vs 19:00) and building unit number (B1F vs B103); the address itself (円山町13-9, Maison Wakabayashi) was consistent across multiple independent sources.
closed_days: 日曜日（月曜はランチ休み・ディナーのみ営業）
cuisine: 定食・和食（地元の小さな食堂）
cultural_comfort_note: Menu, signage, and reviews are entirely in Japanese with no evidence of English support, so basic Japanese or pointing at the daily specials board will help.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng set to null. Address and multiple sources place it within about 2 minutes' walk of Shinsen Station and about 9 minutes' walk from Shibuya Station, i.e. well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target point.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere. This is a traditional Japanese home-style diner; dishes almost certainly use regular soy sauce (contains wheat) and bonito/dashi-based miso soup and sauces, with no indication of a gluten-free alternative. Not a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff — and even then, cross-contamination in a small home-style kitchen is likely unavoidable.
neighborhood: 神泉（渋谷区円山町）／渋谷駅徒歩9分
seats_text: Small counter and a couple of tables, about 13 seats total
vegan_detail: Set meals are centered on a fish or meat main dish and miso soup, and traditional dashi in Japanese home cooking is typically bonito (fish)-based. No vegan menu, vegetable-only teishoku option, or dashi substitution has been documented at this shop. Not a reliable choice for vegan diners without direct confirmation, and even then hidden animal-based dashi in side dishes is a real risk.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町1-1-16 庭のホテル東京 1F

bio.origin: Grew out of the traditional Japanese inn (ryokan) that occupied the site before it became the boutique Hotel Niwa Tokyo; the restaurant now stands as a separate tile-roofed building beside the hotel entrance, reached by a stone path, overlooking an inner courtyard garden.
bio.background: Yukuri carries forward the full-course kaiseki dining tradition of the property's ryokan predecessor while updating it for a contemporary boutique-hotel setting near Suidobashi Station.
bio.specialty: Monthly-changing kaiseki course built around seafood delivered directly from the Boso peninsula and Edo-vegetables sourced from nearby farms, finished with the chef's own hand-cut soba noodles.
bio.philosophy: The name Yukuri is an old Japanese word for "the bonds between people"; it was chosen with the hope that guests would meet, form connections, and unwind over an unhurried meal. The head chef travels personally to source regions to select seasonal ingredients, then prepares them with a blend of traditional technique and modern sensibility so that each monthly course reads like a seasonal narrative.

anecdotes.text: Each monthly kaiseki course is described as woven with a theme and story like an illustrated tale (e-monogatari), meant to be experienced dish by dish with all five senses.
A seasonal opening dish (hajime no gozai) was highlighted by the hotel as evoking the scenery of winter through its texture and natural sweetness.

roles: head chef sources ingredients personally from producing regions

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations, menus, or cross-contamination protocols were found in any source (official site, Tabelog, Ikyu, Hotpepper, or reviews). This is a traditional seafood-centric kaiseki restaurant; assessments of GF/vegan suitability are inferred from general Japanese-kaiseki practice (soy sauce, dashi, tempura typically contain wheat/bonito), not confirmed statements from the restaurant. Diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should call ahead (03-3295-4321) before booking.
closed_days: Sunday dinner service is closed; additional irregular dinner closures occur in some months (e.g., specific dates were noted in May, and Tuesdays in June) — confirm current closures by phone before visiting.
cuisine: 懐石・日本料理・Kaiseki/Japanese
cultural_comfort_note: Housed inside a boutique hotel accustomed to international guests (Tabelog and reservation platforms list an English-language page), but no confirmation of fully English-fluent floor service was found, so expect basic English support rather than guaranteed fluency.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce was mentioned in any source. As a traditional kaiseki restaurant, dishes likely include soy-sauce-based seasonings, dashi, and tempura (all commonly containing wheat) alongside the restaurant's signature hand-cut soba (wheat-containing, cross-contact risk for anyone avoiding gluten). Celiac diners should call ahead to discuss ingredients and cross-contamination before booking.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirmed as 4-minute walk from Suidobashi Station east exit (JR Chuo/Sobu line), well within the 0.8km Suidobashi radius.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:30–22:00 (course L.O. 20:30, food L.O. 21:00, drinks L.O. 21:30). Closed for dinner on Sundays, with additional irregular dinner closures in some months.
name_ja: 日本料理 縁（ゆくり）
neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (Suidōbashi area)
seats_text: 30 seats total, including one private tsuboniwa-garden-view room seating up to 6
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or modification policy was found. The restaurant's signature offerings center on seafood delivered fresh from Boso and a seasonal kaiseki course, which in traditional Japanese cooking typically relies on bonito-based dashi. No mention of plant-based dashi alternatives or vegan course substitutions was found in official or third-party sources; vegan diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区鶯谷町15-10 ロイヤルパレス渋谷 1F(103号)

bio.origin: Kyoto (b. 1967)
bio.background: Oda Akinobu, a Kyoto-born (1967) former music-magazine editor and international touring/artist-booking coordinator, opened Nagi Shokudo in December 2007 in Shibuya's quiet Uguisudani-cho backstreet. He later authored several books about the shop, including "渋谷のすみっこでベジ食堂" ("A Veg Diner in a Corner of Shibuya") and vegetable-recipe collections.
bio.specialty: なぎAセット (Nagi A-Set): soft-cooked brown rice, miso soup, soy-meat karaage (fried soy-meat 'chicken'), and a rotating daily vegetable side — the restaurant's signature set meal (about ¥1,100). Lunch offers a choice of 3 from roughly 14 rotating vegetable side dishes.
bio.philosophy: Oda noticed that many of the touring overseas musicians he booked and worked with were vegetarian, and that Japan had very few vegetarian/vegan restaurants with bold, rice- and drink-friendly flavors. He set out to create a place serving hearty, flavorful all-vegan food where music-scene friends could also gather casually, like a friend's living room.

anecdotes.text: Oda opened the restaurant after realizing, through his work booking international touring artists (many of whom were vegetarian), that Japan lacked vegetarian restaurants serving richly flavored food that pairs well with rice and alcohol — and wanted a spot where his music-industry friends could also gather.

The dining room has a lived-in, 'friend's house' atmosphere with indie rock playing, table seating plus a small raised tatami-style nook, and draws a mixed crowd of women, older men, Japanese residents, and foreign tourists/expats alike.

caveats: Hours follow a consistent weekly pattern but the shop takes irregular ad-hoc closures (不定休) — confirm via phone/official site before a special trip. No independent source confirms gluten-free-specific measures (wheat-free soy sauce, dedicated fryer, or formal cross-contamination protocol); the "no meat/fish/egg/dairy" claim is well corroborated, but celiac-level gluten safety was not documented and should be confirmed directly with staff. Reservation policy and card/cashless acceptance were not explicitly documented in sources reviewed; treat as unconfirmed.
closed_days: 不定休 (no fixed weekly closed day; irregular ad-hoc closures — check ahead)
cuisine: All-vegan Japanese shokudo (set meals, izakaya-style small plates) — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy anywhere on the menu; signature soy-meat karaage and rotating vegetable side dishes.
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, casual neighborhood spot popular with both Japanese regulars and foreign residents/tourists (it is one of the few fully vegan options near Shibuya Station), though no source confirms an English menu or English-speaking staff.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu is rice- and vegetable-forward (brown rice sets, miso soup, vegetable sides), but standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce and miso typically contain wheat, and no source confirms wheat-free substitutes, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocols for gluten. Diners with celiac disease or severe gluten sensitivity should call ahead and confirm specific dishes/condiments directly with staff.
geocode_note: Address (渋谷区鶯谷町15-10) is near Shinsen Station, roughly 0.6-0.8km from Shibuya Scramble crossing — within the target radius. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; geocoding to be done downstream from address_ja.
hours_raw: 12:00-16:00 (L.O.15:00) / 18:00-23:00 (L.O.22:30); Sunday closes at 16:00 (lunch service only, no dinner). 定休日: 不定休 (irregular closures).
neighborhood: 渋谷区鶯谷町 (Uguisudani-cho, Shibuya) — near Shinsen Station, about a 6-7 min walk from Shibuya Station

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares no meat, fish, or animal products at all — every dish on the menu is vegan by default, so there is no risk of animal-product cross-contact from shared equipment with non-vegan dishes.

positives.text: The restaurant uses no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish, making it explicitly safe for customers with egg and dairy allergies as well as vegans/vegetarians.

seats_text: Small casual dining room with table seating plus a small raised (tatami-style) seating nook; exact seat count not documented in sources reviewed.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan menu: multiple independent sources confirm the restaurant uses no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish (肉・魚・卵など動物性食品を使わない). The signature "Nagi A-Set" includes brown rice, miso soup, soy-meat karaage, and rotating vegetable sides; lunch offers ~14 rotating vegetable side dishes with a choice of 3. Desserts are also stated to be dairy- and egg-free. No source specifically confirms dashi is kombu/vegetable-only rather than bonito-based, but the consistent "no fish" claim across sources implies bonito-free preparation.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-17-8 ドメス水道橋ビル1階

bio.background: Tsukemen zuppa opened in September 2021, a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station, as a compact counter-only noodle shop built entirely around meat- and fish-free tsukemen, ramen, and soy-milk tantanmen.
bio.specialty: Vegetable tsukemen (dipping noodles) — a soup and topping set made from vegetables and soy-based ingredients, served with a boiled-egg-and-mayonnaise side dish and fried lotus root.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leaves meat and fish off the entire menu and builds its soups and toppings from vegetables and soy protein instead, positioning itself as Suidobashi's only vegan-focused tsukemen and ramen shop; dishes can also be prepared without the "five pungent" alliums (garlic, onion, etc.) on request.

anecdotes.text: Word of the all-vegetable menu has spread mainly by mouth among vegan visitors from overseas, who make up much of the counter's crowd.
The standard tsukemen set arrives with a boiled egg in mayonnaise-style sauce and a fried lotus root alongside a half salad; telling staff you want the vegan version gets the egg left off.

caveats: WebFetch was returning HTTP 403 for every host during this session (including plain example.com), so this record could not be built from direct page fetches — it is instead triangulated from WebSearch's synthesized snippets across roughly ten independent listings/blogs (Tabelog EN, Vegewel, Hachidory, ramendb, Japanese Heart, tsukemen-tabetai, veg-cat, note.com, chawanbushi, Kudan Japanese School). Core facts (address, hours, price, 7 seats, ticket-machine ordering, egg-removal for vegan) were corroborated across multiple of these sources. Payment method (cash vs. cashless) and any reservation policy were not confirmed by any source, so cash_only/reservation_required are best-effort defaults, not verified facts. No source mentioned any gluten-free noodle, tamari, or dedicated-fryer accommodation.
closed_days: 月曜定休（Closed Mondays）
cuisine: Vegan/vegetarian tsukemen & ramen (soy-milk tantanmen, soy-meat miso/shoyu/salt ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-only ticket machine at the entrance, but the shop is well known among vegan foreign visitors and staff are used to fielding simple vegan-modification requests by ear.
geocode_note: Network geocoding is disabled in this environment; lat/lng are left null for downstream derivation from address_ja. Multiple independent sources describe the shop as a one-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station at 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-17-8, which places it well within the Suidobashi-station 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No source mentions a gluten-free noodle, tamari (wheat-free soy sauce), or any dedicated gluten-free accommodation. The tsukemen and ramen use standard wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce and miso ramen bases most likely contain wheat-based shoyu. The shop's flexibility is aimed at vegan/vegetarian and allium-free requests, not gluten. This is not a place to rely on for celiac-safe dining without directly confirming details with staff in Japanese.
name_ja: つけ麺 ズッパ 水道橋店
neighborhood: 神田三崎町 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda-ku

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base menu already excludes meat and fish across every dish, using soy "meat" and soy milk instead of animal protein and dashi, so there is no shared meat/fish cooking line to cross with a vegan order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegan modifications — such as leaving the boiled egg off the tsukemen set, or preparing the soy-milk tantanmen without the pungent alliums — are made to order once you tell staff after buying your ticket at the vending machine.

positives.text: The kitchen uses no meat or fish anywhere on the menu; soup and toppings are vegetable- and soy-protein-based throughout, marketed as guilt-free even if you finish the dipping broth.
Described as Suidobashi's only vegan-focused tsukemen and ramen shop, popular with vegan and vegetarian diners including foreign tourists.

seats_text: Counter only, 7 seats
vegan_detail: Every dish on the standard menu already excludes meat and fish, using soy "meat" and soy milk in place of animal protein and dashi. The default tsukemen set does come with a boiled egg in mayonnaise-style sauce, but telling staff you want it vegan gets the egg left off; the soy-milk tantanmen and soy-meat ramen variants can likewise be requested vegan and without the pungent alliums (garlic, onion, etc.). No source mentions bonito/katsuo dashi anywhere on the menu.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿2-5-8 志村ビル4F

bio.chef_name: Numanami (owner, given name as referenced in press coverage)
bio.background: Opened April 8, 2016 a one-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station. The restaurant is essentially run by its owner alone, who cooks and hosts the room herself, which is why both lunch and dinner are reservation-based rather than walk-in.
bio.specialty: A faux 'Spanish omelette' made from pumpkin, mountain yam, and tofu in place of egg, and cilantro (pakuchi) gyoza dumplings, described as the most popular item on the menu.
bio.philosophy: The name Kiboko is Swahili for hippopotamus: an animal with a calm, grass-eating image that is in fact powerful, and was revered in ancient Egypt as a protective goddess of women and children. The owner chose the name because she wanted to build a place that supports and heals hard-working women through honest, plant-based food and natural wine.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name comes from the Swahili word for hippopotamus, chosen for the animal's gentle-but-powerful image and its ancient Egyptian association with protecting women and children — the owner wanted Kiboko to be a place that nourishes and heals busy working women.
The owner has hosted in-house workshops on traditional crafts such as kintsugi (gold-seam pottery repair) and miso-making alongside the restaurant's regular service.
The most recommended dish is a Spanish-omelette-style plate made without egg (pumpkin, mountain yam, tofu), and the No. 1 most-ordered item is the coriander/cilantro gyoza dumplings.

caveats: Hours and even the shop's public-facing name have shifted over the restaurant's ~10-year run (older press calls it "slowfood&wine KiboKo"; Tabelog now lists it as "お野菜小皿料理のワインバル KiboKo"), so listings on secondary aggregator sites are inconsistent — the hours below are taken from the current official site (vegan-kiboko.com) rather than the older magazine writeups. Both lunch and dinner are reservation-only (lunch by the day before, dinner fully by reservation), so walk-ins are not reliable. Not a certified celiac-safe kitchen — call ahead (03-6380-1709) if you have celiac disease rather than just a preference.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday
cuisine: ビーガン・野菜小皿料理・自然派ワインバル (Vegan vegetable small-plates and natural wine bar)
cultural_comfort_note: Roughly 40% of the clientele are visitors from abroad (many vegan themselves) and an English menu is available, so English speakers should feel comfortable — just be sure to reserve ahead since the small, owner-run kitchen doesn't take casual walk-ins.
geocode_note: Address confirmed against the official site and Tabelog listing; the shop sits about a 1-2 minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station, roughly 0.4km from the Shinjuku Station reference point — within the target radius. Not geocoded (lat/lng left null per instructions).
gf_detail: The restaurant is indexed by Vegewel's gluten-free filter and multiple guides describe it as having gluten-free menu items, but it is not a dedicated celiac-safe kitchen. It's a small space essentially run by one person, so cross-contact between dishes is possible, and no source confirms wheat-free soy sauce or a dedicated fryer. Diners with celiac disease (rather than a general gluten preference) should call ahead (03-6380-1709) to confirm which specific dishes are truly gluten-free and how they're prepared.
hours_raw: 水〜日 12:00〜17:00（ランチは前日までの要予約）、18:00〜22:00（完全予約制）。定休日：月・火曜日
name_en: Oyasai Kozara Ryori no Wine Bar KiboKo (also known as Slowfood & Wine KiboKo)
name_ja: お野菜小皿料理のワインバル KiboKo（キボコ／旧称 slowfood&wine KiboKo）
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built as 100% vegan from the ground up — no meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or bonito/katsuo dashi is used anywhere in the kitchen — so there is essentially no cross-contact risk from animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Handmade dishes use organic, additive-free vegetables and seasonings, and the concept was built around fully plant-based cooking rather than adapting an omnivorous menu.
Listed on Vegewel's vegetarian/vegan/gluten-free restaurant directory, which filters specifically for gluten-free-friendly menu items.

seats_text: Small, intimate room seating around 18 guests, mostly counter-facing the kitchen
vegan_detail: The kitchen is fully vegan by design — every dish, including sauces and any dashi-style bases, is made without meat, fish, dairy, eggs, or honey. This isn't a case of an omnivorous menu with a few vegan substitutions; the whole restaurant concept is animal-free, so there's no hidden bonito/katsuo dashi or fish sauce to watch for.

lead: 東京純豆腐 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-3-3 恩田セントラルビルB1階

bio.origin: Korean cuisine, chain concept inspired by sundubu encountered in New York
bio.background: Tokyo Sundubu (東京純豆腐) is a Korean soft-tofu-stew (sundubu jjigae) specialty chain run by Beans One Company. According to the company's own history page, the founders fell for sundubu while in New York and wanted Japanese diners to feel that same excitement, which led to the brand's launch in 2006 — the first four shops, opened that year, included Aoyama, Shibuya, Azabudai and this Shinjuku location. The company describes its goal as making sundubu into a household 'national dish' in Japan and, as of 2026, marks 20 years as (by its own account) the largest sundubu-specialty chain in the country.
bio.specialty: Made-to-order sundubu jjigae (soft tofu stew): diners choose a soup base (salt, miso, or white), a spice level (1-6), and rice portion. Tofu is made fresh in-house daily from domestic soybeans and served at the soft, just-set stage before it fully firms.
bio.philosophy: In-house, daily-made tofu from domestic soybeans, paired with a long-simmered broth (beef-bone base with seafood and vegetables, gochujang-seasoned) that the company markets as its point of pride ('だしにこだわったスープ').
roles: Korean sundubu (soft tofu stew) specialty restaurant, part of a nationwide chain

anecdotes.text: The founders discovered sundubu while in New York and wanted to bring that same excitement back to Japan, which is what led to Tokyo Sundubu's 2006 launch, with this Shinjuku shop among the brand's first locations.

caveats: WebFetch returned HTTP 403 for every primary source in this environment (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Hot Pepper, Hitosara, tokyo-sundubu.net, and an allergy-chart blog post), so no page could be fetched directly. All facts below come from search-engine result snippets/summaries that were cross-checked across multiple independent queries until they converged consistently (this matters especially here because there is a similarly-named sister branch, 東京純豆腐 新宿東口店, at a different address/phone/seat-count that early queries initially conflated with this one — the data below has been reconciled to the 新宿3-3-3 / Onda Central Bldg / tabelog ID 13024857 location specifically). Dietary/allergen details (broth allergen list, allergy chart at register) come from chain-wide sourcing (a blog post about the Ikebukuro branch and general ingredient search results) rather than a Shinjuku-store-specific document, so treat gf/vegan specifics as chain-level, not location-verified.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; closed for New Year holidays
cuisine: 韓国料理・スンドゥブ（豆腐鍋）専門店
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is simple (pick soup type, spice level 1-6, and rice amount) and the chain has an English-language Tabelog listing, but in-store menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese, so basic Japanese or pointing/apps will help.
gf_detail: This is not a gluten-free-oriented restaurant. Chain-wide allergen disclosures for the sundubu broth list wheat, soy, and sesame among the ingredients (the soup is built on a gochujang-seasoned, beef-bone-and-seafood base), and abura-age (fried tofu) is used in the standard broth. No dedicated gluten-free menu or prep area is documented for this location. Some branches keep a printed allergy/ingredient chart near the register that staff can walk through with guests on request, but this has not been confirmed specifically for the Shinjuku branch. Celiac diners should ask staff to check the allergy chart for wheat before ordering and should not assume any dish is wheat-free by default.
hours_raw: Mon-Sun, holidays and pre-holidays: 11:00-22:00 (food last order 21:30)
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment). Address is confirmed via multiple independent listings as Shinjuku 3-3-3, Onda Central Building B1F, a 1-minute walk from Toei Shinjuku Line Shinjuku-sanchome Station — this places it roughly 400m from Shinjuku Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
name_ja: 東京純豆腐 新宿店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目（新宿区新宿3丁目）

gf_cross_contamination.text: The standard sundubu broth used chain-wide contains clams, fried tofu (abura-age), egg, and shellfish extract, and is prepared in a shared kitchen alongside meat and seafood dishes, so cross-contact with wheat/gluten-containing ingredients is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chain-wide allergen listings for the soup include wheat (小麦), soy, and sesame among the ingredients used in the gochujang-based broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All standard sundubu broths include clams, egg, and shellfish extract as base ingredients, so the shared broth is not suitable for vegan diners and cross-contact with animal products is inherent to the standard menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: At least one Tokyo Sundubu branch (Ikebukuro) keeps a printed allergy/ingredient chart near the entrance/register that staff can check with guests on request; this practice is not separately confirmed for the Shinjuku branch.

positives.text: Diners can customize spice level (1-6), soup type, and rice portion at order time, which gives some control over what goes into the bowl even though the base broth itself is fixed.

seats_text: 21 seats, including counter seating
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The base broth used for every standard sundubu on the menu contains clams, fried tofu (abura-age), egg, and shellfish extract, and the wider chain allergen list for the soup also includes chicken, beef, and pork stock components. No vegan or vegetarian sundubu variant is documented for this location; one review noted that diners with a sundubu allergy sometimes order bibimbap instead, but that dish is not confirmed to be vegan either (it typically includes egg and/or meat). Vegan diners should not expect a safe default option here without extensive modification, if any is possible at all.

lead: 魚がし日本一 立喰寿司 渋谷道玄坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-9-1

bio.origin: Part of the "Uogashi Nihon-ichi" chain run by Nippan Co. — Japan's pioneering standing-sushi (tachigui-zushi) concept, whose flagship store opened near Shimbashi Station on June 19, 1989.
bio.background: Uogashi Nihon-ichi popularized the idea of hand-pressed Edomae sushi served fast, at a standing counter, for working commuters — a deliberate throwback to the roadside sushi stalls (sushi yatai) of Edo-period Tokyo roughly 200 years earlier. The Shibuya Dogenzaka branch is one of many locations across the chain, sourcing fish daily from markets stretching from Hokkaido in the north to Kyushu in the south.
bio.specialty: Hand-pressed nigiri sushi made to order at the standing counter, priced individually by piece so diners can eat as much or as little as they like.
bio.philosophy: The chain's guiding principle since its founding is "nana-tate" (七たて) — seven kinds of freshness: freshly caught, freshly filleted, freshly simmered, freshly grilled, freshly fried, freshly cooked rice, and freshly hand-pressed. The idea is to serve quality, reasonably priced ingredients simply — cold food served cold, hot food served hot.

anecdotes.text: The chain's June 19, 1989 opening date at its original Shimbashi store is commemorated on Japanese trivia calendars as related to the standing-sushi format's debut.

caveats: Sources disagree on exact operating hours: several aggregators list Mon-Fri 11:00-23:00 / Sat 11:00-22:00 / Sun 11:00-21:30, while at least one other lists a flat daily 11:30-2:00. Confirm current hours by phone (03-5428-4851) before visiting. No GF/vegan-specific statements from the restaurant itself were found; dietary assessment below is inferred from the general nature of a traditional Edomae standing-sushi menu and should be confirmed with staff on site.
closed_days: None found — appears to operate every day of the year (nenjū mukyū) per multiple listings
cuisine: Tachigui-zushi (standing-counter Edomae sushi) — hand-pressed nigiri sushi, priced per piece, fish sourced daily from markets nationwide
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language menu and point-and-order style make it workable for non-Japanese speakers, but the fast-paced standing-counter format and staff interactions are primarily in Japanese.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); address 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-9-1 is corroborated by multiple sources as roughly 1-5 minutes on foot from Shibuya Station / the Shibuya Scramble crossing, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated prep area is indicated anywhere. As a traditional Edomae sushi counter, nigiri is typically brushed with a soy-based tare/nikiri sauce that ordinarily contains wheat, and the kitchen also handles fried items, so cross-contact risk is likely. Nothing here is confirmed safe for celiac diners — it would need to be asked about directly at the counter, which may be difficult given the fast, Japanese-language-driven service style.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-23:00; Sat 11:00-22:00; Sun 11:00-21:30, per most listings (one source instead states a flat daily 11:30-2:00) — open year-round with no listed regular closed days. Confirm by phone before visiting.
name_en: Uogashi Nihon-ichi Tachigui Sushi Shibuya Dogenzaka
name_ja: 魚がし日本一 立喰寿司 渋谷道玄坂店
neighborhood: Shibuya, Dogenzaka
seats_text: 15 standing counter positions — this is a tachigui (stand-and-eat) sushi bar with no chairs or seated tables
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centric sushi bar — nearly every item on the menu is fish or shellfish based, and rice/other preparations likely rely on dashi containing bonito (katsuobushi), which is standard in Edomae sushi kitchens. No vegetable-only rolls or vegan-specific items were confirmed in available sources. Not a realistic destination for vegan diners without asking staff directly, and options would likely be very limited even then.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-12-5 ニュー千代田ビル1F

bio.background: The owner previously worked in sales at a manufacturing company before moving into the restaurant industry, training for about two years at a restaurant in Nakameguro before opening Ashura near Suidōbashi in Kanda Misakichō in October 2023.
bio.specialty: Ashura's Kaihō Shūmai (阿酒羅の海宝焼売) - handmade shumai dumplings filled with simmered seasonal vegetables and topped with fresh seafood delivered directly from Toyosu Market.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's name comes from Ashura, the multi-armed Buddhist guardian deity, chosen to symbolize a wish to offer guests everything they might want - vegetables, seafood, meat, and sake. The logo's Ashura figure holds vegetables, fish, meat, and a sake cup in its outer arms while its front two hands are pressed together in a gesture of prayer and gratitude toward customers. The kitchen builds much of the menu around seasonal simmered vegetables (nimono) alongside seafood from Toyosu, aiming for food that lets guests eat and drink while staying health-conscious.

anecdotes.text: The name 阿酒羅 (Ashura) is drawn from the three-faced, six-armed Buddhist deity Asura, chosen to represent a wish to serve every food and drink a guest might want - vegetables, fish, meat, and sake - with the figure's front two hands held together in a gesture of gratitude toward customers.

Before opening Ashura, the owner worked in sales at a manufacturing company, then trained for roughly two years at a restaurant in Nakameguro before opening his own place near Suidōbashi in October 2023.

caveats: Restaurant confirmed as real and open via Tabelog listing plus multiple independent Japanese sources (official site, hotpepper, gourmet-calendar, hibana blog), but direct WebFetch of tabelog.com and the official owst.jp site returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so details below are drawn from search-engine summaries of those pages rather than direct page reads. Seat count is inconsistent across sources (20 vs 26 seats cited in different summaries) - reported as a range. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (dedicated menu, ingredient substitutions, cross-contamination protocol) was found anywhere; this appears to be a standard seafood/vegetable-forward creative izakaya with no dietary-accommodation program, so GF/vegan ratings are conservative defaults rather than confirmed facts.
closed_days: No regular closed day found in any source; appears to be open daily including Sundays and holidays.
cuisine: Creative izakaya centered on seasonal simmered vegetables (nimono) and seafood sourced directly from Toyosu Market, plus curated sake pairings; signature dish is a seafood-topped shumai.
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood izakaya with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff, so expect a Japanese-only ordering and reservation experience.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free substitutions, or dedicated fryer were mentioned in any source found. The signature dish is a wheat-wrapper shumai, and as a standard izakaya it almost certainly uses regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce and dashi throughout the menu. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 月～日・祝日・祝前日 17:00-23:30 (料理L.O. 22:30、ドリンクL.O. 23:00); one secondary source lists closing as late as 24:00 on some listings - primary/most consistent figure used is 23:30.
neighborhood: Suidōbashi / Kanda Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Approximately 20-26 seats (sources disagree), counter and table seating in a relaxed, intimate atmosphere.
vegan_detail: Simmered seasonal vegetables (nimono) are a prominent part of the menu, but the restaurant is explicitly seafood-centric (海鮮), the signature shumai is topped with seafood, and dashi used across the menu is very likely bonito-based. No vegan menu, plant-based substitutions, or vegan-safe dashi were confirmed in any source - ask staff directly before ordering.
geocode_note: Address (2-chōme-12-5 Kanda Misakichō, Chiyoda-ku, New Chiyoda Building 1F) is described in multiple sources as a 3-4 minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station's west exit, placing it well within 0.8km of the Suidōbashi Station landmark.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-11-6 エクレ新宿ビル 1F

bio.specialty: Overflowing sea urchin blancmange (こぼれるウニのブランマンジェ) and a rich uni cream pasta — the bistro's signature uni preparations
bio.philosophy: The chef, who is also a certified wine sommelier, wants guests to enjoy wine without formality or pretension (肩肘張らず気軽にワインを楽しんでいただきたい).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature sea urchin dish is the result of roughly 15 years of refinement, with the kitchen still tweaking the recipe to make it better.
The bistro is tucked just off Yasukuni-dori near Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit C3, just before a Starbucks, and is known locally as a casual spot for wine paired with generous portions of uni.

caveats: Hours and closed-days differ slightly across booking-platform listings (some show daily 18:00-23:00 with no closed days, others show a Tabelog-sourced weekly schedule plus a year-end closure); confirm directly before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations, menu, or staff-training information could be found on any source — this is a conventional French/Italian-leaning bistro built around sea urchin, cream, and wine, so it is not recommended for celiac or vegan diners without calling ahead. Could not directly fetch the Tabelog page itself (blocked); details are drawn from Tabelog listing titles/snippets and other booking/review sites (OZmall, Hot Pepper, 4travel, Gnavi) via search.
closed_days: Reported as irregular closures (不定休); one source also cites a year-end/New Year closure around 12/29-1/5. Another source states no fixed closed days. Confirm current schedule directly.
cuisine: フレンチ・ビストロ (French/Italian-leaning casual bistro, known for sea urchin (uni) dishes and wine)
cultural_comfort_note: A Japanese-run neighborhood bistro with Japanese-language menus and service; reservations are commonly made through Japanese booking platforms, so a translation app is useful for asking about ingredients.
geocode_note: Address (Shinjuku 3-11-6) and multiple sources confirm a 2-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit C3, along Yasukuni-dori — well within 0.8km of JR Shinjuku Station. Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; downstream geocoding from address_ja should place it inside the Shinjuku Station target circle.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated-fryer, or wheat-avoidance information was found on any source. The signature dishes are a cream-based sea urchin pasta (wheat pasta) and a blancmange, and the kitchen appears to be a standard French/Italian-style bistro line using flour-based sauces and bread service — there is no indication of gluten-free adaptation. Not recommended for celiac diners without calling ahead to confirm.
hours_raw: Tabelog-sourced: 月〜金 18:00〜23:30 (LO22:00) / 土 17:00〜23:30 (LO22:00) / 日 17:00〜22:30 (LO21:00) / 祝 18:00〜22:30 (LO21:00). Other listings (Hot Pepper/EPARK aggregation) show a simpler 18:00〜23:00 daily, no fixed closed day.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome)
seats_text: Approximately 26 seats, with private rooms available for groups of 8-12
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options were found on any source. This bistro's identity is built around sea urchin (uni) and cream-based dishes, both of which are core to its signature offerings, so it is not suitable for vegan diners.

lead: らーめん 鴨to葱 渋谷宮益坂店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-19-15 宮益坂ビルディング103

bio.origin: Part of the "Kamo to Negi" (鴨to葱, literally "Duck and Scallion") ramen chain, whose original shop opened under the elevated tracks between Okachimachi and Ueno in June 2017. The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka branch is a newer location that opened on May 11, 2025.
bio.background: Kamo to Negi built its reputation on a duck-only ramen concept: broth simmered each morning from nothing but duck bones, scallion, and water, with no added chemical seasoning (無化調). The Shibuya branch sits partway up Miyamasuzaka hill, a short walk from Shibuya Station, and quickly drew queues after opening.
bio.specialty: Confit Duck Ramen (コンフィ鴨らーめん) and the shop's well-known "drinkable oyako-don" (飲める親子丼) — a small rice bowl topped with duck and a barely-set, custard-soft egg meant to be eaten (almost drunk) with a spoon.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen limits the broth to three ingredients — duck, scallion, and water — sourcing duck from producers in Kyoto, Aomori, and Iwate, and finishing bowls with a soy-based tare. Diners choose two of three scallion preparations (including grilled thick white negi and Kyoto's Kujo negi) to customize each bowl. Noodles are a custom blend of whole-grain and white wheat flour made for the chain by an outside noodle maker.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya Miyamasuzaka opening in May 2025 drew a line out the door, with local coverage singling out the drinkable oyako-don as a must-order alongside the duck ramen.
A blogger who visited soon after opening described the duck broth as simple but deep, calling it a worthy addition to the neighborhood's ramen scene.
A longtime fan's essay on the original Ueno-Okachimachi shop traces the chain's story back to its 2017 opening under the train tracks.

caveats: Direct WebFetch of Tabelog and the official kamotonegi.com pages was blocked (HTTP 403) by the network's outbound proxy for this session, so details below are compiled from web-search result snippets covering multiple secondary sources (portal listings, local news blogs, review sites) rather than one fully-fetched authoritative page. Reported opening hours have also changed at least once (10:00-22:30 at the May 2025 opening, changed to 10:00-next day 4:00 as of June 23, 2025) and exact seat count varies slightly between sources (18 vs 19). No source — official or third-party — mentions any gluten-free or vegan accommodation; this is a duck-bone-broth ramen shop with wheat noodles and a soy-based tare, and should be treated as unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.
closed_days: None reported — open daily, no scheduled closures
cuisine: Ramen (duck specialty)
cultural_comfort_note: Order at an entrance ticket vending machine as at most Tokyo ramen shops; basic Japanese (or pointing at photos/kanji for menu items like 鴨らーめん) helps, though its Shibuya location means some staff English or a picture menu is plausible.
geocode_note: Address is Shibuya 2-19-15 (Miyamasuzaka Building), on the Miyamasuzaka slope roughly 400-500m from Shibuya Scramble Crossing — well within the 0.8km Shibuya target radius.
gf_detail: Noodles are a custom wheat/whole-grain-wheat blend (not gluten-free) and bowls are finished with a soy-based tare, which in Japan is very often wheat-containing soy sauce unless specified otherwise. No gluten-free noodle substitute, GF menu option, or allergen accommodation was mentioned in any source found. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 10:00-翌4:00 (18-hour operation, effective from June 23, 2025); originally 10:00-22:30 at the May 11, 2025 opening. No listed closed days.
name_en: Ramen Kamo to Negi Shibuya Miyamasuzaka
name_ja: らーめん 鴨to葱 渋谷宮益坂店
neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka, Shibuya)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen's finishing tare is soy-based; sources describing the shop as a "duck broth soy sauce ramen" specialist imply standard (wheat-containing) shoyu rather than a wheat-free tamari.

seats_text: Small shop, roughly 18-19 seats reported (a mix of ~12 counter seats and a few 2-person tables); exact figure not consistently confirmed across sources.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is made exclusively from duck bones, scallion, and water — there is no vegetable/kombu-based alternative broth. The menu centers on duck ramen, duck confit, duck chashu, and an egg-and-duck oyako-don. No vegan or vegetarian dish, and no plant-based broth option, was found in any source.

lead: タイガーデン 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町24-10 B1

bio.background: Thai Garden Shibuya (タイガーデン渋谷店) is a Bangkok-style Thai restaurant a few minutes' walk from Shibuya Station's Hachiko exit, staffed by chefs trained in Thailand. The restaurant has been certified as a "Thai Select" establishment by Thailand's Ministry of Commerce, a government mark given to restaurants abroad that serve authentically prepared Thai food.
bio.origin: Thailand (Bangkok-trained kitchen team)
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for the layered, herb-forward balance of sour, spicy, sweet and salty found in home-region Thai cooking, using shrimp paste, fish sauce and fresh herbs rather than a simplified 'Japanized' version of the cuisine, and will adjust spice level to taste.
bio.specialty: Pad Thai and Tom Yum Kung are the dishes most frequently singled out by diners; the Tom Yum Kung is noted for a rounded, not-too-sharp spiciness with a fragrant herbal aroma and complex sour balance.

anecdotes.text: A lunch visit report highlights the restaurant's Pad Thai lunch set (with salad, soup and dessert) as excellent value and a reason to choose this location among Shibuya's many Thai restaurants.
The Tom Yum Kung is described as having 'a rounded, mild spiciness, a refreshing herbal aroma, and a complex flavor combining sourness' rather than being aggressively hot.
The restaurant is listed as a Thai-government 'Thai Select' certified restaurant, a recognition for serving authentic Thai cuisine overseas.

caveats: Multiple JP restaurant-aggregator sites (Tabelog, Gurunavi, Hotpepper, ekiten, gorp, ethnic-magazine, thaiselect.jp) return 403/bot-block on direct fetch in this environment, so facts below were gathered via web-search snippets of those same pages rather than raw page fetches. The listed [official] site (thaigardenspice.com/thai-garden-shibuya) failed DNS resolution (ESERVFAIL) and could not be verified directly. No page found any explicit gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce claim — treat GF confidence as unverified/ask-only, important for celiac safety since Thai cooking commonly uses fish sauce, oyster sauce and soy-based sauces that may contain wheat. Vegan status likewise reflects only a general 'vegetarian requests accommodated' claim, not a confirmed vegan menu or avoidance of fish sauce/shrimp paste.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed days / open year-round), per multiple listing sites (Tabelog, enjoytokyo, gorp).
cuisine: Thai Cuisine, Vegetarian-Friendly
cultural_comfort_note: Located in the heart of Shibuya Center-gai, an area with heavy foreign foot traffic, and listed with an English-language Tabelog page, but no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff was found.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya-ku, Udagawa-cho 24-10) is in the Center-gai area of Shibuya, roughly 300-400m from Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595,139.7005) — well within the 0.8km target radius. Per instructions, no geocoding was performed; lat/lng left null for downstream address-based pin placement.
gf_detail: No source found any dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or explicit statement about wheat in soy sauce or oyster sauce. Rice noodles used for Pad Thai are naturally GF, but overall sauce composition is unconfirmed. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly before ordering.
name_ja: タイガーデン 渋谷店
neighborhood: 渋谷・センター街 (Shibuya, Center-gai)

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant accommodates requests to adjust spice level and vegetarian dietary requests, per multiple listing-site descriptions of the restaurant's service.

positives.text: Kitchen staff trained in Thailand and the restaurant holds Thai Select government certification for authentic Thai cuisine, suggesting attentiveness to traditional recipe composition.

seats_text: Approximately 77-80 seats total, including counter seating (12 seats), sunken-kotatsu style seating (up to 24), a semi-private tatami room (up to 6-8), and whole-floor buyout (up to 32).
vegan_detail: Only a general claim that the restaurant "accommodates vegetarian requests" was found (spice level and vegetarian adjustments); no source confirmed a dedicated vegan menu, or explicit avoidance of fish sauce (nam pla), shrimp paste (kapi), or oyster sauce — all of which are staples of Thai cooking and commonly hidden in sauces and curry pastes. Vegans should confirm specific dish ingredients with staff before ordering.

lead: お好み焼き・もんじゃ焼き お好みもんじゃ 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2丁目8-7

bio.background: This Suidōbashi branch is part of the Okonomi Monja chain operated by Daisho Group, a major Japanese restaurant company. It serves okonomiyaki and monjayaki (Japanese griddle-cooked savory pancakes) centered on a 2-hour all-you-can-eat, all-you-can-drink course offering 80+ dishes (okonomiyaki, monjayaki, yakisoba, yakiudon and more) plus 40+ drinks, served at horigotatsu (sunken-floor) tables suited to groups and parties.
bio.specialty: 2-hour all-you-can-eat okonomiyaki and monjayaki course (80+ dishes)

caveats: Tabelog EN page and several secondary listing pages (Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Gorp official microsite, EnjoyTokyo, Yahoo Map) all returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch tools in this environment, so details were corroborated via search-engine snippets/summaries of those same pages rather than direct page reads; address, phone (03-6261-2630), hours, and all-you-can-eat pricing were consistent across multiple independent sources (Hot Pepper, Daisho Group official store page, Gurunavi). Walk time to Suidōbashi Station is reported as 3 minutes in secondary sources (task brief says 2 minutes) — both are well within the 0.8km target radius. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found in any source; this is a standard wheat-flour-batter okonomiyaki/monjayaki chain restaurant with no indication of allergy accommodation.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed day; open year-round)
cuisine: Okonomiyaki & monjayaki (Japanese savory griddle pancakes), izakaya-style with all-you-can-eat/drink course
cultural_comfort_note: Standard Japanese chain izakaya experience with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff; ordering (especially the all-you-can-eat course) is likely Japanese-only.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding disabled); address confirmed as within walking distance of JR/Toei Suidōbashi Station via multiple listing sources, consistent with the target 0.8km radius.
gf_detail: Okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter here is standard wheat flour with no gluten-free alternative mentioned anywhere. The venue runs a shared-teppan all-you-can-eat format, which raises cross-contact risk further. No sources mention gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free soy sauce. Not recommended for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
name_ja: お好み焼き・もんじゃ焼き お好みもんじゃ 水道橋店
neighborhood: 千代田区神田三崎町 (near Suidōbashi Station)
seats_text: Not specified in available sources; venue has horigotatsu (sunken-floor) seating suited to groups and parties.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are indicated in any source. Okonomiyaki and monjayaki batter and toppings typically include egg, pork/seafood, and bonito (katsuo)-based dashi and sauces, none of which are flagged as substitutable here. Vegan diners should assume no accommodation without directly contacting the restaurant.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-23-1 都里ビル B1F

bio.background: A hushed, Shōwa-era kissaten reached by a stairway down into a basement near Shinjuku's east side, open since 1985. The dim, quiet room has individually partitioned counter seats so solo customers can sit undisturbed with their coffee, and shelves lined with roughly 1,500 antique cups and saucers from makers like Wedgwood, Meissen, Ginori and Royal Copenhagen — each order is served in a different cup, with a card explaining that day's maker.
bio.specialty: The 'Otona no Shortcake' (Adult's Shortcake) — capped at 16 slices a day and made only when good strawberries are available, with a housemade sponge and fresh dairy cream (35% fat, no preservatives or stabilizers) piled with whole strawberries.
bio.philosophy: Coffee beans are roasted in-house every day and brewed to order using a slow, low-temperature extraction method the shop says it has kept largely unchanged for decades, then served hot, often enough for two or three cups per pot.

anecdotes.text: Because the shortcake is capped at 16 pieces a day and only appears when the strawberries are good, regulars call it the 'phantom shortcake' (幻のショートケーキ).
The coffee is slow, cold/low-temperature extracted and served from a pot — enough for two or three cups per order — a method the shop has kept close to unchanged for decades.

caveats: Dietary information is inferred, not shop-confirmed: no gluten-free or vegan menu, allergy protocol, or ingredient statement was found in any source. Reported hours are inconsistent across listings (most say 12:30-23:00 daily; one blog lists shorter Sunday/holiday hours), so confirm before visiting. This coffee house should not be confused with the separate Tabelog listing for '自家焙煎珈琲 凡' at Shinjuku-Sanchome (id 13304191), which may be the same lineage under a different listing.
closed_days: No fixed closed day reported (open year-round); some sources list shorter hours on Sunday/holidays rather than a closure.
cuisine: 自家焙煎珈琲 / 喫茶店 (self-roasted coffee / traditional kissaten)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, quiet Shōwa-era kissaten with no confirmed English menu — expect a Japanese-only ordering experience typical of an old-school Tokyo coffee shop, with individually partitioned counter seating that discourages loud conversation.
geocode_note: Address (東京都新宿区新宿3-23-1, basement of the 都里ビル) is confirmed consistently across multiple directory sources (Tabelog, Ekiten, Gnavi, Hotpepper). It sits near the Shinjuku-Sanchome intersection, roughly 300m from JR Shinjuku Station and close to both the Toei Ōedo Line 'Shinjuku-nishiguchi' and Marunouchi Line 'Shinjuku-sanchōme' stations — well within the Shinjuku Station target radius. Coordinates left null per instructions (no network geocoding); pin should be derived downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee-focused kissaten with no stated gluten-free protocol, allergy menu, or dedicated prep area found in any source. Plain black coffee is inherently wheat-free, but the only confirmed food item — the signature shortcake — uses a housemade wheat-flour sponge. No other confirmed gluten-free food options were found. Celiac diners should ask staff directly and expect food choices to be very limited to none.
hours_raw: Most sources: 12:30-23:00, L.O. 21:30, open daily/no fixed closed day. One blog gives a differing schedule (Mon-Sat 13:00-22:00 L.O.21:30; Sun/holidays 13:00-21:00) — hours may have changed or vary by source; confirm before visiting.
neighborhood: 新宿西口駅 / 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku, near Shinjuku Station east side / Shinjuku-Sanchōme)
seats_text: Small basement kissaten with individually partitioned counter seating plus some tables; exact seat count not published in available sources.
vegan_detail: Only unadorned black coffee is confirmed vegan-friendly. The shop's signature shortcake is built on fresh dairy cream (35% fat) and a housemade sponge (typically egg/dairy-based), and no vegan food items, plant-milk alternatives, or broth/dashi ingredient details were found in available sources.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷1-12-9 東豊エステートビル B1F

bio.origin: Opened in 1975, the Miyamasuzaka store is the oldest currently operating location of the RAKERU chain, whose company was founded in 1963.
bio.background: RAKERU began as a small coffee/egg-dish shop where the founders made bread and egg dishes fresh each day; the omurice and a proprietary sweet egg bread ('RAKERU bread') became the flagship items and the chain grew into roughly 38 stores nationwide. The name RAKERU is a Japanese phonetic rendering of the biblical name Rachel, chosen to be familiar to Japanese ears while preserving the original pronunciation.
bio.specialty: Fluffy egg omurice (omelette rice), served over ketchup rice, dry curry, or pilaf, alongside the shop's signature sweet 'RAKERU bread'
bio.philosophy: Sincere, made-to-order cooking centered on eggs — the founders' original idea of 'heartwarming, delicious bread and egg dishes' still guides the menu.

anecdotes.text: The Miyamasuzaka store, opened in 1975, is the oldest surviving RAKERU shop and is described as the brand's birthplace for its fluffy omurice.
In 2016 the Shibuya Miyamasuzaka store exclusively revived a limited 40th-anniversary omurice menu, marking its status as the flagship original store.
The restaurant is located in a basement space about a 4-5 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station, with a red-gingham, Alice-in-Wonderland-inspired dining room.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific menu, ingredient list, or allergy-handling information was found in any available source. This is a traditional egg- and butter-centered yoshoku restaurant (omurice specialist with a signature wheat-based sweet bread); dishes are not vegan and gluten-free safety cannot be confirmed. Hours/seat figures vary slightly between directory listings (11:00-21:30 vs 11:00-22:00 short hours), so hours_week reflects the most consistently cited figure and should be reconfirmed before visiting.
closed_days: None found — listed as open year-round (年中無休) in available sources
cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese Western-style food), Omurice specialist
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, Japanese-oriented omurice specialty shop near Shibuya Station; no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so a translation app is useful, though the concept (omelette over rice) is easy to point at and order.
geocode_note: Address (Shibuya 1-12-9, basement of Toho Estate Building) places it directly on Miyamasuzaka slope, roughly a 4-5 minute walk from JR Shibuya Station and well within the Shibuya Scramble 0.8km radius; confirmed consistently across Tabelog, Hot Pepper, and Gnavi listings. Lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed).
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, wheat-free soy sauce, or dedicated-fryer information was found in any source checked (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, official site, blogs). The signature dishes center on demi-glace/ketchup rice sauces and the shop's own wheat-based sweet "RAKERU bread," both typical wheat-containing yoshoku preparations. Diners with celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity should not assume this restaurant is safe without calling ahead, and no such confirmation could be found.
hours_raw: Most consistently cited: 11:00-21:30 daily, no closed days (年中無休). One directory listed 11:00-22:00 with a shortened-hours note of 11:00-21:00; treat as approximate and reconfirm before visiting.
neighborhood: 宮益坂 (Miyamasuzaka), Shibuya
seats_text: Approximately 68 seats (per directory listing; unconfirmed on official site)
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, plant-based substitution, or vegan-specific mention was found. The restaurant's entire identity is built around egg dishes (omurice/omelette rice) and a butter/dairy-based bread, making it fundamentally incompatible with a vegan diet as currently documented.

lead: ロクメイコーヒー 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2丁目24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア 2階（〒150-6101）

bio.origin: 奈良県 (Nara)
bio.background: Rokumei Coffee traces back to a Nara coffee shop called ROCOCO, founded in 1974. Koji Ida took over the family business and began in-house specialty roasting in 2010, earned CQI Q Grader certification in 2013, and won the Japan Coffee Roasting Championship (JCRC) in 2018. The Shibuya Scramble Square shop, which opened March 5, 2026, is the brand's second Kanto-region location (after Tokyo Station's GRANSTA Marunouchi shop), sitting on the mall's 2nd floor directly connected to Shibuya Station.
bio.philosophy: The company roasts 100% specialty-grade coffee, favoring a light roast shipped out soon after roasting to preserve the beans' natural fruit-forward aroma; each shop location gets its own seasonal, location-exclusive blend.
bio.specialty: Shibuya Scramble Square limited blend (roaster-updated seasonally) and the shop's signature black bean & roasted-houjicha pound cake

anecdotes.text: Ida took over his family's Nara kissaten ROCOCO, started self-roasting in 2010, became a CQI-certified Q Grader in 2013, and won the 2018 Japan Coffee Roasting Championship before the brand expanded from Nara to Tokyo.
The Shibuya Scramble Square shop opened March 5, 2026 as Rokumei's second Kanto-area location, with a location-exclusive 'Shibuya Scramble Square Blend' that the roaster updates seasonally.

The black bean and roasted houjicha-tea pound cake is described as a Japanese-style cake made with a houjicha cream infused with a hint of miso, balanced against the mild sweetness of black beans, baked with minimal additives.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan certification, dedicated prep area, or plant-milk menu could be confirmed from available sources — this is a standard specialty-coffee cafe, not a dietary-focused venue. Black coffee/espresso is naturally free of gluten and animal products, but the shop's signature baked sweets (e.g., the black bean & houjicha pound cake) are wheat-flour based and likely contain dairy/eggs; no allergen menu or staff protocol was found, so celiac or strict vegan diners should ask staff directly before ordering food items.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed day; follows Shibuya Scramble Square building calendar)
cuisine: Specialty Coffee, Limited Shibuya Blend, Organic-leaning Sweets
cultural_comfort_note: A modern counter-service specialty-coffee shop inside a major tourist-heavy mall directly connected to Shibuya Station, with mall-wide English signage and international credit card acceptance, though no English menu or staff-language info was independently confirmed.
gf_detail: Plain coffee and espresso drinks are naturally gluten-free, but no gluten-free labeling, dedicated prep equipment, or allergen menu was found in any source for the baked sweets (pound cakes etc.), which are wheat-flour based. Cross-contamination risk in shared prep areas is unconfirmed either way — celiac diners should stick to black coffee/espresso and ask staff before eating any food item.
hours_raw: 平日 8:00〜21:00／土日祝 9:00〜21:00（土日祝は10:00までテイクアウト・物販のみ、イートインは10:00から）
name_ja: ロクメイコーヒー 渋谷スクランブルスクエア店
neighborhood: 渋谷・スクランブルスクエア2階 (Shibuya Scramble Square, 2F)
seats_text: About 6 counter/window-facing seats — a slim single row geared more toward takeout than lingering in-shop
vegan_detail: Black coffee and straight espresso drinks are vegan by nature, but no plant-milk (soy/oat) option for lattes and no vegan-certified sweets were confirmed in any source; the shop's signature pound cakes are Japanese-style baked goods that typically include dairy and egg, so they cannot be assumed vegan without asking staff.

lead: ニューヨーカーズカフェ 水道橋東口店
address_ja: 東京都文京区本郷1-4-1 全水道会館1F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Japan (Ginza Renoir Co., Ltd. chain brand)
bio.background: NEW YORKER'S Cafe is a self-service espresso cafe brand created by Ginza Renoir, the long-running Japanese kissaten (traditional coffee shop) company. When Ginza Renoir hit a business downturn during Japan's deflationary period, management converted one of its underperforming full-service Takadanobaba locations into a self-service, New York-style espresso bar as an experiment. The revamped shop drew a completely new customer base of women and students who had never visited the original waiter-service Renoir cafes, and sales roughly tripled, leading the company to roll the concept out to additional locations including this Suidobashi east-exit branch.
bio.specialty: Hand-poured espresso and coffee drinks alongside bread-based light meals such as salmon and cream cheese bagels, prosciutto and camembert focaccia, and roast chicken and cheddar focaccia, plus multiple breakfast sets (soup, bagel, hot dog, focaccia or buttered toast, each with a mini salad).
bio.philosophy: Branded around the concept of "My Style Cafe" and a space designed to feel like "a New York-inspired oasis in the city," the cafe aims to let each guest use the space in their own way — for coffee alone, a quick meal, work, or study — in a stylish, espresso-bar-style setting distinct from its parent chain's traditional waiter-service kissaten.
roles: Cafe chain operated by Ginza Renoir Co., Ltd.

anecdotes.text: The NEW YORKER'S Cafe concept began as a make-or-break experiment: Ginza Renoir converted a money-losing, waiter-service Takadanobaba shop into a self-service espresso cafe, and it ended up tripling sales by attracting younger and female customers the original brand had not reached.

caveats: This is a nationwide self-service cafe chain (Ginza Renoir's NEW YORKER'S Cafe brand), not a gluten-free or vegan specialist — no dedicated GF or vegan menu could be confirmed from official sources, so celiac and strict-vegan diners should treat it as a fallback coffee/light-bite stop rather than a full meal option. Payment-method and precise reservation policy were not independently confirmed beyond general chain norms; verify by phone (03-3818-4584) if it matters. Distance to Suidobashi Station is confirmed via the widely reported "1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station east exit" description and the Hongo 1-4-1 address, not by geocoded coordinates.
closed_days: 年中無休 (no regular closed days; open every day of the year per store listing)
cuisine: カフェ・喫茶 (self-service espresso cafe / kissaten)
cultural_comfort_note: A self-service chain cafe with visual menu boards and standard order-at-counter flow, so basic greetings and pointing at the menu are enough even without Japanese fluency.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng set to null). Location confirmed via address (Hongo 1-4-1, Bunkyo-ku) and multiple listings describing it as roughly a 1-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station's east exit, which places it well within 0.8km of the Suidobashi Station target point.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, gluten-free bread substitute, or allergen chart could be found on the official Ginza Renoir / NEW YORKER'S Cafe site or in review-site listings. The food menu centers on wheat-based items — bagels, focaccia sandwiches, and toast — so cross-contamination and hidden-wheat risk (e.g., sauces, dressings) are unconfirmed and should be assumed present. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation from staff.
