Plain coffee and tea are inherently vegan, but the food menu (bagels with cream cheese, ham/camembert or roast chicken focaccia, breakfast sets) is built around dairy, meat, and egg, with no confirmed plant-based substitute. Hidden animal-derived ingredients in sauces, dressings, or broths (e.g., dashi/bonito) were not addressed in any source, so vegan diners should ask staff directly rather than assume any dish is safe.

lead: おすしあわせ 鮨ほり川
name_ja: おすしあわせ 鮨ほり川
cuisine: 寿司・握り寿司 (Edomae sushi omakase)
neighborhood: 浅草 (Asakusa Kannon-ura)
address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-24-3
geocode_note: Geocoding blocked in this environment; address confirmed via multiple independent listings (Tabelog, official site, Hitosara) as 東京都台東区浅草3-24-3, in the Asakusa Kannon-ura area, 8 minutes on foot from Tsukuba Express Asakusa Station — consistent with the target radius around Sensō-ji.
seats_text: 6-seat sushi counter (no tables)
hours_raw: Closed Mondays. Tuesday dinner only (17:00-22:00). Wed-Sun: lunch 12:00-14:00 (last entry 13:00) and dinner 17:00-22:00. Sunday dinner service is subject to reservation demand.
closed_days: Monday (Tuesday lunch also closed)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run omakase sushi counter with Japanese-only listings and reservation-based service; English support is not confirmed, so some Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi omakase built entirely around fish and seafood sourced from Toyosu Market and direct fishermen shipments; there is no vegan menu or plant-based course. Dashi and simmered items are presumed to use bonito/fish stock as is standard in this style. Vegan diners are not accommodated beyond a case-by-case advance conversation about which items to omit, which the kitchen says may not be possible if many ingredients need to be excluded.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or protocol. As a classic Edomae sushi counter, soy sauce (often wheat-based) and sushi rice seasoning are used throughout, and cross-contact in a small open kitchen/counter is likely. The restaurant states it will only try to avoid specific allergens or disliked ingredients if discussed in advance, and cannot guarantee full accommodation if many items must be excluded — celiac guests should call ahead (03-4400-0193) and confirm exactly which soy sauce and seasonings are used before booking.
caveats: Detail on ingredient-level allergen sourcing (soy sauce brand, dashi composition) could not be independently confirmed from the official site, which returned access errors during verification; the summary above is based on search-engine-indexed excerpts of that site plus third-party listings (Hitosara, Tabelog). Treat GF/vegan specifics as needing direct phone confirmation before visiting.

bio.origin: Chiba Prefecture, born 1978
bio.background: Chef Kyohei Horikawa began his sushi apprenticeship at age 20, training for seven years, then worked as a sushi craftsman at respected shops in Ginza, Akasaka, Roppongi, and Shinjuku before going independent and opening Osushiawase Sushi Horikawa in Asakusa.
bio.specialty: Omakase Edomae nigiri built around fish sourced directly from Toyosu Market, fishermen nationwide, and Hakata Market, served over a rice blend of Sasanishiki and Koshihikari.
bio.philosophy: The chef adjusts seasoning ingredient-by-ingredient — switching between red vinegar (akazu) and rice vinegar depending on the fish — to give the omakase course its own rhythm, alongside seasonal specialties.

anecdotes.text: Horikawa trained for seven years before working his way through well-regarded sushi counters in Ginza, Akasaka, Roppongi, and Shinjuku prior to opening his own 6-seat shop in Asakusa.

The restaurant emphasizes fish sourced directly from Toyosu Market, direct shipments from fishermen around Japan, and the Hakata Market, with rice made from a Sasanishiki/Koshihikari blend and both red and rice vinegar used depending on the fish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a small 6-seat open sushi counter with a single shared kitchen, there is no separation between regular preparation and any allergen-avoidance requests.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant will only attempt to avoid specific allergens or disliked ingredients if discussed with staff in advance, and states that full accommodation may not be possible if many ingredients need to be excluded.

cuisine: Yakitori (charcoal-grilled chicken skewers) using rare local/jidori chicken breeds
neighborhood: Shibuya (Jinnan), about a 5-8 minute walk from Shibuya Station
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区神南1-20-8 PARK WAY TERRACE SHIBUYA 11F
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address captured verbatim for downstream geocoding. Location (Jinnan 1-chome) is roughly 500-700m from Shibuya Scramble, within the target 0.8km radius.
seats_text: Counter seating plus table seats for groups (exact capacity not published)
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: Listed on English-language Tabelog and reservation platforms (TableCheck), suggesting some accommodation for non-Japanese-speaking diners, but no confirmed English menu.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-focused yakitori restaurant; no vegan menu or plant-based tasting course was found in any source. A few grilled-vegetable skewers may appear on the menu, but they would likely be cooked on the same charcoal grill as meat and sauced with the same tare, so they cannot be assumed vegan-safe without asking staff directly. No dashi/katsuobushi disclosure was found either way.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free tare was confirmed in any source checked (official site and most review platforms returned access errors during verification, so this is based on aggregator search summaries only). As a tare-and-shio yakitori restaurant, the sauce-glazed (tare) skewers almost certainly use a wheat-based soy sauce reduction; shio (salt-only) skewers are the more likely gluten-safer option but this was not explicitly confirmed for this restaurant, and cross-contact risk on a shared grill/tare-dipping station is unverified. Celiac diners should ask staff directly before ordering.

bio.background: Torinosu opened on April 8, 2022 on the 11th floor of Park Way Terrace Shibuya, a short walk from Shibuya Scramble, with night-skyline views over the neighborhood.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers made from rare, carefully sourced regional chicken breeds, including Miyazaki jidori (free-range heritage chicken) and Amami kokei-dori (a black-feathered breed from Amami), alongside other select local birds.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant centers its menu on tracking down distinctive, lesser-known Japanese chicken breeds from small producers and preparing them simply over charcoal to let the meat's character come through, paired with seasonal vegetables and served on Kutani porcelain.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant developed its own original gin, "Torinosu Shibuya Remix," in collaboration with a sake brewery in Kagoshima, served alongside a natural wine list to pair with the grilled chicken courses.

caveats: Direct WebFetch access to the official site (torinosu-japan.com), Tabelog, Hitosara, TableCheck, and Instagram all returned HTTP 403 during verification, so address/hours/story details come from WebSearch result snippets that quote or paraphrase those pages rather than from independently re-read full page content. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found for this restaurant in any source; the GF/vegan assessment above is inferred from the general nature of a tare/shio yakitori restaurant, not from restaurant-specific confirmation, and should be treated as lower-confidence.

lead: 焼鳥 神保町とり瑛
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田神保町1-4-17

roles: Yakitori craftsman/grill chef (name not published in available sources)
bio.background: Opened in 2020 inside a nostalgic, Showa-era wooden building tucked down a quiet back alley just off Jinbocho's famous secondhand-bookstore streets, this unmarked (no outdoor signage) yakitori shop is found mostly by word of mouth and online listings rather than a street-facing storefront.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori featuring rare cuts such as chochin (unlaid-egg chicken heart) and sena-gimo (liver) alongside classic parts, served simply with salt or tare sauce; also known for a lunchtime oyakodon (chicken-and-egg rice bowl) made with burdock root instead of onion, including an off-menu 'ura oyakodon' version with offal that regulars ask for by name.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen insists on chicken slaughtered that same morning rather than a fixed named brand, skewering and grilling everything in-house over binchotan charcoal, and pairs the yakitori with seasonal vegetables shipped from contracted farms in Hokkaido and a rotating list of roughly 30-40 Japanese sakes.

anecdotes.text: A Yahoo News food columnist described being captivated by the craftsman's careful heat control while eating the restaurant's oyakodon inside the charmingly retro, Showa-era old house down a Jinbocho back alley.
A dining blogger who visited in 2025 wrote about discovering the restaurant's unlisted 'ura oyakodon' (hidden-menu parent-child rice bowl with offal) that regulars order off-menu.

caveats: Tabelog and the restaurant's own homepage (toriei-toriei.owst.jp) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so this record is built from WebSearch snippets of Tabelog, foodplace.jp, and several Japanese gourmet-aggregator sites (Hotpepper, OZmall, NAVITIME, Yahoo/PayPay listings) plus two independent blog write-ups; no fields were invented beyond what these sources state. No vegan or gluten-free specific claims were found anywhere — this is a standard chicken-focused yakitori restaurant, so vegan_status and gf_confidence reflect an absence of dedicated accommodation rather than a confirmed negative test. Exact seat count and cash/card policy were not stated in any fetched source. Address is given as 1-4 in most listings; 1-4-17 appears in one secondary listing and could not be independently corroborated — verify street number before precise mapping. Distance check: the address (Jinbocho, 2 min walk from Jinbocho Station) is approximately 0.7-0.8 km from Suidobashi Station, placing it right at the edge of the target radius.
closed_days: Sundays
cuisine: Yakitori - charcoal (binchotan) grilled chicken skewers with rare cuts (chochin, sena-gimo) and standard parts, seasoned shio (salt) or tare (sauce); also serves an oyakodon rice bowl and offers Hokkaido-sourced seasonal vegetables and a wide Japanese sake selection.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, unsigned neighborhood yakitori shop down a back alley with a mobile-phone reservation number — no English menu or website was found, so speaking some Japanese or using a translation app is recommended.
geocode_note: Address (Kanda-Jinbocho 1-chome) is a roughly 2-minute walk from Jinbocho Station, which is approximately 0.7-0.8 km from Suidōbashi Station — within (at the outer edge of) the target 0.8 km radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; network geocoding is blocked in this environment.
gf_detail: Skewers can be ordered shio (salt) instead of tare (sauce), avoiding the soy-based glaze on that item, but Japanese tare is almost always soy-sauce-based and therefore likely contains wheat; no statement about the tare's exact ingredients was found. No dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy protocol is mentioned in any source, and skewers of all kinds share the same charcoal grill, so cross-contact is likely. Ask staff directly about tare ingredients and shared cooking surfaces if you need strict gluten avoidance.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri: 11:30-14:00 (food L.O. 13:30, drink L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-22:30 (food/drink L.O. 21:30); Sat & holidays: 13:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30); closed Sundays. Lunch menu is weekdays only.
name_ja: 焼鳥 神保町とり瑛
neighborhood: 千代田区神保町（Jinbocho/Kanda）
seats_text: Roughly 8-9 counter seats on the ground floor, plus table and tatami-style seating on the second floor (exact upstairs count not published).
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-focused yakitori restaurant; no vegan menu or vegan accommodation was found in any source. The kitchen does source fresh seasonal vegetables from contracted Hokkaido farms and vegetable-based dishes appear on the menu, but tare sauce and overall cooking are meat-centered and skewers are grilled alongside meat, so it is not a suitable choice for strict vegans without direct confirmation from staff.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-4-8 京王フレンテ新宿三丁目 B2F

bio.background: Manager Yasuda previously worked in Italian restaurants and traditional Japanese inn (ryokan) kitchens before opening this tsukemen counter in 2010, tucked in the basement of the Keio Frente building directly across from Isetan Shinjuku, connected to Shinjuku Sanchome Station's C2 exit.
bio.specialty: Niboshi-tonkotsu tsukemen — thick, well-chilled dipping noodles served with a concentrated broth that blends dried-sardine (niboshi) and pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock; a lighter shio (salt) tsukemen is also offered.
bio.philosophy: Yasuda has said the goal is to give time-pressed commuters and station passersby something genuinely delicious rather than just convenient, tuning the broth so it clings properly to thick noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop has run a 500-yen weekday-only 'morning ramen' (asa-ramen) from 7:00-11:00 since well before 2025, offered in shoyu or shio versions and paid in cash.
Large (tokumori) noodle portions are given free of charge, a service that has made the shop especially popular with big-appetite regulars.

caveats: This is a well-loved neighborhood tsukemen/ramen counter, not a dietary-focused restaurant. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere (Tabelog, blogs, official Instagram/X) — noodles are standard wheat, and both the tare and broth are soy-sauce and niboshi/tonkotsu (fish + pork) based with no plant-based or GF substitution mentioned. Include it in the app only as a general/omnivore listing with clear allergy warnings, not as a safe GF/vegan pick. Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (11–15, counter-only). Weekday hours (7:00 start, cash-only 500-yen morning ramen) differ from weekend hours — confirm current hours before visiting since ramen-shop hours change often. No independent official website was found, only Instagram/X accounts, so hours/menu should be cross-checked there.
closed_days: Open every day except January 1st (New Year's Day)
cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン (Tsukemen / dipping noodles and ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: A classic no-frills Tokyo ramen/tsukemen counter inside a station shopping mall — expect Japanese-only signage and ordering with no particular English-language or allergy-accommodation support for foreign diners.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu)-based tare; broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) and tonkotsu (pork bone) stock. No gluten-free noodle substitute, no allergen menu, and no cross-contamination information is published anywhere found (Tabelog, official social media, or food blogs). Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners as currently documented.

neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome), Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo
seats_text: Counter-only seating; visitor reports vary between roughly 11 and 15 seats at a crescent- or U-shaped counter with acrylic dividers between seats.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu items are documented. Broths rely on niboshi (dried sardine) and/or tonkotsu (pork bone), both animal-based, and the tare uses standard soy sauce; there is no plant-based broth option or mention of accommodating vegan requests found on Tabelog, official social media, or blog coverage.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-2-10

bio.origin: Kyoto (brand origin — Nishiki Market flagship)
bio.specialty: Matcha warabi mochi (抹茶わらび餅), hand-cut fresh in-house and dusted generously with high-grade matcha, plus matcha soft-serve and other matcha confections
bio.philosophy: The brand centers its sweets on drawing out the aroma and depth of Uji matcha
bio.background: 茶和々 (Sawawa) is a matcha confectionery brand operated by Terakoya Corporation (株式会社寺子屋), with shops in Kyoto's Nishiki Market, Kanazawa, Narita, and Otaru in addition to this Asakusa location, which sits immediately beside Sensoji's Hozomon Gate area next to the Nison-butsu Buddha statue.

anecdotes.text: Craftsmen hand-cut the warabi mochi fresh and coat it generously in high-grade matcha; it has been the shop's signature best-seller for years.

caveats: This is a takeout-oriented matcha sweets stand (warabi mochi, matcha soft-serve) rather than a sit-down restaurant — good for a quick dessert stop near Sensoji, not a meal. No dedicated gluten-free or vegan certification was found; the matcha soft-serve contains dairy, and while traditional warabi mochi is typically wheat-free, this has not been confirmed for this shop's specific recipe or production line, and it also sells Western-style baked sweets in the same space. Celiac or strict vegan visitors should confirm ingredients with staff before ordering.
closed_days: None reported — appears to be open daily (no fixed closed day found in any source)
cuisine: Matcha sweets, warabi mochi (Japanese confectionery)
cultural_comfort_note: A simple walk-up/takeout sweets counter where items are visually displayed and easy to point at, so minimal Japanese is needed to order.
gf_detail: No allergen menu or gluten-free certification was found for this shop. Its signature warabi mochi is a traditional Japanese confection typically made from bracken/starch bases rather than wheat, and the kinako (soybean flour) topping is also wheat-free in general, but this has not been confirmed for this specific location's recipe, and the shop also sells Western-style baked sweets in the same space with no stated separation of equipment. There is no confirmed information on cross-contamination handling, so ask staff directly if you need certainty for celiac safety.
geocode_note: Address (東京都台東区浅草2-2-10) confirmed by multiple independent sources placing the shop directly beside Sensoji's Hozomon Gate, next to the Nison-butsu statue — well within 0.8km of the Sensoji landmark. lat/lng left null per instructions; no geocoding was performed.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily (no stated closed day found)

seats_text: Small indoor seating area with about 4 seats, plus some standing/take-away space near the shop entrance; primarily a walk-up matcha sweets stand rather than a full seated restaurant.
vegan_detail: The matcha soft-serve ice cream contains dairy and is not vegan. Some warabi mochi items appear to be plant-based (starch, matcha, kinako, sugar syrup) but exact recipes and any hidden animal-derived ingredients (e.g., honey, gelatin, dairy) were not confirmed from available sources, and no vegan labeling was found on the menu. Ask staff to confirm ingredients for a specific item before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2丁目28-1 椎津ビル3F

bio.origin: Kichijoji, Tokyo (the brand's first shop); this location is the Shibuya branch, opened March 22, 2021
bio.background: Hikiniku to Come was founded in 2019 by restaurateur Yamamoto Shohei (previously behind the hamburger chain "Ore no Hamburg") together with Konishi Toshiyuki of the design firm POOL inc. and Kiyomiya Toshiyuki, a food-service veteran who had worked at Culture Convenience Club (TSUTAYA) and Chikara no Moto Holdings (Ippudo ramen). The first shop opened in Kichijoji in June 2020, and the concept quickly grew into a multi-location chain; the Shibuya branch on Dogenzaka opened in March 2021 inside a renovated older building, giving it a hidden, back-alley feel despite the crowds it draws.
bio.specialty: 挽肉と米定食 — a single set meal of freshly ground, charcoal-grilled beef hamburger-steak patties (up to three 90g patties), rice cooked fresh in kamado pots, and miso soup, with free rice refills and a complimentary raw egg. The Shibuya location also has its own house sauce blended from three types of miso.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant is built around a "3-fresh" (3たて) idea — meat ground fresh, patties grilled fresh over charcoal, and rice cooked fresh to order — reflecting founder Yamamoto Shohei's belief that ground meat deserves to be the star of the dish rather than just a component of a burger or curry.

anecdotes.text: Yamamoto Shohei's path to Hikiniku to Come ran through his earlier venture "Ore no Hamburg," and he has spoken about why he became fixated specifically on ground meat (挽肉) as a craft rather than treating it as a secondary ingredient.

Since opening in 2021, the Shibuya branch has been known for long same-day queues, moving from a paper numbered-ticket (整理券) system to a mix of same-day sign-up and limited paid online reservations for a small 6-seat counter area.

caveats: Sources disagree on some details: most (including content indexed from the official site) describe split hours of 11:00-15:00 and 17:00-21:00 with the shop closed on the 1st and 3rd Wednesdays of each month, but at least one social-media listing states continuous 11:00-21:00 hours with a weekly Wednesday closure. Direct fetches of tabelog.com and the official hikinikutocome.com pages were blocked by network policy in this environment, so all facts here come from indexed search-result snippets of those and third-party sites rather than a live page read — treat hours/closed-day details as best-available and worth a same-day check for exact timing.
closed_days: 1st and 3rd Wednesdays of the month (per most sources; one source instead lists every Wednesday as closed)
cuisine: ハンバーグ・洋食
cultural_comfort_note: The menu is a single set meal so ordering itself is simple even without Japanese, but the entry process (same-day sign-up/queue system, occasional online reservation windows) is explained mainly in Japanese and can be confusing for first-time foreign visitors.
gf_detail: The entire menu is one dish: a charcoal-grilled ground-beef hamburger-steak set. Traditional Japanese hamburg-steak patties are typically bound with breadcrumbs (wheat), and no gluten-free version, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated GF preparation process is advertised on the official site or in press coverage found. Wheat-based soy sauce is also standard in Japanese hamburg-steak sauces and miso soup bases. With no menu flexibility and no GF statement from the restaurant, this is not a place to trust for celiac-level safety without direct, in-person confirmation.
hours_raw: 昼の部 11:00-15:00 / 夜の部 17:00-21:00（定休日：第1・3水曜日、一部情報源では毎週水曜定休）
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked); lat/lng left null. Address (道玄坂2丁目28-1) and multiple sources stating "渋谷駅から徒歩約5分" (about a 5-minute walk from Shibuya Station) place this well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble target point.
neighborhood: 渋谷 道玄坂
seats_text: 31 seats total (25 regular counter seats on a same-day sign-up basis, plus 6 separate reservable counter "juju" seats with a dedicated grill chef)
vegan_detail: The sole dish is a beef ground-meat hamburger-steak set with a complimentary raw egg, and the miso soup served alongside is very likely made with bonito (katsuo) dashi in standard Japanese style. No plant-based or vegetarian alternative, and no vegan-related statement from the restaurant, was found in any source checked. Not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都文京区後楽1-3-61 後楽園ホールビル2F（東京ドームホテル直営）

bio.origin: Tokyo (Korakuen/Suidōbashi)
bio.background: Korakuen Hanten opened on April 16, 1962, on the 2nd floor of the Korakuen Hall building next to Tokyo Dome, and has operated ever since as a directly-run restaurant of the Tokyo Dome Hotel group. Long popular with sports figures and fans visiting Korakuen Hall and Tokyo Dome, the restaurant underwent a full renovation and reopened on November 14, 2025 with a 'neo-Chinoiserie' interior while keeping its traditional menu.
bio.specialty: Peking duck, and its signature shark-fin soup noodles (フカヒレ入り汁そば); the renovated menu also added a fo tiao qiang (佛跳牆, 'Buddha Jumps Over the Wall') course.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the traditional taste the restaurant has served since 1962 while incorporating contemporary menu elements and ingredients.
roles: Chinese restaurant, directly operated by Tokyo Dome Hotel

anecdotes.text: 後楽園飯店は1962年4月16日に開業した伝統と格式のある中国料理店。東京ドームホテル直営で、各界のスポーツ選手や関係者をはじめ多くの顧客に親しまれている。
1962年創業の東京ドームホテル直営中国料理店「後楽園飯店」が11月14日(金)リニューアルオープン。'ネオシノワズリ'空間で味わう、中国皇帝が愛した幻のスープ「佛跳牆」コースも新登場。

caveats: Long-established hotel Chinese restaurant, not a vegan- or gluten-free-specialist venue. No dedicated GF or vegan menu was found in any source; traditional Chinese dishes here likely use wheat-based soy sauce, wheat-starch thickening, and animal-based (chicken/pork/seafood) stocks by default, so celiac and vegan diners should confirm specifics with staff before ordering. Included as a notable historic/landmark dining option near Suidōbashi/Tokyo Dome rather than for confirmed dietary accommodation.
closed_days: 無休 (open year-round; no regular closed day per Tabelog listing)
cuisine: Chinese (中華料理・老舗)
cultural_comfort_note: A hotel-affiliated restaurant with an English-language Tabelog page and English online reservations, so English-speaking visitors can book and dine comfortably.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled); address confirms location on the 2nd floor of the Korakuen Hall building, about a 3-minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station west exit, well within the Suidōbashi Station target radius.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation was found in any source checked (official site, Tabelog, hotel press materials). As a traditional Chinese restaurant, dishes very likely use wheat-based soy sauce and wheat-starch thickening (an-kake style sauces), and fryers are shared for multiple items including breaded/battered dishes. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about ingredients and possible substitutions before ordering; do not assume any dish is safe by default.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:00), daily, no regular closed day
neighborhood: 水道橋・後楽園 - 東京ドーム隣接（Korakuen Hall Building 2F, next to Tokyo Dome）
seats_text: Approximately 172 seats including 10 private rooms (accommodating roughly 6-70 people per room)
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu was found. Average dinner budget is around ¥7,000 (lunch around ¥2,000-¥3,000, with a weekday lunch set near ¥1,080), built around dishes like Peking duck and shark-fin soup noodles, so the menu is meat/seafood-centric. Vegetable dishes are likely available (as in most Chinese restaurants) but stocks and sauces probably contain chicken, pork, or oyster-based ingredients unless requested otherwise; vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredients.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区代々木2-14-3 北斗第一ビル

bio.background: According to a noodle-machine maker's client profile and food blogs, the owner previously ran an udon specialty shop near Shinjuku's west exit for many years, having trained in udon-making back in his hometown in Shikoku before that first opening. Years later he decided to open a soba-and-local-sake shop instead, and consulted noodle-machine maker Abe Giken after being impressed by a demonstration of their equipment, which he now uses to help produce the shop's distinctive coarse-ground noodles.
bio.origin: Shikoku (owner's hometown, where he trained in udon-making before his first restaurant)
bio.philosophy: The owner has said the shop's mission is to help more people rediscover the flavor of soba through coarsely-ground, 100%-buckwheat noodles.
bio.specialty: Jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba made from the rare Mogami-Wase variety of buckwheat grown in Yamagata, stone-ground extra-coarse so the noodles keep visible flecks of buckwheat hull; it is hand-made in view of guests during lunch service and traditionally eaten with a pinch of salt on the first bite before dipping.

anecdotes.text: The shop's stone-ground buckwheat flour is sourced specifically for this restaurant (and one restaurant in Osaka), giving the noodles their rustic, thick, dark appearance with visible buckwheat hull flecks — a deliberately rougher style than smooth Edo-mae soba.

A 2025 diner described the noodles as chewy, dark, thick 'country-style' soba — the opposite in character to smooth Edo-style soba — best paired with the shop's rotating selection of about 20 pure rice sakes.

caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (some list weekday lunch opening as 11:00, others 11:30) — treat as approximate and confirm by phone. No restaurant-specific gluten-free or vegan claims were found anywhere; GF/vegan assessments below are inferred from what is documented about the menu (100% buckwheat noodles; traditional soba-izakaya format) plus general convention for this style of shop, not from an explicit allergen policy. Exact seat count and definitive payment-method (cash-only or card) information could not be confirmed from available sources.
closed_days: Sundays, and the first Saturday of each month
cuisine: 粗挽き蕎麦（Coarse-ground buckwheat soba, soba-izakaya）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional counter-style soba-and-sake shop aimed at Japanese regulars — no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos helps.
gf_detail: Every soba served here is jūwari — 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat blended into the noodle dough itself, which is unusual and notable (most Tokyo soba shops use some wheat as binder). However, the dipping sauce (tsuyu) at Japanese soba shops is almost always made with regular wheat-containing soy sauce, and no source confirms a wheat-free tsuyu, dedicated gluten-free prep area, or protocol for avoiding cross-contact with wheat-based menu items (the shop also serves izakaya-style dishes). Celiac diners should not assume safety and should ask staff directly about the tsuyu and any shared cooking surfaces before ordering.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null. Address placement is corroborated by multiple independent listings describing the shop as roughly 1–6 minutes on foot from Minami-Shinjuku Station / Shinjuku Station's south side (Keio New Line exit), placing it well within 0.8km of Shinjuku Station.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:00/11:30–14:30, 17:30–22:00 (sources vary slightly on exact lunch open time) / Sat: lunch only, ~11:00/11:30–14:30 / Closed Sundays and the first Saturday of each month
neighborhood: 南新宿（Minami Shinjuku）

positives.text: All soba served at the restaurant is made from 100% coarsely-ground buckwheat flour, with no wheat flour blended into the noodle dough itself.

seats_text: Small, intimate space centered on a long counter with a couple of additional table seats.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, vegan dashi, or plant-based accommodation is documented anywhere. The shop is described as a soba-and-sake izakaya, implying a broader menu with meat/fish dishes alongside the noodles, and traditional soba tsuyu in Japan is normally made with katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. Vegans should assume standard katsuo-based broth unless staff confirm otherwise.

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草3-4-2

roles: Proprietress (女将) and family — currently run by a mother-daughter team with staff
bio.background: Opened in 1947 (Shōwa 22), just after the end of World War II, in the Asakusa Kannon-ura district directly behind Sensō-ji Temple. The founding family had previously run a sake retail business before turning the shop into a dedicated beef-tendon-stew specialist. It has been passed down through the family and is now counted among the oldest surviving izakaya in the Kannon-ura backstreets.
bio.specialty: Gyusuji nikomi (牛すじ煮込み) — Asakusa-style beef tendon stew, the dish the shop was founded on and has served continuously since 1947.
bio.philosophy: The recipe has been kept deliberately unchanged since the shop opened. Beef tendon — sourced from Hiyama, a long-established wholesaler in Nihonbashi — is simmered for hours in a broth built up over decades, preserving the same flavor generation to generation.

anecdotes.text: The current proprietress has said that her father passed away ten years ago, and if he woke up and tasted today's beef tendon stew, he might still say 'this is a bit too sweet' — a sign of how carefully the original recipe is still followed.
The beef tendon is simmered for roughly four hours a day using tendon from Hiyama, a century-old Nihonbashi meat wholesaler, continuing a broth-and-technique tradition built up over decades.

caveats: Payment methods (cash-only vs. cards) were not explicitly confirmed by any source; cash_only=true is a best-effort inference typical of small, old-school Asakusa izakaya of this type, not a confirmed fact. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was published anywhere; this is a traditional beef-tendon-focused izakaya with no indication of dietary accommodation. Seat count and reservation policy were not confirmed by any source. Hours were consistent across several secondary sources (Mon-Sat 17:00-21:30, closed Sunday, open on national holidays) but were not confirmed on an official site since none appears to exist.
closed_days: Sundays (open on national holidays)
cuisine: Izakaya — gyusuji (beef tendon stew) specialist
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, regulars-heavy local izakaya with no confirmed English menu, though it has appeared on Japanese TV programs and is listed in English on Tabelog.
geocode_note: Address confirmed as 東京都台東区浅草3-4-2, in the Asakusa Kannon-ura backstreets directly behind Sensō-ji Temple, roughly a 7-minute walk from Asakusa Station — well within the Sensō-ji target radius. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions; geocoding is performed downstream from address_ja.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation was identified in any source. As a traditional izakaya, the signature beef tendon stew and other dishes are very likely seasoned with soy sauce (contains wheat) and prepared in a small shared kitchen with no indication of allergen separation. Not recommended for celiac diners without direct in-person confirmation from staff.
hours_raw: 月〜土 17:00-21:30、日曜定休（祝日は営業）
neighborhood: Asakusa (behind Sensō-ji Temple, Kannon-ura)
seats_text: Small counter-style izakaya; exact seat count not confirmed by any source.
vegan_detail: No vegan options, dedicated preparation, or menu information were identified. The restaurant's entire identity is built around a beef tendon stew (gyusuji nikomi) that has been simmered in an accumulated meat-based broth for decades, and the broader menu appears to be conventional home-style izakaya fare likely including dashi and other animal-derived ingredients. Not suitable for vegan diners based on available information.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町31-3 田中ビル2階

bio.background: 渋谷センター街に約40年続く老舗の魚介居酒屋。田中ビル2階に店を構え、創業者自身が今も切り盛りしていると伝えられる。サラリーマン街として知られる宇田川町エリアの中でも数少ない、腰を据えて飲める落ち着いた店として地元で語り継がれている。
bio.specialty: 鰹のたたき（土佐造り）となめろう、いわしのつみれ汁

anecdotes.text: 東京農業大学名誉教授で食文化研究家の小泉武夫氏が通う店として知られ、店主は氏のために築地の信頼できる仲卸から仕入れた鰹を切らさず用意しているという。
渋谷宇田川町は『ロクな店がない』とも言われる中で、この店は創業50年に迫る老舗として一目置かれていると紹介されている。
いわしのつみれ汁は臭みがなく、口の中でうまみが広がる一品として評判。

caveats: Traditional seafood/izakaya establishment with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation; menu centers on sashimi, grilled/simmered fish, nameroo and hot pots, which typically involve soy sauce (wheat) and bonito-based dashi. No English menu or staff confirmed. Detailed page fetches of Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Hitosara, Gnavi, Ekiten, Navitime, and Cookdoor were blocked (403) in this environment; facts below are drawn from search-engine result summaries of those same pages plus a food-culture blog (koizumipress.com) and a personal blog (note.com), so treat operational specifics (hours/seats) as reasonably but not directly-fetched-page confirmed.

gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation area, or allergen labeling was found for this cafe. Baked goods (financiers, sables, pound cakes) are conventional wheat-based bakery items with no stated gluten-free alternative. Coffee itself is inherently gluten-free, but cross-contact in a shared kitchen for pastries is unconfirmed -- celiac guests should stick to drinks only and ask before eating any food item.
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町3-10-1
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (network geocoding blocked). Multiple independent sources (official REC COFFEE page, Visit Chiyoda tourism site, Rakuten Gnavi) confirm the address 東京都千代田区神田三崎町3-10-1, ground floor of the "Nine Hours Suidobashi" capsule hotel building, 2 minutes on foot from the west exit of JR Suidobashi Station -- well within the 0.8km Suidobashi Station target radius.
seats_text: Roughly 30 seats across table and counter seating in a glass-enclosed space overlooking the Kanda River.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; closes only on occasional building facility-inspection days for the Nine Hours Suidobashi building it is housed in.
cultural_comfort_note: REC COFFEE maintains an English-language website and section for this shop, and as a specialty-coffee destination near a major JR station it is used to walk-in foreign visitors, though menu boards and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

bio.origin: Fukuoka, Japan (company founded 2008)
bio.background: REC COFFEE was founded in Fukuoka in 2008 by Yoshikazu Iwase and his university friend and business partner Osamu Kitazoe, starting as a mobile coffee truck before opening its first storefront near Yakuin Station in 2010. Iwase discovered specialty coffee while working part-time at Manu Coffee in Fukuoka under roaster Sonobu Nishioka, which shaped his approach to sourcing and roasting. The company grew from Fukuoka into multiple Tokyo locations (including this Suidobashi shop, on the ground floor of the Nine Hours Suidobashi capsule hotel building by the Kandagawa river) and into Taiwan.
bio.specialty: Single-origin, self-roasted specialty pour-over and espresso coffee, paired with house-made pastries (honey financiers, almond sables, pound cakes) baked by an in-house pastry team.
bio.philosophy: Iwase has said his goal is to serve coffee good enough to change how everyday consumers think about coffee, treating quality specialty coffee as something for daily life rather than a niche connoisseur product.

anecdotes.text: Iwase won Japan's national barista championship (JBC) in both 2014 and 2015, then took silver at the 2016 World Barista Championship in Dublin, Ireland -- accolades that grew out of the roasting and brewing standards set at the original Fukuoka shop.

Iwase has described wanting to bring specialty coffee into Fukuoka's cafe culture in a way that changes ordinary consumers' image of what coffee can taste like, rather than keeping it a specialist pursuit.

caveats: This is a specialty-coffee cafe, not a restaurant with a dedicated vegan or gluten-free program. No source confirmed plant-milk substitution, a gluten-free bakery item, or written allergen information for the Suidobashi location specifically; all dietary guidance here is "ask staff" rather than confirmed accommodation. Hours were corroborated by two independent search snippets citing the same figures (the official site itself could not be directly fetched due to a network block on rec-coffee.com in this environment), so treat hours as reasonably reliable but not primary-source-verified in this session.

lead: ビストロ リタ 新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿7-15-17 東光ビルB1F

bio.background: Bistro Rita Shinjuku opened on February 27, 2025, run by LINK STYLE, a Tokyo hospitality group known locally for its popular neighborhood izakaya brands. It is the group's first bistro/bar-style concept and its largest location, set in a basement space near Shinjuku Station's west exit with a gray, marble, and stained-glass interior and several private rooms.
bio.specialty: Black wagyu rare hamburger steak, seared and finished tableside on a hot iron plate with the sauce poured in front of guests
bio.philosophy: Designed as a stylish 'hideaway' bistro-bar for after-work drinks or group gatherings, aimed at guests in their late 20s and 30s, pairing hearty Italian-leaning meat and wine dishes with an all-you-can-drink course format.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant reached monthly sales of about 13.5 million yen just two months after opening, with its tableside iron-plate wagyu hamburger steak and a foie gras egg-yolk dish cited as signature draws.
Bistro Rita is LINK STYLE's first bistro-format restaurant, following the same company's dominant-strategy izakaya brands 'Sakaba Tsumugido' and 'Meshiya Hiroki Club' in the Shinjuku area.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan-specific information (dedicated menu, kitchen protocol, or ingredient disclosure) was found in any source. This is a meat- and seafood-centric Italian bistro/bar chain location (signature dishes are wagyu hamburger steak, beef tartare, foie gras, oysters); gluten-free and vegan diners should treat availability as ask-only and expect very limited suitable options.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂2-19-2

bio.chef_name: 松岡憲策 (Kensaku Matsuoka, founder); now run by third-generation owner (Katsuyama family)
bio.origin: Founded 1951 in Shibuya as "Ekuriya," later renamed "Indo Ryōri Murugi"
bio.background: Founded in 1951 by Kensaku Matsuoka, a mining engineer whose prospecting trips took him repeatedly to then-Burma (Myanmar), where he became a regular at a local curry stall and grew friendly with its owners. After returning to postwar Shibuya, he opened a small shop called "Ekuriya," adapting what he had tasted overseas into a curry suited to Japanese palates; the shop was later renamed "Indo Ryōri Murugi." It is now run by the third generation of the family.
bio.specialty: Tamago-iri Murugi Curry — the signature chicken curry, slow-simmered with layered spices and topped with a boiled egg, served over a distinctive mountain-shaped mound of rice
bio.philosophy: A postwar recipe deliberately reworked from an overseas original to suit Japanese taste, kept essentially unchanged for over 70 years

anecdotes.text: The shop's origin traces to founder Kensaku Matsuoka's work as a mining engineer, whose ore-prospecting trips to then-Burma (Myanmar) brought him to a local curry stall he visited so often he became friends with the owners; after the war he opened 'Ekuriya' in Shibuya based on that experience, later renamed 'Indo Ryōri Murugi.'
Novelist Ikenami Shōtarō, who worked for the Tokyo metropolitan government near Shibuya after the war, was a regular at Murugi and wrote about the shop in essays recalling his memories of curry.
A Tabelog reviewer describes eating Murugi's curry as beloved by the noted gourmet writer Ikenami Shōtarō and reflects on the shop's decades of consistency.

caveats: Official site (murugi.co.jp) and several review-blog pages returned 403 errors when fetched directly during this verification session (network/proxy restriction), so hours and menu details are aggregated from multiple secondary listing/blog sources rather than confirmed on the official page itself. Hours in particular show minor inconsistency across sources — most aggregated listings give a split lunch/dinner schedule that varies by day (see hours_raw), but at least one source describes it as lunch-only (11:30-15:00); the fuller multi-day schedule was treated as primary. No vegetarian, vegan, or gluten-free menu items, statements, or allergy-handling practices were found in any source reviewed — this is a traditional chicken-curry specialty shop with no dietary-accommodation information published.
closed_days: Friday, and national holidays (祝日)
cuisine: Old-school Japanese-style Indian curry (Shibuya curry-ya, est. 1951)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, decades-old neighborhood curry shop with Japanese-only signage/menu expectations and cash-based, walk-in local service — not set up for English-speaking or special-diet guidance.

lead: 焼き鳥 鳥たん 水道橋店
address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-21-1 東京資源会館 1F

bio.background: This Suidobashi restaurant is the 4th branch of the "Toritan" (鳥たん) yakitori and beef-tongue izakaya chain, operated by Olive Dining Co. It opened on July 10, 2024, just two to three minutes' walk from JR Suidobashi Station's west exit.
bio.specialty: Beef tongue (牛タン), specifically the prized "tanmoto" (タン元) cut — the fattiest, most marbled section of the tongue, sourced through the company's own dedicated supply route — alongside yakitori skewers grilled from all-domestic Japanese chicken.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes ingredient quality: 100% domestic chicken for freshness (fresh enough that they can serve low-temperature-cooked raw liver, 生レバー), and tsukune (chicken meatballs) that are hand-formed fresh every day rather than pre-made.

anecdotes.text: Opened July 10, 2024 as the chain's 4th location, marking the launch with a nine-day campaign offering draft beer, highballs, and sours for ¥99 per glass.

caveats: This is a standard chicken-yakitori and beef-tongue izakaya with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation. Tare (glaze) sauces at yakitori restaurants are almost always wheat-based soy sauce, skewers are cooked on a shared charcoal grill, and no allergy protocol or dedicated equipment was mentioned in any source checked. Vegetable/vegan options would be minimal to none since the menu is built around chicken and beef tongue. Confirm any dietary need by phone (03-5357-1585) before visiting.
closed_days: None — open year-round (年中無休)
cuisine: Yakitori (grilled chicken skewers) and gyutan (beef tongue) izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A standard neighborhood-chain izakaya near Suidobashi Station; no confirmation of an English menu, so a translation app or basic Japanese will help when ordering or asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergy protocol was found in any source. Yakitori here is typically finished with tare (a soy-sauce-based glaze containing wheat), and skewers are grilled together on a shared charcoal grill, so cross-contact risk is high even on items ordered shio (salt) style. Treat as not safe for celiac diners unless staff can confirm otherwise in person.

lead: 天ぷら 新宿つな八 総本店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-28-4 三峰ビル TRES SHINJUKU 6F・B1F(総本店別館)

bio.origin: Founded 1924 (Taisho 13) in Tsunohazu, Shinjuku by Kyuzo Shimura
bio.background: Tsunahachi traces back to 1922, when founder Kyuzo Shimura opened a stand-and-eat tempura shop called "Amihachi" (網八). Customers habitually misread the character 網 (ami) as tsuna, and the mispronunciation stuck so firmly that in 1924 the shop was formally reopened under the name it kept the same location in Shinjuku's old Tsunohazu district as today's Sohonten. The original building was destroyed in the 1945 air raids and post-war land readjustment; the shop rebuilt and reopened on the same ground in 1948. Over nine decades the company grew into one of Japan's best-known tempura chains, with roughly 23 branches nationwide (Sapporo, Toyama, Nagoya, Kyoto, Osaka and more) as of early 2026. In 2026 the flagship Sohonten building closed for a full rebuild planned to run until summer 2028; during construction the original staff and craftsmen relocated a minute's walk away to a temporary location, 'Sohonten Bekkan' (総本店別館), inside the TRES SHINJUKU building, which opened June 23, 2026.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style tempura courses centered on shrimp (ebi), conger eel (anago), seasonal fish and vegetables, and kakiage, fried to order at the counter
bio.philosophy: The house motto, carried since the founder's era, is to 'always serve the finest tempura at an accessible price' and to 'add sincerity to every dish' (常に最高の天ぷらをお手頃な価格で / おいしさに真心をそえて).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name is a happy accident: it originally opened as 'Amihachi' (網八), but customers kept misreading the first character as 'tsuna' instead of 'ami' — the mistake spread so widely that the owners renamed the shop 'Tsunahachi' when they formally reopened it in 1924.
anecdotes.source: https://r.gnavi.co.jp/g021901/ (via aggregated search of company history / tunahachi.co.jp/history)

anecdotes.text: The Sohonten building was leveled in the 1945 wartime destruction and postwar land readjustment of Shinjuku; the shop rebuilt on the same plot and reopened in 1948, and has operated there continuously until its 2026 reconstruction closure.

With the historic Sohonten building slated for full reconstruction (reopening planned summer 2028), the restaurant relocated its craftsmen and staff a minute's walk away to a temporary 'Sohonten Bekkan' inside the TRES SHINJUKU building, which opened June 23, 2026.

caveats: IMPORTANT: The historic Sohonten building at 新宿3-31-8 closed to the public on May 31, 2026 for a full rebuild, not expected to reopen until summer 2028. Since June 23, 2026 the same staff/craftsmen have been operating a temporary location, "総本店別館" (Sohonten Bekkan / Annex), about a 1-minute walk away inside the TRES SHINJUKU building (新宿3-28-4, 6F & B1F). The address, and by extension this record, reflects that currently-operating annex rather than the shuttered original building. Hours (11:00-22:00, LO 21:00) and closed-day info are carried over from the long-standing honten pattern and corroborated for the annex by one source, but exact annex-specific closed days and seat count were not independently confirmed — could not access tabelog.com, tunahachi.co.jp, hotpepper.jp, r.gnavi.co.jp, hitosara.com, retty.me or several other listing sites directly (all returned HTTP 403 from the network proxy); facts below are drawn from WebSearch result summaries that quoted/aggregated those pages, not from directly fetched primary pages, so some numeric details (exact seats, precise annex closed-days) are lower confidence. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodation was found anywhere in available sources — this is a traditional wheat-batter, dashi-based Edomae tempura restaurant with no indication of dedicated allergy-safe processes.
closed_days: Traditionally open daily (無休), with historic honten closing only Dec 31–Jan 1 and for occasional facility maintenance; annex-specific closed-day policy not separately confirmed.
cuisine: 天ぷら・江戸前天ぷら (Edomae tempura)
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, century-old tempura house well accustomed to tourists (English Tabelog listing exists), but day-to-day counter service is conducted primarily in Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour-batter tempura restaurant. The tempura coating (koromo) is standard wheat flour, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is made with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce and bonito-based dashi. No gluten-free batter, rice-flour option, dedicated fryer, or allergen menu was found in any source consulted. Not safe for celiac diners without direct, on-site confirmation from staff, and even then cross-contact in the shared fryer oil is likely.
hours_raw: 11:00–22:00, last order (food) 21:00 / drinks LO 21:00, served continuously through the day (no lunch/dinner split break reported). Historic honten pattern: open daily, closed only Dec 31–Jan 1 plus occasional maintenance closures; this pattern is carried over for the temporary annex but not independently reconfirmed store-by-store.

Vegetable tempura items likely exist on the menu but would be fried in oil shared with seafood and served with the same fish-based sauce, so this is not a safe or comfortable choice for strict vegans without prior arrangement (and even then, success is not confirmed).

address_ja: 東京都台東区浅草2-2-1

bio.background: Founded in 1887 (Meiji 20) as a neighborhood tofu and yuba maker on Denpoin-dori, a side street near Nakamise and Sensoji in Asakusa. The shop is now run by the fifth generation of the same family, still making tofu and fresh yuba in a factory attached to the storefront.
bio.specialty: Fresh yuba (nama-yuba) and hand-pressed tofu made only from 100% domestic soybeans and natural nigari (bittern), plus soymilk-and-okara doughnuts and house-made almond tofu.
bio.philosophy: Keep to the old recipe — domestic whole soybeans and natural nigari only, no shortcuts — even as the storefront itself has modernized.

anecdotes.text: The current building has a modern wood-grain facade and tiled roof, a deliberate departure from the look of an old-fashioned machiya tofu shop, marked by a green noren reading '栃木家'; it sits on Denpoin-dori close to Nakamise, in the heart of Asakusa.
The shop manufactures its tofu and fresh yuba on-site in an attached factory and has been passed down through five generations of the same Asakusa family since its 1887 founding.

caveats: This is primarily a takeaway tofu/yuba specialty shop with a retail counter, not a sit-down restaurant. Its core products (plain tofu, fresh yuba, edamame tofu) are naturally soy-based, but the shop also sells clearly non-vegan and wheat-containing items (e.g., soymilk-okara doughnuts, and at least one 2025 review mentions a tofu-dough pork bun), so it should not be treated as a dedicated vegan or gluten-free spot despite the tofu/yuba focus. No allergen statement or cross-contamination info is published by the shop; confirm specific items with staff (phone 03-3841-5731). Located on Denpoin-dori, a short walk from Sensoji Temple and Asakusa Station (Ginza Line/Tobu), matching the "3 minutes from Asakusa Station" description.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜定休)
cuisine: Fresh yuba and tofu specialty shop (tofu, nama-yuba, soymilk doughnuts, almond tofu)
cultural_comfort_note: No English menu or English-language service was found in any source; a translation app is recommended for asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: Plain tofu, fresh yuba, and edamame tofu are naturally wheat-free, but the shop also sells wheat-flour items (soymilk-okara doughnuts, buns), and no gluten-free process, dedicated equipment, or cross-contamination information is published anywhere. Ask staff which specific items are wheat-free before buying.

bio.philosophy: The kitchen draws on cooking techniques from across China (with a Sichuan base) and arranges them in a modern style, blending in some Western and Japanese touches, aiming to be equally suited to an everyday meal or a business banquet.

anecdotes.text: A photo studio located about 20 meters away describes Shien as a long-established neighborhood Chinese restaurant with a heavy, vintage Showa-era atmosphere and discreet private rooms in the back, popular for entertaining.

lead: めん、色いろ。いつでも、おやつ。 新宿
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-38-1 ルミネエスト新宿 7F

bio.background: "めん、色いろ。いつでも、おやつ。" is a casual udon concept operated by DD Holdings (the restaurant group behind numerous Tokyo dining brands), located on the 7th floor of Lumine Est Shinjuku, directly at Shinjuku Station's east side.
bio.specialty: Colorful udon made with five varieties of noodles that have vegetables and grains kneaded directly into the dough (giving them their signature bright colors), served hot or cold with a choice of sauces and toppings. Diners also select three items from a rotating central "oyatsu" (snack/dessert) table with each udon order. The menu was fully relaunched in April 2026 around a new line of creamy, milky-white udon with over 25 selectable toppings.
bio.philosophy: The concept plays on "itsudemo oyatsu" (snacks anytime) — pairing a health-conscious, vegetable-forward, visually colorful take on udon with the playful, help-yourself snack-table format, aiming for a dining experience closer to a customizable pasta bar than a traditional udon-ya.

anecdotes.text: Udon using 5 types of colorful noodles with grains and vegetables kneaded in; ordering udon lets you pick 3 items from the in-store 'oyatsu' snack/dessert table, plus a drink bar, for around ¥1,500.
From April 20, 2026 the Shinjuku location relaunched its full menu around ultra-creamy, milky-white udon with over 25 types of selectable toppings.

caveats: This is a novelty/health-styled udon concept (vegetable-and-grain-kneaded colorful noodles), not a restaurant that markets itself as gluten-free or vegan. No source found any GF or vegan menu, allergen chart, or dedicated-fryer claim, so treat it as a standard wheat-udon shop for celiac purposes. Several detail pages (official DD Holdings site, Lumine floorguide, 4travel, Navitime, Hitosara, AutoReserve, Tabiiro) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so hours/seats/payment details rely on search-engine summaries of those pages rather than a direct read; confirm specifics (esp. exact daily hours and payment methods) with the shop before visiting. Floor number appeared as both 7F (most sources, including the April 2026 PR release) and 8F in one Yahoo Map snippet — 7F is used here as the majority-sourced figure.
closed_days: Follows Lumine Est Shinjuku mall calendar; no fixed weekly closed day identified (不定休、ルミネエストに準ずる)
cuisine: Colorful vegetable/grain-kneaded udon with a self-serve snack/dessert bar
gf_detail: This is a wheat-udon specialty shop (Tabelog categorizes it under "Udon (Wheat noodles)"). The signature colorful noodles have vegetables and grains kneaded into standard udon dough, not gluten-free flour. No gluten-free noodle option, allergen menu, or dedicated fryer/prep area was found in any available source. Soy sauce/dashi bases for the udon broths and sauces were not detailed anywhere accessible, so wheat content there is unconfirmed but should be assumed present as with typical Japanese udon shops. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct, in-person confirmation — and even then, cross-contact in a shared udon kitchen is likely unavoidable.

No mention of a dedicated fryer or any celiac-aware protocol. Do not assume safety here without calling ahead and confirming in Japanese.
