neighborhood: 道玄坂／神泉、渋谷（渋谷マークシティ近く、渋谷駅から徒歩約4分）
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding is disabled in this environment; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding). Address is Dogenzaka 1-20-12, immediately behind/near Shibuya Mark City — well within the 0.8km radius of Shibuya Scramble crossing (35.6595, 139.7005).
seats_text: Small basement izakaya described as having a handful of counter seats plus 2 box/booth seats.
hours_raw: Sources conflict slightly: one aggregator quotes the official site as 平日17:00〜28:00（late night, past midnight）／日曜・祝日18:00〜23:00, while another lists dinner service simply as 18:00〜24:00. Table above uses the conservative 17:00-24:00 (Mon-Sat) / 18:00-23:00 (Sun) reading; true weekday closing may run later into the night. Confirm by phone (03-3770-7522) before a late visit.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day was confirmed in any source; HotPepper's listing directs customers to inquire directly with the restaurant for closure details.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, Japanese-language-oriented regional izakaya tucked in a Dogenzaka basement — no English menu or signage was confirmed, so some Japanese ability or a translation app will help.
caveats: The restaurant's own site (lacela.net) and the Tabelog listing itself could not be directly fetched in this environment (network policy blocked those hosts), so this record is compiled from search-engine snippets of the official site plus secondary aggregators (HotPepper, Jorudan/Bimi, Yahoo Map) and a 2018 Aomori-tourism blog feature. Hours and closed-day details show inconsistency across sources and should be reconfirmed by phone before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation of any kind was found; this is a traditional, dashi- and soy-sauce-heavy Aomori izakaya, included here as a MOM_AND_POP regional-cuisine spot rather than for dietary suitability.

roles: Run by Aomori-born siblings from Tsuruta, Kitatsugaru; the elder sister, Hitoe, tends the evening izakaya service
bio.origin: Tsugaru region, Aomori Prefecture
bio.background: Rasera is a Tsugaru/Aomori regional-cuisine izakaya in a Dogenzaka basement near Shibuya Mark City, opened to bring a taste of home to Aomori natives (and curious Tokyoites) via vegetables, fish, and sake shipped directly from Tsugaru. It is run by a brother-and-sister pair originally from Tsuruta-cho in Kitatsugaru, Aomori; the sister, Hitoe, is the face behind the counter at night.
bio.specialty: Ika no Goroyaki — grilled squid liver from Ajigasawa, Aomori — is cited as the shop's signature dish, alongside Tsugaru staples like kayaki-miso (scallop-shell simmered miso), shoyu-zuke nagaimo, Towada-style bara-yaki, and senbei-jiru hot pot.
bio.philosophy: The name plays on Tsugaru dialect and the ritual shout of Aomori's Nebuta festival ('Rasera, Rasera!'), meant to carry a bit of Aomori's spirit and hospitality into a Shibuya basement.

anecdotes.text: Described in a bar-hopping feature on Tokyo's Aomori-themed izakayas as a night spot where the elder of two Kitatsugaru-born siblings, Hitoe, pours Aomori sake and cooks home-style dishes from the region she grew up in.
The restaurant's name draws on Tsugaru dialect and Nebuta-festival chants so that the name itself signals its Aomori identity to visitors before they even sit down.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区西新宿1-4-10

bio.origin: Founded in 1961 (Shōwa 36) in Nerima Ward's Tagashira, the shop later moved to Nakano, then to Shinjuku's Shokuan-dōri, before settling at its current West Shinjuku location behind Bic Camera, near Shinjuku Station's west exit.
bio.specialty: Chāshū Zaru — a cold dipping-noodle (tsukemen) bowl piled with seven or more slices of pork char siu, each over a centimeter thick, served alongside a tangy, savory soy-based dipping broth and thick, chewy curly noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's founder, referred to as Mr. Takano, is described as the figure whose ramen and career later became the root of the well-known 'Horiuchi' shōyū-ramen family tree in Shinjuku, after moves through Nerima and Nakano before landing at the current West Shinjuku spot.

Reviewers repeatedly describe the chāshū zaru as burying the bowl in meat — thick-cut pork slices covering the noodles almost entirely — a signature so consistent that customers say the taste 'hasn't changed' across decades of visits.

caveats: This is a traditional 1961-founded shōyū ramen/tsukemen counter shop with wheat noodles and a pork/soy-based broth — no gluten-free or vegan accommodation exists. It qualifies as a verified, long-running neighborhood institution near Shinjuku Station's west exit, but is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners; include only as a general mom-and-pop landmark, not a GF/vegan pick.
closed_days: 無休（年末年始を除く） — open daily except around New Year's
cuisine: ラーメン（醤油）／つけ麺
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a cash-only ticket machine with a short, simple menu (ramen or tsukemen), so basic Japanese or pointing at photos is enough — no English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen and tsukemen noodles only; the broth is shōyū (soy sauce) based, which contains wheat, and the char siu-heavy bowls are cooked in a shared, single-menu kitchen. No gluten-free noodle substitute, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen accommodation is mentioned anywhere in available sources.
geocode_note: No geocoding performed (network geocoding disabled in this environment); lat/lng left null. Address and multiple sources place the shop about a 1-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's Nishiguchi (west) exit D2, directly behind the Bic Camera/former Haruku building — well within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: 11:00–23:00, open every day of the week (only closed around New Year's holidays)
neighborhood: 西新宿 (Nishi-Shinjuku) / Shinjuku Station West Exit
seats_text: Counter-only seating, roughly 12-15 seats (sources vary slightly)
vegan_detail: Pork-centric shop built around a shōyū/animal-based broth and a signature bowl piled with 7+ thick slices of char siu; no vegetarian or vegan menu item, plant-based broth, or dashi/katsuobushi-free option is documented anywhere.

lead: ジカセイ メンショウ
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町15-1 SHIBUYA PARCO B1F

bio.origin: Branch of the MENSHO ramen group (flagship San Francisco location holds a Michelin star); this is the group's Shibuya PARCO location, opened as its 10th Japan branch under a "WAGYU × RAMEN" concept.
bio.specialty: For vegan diners: vegan dandan noodles (with or without soup) and vegan miso ramen (regular or spicy). The house's overall signature is chicken-paitan ramen topped with A5 Japanese wagyu.
bio.philosophy: "We honor the ingredients and connect with the people behind them. A bowl rooted in its place. From ramen, culture emerges, and happiness spreads." — chef Tomoharu Shono
bio.background: Jikasei MENSHO is the Shibuya branch of ramen chef Tomoharu Shono's MENSHO group, whose San Francisco outpost was awarded a Michelin star. The Shibuya PARCO shop pairs the group's wagyu-topped chicken-paitan ramen with a separate line of four dedicated vegan bowls, and uses noodles kneaded with quinoa.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its vegan menu as part of a broader effort to make ramen enjoyable for guests with religious or allergy-related dietary restrictions, alongside stated interest in raising environmental awareness through ramen.
English-language reviewers highlight friendly, accommodating staff and a clearly marked vegan menu, describing it as a convenient stop near Shibuya Scramble for a warm, flavorful bowl.

caveats: Noodles are wheat-based dough with quinoa kneaded in — described by the restaurant only as "lower in gluten," not gluten-free — so this is not appropriate for celiac disease or strict gluten-free needs. The four vegan ramen items are prepared in a kitchen that also cooks chicken-paitan, wagyu, and pork-based ramen; ask staff about cross-contact if that matters to you. As a shopping-mall restaurant, hours/closures can shift with Shibuya PARCO's calendar, so confirm before visiting. Direct verification via Tabelog and the official site was blocked by network restrictions in this environment; facts here are drawn from search-indexed content of those and other pages (official MENSHO site, HappyCow, Ramen Gateway, and Japanese press/blog coverage) rather than a direct primary-source fetch.
closed_days: 不定休（渋谷PARCOの営業日に準ずる）— no fixed weekly closed day; follows Shibuya PARCO's operating calendar.
cuisine: Vegan Ramen (ramen specialty shop with four dedicated vegan bowls; also serves chicken-paitan/wagyu and other non-vegan ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering runs through a touch-panel/self-checkout system with English menu options, and the shop is described as extremely popular with foreign tourists, making it an easy, low-stress stop for non-Japanese speakers.
gf_detail: The signature noodles are wheat-based dough with quinoa kneaded in to reduce carbohydrate/gluten content — sources describe this as "lower in gluten," explicitly not gluten-free. No dedicated gluten-free noodle, dedicated fryer, or celiac-safe protocol was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac disease or a strict gluten-free diet; cross-contamination in the shared kitchen (which also handles wheat-containing soy-sauce ramen) is not controlled for.
geocode_note: Address (Udagawa-cho 15-1, Shibuya PARCO B1F) places this a few hundred meters from Shibuya Scramble crossing — well within the 0.8km target radius. Do not geocode; lat/lng left null per instructions.
hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (some secondary sources give slightly different weekday/weekend last-order times — 21:40 weekdays / 20:40 weekends — so confirm current hours before visiting, as this is a shopping-mall tenant).
name_ja: ジカセイ メンショウ
neighborhood: Shibuya (Udagawa-cho, inside Shibuya PARCO B1F), ~5 min walk from Shibuya Station

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles use a wheat-based dough with quinoa mixed in only to reduce gluten content, not eliminate it; the shop shares a kitchen that also makes wheat-containing soy-sauce ramen.
gf_cross_contamination.source: web search synthesis of Japanese press coverage (menya-shono.com / general reporting)

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dandan and vegan miso ramen are prepared in the same kitchen as the restaurant's chicken-paitan and A5-wagyu ramen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Cross-contamination in the cooking environment cannot be guaranteed; guests with severe allergies are advised to confirm directly with the shop before ordering.

positives.text: English-language reviewers praise friendly, accommodating staff and a clearly marked vegan menu, calling it a convenient, warming stop near Shibuya Crossing.
Ordering screens include English options, easing the process for non-Japanese-speaking vegan diners.
positives.source: web search synthesis (Tabelog EN / Ramen Gateway listings)

seats_text: Counter and table seating, approximately 23 seats
vegan_detail: Four dedicated vegan ramen items are offered alongside the main (non-vegan) menu: vegan dandan noodles (with soup), vegan soupless (mazesoba-style) dandan noodles, vegan miso ramen, and spicy vegan miso ramen. These are prepared as fully plant-based bowls separate from the restaurant's chicken-paitan and A5-wagyu ramen, though made in the same shared kitchen. No source found explicitly detailing whether shared broth pots or hidden bonito/katsuo dashi are used elsewhere on the menu that could risk cross-contact for strict vegans concerned about shared equipment; ask staff if that matters to you.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町2-1-7

bio.origin: Founded in 1981 by owner Onodera, an independent-minded soba craftsman who set out to make soba unbound by the old guild techniques of the era.
bio.background: 松翁 opened in Kanda Sarugakucho in 1981 as part of a new wave of independent, self-taught soba shops that broke from the established techniques of the old-line Edo soba lineages. Tucked a short walk from Jimbocho and Suidobashi stations, it became a favorite of local salarymen and soba enthusiasts, and count novelist Shotaro Ikenami among its regular patrons in his lifetime.
bio.specialty: Namagona-uchi zaru soba, hand-made with 100% buckwheat flour, and the shop's signature two-color "awase-mori" plate pairing a plain soba with a seasonal kawari-soba; tempura (shrimp and conger eel kept live in a tank by the entrance) is fried to order and brought to the table piece by piece.
bio.philosophy: A soba-zen approach that treats the meal as an unhurried sequence: soba-zakura small dishes and sake first, tempura fried and served one piece at a time, and the noodles eaten last so nothing arrives lukewarm.

anecdotes.text: Novelist Shotaro Ikenami, one of Japan's best-loved food-and-fiction writers, was a regular customer of the shop.
The shop keeps live shrimp and conger eel in a tank by the entrance and fries tempura to order, bringing each piece to the table as it's ready.

caveats: Address and hours were confirmed via aggregator sites (Navitime, Gnavi, Ekiten, map listings) and older food-blog write-ups because Tabelog and several primary blog URLs returned 403 errors to automated fetching; no official restaurant website could be located. No GF/vegan-specific sourcing (soy sauce type, dedicated equipment) was found — dietary ratings below are inferred conservatively from general knowledge of traditional Edo soba-shop practice (bonito dashi, wheat-based soy sauce, tempura batter, shared boiling water for noodles) rather than restaurant-specific confirmation, since no page addressed allergens directly.
closed_days: Sundays, national holidays, New Year period, and mid-August Obon break
cuisine: Soba (handmade, Edo-style) with tempura and sake pairings
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, cash-only neighborhood soba shop with no English menu or online presence found; a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will go a long way.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions; address confirmed as within walking distance (about 5-6 minutes) of Jimbocho Station and close to Suidobashi Station, placing it inside the Suidōbashi Station target radius.
gf_detail: The zaru soba is made namagona-uchi style with 100% buckwheat flour (no wheat binder in that noodle), which is unusual and noteworthy. However, this is a traditional, non-specialized soba shop: the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is soy-sauce based (soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-containing unless stated otherwise), the shop also serves wheat-containing "kawari-soba" and tempura (wheat-flour batter) on the same menu, and there is no indication the noodles are boiled in separate water from any wheat-containing items or that a dedicated fryer is used. No allergen or celiac-specific information was found from the restaurant itself. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff directly about the tsuyu recipe and boiling/frying setup before ordering, and should not assume safety from the 100% buckwheat noodle alone.
neighborhood: Kanda Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku (near Jimbocho Station, close to Suidobashi Station)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No restaurant-specific statement on separating gluten-free (100% buckwheat) noodle boiling water from wheat-containing noodles or tempura batter was found; this is a traditional soba shop without a specialized allergen protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No confirmation of a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce used in soba tsuyu typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares shrimp and conger-eel tempura and bonito-based dashi alongside the soba, so cross-contact with animal products is likely for any vegan order.

positives.text: The zaru soba is made with 100% buckwheat flour (namagona-uchi), meaning the noodle itself, unlike standard nihachi soba, contains no wheat binder.

seats_text: Table seating for about 24
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba-and-tempura shop, not vegan-oriented. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is dashi-based and almost certainly includes bonito (katsuobushi), and the tempura menu centers on live shrimp and conger eel. No plant-based dashi or vegan menu option was found in any source reviewed. A plain zaru soba with soy-based tsuyu might be closest to vegan-adjacent, but the bonito dashi would need to be confirmed or substituted by staff, which is unconfirmed.

lead: アインソフ ジャーニー新宿店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-8-9 新宿Qビル1F

bio.background: AIN SOPH. is a Tokyo vegan restaurant group founded in 2009; its name comes from the Hebrew phrase for 'great source.' The Shinjuku Journey location, opened in Shinjuku-Sanchome, is the birthplace of the group's now-famous 'Heavenly Vegan Pancakes,' and the brand has since grown to additional locations in Ginza, Ikebukuro, and Kyoto.
bio.specialty: Heavenly Vegan Pancakes (天上のヴィーガンパンケーキ) — egg- and dairy-free pancakes refined over successive chefs — alongside spice-forward plates like green curry and dry curry served on dishware sourced from Morocco.
bio.philosophy: The 'Journey' name reflects a concept of 'creating the self one wants to be': a space designed so vegan and non-vegan diners can share the same table and menu without compromise.

anecdotes.text: The founder has described being inspired to start AIN SOPH. after an experience during a tea ceremony, where the flavor of the tea evoked a sense of nature and the power of life, prompting the idea of a restaurant as 'a place to return to one's origin.'

caveats: Floor listing varies slightly by source (1F vs 1F-2F vs B1F-2F of Shinjuku Q Building) and seat count is cited as approximately 60-62; the core street address (新宿区新宿3-8-9) is consistent across all sources. Lunch/dinner close times also vary slightly between sources (some list uniform daily hours, others list weekday vs weekend differences); the weekday/weekend split is used here as it comes from a more detailed source. lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no geocoding performed) — downstream geocoding should use the address above.
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular closures — check official site/calendar before visiting）
cuisine: ビーガン・野菜料理・インターナショナル (Vegan / plant-based international cuisine)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant runs an official English-language website and marks gluten-free/allergen items in English on the menu, making it easy for non-Japanese-speaking diners to navigate.
geocode_note: Address confirmed by multiple independent sources as 東京都新宿区新宿3-8-9 新宿Qビル, directly by Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line Shinjuku-Sanchome Station Exit C5 (0-3 min walk) and about 7-8 min from JR Shinjuku Station East Exit — well within 0.8km of the Shinjuku Station landmark. No network geocoding was performed; lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from this address.
gf_detail: The menu clearly marks gluten-free items in English, and the signature pancakes have been reformulated using rice flour. However, this is a shared kitchen, not a dedicated allergen-free facility: reviewers report that French fries are cooked in the same fryer as wheat-containing items, and at least one dish labeled gluten-free was found to use a miso that is not gluten-free. The restaurant does not guarantee zero cross-contamination, so celiac diners should confirm each dish with staff before ordering.
name_ja: アインソフ ジャーニー 新宿店
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目

gf_cross_contamination.text: AIN SOPH's kitchen is a shared space where wheat and non-wheat items are prepared together; it is not a dedicated allergen-free facility and the restaurant cannot guarantee that cross-contamination will not occur.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free and other allergen items are clearly marked in English on the menu, but a diner who asked staff directly learned that some items labeled gluten-free actually shared a fryer with wheat items or used a non-gluten-free miso — confirming each dish individually with staff is recommended.

positives.text: Diners have praised the Shinjuku location for its convenient location and clearly available gluten-free options on the menu.

seats_text: 約60席（一部情報源では62席との記載あり）
vegan_detail: The entire menu is 100% plant-based (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and includes an 'oriental vegan' selection that additionally omits garlic, onion, and other strong-flavored roots for diners following that tradition. No animal products are used anywhere in the kitchen.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-31-1 大伸第2ビルB1

bio.origin: Founder was inspired by a bowl of soup curry he ate in Sapporo, Hokkaido, and later opened his own shop in Tokyo
bio.background: Tokyo Dominica opened its doors in Shinjuku on March 11, 2011 — the exact day of the Great East Japan Earthquake. With trains across Tokyo stopped and the city in chaos, almost no customers could reach the brand-new shop, but the staff kept going because they wanted people to taste their soup curry. The shop grew out of the founder's own experience: he had once eaten soup curry in Sapporo and was moved enough by it to bring the dish to Tokyo. The company later opened a sister shop, Yellow Spice, in Ginza in 2012, and another in Osaka in 2019.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds its soup from dozens of spices simmered with a Japanese-style dashi stock, aiming for a curry that is easy to enjoy on a first bite but rewards repeat visits with its layered, evolving spice character — bringing Sapporo-style soup curry into everyday Japanese food culture rather than treating it as a novelty.
bio.specialty: Build-your-own Sapporo-style soup curry: diners choose from five soup bases (yellow, black — with pork bone for richness, red — tomato and garlic, white — soy milk, and deep yellow — coconut milk), roughly twenty spice levels, and a wide range of toppings.

anecdotes.text: Tokyo Dominica opened in Shinjuku on the day of the 2011 Tohoku earthquake, when public transport across the city ground to a halt and the new shop had almost no customers on its first day — the staff pushed through the rocky start because they wanted people to taste their soup curry.
The shop's founder had eaten soup curry in Sapporo years earlier and was moved enough by the experience to open his own soup curry restaurant in Tokyo.

caveats: Address, phone, and the gluten-free/dashi details below come from secondary sources (aggregator listings and a personal blog) because Tabelog and the restaurant's own site returned network errors (403) when fetched directly in this session and could not be independently re-verified. The "gluten-free" claim traces to a single blogger's description, not an official statement from the restaurant, so it is flagged with low confidence — diners with celiac disease or severe allergies should confirm directly with staff before ordering. Seat count and exact phone number could not be confirmed (sources gave conflicting phone numbers).
closed_days: No fixed weekly closed day; hours are irregular (不定休) and the shop often closes for the day once that day's soup curry sells out.
cuisine: スープカレー (Sapporo-style soup curry)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, casual soup-curry counter shop with mostly Japanese-language signage, but the point-and-choose format (soup color, spice level 1-20, toppings) makes ordering manageable even with only basic Japanese.
geocode_note: Not geocoded in this environment (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address (Shinjuku 3-chome) is described in sources as about a 1-minute walk from Shinjuku-sanchome Station Exit A1, which is roughly 0.6-0.7km from JR Shinjuku Station — within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: One independent blog post calls this shop's curry the "original gluten-free curry" (元祖グルテンフリーカレー), and like most Sapporo-style soup curry it is not thickened with the wheat-flour roux used in standard Japanese curry-rice sauce. However, this claim is not corroborated on the restaurant's own official materials, the soup is seasoned with a "Japanese-style dashi" that likely includes ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce, and several toppings (e.g., fried chicken) are typically flour-battered and cooked in a shared fryer with no confirmed dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep process. Treat any gluten-free claim as unverified — ask staff in detail about the specific soup base, seasoning, and toppings before ordering if you have celiac disease.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku-sanchome)

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or prep line is confirmed; fried toppings are likely cooked in shared equipment.
gf_cross_contamination.source: inferred, not directly stated by the restaurant

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant describes its soup as built on dozens of spices plus a "wa-fu dashi" (Japanese-style stock); Japanese-style dashi is conventionally made with bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and/or kelp, and the restaurant does not state that a fish-free or plant-based stock option exists.

seats_text: Small counter-and-table dining room; seating is limited and fills up quickly during lunch and dinner rushes.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu, marking, or plant-based stock option was found in any source. The soup base is described by the restaurant as using a "Japanese-style dashi," which in standard Japanese cooking is typically bonito (fish) and/or kelp based — there is no indication this shop offers a fish-free alternative, so the curry is very unlikely to be vegan-safe as served. Vegans should assume the soup base contains fish-derived dashi unless staff confirm otherwise.

lead: 眞巴石 シンバセイ 薬膳火鍋 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区宇田川町33-12 J&Rビル5F

roles: Sichuan-trained hot pot cooks (chain concept, no individually named chef confirmed)
bio.origin: Yakuzen (medicinal) hot pot restaurant chain with locations in Shibuya and Ueno, Tokyo
bio.background: The restaurant specializes in yakuzen hot pot prepared by cooks trained in authentic Sichuan technique, built on the traditional Chinese idea of 医食同源 (food and medicine share the same root).
bio.specialty: Yakuzen hot pot with a choice of four broth bases — Sichuan-style spicy mala, a chicken white broth simmered over 10 hours, a mushroom broth, and a tomato broth — plus a build-your-own dipping sauce bar of 10-plus condiments (sesame paste, doubanjiang, soy sauce, oyster sauce, chopped chives and more).
bio.philosophy: The broths are simmered with over 30 herbal and medicinal ingredients on the premise that the right soup can support detox, recovery from fatigue, and skin health.

anecdotes.text: 店の中央に胡麻油や芝麻醤、豆板醤、醤油などの調味料が10種類以上置かれ、自分でオリジナルのタレを作れる（1人200円で2皿分、お代わり自由）。
鶏白湯スープは10時間以上煮込まれ、コラーゲンが豊富で旨味が濃い。
30種類以上の漢方食材が配合されたスープが、デトックスや疲労回復、美肌効果につながるとされる。

caveats: This is a Chinese/Sichuan-style build-your-own hot pot restaurant, not a vegan- or gluten-free-dedicated spot. Diners select their own vegetables, tofu, mushrooms and meat/seafood to cook in a shared pot, and mix a dipping sauce from a shared condiment bar that includes soy sauce and oyster sauce. No vegan menu, allergen menu, or gluten-free accommodation could be confirmed from available sources — ask staff directly about broth ingredients (one confirmed base is a 10+-hour chicken broth) and sauce contents before ordering if avoiding gluten or animal products is essential.
closed_days: No regular weekly closed day; closed only Dec 31 and Jan 1 per multiple listings (年中無休、12/31・1/1を除く).
cuisine: 薬膳火鍋・中華（四川式火鍋、しゃぶしゃぶ）
cultural_comfort_note: Listings and menus found are in Japanese with online reservation via Japanese portals (Hot Pepper, Gurunavi, Tabelog); no confirmation of an English menu or English-speaking staff.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen guide was found. This is a DIY hot pot restaurant: guests cook shared raw ingredients (vegetables, tofu, mushrooms, meat, noodles) in a communal pot and mix their own dipping sauce from a bar of 10+ condiments that includes soy sauce and oyster sauce — both typically wheat-containing in Japan — plus sesame paste and doubanjiang, all served with shared utensils. One of the four broth bases is a long-simmered chicken broth; the ingredient makeup of the other three (mala, mushroom, tomato) is not confirmed. Because sauces and pots are shared and self-served, cross-contact with wheat-based condiments is likely. Anyone needing a gluten-free meal should ask staff in detail before ordering.
name_en: Yakuzen Hinabe Shinbasei Shibuya Ten (Yakuzen Hot Pot Shinbasei, Shibuya)
name_ja: 薬膳火鍋 真巴石 渋谷店（シンバセイ）
neighborhood: 渋谷センター街・宇田川町（渋谷駅から徒歩3〜5分）

gf_cross_contamination.text: 店の中央に胡麻油、芝麻醤、豆板醤、醤油など10種類以上の調味料が置かれ、客が自分でタレを作る仕組み（共有の調味料・器具を使用）。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 自家製タレ作りの調味料バーに醤油（しょうゆ）が含まれる。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 4種類のスープベースの一つは10時間以上煮込んだ鶏白湯スープ。タレ作りの調味料にオイスターソースを含む。

positives.text: 火鍋形式のため、客が自分で新鮮な野菜・きのこ・豆腐などの具材を選んで鍋に入れて調理できる。

seats_text: Mix of counter seating (2-seat window counter), semi-private seating for 7-8, and private rooms for 15-22 or 16-20 guests; whole-venue buyout available for up to 50 guests.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu was found. The hot pot format lets guests choose vegetables, mushrooms, and tofu for their pot, but the shared self-serve sauce bar includes oyster sauce (not vegan), and one of the four confirmed broth bases is a chicken broth simmered over 10 hours; the mushroom and tomato broth bases' ingredients (whether they use meat/bonito-based stock) are not confirmed from available sources. Vegans should ask staff specifically about broth composition and avoid the chicken broth and oyster-sauce-based condiments.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田猿楽町2-7-11 ハマダビルヂング 2F（〒101-0064）

roles: Owner-chef (name in Japanese order: 小城正樹, romanized Koshiro Masaki / Oshiro Masaki — exact preferred romanization unconfirmed)
bio.background: Before opening his own shop, the owner worked at the original インド富士 (India Fuji) curry restaurant in Higashi-Koganei. In February 2016 he opened インド富士子 (India Fujiko) as that restaurant's sister shop in Koenji, running an 8-seat counter that became a cult favorite among Tokyo curry enthusiasts. In October 2021 the shop relocated to Suidōbashi (Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku), where it now runs a dual identity: a South Indian-style curry lunch counter by day under the name インド富士子, and an evening spice/izakaya bar called ムンド不二 (Mundo Fuji) by night.
bio.origin: Sister shop lineage from インド富士, Higashi-Koganei
bio.specialty: Lunch curry plate — choose 2 of the day's rotating curries (e.g. pork vindaloo, spinach chicken curry, fish curry, dal curry) served over basmati rice, ¥1,300
bio.philosophy: A personal, idiosyncratic take on South Indian cooking informed by firsthand experience of South Indian food culture, prized by curry devotees for depth and individuality rather than a standardized 'Indian restaurant' menu.

anecdotes.text: Described by a travel-blog reviewer as 'a hidden gem serving a distinctive South Indian plate backed by firsthand experience in India, for curry maniacs' (水道橋発のインドカレー店「インド富士子」～現地での体験に裏打ちされた個性的な南インドプレートをカレーマニアに提供する隠れた名店).
The owner was interviewed alongside musician Saya of Tenniscoats and Koiken of Atarashii Hito about the shop's identity and its move to Suidōbashi.

In an October 2023 social post marking two years at the Suidōbashi location, the owner wrote candidly that the shop had barely survived with supporters' help and was 'honestly about at its limit,' asking people to visit while it was still open.

caveats: Original target sheet listed the neighborhood as 神田三崎町, but every current source places this shop at 神田猿楽町2-7-11 (Hamada Building 2F) — still about a 4-minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station's east exit, i.e. within the same Suidōbashi target radius. In October 2023 the owner publicly indicated the business was financially struggling ('about at its limit'); no explicit closure notice was found and there are indications of activity into 2024/2025, but given this is a small independent operation, confirm current operating status/hours before a visit. No page found that discusses gluten-free or vegan accommodation specifically — dietary ratings below reflect general Indian-curry-shop caution, not a confirmed policy from the restaurant itself. Seat count could not be reliably confirmed (one secondary source suggested ~19 total seats post-relocation vs. 8 counter seats at the old Koenji location) so it is left unconfirmed.
closed_days: Sundays, Mondays, and public holidays (定休日: 日・月・祝)
cuisine: South Indian-style curry (lunch); spice/izakaya bar "ムンド不二" (evening)
cultural_comfort_note: Small, cash-only, regulars-heavy indie shop with a Japanese-only social-media presence and no confirmed English menu, so basic Japanese or pointing at photos will help.
gf_detail: No page mentions gluten-free accommodation, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free preparation. This is spice-forward South Indian-style curry (not the roux-based Japanese curry that reliably contains wheat), served with basmati rice, which is naturally lower-risk than a wheat-roux curry house, but ingredients like ghee, marinades, or fried items (e.g. "Chicken 65") could involve flour or shared equipment. Nothing confirms dedicated GF prep or cross-contamination controls, so celiac diners should ask staff directly about each curry and any fried items before ordering.
geocode_note: Do not geocode here (network geocoding blocked); address above is 千代田区神田猿楽町2-7-11 ハマダビルヂング2F, confirmed by multiple sources as roughly a 4-minute walk from JR Suidōbashi Station's east exit — well within the 0.8km Suidōbashi target radius.
hours_raw: Lunch (curry, as インド富士子): Tue-Fri 12:00-14:30. Dinner (spice bar, as ムンド不二): Tue-Sat 18:00-23:00. Closed Sundays, Mondays, and public holidays.
neighborhood: Kanda-Sarugakucho, Chiyoda-ku (Suidōbashi Station area)
seats_text: Small counter and a few tables; exact seat count not reliably confirmed (secondary sources suggest roughly high-teens seats since the 2021 Suidōbashi relocation, up from 8 counter seats at the earlier Koenji location).
vegan_detail: The rotating daily curry lineup is meat/fish-forward (pork vindaloo, chicken, fish curry) but typically includes a dal (lentil) curry, which is plant-based unless prepared with ghee or other animal fat — not confirmed either way. No mention anywhere of a dedicated vegan menu, and no information on whether shared pots/utensils are used across meat and vegetarian curries. Vegan diners should ask which of the day's curries are meat/fish-free and whether ghee is used.

lead: 油そば総本店 新宿東口アルタ裏店
address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-21-2 柳屋角筈ビル 1F・B1F

bio.background: Part of the 東京油組総本店 (Tokyo Abura Gumi Sohonten) abura-soba chain, which the operator's own promotional material traces to a long-established (multi-decade) noodle manufacturer that branched into running its own oil-noodle shops across Tokyo. This Shinjuku East Exit branch sits directly behind Studio Alta, tucked into a building with counter seating on the ground floor and further seating in the basement, and runs 24 hours a day so it doubles as a late-night/after-the-last-train option.
bio.specialty: 特製油そば (tokusei abura-soba) — thick, springy noodles tossed (not served in broth) with a proprietary blended soy-sauce tare, topped with menma, sliced pork chashu, negi and nori; a spicy miso version is also offered. Diners finish the bowl themselves with a few circles of vinegar and rayu chili oil, mixing before eating.
bio.philosophy: The chain markets abura-soba as a lighter, lower-calorie alternative to broth ramen, built around the quality of the tare (sauce) and noodle rather than a long-simmered soup, with all bowl sizes (regular/large/extra-large) priced the same so portion size is the diner's choice.

anecdotes.text: House description: the tare blends several different soy sauces to build depth of flavor under the oil-noodle format, with a separate spicy-miso tare balancing heat and miso richness.
Blog account of visiting the chain describes it as a specialty shop built around a rich soy-sauce tare finished tableside with vinegar and chili oil.

caveats: Standard bowl uses wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare with pork chashu; no gluten-free noodle substitute, allergen breakdown, or vegan/vegetarian menu option was found in any source consulted. Treat as an omnivore ramen-style stop only — not suitable to recommend for celiac or strict vegan diners without the shop confirming otherwise on-site.
closed_days: None — open 24 hours a day, 365 days a year (no scheduled closures found in any source).
cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば (abura-soba / oil-based mixed noodles)
cultural_comfort_note: Order and pay at a Japanese-language ticket vending machine near the entrance; no English menu was confirmed, though the shop is well used to late-night and international foot traffic in the Alta-back area.
geocode_note: Not geocoded (network geocoding disabled). Multiple sources place the shop about a 0-minute walk from Shinjuku Station Tokyo Metro Exit B12 (north side), directly behind Studio Alta — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station radius.
gf_detail: The signature dish is a wheat-noodle abura-soba tossed in a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free noodle alternative or allergen menu was found in any source. Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) is typically wheat-based, and this shop's tare is described only as "a blend of several soy sauces," with no gluten-free claim anywhere. This is a small, ticket-machine-driven ramen counter with no indication of allergen protocols, so cross-contamination risk should be assumed high. Not recommended for celiac diners.
hours_raw: 24時間営業・年中無休 (Open 24 hours, 365 days a year)
name_en: Aburasoba Sohonten Shinjuku Higashiguchi Alta Ura-ten
name_ja: 油そば総本店 新宿東口アルタ裏店
neighborhood: 新宿東口 (Shinjuku East Exit / behind Studio Alta)
seats_text: 1F: 9 counter seats; B1F: 9 counter seats + 8 table seats (26 seats total across two floors)
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu option was found in any source. The default bowl includes pork chashu, and the proprietary soy-sauce tare's full ingredient list (which may include animal-derived stock or extracts) is not disclosed. There is no dashi-based soup here (it's a tossed, not brothed, noodle), but the tare itself is undisclosed and unconfirmed as plant-based. Do not assume vegan-safe without asking staff directly.

lead: つるとんたん UDON NOODLE Brasserie 渋谷店
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-24-12 渋谷スクランブルスクエア13F（郵便番号150-6113）

bio.origin: Sōemonchō, Osaka (1989); founder grew up in Sanuki, Kagawa
bio.background: TsuruTonTan traces back to 1978, when Kato Seiichi — who grew up in the udon country of Sanuki, Kagawa — opened a hand-pulled udon shop called Honke Sanuki in Osaka, at a time when chewy, Sanuki-style udon wasn't common there. That shop's technique became the basis for TsuruTonTan, which opened in Osaka's Sōemonchō district in 1989, blending Sanuki noodle-making with Osaka's food culture. The brand is now run by Kato Pleasure Group and has grown into a chain across Osaka, Tokyo, and other cities, plus locations in the United States. The Shibuya Scramble Square branch opened in 2019 as the chain's seventh Tokyo location.
bio.specialty: Oversized bowls of udon made fresh at each location — noodles kneaded, cut, and boiled to order — served in dozens of styles from classic bukkake and curry udon to cream-based seafood bowls.
bio.philosophy: Each restaurant makes its own noodles daily rather than using a central factory, aiming to serve udon at its peak just after boiling. The broth recipe — Hokkaido Rishiri kombu paired with Kagoshima Makurazaki bonito flakes — has reportedly stayed unchanged for more than three decades.

anecdotes.text: The Shibuya location sits on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, offering views over the famous scramble crossing while diners eat; it opened in 2019 as the chain's 7th Tokyo shop.

TsuruTonTan's dashi is built from thick kombu from Hokkaido's cold Rishiri waters and quality bonito flakes from Kagoshima's Makurazaki, a combination the company says has defined its flavor since founding.

caveats: Tabelog, the official tsurutontan.co.jp pages, and several other listing sites returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch during this verification (network/proxy policy, not a site-down issue), so details below are cross-referenced from multiple independent search-indexed sources (aggregator listings, the official site's indexed content, Wikipedia's indexed summary, and product/ingredient pages) rather than a single direct fetch of tsurutontan.co.jp or Tabelog. Vegan/gluten-free assessment is based on brand-wide ingredient composition (wheat udon noodles, kombu-bonito dashi) rather than a location-specific allergy statement for the Shibuya branch — diners with celiac disease or strict vegan requirements should confirm directly with staff before ordering. Some low-quality aggregator sites claimed "gluten-free udon noodles are available" here, but this could not be corroborated by any primary or reputable secondary source and was excluded as unreliable.
closed_days: None (open year-round, no listed regular closures)
cuisine: Udon
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is done via tablet with English, Korean, and Chinese language support, and the location inside Shibuya Scramble Square regularly serves international visitors.
geocode_note: lat/lng left null per instructions (no network geocoding performed). Address places the restaurant on the 13th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square, directly atop/adjacent to Shibuya Station — well within 0.8km of the Shibuya Scramble Square target-area landmark.
gf_detail: The udon here is traditional wheat-flour noodle, made fresh in-house at each location — there is no confirmed gluten-free noodle substitute for this restaurant in any source checked. The broth (kake-jiru) is soy-sauce based and soy sauce traditionally contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or kitchen process was found. This is not a safe option for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00 (L.O. food 22:00), daily, no listed closed days
name_ja: つるとんたん UDON NOODLE Brasserie 渋谷店
neighborhood: Shibuya (渋谷)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The noodle broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) base and the noodles are standard wheat udon, made fresh at each location — wheat is present throughout the core menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dashi is built from Hokkaido Rishiri kombu and Kagoshima Makurazaki bonito (fish) flakes, and is used as the base for most dishes on the standard menu, including many vegetable-topped bowls, so most dishes are not vegan by default.

positives.text: Table-side ordering tablets support English, Korean, and Chinese, which can make it easier to ask staff about ingredients even without shared Japanese.

seats_text: Approximately 108 seats, including counter seating and sofa seating, with skyline views over Shibuya.
vegan_detail: The house dashi (broth) used across the standard menu is fish-based (kombu and bonito flakes), so the great majority of dishes, including many vegetable-topped bowls, are not vegan even when they look plant-based. No confirmed dedicated vegan menu or fish-free broth option was found for this Tokyo (Shibuya) location — claims of vegan udon appear tied to other TsuruTonTan branches (e.g., in the US) and could not be verified for Shibuya specifically. Vegan diners should not assume any dish is animal-free without asking staff directly, and even then cross-contact with the shared fish-based broth is likely.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区神田三崎町2-15-5 三崎町SSビル 1F

roles: Owner / Chef: Matsumura Yasushi (松村康史)
bio.background: The owner, Matsumura Yasushi, spent 36 years working in French cuisine, rising to executive head chef at the former Kyoto ANA Hotel. Wanting to serve carefully made food at a price more people could enjoy, he moved from haute French kitchens into ramen, opening this small counter shop in Suidobashi in 2018 as the starting point of his ramen career.
bio.specialty: Niboshi shoyu chuka soba — a soy-sauce ramen built on a broth of dried sardines (niboshi) from Kujukushima in Nagasaki Prefecture, simmered together with chicken, paired with noodles made specially for the shop by a long-established Asakusa noodle maker.
bio.philosophy: Bringing the discipline and refinement of French technique into a humble bowl of ramen, so that thoughtfully crafted food can reach more people at an accessible price.

anecdotes.text: Matsumura's ramen career began at this Suidobashi counter in 2018; its success led him to open Tsukesoba Kanda Katsumoto and the Michelin-recognized Ginza Hachigo, both of which now draw daily lines.

The shop's original operating company went through bankruptcy proceedings in March 2022 and reopened under new management (Food Operation Japan Co., Ltd.) that May, continuing at the same Suidobashi counter.

caveats: Primary pages (official Tabelog listing, restaurant's own site, several blogs) could not be directly fetched in this environment (blocked/403), so details are compiled from web-search snippets of those pages rather than direct page reads. Reported hours conflict somewhat across sources (some cite 11:00-22:00 weekdays/11:00-18:00 Sunday with no closed day; others cite an earlier 11:00-20:00/21:00 close with irregular Sunday/Monday closures, and ramen shops of this type often close early once the soup sells out) — confirm current hours via the shop's official social accounts (X: @chu_katsumoto, Instagram: @katsumoto_suidobashi) before visiting.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (year-round except New Year holidays), per most sources — but may close for the day early if the soup sells out, and some sources report irregular Sunday/Monday closures, so this is not fully certain.
cuisine: Ramen (niboshi-shoyu / dried-sardine soy-sauce ramen)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a Japanese-language ticket vending machine (券売機) and payment is cash only, so a little familiarity with basic Japanese ramen terms (or pointing at photos on the machine) helps, though the shop is popular enough with visitors that queuing and ordering are fairly self-explanatory.
geocode_note: Not geocoded in this environment (network geocoding disabled); lat/lng left null for downstream geocoding from address_ja. Address and multiple sources place the shop a 1-3 minute walk from JR/Toei Suidobashi Station, comfortably within the target radius of that landmark.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen noodles are used, made specially for the shop by a long-established Tokyo noodle maker, and the broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which is typically wheat-containing. No gluten-free noodle substitute or dedicated allergen protocol was found in any source, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strictly gluten-free diners.
hours_raw: Most-cited hours: Mon-Sat 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:45), Sun 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:45); no fixed closed day except New Year holidays. An older/alternate source instead reports Mon-Sat 11:00-20:00 or 21:00 (closing when soup runs out) with Sunday and irregular Monday closures. Treat with some caution and reconfirm before visiting.
neighborhood: Kanda Misakicho, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidobashi Station)
seats_text: 17 counter seats (no tables)
vegan_detail: The broth is built on dried sardines (niboshi) simmered with chicken, and the noodles are standard wheat noodles — there is no plant-based broth or noodle option, so this is not vegan or vegetarian.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-32-5 日原ビルB1

bio.origin: Tokyo (b. 1956)
bio.background: Founded as "Kappou Nakajima" in Ginza in 1931 by the grandfather of current chef-owner Nakajima Teiji, who had been the first head chef at Kitaoji Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo. Nakajima's father later opened the Shinjuku branch. Nakajima Teiji took over as owner in 1980 after training in Kyoto, and also teaches at culinary schools.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kappo/kaiseki courses built around fish and other ingredients the chef selects each morning; a sardine (iwashi) preparation in the lunch set is called out by name in diner reviews.
bio.philosophy: Continues a culinary lineage traced to Kitaoji Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo, combining Kyoto-trained kaiseki fundamentals with an emphasis on drawing out the natural flavor of each season's ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Chef Nakajima personally visits the market each morning to select the day's seasonal ingredients, continuing a family tradition begun by his grandfather at Rosanjin's Hoshigaoka Saryo.
A diner review of the restaurant is titled "鰯三昧嬉しやランチ" (a joyful feast-of-sardines lunch), referencing the sardine-centered lunch set as a house favorite.

caveats: Could not directly fetch Tabelog, Hitosara, Gnavi, Hotpepper, or the official site (all returned HTTP 403 to the fetch tool); all details are drawn from search-result snippets of these pages, cross-checked across at least two independent sources where possible. Sources disagree slightly on exact dinner closing time (21:00 LO vs 22:00/LO20:00 in an older snippet) and on which days are closed — the most recent snippet notes the closed-days policy changed as of March 2024 to Sunday+Monday+holidays, which is what is used here. No restaurant-specific statement on gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found anywhere; the GF/vegan assessment below is inferred from the restaurant's traditional fish-and-soy-sauce-based kappo/kaiseki style, not from an explicit restaurant statement.
closed_days: 日曜・月曜・祝日 (Sunday, Monday, and public holidays)
cuisine: 日本料理・割烹（懐石料理）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, reservation-based kappo restaurant with a bilingual (JP/EN) website but reservations and service are primarily conducted in Japanese, so calling ahead or booking through a Japanese-capable platform is recommended.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu, ingredient breakdown, or dedicated preparation process could be confirmed from any source. As a traditional kappo/kaiseki restaurant, dishes almost certainly include soy sauce (standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based) and fried ("揚げ物") courses, both common wheat sources, and no source mentioned tamari or a gluten-free adaptation. Guests with celiac disease should not assume safety here without calling ahead and confirming directly with the restaurant.
geocode_note: Address (新宿3-32-5) is confirmed by multiple sources as a 2-minute walk from Tokyo Metro Shinjuku-sanchome Station and a 3-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station (South/East Exit) — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius. lat/lng left null per instructions; no network geocoding performed.
neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)
seats_text: Counter seating plus several private rooms, approximately 30 seats total
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centered kappo/kaiseki restaurant; the chef personally sources fish each morning and courses include sashimi, grilled fish, and traditional dashi (Japanese kaiseki dashi is typically bonito/katsuo-based). No vegan menu, plant-based substitution, or dashi alternative was found in any source, so it should not be assumed vegan-friendly.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田1-4-11 サンポウ水道橋ビル1F

bio.chef_name: Matsuhashi (owner/founder, family name from Tabelog/blog reviews; given name not confirmed)
bio.origin: Kanda Misakicho / Nishi-Kanda, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo
bio.background: The shop takes its name from 愛光舎 (Aikousha), a Meiji-era dairy/milk shop that the current owner's great-grandfather ran in the same Kanda Misakicho neighborhood; the family's milk business passed through three generations on nearby ground before the site became a hamburger counter around 2003. In July 2025 the shop relocated a few minutes away to Nishi-Kanda after more than a decade in its original spot, reopening in a refreshed space under the same name.
bio.specialty: Hand-formed 100% beef patty burgers served deconstructed — patty and toppings on the plate with the bun set on top as a lid for the diner to assemble — paired with house-made potato chips that take two full days to prepare.
bio.philosophy: Owner Matsuhashi says he never copied another shop's recipes; he sketches out 'what would taste good' in his head before developing a dish, refining American-style touches like mayonnaise application and bean preparation with guidance from relatives in Alabama.
roles: founder (of current hamburger concept; shop name inherited from great-grandfather's dairy business)

anecdotes.text: The shop's name preserves the memory of 愛光舎, the owner's great-grandfather's Meiji-era milk shop that once operated in the same Kanda Misakicho area.
Matsuhashi builds recipes from his own imagination rather than borrowing from other burger shops, consulting American relatives in Alabama on techniques.
The burger is served unassembled with the bun as a lid so customers build it themselves, and the side potato chips are made in-house over a two-day process.
After more than ten years in Kanda Misakicho, the shop relocated to nearby Nishi-Kanda and reopened on July 11, 2025.

caveats: This shop relocated in July 2025 from its original address in Kanda Misakicho (神田三崎町) to a nearby site in Nishi-Kanda (西神田1-4-11), still a 3-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station East Exit — well within the Suidobashi target radius. The Tabelog URL given for verification (.../13006525/) is now marked "[Relocated]" on Tabelog; the current active listing is at tabelog.com/en/tokyo/A1310/A131003/13311990/. No official restaurant website was found (only an Instagram account). Hours often end earlier than listed because the shop sells out. No gluten-free or vegan-specific accommodations, dedicated fryer info, or allergy-handling statements were found in any source — this is a standard wheat-bun beef burger shop with no indication it caters to those diets; treat GF/vegan claims as unconfirmed and low-confidence.
closed_days: Wednesdays (定休日); hours also end early on other days once sold out
cuisine: Premium Hamburger (100% beef hand-formed patties)
cultural_comfort_note: Small, old-school cash-only counter shop with no indication of English menus or staff — manageable with basic pointing/ordering but not set up for non-Japanese speakers.
geocode_note: Lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address confirms location is roughly a 3-minute walk from JR Suidobashi Station East Exit and about 5 minutes from Jimbocho Station, placing it well within 0.8km of the Suidobashi Station landmark (35.7017, 139.7539).
gf_detail: No gluten-free bun, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation was mentioned in any source. The burger is served deconstructed with a standard wheat bun used as a "lid" the diner adds themselves, so a diner could in theory skip the bun, but no gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free sauce/dressing information was confirmed anywhere. Treat as not gluten-free-aware; sauces (mayonnaise-based dressings) and any flour-dusted components are unconfirmed for wheat content.
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri/Sat 11:00–17:30, Sun/holidays 11:00–15:30, closed Wednesdays; closes early once sold out (売り切れ次第終了)
neighborhood: Nishi-Kanda / Suidōbashi East, Chiyoda-ku
seats_text: Small counter-style shop with limited seating; fills quickly around lunchtime
vegan_detail: No vegan or plant-based patty/menu option was found in any source; this is a beef-focused burger shop with mayonnaise-based dressings and cheese options, and no indication of accommodating vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-35-5 2F

bio.origin: Tokyo, Shinjuku
bio.background: 魚真 (Uoshin) Shinjuku is a seafood-focused izakaya and sushi counter run by Uoshin Co., Ltd. (株式会社シン), which operates a small handful of Uoshin locations built around a fresh-fish-wholesaler concept — fish is sourced daily and served the same day.
bio.specialty: "Nokke-zushi" (のっけ寿司) — a hand roll piled high with sea urchin (uni), negitoro, crab, and salmon roe; also known for its daily-changing sashimi platter of seasonal fish.
bio.philosophy: Fish delivered fresh each day is prepared multiple ways — sashimi, grilled, simmered, deep-fried (tempura/furai), and sake-steamed — with counter seating so diners can watch the chefs work.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's signature 'nokke-zushi' piles uni, negi-toro, crab, and salmon roe on top of a hand roll, a specialty shared across Uoshin's locations.
Fish bought fresh daily is served as sashimi or cooked as grilled fish, simmered fish, fried/tempura, or sake-steamed, with counter seats where guests can watch the chef prepare each dish.

caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodation, allergy protocol, or dedicated-fryer information could be confirmed from any public source. bio.origin: First stand-alone bakery from WOODBERRY COFFEE, a Tokyo specialty coffee roaster (opened its first café in Yoga); the bakery opened along Meiji-dori between Shibuya and Ebisu in spring 2024 as the brand's first bake shop.
bio.background: WOODBERRY COFFEE built a following through its Shibuya-area specialty coffee shops before extending the brand into baking, opening this dedicated bake shop as a production base for vegan and gluten-free-recipe breads and pastries.
bio.specialty: Organic, vegan-recipe breads and baked goods (including gluten-free items and, more recently, naturally leavened sourdough), plus lunch plates built around organic vegetables.
bio.philosophy: Described by the shop itself as a bakery that is "kind to both your body and the planet" — using carefully selected organic ingredients and vegan/gluten-free recipes for a wellness- and sustainability-minded neighborhood bakery.

anecdotes.text: The bakery's own opening announcement frames it as WOODBERRY COFFEE's first bake shop, with a "park in the town" interior of wood and tile and large street-facing windows, built around the concept of body- and earth-friendly baking.
A Japanese food blogger described the vegan breads and pastries as not leaving the stomach feeling heavy, and noted the shop draws visible lines from the street at busy times.
A magazine feature framed the shop's appeal as an 'organic lunch and sweets that are kind to the earth and the body' in the Shibuya area.

bio.background: Nojima began as a fishmonger ("魚仁") founded in the Meiji era (around 1910); the current owner-chef, born in 1937, started helping his father at the fish shop after middle school and has worked in the trade for roughly 68 years. The shop shifted into serving cooked fish meals to customers starting in 1989. The owner still rises at 5am most days to buy fish himself at the Toyosu market, relying on decades of experience to judge quality, and personally fillets every fish and tallies the bill by mental arithmetic at the register.
bio.origin: Tokyo
bio.philosophy: Only natural, wild-caught fish is used — no frozen or farmed fish — reflecting the eye for quality the owner developed over decades as a fishmonger before the shop began serving meals.
bio.specialty: Tennen buri-don (天然ぶり丼) — a bowl of wild yellowtail sashimi dressed in a soy-and-wasabi marinade, served with miso soup, potato salad, kelp tsukudani and pickles; also grilled and simmered fish sets (aji tataki, kinki, isaki, buri kama).

anecdotes.text: At 80 the owner gave up his driver's license and now gets around by bicycle, with a set routine of a morning coffee in Ebisu and an afternoon break at a nearby Doutor.
He still personally negotiates for and buys the day's fish at the market at 5am, and does all the filleting and register accounting himself, including tallying bills by mental math.

caveats: Hours vary slightly across sources, especially for Saturday (some list 18:00-22:00 only, others list an 11:00-14:00-or-sold-out lunch service) — call ahead to confirm. Payment methods (cash vs. card) could not be confirmed from available sources. Seat count is reported inconsistently across sources (ranging from a small ~8-seat counter to a larger multi-floor layout); treated as unconfirmed. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations were found in any source — this is a traditional Japanese fish restaurant with no indication of dietary-specific menu options; the signature yellowtail bowl is dressed in a soy-sauce-based (wheat) marinade and all core dishes are fish-based, so it is not suitable for celiac or vegan diners without independent confirmation from the restaurant.

bio.origin: Owner reportedly grew up in Canada with family roots in the American South
bio.background: Midtown BBQ began as a small steak-and-smokehouse concept in Nagoya and built a loyal following among BBQ fans in Japan before expanding to Yokohama and this Tokyo (Iidabashi) location. Rather than following one single regional American barbecue tradition, the kitchen blends several influences, making its own rubs and smoking meats in-house.
bio.specialty: A5 Wagyu Brisket, slow-smoked over many hours — described as the dish that put the restaurant on the map
bio.philosophy: Real, slow-smoked American barbecue done with a mix of regional influences rather than a single house style, paired with char-grilled steaks, burgers, and craft beer in a casual, come-as-you-are setting.

anecdotes.text: Midtown BBQ started as a small steak and smokehouse in Nagoya and built a loyal following of BBQ lovers in Japan before growing into locations in Yokohama and Tokyo.
The A5 Wagyu Brisket is considered the dish that made Midtown BBQ famous, made without adherence to one specific regional barbecue recipe.

caveats: Direct fetches of Tabelog, the official Midtown BBQ site, TableCheck, and several aggregator pages (EPARK, HotPepper, Wanderlog, TripAdvisor, artisansmoker.com) were blocked by network policy (403) in this environment, so those details are reconstructed from search-result snippets rather than full page content. bio.background: Founded in 2010 by an owner originally from Shanghai, opened in Shinjuku to bring authentic Shanghai-style xiaolongbao and dim sum to Tokyo.
bio.origin: Shanghai, China
bio.specialty: Hand-made xiaolongbao (soup dumplings), including a grilled/pan-fried version (焼き小籠包) with a crisp seared bottom and juicy broth inside a chewy, made-to-order skin.
bio.philosophy: Dumpling skins are hand-rolled and filled to order in an open 'xiaolongbao room' visible from the entrance, so every order is freshly wrapped and cooked rather than pre-made; filling is 100% domestic (Japanese) pork.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant description highlights that skins are made from scratch and cooked to order so they arrive freshly baked and hot, with a chewy skin and crispy bottom, and that at the kitchen entrance there is a dedicated 'xiaolongbao room' where staff continuously stretch dough and wrap filling for guests to watch.
Listed as a Shinjuku dim sum/yum-cha specialist near Shinjuku Station's central east exit, known specifically for its grilled xiaolongbao (焼き小籠包).

caveats: This is a mainstream, meat-focused Shanghai dim sum restaurant with no evidence of gluten-free or vegan accommodation; it is included/flagged here as OMNI (not GF/VEGAN/BOTH) and celiac or vegan diners should treat it as unsuitable absent direct confirmation. bio.origin: Shinsen, Shibuya, Tokyo
bio.background: Opened around May 2023 near Shinsen Station as the second location of the brothers' popular Shibuya standing-bar izakaya "Otonage" (大人気). Elder brother Kenta trained at a culinary school and a Japanese-cuisine restaurant before apprenticing within the Osaka-based SASAYA group (yakitori, kamameshi, oden izakayas across roughly 47 locations); younger brother Kota came from consulting and real-estate work before joining Tokyo's "Product of Time" restaurant-development group.
bio.specialty: Playful, craft-driven izakaya small plates (reported items include yellowtail with sweet ginger and a chrysanthemum-topped takoyaki) alongside an extensive spirits, highball and craft-gin list.
bio.philosophy: The name "嚔" (kushami, "sneeze") is styled "Achuu" — the sneeze sound people jokingly say happens when someone is gossiping about you. The bar deliberately does not publish its own address, aiming to spread purely by word of mouth and build a devoted, in-the-know following through old-fashioned personal connection rather than social-media marketing.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on the Japanese word for "sneeze": the idea is that when people talk about the bar behind its back, that gossip should spread the way a sneeze spreads — so the concept intentionally withholds the address and lets rumor do the marketing.

Achuu is the Sunada brothers' second Shibuya venture after their hit standing bar Otonage; Kenta apprenticed through a 47-restaurant Osaka izakaya group before returning to Tokyo, while Kota's background is in consulting and real estate before moving into restaurant production.
A visitor review describes the cooking as "quality that's on another level" and notes that although the menu item names are full of playful humor, the food itself is seriously accomplished.

caveats: IMPORTANT DISCREPANCY: This listing's premise ("gluten-free specialties," "gluten-free takoyaki") could NOT be confirmed anywhere in public sources. Extensive targeted searches (Tabelog menu/review pages, food-industry write-ups, gourmet blogs) turned up no mention of gluten-free (グルテンフリー) preparation, rice-flour takoyaki, or any celiac/allergy accommodation at this venue. It appears to be a conventional, cash-only, standing-bar izakaya run by the Sunada brothers, known for playful menu names, craft spirits, and word-of-mouth-only marketing (the shop deliberately does not publish its own address) — not for gluten-free or vegan cooking. Treat the GF/vegan fields below as "no confirmed information," not as a verified accommodation. Hours are described by sources as approximate ("アバウト"); one older source claimed a Sunday closure while more recent sources say the bar is open daily (無休) — flagged as irregular pending confirmation. Direct fetches of Tabelog, Tokyo Calendar, Livedoor, and other Japanese source pages were blocked (HTTP 403) by the environment's outbound proxy for this session, so all findings here are drawn from search-engine result snippets of those pages rather than full page fetches; nothing was invented beyond what those snippets stated.

vegan_detail: All signature dishes are built on a fish (tai/sea bream) broth from Ehime Prefecture, and the tantanmen style typically includes meat-based elements (ground pork, sesame paste). gf_detail: The restaurant's identity is built around its "specially developed" wavy wheat noodles paired with the tai broth, and shoyu/soy-based seasoning is standard for this style of ramen (wheat-containing soy sauce). No gluten-free noodle substitute, dedicated GF menu, or allergy-specific handling was found in any source. This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop; it should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners absent direct confirmation from staff.

caveats: Direct fetches of the Tabelog page and the restaurant's official site both returned HTTP 403 in this environment, so all details are drawn from search-result snippets of secondary sources (tabelog review pages, hotpepper, gnavi, oishi-labo, note.com, e-sousyoku trend report) rather than a single primary-source read; several secondary summaries gave slightly conflicting hours (e.g., one paraphrase suggested Sat/Sun 11:00-23:00), and the hours_week above reflects the most detailed and internally consistent source, which also carried a dated "as of July" caveat matching the current month. Cash-only status could not be confirmed either way. Address is Kanda Misakicho 3-chome, Chiyoda-ku (near Suidobashi/Kudanshita Stations), not Kudankita as the target label suggested, but multiple independent listings agree on this address. The restaurant is confirmed to be a branch (opened 2020) of a multi-location French-ramen chain founded by 縁petit株式会社 (also operating "Dakishimetai" in Osaka), so it is not an independent single-shop operation.

bio.background: The shop's chef/owner, Saka Shota, spent roughly 14 years learning and practicing French cuisine before turning to ramen, and built Koishitai around applying French culinary technique to a ramen bowl.
bio.specialty: Tai-tantanmen (鯛担麺): a rich, creamy broth made by slow-simmering abundant red sea bream (tai) from Uwajima, Ehime Prefecture, using a French long-simmer technique, paired with specially developed wavy noodles designed to hold the broth; the same tai broth is also used in the shop's popular sea-bream rice dish.
bio.philosophy: Fusing French cuisine technique with the ramen format, and treating each bowl as a small piece of visual craftsmanship — the shop deliberately breaks from conventional ramen-shop presentation with a stylish, modern interior and plating.

anecdotes.text: A French-trained chef who spent about 14 years in French cuisine fell in love with ramen and fused the two disciplines to create Koishitai's tai-broth ramen.

Koishitai is the Suidobashi/Kudanshita branch of a tai-tantanmen concept whose sister restaurant, 'Dakishimetai,' originated in Osaka's Higobashi neighborhood, opened by 縁petit株式会社 as part of a French-ramen chain expanding nationally since 2020.
