bio.origin: Kanagawa Prefecture
bio.background: Iwase came to sushi relatively late, having started his culinary career at 21 after an earlier stint as a salaryman. He trained within the well-regarded "Sushi Sho" (すし匠) lineage of Edomae sushi restaurants, working at Sushi Sho Masa in Nishi-Azabu, Sushi Subaru in Aoyama, and Takumi Tatsuhiro in Shinjuku before striking out on his own in September 2012. The restaurant later relocated to its current Nishi-Shinjuku location near Tochomae Station in 2017.
bio.specialty: An alternating omakase of nigiri and otsumami (small savory side dishes) in the "Sushi Sho style," running to roughly 33-35 courses in total. Fish is sourced directly from the Yanagibashi Rengo Ichiba market in Hakata, Fukuoka, with Iwase reportedly traveling there himself to select seasonal seafood before it is shipped to the restaurant.
bio.philosophy: Food writers describe the restaurant's approach as "呑ます鮨" (sushi that makes you want to drink) — richly seasoned tsumami dishes interspersed with delicate nigiri, built around the rhythm of pairing food with sake rather than nigiri alone.

anecdotes.text: A restaurant reviewer describes Iwase's food as carrying on the Sushi Sho lineage in his own style, characterizing the meal as "呑ます鮨" because the richness of the otsumami keeps guests reaching for their sake cup between bites of nigiri.

Iwase trained through several branches of the Sushi Sho family of restaurants (Nishi-Azabu, Aoyama, Shinjuku) before opening his own counter in 2012, later moving to the current Nishi-Shinjuku address in 2017.

caveats: Aggregator sites disagree slightly on exact hours (some list a 16:00-23:00 dinner service with Wed/Sat lunch, others list 15:00-21:30 with Wed-only lunch) and on the closed-Sunday pattern (1st & 3rd Sunday vs. only-2nd-Sunday-open). The record here uses the version corroborated by the most recent, mutually consistent sources. Direct fetches of the official site (sushiiwase.gorp.jp) and Tabelog returned 403 errors in this environment, so all facts are drawn from search-result excerpts of those and third-party listing/blog sites rather than a full page read — treat hours/price as indicative and confirm by phone (03-6279-0149) before visiting. No restaurant-specific statements about gluten-free or vegan accommodation were found anywhere; this is an unmodified, entirely seafood-based Edomae omakase counter.
closed_days: Mondays; 1st and 3rd Sundays of the month (open other Sundays)
cuisine: 本格江戸前鮨 / 握り寿司 (Traditional Edomae nigiri sushi, omakase-only)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, ten-seat, chef-owned sushi counter with no evidence of an English menu or English-speaking staff; reservations are typically made by phone or Japanese booking platforms, so going with a Japanese-speaking companion or a hotel concierge's help is recommended.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per instructions (lat/lng left null). Multiple independent sources place the address roughly a 7-minute walk from Shinjuku Station's Keio New Line exit (Nishi-Shinjuku 3-chome), which is within the Shinjuku Station 0.8km target radius; it is also near Tochomae Station.
gf_detail: No gluten-free protocol or menu was found for this restaurant. It serves a single fixed omakase course of nigiri alternated with tsumami side dishes — traditional Edomae nigiri is finished with a soy-based nikiri/tsume sauce (soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the tsumami courses likely include dashi-based preparations. Because the menu is chef's-choice with no à la carte substitution described anywhere, there is no confirmed way to avoid wheat-based soy sauce or ensure no cross-contact; not recommended as a safe choice for celiac diners without direct, advance confirmation from the restaurant by phone.
hours_raw: Dinner (omakase only): Tue-Sat 15:00-21:30; Lunch: Wed 12:00-14:30 (some sources also list Sat lunch); Closed Mondays and the 1st & 3rd Sunday of each month.
neighborhood: Nishi-Shinjuku (near Tochomae Station), Shinjuku
seats_text: 10-seat counter only
vegan_detail: This is an entirely seafood-based Edomae omakase counter (nigiri and fish/seafood-based tsumami served in a single fixed course) with no vegetarian or vegan menu, alternative, or accommodation found in any source. Not suitable for vegan diners.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区桜丘町29-5 石島ビル1F

bio.origin: The chef trained at the respected "Shina Soba Kazuya" (支那そばかづ屋) in Meguro before opening this shop independently in Shibuya in November 2012.
bio.background: Ramen Kokū is a tiny, tucked-away 9-seat counter shop in the quiet Sakuragaoka backstreets near Shibuya Station, run by an alum of Meguro's well-regarded Kazuya lineage of clear-broth ramen makers.
bio.philosophy: Follows the refined "shina soba" style of its parent lineage: light-looking but deeply flavorful shoyu and shio broths built from animal and seafood dashi, without heavy seasoning. The shop's name, Kokū (穀雨), is one of the 24 traditional solar terms and refers to the spring rains that nourish young grain.
bio.specialty: Wonton ramen (ワンタンメン) — particularly the shio wonton men, often ordered with extra wontons — alongside a shio/shoyu ramen and a tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from 穀雨 (Kokū), one of the 24 traditional Japanese solar terms meaning the spring rains that nourish all grains.
Described by a food blogger as "a Kazuya alum's deeply flavorful clear-broth ramen" (「かづ屋」出身が作る滋味深い清湯ラーメン).

caveats: Direct WebFetch of Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Yahoo Loco, Ekiten, and most Japanese blog URLs returned HTTP 403 (bot-blocked); all facts below were cross-verified via WebSearch's indexed snippets across at least two independent sources (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, NAVITIME, ikkos-films.com, chawanbushi.com) rather than a single direct fetch. No mention of gluten-free or vegan accommodation was found anywhere — this is a traditional wheat-noodle, animal/seafood-broth shina-soba style shop.
closed_days: Sundays, public holidays, and 1st/3rd/5th Saturdays (open for lunch only on 2nd/4th Saturdays)
cuisine: Ramen (shina-soba style shoyu/shio, clear broth, wonton)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only 9-seat counter ramen shop with no English menu or signage found in any source; some Japanese ability will help when ordering.
gf_detail: Traditional wheat ramen noodles and wheat-wrapper wontons; the shoyu-based broths also contain wheat via soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle option, dedicated allergy preparation, or any related accommodation was found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
geocode_note: lat/lng intentionally left null per instructions (no network geocoding). Address places the shop in Sakuragaoka-cho, roughly 400-500m from Shibuya Scramble/Shibuya Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 12:00–15:00 (L.O.), 18:00–20:00 (L.O.); Sat 12:00–15:00 (L.O., open only 2nd & 4th Saturdays); Closed Sun, public holidays, and 1st/3rd/5th Saturdays
neighborhood: 桜丘町 (Sakuragaoka-cho), Shibuya
seats_text: 9 counter seats only
vegan_detail: Broths are built on animal (pork/chicken) and seafood (gyokai) dashi in the shina-soba tradition; the signature dishes are pork chashu and pork/shrimp wontons. No vegetarian or vegan menu items, plant-based broth, or substitution options were found in any source.

address_ja: 東京都渋谷区猿楽町3-7 代官山木下ビル1F

roles: cafe attached to a fitness/swim training complex
bio.background: FLUX CAFE occupies the ground floor of the Daikanyama Kinoshita Building, the same small building that houses the swim facility AQUALAB and the training gym FLUX CONDITIONINGS on the upper floors. The whole complex is run by IMPRINT, the company headed by Olympic gold-medal swimmer Kosuke Kitajima, who serves as general manager of the training side of the operation. The café was built to feed the athletes and gym members training upstairs, then opened its doors to the neighborhood as a health-minded lunch and coffee spot.
bio.origin: Daikanyama, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo — part of an athletic training complex founded by swimmer Kosuke Kitajima's company IMPRINT
bio.specialty: Obanzai lunch set — five rotating home-style Japanese side dishes with miso soup and rice, built around the café's low-fat, low-gluten, high-protein 'athlete's meal' concept
bio.philosophy: The kitchen organizes its menu around two ideas: home-style Japanese obanzai cooking kept as close to gluten-free as practical, and 'athlete's meals' that are low-fat, low-gluten and high-protein for people training hard next door.

anecdotes.text: The café concept pairs 'gluten-free-leaning obanzai home cooking' with 'athlete's meals' — Japanese-based healthy set meals that are low-fat, low-gluten and high-protein, including the five-dish obanzai lunch set with miso soup and rice.
Noodles used for ramen/udon-style dishes and the dorayaki are made with rice flour instead of wheat flour, and the fried chicken is coated in potato starch rather than wheat flour.

FLUX CAFE sits in the same Daikanyama Kinoshita Building as the AQUALAB swim facility and FLUX CONDITIONINGS training gym, both part of the IMPRINT complex overseen by Olympic swimmer Kosuke Kitajima.

caveats: Menu is omnivorous (chicken, egg, dashi-based dishes) with a gluten-reduction focus rather than a certified/strict gluten-free kitchen — soy sauce used contains some wheat, and no dedicated GF fryer or vegan menu could be confirmed. Location sits roughly midway between Shibuya and Daikanyama Stations (walking-distance estimates from independent sources range from about 0.6km to just over 1km to Shibuya Station/Scramble), so it is close to but near the edge of the Shibuya Scramble 0.8km radius — flagging for downstream geocoding to confirm precisely.
closed_days: Irregular closure on the 4th Friday of each month; otherwise open daily
cuisine: Gluten-Free-Leaning Japanese Homestyle Dishes (Obanzai), Athlete's Meals
cultural_comfort_note: The café has an English-language Tabelog listing and Uber Eats presence and sits in cosmopolitan Daikanyama, but menus and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.
gf_detail: The kitchen avoids wheat flour in most dishes — noodles (ramen/udon-style) and dorayaki are made with rice flour, and fried chicken is coated in potato starch rather than wheat flour — but the soy sauce used in seasoning still contains some wheat/gluten, so the menu is described as "close to gluten-free" rather than certified gluten-free. Staff can suggest suitable dishes if asked, but there is no confirmation of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or fully segregated prep for celiac-level safety.
geocode_note: Do not geocode here (network geocoding blocked); address_ja above is the authoritative source for downstream geocoding. Address is 東京都渋谷区猿楽町3-7, Daikanyama Kinoshita Building — same building as AQUALAB and FLUX CONDITIONINGS. Sources give inconsistent walking-distance estimates to Shibuya Station/Scramble (roughly 6-11 minutes / ~0.6-1.0km depending on exit used), and about 7 minutes from Daikanyama Station's north exit, so it sits near the edge of the 0.8km Shibuya Scramble radius — worth confirming precisely once geocoded downstream.
name_ja: フラックス カフェ
neighborhood: Daikanyama / Sarugaku-cho, Shibuya-ku

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy sauce used in the kitchen contains a small amount of wheat/gluten; aside from that, wheat flour itself is largely avoided elsewhere in the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Diners who are mindful of gluten can ask staff, who will suggest suitable menu items accordingly.

positives.text: Noodles used for ramen/udon-style dishes and the dorayaki are made with rice flour, and the fried chicken is coated in potato starch instead of wheat flour, keeping most of the menu low in gluten.

seats_text: Approximately 38 seats (25 counter seats and 13 table seats per Tabelog); some listings additionally mention outdoor terrace seating.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or clear vegan labeling was found. Signature dishes (obanzai sets, oyakodon, chicken bowls) are built around egg, chicken and likely dashi/bonito, so the menu leans omnivorous rather than plant-based. Vegan diners should ask staff directly about ingredient substitutions and dashi base before ordering.

address_ja: 東京都千代田区西神田2-1-11 エスティエラ水道橋 1F・2F

bio.origin: The restaurant's flagship location is in Nakameguro; the Suidobashi branch opened on August 15, 2015 as the group's second location.
bio.background: The head chef is from Tamil Nadu in South India and previously worked at well-known hotels there before bringing his cooking to Tokyo.
bio.specialty: South Indian thali-style 'Meals' sets, dosa, and biryani
bio.philosophy: The kitchen imports its spices and herbs directly from India and cooks South Indian dishes without altering their original flavors. Although the owners are not Muslim themselves, they prepare the entire food menu to halal standards as part of their commitment to quality and hospitality for all guests.

anecdotes.text: スパイスやハーブは全てインドから直輸入したものを使用しており、シェフはタミルナドゥ州の有名なホテルで働いていた南インド出身の方です。
イスラム教徒ではありませんがフードメニューは全てハラールにこだわっています。
水道橋店は2015年8月15日にオープンした2号店で、本店は中目黒にあります。

caveats: Name/URL correction: the task's target name "スリバラジ" and Tabelog ID 13285476 do not match any real listing — that ID resolves to a completely different restaurant (SUN&MOON Asian Dining&Bar, Iwamotocho). Multiple independent sources (Visit Chiyoda official tourism site, Tabelog, search-ethnic.com, japanese-heart.com, Uber Eats, Retty) consistently confirm the real, currently operating restaurant at this Suidobashi location is 南インド料理シリバラジ水道橋店 (Sri Balaji Suidobashi), Tabelog ID 13185693, so that is the record verified here. A proxy/network outage blocked all WebFetch page loads during this verification (even to unrelated test domains), so all facts below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of the cited pages rather than full page fetches — treat hours, seat count, and bio details as reasonably reliable but not independently cross-checked line-by-line. No explicit statement was found addressing asafoetida/hing (a common hidden-wheat risk in Indian cooking), dedicated fryer use, or ghee-free vegan prep — diners with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should confirm directly with staff.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (定休日なし); irregular closures around New Year's holidays and occasional special events.
cuisine: 南インド料理 (South Indian cuisine)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant maintains a full English-language official website with an English menu page, making it easier for non-Japanese-speaking diners to browse the menu and order with confidence.
geocode_note: Not geocoded per policy (lat/lng left null). Address is Nishikanda 2-1-11, Chiyoda-ku, in the Estiera Suidobashi building; multiple sources independently describe the location as a 5-minute walk from Suidobashi Station, well within the 0.8km target radius.
gf_detail: South Indian dishes such as dosa, idli, and rice-based "Meals" sets are naturally rice/lentil-based and often gluten-free by tradition, but no source found addresses whether asafoetida (hing, a very common hidden-wheat ingredient in Indian cooking), spice blends, or fryers are handled to avoid cross-contact with wheat. No dedicated gluten-free fryer or process was confirmed. Diners with celiac disease should ask staff about specific dishes before ordering.
hours_raw: Lunch: Mon-Sun (incl. day-before-holidays and holidays) 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30). Dinner: Mon-Sun (incl. day-before-holidays and holidays) 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). No fixed closed day.
name_ja: 南インド料理 シリバラジ 水道橋店
neighborhood: 水道橋 (Suidobashi), Chiyoda-ku, near Jimbocho

positives.text: 南インドのベジタリアンメニュー代表のサンバルをはじめ、豆のペーストを揚げたワダなどヘルシーでユニークな料理がたくさんあります。また、米と魚のカレーや新鮮な野菜を使用したベジタリアンのメニューもお任せくださいとのことです。

seats_text: 70 seats total; private/party hire available for 15-40 guests
vegan_detail: The restaurant offers South Indian vegetarian dishes (e.g., sambar, vada, vegetable-based "Meals") alongside meat and fish curries, and the whole food menu is prepared to halal standards. However, South Indian cooking commonly uses ghee (dairy) and no source confirmed ghee-free/vegan-specific preparation or dashi-free sauces, so this is vegetarian-friendly with likely-but-unconfirmed vegan options — vegan diners should confirm specific dishes with staff.

address_ja: 東京都新宿区新宿3-31-5 ペガサス館1F

bio.background: A long-established neighborhood Chinese restaurant (町中華, machi-chuka) near Shinjuku Sanchome Station, in business for more than 40 years. The shop occupies the ground floor of the Pegasus-kan building with entrances from both the Shinjuku Higashiguchi shopping street and the Marui department store side, and has long been a go-to spot for local office workers and shoppers for filling, good-value meals.
bio.specialty: Seafood ankake noodles (海鮮うま煤そば) — thick-sauced noodles topped with seafood — along with classic town-Chinese staples such as cold ramen (冷やし中華), tanmen, dandan noodles, and generous set meals like fried chicken cutlet or stir-fried liver with chives served with rice.

anecdotes.text: A Rocket News 24 writer visiting in January 2025 called the seafood ankake noodles so good they didn't want the bowl to end, praising the shop as a 40-plus-year-old neighborhood institution just three minutes from Shinjuku Station's east exit.

caveats: Could not directly fetch several source pages (Tabelog, e-shinjuku.or.jp, Hotpepper, Gnavi, Retty, blog posts) due to a network/proxy block in this session; facts below are drawn from Google-indexed search snippets of those pages, which is a lower-confidence sourcing method than a direct page fetch. Closed-day status is inconsistent across sources: most listings (including the shop's shopping-street association page and general aggregators) report no fixed closed day (年中無休), but at least one Tabelog-linked index entry lists Wednesday as a closed day. Treat closed_days as unconfirmed and call ahead (03-3352-3337) to verify before a special trip. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found anywhere in available sources — this is an old-school wheat-noodle-and-soy-sauce-based town Chinese kitchen with no indication of allergy-specific menu options, so the GF/vegan ratings below are conservative defaults rather than confirmed accommodations. No official restaurant website was located.
closed_days: No confirmed regular closed day; most sources report open year-round (年中無休), but one indexed listing states Wednesday closed — unverified, call ahead to confirm.
cuisine: 中華料理 (Chinese, town-Chinese/machi-chuka style)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a classic local machi-chuka diner with no English website found and no confirmed English menu; expect Japanese-only signage and ordering, though pointing at a photo menu should work fine.
gf_detail: No gluten-free options or labeling found. The menu is built around wheat noodles (ramen, tanmen, dandan noodles, cold ramen) and wheat-wrapper gyoza, and Chinese-style cooking here relies on soy sauce (wheat-containing) for seasoning. No evidence of a dedicated gluten-free fryer or GF-adapted dishes; not recommended for celiac diners.
geocode_note: Address (新宿区新宿3-31-5) and multiple sources place it about 1 minute from Shinjuku Sanchome Station (Marunouchi Line Exit A5) and roughly 3 minutes on foot from Shinjuku Station's east exit — well within the 0.8km Shinjuku Station target radius. neighborhood: 新宿三丁目 (Shinjuku Sanchome)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese-style soy sauce (containing wheat) appears to be used throughout the menu, as is typical for machi-chuka cooking; no wheat-free soy sauce alternative was found in any source.

seats_text: About 70 seats, with separate entrances from the Shinjuku Higashiguchi shopping street and the Marui department store side
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options identified. The menu centers on meat dishes (fried chicken cutlet sets, stir-fried liver with chives) and noodle soups that are very likely made with meat/bone-based broths and oyster sauce, standard for this style of town-Chinese cooking. No vegan labeling or plant-based substitution found in any source.

lead: もつ鍋と焼酎のお店 とうか
address_ja: 東京都渋谷区道玄坂1-11-3 フォースワンビル2F

bio.background: The restaurant's chef previously worked as a cook aboard the luxury cruise liner Queen Elizabeth 2 and at the Hilton Tokyo before opening this motsunabe and shochu izakaya near Shibuya Station.
bio.specialty: Wagyu beef motsunabe (offal hot pot)
bio.philosophy: Built around carefully selected wagyu beef offal and a range of hot-pot broth styles, paired with a large selection of shochu.

anecdotes.text: The wagyu beef offal is prized by regulars for its crisp texture and the sweet, fatty flavor it releases into the broth; the hot pot broth is offered in five flavors, including soy sauce, miso, and pork bone.
The lunchtime wagyu hamburger steak is made from A5-grade wagyu neck meat mixed with salt, pepper, panko breadcrumbs, and onion, and diners choose from ponzu, demi-glace, or yakiniku sauce.
The menu also features raw ham, smoked duck, thick fried omelet, fried squid karaage, and gyoza dumplings alongside the hot pot courses.

caveats: Most target pages (Tabelog, official-style domains touka.gorp.jp / touka1.owst.jp, Retty, Hotpepper, Gurunavi, shibuyachuogai.com, san-tatsu.jp) returned HTTP 403 to direct WebFetch in this environment, so details below are drawn from WebSearch result snippets/summaries of those same pages rather than a full direct fetch; all facts are attributed to the source URL that reported them. No gluten-free or vegan-specific information was found for this restaurant in any source — it is a conventional meat/offal-centered izakaya. Payment method (cash vs. card) was not explicitly confirmed in any source.
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closed days; open daily)
cuisine: Motsunabe (wagyu beef offal hot pot), shabu-shabu, shochu izakaya
cultural_comfort_note: A standard Japanese-language izakaya near Shibuya Station with no confirmed English menu or English-speaking staff, though it is listed on Tabelog's English site.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on wagyu beef offal, shabu-shabu meats, raw ham, and smoked duck; no vegetarian or vegan dishes were identified in any source, and hot-pot broths are meat-based.
